Let $\tilde{X}$ be its horizontal lift, which exists and is smooth by Proposition \ref{prop:horizontal-lift-of-vector-field}. At the point $x$,
\[
\tilde{X}_x \;=\; L_x\bigl(X_{\pi(x)}\bigr) \;=\; L_x\bigl(d\pi_x(v)\bigr) \;=\; v,
\]
the last equality by the uniqueness clause of Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-lift-linear-spec}: $L_x$ is a right inverse of $d\pi_x$ whose image is $H_x$, so it inverts $d\pi_x$ on horizontal vectors. This is the only place the hypothesis $v \in H_x$ is used, and it cannot be dropped: every horizontal lift is horizontal at every point, so a vector outside $H_x$ is not the value of any lift.

blocks.label: ex:riemannian-submersions
blocks.title: Riemannian Submersions

blocks.tex:
Given a Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ and a surjective submersion $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$, it is almost never the case that there is a metric on $M$ that makes $\pi$ into a Riemannian submersion. It is not hard to see why: for this to be the case, whenever $p, q \in M$ are two points in the same fiber $\pi^{-1}(y)$, the linear maps $(d\pi_p|_{H_p})^{-1}: T_y M \to H_p$ both have to pull $\tilde{g}$ back to the same inner product on $T_y M$.

There is, however, an important special case in which there is such a metric. Suppose $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a smooth surjective submersion, and $G$ is a group acting on $\tilde{M}$. (See Appendix C for a review of the basic definitions and terminology regarding group actions on manifolds.) We say that the action is \emph{vertical} if every element $\varphi \in G$ takes each fiber to itself, meaning that $\pi(\varphi \cdot p) = \pi(p)$ for all $p \in M$. The action is \emph{transitive on fibers} if for each $p, q \in \tilde{M}$ such that $\pi(p) = \pi(q)$, there exists $\varphi \in G$ such that $\varphi \cdot p = q$.

If in addition $\tilde{M}$ is endowed with a Riemannian metric, the action is said to be an \emph{isometric action} or an \emph{action by isometries}, and the metric is said to be \emph{invariant under $G$}, if the map $x \mapsto \varphi \cdot x$ is an isometry for each $\varphi \in G$. In that case, provided the action is effective (so that different elements of $G$ define different isometries of $\tilde{M}$), we can identify $G$ with a subgroup of $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$. Since an isometry is, in particular, a diffeomorphism, every isometric action is an action by diffeomorphisms.

blocks.label: def:riemannian-submersion
blocks.title: Riemannian submersion
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ and $(M,g)$ be Riemannian manifolds. A smooth submersion $\pi : \tilde{M} \to M$ is a \emph{Riemannian submersion} if for every $x \in \tilde{M}$ and all $v,w \in H_x$,
\[
g_{\pi(x)}(d\pi_x v, d\pi_x w) = \tilde{g}_x(v,w),
\]
that is, $d\pi_x$ restricts to a linear isometry of $H_x$ onto $T_{\pi(x)}M$. Surjectivity of $d\pi_x|_{H_x}$ is automatic from Lemma \ref{lem:tangent-space-horizontal-vertical-splitting}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsRiemannianSubmersion
lean.code:
def IsRiemannianSubmersion (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (g' : RiemannianMetric I' M')
    (π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) : Prop :=
  IsSubmersion π ∧
    ∀ (x : M) (v w : TangentSpace I x), v ∈ horizontalSpace g π x →
      w ∈ horizontalSpace g π x →
        g'.inner (π x) (mfderiv I I' π x v) (mfderiv I I' π x w) = g.inner x v w

blocks.label: def:submersion-inner
blocks.title: The inner product induced on the base
blocks.body:
Let $\pi : \tilde{M} \to M$ be a smooth submersion and $\tilde{g}$ a Riemannian metric on $\tilde{M}$. For $x \in \tilde{M}$, the \emph{induced form at $x$} is the bilinear form on $T_{\pi(x)}M$
\[
\beta_x(u,v) \;=\; \tilde{g}_x(L_x u, L_x v),
\]
where $L_x$ is the horizontal lift of Definition \ref{def:horizontal-lift-tangent}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.submersionInner
lean.code:
noncomputable def submersionInner (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) (x : M) :
    TangentSpace I' (π x) →L[ℝ] TangentSpace I' (π x) →L[ℝ] ℝ :=
  -- `TangentSpace` carries no norm, so both types must be pinned to the model spaces,
  -- for which they are definitionally equal; this is the `pullbackForm` idiom.
  let L : E' →L[ℝ] E := horizontalLiftAt g π x
  let B : E →L[ℝ] E →L[ℝ] ℝ := g.inner x
  (B.bilinearComp L L : E' →L[ℝ] E' →L[ℝ] ℝ)

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] [IsManifold I' ∞ M'] in
@[simp]

deps.title: The horizontal lift of a tangent vector
deps.label: def:horizontal-lift-tangent

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-submersion-inner-eq
blocks.title: A Riemannian submersion's base metric is the induced form
blocks.body: If $\pi : (\tilde{M},\tilde{g}) \to (M,g)$ is a Riemannian submersion then $g_{\pi(x)} = \beta_x$ for every $x \in \tilde{M}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsRiemannianSubmersion.inner_eq
lean.code:
theorem IsRiemannianSubmersion.inner_eq {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {g' : RiemannianMetric I' M'}
    {π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯} (h : IsRiemannianSubmersion g g' π) (x : M) :
    g'.inner (π x) = submersionInner g π x := by
  ext u v
  rw [submersionInner_apply,
    ← h.2 x _ _ (horizontalLiftAt_mem g π x u) (horizontalLiftAt_mem g π x v),
    mfderiv_horizontalLiftAt g π h.1 x u, mfderiv_horizontalLiftAt g π h.1 x v]

deps.title: The horizontal lift is the unique $B$-orthogonal right inverse
deps.label: lem:horizontal-lift-linear-spec

deps.title: The inner product induced on the base
deps.label: def:submersion-inner

blocks.tex: Let $u,v \in T_{\pi(x)}M$. The vectors $L_x u$ and $L_x v$ are horizontal, so the Riemannian submersion condition gives $g_{\pi(x)}(d\pi_x L_x u, d\pi_x L_x v) = \tilde{g}_x(L_x u, L_x v) = \beta_x(u,v)$. But $d\pi_x L_x u = u$ and $d\pi_x L_x v = v$, so the left side is $g_{\pi(x)}(u,v)$.

blocks.label: prop:riemannian-submersion-metric-unique
blocks.title: Uniqueness of the submersion metric
blocks.body: Let $\tilde{g}$ be a Riemannian metric on $\tilde{M}$ and let $\pi : \tilde{M} \to M$ be a surjective smooth submersion. There is \emph{at most one} Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$ making $\pi$ a Riemannian submersion. This is the uniqueness half of Theorem \ref{thm:group-action-riemannian-submersion}, and it needs no group action.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isRiemannianSubmersion_unique
lean.code:
theorem isRiemannianSubmersion_unique {g : RiemannianMetric I M}
    {g₁ g₂ : RiemannianMetric I' M'} {π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯}
    (h₁ : IsRiemannianSubmersion g g₁ π) (h₂ : IsRiemannianSubmersion g g₂ π)
    (hsurj : Function.Surjective π) : g₁ = g₂ := by
  refine RiemannianMetric.ext_inner fun y => ?_
  obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := hsurj y
  rw [h₁.inner_eq x, h₂.inner_eq x]

end Defs

/-! ## The group action

Lee's hypotheses on the `G`-action, stated separately as he states them.  The
action enters the proof only through `submersionInner_smul`: it is what makes the
inner product on the base independent of the chosen point of the fibre. -/

section Action

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]
  {E' : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E'] [NormedSpace ℝ E'] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E']
  {H' : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H'] {I' : ModelWithCorners ℝ E' H'}
  {M' : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M'] [ChartedSpace H' M'] [IsManifold I' ∞ M']
  {G : Type*} [Group G] [MulAction G M]

variable (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯)

/-- **The action is by isometries** (Lee, §"Riemannian Submersions"): each
`x ↦ a • x` is smooth and its differential preserves `g`.

Lee phrases this as "the map `x ↦ φ · x` is an isometry for each `φ ∈ G`".  Being
a group action by smooth maps, each `a • ·` is automatically a diffeomorphism
(with inverse `a⁻¹ • ·`), so no invertibility need be assumed — it is derived in
`IsIsometricAction.mfderiv_smul_inv_smul`. -/
structure IsIsometricAction : Prop where
  /-- Each group element acts smoothly. -/
  contMDiff : ∀ a : G, ContMDiff I I ∞ (fun x : M => a • x)
  /-- Each group element preserves the metric. -/
  inner_smul : ∀ (a : G) (x : M) (v w : TangentSpace I x),
    g.inner (a • x) (mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x v)
      (mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x w) = g.inner x v w

deps.title: A Riemannian submersion's base metric is the induced form
deps.label: lem:riemannian-submersion-inner-eq

blocks.tex: A Riemannian metric is determined by its inner products. Let $y \in M$ and, by surjectivity, write $y = \pi(x)$. If $g_1$ and $g_2$ both make $\pi$ a Riemannian submersion then Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-submersion-inner-eq} gives $(g_1)_y = \beta_x = (g_2)_y$.
\noindent The existence half is where the group action enters, through the following three lemmas.

blocks.label: lem:mfderiv-smul-inv-smul
blocks.title: The action's differential is invertible
blocks.body: Let $G$ act on $\tilde{M}$ by smooth maps. For $a \in G$ and $x \in \tilde{M}$, the differentials of $a \cdot {-}$ at $x$ and of $a^{-1} \cdot {-}$ at $a \cdot x$ are mutually inverse.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsIsometricAction.mfderiv_smul_inv_smul
lean.code:
theorem IsIsometricAction.mfderiv_smul_inv_smul (h : IsIsometricAction (G := G) g) (a : G) (x : M)
    (w : TangentSpace I (a • x)) :
    mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x
        (mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a⁻¹ • y) (a • x) w) = w := by
  have hcomp : ((fun y : M => a • y) ∘ (fun y : M => a⁻¹ • y)) = id :=
    funext fun y => smul_inv_smul a y
  have h1 := mfderiv_comp_apply_of_eq (I := I) (I' := I) (I'' := I) (x := a • x)
    (g := fun y : M => a • y) (f := fun y : M => a⁻¹ • y)
    (h.mdifferentiableAt a x) (h.mdifferentiableAt a⁻¹ (a • x)) (inv_smul_smul a x) w
  rw [← h1, hcomp, mfderiv_id]
  rfl

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] [IsManifold I' ∞ M'] in

blocks.tex: Apply the chain rule to $(a \cdot {-}) \circ (a^{-1} \cdot {-}) = \mathrm{Id}$ at the point $a \cdot x$, noting $a^{-1} \cdot (a \cdot x) = x$, and use that the differential of the identity is the identity.

blocks.label: lem:horizontal-space-smul
blocks.title: A vertical action preserves the splitting
blocks.body: Let $\pi : \tilde{M} \to M$ be a smooth submersion and let $G$ act on $\tilde{M}$ by smooth maps preserving $\tilde{g}$, with $\pi(a \cdot x) = \pi(x)$ for all $a, x$. Then $d(a \cdot {-})_x$ carries $V_x$ onto $V_{a \cdot x}$, and carries $H_x$ into $H_{a \cdot x}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.horizontalSpace_smul
lean.code:
theorem horizontalSpace_smul (h : IsIsometricAction (G := G) g)
    (hvert : IsVerticalAction π (G := G)) (a : G) (x : M)
    {v : TangentSpace I x} (hv : v ∈ horizontalSpace g π x) :
    mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x v ∈ horizontalSpace g π (a • x) := by
  intro w hw
  obtain ⟨z, hz, rfl⟩ := verticalSpace_smul_surj h hvert a x hw
  rw [h.inner_smul a x v z]
  exact hv z hz

omit [IsManifold I' ∞ M'] in

blocks.tex:
Differentiating $\pi \circ (a \cdot {-}) = \pi$ gives $d\pi_{a \cdot x} \circ d(a \cdot {-})_x = d\pi_x$. Hence if $d\pi_x z = 0$ then $d\pi_{a\cdot x}(d(a\cdot{-})_x z) = 0$, so $d(a \cdot {-})_x$ maps $V_x$ into $V_{a \cdot x}$. Conversely, given $w \in V_{a \cdot x}$, put $z = d(a^{-1} \cdot {-})_{a \cdot x} w$; by Lemma \ref{lem:mfderiv-smul-inv-smul}, $d(a\cdot{-})_x z = w$, and then $d\pi_x z = d\pi_{a \cdot x} w = 0$, so $z \in V_x$. Thus $V_{a \cdot x}$ is the image of $V_x$.

Now let $v \in H_x$ and $w \in V_{a \cdot x}$. Write $w = d(a \cdot {-})_x z$ with $z \in V_x$, as just shown. Since the action preserves $\tilde{g}$,
\[
\tilde{g}_{a \cdot x}(d(a\cdot{-})_x v, w) = \tilde{g}_{a \cdot x}(d(a\cdot{-})_x v, d(a\cdot{-})_x z) = \tilde{g}_x(v,z) = 0,
\]
the last step because $v$ is horizontal and $z$ is vertical. So $d(a\cdot{-})_x v \in H_{a \cdot x}$.

blocks.label: lem:submersion-inner-smul
blocks.title: The induced form is constant on fibers
blocks.body: With hypotheses as in Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-space-smul}, $\beta_{a \cdot x} = \beta_x$ for every $a \in G$ and $x \in \tilde{M}$ (an identity of bilinear forms on $T_{\pi(x)}M = T_{\pi(a \cdot x)}M$).

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.submersionInner_smul
lean.code:
theorem submersionInner_smul (hπ : IsSubmersion π) (h : IsIsometricAction (G := G) g)
    (hvert : IsVerticalAction π (G := G)) (a : G) (x : M) :
    ((submersionInner g π (a • x) : _) : E' →L[ℝ] E' →L[ℝ] ℝ)
      = ((submersionInner g π x : _) : E' →L[ℝ] E' →L[ℝ] ℝ) := by
  -- `L_{a • x} = d(a • ·)_x ∘ L_x`, by uniqueness of the horizontal lift at `a • x`.
  have key : ∀ u : E', ((horizontalLiftAt g π (a • x) u : _) : E)
      = mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x (horizontalLiftAt g π x u) := by
    have huniq := (horizontalLiftAt_unique g π hπ (a • x)
      (L := ((mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x : _) : E →L[ℝ] E).comp
        ((horizontalLiftAt g π x : _) : E' →L[ℝ] E))
      (fun u => by
        show mfderiv I I' π (a • x)
          (mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x (horizontalLiftAt g π x u)) = u
        rw [mfderiv_comp_smul h hvert a x]
        exact mfderiv_horizontalLiftAt g π hπ x u)
      (fun u => horizontalSpace_smul h hvert a x (horizontalLiftAt_mem g π x u))).symm
    exact fun u => congrArg (fun L => L u) huniq
  ext u v
  show g.inner (a • x) (horizontalLiftAt g π (a • x) u) (horizontalLiftAt g π (a • x) v)
    = g.inner x (horizontalLiftAt g π x u) (horizontalLiftAt g π x v)
  rw [show (horizontalLiftAt g π (a • x) u : TangentSpace I (a • x))
        = mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x (horizontalLiftAt g π x u) from key u,
    show (horizontalLiftAt g π (a • x) v : TangentSpace I (a • x))
        = mfderiv I I (fun y : M => a • y) x (horizontalLiftAt g π x v) from key v]
  exact h.inner_smul a x _ _

end Action

/-! ## Theorem 2.28: existence of the quotient metric

Everything above is in place: `submersionInner` is the only candidate
(`isRiemannianSubmersion_unique`), and `submersionInner_smul` says it does not
depend on which point of the fibre computes it.  What remains is to turn that
into a `RiemannianMetric` on `M'` — to choose a point in each fibre, and to check
that the resulting field is symmetric, positive definite and *smooth*.

The choice is `Function.surjInv`, which is where surjectivity of `π` is used; the
choice is harmless precisely by `submersionInner_congr`.  Smoothness is the only
analytic step, and it is the reason Appendix A.17 exists: `surjInv` is not
smooth — it is not even continuous — so it cannot be differentiated.  Instead one
replaces it, *near each point*, by a smooth local section `σ`, which represents
the same field because the fibre point does not matter, and then

  `g'_y = g_{σ y}(L_{σ y} ·, L_{σ y} ·)`

is a composite of smooth things: `σ` is smooth, `g` is a smooth 2-tensor field,
and `x ↦ L_x` is smooth in tangent coordinates
(`contMDiffAt_horizontalLiftAt_inTangentCoordinates`).  Assembling those three is
`contMDiffAt_bilinearCompOf`, whose generality — an *arbitrary* smooth family of
linear maps rather than a differential — exists exactly for this: `L` is not the
differential of any map.

`I.Boundaryless` is inherited from `LeeLib.AppendixA.exists_localSection`, and is
needed only upstairs; `M'` may have boundary. -/

section Existence

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H} [I.Boundaryless]
  -- … (truncated)

deps.title: The horizontal lift is the unique $B$-orthogonal right inverse
deps.label: lem:horizontal-lift-linear-spec

deps.title: A vertical action preserves the splitting
deps.label: lem:horizontal-space-smul

deps.title: The inner product induced on the base
deps.label: def:submersion-inner

blocks.tex:
Write $\Phi = d(a \cdot {-})_x$. We claim $L_{a \cdot x} = \Phi \circ L_x$. By the uniqueness in Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-lift-linear-spec} it suffices to check that $\Phi \circ L_x$ is a horizontal right inverse of $d\pi_{a \cdot x}$. It is a right inverse: $d\pi_{a \cdot x}(\Phi(L_x u)) = d\pi_x(L_x u) = u$, using $d\pi_{a \cdot x} \circ \Phi = d\pi_x$ from the proof of Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-space-smul}. It is horizontal: $L_x u \in H_x$, so $\Phi(L_x u) \in H_{a \cdot x}$ by Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-space-smul}.

Therefore, for $u,v \in T_{\pi(x)}M$,
\[
\beta_{a \cdot x}(u,v) = \tilde{g}_{a \cdot x}(\Phi(L_x u), \Phi(L_x v)) = \tilde{g}_x(L_x u, L_x v) = \beta_x(u,v),
\]
the middle step because the action preserves $\tilde{g}$.

blocks.label: lem:submersion-inner-congr
blocks.title: The induced form depends only on the fiber
blocks.body: With hypotheses as in Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-space-smul}, suppose in addition that $G$ acts transitively on the fibers of $\pi$. If $\pi(x) = \pi(y)$ then $\beta_x = \beta_y$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.submersionInner_congr
lean.code:
theorem submersionInner_congr (hπ : IsSubmersion π) (h : IsIsometricAction (G := G) g)
    (hvert : IsVerticalAction π (G := G)) (htrans : IsFibreTransitiveAction π (G := G))
    {x y : M} (hxy : π x = π y) :
    ((submersionInner g π y : _) : E' →L[ℝ] E' →L[ℝ] ℝ)
      = ((submersionInner g π x : _) : E' →L[ℝ] E' →L[ℝ] ℝ) := by
  obtain ⟨a, rfl⟩ := htrans x y hxy
  exact submersionInner_smul hπ h hvert a x

omit [I.Boundaryless] [IsManifold I' ∞ M'] in

deps.title: A vertical action preserves the splitting
deps.label: lem:horizontal-space-smul

deps.title: The inner product induced on the base
deps.label: def:submersion-inner

deps.title: The induced form is constant on fibers
deps.label: lem:submersion-inner-smul

blocks.tex: By transitivity on fibers there is $a \in G$ with $y = a \cdot x$, and then $\beta_y = \beta_{a \cdot x} = \beta_x$ by Lemma \ref{lem:submersion-inner-smul}.

blocks.label: lem:horizontal-lift-in-coordinates-smooth
blocks.title: The horizontal lift is smooth in tangent coordinates
blocks.body: Let $\pi : \tilde{M} \to M$ be a smooth submersion of a Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ and let $x_0 \in \tilde{M}$. The family of linear maps $x \mapsto L_x : T_{\pi(x)}M \to T_x\tilde{M}$, read in tangent coordinates around $x_0$ and around $\pi(x_0)$, is smooth at $x_0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.contMDiffAt_horizontalLiftAt_inTangentCoordinates
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffAt_horizontalLiftAt_inTangentCoordinates (hπ : IsSubmersion π) (x₀ : M) :
    ContMDiffAt I 𝓘(ℝ, E' →L[ℝ] E) ∞
      (inTangentCoordinates I' I (fun x => π x) id (fun x => horizontalLiftAt g π x) x₀) x₀ := by
  have hx₀ : x₀ ∈ (trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀).baseSet := by simp
  have hy₀ : π x₀ ∈ (trivializationAt E' (TangentSpace I') (π x₀)).baseSet := by simp
  -- The trivialized metric is still positive definite: `θ` is an isomorphism.
  have hBpos : ∀ v : E, v ≠ 0 → 0 < metricInCoordinates g x₀ x₀ v v := by
    intro v hv
    rw [metricInCoordinates_apply g x₀ x₀ (mem_chart_source H x₀)]
    refine g.pos x₀ _ ?_
    exact fun h => hv (((trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀).continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ x₀
      hx₀).symm.map_eq_zero_iff.mp h)
  -- The trivialized differential is still surjective: it is `dπ` between two isomorphisms.
  have hAsurj : Function.Surjective (mfderivInCoordinates π x₀ x₀) := by
    intro w
    set ι₀ := (trivializationAt E' (TangentSpace I') (π x₀)).continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ (π x₀) hy₀
    set θ₀ := (trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀).continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ x₀ hx₀
    obtain ⟨s, hs⟩ := hπ x₀ (ι₀.symm w)
    refine ⟨θ₀ s, ?_⟩
    rw [mfderivInCoordinates_apply]
    have hθ : (trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀).symmL ℝ x₀ (θ₀ s) = s := θ₀.symm_apply_apply s
    rw [hθ, hs, ← Bundle.Trivialization.coe_continuousLinearEquivAt_eq (R := ℝ) _ hy₀]
    exact ι₀.apply_symm_apply w
  -- Near `x₀`, the lift in tangent coordinates is the lift of the trivialized data.
  have key : (inTangentCoordinates I' I (fun x => π x) id (fun x => horizontalLiftAt g π x) x₀)
      =ᶠ[nhds x₀] (fun x => horizontalLift (metricInCoordinates g x₀ x)
        (mfderivInCoordinates π x₀ x)) := by
    filter_upwards [((chartAt H x₀).open_source).mem_nhds (mem_chart_source H x₀),
      π.contMDiff.continuous.continuousAt.preimage_mem_nhds
        (((chartAt H' (π x₀)).open_source).mem_nhds (mem_chart_source H' (π x₀)))]
      with x hx hy
    have hxb : x ∈ (trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀).baseSet := by simpa using hx
    have hyb : π x ∈ (trivializationAt E' (TangentSpace I') (π x₀)).baseSet := by simpa using hy
    set θ := (trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀).continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ x hxb
    set ι := (trivializationAt E' (TangentSpace I') (π x₀)).continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ (π x) hyb
    have hB' : ∀ a b : E, metricInCoordinates g x₀ x a b = g.inner x (θ.symm a) (θ.symm b) :=
      metricInCoordinates_apply g x₀ x hx
    have hA' : ∀ a : E, mfderivInCoordinates π x₀ x a = ι (mfderiv I I' π x (θ.symm a)) := by
      intro a
      rw [mfderivInCoordinates_apply,
        ← Bundle.Trivialization.coe_continuousLinearEquivAt_eq (R := ℝ) _ hyb]
      rfl
    ext u
    rw [horizontalLift_congr (E := E) (E' := E') (F := E) (F' := E')
      (inner_pos g x) (hπ x) θ ι hB' hA' u]
    show inTangentCoordinates I' I (fun x => π x) id (fun x => horizontalLiftAt g π x) x₀ x u = _
    simp only [inTangentCoordinates, id_eq]
    rw [ContinuousLinearMap.inCoordinates_eq hyb hxb]
    rfl
  rw [key.contMDiffAt_iff]
  exact contMDiffAt_horizontalLift (contMDiffAt_metricInCoordinates g x₀)
    (contMDiffAt_mfderivInCoordinates π x₀) hBpos hAsurj

deps.title: The horizontal lift is the unique $B$-orthogonal right inverse
deps.label: lem:horizontal-lift-linear-spec

deps.title: The horizontal lift depends smoothly on the data
deps.label: lem:horizontal-lift-smooth-in-data

deps.title: The horizontal lift of a tangent vector
deps.label: def:horizontal-lift-tangent

blocks.tex: Read in the trivializations $\theta$ of $T\tilde{M}$ at $x_0$ and $\iota$ of $TM$ at $\pi(x_0)$, the data of $L_x$ becomes the pair $(B(x), A(x))$, where $B(x)$ is $\tilde{g}_x$ and $A(x)$ is $d\pi_x$ so read; $B$ is smooth at $x_0$ because $\tilde{g}$ is a smooth $2$-tensor field, and $A$ is smooth at $x_0$ because $\pi$ is smooth. Moreover $B(x_0)$ is positive definite and $A(x_0)$ is surjective, since $\theta$ and $\iota$ are isomorphisms and $\pi$ is a submersion. By the naturality of the lift under a pair of linear isomorphisms, the lift of the transported data is the transported lift, that is $\theta \circ L_x \circ \iota^{-1} = L(B(x), A(x))$, which is exactly $L_x$ read in tangent coordinates. Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-lift-smooth-in-data} says the right-hand side is smooth at $x_0$.

blocks.label: thm:group-action-riemannian-submersion
blocks.title: thm:group-action-riemannian-submersion
blocks.body: Let $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ be a Riemannian manifold, let $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ be a surjective smooth submersion, and let $G$ be a group acting on $\tilde{M}$. If the action is isometric, vertical, and transitive on fibers, then there is a unique Riemannian metric on $M$ such that $\pi$ is a Riemannian submersion.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.existsUnique_isRiemannianSubmersion_metric
lean.code:
theorem existsUnique_isRiemannianSubmersion_metric (hπ : IsSubmersion π)
    (h : IsIsometricAction (G := G) g) (hvert : IsVerticalAction π (G := G))
    (htrans : IsFibreTransitiveAction π (G := G)) (hsurj : Function.Surjective π) :
    ∃! g' : RiemannianMetric I' M', IsRiemannianSubmersion g g' π :=
  ⟨quotientMetric g π hπ h hvert htrans hsurj,
    isRiemannianSubmersion_quotientMetric hπ h hvert htrans hsurj,
    fun _ hg' => isRiemannianSubmersion_unique hg'
      (isRiemannianSubmersion_quotientMetric hπ h hvert htrans hsurj) hsurj⟩

deps.title: The horizontal lift is smooth in tangent coordinates
deps.label: lem:horizontal-lift-in-coordinates-smooth

deps.title: Transport by a Smooth Family of Linear Maps is Smooth
deps.label: lem:bilinear-comp-of-family-smooth

deps.title: The inner product induced on the base
deps.label: def:submersion-inner

deps.title: The induced form depends only on the fiber
deps.label: lem:submersion-inner-congr

blocks.tex:
Uniqueness is Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-submersion-metric-unique}. For existence, define $g_y = \beta_x$ for any $x$ with $\pi(x) = y$; this is legitimate because if $\pi(x) = \pi(x')$ then $\beta_x = \beta_{x'}$ by Lemma \ref{lem:submersion-inner-congr} (whose hypotheses hold: the action is isometric, vertical, and transitive on fibers).

$g_y$ is symmetric because $\tilde{g}_x$ is. It is positive definite: if $u \neq 0$ then $L_x u \neq 0$, since $d\pi_x(L_x u) = u \neq 0$, and hence $\beta_x(u,u) = \tilde{g}_x(L_x u, L_x u) > 0$.

For smoothness, fix $y_0 \in M$ and choose $x_0$ with $\pi(x_0) = y_0$. By Proposition \ref{prop:local-section-manifold} there is a smooth local section $\sigma$ of $\pi$ near $y_0$ with $\sigma(y_0) = x_0$. For $y$ near $y_0$ we have $\pi(\sigma(y)) = y$, so by well-definedness $g_y = \beta_{\sigma(y)}$, that is,
\[
g_y(u,v) = \tilde{g}_{\sigma(y)}(L_{\sigma(y)} u, L_{\sigma(y)} v).
\]
The right-hand side is the transport of the smooth field $\tilde{g}$ by the family of linear maps $y \mapsto L_{\sigma(y)} : T_yM \to T_{\sigma(y)}\tilde{M}$, in the sense of Definition \ref{def:bilinear-comp-of-family}, so Lemma \ref{lem:bilinear-comp-of-family-smooth} applies once its two hypotheses are checked. The base map $\sigma$ is smooth at $y_0$; and the family $y \mapsto L_{\sigma(y)}$ is smooth at $y_0$ in tangent coordinates, being the composite of $\sigma$ with $x \mapsto L_x$, which is smooth in tangent coordinates by Lemma \ref{lem:horizontal-lift-in-coordinates-smooth}. (Here the reference points match: $\sigma(y_0) = x_0$ upstairs and $\pi(x_0) = y_0$ downstairs.) Hence $g$ is smooth at $y_0$, and $y_0$ was arbitrary, so $g$ is a smooth $2$-tensor field.

Note that this is the step for which Lemma \ref{lem:bilinear-comp-of-family-smooth} is stated for an arbitrary family of linear maps rather than for a family of differentials: $L_{\sigma(y)}$ is not $d\sigma_y$, and is not the differential of any map --- $\sigma$ is a section of $\pi$, but not a horizontal one.

Finally $g$ makes $\pi$ a Riemannian submersion: for $v,w \in H_x$, $L_x(d\pi_x v) = v$ and $L_x(d\pi_x w) = w$, so $g_{\pi(x)}(d\pi_x v, d\pi_x w) = \beta_x(d\pi_x v, d\pi_x w) = \tilde{g}_x(v,w)$.
The next corollary describes one important situation to which the preceding theorem applies.

blocks.label: cor:quotient-manifold-lie-group-riemannian-metric
blocks.title: cor:quotient-manifold-lie-group-riemannian-metric
blocks.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is a Riemannian manifold, and $G$ is a Lie group acting smoothly, freely, properly, and isometrically on $\tilde{M}$. Then the orbit space $M = \tilde{M}/G$ has a unique smooth manifold structure and Riemannian metric such that $\pi$ is a Riemannian submersion.

blocks.tex: Under the given hypotheses, the quotient manifold theorem (Thm. C.17) shows that $M$ has a unique smooth manifold structure such that the quotient map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a smooth submersion. It follows easily from the definitions in that case that the given action of $G$ on $\tilde{M}$ is vertical and transitive on fibers. Since the action is also isometric, Theorem 2.28 shows that $M$ inherits a unique Riemannian metric making $\pi$ into a Riemannian submersion.
Here is an important example of a Riemannian metric defined in this way. A larger class of such metrics is described in Problem 2-7.

blocks.label: ex:fubini-study-metric
blocks.title: The Fubini--Study Metric
blocks.body:
Let $n$ be a positive integer, and consider the complex projective space $\mathbb{CP}^n$ defined in Example C.19. That example shows that the map $\pi : \mathbb{C}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\} \to \mathbb{CP}^n$ sending each point in $\mathbb{C}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\}$ to its span is a surjective smooth submersion. Identifying $\mathbb{C}^{n+1}$ with $\mathbb{R}^{2n+2}$ endowed with its Euclidean metric, we can view the unit sphere $S^{2n+1}$ with its round metric $\bar{g}$ as an embedded Riemannian submanifold of $\mathbb{C}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\}$. Let $p: S^{2n+1} \to \mathbb{CP}^n$ denote the restriction of the map $\pi$. Then $p$ is smooth, and it is surjective because every 1-dimensional complex subspace contains elements of unit norm. We need to show that it is a submersion. Let $z_0 \in S^{2n+1}$ and set $\zeta_0 = p(z_0) \in \mathbb{CP}^n$. Since $\pi$ is a smooth submersion, it has a smooth local section $\sigma : U \to \mathbb{C}^{n+1}$ defined on a neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ and satisfying $\sigma(\zeta_0) = z_0$ (Thm. A.17). Let $\nu: \mathbb{C}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\} \to S^{2n+1}$ be the radial projection onto the sphere:
\[
\nu(z) = \frac{z}{|z|}.
\]

Since dividing an element of $\mathbb{C}^{n+1}$ by a nonzero scalar does not change its span, it follows that $p \circ \nu = \pi$. Therefore, if we set $\bar{\sigma} = \nu \circ \sigma$, we have $p \circ \bar{\sigma} = p \circ \nu \circ \sigma = \pi \circ \sigma = \text{Id}_U$, so $\bar{\sigma}$ is a local section of $p$. By Theorem A.17, this shows that $p$ is a submersion.

Define an action of $S^1$ on $S^{2n+1}$ by complex multiplication:
\[
\lambda \cdot (z^1,\ldots,z^{n+1}) = (\lambda z^1,\ldots,\lambda z^{n+1}),
\]
for $\lambda \in S^1$ (viewed as a complex number of norm 1) and $z = (z^1,\ldots,z^{n+1}) \in S^{2n+1}$. This is easily seen to be isometric, vertical, and transitive on fibers of $p$. By Theorem 2.28, therefore, there is a unique metric on $\mathbb{CP}^n$ such that the map $p: S^{2n+1} \to \mathbb{CP}^n$ is a Riemannian submersion. This metric is called the \textbf{Fubini--Study metric}; you will have a chance to study its geometric properties in Problems 3-19 and 8-13.

blocks.tex: Another important special case of Riemannian submersions occurs in the context of covering maps. Suppose $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ and $(M, g)$ are Riemannian manifolds. A smooth covering map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is called a \textbf{Riemannian covering} if it is a local isometry.

blocks.label: def:smooth-covering-map
blocks.title: def:smooth-covering-map
blocks.body:
A smooth map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a \emph{smooth covering map} if it is surjective, is a topological covering map, and is a local diffeomorphism.

By Proposition A.49(c) a topological covering map is a smooth covering map, in the sense of Appendix A, exactly when it is a local diffeomorphism, so this description agrees with the sheetwise one.

deps.title: Elementary Properties of Smooth Covering Maps
deps.label: prop:elementary-covering-maps

blocks.label: def:covering-automorphism-group
blocks.title: def:covering-automorphism-group
blocks.body: Let $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ be a smooth covering map. A \emph{covering automorphism} of $\pi$ is a diffeomorphism $\varphi: \tilde{M} \to \tilde{M}$ satisfying $\pi \circ \varphi = \pi$. The covering automorphisms form a group $\operatorname{Aut}_\pi(\tilde{M})$ under composition, which acts on $\tilde{M}$ by evaluation.

blocks.label: def:normal-covering-fibre-transitive
blocks.title: def:normal-covering-fibre-transitive
blocks.body:
A smooth covering map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a \emph{normal covering} if $\operatorname{Aut}_\pi(\tilde{M})$ acts transitively on each fiber of $\pi$.

By Proposition C.21 this holds if and only if the image of $\pi_*: \pi_1(\tilde{M}, \tilde{x}) \to \pi_1(M, \pi(\tilde{x}))$ is a normal subgroup of $\pi_1(M, \pi(\tilde{x}))$, which is the condition the name refers to.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsNormalCovering
lean.code:
def IsNormalCovering (π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) : Prop :=
  IsFibreTransitiveAction (G := CoveringAut π) π

blocks.label: def:covering-automorphism-invariant-metric
blocks.title: def:covering-automorphism-invariant-metric
blocks.body: Let $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ be a smooth covering map. A metric $\tilde{g}$ on $\tilde{M}$ is \emph{invariant under all covering automorphisms} if $\varphi^*\tilde{g} = \tilde{g}$ for every $\varphi \in \operatorname{Aut}_\pi(\tilde{M})$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsCoveringAutInvariant
lean.code:
def IsCoveringAutInvariant (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) : Prop :=
  ∀ φ : CoveringAut π, IsMetricPreserving g g (φ : M → M)

blocks.label: def:riemannian-covering
blocks.title: def:riemannian-covering
blocks.body: Let $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ and $(M, g)$ be Riemannian manifolds. A smooth covering map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a \emph{Riemannian covering} if $\pi^*g = \tilde{g}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsRiemannianCovering
lean.code:
def IsRiemannianCovering (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (g' : RiemannianMetric I' M')
    (π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) : Prop :=
  IsSmoothCoveringMap π ∧ IsMetricPreserving g g' (π : M → M')

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] in

blocks.tex: Lee phrases the definition as ``a smooth covering map that is a local isometry''; the two agree, because a covering map is already a local diffeomorphism.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-covering-local-isometry
blocks.title: A Riemannian Covering Is a Local Isometry
blocks.body: Every Riemannian covering is a local isometry.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsRiemannianCovering.isLocalIsometry
lean.code:
theorem IsRiemannianCovering.isLocalIsometry
    {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {g' : RiemannianMetric I' M'} {π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯}
    (h : IsRiemannianCovering g g' π) : IsLocalIsometry g g' (π : M → M') :=
  ⟨h.1.isLocalDiffeomorph, h.2⟩

/-! ## An equidimensional Riemannian submersion is a local isometry -/

variable {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {g' : RiemannianMetric I' M'} {π : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯}

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] in

blocks.tex: A smooth covering map is a local diffeomorphism, and a Riemannian covering satisfies $\pi^*g = \tilde{g}$ by definition; a local diffeomorphism that preserves the metric is a local isometry. No hypothesis on dimensions is needed: unlike in Exercise \ref{exer:characterize-local-isometry}, the local diffeomorphism is given rather than deduced, so the inverse function theorem does not enter.

blocks.label: prop:equidimensional-riemannian-submersion-local-isometry
blocks.title: prop:equidimensional-riemannian-submersion-local-isometry
blocks.body: Let $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ be a smooth submersion whose differential $d\pi_x$ is injective at every $x \in \tilde{M}$, and let $\tilde{g}$ and $g$ be metrics on $\tilde{M}$ and $M$. Then $\pi$ is a Riemannian submersion if and only if $\pi^*g = \tilde{g}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isRiemannianSubmersion_iff_isMetricPreserving
lean.code:
theorem isRiemannianSubmersion_iff_isMetricPreserving (hπ : IsSubmersion π)
    (hinj : ∀ x : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' π x)) :
    IsRiemannianSubmersion g g' π ↔ IsMetricPreserving g g' (π : M → M') := by
  refine ⟨fun h p => ?_, fun h => ⟨hπ, fun x v w _ _ => ?_⟩⟩
  · ext v w
    rw [pullbackForm_apply]
    exact h.2 p v w (by rw [horizontalSpace_eq_top g π (hinj p)]; trivial)
      (by rw [horizontalSpace_eq_top g π (hinj p)]; trivial)
  · exact h.inner_mfderiv x v w

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] in

blocks.tex:
Suppose first that $\pi$ is a Riemannian submersion, and fix $x \in \tilde{M}$. The vertical space $V_x = \ker d\pi_x$ is trivial because $d\pi_x$ is injective, so the horizontal space $H_x$, consisting of the vectors $g$-orthogonal to every vertical vector, is all of $T_x\tilde{M}$. The defining condition of a Riemannian submersion therefore applies to every pair $v, w \in T_x\tilde{M}$ and reads $g_{\pi(x)}(d\pi_x v, d\pi_x w) = \tilde{g}_x(v, w)$, which is $(\pi^*g)_x = \tilde{g}_x$.

Conversely, suppose $\pi^*g = \tilde{g}$. Then $g_{\pi(x)}(d\pi_x v, d\pi_x w) = \tilde{g}_x(v, w)$ for all $v, w \in T_x\tilde{M}$, and in particular for horizontal $v$ and $w$; since $\pi$ is a submersion by hypothesis, $\pi$ is a Riemannian submersion.

blocks.label: prop:riemannian-covering-metric
blocks.title: prop:riemannian-covering-metric
blocks.body: Suppose $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a smooth normal covering map, and $\tilde{g}$ is any metric on $\tilde{M}$ that is invariant under all covering automorphisms. Then there is a unique metric $g$ on $M$ such that $\pi$ is a Riemannian covering.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.existsUnique_isRiemannianCovering_metric
lean.code:
theorem existsUnique_isRiemannianCovering_metric (hπ : IsSmoothCoveringMap π)
    (hnormal : IsNormalCovering π) (hinv : IsCoveringAutInvariant g π) :
    ∃! g' : RiemannianMetric I' M', IsRiemannianCovering g g' π := by
  have hsub : IsSubmersion π := hπ.isSubmersion
  have hbridge : ∀ g' : RiemannianMetric I' M',
      IsRiemannianSubmersion g g' π ↔ IsRiemannianCovering g g' π := fun g' =>
    (isRiemannianSubmersion_iff_isMetricPreserving hsub hπ.injective_mfderiv).trans
      ⟨fun h => ⟨hπ, h⟩, fun h => h.2⟩
  obtain ⟨g', hg', huniq⟩ := existsUnique_isRiemannianSubmersion_metric (G := CoveringAut π)
    hsub (isIsometricAction_coveringAut hinv) isVerticalAction_coveringAut hnormal hπ.surjective
  exact ⟨g', (hbridge g').mp hg', fun g'' h => huniq g'' ((hbridge g'').mpr h)⟩

deps.title: Elementary Properties of Smooth Covering Maps
deps.label: prop:elementary-covering-maps

blocks.tex: Proposition A.49 shows that $\pi$ is a surjective smooth submersion. The automorphism group acts vertically by definition, and Proposition C.21 shows that it acts transitively on fibers when the covering is normal. It then follows from Theorem 2.28 that there is a unique metric $g$ on $M$ such that $\pi$ is a Riemannian submersion. Since $\pi$ is a local diffeomorphism, each $d\pi_x$ is injective, so by Proposition \ref{prop:equidimensional-riemannian-submersion-local-isometry} the metrics making $\pi$ a Riemannian submersion are exactly those making $\pi$ a Riemannian covering. Hence $g$ is the unique metric on $M$ for which $\pi$ is a Riemannian covering.

blocks.label: prop:riemannian-quotient-metric
blocks.title: prop:riemannian-quotient-metric
blocks.body: Suppose $(\widetilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is a Riemannian manifold, and $\Gamma$ is a discrete Lie group acting smoothly, freely, properly, and isometrically on $\widetilde{M}$. Then $\widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ has a unique Riemannian metric such that the quotient map $\pi: \widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ is a normal Riemannian covering.

blocks.tex: Proposition C.23 shows that $\pi$ is a smooth normal covering map, and Proposition 2.31 shows that $M = \widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ has a unique Riemannian metric such that $\pi$ is a Riemannian covering.

blocks.label: cor:isometric-to-quotient
blocks.title: cor:isometric-to-quotient
blocks.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(\widetilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ are connected Riemannian manifolds, $\pi: \widetilde{M} \to M$ is a normal Riemannian covering map, and $\Gamma = \text{Aut}_\pi(\widetilde{M})$. Then $M$ is isometric to $\widetilde{M}/\Gamma$.

blocks.tex: Proposition C.20 shows that with the discrete topology, $\Gamma$ is a discrete Lie group acting smoothly, freely, and properly on $\widetilde{M}$, and then Proposition C.23 shows that $\widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ is a smooth manifold and the quotient map $q: \widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ is a smooth normal covering map. The fact that both $\pi$ and $q$ are normal coverings implies that $\Gamma$ acts transitively on the fibers of both maps, so the two maps are constant on each other's fibers. Proposition A.19 then implies that there is a diffeomorphism $F: M \to \widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ that satisfies $q \circ F = \pi$. Because both $q$ and $\pi$ are local isometries, $F$ is too, and because it is bijective it is a global isometry.

blocks.label: ex:real-projective-space-metric
blocks.title: ex:real-projective-space-metric
blocks.body: The two-element group $\Gamma = \{\pm1\}$ acts smoothly, freely, properly, and isometrically on $S^n$ by multiplication. Example C.24 shows that the quotient space is diffeomorphic to the real projective space $\mathbb{RP}^n$ and the quotient map $q: S^n \to \mathbb{RP}^n$ is a smooth normal covering map. Because the action is isometric, Proposition 2.32 shows that there is a unique metric on $\mathbb{RP}^n$ such that $q$ is a Riemannian covering.

blocks.label: ex:open-mobius-band
blocks.title: The Open Möbius Band
blocks.body: The open Möbius band is the quotient space $M = \mathbb{R}^2/\mathbb{Z}$, where $\mathbb{Z}$ acts on $\mathbb{R}^2$ by $n \cdot (x,y) = (x+n, (-1)^n y)$. This action is smooth, free, proper, and isometric, and therefore $M$ inherits a flat Riemannian metric such that the quotient map is a Riemannian covering. (See Problem 2-8.)

blocks.tex: Every Riemannian metric yields an abundance of useful constructions on manifolds, besides the obvious ones of lengths of vectors and angles between them. In this section we describe the most basic ones. Throughout this section $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary.

One elementary but important property of Riemannian metrics is that they allow us to convert vectors to covectors and vice versa. Given a Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$, we define a bundle homomorphism $\tilde{g} : TM \to T^* M$ by setting
\[
\tilde{g}(v)(w) = g_p(v,w)
\]
for all $p \in M$ and $v,w \in T_p M$. If $X$ and $Y$ are smooth vector fields on $M$, this yields
\[
\tilde{g}(X)(Y) = g(X,Y),
\]
which implies, first, that $\tilde{g}(X)(Y)$ is linear over $C^\infty(M)$ in $Y$ and thus $\tilde{g}(X)$ is a smooth covector field by the tensor characterization lemma (Lemma B.6); and second, that the covector field $\tilde{g}(X)$ is linear over $C^\infty(M)$ as a function of $X$, and thus $\tilde{g}$ is a smooth bundle homomorphism.

Given a smooth local frame $(E_i)$ and its dual coframe $(e^i)$, let $g = g_{ij} e^i \otimes e^j$ be the local expression for $g$. If $X = X^i E_i$ is a smooth vector field, the covector field $\tilde{g}(X)$ has the coordinate expression
\[
\tilde{g}(X) = (g_{ij}X^i)e^j.
\]
Thus the matrix of $\tilde{g}$ in any local frame is the same as the matrix of $g$ itself. Given a vector field $X$, it is standard practice to denote the components of the covector field $\tilde{g}(X)$ by
\[
X_j = g_{ij}X^i,
\]
so that
\[
\tilde{g}(X) = X_j e^j,
\]
and we say that $\tilde{g}(X)$ is obtained from $X$ by \textit{lowering an index}. With this in mind, the covector field $g(X)$ is denoted by $X^\flat$ and called $X$ \textit{flat}, borrowing from the musical notation for lowering a tone.

Because the matrix $(g_{ij})$ is nonsingular at each point, the map $\tilde{g}$ is invertible, and the matrix of $\tilde{g}^{-1}$ is just the inverse matrix of $(g_{ij})$. We denote this inverse matrix by $(g^{ij})$, so that $g^{ij}g_{jk} = g_{kj}g^{jl} = \delta_k^l$. The symmetry of $g_{ij}$ easily implies that $(g^{ij})$ is also symmetric in $i$ and $j$. In terms of a local frame, the inverse map $\tilde{g}^{-1}$ is given by
\[
\tilde{g}^{-1}(\omega) = \omega^i E_i,
\]
where
\begin{equation}
\omega^i = g^{ij}\omega_j. \tag{2.13}
\end{equation}

If $\omega$ is a covector field, the vector field $\tilde{g}^{-1}(\omega)$ is called (what else?) $\omega$ \textit{sharp} and denoted by $\omega^\sharp$, and we say that it is obtained from $\omega$ by \textit{raising an index}. The two inverse isomorphisms $\flat$ and $\sharp$ are known as the \textit{musical isomorphisms}.

We record the fibrewise content of the preceding discussion: at each point, $\flat$ is the map $v \mapsto g_p(v,\cdot)$, it is a bijection, and $\sharp$ is its inverse.

blocks.label: def:flat-map
blocks.title: Lowering an index
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and $p \in M$. The \emph{flat map} at $p$ is the linear map $\flat : T_p M \to T_p^* M$ given by $v^\flat = g_p(v, \cdot)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.flat
lean.code:
noncomputable def flat : TangentSpace I p →L[ℝ] (TangentSpace I p →L[ℝ] ℝ) := g.inner p

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] in

blocks.label: lem:flat-bijective
blocks.title: The flat map is a bijection
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$ and $p \in M$. Then $\flat : T_p M \to T_p^* M$ is bijective.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.flat_bijective
lean.code:
theorem flat_bijective : Function.Bijective (flat g p) :=
  ⟨Function.LeftInverse.injective (sharp_flat g p),
    Function.RightInverse.surjective (flat_sharp g p)⟩

blocks.tex: Since $g_p$ is an inner product on the finite-dimensional real vector space $T_p M$, the space $T_p M$ is a finite-dimensional real inner product space, hence complete. The Riesz representation theorem states that the map sending $v \in T_p M$ to the covector $g_p(v,\cdot)$ is a linear isometric isomorphism of $T_p M$ onto its dual $T_p^* M$. That map is precisely $\flat$, so $\flat$ is bijective.

blocks.label: def:sharp-map
blocks.title: Raising an index
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$ and $p \in M$. The \emph{sharp map} at $p$ is the inverse $\sharp = \flat^{-1} : T_p^* M \to T_p M$, which exists by Lemma \ref{lem:flat-bijective}. Thus $\alpha^\sharp$ is the unique $v \in T_p M$ with $g_p(v,\cdot) = \alpha$, characterized by
\[
g_p(\alpha^\sharp, w) = \alpha(w) \qquad \text{for all } w \in T_p M .
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sharp
lean.code:
noncomputable def sharp (a : TangentSpace I p →L[ℝ] ℝ) : TangentSpace I p :=
  letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  haveI : FiniteDimensional ℝ (TangentSpace I p) := inferInstanceAs (FiniteDimensional ℝ E)
  (InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ (TangentSpace I p)).symm a

deps.title: The flat map is a bijection
deps.label: lem:flat-bijective

blocks.tex:
Probably the most important application of the sharp operator is to extend the classical gradient operator to Riemannian manifolds. If $g$ is a Riemannian metric on $M$ and $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ is a smooth function, the \emph{gradient of $f$} is the vector field
$\operatorname{grad} f = (df)^\#$ obtained from $df$ by raising an index. Unwinding the definitions,
we see that $\operatorname{grad} f$ is characterized by the fact that
\begin{equation}
df_p(w) = \langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w \rangle \quad \text{for all } p \in M, w \in T_p M.
\tag{2.14}
\end{equation}
and has the local basis expression
\[
\operatorname{grad} f = (g^{ij} E_i f) E_j.
\]

Thus if $(E_i)$ is an orthonormal frame, then $\operatorname{grad} f$ is the vector field whose
components are the same as the components of $df$; but in other frames, this will
not be the case.

blocks.label: def:gradient
blocks.title: The gradient
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$ and $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ smooth. The \emph{gradient} of $f$ is $\operatorname{grad} f = (df)^\sharp$, so that $\operatorname{grad} f|_p = (df_p)^\sharp \in T_p M$ for each $p \in M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.grad
lean.code: noncomputable def grad : TangentSpace I p := sharp g p (extDerivFun (I := I) f p)

blocks.label: lem:gradient-characterization
blocks.title: Characterization of the gradient
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$ and $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ smooth. Then (2.14) holds: $df_p(w) = \langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w\rangle$ for all $p \in M$ and $w \in T_p M$. Moreover $\operatorname{grad} f|_p$ is the unique vector with this property.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerAt_grad
lean.code:
@[simp] theorem innerAt_grad (w : TangentSpace I p) :
    g.innerAt p (grad g f p) w = extDerivFun (I := I) f p w :=
  innerAt_sharp g p _ w

deps.title: The flat map is a bijection
deps.label: lem:flat-bijective

blocks.tex: By Definition \ref{def:sharp-map}, $\alpha^\sharp$ is characterized by $g_p(\alpha^\sharp, w) = \alpha(w)$ for all $w$. Taking $\alpha = df_p$ gives $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w\rangle = df_p(w)$, which is (2.14). For uniqueness, suppose $v \in T_p M$ also satisfies $\langle v, w\rangle = df_p(w)$ for all $w$. Then $v^\flat = (\operatorname{grad} f|_p)^\flat$, and $\flat$ is injective by Lemma \ref{lem:flat-bijective}, so $v = \operatorname{grad} f|_p$.

blocks.label: lem:gradient-orthogonal-kernel
blocks.title: The gradient is orthogonal to the kernel of the differential
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$, let $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ be smooth, and let $p \in M$. If $w \in T_p M$ satisfies $df_p(w) = 0$, then $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w \rangle = 0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerAt_grad_eq_zero_of_mem_ker
lean.code:
theorem innerAt_grad_eq_zero_of_mem_ker (w : TangentSpace I p)
    (hw : mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f p w = 0) : g.innerAt p (grad g f p) w = 0 := by
  rw [innerAt_grad]
  show (NormedSpace.fromTangentSpace (f p)).toContinuousLinearMap (mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f p w) = 0
  rw [hw, map_zero]

blocks.tex: By (2.14), $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w\rangle = df_p(w) = 0$.

blocks.label: lem:gradient-eq-zero-iff
blocks.title: Critical points are the zeros of the gradient
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$, let $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ be smooth, and let $p \in M$. Then $\operatorname{grad} f|_p = 0$ if and only if $df_p = 0$. Hence the regular points of $f$ are exactly the points where $\operatorname{grad} f$ does not vanish.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.grad_eq_zero_iff
lean.code:
theorem grad_eq_zero_iff : grad g f p = 0 ↔ extDerivFun (I := I) f p = 0 := by
  constructor
  · intro h
    refine ContinuousLinearMap.ext fun w => ?_
    rw [← innerAt_grad g f p w, h]
    simp [RiemannianMetric.innerAt]
  · intro h
    refine flat_injective g p (ContinuousLinearMap.ext fun w => ?_)
    rw [flat_apply, innerAt_grad, h]
    simp

deps.title: The flat map is a bijection
deps.label: lem:flat-bijective

blocks.tex: If $\operatorname{grad} f|_p = 0$ then for every $w \in T_p M$, (2.14) gives $df_p(w) = \langle 0, w\rangle = 0$, so $df_p = 0$. Conversely, if $df_p = 0$ then for every $w$ we have $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w\rangle = df_p(w) = 0 = \langle 0, w \rangle$, so $(\operatorname{grad} f|_p)^\flat = 0^\flat$, and $\flat$ is injective by Lemma \ref{lem:flat-bijective}, whence $\operatorname{grad} f|_p = 0$.

Lemma \ref{lem:gradient-orthogonal-kernel} has a converse, which is what makes the gradient a faithful record of the differential: orthogonality to $\operatorname{grad} f|_p$ detects the kernel of $df_p$ exactly.

blocks.label: lem:gradient-orthogonal-iff
blocks.title: Orthogonality to the gradient characterizes the kernel of the differential
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$, let $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ be smooth, let $p \in M$, and let $w \in T_p M$. Then $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w\rangle = 0$ if and only if $df_p(w) = 0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerAt_grad_eq_zero_iff
lean.code:
theorem innerAt_grad_eq_zero_iff (w : TangentSpace I p) :
    g.innerAt p (grad g f p) w = 0 ↔ mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f p w = 0 := by
  refine ⟨fun h => ?_, innerAt_grad_eq_zero_of_mem_ker g f p w⟩
  rw [innerAt_grad] at h
  -- `extDerivFun f p w` is `fromTangentSpace (f p) (df_p w)`, and
  -- `fromTangentSpace` is a continuous linear equivalence, hence injective.
  have h' : (NormedSpace.fromTangentSpace (f p)).toContinuousLinearMap
      (mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f p w) = 0 := h
  have := (NormedSpace.fromTangentSpace (f p)).injective
    (a₁ := mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f p w) (a₂ := 0) (by simpa using h')
  exact this

end GradKer

/-! ### The regular-point set -/

section RegularSet

variable (I) in

blocks.tex: Both directions are immediate from (2.14): $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_p, w\rangle = df_p(w)$, so one side vanishes precisely when the other does.

The next proposition shows that the gradient has the same geometric interpretation
on a Riemannian manifold as it does in Euclidean space. If $f$ is a smooth
real-valued function on a smooth manifold $M$, recall that a point $p \in M$ is called a
\emph{regular point of $f$} if $df_p \neq 0$, and a \emph{critical point of $f$} otherwise; and a level set
$f^{-1}(c)$ is called a \emph{regular level set} if every point of $f^{-1}(c)$ is a regular point of $f$
(see Appendix A). Corollary A.26 shows that each regular level set is an embedded
smooth hypersurface in $M$.

blocks.label: def:regular-set
blocks.title: The regular set
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ smooth. The \emph{regular set} of $f$ is $\mathcal{R} = \{x \in M : df_x \neq 0\}$, the set of regular points of $f$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.regularSet
lean.code: def regularSet (f : M → ℝ) : Set M := {x : M | mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f x ≠ 0}

blocks.label: lem:regular-set-eq-gradient-nonvanishing
blocks.title: The regular set is where the gradient does not vanish
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with $\dim M < \infty$ and $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ smooth. Then $x \in \mathcal{R}$ if and only if $\operatorname{grad} f|_x \neq 0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.mem_regularSet_iff_grad_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem mem_regularSet_iff_grad_ne_zero (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (f : M → ℝ)
    (x : M) : x ∈ regularSet I f ↔ grad g f x ≠ 0 := by
  rw [mem_regularSet_iff, ne_eq, ne_eq, grad_eq_zero_iff]
  refine not_congr ⟨fun h => ?_, fun h => ?_⟩
  · ext w
    simp only [ContinuousLinearMap.zero_apply]
    exact congrArg (NormedSpace.fromTangentSpace (f x)).toContinuousLinearMap
      (congrFun (congrArg DFunLike.coe h) w) |>.trans (by simp)
  · ext w
    have := congrFun (congrArg DFunLike.coe h) w
    simp only [ContinuousLinearMap.zero_apply] at this ⊢
    exact (NormedSpace.fromTangentSpace (f x)).injective (by simpa using this)

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [I.Boundaryless] in

deps.title: Critical points are the zeros of the gradient
deps.label: lem:gradient-eq-zero-iff

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:gradient-eq-zero-iff}, $\operatorname{grad} f|_x = 0$ if and only if $df_x = 0$. Negating both sides gives the claim.

The reduction of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface} to the regular-value case rests on $\mathcal{R}$ being open, which is what makes it an open submanifold on which $f$ has $c$ as a regular value.

blocks.label: lem:mfderiv-nonvanishing-open
blocks.title: The non-vanishing locus of a differential is open
blocks.body: Let $F : M \to N$ be a smooth map of smooth manifolds. Then $\{x \in M : dF_x \neq 0\}$ is open in $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isOpen_setOf_mfderiv_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem isOpen_setOf_mfderiv_ne_zero
    {E' : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E'] [NormedSpace ℝ E']
    {H' : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H'] {I' : ModelWithCorners ℝ E' H'}
    {M' : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M'] [ChartedSpace H' M'] [IsManifold I' ∞ M']
    {f : M → M'} (hf : ContMDiff I I' ∞ f) :
    IsOpen {x : M | mfderiv I I' f x ≠ 0} := by
  rw [isOpen_iff_mem_nhds]
  intro x₀ hx₀
  set sT := trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀ with hsT
  set tT := trivializationAt E' (TangentSpace I') (f x₀) with htT
  have hx₀s : x₀ ∈ sT.baseSet := mem_baseSet_trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀
  have hfx₀ : f x₀ ∈ tT.baseSet := mem_baseSet_trivializationAt E' (TangentSpace I') (f x₀)
  set D := inTangentCoordinates I I' id f (fun x => mfderiv I I' f x) x₀ with hD
  have hDsmooth : ContMDiffAt I 𝓘(ℝ, E →L[ℝ] E') ∞ D x₀ :=
    ContMDiffAt.mfderiv_const (I := I) (I' := I') hf.contMDiffAt (by simp)
  -- In tangent coordinates the differential is conjugated by the two trivializations.
  have hDu : ∀ x, ∀ hx : x ∈ sT.baseSet, ∀ hfx : f x ∈ tT.baseSet, ∀ u : E,
      D x u = tT.continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ (f x) hfx
        (mfderiv I I' f x ((sT.continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ x hx).symm u)) := by
    intro x hx hfx u
    rw [hD]
    simp only [inTangentCoordinates, id_eq]
    rw [ContinuousLinearMap.inCoordinates_eq hx hfx]
    rfl
  -- Hence it vanishes exactly where the differential does.
  have key : ∀ x, ∀ hx : x ∈ sT.baseSet, ∀ hfx : f x ∈ tT.baseSet,
      (D x = 0 ↔ mfderiv I I' f x = 0) := by
    intro x hx hfx
    constructor
    · intro h
      ext v
      have hu := hDu x hx hfx (sT.continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ x hx v)
      rw [h] at hu
      simp only [ContinuousLinearMap.zero_apply, ContinuousLinearEquiv.symm_apply_apply] at hu
      have := (tT.continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ (f x) hfx).map_eq_zero_iff.mp hu.symm
      simp [this]
    · intro h
      ext u
      rw [hDu x hx hfx u, h]
      simpa using (tT.continuousLinearEquivAt ℝ (f x) hfx).map_zero
  have hDx₀ : D x₀ ≠ 0 := fun h => hx₀ ((key x₀ hx₀s hfx₀).mp h)
  have hbase : {x : M | f x ∈ tT.baseSet} ∈ 𝓝 x₀ :=
    hf.continuous.continuousAt (tT.open_baseSet.mem_nhds hfx₀)
  filter_upwards [hDsmooth.continuousAt (isOpen_ne.mem_nhds hDx₀),
    sT.open_baseSet.mem_nhds hx₀s, hbase] with x hx hxs hxf
  exact fun h0 => hx ((key x hxs hxf).mpr h0)

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [I.Boundaryless] in

blocks.tex:
Fix $x_0$ with $dF_{x_0} \neq 0$, and read $x \mapsto dF_x$ in tangent coordinates around $x_0$ and $F(x_0)$: choose trivializations $\Phi$ of $TM$ over a neighbourhood of $x_0$ and $\Psi$ of $TN$ over a neighbourhood of $F(x_0)$, and set
\[
  D(x) = \Psi_{F(x)} \circ dF_x \circ \Phi_x^{-1},
\]
a map into the fixed vector space $\operatorname{Hom}(E, E')$ of the two model spaces, defined for $x$ in the (open) set where both trivializations apply and $F(x)$ lies in the second base set. Since $F$ is smooth, $D$ is smooth, hence continuous, at $x_0$.

Because $\Phi_x$ and $\Psi_{F(x)}$ are linear isomorphisms on the relevant fibres, $D(x) = 0$ if and only if $dF_x = 0$. In particular $D(x_0) \neq 0$. As $\{L : L \neq 0\}$ is open in $\operatorname{Hom}(E,E')$ and $D$ is continuous at $x_0$, we get $D(x) \neq 0$, and hence $dF_x \neq 0$, for all $x$ in a neighbourhood of $x_0$. So every point of the set is interior to it.

blocks.label: lem:regular-set-open
blocks.title: The regular set is open
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $f : M \to \mathbb{R}$ smooth. Then the regular set $\mathcal{R}$ of $f$ is open in $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isOpen_regularSet
lean.code:
theorem isOpen_regularSet {f : M → ℝ} (hf : ContMDiff I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞ f) :
    IsOpen (regularSet I f) :=
  isOpen_setOf_mfderiv_ne_zero hf

end RegularSet

/-! ### Proposition 2.37: `grad f` is normal to a regular level set -/

section Normal

variable {f : M → ℝ} (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (hf : ContMDiff I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞ f)
  (n : ℕ) [Fact (Module.finrank ℝ E = n + 1)] (c : ℝ)

deps.title: The non-vanishing locus of a differential is open
deps.label: lem:mfderiv-nonvanishing-open

blocks.tex: This is Lemma \ref{lem:mfderiv-nonvanishing-open} applied to $F = f$ with $N = \mathbb{R}$, since $\mathcal{R} = \{x : df_x \neq 0\}$ by definition.

For a regular \emph{value} $c$ — one for which every point of $f^{-1}(c)$ is regular — the level set is a hypersurface by Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one}, and the gradient is normal to it.

blocks.label: prop:gradient-normal-regular-level-set
blocks.title: The gradient is normal to a regular level set
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold of dimension $n+1$ without boundary, let $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and let $c$ be a regular value of $f$. Then for every $x_0 \in f^{-1}(c)$ and every $w \in T_{x_0} f^{-1}(c)$,
\[
  \bigl\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_{x_0},\, d(\iota_c)_{x_0}(w) \bigr\rangle = 0 ,
\]
where $\iota_c : f^{-1}(c) \hookrightarrow M$ is the inclusion. That is, $\operatorname{grad} f$ is normal to $f^{-1}(c)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerAt_grad_mfderiv_levelSet_val_eq_zero
lean.code:
theorem innerAt_grad_mfderiv_levelSet_val_eq_zero
    (hreg : ∀ x : M, f x = c → mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f x ≠ 0)
    (x₀ : (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M)) (w : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) :
    letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
    g.innerAt ↑x₀ (grad g f ↑x₀)
        (mfderiv (𝓡 n) I ((↑) : (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M) → M) x₀ w) = 0 := by
  letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
  exact innerAt_grad_eq_zero_of_mem_ker g f ↑x₀ _
    (mfderivReal_mfderiv_levelSet_val hf n c hreg x₀ w)

deps.title: The gradient is orthogonal to the kernel of the differential
deps.label: lem:gradient-orthogonal-kernel

deps.title: The tangent space of a regular level set is the kernel of the differential
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-tangent-kernel

deps.title: A regular level set of a real-valued function is a smooth hypersurface
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one

blocks.tex: Since $f \circ \iota_c$ is constant, the chain rule gives $df_{x_0}(d(\iota_c)_{x_0}(w)) = 0$ for every $w$, which is the inclusion $\subseteq$ of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-tangent-kernel}. So $d(\iota_c)_{x_0}(w) \in \ker df_{x_0}$, and Lemma \ref{lem:gradient-orthogonal-kernel} applies.

blocks.label: prop:tangent-space-regular-level-set-gradient-orthogonal
blocks.title: The tangent space of a regular level set is the orthogonal complement of the gradient
blocks.body:
In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:gradient-normal-regular-level-set}, conversely, every $u \in T_{x_0} M$ with $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_{x_0}, u\rangle = 0$ lies in the image of $d(\iota_c)_{x_0}$. Hence
\[
  d(\iota_c)_{x_0}\bigl(T_{x_0} f^{-1}(c)\bigr) = \bigl(\operatorname{grad} f|_{x_0}\bigr)^{\perp} ,
\]
so the normal space of $f^{-1}(c)$ at $x_0$ is the line spanned by $\operatorname{grad} f|_{x_0}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_mfderiv_levelSet_val_eq_of_innerAt_grad_eq_zero
lean.code:
theorem exists_mfderiv_levelSet_val_eq_of_innerAt_grad_eq_zero
    (hreg : ∀ x : M, f x = c → mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f x ≠ 0)
    (x₀ : (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M)) (u : E)
    (hu : g.innerAt ↑x₀ (grad g f ↑x₀) u = 0) :
    letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
    ∃ w : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n),
      (mfderiv (𝓡 n) I ((↑) : (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M) → M) x₀ w : E) = u := by
  letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
  have hker : u ∈ levelHyperplane (I := I) f ↑x₀ :=
    (mem_levelHyperplane_iff (I := I) f ↑x₀ u).2
      ((innerAt_grad_eq_zero_iff g f ↑x₀ u).1 hu)
  rw [← range_mfderiv_levelSet_val hf n c hreg x₀] at hker
  -- `LinearMap.range` is `Set.range` on the nose, so membership destructures
  -- directly; `LinearMap.mem_range` cannot fire here because its
  -- `RingHomSurjective` instance argument is still a metavariable.
  obtain ⟨w, hw⟩ := hker
  exact ⟨w, hw⟩

end Normal

/-!
### Proposition 2.37 for a possibly-critical level set

Lee's `M_c = f⁻¹(c) ∩ ℛ` allows the level set to contain critical points, and
keeps only its regular part.  Everything above is stated for a *regular value*
(`hreg`: `df_x ≠ 0` at every point of `f⁻¹(c)`), which is the case Lee's own
proof reduces to and the case his applications use.

The reduction of the general statement to this one is carried out in
`RegularSetHypersurface.lean`, on top of the openness of `ℛ` proved here
(`isOpen_regularSet`): `ℛ` is an open submanifold of `M`, `M_c` is the level set
of `f|_ℛ` at `c`, and `c` is a regular value of that restriction *by
construction*, because restricting to an open set does not change the
differential.

Openness did not need the chart comparison an earlier note here called for:
rather than identify `df_x` with the derivative of a chart representative,
`isOpen_setOf_mfderiv_ne_zero` reads `x ↦ df_x` in *tangent* coordinates, where
mathlib's `ContMDiffAt.mfderiv_const` already supplies smoothness and the
trivialization equivalences make vanishing of the coordinate representation
equivalent to vanishing of `df_x`.

The remaining transfer needed `d(ℛ ↪ M) = id` for `ℛ` open in an *arbitrary*
manifold.  An earlier note here cited `OpenSubmanifold.lean` for that, but at the
time that file proved it only for an open subset of a normed space (the flat case
`W : Opens F` used by `PolarCoordinates.lean`); the general form has since been
added there (`mfderiv_opens_subtypeVal`, `mfderiv_opens_restrict`).
-/

deps.title: Orthogonality to the gradient characterizes the kernel of the differential
deps.label: lem:gradient-orthogonal-iff

deps.title: The tangent space of a regular level set is the kernel of the differential
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-tangent-kernel

blocks.tex: If $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_{x_0}, u\rangle = 0$ then $df_{x_0}(u) = 0$ by Lemma \ref{lem:gradient-orthogonal-iff}, so $u \in \ker df_{x_0}$. By Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-tangent-kernel} the image of $d(\iota_c)_{x_0}$ is all of $\ker df_{x_0}$, so $u$ is in that image. Combined with Proposition \ref{prop:gradient-normal-regular-level-set} this gives the displayed equality of subspaces; the normal space is then the orthogonal complement of a hyperplane, hence the line spanned by $\operatorname{grad} f|_{x_0}$, which is nonzero by Lemma \ref{lem:regular-set-eq-gradient-nonvanishing}.

Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface} is the same statement for the possibly-critical level set $f^{-1}(c)$, of which only the regular part $M_c = f^{-1}(c) \cap \mathcal{R}$ is retained. It reduces to the regular-value case above once one knows that $\mathcal{R}$ is open, so that $M_c$ is a regular level set of the restriction $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$ on the open submanifold $\mathcal{R}$. Only the case $N = \mathbb{R}$ of the regular level set theorem is used, so Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one} suffices and the general Corollary \ref{cor:regular-level-set} is not needed.

An open subset $U \subseteq M$ is a smooth manifold of the same dimension, its charts being those of $M$ restricted. The two lemmas below say that this identification is invisible to differentials, which is what makes the reduction to the regular-value case work.

blocks.label: lem:opens-inclusion-differential
blocks.title: The differential of an open-submanifold inclusion
blocks.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $U \subseteq M$ open, regarded as an open submanifold. Then for each $x \in U$ the differential of the inclusion $\iota : U \hookrightarrow M$ is the identity,
\[
  d\iota_x = \operatorname{id} : T_x U \to T_x M ,
\]
under the identification of $T_x U$ with $T_x M$ induced by the charts. In particular $d\iota_x$ is an isomorphism.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.mfderiv_opens_subtypeVal
lean.code:
theorem mfderiv_opens_subtypeVal (U : Opens M) (x : U) :
    mfderiv I I (fun y : U => (y : M)) x = ContinuousLinearMap.id ℝ E :=
  (hasMFDerivAt_opens_subtypeVal U x).mfderiv

blocks.tex: The chart of $U$ at $x$ is the chart of $M$ at $x$ restricted to $U$, so the two charts agree at every point of $U$ near $x$. The coordinate representation of $\iota$ is therefore $\kappa \circ \iota \circ \kappa^{-1}$ for one and the same chart $\kappa$, which is the identity on the chart's image. Differentiating the identity gives $d\iota_x = \operatorname{id}$.

blocks.label: lem:opens-restrict-differential
blocks.title: Restriction to an open set preserves the differential
blocks.body:
Let $F : M \to M'$ be a smooth map, $U \subseteq M$ open, and $x \in U$. Then
\[
  d(F|_U)_x = dF_x .
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.mfderiv_opens_restrict
lean.code:
theorem mfderiv_opens_restrict (U : Opens M) (F : M → M') {x : U}
    (hF : MDifferentiableAt I I' F ↑x) :
    mfderiv I I' (fun y : U => F ↑y) x = mfderiv I I' F ↑x := by
  have hcomp : mfderiv I I' ((fun z : M => F z) ∘ (fun y : U => (y : M))) x
      = (mfderiv I I' F ↑x).comp (mfderiv I I (fun y : U => (y : M)) x) :=
    mfderiv_comp x hF (hasMFDerivAt_opens_subtypeVal U x).mdifferentiableAt
  rw [Function.comp_def] at hcomp
  rw [hcomp, mfderiv_opens_subtypeVal]
  ext v
  rfl

end General

/-! ## The flat case: an open subset of a normed space

Here the ambient manifold is the model space itself, `I = 𝓘(ℝ, F)`, so the
general results above apply verbatim. -/

section Flat

variable {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℝ F] [FiniteDimensional ℝ F]

deps.title: The differential of an open-submanifold inclusion
deps.label: lem:opens-inclusion-differential

blocks.tex: $F|_U = F \circ \iota$ for the inclusion $\iota : U \hookrightarrow M$, so the chain rule gives $d(F|_U)_x = dF_x \circ d\iota_x$, and $d\iota_x = \operatorname{id}$ by Lemma \ref{lem:opens-inclusion-differential}.

blocks.label: lem:regular-restrict-regular-value
blocks.title: Every value is a regular value of $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and $\mathcal{R}$ the regular set of $f$, regarded as an open submanifold of $M$ (Lemma \ref{lem:regular-set-open}). Then $d(f|_{\mathcal{R}})_x \neq 0$ for every $x \in \mathcal{R}$; that is, every $c \in \mathbb{R}$ is a regular value of $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.regularRestrict_regular
lean.code:
theorem regularRestrict_regular (y : regularOpens hf) :
    mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) (regularRestrict hf) y ≠ 0 := by
  rw [mfderiv_regularRestrict]
  exact y.2

deps.title: Restriction to an open set preserves the differential
deps.label: lem:opens-restrict-differential

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:opens-restrict-differential}, $d(f|_{\mathcal{R}})_x = df_x$, and $df_x \neq 0$ because $x \in \mathcal{R}$ by the definition of the regular set.

blocks.title: The regular part of an arbitrary level set

blocks.tex: Throughout, $M_c$ denotes $(f|_{\mathcal{R}})^{-1}(c)$, the level set of the restriction inside the open submanifold $\mathcal{R}$; Lemma \ref{lem:regular-part-image} identifies it with Lee's subset $f^{-1}(c) \cap \mathcal{R}$ of $M$.

blocks.label: prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface
blocks.title: prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface
blocks.body:
Suppose $M$ is a smooth $(n+1)$-manifold without boundary, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and
$\mathcal{R} \subseteq M$ is the set of regular points of $f$. For each $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the set $M_c = f^{-1}(c) \cap \mathcal{R}$ is a smooth $n$-manifold.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isManifold_regularLevelSet
lean.code:
theorem isManifold_regularLevelSet :
    letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
    IsManifold (𝓡 n) ∞ (regularLevelSet hf c) :=
  isManifold_levelSet (contMDiff_regularRestrict hf) n c (regularRestrict_hreg hf c)

/-! ### The inclusion into `M` -/

deps.title: Every value is a regular value of $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$
deps.label: lem:regular-restrict-regular-value

deps.title: A regular level set of a real-valued function is a smooth hypersurface
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:regular-set-open} the set $\mathcal{R}$ is open, so it is an open submanifold of $M$ of the same dimension $n+1$. Then $M_c = (f|_{\mathcal{R}})^{-1}(c)$, and $c$ is a regular value of $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$ by Lemma \ref{lem:regular-restrict-regular-value}. Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one}, applied to $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$ on the manifold $\mathcal{R}$, gives the slice-chart atlas making $M_c$ a smooth $n$-manifold.

blocks.label: lem:regular-part-image
blocks.title: The regular part, as a subset of $M$
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface}, the image of $M_c = (f|_{\mathcal{R}})^{-1}(c)$ under the inclusion $M_c \hookrightarrow \mathcal{R} \hookrightarrow M$ is exactly $f^{-1}(c) \cap \mathcal{R}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.range_regularLevelSetIncl
lean.code:
theorem range_regularLevelSetIncl :
    range (regularLevelSetIncl hf c) = f ⁻¹' {c} ∩ regularSet I f := by
  ext x
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨z, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨z.2, (z : regularOpens hf).2⟩
  · rintro ⟨hxc, hxr⟩
    exact ⟨⟨⟨x, hxr⟩, hxc⟩, rfl⟩

blocks.tex: A point of $M$ lies in the image if and only if it lies in $\mathcal{R}$ and $f$ takes the value $c$ there, which is the definition of $f^{-1}(c) \cap \mathcal{R}$.

blocks.label: lem:regular-part-embedded
blocks.title: The regular part is embedded
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface}, the inclusion $M_c \hookrightarrow M$ is a topological embedding.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isEmbedding_regularLevelSetIncl
lean.code:
theorem isEmbedding_regularLevelSetIncl :
    letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
    Topology.IsEmbedding (regularLevelSetIncl hf c) :=
  Topology.IsEmbedding.subtypeVal.comp Topology.IsEmbedding.subtypeVal

blocks.tex: It is the composite of the inclusions $M_c \hookrightarrow \mathcal{R}$ and $\mathcal{R} \hookrightarrow M$, each of which carries the subspace topology and so is a topological embedding; a composite of embeddings is an embedding.

blocks.label: lem:regular-part-inclusion-smooth
blocks.title: The inclusion of the regular part is smooth
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface}, the inclusion $M_c \hookrightarrow M$ is smooth.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.contMDiff_regularLevelSetIncl
lean.code:
theorem contMDiff_regularLevelSetIncl :
    letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
    ContMDiff (𝓡 n) I ∞ (regularLevelSetIncl hf c) := by
  letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
  exact contMDiff_subtype_val.comp
    (contMDiff_levelSet_val (contMDiff_regularRestrict hf) n c (regularRestrict_hreg hf c))

deps.title: The inclusion of a regular level set is a smooth immersion
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-inclusion-smooth

blocks.tex: It is the composite of $M_c \hookrightarrow \mathcal{R}$, smooth by Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-inclusion-smooth} applied to $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$, with the inclusion $\mathcal{R} \hookrightarrow M$ of an open submanifold, which is smooth.

blocks.label: lem:regular-part-inclusion-immersion
blocks.title: The inclusion of the regular part is an immersion
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface}, the differential of the inclusion $M_c \hookrightarrow M$ is injective at every point. With Lemma \ref{lem:regular-part-embedded}, $M_c$ is an embedded smooth hypersurface in $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.injective_mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl
lean.code:
theorem injective_mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl (z : regularLevelSet hf c) :
    letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
    Injective (mfderiv (𝓡 n) I (regularLevelSetIncl hf c) z) := by
  letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
  rw [mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl hf c n z]
  exact mfderiv_levelSet_val_injective (contMDiff_regularRestrict hf) n c
    (regularRestrict_hreg hf c) z

deps.title: The differential of an open-submanifold inclusion
deps.label: lem:opens-inclusion-differential

deps.title: The inclusion of a regular level set is a smooth immersion
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-inclusion-smooth

blocks.tex: The differential of the composite $M_c \hookrightarrow \mathcal{R} \hookrightarrow M$ is $d(\mathcal{R} \hookrightarrow M) \circ d(M_c \hookrightarrow \mathcal{R})$, and the first factor is the identity by Lemma \ref{lem:opens-inclusion-differential}. So the differential equals that of $M_c \hookrightarrow \mathcal{R}$, which is injective by Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-inclusion-smooth} applied to $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$.

blocks.label: lem:regular-part-tangent-kernel
blocks.title: The tangent space of the regular part
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface}, for every $x \in M_c$ the image of the differential of the inclusion $M_c \hookrightarrow M$ is exactly $\ker df_x$; that is, $T_x M_c = \ker df_x$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.range_mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl
lean.code:
theorem range_mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl (z : regularLevelSet hf c) :
    letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
    (mfderiv (𝓡 n) I (regularLevelSetIncl hf c) z :
        EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] E).range
      = levelHyperplane (I := I) f (regularLevelSetIncl hf c z) := by
  letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
  have h := range_mfderiv_levelSet_val (contMDiff_regularRestrict hf) n c
    (regularRestrict_hreg hf c) z
  have hhyp : levelHyperplane (I := I) (regularRestrict hf) ↑z
      = levelHyperplane (I := I) f (regularLevelSetIncl hf c z) := by
    show LinearMap.ker (levelDifferential (I := I) (regularRestrict hf) ↑z : E →ₗ[ℝ] ℝ)
      = LinearMap.ker (levelDifferential (I := I) f (regularLevelSetIncl hf c z) : E →ₗ[ℝ] ℝ)
    rw [levelDifferential, levelDifferential, mfderiv_regularRestrict]
    rfl
  rw [mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl hf c n z, ← hhyp]
  exact h

deps.title: Restriction to an open set preserves the differential
deps.label: lem:opens-restrict-differential

deps.title: The tangent space of a regular level set is the kernel of the differential
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-tangent-kernel

deps.title: The differential of an open-submanifold inclusion
deps.label: lem:opens-inclusion-differential

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:opens-inclusion-differential} the differential of $M_c \hookrightarrow M$ equals that of $M_c \hookrightarrow \mathcal{R}$, whose image is $\ker d(f|_{\mathcal{R}})_x$ by Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-tangent-kernel} applied to $f|_{\mathcal{R}}$. Finally $d(f|_{\mathcal{R}})_x = df_x$ by Lemma \ref{lem:opens-restrict-differential}, so the two kernels coincide.

blocks.label: prop:gradient-normal-regular-part
blocks.title: $\operatorname{grad} f$ is normal to the regular part
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold of dimension $n+1$ without boundary, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and $c \in \mathbb{R}$. Then for every $x \in M_c$ and every $w \in T_x M_c$,
\[
  \bigl\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_{x},\, d\iota_{x}(w) \bigr\rangle = 0 ,
\]
where $\iota : M_c \hookrightarrow M$ is the inclusion. That is, $\operatorname{grad} f$ is everywhere normal to $M_c$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerAt_grad_mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl_eq_zero
lean.code:
theorem innerAt_grad_mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl_eq_zero (g : RiemannianMetric I M)
    (z : regularLevelSet hf c) (w : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) :
    letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
    g.innerAt (regularLevelSetIncl hf c z) (grad g f (regularLevelSetIncl hf c z))
        (mfderiv (𝓡 n) I (regularLevelSetIncl hf c) z w) = 0 := by
  letI := regularLevelSetChartedSpace hf c n
  exact innerAt_grad_eq_zero_of_mem_ker g f _ _
    (mfderivReal_mfderiv_regularLevelSetIncl hf c n z w)

deps.title: The gradient is orthogonal to the kernel of the differential
deps.label: lem:gradient-orthogonal-kernel

deps.title: The tangent space of the regular part
deps.label: lem:regular-part-tangent-kernel

blocks.tex: Since $f$ is constant on $M_c$, the chain rule gives $df_x(d\iota_x(w)) = 0$ for every $w \in T_x M_c$, so $d\iota_x(w) \in \ker df_x$. Lemma \ref{lem:gradient-orthogonal-kernel} then gives $\langle \operatorname{grad} f|_x, d\iota_x(w)\rangle = 0$.

blocks.label: lem:gradient-nonvanishing-regular-part
blocks.title: $\operatorname{grad} f$ does not vanish on the regular part
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:gradient-normal-regular-part}, $\operatorname{grad} f|_x \neq 0$ for every $x \in M_c$. Together with Proposition \ref{prop:gradient-normal-regular-part} and Lemma \ref{lem:regular-part-tangent-kernel}, $\operatorname{grad} f|_x$ spans the normal line of the hypersurface $M_c$ at $x$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.grad_ne_zero_regularLevelSet
lean.code:
theorem grad_ne_zero_regularLevelSet (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (z : regularLevelSet hf c) :
    grad g f (regularLevelSetIncl hf c z) ≠ 0 :=
  (mem_regularSet_iff_grad_ne_zero g f _).1 (z : regularOpens hf).2

deps.title: The regular set is where the gradient does not vanish
deps.label: lem:regular-set-eq-gradient-nonvanishing

blocks.tex: Every point of $M_c$ lies in $\mathcal{R}$, so this is Lemma \ref{lem:regular-set-eq-gradient-nonvanishing}.

The flat and sharp operators can be applied to tensors of any rank, in any index
position, to convert tensors from covariant to contravariant or vice versa. Formally,
this operation is defined as follows: if $F$ is any $(k,l)$-tensor and $i \in \{1, \ldots, k + l\}$ is
any covariant index position for $F$ (meaning that the $i$th argument is a vector, not a
covector), we can form a new tensor $F^\#$ of type $(k + 1, l - 1)$ by setting
\[
F^\#(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{k+l}) = F(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{i-1}, \alpha_i^*, \alpha_{i+1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k+l})
\]
whenever $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{k+l}$ are vectors or covectors as appropriate. In any local frame,
