      hcont.isOpen_inter_preimage huopen isOpen_Iio, ⟨hpu, hlt⟩,
      (isLocalFrameOn_negMember hY ⟨0, hpos⟩).mono Set.inter_subset_left, ?_⟩
    intro x hx
    rw [hchar _ hx, normalRestrict_negMember]
    exact neg_pos.mpr hx.2
  · -- the frame is already positively oriented near `p`
    refine ⟨u ∩ s ⁻¹' (Set.Ioi 0), Y, hcont.isOpen_inter_preimage huopen isOpen_Ioi, ⟨hpu, hgt⟩,
      hY.mono Set.inter_subset_left, ?_⟩
    intro x hx
    rw [hchar _ hx]
    exact hx.2

deps.title: Smoothness of the Value on a Frame
deps.label: lem:normal-restrict-frame-smooth

deps.title: The Orientation Induced by a Unit Normal
deps.label: def:induced-orientation

deps.title: The Adapted Frame is an Ambient Basis
deps.label: lem:adapted-frame-ambient-basis

deps.title: Negating One Member of a Local Frame
deps.label: lem:frame-neg-member

deps.title: A Nonvanishing Top Form Orients the Space
deps.label: lem:nonvanishing-top-form-orientation

deps.title: Negating a Frame Member Negates the Value
deps.label: lem:normal-restrict-neg-member

blocks.tex:
Fix $p \in M$. By Proposition \ref{prop:existence-orthonormal-frames} there is an open
neighbourhood $u$ of $p$ carrying a smooth $g$-orthonormal local frame $(E_1, \ldots, E_n)$ for
$TM$. Consider
\[
  s(x) = \bigl((N \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x(E_1|_x, \ldots, E_n|_x),
  \qquad x \in u .
\]
By Lemma \ref{lem:normal-restrict-frame-smooth} the function $s$ is smooth on $u$, hence
continuous. It never vanishes: by Definition \ref{def:induced-orientation} the form
$\bigl((N \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x$ is a nonzero alternating $n$-form on $T_x M$, and
a nonzero alternating $n$-form on an $n$-dimensional space does not vanish on a basis, $(E_i|_x)$
being one. Moreover, by Lemma \ref{lem:nonvanishing-top-form-orientation}, $(E_i|_x)$ induces the
induced orientation of $M$ at $x$ if and only if $s(x) > 0$.

Since $s$ is continuous and $s(p) \neq 0$, exactly one of the following holds.

If $s(p) > 0$, then $u' = u \cap s^{-1}\bigl((0,\infty)\bigr)$ is an open neighbourhood of $p$, and
on it $(E_i)$ is a smooth local frame inducing the induced orientation at every point.

If $s(p) < 0$, then $u' = u \cap s^{-1}\bigl((-\infty,0)\bigr)$ is an open neighbourhood of $p$.
Because $n \geq 1$ there is an index $i_0$, and by Lemma \ref{lem:frame-neg-member} the family
$(E_i')$ obtained from $(E_i)$ by replacing $E_{i_0}$ with $-E_{i_0}$ is again a smooth local
frame over $u'$. By Lemma \ref{lem:normal-restrict-neg-member} its value satisfies
$\bigl((N \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x(E_1'|_x, \ldots, E_n'|_x) = -s(x) > 0$ for every
$x \in u'$, so $(E_i')$ induces the induced orientation at every point of $u'$.

In both cases $p$ has an open neighbourhood carrying a smooth local frame that is positively
oriented for the induced orientation throughout. As $p$ was arbitrary, the induced orientation is
smooth.

blocks.label: rem:hypersurface-orientation-dimension
blocks.title: rem:hypersurface-orientation-dimension
blocks.body:
The hypothesis $n \geq 1$ in Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-orientable-of-normal} cannot be
dropped. A smooth orientation is by Definition \ref{def:smooth-orientation} one realized near each
point by a positively oriented smooth local frame; a $0$-manifold admits only the empty frame,
whose orientation is the ray of the constant $1$. But $T_x M$ carries two orientations even when
$n = 0$, and for a $0$-manifold immersed in a $1$-manifold the induced orientation is the ray of
the number $dV_{\tilde g}(N_x) = \pm 1$, which may be $-1$. In that case the induced orientation
is not realized by any frame, though $M$ is of course still orientable.

deps.title: The Orientation Induced by a Unit Normal
deps.label: def:induced-orientation

blocks.label: prop:hypersurface-volume-form-if
blocks.title: Hypersurface Volume Form: the Sufficiency Half
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde M, \tilde g)$ be an oriented Riemannian $(n+1)$-manifold with $n \geq 1$, let
$F : M \to \tilde M$ present the $n$-manifold $M$ as a smooth immersion, let $g$ be a metric on
$M$ that $F$ preserves, and suppose $M$ admits a smooth global unit normal field $N$. Then $M$ is
orientable, and it carries a smooth orientation for which
\[
  dV_g = (N \lrcorner dV_{\tilde{g}})|_M. \tag{2.17}
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_smoothOrientation_volumeForm_eq_normalRestrict
lean.code:
theorem exists_smoothOrientation_volumeForm_eq_normalRestrict (hpos : 0 < finrank ℝ E)
    (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' (f : M → M')) (hN : ∀ y, IsUnitNormalAt I g' (f : M → M') N y)
    (hNsmooth : IsSmoothNormalField I f N) (ho' : IsSmoothOrientation o') :
    ∃ o : PointwiseOrientation I M, IsSmoothOrientation o ∧
      ∀ x, g.volumeForm o x = normalRestrict I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim N x :=
  ⟨inducedOrientation g g' o' (f : M → M') hdim N h hN,
    isSmoothOrientation_inducedOrientation hpos h hN hNsmooth ho',
    volumeForm_inducedOrientation_eq_normalRestrict g h hN hdim⟩

deps.title: The Orientation Induced by a Unit Normal
deps.label: def:induced-orientation

blocks.tex:
Take the induced orientation of $M$. It is smooth by Proposition
\ref{prop:hypersurface-orientable-of-normal}, so $M$ is orientable, and (2.17) holds for it by
Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-volume-form-induced}.

Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-volume-form-if} produces an orientation from a normal. The
converse produces a normal from an orientation, and rests on the fact that a hypersurface has
\emph{exactly two} unit normals at each point, of which the orientation selects one.

blocks.label: lem:two-unit-normals
blocks.title: A Hypersurface Has Exactly Two Unit Normals at Each Point
blocks.body:
Let $F : M \to \tilde M$ present the $n$-manifold $M$ as a smooth immersion in the Riemannian
$(n+1)$-manifold $(\tilde M, \tilde g)$, let $x \in M$, and suppose the normal space $N_x M$ is
the line spanned by a unit normal $w_0$. Then every unit normal at $x$ is $w_0$ or $-w_0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.eq_or_eq_neg_of_isUnitNormalVecAt
lean.code:
theorem eq_or_eq_neg_of_isUnitNormalVecAt {f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯}
    {w₀ w : TangentSpace I' (f x)} (hw₀ : IsUnitNormalVecAt I g' (f : M → M') x w₀)
    (hspan : span ℝ ({w₀} : Set (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x))
      = normalSpace g' f x)
    (hw : IsUnitNormalVecAt I g' (f : M → M') x w) : w = w₀ ∨ w = -w₀ := by
  have hmem : w ∈ normalSpace g' f x := hw.mem_normalSpace
  rw [← hspan] at hmem
  obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := Submodule.mem_span_singleton.mp hmem
  -- `hc` lives in the pullback fibre; re-ascribing it to `TangentSpace I' (f x)` is `rfl` and is
  -- what lets `subst` see a variable on the right.
  have hc' : (c • w₀ : TangentSpace I' (f x)) = w := hc
  subst hc'
  have hcc : c * c = 1 := by
    have e1 : g'.inner (f x) (c • w₀) = c • (g'.inner (f x) w₀) :=
      (g'.inner (f x)).map_smul c w₀
    have e2 : g'.inner (f x) w₀ (c • w₀) = c • (g'.inner (f x) w₀ w₀) :=
      (g'.inner (f x) w₀).map_smul c w₀
    have h1 : g'.inner (f x) (c • w₀) (c • w₀) = c * (c * g'.inner (f x) w₀ w₀) := by
      rw [e1, ContinuousLinearMap.smul_apply, e2]
      simp [smul_eq_mul]
    rw [hw.unit, hw₀.unit] at h1
    simpa using h1.symm
  rcases mul_self_eq_one_iff.mp hcc with rfl | rfl
  · left; simp
  · right; simp

deps.title: Local Orthonormal Frames for the Normal Space
deps.label: lem:normal-space-orthonormal-frame

blocks.tex:
A unit normal $w$ lies in $N_x M = \mathbb{R} \cdot w_0$, so $w = c\, w_0$ for some
$c \in \mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
  1 = \langle w, w \rangle_{\tilde g} = c^2 \langle w_0, w_0 \rangle_{\tilde g} = c^2,
\]
so $c = \pm 1$.

blocks.label: lem:pointwise-unit-normal-span
blocks.title: The Normal Space of a Hypersurface Is a Line
blocks.body:
In the situation of Lemma \ref{lem:two-unit-normals}, every $x \in M$ admits a unit normal $w_0$
with $N_x M = \mathbb{R} \cdot w_0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isUnitNormalVecAt_span
lean.code:
theorem exists_isUnitNormalVecAt_span (g' : RiemannianMetric I' M') (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯)
    (himm : ∀ y : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f y))
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1) (x : M) :
    ∃ w₀ : TangentSpace I' (f x), IsUnitNormalVecAt I g' (f : M → M') x w₀ ∧
      span ℝ ({w₀} : Set (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x))
        = normalSpace g' f x := by
  obtain ⟨v, hn, Nl, hvopen, hxv, hsmooth, hon, hnormal, hspan⟩ :=
    exists_orthonormalFrame_normalSpace g' f himm x
  have h0 : 0 < finrank ℝ E' - finrank ℝ E := by omega
  refine ⟨Nl ⟨0, h0⟩ x, ⟨?_, ?_⟩, ?_⟩
  · intro z
    exact hnormal x hxv ⟨0, h0⟩ _ ⟨z, rfl⟩
  · simpa using hon x hxv ⟨0, h0⟩ ⟨0, h0⟩
  · rw [← hspan x hxv]
    congr 1
    ext z
    simp only [Set.mem_range]
    constructor
    · rintro rfl
      exact ⟨_, rfl⟩
    · rintro ⟨j, rfl⟩
      have hj := j.isLt
      congr 2
      exact Fin.ext (by omega)

/-! ## An orientation selects a unit normal -/

/-- **`w` is a unit normal at `x` inducing the orientation `o x`.**

The condition is stated against *every* basis realizing `o x` rather than through
`rayOfNeZero`, whose proof argument would itself depend on `w`.  It is equivalent to
`inducedOrientation … x = o x` once a basis realizing `o x` exists, which is what
`0 < finrank ℝ E` buys; that is `inducedOrientation_unitNormalOfOrientation`.

Every binder is written out rather than inherited from the section: `variable (I g o o') in`
silently drops the ones the body does not mention (here `g`), which shifts every later argument
and produces baffling type errors at each use site. -/
structure IsOrientedUnitNormalVecAt (I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H) (g' : RiemannianMetric I' M')
    (o : PointwiseOrientation I M) (o' : PointwiseOrientation I' M') (F : M → M')
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1)
    (x : M) (w : TangentSpace I' (F x)) : Prop where
  /-- `w` is a unit normal. -/
  isUnitNormal : IsUnitNormalVecAt I g' F x w
  /-- `(w ⌟ dV_g̃)|_M` is positive on every basis realizing `o x`. -/
  pos : ∀ b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) ℝ (TangentSpace I x), b.orientation = o x →
    0 < normalRestrictVec I g' o' F hdim x w b

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] [IsManifold I ∞ M] in

deps.title: Local Orthonormal Frames for the Normal Space
deps.label: lem:normal-space-orthonormal-frame

blocks.tex:
By Lemma \ref{lem:normal-space-orthonormal-frame} some neighbourhood of $x$ carries
$\dim \tilde M - \dim M$ smooth sections whose values at each of its points form an orthonormal
basis of the normal space. Here $\dim \tilde M - \dim M = 1$, so there is exactly one such
section; its value $w_0$ at $x$ is a unit normal and spans $N_x M$.

blocks.label: lem:normal-restrict-neg-normal
blocks.title: Negating the Normal Negates the Contracted Form
blocks.body:
In the situation of Lemma \ref{lem:two-unit-normals}, for every $w \in T_{F(x)}\tilde M$,
\[
  \bigl(((-w) \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x = -\bigl((w \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x .
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.normalRestrictVec_neg
lean.code:
theorem normalRestrictVec_neg (w : TangentSpace I' (F x)) :
    normalRestrictVec I g' o' F hdim x (-w) = - normalRestrictVec I g' o' F hdim x w := by
  classical
  ext b
  rw [normalRestrictVec_apply, consNormalVec_neg]
  rw [show (-w) = -(consNormalVec I F hdim x w b ((finCongr hdim).symm 0)) by
    rw [consNormalVec_symm_zero]]
  show (g'.volumeForm o' (F x)).toAlternatingMap
      (Function.update (consNormalVec I F hdim x w b) ((finCongr hdim).symm 0)
        (-(consNormalVec I F hdim x w b ((finCongr hdim).symm 0)))) = _
  rw [AlternatingMap.map_update_neg, Function.update_eq_self]
  rfl

/-! ## The normal space of a hypersurface is a line -/

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] [IsManifold I ∞ M] in

blocks.tex:
The contracted form places $w$ in the first slot of $dV_{\tilde g}$, and $dV_{\tilde g}$ is
multilinear, so negating that slot negates the value. No orthogonality, unit length or continuity
is used.

blocks.label: lem:basis-realizing-orientation
blocks.title: A Basis Realizing a Prescribed Orientation
blocks.body:
Let $M$ be an $n$-manifold with $n \geq 1$, let $o$ be a pointwise orientation of $M$ and let
$x \in M$. Then $T_x M$ has a basis that is positively oriented for $o(x)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_orientation_eq
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_orientation_eq (hpos : 0 < finrank ℝ E) (o : PointwiseOrientation I M)
    (x : M) : ∃ b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) ℝ (TangentSpace I x), b.orientation = o x := by
  classical
  haveI : Nonempty (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) := Fin.pos_iff_nonempty.mp hpos
  exact ⟨(Module.finBasis ℝ E : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) ℝ (TangentSpace I x)).adjustToOrientation
      (o x), Module.Basis.orientation_adjustToOrientation _ _⟩

deps.title: A Nonvanishing Top Form Orients the Space
deps.label: lem:nonvanishing-top-form-orientation

blocks.tex:
Take any basis of $T_x M$. Its orientation is either $o(x)$, in which case it serves, or the
opposite one, in which case negating one of its members — possible because $n \geq 1$ — reverses
its orientation and produces the required basis.

blocks.label: def:oriented-unit-normal
blocks.title: A Unit Normal Inducing a Given Orientation
blocks.body:
Let $o$ be a pointwise orientation of $M$ and $x \in M$. A unit normal $w$ at $x$
\emph{induces $o$ at $x$} if
$\bigl((w \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x(b_1,\ldots,b_n) > 0$ for every basis
$(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ of $T_x M$ that is positively oriented for $o(x)$.

deps.title: The Orientation Induced by a Unit Normal
deps.label: def:induced-orientation

deps.title: A Nonvanishing Top Form Orients the Space
deps.label: lem:nonvanishing-top-form-orientation

blocks.label: lem:oriented-unit-normal-iff-pos
blocks.title: One Basis Suffices
blocks.body:
In the situation of Definition \ref{def:oriented-unit-normal}, let $b$ be one basis of $T_x M$
that is positively oriented for $o(x)$. Then the unit normal $w$ induces $o$ at $x$ if and only if
$\bigl((w \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x(b) > 0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt_iff_pos
lean.code:
theorem isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt_iff_pos (o : PointwiseOrientation I M) {g : RiemannianMetric I M}
    {f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯} (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' (f : M → M')) {x : M}
    {w : TangentSpace I' (f x)} (hw : IsUnitNormalVecAt I g' (f : M → M') x w)
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1)
    (b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) ℝ (TangentSpace I x)) (hb : b.orientation = o x) :
    IsOrientedUnitNormalVecAt I g' o o' (f : M → M') hdim x w ↔
      0 < normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim x w b := by
  have hform : (normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim x w).toAlternatingMap ≠ 0 :=
    normalRestrictVec_toAlternatingMap_ne_zero (g := g) h hw hdim
  refine ⟨fun hor => hor.pos b hb, fun hp => ⟨hw, ?_⟩⟩
  intro b' hb'
  have hray : rayOfNeZero ℝ (normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim x w).toAlternatingMap
      hform = b.orientation :=
    (rayOfNeZero_eq_basis_orientation_iff b
      (normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim x w).toAlternatingMap hform).mpr hp
  rw [hb, ← hb'] at hray
  exact (rayOfNeZero_eq_basis_orientation_iff b'
    (normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim x w).toAlternatingMap hform).mp hray

deps.title: The Adapted Frame is an Ambient Basis
deps.label: lem:adapted-frame-ambient-basis

deps.title: A Nonvanishing Top Form Orients the Space
deps.label: lem:nonvanishing-top-form-orientation

deps.title: A Unit Normal Inducing a Given Orientation
deps.label: def:oriented-unit-normal

blocks.tex:
The form $\bigl((w \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x$ is a nonzero $n$-form, so by Lemma
\ref{lem:nonvanishing-top-form-orientation} its positivity on $b$ says exactly that its ray equals
the orientation determined by $b$, which is $o(x)$. That is a statement about rays and mentions no
basis, so by the same lemma it implies positivity on every other basis positively oriented for
$o(x)$.

blocks.label: prop:existsunique-oriented-unit-normal
blocks.title: An Orientation Selects a Unit Normal
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde M, \tilde g)$ be an oriented Riemannian $(n+1)$-manifold with $n \geq 1$, let
$F : M \to \tilde M$ present the $n$-manifold $M$ as a smooth immersion, let $g$ be a metric on
$M$ that $F$ preserves, and let $o$ be a pointwise orientation of $M$. Then each $x \in M$ has
exactly one unit normal inducing $o$ at $x$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.existsUnique_isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt
lean.code:
theorem existsUnique_isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt (hpos : 0 < finrank ℝ E)
    {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯} (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' (f : M → M'))
    (himm : ∀ y : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f y))
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1) (x : M) :
    ∃! w : TangentSpace I' (f x),
      IsOrientedUnitNormalVecAt I g' o o' (f : M → M') hdim x w := by
  classical
  obtain ⟨w₀, hw₀, hspan⟩ := exists_isUnitNormalVecAt_span g' f himm hdim x
  obtain ⟨b, hb⟩ := exists_basis_orientation_eq hpos o x
  have hchar : ∀ w : TangentSpace I' (f x), (hw : IsUnitNormalVecAt I g' (f : M → M') x w) →
      (IsOrientedUnitNormalVecAt I g' o o' (f : M → M') hdim x w ↔
        0 < normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim x w b) :=
    fun w hw => isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt_iff_pos (o' := o') o h hw hdim b hb
  -- pick the sign
  rcases lt_or_gt_of_ne (normalRestrictVec_apply_basis_ne_zero (o' := o') h hw₀ hdim b)
    with hlt | hgt
  · refine ⟨-w₀, (hchar _ hw₀.neg).mpr ?_, ?_⟩
    · rw [normalRestrictVec_neg]
      exact neg_pos.mpr hlt
    · intro w hw
      rcases eq_or_eq_neg_of_isUnitNormalVecAt hw₀ hspan hw.isUnitNormal with rfl | rfl
      · exact absurd ((hchar _ hw₀).mp hw) (asymm hlt)
      · rfl
  · refine ⟨w₀, (hchar _ hw₀).mpr hgt, ?_⟩
    intro w hw
    rcases eq_or_eq_neg_of_isUnitNormalVecAt hw₀ hspan hw.isUnitNormal with rfl | rfl
    · rfl
    · have := (hchar _ hw₀.neg).mp hw
      rw [normalRestrictVec_neg] at this
      exact absurd (neg_pos.mp this) (asymm hgt)

variable (I g g' o o') in

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Hypersurface Is a Line
deps.label: lem:pointwise-unit-normal-span

deps.title: Negating the Normal Negates the Contracted Form
deps.label: lem:normal-restrict-neg-normal

deps.title: A Basis Realizing a Prescribed Orientation
deps.label: lem:basis-realizing-orientation

deps.title: A Hypersurface Has Exactly Two Unit Normals at Each Point
deps.label: lem:two-unit-normals

deps.title: A Unit Normal Inducing a Given Orientation
deps.label: def:oriented-unit-normal

blocks.tex:
By Lemma \ref{lem:pointwise-unit-normal-span} there is a unit normal $w_0$ at $x$ spanning
$N_x M$, and by Lemma \ref{lem:two-unit-normals} the only unit normals at $x$ are $\pm w_0$. By
Lemma \ref{lem:basis-realizing-orientation} fix a basis $b$ of $T_x M$ positively oriented for
$o(x)$, and set $t = \bigl((w_0 \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x(b)$. As in Proposition
\ref{prop:hypersurface-orientable-of-normal}, $t \neq 0$, because a nonzero $n$-form does not
vanish on a basis.

If $t > 0$ then $w_0$ induces $o$ at $x$ by Lemma \ref{lem:oriented-unit-normal-iff-pos}, and
$-w_0$ does not, since by Lemma \ref{lem:normal-restrict-neg-normal} its value on $b$ is $-t < 0$.
If $t < 0$ the roles are exchanged. Either way exactly one of $\pm w_0$ induces $o$ at $x$.

blocks.label: def:unit-normal-of-orientation
blocks.title: The Unit Normal Determined by an Orientation
blocks.body:
In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:existsunique-oriented-unit-normal}, the
\emph{unit normal determined by $o$} assigns to each $x \in M$ the unique unit normal at $x$
inducing $o$ at $x$. This is a global normal field, defined with no choices beyond the sign that
$o$ itself fixes.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.unitNormalOfOrientation
lean.code:
def unitNormalOfOrientation
    (hpos : 0 < finrank ℝ E) {f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯}
    (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' (f : M → M'))
    (himm : ∀ y : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f y))
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1) (y : M) : TangentSpace I' (f y) :=
  (existsUnique_isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt (o := o) (o' := o') hpos h himm hdim y).exists.choose

variable (I g g' o o') in

deps.title: A Unit Normal Inducing a Given Orientation
deps.label: def:oriented-unit-normal

deps.title: An Orientation Selects a Unit Normal
deps.label: prop:existsunique-oriented-unit-normal

blocks.label: prop:unit-normal-of-orientation-smooth
blocks.title: The Normal Determined by an Orientation Is Smooth
blocks.body:
In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:existsunique-oriented-unit-normal}, suppose in addition
that $o$ is a smooth orientation of $M$ and that the orientation of $\tilde M$ is smooth. Then the
unit normal determined by $o$ is a smooth global unit normal field.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isSmoothNormalField_unitNormalOfOrientation
lean.code:
theorem isSmoothNormalField_unitNormalOfOrientation (hpos : 0 < finrank ℝ E)
    {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯} (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' (f : M → M'))
    (himm : ∀ y : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f y))
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1)
    (ho : IsSmoothOrientation o) (ho' : IsSmoothOrientation o') :
    IsSmoothNormalField I f (unitNormalOfOrientation I g g' o o' hpos h himm hdim) := by
  classical
  intro p
  obtain ⟨u, Y, huopen, hpu, hY, hYo⟩ := ho p
  obtain ⟨v, hn, Nl, hvopen, hpv, hsmooth, hon, hnormal, hspan⟩ :=
    exists_orthonormalFrame_normalSpace g' f himm p
  have h0 : 0 < finrank ℝ E' - finrank ℝ E := by omega
  set W : (y : M) → TangentSpace I' (f y) := Nl ⟨0, h0⟩ with hWdef
  have hWunit : ∀ y ∈ v, IsUnitNormalVecAt I g' (f : M → M') y (W y) := by
    intro y hy
    exact ⟨fun z => hnormal y hy ⟨0, h0⟩ _ ⟨z, rfl⟩, by simpa using hon y hy ⟨0, h0⟩ ⟨0, h0⟩⟩
  have hWsmooth : ∀ y ∈ v, IsSmoothNormalAt I f W y :=
    fun y hy => (hsmooth ⟨0, h0⟩).contMDiffAt (hvopen.mem_nhds hy)
  set c : Set M := u ∩ v with hcdef
  have hcopen : IsOpen c := huopen.inter hvopen
  have hpc : p ∈ c := ⟨hpu, hpv⟩
  set s : M → ℝ := fun y => normalRestrict I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim W y (Y · y) with hsdef
  have hscont : ContinuousOn s c :=
    (contMDiffOn_normalRestrict_frame (hdim := hdim) ho' (hY.mono Set.inter_subset_left) hcopen
      (fun y hy => hWsmooth y hy.2)).continuousOn
  -- the value of the form on the frame `Y` *is* `s`, once the frame is read as a basis
  have hsval : ∀ y, ∀ hy : y ∈ c,
      normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim y (W y) ⇑(hY.toBasisAt hy.1) = s y := by
    intro y hy
    have hcoe : ⇑(hY.toBasisAt hy.1) = fun i => Y i y := funext fun i => hY.toBasisAt_coe hy.1 i
    -- `normalRestrict … W y` *is* `normalRestrictVec … y (W y)`, so only beta remains
    rw [hcoe, hsdef]
    rfl
  have hsne : ∀ y, ∀ hy : y ∈ c, s y ≠ 0 := by
    intro y hy
    rw [← hsval y hy]
    exact normalRestrictVec_apply_basis_ne_zero (o' := o') h (hWunit y hy.2) hdim _
  -- the sign of `s` decides which of `±W` is the selected normal
  have hcharW : ∀ y, ∀ hy : y ∈ c,
      (IsOrientedUnitNormalVecAt I g' o o' (f : M → M') hdim y (W y) ↔ 0 < s y) := by
    intro y hy
    rw [← hsval y hy]
    exact isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt_iff_pos (o' := o') o h (hWunit y hy.2) hdim _ (hYo y hy.1)
  have hcharNegW : ∀ y, ∀ hy : y ∈ c,
      (IsOrientedUnitNormalVecAt I g' o o' (f : M → M') hdim y (-(W y)) ↔ s y < 0) := by
    intro y hy
    rw [isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt_iff_pos (o' := o') o h (hWunit y hy.2).neg hdim _ (hYo y hy.1),
      normalRestrictVec_neg]
    show 0 < -(normalRestrictVec I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim y (W y) ⇑(hY.toBasisAt hy.1)) ↔ _
    rw [hsval y hy, neg_pos]
  rcases lt_or_gt_of_ne (hsne p hpc) with hlt | hgt
  · -- near `p` the form is negative on `Y`, so the selected normal is `-W` there
    have hopen : IsOpen (c ∩ s ⁻¹' (Set.Iio 0)) := hscont.isOpen_inter_preimage hcopen isOpen_Iio
    have heq : ∀ y ∈ c ∩ s ⁻¹' (Set.Iio 0),
        unitNormalOfOrientation I g g' o o' hpos h himm hdim y = (-W) y := fun y hy =>
      (eq_unitNormalOfOrientation I g g' o o' hpos h himm hdim
        ((hcharNegW y hy.1).mpr hy.2)).symm
    refine ContMDiffAt.congr_of_eventuallyEq ((hWsmooth p hpv).neg_section) ?_
    filter_upwards [hopen.mem_nhds ⟨hpc, hlt⟩] with y hy
    exact congrArg (TotalSpace.mk' E' y) (heq y hy)
  -- … (truncated)

deps.title: Smoothness of the Value on a Frame
deps.label: lem:normal-restrict-frame-smooth

deps.title: The Unit Normal Determined by an Orientation
deps.label: def:unit-normal-of-orientation

deps.title: Local Orthonormal Frames for the Normal Space
deps.label: lem:normal-space-orthonormal-frame

deps.title: Negating the Normal Negates the Contracted Form
deps.label: lem:normal-restrict-neg-normal

deps.title: An Orientation Selects a Unit Normal
deps.label: prop:existsunique-oriented-unit-normal

blocks.tex:
Write $N$ for the unit normal determined by $o$ and fix $p \in M$. Because $o$ is smooth, some
open $u \ni p$ carries a smooth local frame $(E_1, \ldots, E_n)$ of $TM$ that is positively
oriented for $o$ at every point of $u$. By Lemma \ref{lem:normal-space-orthonormal-frame} some
open $v \ni p$ carries a smooth local unit normal $W$. Put $c = u \cap v$ and
\[
  s(x) = \bigl((W \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x(E_1|_x, \ldots, E_n|_x), \qquad x \in c .
\]
By Lemma \ref{lem:normal-restrict-frame-smooth} $s$ is smooth on $c$, hence continuous, and it
never vanishes there, a nonzero $n$-form not vanishing on a basis.

By Lemma \ref{lem:oriented-unit-normal-iff-pos}, $W_x$ induces $o$ at $x \in c$ exactly when
$s(x) > 0$; and by Lemma \ref{lem:normal-restrict-neg-normal} the value of the form on $(E_i|_x)$
for $-W_x$ is $-s(x)$, so $-W_x$ induces $o$ at $x$ exactly when $s(x) < 0$. Since $s(p) \neq 0$,
one of these two cases holds on the open neighbourhood $c \cap s^{-1}\bigl((0,\infty)\bigr)$ or
$c \cap s^{-1}\bigl((-\infty,0)\bigr)$ of $p$; by the uniqueness in Proposition
\ref{prop:existsunique-oriented-unit-normal}, $N = W$ on the first and $N = -W$ on the second.
Both $W$ and $-W$ are smooth on $v$, so $N$ is smooth at $p$. As $p$ was arbitrary, $N$ is a
smooth global unit normal field.

blocks.label: prop:hypersurface-volume-form-only-if
blocks.title: Hypersurface Volume Form: the Necessity Half
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde M, \tilde g)$ be an oriented Riemannian $(n+1)$-manifold with $n \geq 1$, let
$F : M \to \tilde M$ present the $n$-manifold $M$ as a smooth immersion, let $g$ be a metric on
$M$ that $F$ preserves, and suppose $M$ carries a smooth orientation $o$. Then $M$ admits a
smooth global unit normal field $N$, and for it
\[
  dV_g = (N \lrcorner dV_{\tilde{g}})|_M. \tag{2.17}
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isSmoothNormalField_volumeForm_eq_normalRestrict
lean.code:
theorem exists_isSmoothNormalField_volumeForm_eq_normalRestrict (hpos : 0 < finrank ℝ E)
    {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯} (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' (f : M → M'))
    (himm : ∀ y : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f y))
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1)
    (ho : IsSmoothOrientation o) (ho' : IsSmoothOrientation o') :
    ∃ N : (y : M) → TangentSpace I' (f y),
      (∀ y, IsUnitNormalAt I g' (f : M → M') N y) ∧ IsSmoothNormalField I f N ∧
        ∀ x, g.volumeForm o x = normalRestrict I g' o' (f : M → M') hdim N x := by
  refine ⟨unitNormalOfOrientation I g g' o o' hpos h himm hdim, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · exact fun y => isUnitNormalAt_iff_vec.mpr
      ((isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt_unitNormalOfOrientation I g g' o o' hpos h himm hdim
        y).isUnitNormal)
  · exact isSmoothNormalField_unitNormalOfOrientation hpos h himm hdim ho ho'
  · intro x
    have hN : ∀ y, IsUnitNormalAt I g' (f : M → M')
        (unitNormalOfOrientation I g g' o o' hpos h himm hdim) y := fun y =>
      isUnitNormalAt_iff_vec.mpr
        ((isOrientedUnitNormalVecAt_unitNormalOfOrientation I g g' o o' hpos h himm hdim
          y).isUnitNormal)
    have hor := inducedOrientation_unitNormalOfOrientation (o' := o') hpos h himm hdim hN
    have hkey := volumeForm_inducedOrientation_eq_normalRestrict g h hN hdim (o' := o') x
    rwa [hor] at hkey

deps.title: The Normal Determined by an Orientation Is Smooth
deps.label: prop:unit-normal-of-orientation-smooth

deps.title: The Unit Normal Determined by an Orientation
deps.label: def:unit-normal-of-orientation

deps.title: The Orientation Induced by a Unit Normal
deps.label: def:induced-orientation

deps.title: A Basis Realizing a Prescribed Orientation
deps.label: lem:basis-realizing-orientation

blocks.tex:
Let $N$ be the unit normal determined by $o$; it is a smooth global unit normal field by
Proposition \ref{prop:unit-normal-of-orientation-smooth}. Its induced orientation is $o$ itself:
at each $x$, pick by Lemma \ref{lem:basis-realizing-orientation} a basis $b$ of $T_x M$ positively
oriented for $o(x)$; then $\bigl((N \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M\bigr)_x(b) > 0$ because $N_x$
induces $o$ at $x$, so by Lemma \ref{lem:nonvanishing-top-form-orientation} the ray of that form
— which is by definition the induced orientation at $x$ — equals the orientation of $b$, namely
$o(x)$. Now (2.17) is Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-volume-form-induced} applied to $N$.

blocks.label: prop:hypersurface-volume-form
blocks.title: prop:hypersurface-volume-form
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde M, \tilde g)$ be an oriented Riemannian $(n+1)$-manifold with $n \geq 1$, let
$F : M \to \tilde M$ present the $n$-manifold $M$ as a smooth immersion, and let $g$ be a metric
on $M$ that $F$ preserves — for instance the induced metric. Then $M$ is orientable if and only
if there exists a smooth global unit normal vector field $N$ for $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isSmoothOrientation_iff_exists_isSmoothNormalField
lean.code:
theorem exists_isSmoothOrientation_iff_exists_isSmoothNormalField (hpos : 0 < finrank ℝ E)
    {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯} (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' (f : M → M'))
    (himm : ∀ y : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f y))
    (hdim : finrank ℝ E' = finrank ℝ E + 1) (ho' : IsSmoothOrientation o') :
    (∃ o : PointwiseOrientation I M, IsSmoothOrientation o) ↔
      ∃ N : (y : M) → TangentSpace I' (f y),
        (∀ y, IsUnitNormalAt I g' (f : M → M') N y) ∧ IsSmoothNormalField I f N := by
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨o, ho⟩
    obtain ⟨N, hN, hNsmooth, -⟩ :=
      exists_isSmoothNormalField_volumeForm_eq_normalRestrict (o := o) (o' := o')
        hpos h himm hdim ho ho'
    exact ⟨N, hN, hNsmooth⟩
  · rintro ⟨N, hN, hNsmooth⟩
    exact ⟨inducedOrientation g g' o' (f : M → M') hdim N h hN,
      isSmoothOrientation_inducedOrientation (hdim := hdim) hpos h hN hNsmooth ho'⟩

deps.title: Hypersurface Volume Form: the Necessity Half
deps.label: prop:hypersurface-volume-form-only-if

deps.title: The Normal Determined by an Orientation Is Smooth
deps.label: prop:unit-normal-of-orientation-smooth

deps.title: Hypersurface Volume Form: the Sufficiency Half
deps.label: prop:hypersurface-volume-form-if

deps.title: The Orientation Induced by a Unit Normal
deps.label: def:induced-orientation

deps.title: Local Orthonormal Frames for the Normal Space
deps.label: lem:normal-space-orthonormal-frame

blocks.tex:
If $N$ is a smooth global unit normal field, then the orientation it induces (Definition
\ref{def:induced-orientation}) is smooth by Proposition
\ref{prop:hypersurface-orientable-of-normal}, so $M$ is orientable. Conversely, if $M$ carries a
smooth orientation $o$, then the unit normal determined by $o$ is a smooth global unit normal
field by Proposition \ref{prop:unit-normal-of-orientation-smooth}.

In either direction the volume form is the contraction of the ambient one against the normal:
equation (2.17),
\[
  dV_g = (N \lrcorner dV_{\tilde g})|_M ,
\]
holds for the orientation and normal that the respective direction produces. That is
Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-volume-form-if} for a given normal and Proposition
\ref{prop:hypersurface-volume-form-only-if} for a given orientation.

When $M$ is not orientable, we can still define integrals of functions, but now we have to use \emph{densities} instead of differential forms (see pp.~405--406).

blocks.title: The density of an inner product space

blocks.tex:
A density on a vector space is the unoriented analogue of a top-degree form: it assigns an
unsigned volume to an $n$-tuple of vectors, and consequently transforms by $|\det|$ rather than
by $\det$. Because no sign is involved, no orientation is needed anywhere in this subsection,
and that is precisely what makes Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-density} available on every
Riemannian manifold rather than only on the orientable ones.

blocks.label: def:density-vector-space
blocks.title: Density of a Vector Space

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsDensity

blocks.label: def:density-pointwise
blocks.title: The Density of an Inner Product Space
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional real inner product space. For $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$ set
\[
|v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n| := \sqrt{\det \bigl( \langle v_i, v_j \rangle \bigr)_{i,j=1}^{n}},
\]
the square root of the determinant of the Gram matrix of $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$. The Gram matrix
is positive semidefinite, so its determinant is nonnegative and the square root is defined.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.densityL
lean.code: def densityL (v : Fin n → V) : ℝ := Real.sqrt ((Matrix.gram ℝ v).det)

blocks.label: prop:density-abs-det
blocks.title: The Determinant Formula for the Density
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional real inner product space, let $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ be any
orthonormal basis of $V$, and let $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$. Then
\[
|v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n| = \bigl| \det \bigl( \langle e_i, v_j \rangle \bigr)_{i,j=1}^{n} \bigr|.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.densityL_eq_abs_det
lean.code:
theorem densityL_eq_abs_det (e : OrthonormalBasis (Fin n) ℝ V) (v : Fin n → V) :
    densityL v = |(Matrix.of fun i j => ⟪e i, v j⟫_ℝ).det| := by
  classical
  set m : Matrix (Fin n) (Fin n) ℝ := Matrix.of fun i j => ⟪e i, v j⟫_ℝ with hm
  have hgram : Matrix.gram ℝ v = mᴴ * m := by
    have h := Matrix.gram_eq_conjTranspose_mul (𝕜 := ℝ) e v
    rw [h]
    congr 1 <;> · ext i j; simp [hm, e.repr_apply_apply]
  rw [densityL_def, hgram, Matrix.det_mul, Matrix.det_conjTranspose, RCLike.star_def,
    starRingEnd_apply, star_trivial, ← sq, Real.sqrt_sq_eq_abs]

deps.title: The Density of an Inner Product Space
deps.label: def:density-pointwise

blocks.tex:
Write $M = (\langle e_i, v_j \rangle)_{i,j}$. Expanding each $v_j$ in the orthonormal basis gives
$v_j = \sum_k \langle e_k, v_j \rangle e_k$, so
\[
\langle v_i, v_j \rangle
= \Bigl\langle \sum_k \langle e_k, v_i \rangle e_k, \; \sum_l \langle e_l, v_j \rangle e_l \Bigr\rangle
= \sum_k \langle e_k, v_i \rangle \langle e_k, v_j \rangle,
\]
by orthonormality of $(e_k)$; that is, the Gram matrix of $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ equals
$M^{\mathsf{T}} M$. Hence
$\det (\langle v_i, v_j \rangle) = \det(M^{\mathsf{T}}) \det(M) = (\det M)^2$, and taking the
square root gives $\sqrt{(\det M)^2} = |\det M|$.

blocks.label: prop:density-value-one
blocks.title: The Density of an Orthonormal Basis
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional real inner product space and let $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ be an
orthonormal basis of $V$. Then $|e_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge e_n| = 1$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.densityL_apply_orthonormalBasis
lean.code:
theorem densityL_apply_orthonormalBasis (e : OrthonormalBasis (Fin n) ℝ V) :
    densityL (e ·) = 1 := by
  rw [densityL_eq_abs_det e]
  rw [show (Matrix.of fun i j => ⟪e i, e j⟫_ℝ) = (1 : Matrix (Fin n) (Fin n) ℝ) by
    ext i j
    rw [Matrix.of_apply, Matrix.one_apply, orthonormal_iff_ite.mp e.orthonormal i j]]
  simp

deps.title: The Determinant Formula for the Density
deps.label: prop:density-abs-det

blocks.tex:
Apply Proposition \ref{prop:density-abs-det} with $v_i = e_i$. The matrix
$(\langle e_i, e_j \rangle)_{i,j}$ is the identity matrix by orthonormality, and its determinant
is $1$, so the density is $|1| = 1$.

blocks.label: prop:density-transformation-law
blocks.title: The Transformation Law
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional real inner product space, let $f : V \to V$ be linear and let
$v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$. Then
\[
|f v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge f v_n| = |\det f| \cdot |v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n|.
\]
In particular $|{\cdot} \wedge \cdots \wedge {\cdot}|$ is a density on $V$ in the sense of
Definition \ref{def:density-vector-space}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.densityL_comp
lean.code:
theorem densityL_comp (e : OrthonormalBasis (Fin n) ℝ V) (f : V →ₗ[ℝ] V) (v : Fin n → V) :
    densityL (f ∘ v) = |LinearMap.det f| * densityL v := by
  classical
  haveI : FiniteDimensional ℝ V := e.toBasis.finiteDimensional_of_finite
  -- the matrix of `f ∘ v` in `e` is the matrix of `f` times the matrix of `v`
  have hmul : (Matrix.of fun i j => ⟪e i, (f ∘ v) j⟫_ℝ)
      = (LinearMap.toMatrix e.toBasis e.toBasis f) * (Matrix.of fun i j => ⟪e i, v j⟫_ℝ) := by
    ext i j
    rw [Matrix.of_apply, Matrix.mul_apply]
    show ⟪e i, f (v j)⟫_ℝ = _
    -- expand `v j` in the orthonormal basis `e` and use linearity of `f`
    conv_lhs => rw [← e.sum_repr (v j)]
    rw [map_sum, inner_sum]
    refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun k _ => ?_
    rw [map_smul, real_inner_smul_right, LinearMap.toMatrix_apply, e.coe_toBasis,
      e.coe_toBasis_repr_apply, e.repr_apply_apply, e.repr_apply_apply, mul_comm,
      Matrix.of_apply]
  rw [densityL_eq_abs_det e, densityL_eq_abs_det e, hmul, Matrix.det_mul, abs_mul,
    LinearMap.det_toMatrix]

deps.title: The Determinant Formula for the Density
deps.label: prop:density-abs-det

blocks.tex:
Fix an orthonormal basis $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ of $V$ and let $A = (\langle e_i, f e_k \rangle)_{i,k}$
be the matrix of $f$ in that basis, so that $\det A = \det f$. Expanding $v_j$ in the basis and
using linearity of $f$,
\[
\langle e_i, f v_j \rangle
= \Bigl\langle e_i, \; f \Bigl( \sum_k \langle e_k, v_j \rangle e_k \Bigr) \Bigr\rangle
= \sum_k \langle e_i, f e_k \rangle \langle e_k, v_j \rangle,
\]
so the matrix of $(f v_1, \ldots, f v_n)$ against $(e_i)$ is $A \cdot M$, where
$M = (\langle e_i, v_j \rangle)_{i,j}$. By Proposition \ref{prop:density-abs-det} applied twice,
\[
|f v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge f v_n| = |\det(AM)| = |\det A| \, |\det M|
= |\det f| \cdot |v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n|. \qedhere
\]

blocks.label: prop:density-positive
blocks.title: Positivity of the Density
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional real inner product space and let $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ be linearly
independent. Then $|v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n| > 0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.densityL_pos
lean.code:
theorem densityL_pos {v : Fin n → V} (hv : LinearIndependent ℝ v) : 0 < densityL v := by
  rw [densityL_def, Real.sqrt_pos]
  exact (Matrix.posDef_gram_of_linearIndependent (𝕜 := ℝ) hv).det_pos

deps.title: The Density of an Inner Product Space
deps.label: def:density-pointwise

blocks.tex:
The Gram matrix of a linearly independent family is positive definite: for $c \neq 0$ the vector
$w = \sum_j c_j v_j$ is nonzero, and $c^{\mathsf{T}} (\langle v_i, v_j \rangle) c = \langle w, w \rangle > 0$.
A positive definite matrix has positive determinant, so the square root defining
$|v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n|$ is a square root of a positive number.

blocks.label: prop:density-uniqueness-pointwise
blocks.title: Fibrewise Uniqueness of the Density
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional real inner product space, let $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ be an orthonormal
basis of $V$, and let $\mu$ be a density on $V$ with $\mu(e_1, \ldots, e_n) = 1$. Then
$\mu(v_1, \ldots, v_n) = |v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n|$ for all $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.densityL_unique
lean.code:
theorem densityL_unique (e : OrthonormalBasis (Fin n) ℝ V) (μ : (Fin n → V) → ℝ)
    (hμ : IsDensity μ) (h1 : μ (e ·) = 1) :
    μ = densityL := by
  funext v
  set f : V →ₗ[ℝ] V := e.toBasis.constr ℝ v with hf
  have hfe : f ∘ (e ·) = v := by
    funext i
    show f (e i) = v i
    rw [hf, ← e.coe_toBasis, Basis.constr_basis]
  rw [← hfe, hμ f (e ·), h1, mul_one, densityL_comp e f (e ·),
    densityL_apply_orthonormalBasis e, mul_one]

deps.title: The Density of an Orthonormal Basis
deps.label: prop:density-value-one

blocks.tex:
Let $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$ and let $f : V \to V$ be the unique linear map with $f e_i = v_i$ for
every $i$. By the transformation law (B.14) for $\mu$ and the hypothesis $\mu(e_1,\ldots,e_n) = 1$,
\[
\mu(v_1, \ldots, v_n) = \mu(f e_1, \ldots, f e_n) = |\det f| \, \mu(e_1, \ldots, e_n) = |\det f|.
\]
By Proposition \ref{prop:density-transformation-law} and Proposition \ref{prop:density-value-one}
the same computation gives
$|v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n| = |\det f| \cdot |e_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge e_n| = |\det f|$.
The two agree.

blocks.label: prop:density-eq-abs-volume-form
blocks.title: The Density Is the Absolute Value of the Volume Form
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional real inner product space with $n > 0$, let $o$ be an orientation of
$V$, and let $\omega$ be the volume form of $(V, o)$. Then
$|v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n| = |\omega(v_1, \ldots, v_n)|$ for all $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.densityL_eq_abs_volumeForm
lean.code:
theorem densityL_eq_abs_volumeForm [Fact (finrank ℝ V = n)] [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] (hn : 0 < n)
    (o : Orientation ℝ V (Fin n)) (v : Fin n → V) :
    densityL v = |o.volumeForm v| := by
  classical
  obtain ⟨e, he⟩ : ∃ e : OrthonormalBasis (Fin n) ℝ V, e.toBasis.orientation = o :=
    ⟨o.finOrthonormalBasis hn Fact.out, o.finOrthonormalBasis_orientation hn Fact.out⟩
  rw [densityL_eq_abs_det e, ← Orientation.volumeFormL_apply,
    volumeFormL_apply_eq_det o e he v]

end Pointwise

/-! ## Smooth densities on a manifold -/

section Manifold

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]

variable (I M) in
/-- **A smooth density on `M`** (Lee, Appendix B).

A pointwise family `μ_x` of densities on the tangent spaces, obeying the transformation law
(B.14) at each point, and *smooth* in the sense that pairing it with any smooth local frame
gives a smooth function.

Smoothness is stated against frames because mathlib has no density bundle to be a section of
— see the header.  It is the honest analogue of `IsSmoothOrientation`, which the volume form
needs for the same reason, and unlike that predicate it costs nothing: it is not an extra
hypothesis on `M`, just the smoothness of `μ` itself. -/
structure SmoothDensity where
  /-- The value of the density on a tuple of tangent vectors at `x`. -/
  toFun : ∀ x : M, (Fin (finrank ℝ E) → TangentSpace I x) → ℝ
  /-- Each `μ_x` is a density on `T_x M` — Lee's (B.14). -/
  isDensity : ∀ x : M, IsDensity (toFun x)
  /-- Pairing with a smooth local frame gives a smooth function. -/
  contMDiffOn : ∀ {u : Set M} {Y : Fin (finrank ℝ E) → (x : M) → TangentSpace I x},
    IsLocalFrameOn I E ∞ Y u → ContMDiffOn I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞ (fun x => toFun x (Y · x)) u

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] in
@[ext]

deps.title: The Density of an Inner Product Space
deps.label: def:density-pointwise

deps.title: The Determinant Formula for the Density
deps.label: prop:density-abs-det

blocks.tex:
Choose an orthonormal basis $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ of $V$ inducing $o$; this is possible because
$n > 0$. The determinant formula for the volume form gives
$\omega(v_1, \ldots, v_n) = \det (\langle e_i, v_j \rangle)_{i,j}$, while Proposition
\ref{prop:density-abs-det} gives
$|v_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge v_n| = |\det (\langle e_i, v_j \rangle)_{i,j}|$. Taking absolute values
of the former yields the latter.

blocks.title: Smooth densities and the Riemannian density

blocks.label: def:smooth-density
blocks.title: Smooth Density
blocks.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth $n$-manifold. A \emph{smooth density} on $M$ is a family $\mu$ assigning to
each $x \in M$ a density $\mu_x$ on $T_x M$, such that for every smooth local frame
$(Y_1, \ldots, Y_n)$ on an open set $u \subseteq M$ the function
$x \mapsto \mu_x(Y_1|_x, \ldots, Y_n|_x)$ is smooth on $u$.

blocks.label: def:riemannian-density-pointwise
blocks.title: The Riemannian Density, Pointwise
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian $n$-manifold. For $x \in M$ and $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in T_x M$ let
\[
\mu_x(v_1, \ldots, v_n) := \sqrt{\det \bigl( \langle v_i, v_j \rangle_g \bigr)_{i,j=1}^{n}}
\]
be the density of the inner product space $(T_x M, g_x)$ of Definition
\ref{def:density-pointwise}. No orientation of $M$ enters this definition.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.densityAt

deps.title: The Density of an Inner Product Space
deps.label: def:density-pointwise

blocks.label: prop:density-smooth
blocks.title: Smoothness of the Riemannian Density
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian $n$-manifold and let $(Y_1, \ldots, Y_n)$ be a smooth local frame on
an open set $u \subseteq M$. Then $x \mapsto \mu_x(Y_1|_x, \ldots, Y_n|_x)$ is smooth on $u$; that
is, $\mu$ is a smooth density.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.contMDiffOn_densityAt
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffOn_densityAt {u : Set M} {Y : Fin (finrank ℝ E) → (x : M) → TangentSpace I x}
    (hY : IsLocalFrameOn I E ∞ Y u) :
    ContMDiffOn I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞ (fun x => g.densityAt x (Y · x)) u := by
  classical
  intro x hx
  -- the Gram entries are smooth
  have hentry : ∀ i j, ContMDiffWithinAt I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
      (fun q => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)) u x := fun i j =>
    g.contMDiffWithinAt_innerAt (hY.contMDiffOn i x hx) (hY.contMDiffOn j x hx)
  -- a determinant is a polynomial in its entries
  have hdet : ContMDiffWithinAt I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
      (fun q => (Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)).det) u x := by
    simp only [Matrix.det_apply']
    refine ContMDiffWithinAt.sum fun σ _ => ?_
    exact contMDiffWithinAt_const.mul (ContMDiffWithinAt.prod fun i _ => hentry (σ i) i)
  -- the determinant is positive at `x`, so `√` is smooth there
  have hpos : 0 < (Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner x (Y i x) (Y j x)).det := by
    have := g.densityAt_pos (x := x) (v := (Y · x)) (hY.linearIndependent hx)
    rw [densityAt, Real.sqrt_pos] at this
    exact this
  show ContMDiffWithinAt I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
    (fun q => Real.sqrt ((Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)).det)) u x
  exact (Real.contDiffAt_sqrt (ne_of_gt hpos)).comp_contMDiffWithinAt
    (f := fun q => (Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)).det) hdet

deps.title: Smoothness of the pairing of vector fields
deps.label: lem:inner-product-vector-fields-smooth

blocks.tex:
The entries $x \mapsto \langle Y_i|_x, Y_j|_x \rangle_g$ of the Gram matrix are smooth on $u$,
being pairings of smooth vector fields under $g$. A determinant is a polynomial in the matrix
entries, so $x \mapsto \det (\langle Y_i|_x, Y_j|_x \rangle_g)$ is smooth on $u$. At every $x \in u$
the vectors $(Y_1|_x, \ldots, Y_n|_x)$ are linearly independent, so this determinant is strictly
positive by Proposition \ref{prop:density-positive}; since the square root is smooth away from
$0$, the composite $x \mapsto \sqrt{\det (\langle Y_i|_x, Y_j|_x \rangle_g)}$ is smooth on $u$.

blocks.label: prop:riemannian-density
blocks.title: The Riemannian Density

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.riemannian_density_existsUnique
lean.code:
theorem riemannian_density_existsUnique :
    ∃! μ : SmoothDensity I M, μ.IsRiemannianFor g := by
  refine ⟨g.density, fun u Y hY hon x hx => g.densityAt_apply_eq_one (hon x hx), ?_⟩
  intro μ hμ
  refine SmoothDensity.ext (funext fun x => ?_)
  letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  -- an orthonormal frame near `x`, hence an orthonormal basis of `T_x M`
  obtain ⟨u, Y, hu, hxu, hY, hon⟩ := exists_orthonormalFrame_nhds g x
  have hon' : Orthonormal ℝ (hY.toBasisAt hxu) := RiemannianMetric.orthonormal_toBasisAt g hY hon hxu
  set e : OrthonormalBasis (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) ℝ (TangentSpace I x) :=
    (hY.toBasisAt hxu).toOrthonormalBasis hon' with he
  have hecoe : ∀ i, e i = Y i x := by
    intro i
    rw [he, Basis.coe_toOrthonormalBasis, IsLocalFrameOn.toBasisAt_coe]
  -- `μ_x` and `densityL` are two densities agreeing on that basis
  have h1 : μ.toFun x (e ·) = 1 := by
    rw [show (fun i => e i) = (Y · x) from funext hecoe]
    exact hμ u Y hY hon x hxu
  have hxu' := densityL_unique e (μ.toFun x) (μ.isDensity x) h1
  funext v
  rw [RiemannianMetric.density_toFun, g.densityAt_eq_densityL x]
  exact congrFun hxu' v

deps.title: The Density of an Orthonormal Basis
deps.label: prop:density-value-one

blocks.tex:
\emph{Existence.} Take $\mu$ to be the family of Definition \ref{def:riemannian-density-pointwise}.
Each $\mu_x$ is a density on $T_x M$ by Proposition \ref{prop:density-transformation-law}, and
$\mu$ is smooth by Proposition \ref{prop:density-smooth}, so $\mu$ is a smooth density. If
$(E_1, \ldots, E_n)$ is a local orthonormal frame on $u$ then $(E_1|_x, \ldots, E_n|_x)$ is an
orthonormal basis of $(T_x M, g_x)$ for each $x \in u$, so $\mu_x(E_1|_x, \ldots, E_n|_x) = 1$ by
Proposition \ref{prop:density-value-one}; this is (2.18). It is positive by Proposition
\ref{prop:density-positive}, since the values of a frame are linearly independent.

\emph{Uniqueness.} Let $\nu$ be a smooth density satisfying (2.18) and let $x \in M$. By
Proposition \ref{prop:existence-orthonormal-frames} there are a neighbourhood $u$ of $x$ and a
local orthonormal frame $(E_1, \ldots, E_n)$ on $u$. Then $(E_1|_x, \ldots, E_n|_x)$ is an
orthonormal basis of $T_x M$ and $\nu_x(E_1|_x, \ldots, E_n|_x) = 1$ by (2.18). Since $\nu_x$ is a
density on $T_x M$ taking the value $1$ on an orthonormal basis, Proposition
\ref{prop:density-uniqueness-pointwise} gives $\nu_x = \mu_x$. As $x$ was arbitrary, $\nu = \mu$.

In advanced calculus, you have undoubtedly been introduced to three important
differential operators involving vector fields on $\mathbb{R}^3$: the \emph{gradient} (which takes real-valued functions to vector fields), \emph{divergence} (vector fields to functions), and \emph{curl}
(vector fields to vector fields). We have already described how the gradient operator
can be generalized to Riemannian manifolds (see equation (2.14)); now we can show
that the divergence operator also generalizes easily to that setting. Problem 2-27
describes a similar, but more limited, generalization of the curl.

Suppose $(M, g)$ is an oriented Riemannian $n$-manifold with or without boundary,
and $dV_g$ is its volume form. If $X$ is a smooth vector field on $M$, then $X \lrcorner dV_g$ is
an $(n-1)$-form. The exterior derivative of this $(n-1)$-form is a smooth $n$-form, so
it can be expressed as a smooth function multiplied by $dV_g$. That function is called
the \emph{divergence of } $X$, and denoted by $\operatorname{div} X$; thus it is characterized by the following
formula:
\begin{equation}
d(X \lrcorner dV_g) = (\operatorname{div} X)dV_g. \tag{2.19}
\end{equation}

Even if $M$ is nonorientable, in a neighborhood of each point we can choose an
orientation and define the divergence by (2.19), and then note that reversing the orientation changes the sign of $dV_g$ on both sides of the equation, so $\operatorname{div} X$ is well
defined, independently of the choice of orientation. In this way, we can define the
divergence operator on \emph{any} Riemannian manifold with or without boundary, by
requiring that it satisfy (2.19) for any choice of orientation in a neighborhood of
each point.

The most important application of the divergence operator is the \emph{divergence theorem}, which you will be asked to prove in Problem 2-22.

Using the divergence operator, we can define another important operator, this one
acting on real-valued functions. The \emph{Laplacian} (or \emph{Laplace--Beltrami operator}) is
the linear operator $\Delta: C^\infty(M) \to C^\infty(M)$ defined by
\begin{equation}
\Delta u = \operatorname{div}(\operatorname{grad} u). \tag{2.20}
\end{equation}

(Note that many books, including [LeeSM] and the first edition of this book, define
the Laplacian as $-\operatorname{div}(\operatorname{grad} u)$. The main reason for choosing the negative sign is
so that the operator will have nonnegative eigenvalues; see Problem 2-24. But the
definition we give here is much more common in Riemannian geometry.)

The next proposition gives alternative formulas for these operators.

blocks.label: prop:coordinate-representations-divergence-laplacian
blocks.title: prop:coordinate-representations-divergence-laplacian
blocks.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary,
and let $(x^i)$ be any smooth local coordinates on an open set $U \subseteq M$. The coordinate
representations of the divergence and Laplacian are as follows:
\[
\operatorname{div}\left(X^i \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}} \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\left(\sqrt{\det g}\, X^i\right),
\]
\[
\Delta u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}} \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\left(\sqrt{\det g}\, g^{ij} \frac{\partial u}{\partial x^j}\right),
\]
where $\det g = \det(g_{kl})$ is the determinant of the component matrix of $g$ in these coordinates. On $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean metric and standard coordinates, these reduce to

\[
\operatorname{div}\left(X^i \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial X^i}{\partial x^i},
\]

\[
\Delta u = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial^2 u}{(\partial x^i)^2}.
\]

blocks.tex: \textit{Proof.} Problem 2-21. \hfill $\square$

Perhaps the most important tool that a Riemannian metric gives us is the ability to measure lengths of curves and distances between points. Throughout this section, $(M, g)$ denotes a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary.

Without further qualification, a \textbf{curve} in $M$ always means a \textbf{parametrized curve}, that is, a continuous map $\gamma: I \to M$, where $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is some interval. Unless otherwise specified, we will not worry about whether the interval is bounded or unbounded, or whether it includes endpoints or not. To say that $\gamma$ is a \textbf{smooth curve} is to say that it is smooth as a map from the manifold (with boundary) $I$ to $M$. If $I$ has one or two endpoints and $M$ has empty boundary, then $\gamma$ is smooth if and only if it extends to a smooth curve defined on some open interval containing $I$. (If $\partial M \neq \emptyset$, then smoothness of $\gamma$ has to be interpreted as meaning that each coordinate representation of $\gamma$ has a smooth extension to an open interval.) A \textbf{curve segment} is a curve whose domain is a compact interval.

A smooth curve $\gamma: I \to M$ has a well-defined velocity $\gamma'(t) \in T_{\gamma(t)} M$ for each $t \in I$. We say that $\gamma$ is a \textbf{regular curve} if $\gamma'(t) \neq 0$ for $t \in I$. This implies that $\gamma$ is an immersion, so its image has no \textquotedblleft corners\textquotedblright\ or \textquotedblleft kinks.\textquotedblright

We wish to use curve segments as \textquotedblleft measuring tapes\textquotedblright\ to define distances between points in a connected Riemannian manifold. Many aspects of the theory become technically much simpler if we work with a slightly larger class of curve segments instead of just the regular ones. We now describe the appropriate class of curves.

If $[a, b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is a closed bounded interval, a \textbf{partition of $[a,b]$} is a finite sequence $(a_0, \ldots, a_k)$ of real numbers such that $a = a_0 < a_1 < \cdots < a_k = b$. Each interval $[a_{i-1}, a_i]$ is called a \textbf{subinterval of the partition}. If $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, a (continuous) curve segment $\gamma: [a, b] \to M$ is said to be
\emph{piecewise regular} if there exists a partition $(a_0,\ldots,a_k)$ of $[a,b]$ such that $\gamma|_{[a_{i-1},a_i]}$ is a regular curve segment (meaning it is smooth with nonvanishing velocity) for $i=1,\ldots,k$. For brevity, we refer to a piecewise regular curve segment as an \textbf{admissible curve}, and any partition $(a_0,\ldots,a_k)$ such that $\gamma|_{[a_{i-1},a_i]}$ is smooth for each $i$ an \textbf{admissible partition for $\gamma$}. (There are many admissible partitions for a given admissible curve, because we can always add more points to the partition.) It is also convenient to consider any map $\gamma: \{a\} \to M$ whose domain is a single real number to be an admissible curve.

Suppose $\gamma$ is an admissible curve and $(a_0,\ldots,a_k)$ is an admissible partition for it. At each of the intermediate partition points $a_1,\ldots,a_{k-1}$, there are two one-sided velocity vectors, which we denote by
\[
\gamma'(a_i^-) = \lim_{t \nearrow a_i} \gamma'(t),
\]
\[
\gamma'(a_i^+) = \lim_{t \searrow a_i} \gamma'(t).
\]
They are both nonzero, but they need not be equal.

If $\gamma: I \to M$ is a smooth curve, we define a \emph{reparametrization} of $\gamma$ to be a curve of the form $\tilde{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \varphi: I' \to M$, where $I' \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is another interval and $\varphi: I' \to I$ is a diffeomorphism. Because intervals are connected, $\varphi$ is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing on $I'$. We say that $\tilde{\gamma}$ is a \emph{forward reparametrization} if $\varphi$ is increasing, and a \emph{backward reparametrization} if it is decreasing.

For an admissible curve $\gamma: [a,b] \to M$, we define a \emph{reparametrization of $\gamma$} a little more broadly, as a curve of the form $\tilde{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \varphi$, where $\varphi: [c,d] \to [a,b]$ is a homeomorphism for which there is a partition $(c_0,\ldots,c_k)$ of $[c,d]$ such that the restriction of $\varphi$ to each subinterval $[c_{i-1},c_i]$ is a diffeomorphism onto its image.

If $\gamma: [a,b] \to M$ is an admissible curve, we define the \emph{length of $\gamma$} to be
\[
L_g(\gamma) = \int_a^b |\gamma'(t)|_g \, dt.
\]
The integrand is bounded and continuous everywhere on $[a,b]$ except possibly at the finitely many points where $\gamma$ is not smooth, so this integral is well defined.

blocks.label: def:length-of-curve
blocks.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
blocks.body:
For a curve $\gamma$ into $M$ and $a \le b$, the \emph{length} of $\gamma$ on $[a,b]$ is
\[
  L_g(\gamma|_{[a,b]}) \;=\; \int_{[a,b]} |\gamma'(t)|_g \, dt \;\in\; [0,\infty],
\]
the integral being taken in the Lebesgue sense, so that it is defined for every curve and takes values in $[0,\infty]$. For an admissible curve on a compact interval the integrand is bounded, so the value is finite and agrees with $L_g(\gamma)$ above.

blocks.label: prop:length-additive
blocks.title: Additivity of Length
blocks.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary and $\gamma$ is an admissible curve. If $a \le c \le b$, then
\[
  L_g(\gamma|_{[a,c]}) + L_g(\gamma|_{[c,b]}) = L_g(\gamma|_{[a,b]}).
\]

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

blocks.label: prop:length-reparam-invariant
blocks.title: Parameter Independence of Length
blocks.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary and $\gamma$ is an admissible curve on $[a,b]$. If $\tilde{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \varphi$ is a forward reparametrization of $\gamma$, then $L_g(\tilde{\gamma}) = L_g(\gamma)$.

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

blocks.label: prop:length-isometry-invariant
blocks.title: Isometry Invariance of Length
blocks.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ and $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ are Riemannian manifolds with or without boundary, $\varphi: M \to \tilde{M}$ is a smooth metric-preserving map, and $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ is an admissible curve. Then
\[
  L_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi \circ \gamma) = L_g(\gamma).
\]
In particular this holds when $\varphi$ is a local isometry, since a local isometry preserves the metric.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsMetricPreserving.pathELength_comp
lean.code:
theorem IsMetricPreserving.pathELength_comp (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' φ)
    (hφ : ContMDiff I I' ∞ φ) {γ : ℝ → M} {a b : ℝ}
    (hγ : ContMDiffOn 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) I 1 γ (Icc a b)) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    pathELength I' (φ ∘ γ) a b = pathELength I γ a b := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  rw [pathELength_eq_lintegral_mfderiv_Ioo, pathELength_eq_lintegral_mfderiv_Ioo]
  refine setLIntegral_congr_fun measurableSet_Ioo (fun t ht => ?_)
  have hγt : MDifferentiableAt 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) I γ t :=
    (hγ.mdifferentiableOn one_ne_zero t (Ioo_subset_Icc_self ht)).mdifferentiableAt
      (Icc_mem_nhds ht.1 ht.2)
  rw [mfderiv_comp_apply t (hφ.contMDiffAt.mdifferentiableAt (by simp)) hγt]
  exact h.enorm_mfderiv (γ t) _

end Length

/-! ### Invariance of the Riemannian distance -/

section Distance

variable {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {g' : RiemannianMetric I' M'} {φ : M → M'}

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

blocks.tex:
Since $\varphi$ preserves the metric, each differential $d\varphi_p$ is a linear isometry, so for every $p \in M$ and $v \in T_p M$,
\[
  |d\varphi_p(v)|_{\tilde{g}}^2 = \tilde{g}_{\varphi(p)}(d\varphi_p v, d\varphi_p v) = g_p(v,v) = |v|_g^2 .
\]
At every $t$ in the open interval $(a,b)$ the curve $\gamma$ is differentiable, so the chain rule gives $(\varphi \circ \gamma)'(t) = d\varphi_{\gamma(t)}(\gamma'(t))$, and therefore
\[
  |(\varphi \circ \gamma)'(t)|_{\tilde{g}} = |d\varphi_{\gamma(t)}(\gamma'(t))|_{\tilde{g}} = |\gamma'(t)|_g .
\]
The two integrands agree on $(a,b)$, which differs from $[a,b]$ by a set of measure zero, so the integrals agree.

Lee's Proposition 2.47 collects three properties of the length functional: additivity, parameter independence, and isometry invariance. They are recorded above as Propositions \ref{prop:length-additive}, \ref{prop:length-reparam-invariant} and \ref{prop:length-isometry-invariant} respectively, each stated separately because each is proved by a different argument: the first two are properties of the integral defining the length and hold on any manifold whose tangent spaces are normed, while only the third refers to a metric at all.

\begin{exercise}[Prove Proposition 2.47]

\leanok
\dcref{ch2:2.48}
\uses{prop:length-additive,prop:length-reparam-invariant,prop:length-isometry-invariant}
\end{exercise}

Suppose $\gamma : [a, b] \to M$ is an admissible curve. The \textbf{arc-length function} of $\gamma$ is the function $s : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[s(t) = L_g(\gamma|_{[a,t]}) = \int_a^t |\gamma'(u)|_g \, du.\]

It is continuous everywhere, and it follows from the fundamental theorem of calculus that it is smooth wherever $\gamma$ is, with derivative $s'(t) = |\gamma'(t)|$.

For this reason, if $\gamma: I \to M$ is any smooth curve (not necessarily a curve segment), we define the \textbf{speed} of $\gamma$ at any time $t \in I$ to be the scalar $|\gamma'(t)|$. We say that $\gamma$ is a \textbf{unit-speed curve} if $|\gamma'(t)| = 1$ for all $t$, and a \textbf{constant-speed curve} if $|\gamma'(t)|$ is constant. If $\gamma$ is a piecewise smooth curve, we say that $\gamma$ has \textbf{unit speed} if $|\gamma'(t)| = 1$ wherever $\gamma$ is smooth.

If $\gamma : [a, b] \to M$ is a unit-speed admissible curve, then its arc-length function has the simple form $s(t) = t - a$. If, in addition, its parameter interval is of the form $[0, b]$ for some $b > 0$, then the arc-length function is $s(t) = t$. For this reason, a unit-speed admissible curve whose parameter interval is of the form $[0, b]$ is said to be \textbf{parametrized by arc length}.

blocks.label: prop:reparametrization-by-arc-length
blocks.title: prop:reparametrization-by-arc-length

blocks.tex:
Suppose $\gamma : I \to M$ is a regular curve. Choose an arbitrary $t_0 \in I$, and define $s : I \to \mathbb{R}$ by
\[s(t) = \int_{t_0}^t |\gamma'(u)|_g \, du.\]

Since $s'(t) = |\gamma'(t)|_g > 0$, it follows that $s$ is a strictly increasing local diffeomorphism, and thus maps $I$ diffeomorphically onto an interval $I' \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. If we let $\varphi = s^{-1} : I' \to I$, then $\tilde{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \varphi$ is a forward reparametrization of $\gamma$, and the chain rule gives
\[|\tilde{\gamma}'(t)|_g = |\varphi'(t)\gamma'(\varphi(t))|_g = \frac{1}{s'(\varphi(t))}|\gamma'(\varphi(t))|_g = 1.\]

Thus $\tilde{\gamma}$ is a unit-speed reparametrization of $\gamma$.

Now let $\gamma : [a, b] \to M$ be an admissible curve. We prove the existence statement in part (b) by induction on the number of smooth segments in an admissible partition. If $\gamma$ has only one smooth segment, then it is a regular curve segment, and (b) follows by applying (a) in the special case $I = [a, b]$ and choosing $t_0 = a$. Assuming that the result is true for admissible curves with $k$ smooth segments, suppose $\gamma$
has an admissible partition $(a_0, \ldots, a_{k+1})$. The inductive hypothesis gives piecewise regular homeomorphisms $\varphi : [0,c] \to [a, a_k]$ and $\psi : [0,d] \to [a_k, b]$ such that $\gamma \circ \varphi$ is an arc-length reparametrization of $\gamma|_{[a,a_k]}$ and $\gamma \circ \psi$ is an arc-length reparametrization of $\gamma|_{[a_k,b]}$. If we define $\bar{\varphi}: [0, c+d] \to [a,b]$ by
\[
\bar{\varphi}(s) = \begin{cases}
\varphi(s), & s \in [0,c], \\
\psi(s-c), & s \in [c, c+d],
\end{cases}
\]
then $\gamma \circ \bar{\varphi}$ is a reparametrization of $\gamma$ by arc length.

To prove uniqueness, suppose that $\tilde{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \varphi$ and $\hat{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \psi$ are both forward reparametrizations of $\gamma$ by arc length. Since both have the same length, it follows that $\varphi$ and $\psi$ both have the same parameter domain $[0,c]$, and thus both are piecewise regular homeomorphisms from $[0,c]$ to $[a,b]$. If we define $\eta = \varphi^{-1} \circ \psi: [0,c] \to [0,c]$, then $\eta$ is a piecewise regular increasing homeomorphism satisfying $\tilde{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \varphi \circ \eta = \tilde{\gamma} \circ \eta$. The fact that both $\tilde{\gamma}$ and $\hat{\gamma}$ are of unit speed implies the following equality for all $s \in [0,c]$ except the finitely many values at which $\tilde{\gamma}$ or $\eta$ is not smooth:
\[
1 = |\dot{\tilde{\gamma}}(s)|_g = |\dot{\tilde{\gamma}}(\eta(s))\eta'(s)|_g = |\dot{\tilde{\gamma}}(\eta(s))|_g \eta'(s) = \eta'(s).
\]

Since $\eta$ is continuous and $\eta(0) = 0$, it follows that $\eta(s) = s$ for all $s \in [0,c]$, and thus $\tilde{\gamma} = \hat{\gamma}$.

We are now in a position to introduce one of the most important concepts from classical geometry into the Riemannian setting: distances between points.

Suppose $(M, g)$ is a connected Riemannian manifold with or without boundary. For each pair of points $p, q \in M$, we define the \textbf{Riemannian distance from $p$ to $q$}, denoted by $d_g(p,q)$, to be the infimum of the lengths of all admissible curves from $p$ to $q$. The following proposition guarantees that $d_g(p,q)$ is a well-defined nonnegative real number for each $p, q \in M$.

blocks.label: prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve
blocks.title: prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve
blocks.body: If $M$ is a connected smooth manifold (with or without boundary), then any two points of $M$ can be joined by an admissible curve.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_contMDiffOn_path_of_preconnected
lean.code:
theorem exists_contMDiffOn_path_of_preconnected [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [T2Space M]
    [SigmaCompactSpace M] [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    ∃ γ : ℝ → M, γ 0 = x ∧ γ 1 = y ∧ ContMDiffOn 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) I 1 γ (Set.Icc 0 1) := by
  obtain ⟨g⟩ := exists_riemannianMetric (I := I) (M := M)
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  haveI := g.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  exact exists_contMDiffOn_path I x y

end LeeForm

section Euclidean

/-!
### The Riemannian distance of the Euclidean metric

Lee's touchstone: on `ℝⁿ` with the Euclidean metric `ḡ` of Example 2.6, the
Riemannian distance `d_ḡ` is the ordinary Euclidean distance — straight lines are
the shortest curves.  This is the sanity check that the general Theorem 2.55
computes the expected answer in the one case where the answer is known in advance.
-/

variable (F : Type*) [NormedAddCommGroup F] [InnerProductSpace ℝ F]

deps.title: Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric
deps.label: prop:existence-riemannian-metric

blocks.tex:
Lee argues directly: since a connected manifold is path-connected, $p$ and $q$ are joined by a continuous path $c: [a,b] \to M$; by compactness there is a partition of $[a,b]$ such that each $c([a_{i-1}, a_i])$ lies in a single smooth coordinate ball (or half-ball if $\partial M \neq \emptyset$), and replacing each segment by a straight-line path in coordinates yields an admissible curve between the same points (Fig.~2.2).

The following route reaches the same conclusion from results already established. The statement mentions no metric, but nothing prevents introducing one: by Proposition \ref{prop:existence-riemannian-metric} choose a Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$. By Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-distance-finite}, $d_g(p,q) < \infty$, so $d_g(p,q) < d_g(p,q) + 1$. Since $d_g(p,q)$ is by definition the infimum of the lengths of admissible curves from $p$ to $q$, a bound strictly exceeding the infimum is attained by some competitor: there is an admissible curve $\gamma$ from $p$ to $q$ with $L_g(\gamma) < d_g(p,q) + 1$. In particular such a $\gamma$ exists, which is the assertion; the metric $g$ and the length bound play no part in it and are discarded.

For convenience, if $(M, g)$ is a disconnected Riemannian manifold, we also let $d_g(p,q)$ denote the Riemannian distance from $p$ to $q$, provided that $p$ and $q$ lie in the same connected component of $M$. (See also Problem 2-30.)

It is convenient to have a form of the definition that makes sense before one knows
that $p$ and $q$ can be joined at all, and that ranges over a class of curves closed
under the operations used below.  We therefore take the following as the working
definition, with values in $[0,\infty]$.

blocks.label: def:riemannian-distance
blocks.title: The Riemannian Distance, extended form
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. For $p, q \in M$ set
\[
  d_g(p,q) \;=\; \inf \bigl\{\, L_g(\gamma) \;:\; \gamma : [0,1] \to M \text{ is } C^1,\;
    \gamma(0) = p,\; \gamma(1) = q \,\bigr\} \;\in\; [0, \infty],
\]
the infimum of the empty set being $\infty$.

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

blocks.label: rem:riemannian-distance-c1-vs-admissible
blocks.title: rem:riemannian-distance-c1-vs-admissible
blocks.body:
Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance} takes the infimum over $C^1$ curves, whereas
Lee takes it over admissible (piecewise regular) curves.  Neither class contains the
other: a $C^1$ curve need not be regular, and an admissible curve need not be $C^1$ at
its corners.  The two infima nevertheless agree, because an admissible curve may be
reparametrized so as to become $C^1$ with vanishing velocity at the corners without
changing its length, and conversely a $C^1$ curve may be approximated in length by
regular ones.  That equality is not needed for anything below and is not proved here;
every statement in this section refers to Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}.

blocks.label: prop:connected-manifold-c1-curve
blocks.title: Connected Riemannian Manifolds Are $C^1$-Path Connected
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold. Then any two points of $M$ can be joined
by a $C^1$ curve $\gamma : [0,1] \to M$.

This is Proposition \ref{prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve} in the $C^1$ formulation
of Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}. Lee assumes only that $M$ be a connected smooth
manifold, with no metric; the statement here carries a metric because the proof measures with
it. That costs no generality, since every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric by
Proposition \ref{prop:existence-riemannian-metric}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_contMDiffOn_path
lean.code:
theorem exists_contMDiffOn_path [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    ∃ γ : ℝ → M, γ 0 = x ∧ γ 1 = y ∧ CMDiff[Icc 0 1] 1 γ := by
  have h : riemannianEDist I x y < riemannianEDist I x y + 1 :=
    ENNReal.lt_add_right (riemannianEDist_ne_top I x y) one_ne_zero
  obtain ⟨γ, h0, h1, hγ, -⟩ := exists_lt_of_riemannianEDist_lt h
  exact ⟨γ, h0, h1, hγ⟩

end Finiteness

section MetricSpace

variable (I M) in

blocks.tex:
Let $p, q \in M$. By Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-distance-finite}, $d_g(p,q) < \infty$,
so $d_g(p,q) < d_g(p,q) + 1 < \infty$. By Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance},
$d_g(p,q)$ is the infimum of $L_g(\gamma)$ over $C^1$ curves $\gamma$ from $p$ to $q$; since
$d_g(p,q) + 1$ is strictly greater than that infimum, it is not a lower bound for the set of
such lengths, so the set is nonempty and contains an element $L_g(\gamma) < d_g(p,q)+1$. The
corresponding $\gamma$ is a $C^1$ curve from $p$ to $q$.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-distance-symm
blocks.title: Symmetry of the Riemannian Distance
blocks.body: $d_g(p,q) = d_g(q,p)$ for all $p, q$ in a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-distance-triangle
blocks.title: Triangle Inequality for the Riemannian Distance
blocks.body: $d_g(p,r) \le d_g(p,q) + d_g(q,r)$ for all $p, q, r$ in a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small
blocks.title: Nearby Points Are at Small Distance
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold, let $p \in M$ and let $c > 0$. Then $p$ has a
neighborhood $U$ such that $d_g(p,q) < c$ for every $q \in U$.

This is the content of Lemma \ref{lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood}(a): a point
close to $p$ in a coordinate neighborhood is joined to $p$ by the coordinate segment,
whose $g$-length is controlled by its Euclidean length.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-distance-small-implies-nearby
blocks.title: Points at Small Distance Are Nearby
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and let $U$ be a neighborhood of $p \in M$. Then
there is $c > 0$ such that $\{q \in M : d_g(p,q) < c\} \subseteq U$.

This is the content of Lemma \ref{lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood}(b): a curve
from $p$ that leaves $U$ must cross a coordinate annulus around $p$, and so has
$g$-length bounded below.

blocks.label: prop:riemannian-distance-finite
blocks.title: Finiteness of the Riemannian Distance
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold. Then $d_g(p,q) < \infty$ for all
$p, q \in M$.

This is the conclusion that Proposition \ref{prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve}
exists to supply: it is exactly the assertion that some curve of finite length joins
$p$ to $q$, so that the infimum defining $d_g(p,q)$ is taken over a nonempty set of
finite numbers.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.riemannianEDist_ne_top
lean.code:
theorem riemannianEDist_ne_top [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    riemannianEDist I x y ≠ ⊤ := by
  have h : {z : M | riemannianEDist I x z ≠ ⊤} = univ :=
    IsClopen.eq_univ ⟨isClosed_setOf_riemannianEDist_ne_top I x,
      isOpen_setOf_riemannianEDist_ne_top I x⟩
      ⟨x, by simp only [mem_setOf_eq, riemannianEDist_self]; exact ENNReal.zero_ne_top⟩
  exact (h ▸ mem_univ y : y ∈ {z : M | riemannianEDist I x z ≠ ⊤})

variable (I) in

deps.title: Triangle Inequality for the Riemannian Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-triangle

deps.title: Nearby Points Are at Small Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small

blocks.tex:
Fix $p \in M$ and put $S = \{q \in M : d_g(p,q) < \infty\}$. We show $S$ is open, closed
and nonempty.

\emph{$S$ is open.} Let $q_0 \in S$. By Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small}
applied at $q_0$ with $c = 1$, there is a neighborhood $U$ of $q_0$ with $d_g(q_0, q) < 1$
for every $q \in U$. For such $q$, Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-triangle} gives
\[
  d_g(p,q) \;\le\; d_g(p,q_0) + d_g(q_0,q) \;<\; \infty,
\]
both summands being finite. Hence $U \subseteq S$.

\emph{$S$ is closed.} We show $M \setminus S$ is open. Let $q_0 \in M \setminus S$, so
$d_g(p,q_0) = \infty$. Again there is a neighborhood $U$ of $q_0$ with $d_g(q_0,q) < 1$
for every $q \in U$. Suppose some $q \in U$ had $d_g(p,q) < \infty$. Then by
Lemmas \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-triangle} and \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-symm},
\[
  d_g(p,q_0) \;\le\; d_g(p,q) + d_g(q,q_0) \;=\; d_g(p,q) + d_g(q_0,q) \;<\; \infty,
\]
contradicting $d_g(p,q_0) = \infty$. Hence $U \subseteq M \setminus S$.

\emph{$S$ is nonempty.} The constant curve at $p$ has length $0$, so $d_g(p,p) = 0 < \infty$
and $p \in S$.

Since $M$ is connected and $S$ is a nonempty subset that is both open and closed, $S = M$.
As $p \in M$ was arbitrary, $d_g(p,q) < \infty$ for all $p, q \in M$.

blocks.label: lem:metric-preserving-distance-le
blocks.title: Metric-Preserving Maps Do Not Increase Distance
blocks.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ and $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ are Riemannian manifolds with or without boundary and $\varphi : M \to \tilde{M}$ is a smooth metric-preserving map. Then
\[
  d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x), \varphi(y)) \;\le\; d_g(x,y) \qquad \text{for all } x, y \in M.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsMetricPreserving.riemannianEDist_le
lean.code:
theorem IsMetricPreserving.riemannianEDist_le (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' φ)
    (hφ : ContMDiff I I' ∞ φ) (x y : M) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist I' (φ x) (φ y) ≤ riemannianEDist I x y := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  haveI := g.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  haveI := g'.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  refine le_of_forall_gt_imp_ge_of_dense (fun r hr => ?_)
  obtain ⟨γ, hγa, hγb, hγsmooth, hγlen⟩ := exists_lt_of_riemannianEDist_lt hr
  have hcomp : pathELength I' (φ ∘ γ) 0 1 = pathELength I γ 0 1 := h.pathELength_comp hφ hγsmooth
  have hle : riemannianEDist I' (φ x) (φ y) ≤ pathELength I' (φ ∘ γ) 0 1 :=
    riemannianEDist_le_pathELength
      ((hφ.of_le (by simp)).comp_contMDiffOn hγsmooth) (by simp [hγa]) (by simp [hγb]) zero_le_one
  exact ((hle.trans hcomp.le).trans_lt hγlen).le

blocks.tex: Let $r > d_g(x,y)$. By definition of $d_g$ as an infimum there is an admissible curve $\gamma : [0,1] \to M$ from $x$ to $y$ with $L_g(\gamma) < r$. Then $\varphi \circ \gamma$ is an admissible curve from $\varphi(x)$ to $\varphi(y)$, and by Proposition \ref{prop:length-isometry-invariant} it has $L_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi \circ \gamma) = L_g(\gamma) < r$. Since $d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x),\varphi(y))$ is an infimum over such curves, $d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x),\varphi(y)) < r$. As $r > d_g(x,y)$ was arbitrary and the order is dense, $d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x),\varphi(y)) \le d_g(x,y)$.

The inequality cannot be improved to an equality for metric-preserving maps in general: $\varphi$ carries curves of $M$ to curves of the same length in $\tilde{M}$, but $\tilde{M}$ may contain shorter curves between $\varphi(x)$ and $\varphi(y)$ that do not lie in the image of $\varphi$. Equality requires $\varphi$ to be invertible.

blocks.label: prop:isometry-invariance-riemannian-distance
blocks.title: Isometry Invariance of the Riemannian Distance Function
blocks.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ are connected Riemannian manifolds with or without boundary, and $\varphi: M \to \tilde{M}$ is an isometry. Then $d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x), \varphi(y)) = d_g(x, y)$ for all $x, y \in M$.
