lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsIsometry.riemannianEDist_comp
lean.code:
theorem IsIsometry.riemannianEDist_comp {Φ : Diffeomorph I I' M M' ∞}
    (h : IsIsometry g g' Φ) (x y : M) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist I' (Φ x) (Φ y) = riemannianEDist I x y := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  refine le_antisymm (h.riemannianEDist_le Φ.contMDiff x y) ?_
  have hrev := h.symm.riemannianEDist_le Φ.symm.contMDiff (Φ x) (Φ y)
  rwa [Φ.symm_apply_apply, Φ.symm_apply_apply] at hrev

end Distance

/-! ### Isometries of Euclidean space

A witness that the theory above is not vacuous, and Lee's first example of an
isometry: a linear isometry of inner product spaces is an isometry of the
corresponding Euclidean manifolds (Example 2.6).  Applied to `F = F' = ℝⁿ` these
are the orthogonal maps, the linear part of the rigid motions of Chapter 1.

This matters beyond the example.  `IsIsometry` is a hypothesis of
`IsIsometry.riemannianEDist_comp`, and a hypothesis satisfied only by the
identity would make that theorem say nothing; `isIsometry_euclideanMetric`
exhibits a large supply of genuinely non-identity witnesses.
-/

section EuclideanIsometry

variable {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [InnerProductSpace ℝ F]
  {F' : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F'] [InnerProductSpace ℝ F']

deps.title: Metric-Preserving Maps Do Not Increase Distance
deps.label: lem:metric-preserving-distance-le

blocks.tex:
Applying Lemma \ref{lem:metric-preserving-distance-le} to $\varphi$ gives $d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x),\varphi(y)) \le d_g(x,y)$. By Lemma \ref{lem:isometry-symm}, $\varphi^{-1}$ is an isometry, so the same lemma applied to $\varphi^{-1}$ at the points $\varphi(x), \varphi(y)$ gives
\[
  d_g(x,y) = d_g\bigl(\varphi^{-1}(\varphi(x)), \varphi^{-1}(\varphi(y))\bigr) \le d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x), \varphi(y)).
\]
The two inequalities give equality.

blocks.label: prop:euclidean-linear-isometry-distance
blocks.title: Linear Isometries of Euclidean Space
blocks.body:
Let $F$ and $\tilde{F}$ be inner product spaces with their Euclidean metrics, and let $e : F \to \tilde{F}$ be a linear isometry of $F$ onto $\tilde{F}$. Then $e$ is an isometry of the corresponding Riemannian manifolds, and
\[
  d_{\bar{g}}(e(x), e(y)) = |x - y| \qquad \text{for all } x, y \in F.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isIsometry_euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist
lean.code:
theorem isIsometry_euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist (e : F ≃ₗᵢ[ℝ] F') (x y : F) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) : F → Type _) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F') : F' → Type _) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F').toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F') (e x) (e y) = edist x y := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) : F → Type _) :=
    ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F') : F' → Type _) :=
    ⟨(euclideanMetric F').toRiemannianMetric⟩
  calc riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F') (e x) (e y)
      = riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F) x y :=
        (isIsometry_euclideanMetric e).riemannianEDist_comp x y
    _ = edist x y := euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist F x y

deps.title: Isometry Invariance of the Riemannian Distance Function
deps.label: prop:isometry-invariance-riemannian-distance

blocks.tex: A linear map is its own differential, so $de_x = e$ for every $x$, and the condition $e^* \bar{g} = \bar{g}$ reads $\langle e(v), e(w)\rangle = \langle v, w \rangle$, which holds because $e$ is a linear isometry. Being in addition a linear homeomorphism, $e$ is a diffeomorphism, so it is an isometry. Proposition \ref{prop:isometry-invariance-riemannian-distance} then gives $d_{\bar{g}}(e(x), e(y)) = d_{\bar{g}}(x,y)$, and $d_{\bar{g}}(x,y) = |x-y|$ by Proposition \ref{prop:euclidean-riemannian-distance}.

(Note that unlike lengths of curves, Riemannian distances are not necessarily preserved by \textit{local} isometries; see Problem 2-31.)
\uses{prob:local-isometry-distance}

We wish to show that the Riemannian distance function turns $M$ into a metric space, whose metric topology is the same as its original manifold topology. To do so, we need the following lemmas.

The comparison below rests on two ingredients, isolated here. The first is the joint
smoothness of the metric in the base point and the tangent vector together, which is what
makes the function $(x,v) \mapsto |v|_g$ on the tangent bundle continuous.

blocks.label: lem:metric-energy-smooth
blocks.title: Smoothness of the Energy Function
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. The energy function $E : TM \to \mathbb{R}$, defined on
the total space of the tangent bundle by
\[
E(x,v) = \langle v, v \rangle_g,
\]
is smooth. Consequently $(x,v) \mapsto |v|_g = E(x,v)^{1/2}$ is continuous on $TM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.contMDiff_innerAt_totalSpace
lean.code:
theorem contMDiff_innerAt_totalSpace (g : RiemannianMetric I M) :
    ContMDiff I.tangent 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
      (fun z : TangentBundle I M => g.innerAt z.proj z.2 z.2) := by
  letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  have hid : ContMDiff I.tangent (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E)) ∞
      (fun z : TangentBundle I M => (z : TotalSpace E (TangentSpace I : M → Type _))) :=
    contMDiff_id
  -- Elaborate the pairing with no expected type: fixing the goal first forces typeclass
  -- synthesis to pick the fibre norm coming from `letI` before it can be unified with `g`'s.
  have hpair := ContMDiff.inner_bundle (IM := I.tangent) (IB := I) (F := E)
    (E := (TangentSpace I : M → Type _)) (n := ∞) (M := TangentBundle I M)
    (b := TotalSpace.proj) (v := fun z : TangentBundle I M => z.2)
    (w := fun z : TangentBundle I M => z.2) hid hid
  exact hpair

end AnyManifold

variable {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℝ F]

blocks.tex:
Smoothness of $g$ says that for any smooth map $b : N \to M$ and any two smooth sections
$X, Y$ of the pullback $b^*TM$, the function $p \mapsto \langle X_p, Y_p \rangle_{g_{b(p)}}$ is
smooth on $N$. Apply this with $N = TM$, with $b : TM \to M$ the bundle projection, and with
$X = Y$ the tautological section of $b^*TM$ assigning to a point $(x,v) \in TM$ the vector
$v \in T_x M$ itself. The resulting function is $E$. The final claim holds because
$t \mapsto t^{1/2}$ is continuous on $[0,\infty)$ and $E \geq 0$.

The second ingredient is the analytic heart of the matter, stated with no reference to
manifolds: a family of inner products that varies continuously over a compact set is
comparable to a fixed norm, \emph{uniformly} over that set. The hypotheses are imposed only
on $K$, so the lemma applies to a family defined on any set containing $K$ --- which is what
Lemma \ref{lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood} needs, since there the family is a metric
read in a chart and is undefined outside the chart's image.

blocks.label: lem:norm-comparison-compact-core
blocks.title: Uniform Comparison over a Compact Set
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional real normed vector space, let $q$ assign to each $x \in V$ a
bilinear form $q_x$ on $V$, and let $K \subseteq V$ be compact. Write $S = \{v \in V : |v| = 1\}$
and suppose
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $(x,v) \mapsto q_x(v,v)$ is continuous on $K \times S$;
\item[(ii)] $q_x(v,v) > 0$ for every $x \in K$ and every $v \neq 0$.
\end{enumerate}
Then there are positive constants $c, C$ such that
\[
c|v| \leq q_x(v,v)^{1/2} \leq C|v| \qquad \text{for all } x \in K \text{ and all } v \in V.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_forall_sqrt_comparison
lean.code:
theorem exists_forall_sqrt_comparison {B : Type*} [TopologicalSpace B]
    {q : B → F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ} {K : Set B} (hK : IsCompact K)
    (hcont : ContinuousOn (fun p : B × F => q p.1 p.2 p.2) (K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1))
    (hpos : ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : F, v ≠ 0 → 0 < q x v v) :
    ∃ c C : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ 0 < C ∧ ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : F,
      c * ‖v‖ ≤ Real.sqrt (q x v v) ∧ Real.sqrt (q x v v) ≤ C * ‖v‖ := by
  have hS : IsCompact (K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1) := hK.prod (isCompact_sphere 0 1)
  -- On the sphere, vectors are nonzero.
  have hne : ∀ p : B × F, p ∈ K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1 → p.2 ≠ 0 := by
    rintro ⟨x, v⟩ ⟨-, hv⟩
    simp only [mem_sphere_iff_norm, sub_zero] at hv
    exact norm_ne_zero_iff.mp (by rw [hv]; exact one_ne_zero)
  -- Lower bound: `q` is strictly positive on the compact `K × S`, hence bounded below by
  -- some `c₀ > 0`.  `exists_forall_le'` also covers the degenerate case `K × S = ∅`.
  obtain ⟨c₀, hc₀pos, hc₀⟩ :=
    hS.exists_forall_le' (a := 0) hcont fun p hp => hpos p.1 hp.1 p.2 (hne p hp)
  -- Upper bound: a continuous function on a compact set is bounded.
  obtain ⟨C₀, hC₀⟩ := hS.exists_bound_of_continuousOn hcont
  refine ⟨Real.sqrt c₀, Real.sqrt (max C₀ 1), Real.sqrt_pos.mpr hc₀pos,
    Real.sqrt_pos.mpr (lt_of_lt_of_le zero_lt_one (le_max_right _ _)), fun x hx v => ?_⟩
  rcases eq_or_ne v 0 with rfl | hv
  · simp
  -- Rescale `v` to the unit sphere and use homogeneity.
  set u : F := ‖v‖⁻¹ • v with hu
  have hvne : ‖v‖ ≠ 0 := norm_ne_zero_iff.mpr hv
  have huS : u ∈ sphere (0 : F) 1 := by
    simp only [mem_sphere_iff_norm, sub_zero, hu, norm_smul, norm_inv, norm_norm]
    field_simp
  have hvu : v = ‖v‖ • u := by
    rw [hu, smul_smul, mul_inv_cancel₀ hvne, one_smul]
  have hqv : q x v v = ‖v‖ ^ 2 * q x u u := by
    conv_lhs => rw [hvu]
    exact apply_smul_smul_self (q x) _ _
  have hmem : ((x, u) : B × F) ∈ K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1 := ⟨hx, huS⟩
  have hsplit : Real.sqrt (q x v v) = ‖v‖ * Real.sqrt (q x u u) := by
    rw [hqv, Real.sqrt_mul (sq_nonneg _), Real.sqrt_sq (norm_nonneg _)]
  constructor
  · rw [hsplit, mul_comm (Real.sqrt c₀) ‖v‖]
    exact mul_le_mul_of_nonneg_left (Real.sqrt_le_sqrt (hc₀ _ hmem)) (norm_nonneg _)
  · rw [hsplit, mul_comm (Real.sqrt (max C₀ 1)) ‖v‖]
    refine mul_le_mul_of_nonneg_left (Real.sqrt_le_sqrt ?_) (norm_nonneg _)
    exact le_trans (le_trans (le_abs_self _) (hC₀ _ hmem)) (le_max_left _ _)

end Core

section Bridge

namespace RiemannianMetric

section AnyManifold

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]

blocks.tex:
The set $K \times S$ is compact, being a product of compact sets ($S$ is compact because $V$ is
finite-dimensional). Every $v \in S$ is nonzero, so by (ii) the function $(x,v) \mapsto q_x(v,v)$
is strictly positive on $K \times S$; being continuous there by (i), it is bounded below by some
$c_0 > 0$ and above by some $C_0$. Set $c = c_0^{1/2}$ and $C = \max(C_0,1)^{1/2}$, both positive.

Let $x \in K$ and $v \in V$. If $v = 0$ both sides vanish. Otherwise put $u = v/|v| \in S$, so
that $v = |v|\,u$. Bilinearity gives the homogeneity relation $q_x(\lambda u, \lambda u) =
\lambda^2 q_x(u,u)$, whence $q_x(v,v) = |v|^2 q_x(u,u)$ and therefore
\[
q_x(v,v)^{1/2} = |v| \, q_x(u,u)^{1/2}.
\]
Since $c_0 \leq q_x(u,u) \leq C_0 \leq \max(C_0,1)$, taking square roots and multiplying by
$|v| \geq 0$ gives $c|v| \leq q_x(v,v)^{1/2} \leq C|v|$.

If $K$ or $S$ is empty the two bounds are produced vacuously, so no nondegeneracy assumption is
needed beyond (ii).

Lee's lemma follows for a metric defined on all of $\mathbb{R}^n$; this is the case in which
the tangent bundle is canonically a product, so that the compact set $L$ appearing in the
argument literally is $K \times S^{n-1}$.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-euclidean-norm-comparison-flat
blocks.title: Norm Comparison on $\mathbb{R}^n$
blocks.body:
Let $g$ be a Riemannian metric on $\mathbb{R}^n$ and let $\tilde{g}$ denote the Euclidean
metric. For every compact subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$, there are positive constants
$c, C$ such that for all $x \in K$ and all $v \in T_x \mathbb{R}^n$,
\[
c|v|_{\tilde{g}} \leq |v|_g \leq C|v|_{\tilde{g}}.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean
lean.code:
theorem RiemannianMetric.exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean
    (g : RiemannianMetric 𝓘(ℝ, F) F) {K : Set F} (hK : IsCompact K) :
    ∃ c C : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ 0 < C ∧ ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) x,
      c * (euclideanMetric F).normAt x v ≤ g.normAt x v ∧
        g.normAt x v ≤ C * (euclideanMetric F).normAt x v := by
  obtain ⟨c, C, hc, hC, h⟩ := g.exists_forall_norm_comparison_of_isCompact hK
  refine ⟨c, C, hc, hC, fun x hx v => ?_⟩
  rw [euclideanMetric_normAt]
  exact h x hx v

end LeeStatement

section OpenSubsets

/-!
### Lee's open subset `W ⊆ ℝⁿ`

Lee states Lemma 2.53 for a metric on an open `W ⊆ ℝⁿ` (or `ℝⁿ₊`) rather than on
all of `ℝⁿ`, because he applies it to a metric read in a chart.  Part of that
generality is already available: `exists_forall_sqrt_comparison` imposes its
hypotheses only on `K`, so it accepts a form family defined on any domain
containing `K`, and that is the shape in which Lemma 2.54 uses it.

This section supplies the rest: the statement for a `RiemannianMetric 𝓘(ℝ, F) ↥W`
with `W : Opens F` — a metric on an open subset regarded as a manifold in its own
right, which is literally Lee's hypothesis.  The obstruction is one of transport.
`contMDiff_innerAt_totalSpace` already applies to `↥W`, so the metric is jointly
smooth on `T(↥W)`; what is missing is the identification of `T(↥W)` with the
product `↥W × F`, which turns "jointly smooth on the total space" into the
two-variable continuity the comparison consumes.  For the model space that
identification is `tangentBundleModelSpaceHomeomorph`; for an open subset mathlib
provides no analogue.

**Why it is cheap.**  Every chart of `↥W` is *the same* chart — the inclusion.
`Opens.chartAt_eq` gives `chartAt F x = (chartAt F ↑x).subtypeRestr ⟨x⟩`, whose
only dependence on `x` is through the `Nonempty ↥W` instance `⟨x⟩`; `Nonempty` is
a `Prop`, so definitional proof irrelevance makes `chartAt F x` and `achart F x`
independent of `x` *by `rfl`*.  Hence the tangent bundle's coordinate changes are
all `coordChange i i`, i.e. the identity (`tangentCoordChange_self`), and the
canonical trivialization at any point is the identity map on the nose
(`opens_trivializationAt_apply`).  It has `baseSet = univ`, so it is a single
global chart and no gluing is required. -/

variable {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F]

open TopologicalSpace

deps.title: Uniform Comparison over a Compact Set
deps.label: lem:norm-comparison-compact-core

blocks.tex:
Under the canonical identification of $T\mathbb{R}^n$ with $\mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^n$,
Lemma \ref{lem:metric-energy-smooth} says exactly that $(x,v) \mapsto \langle v,v\rangle_g$ is
continuous on $\mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^n$, so hypothesis (i) of Lemma
\ref{lem:norm-comparison-compact-core} holds for $q_x = g_x$ on $K \times S^{n-1}$; hypothesis
(ii) is the positive definiteness of $g$. That lemma yields $c, C > 0$ with
$c|v| \leq |v|_g \leq C|v|$ for $x \in K$, and $|v|_{\tilde{g}} = |v|$ because the Euclidean
metric assigns to a tangent vector its norm.

Lee states the comparison for a metric defined only on an open subset, which is the form in
which it is applied to a metric read in a chart.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-euclidean-norm-comparison
blocks.title: lem:riemannian-euclidean-norm-comparison

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean_opens
lean.code:
theorem exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean_opens (W : Opens F)
    (g : RiemannianMetric 𝓘(ℝ, F) (W : Type _)) {K : Set W} (hK : IsCompact K) :
    ∃ c C : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ 0 < C ∧ ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) x,
      c * (openSubmanifoldMetric (euclideanMetric F) W).normAt x v ≤ g.normAt x v ∧
        g.normAt x v ≤ C * (openSubmanifoldMetric (euclideanMetric F) W).normAt x v := by
  obtain ⟨c, C, hc, hC, h⟩ := g.exists_forall_norm_comparison_of_isCompact_opens W hK
  refine ⟨c, C, hc, hC, fun x hx v => ?_⟩
  rw [openSubmanifoldMetric_euclidean_normAt]
  exact h x hx v

deps.title: Uniform Comparison over a Compact Set
deps.label: lem:norm-comparison-compact-core

blocks.tex:
Define a continuous function $F: TW \to \mathbb{R}$ by $F(x, v) = |v|_g = g_x(v, v)^{1/2}$ for $x \in W$ and $v \in T_x \mathbb{R}^n$; it is continuous by Lemma \ref{lem:metric-energy-smooth}. Let $K \subseteq W$ be any compact subset, and define $L \subseteq TW$ by
\[
L = \{(x, v) \in TW : x \in K, |v|_{\tilde{g}} = 1\}.
\]

Because $W$ is open in $\mathbb{R}^n$, every chart of $W$ is the inclusion $W \hookrightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$; the tangent bundle $TW$ therefore admits the single global trivialization $TW \cong W \times \mathbb{R}^n$, under which $L$ is the product set $K \times S^{n-1}$, which is compact. Since $F$ is positive on $L$, there are positive constants $c, C$ such that
\[
c \leq |v|_g \leq C \quad \text{for all } (x, v) \in L.
\]

For each $x \in K$ and $v \in T_x \mathbb{R}^n$ with $v \neq 0$, we can write $v = \lambda \tilde{v}$, where $\lambda = |v|_{\tilde{g}}$ and $\tilde{v} = v/\lambda$. Then $(x, \tilde{v}) \in L$, and it follows from the homogeneity of $|\cdot|_g$ that
\[
|v|_g = \lambda |\tilde{v}|_g \leq \lambda C = C|v|_{\tilde{g}}.
\]

The same inequality holds trivially when $v = 0$. Arguing similarly in the other direction, we conclude that (2.21) holds for all $x \in K$ and $v \in T_x \mathbb{R}^n$. The two displayed bounds on $L$, together with the homogeneity step, are the content of Lemma \ref{lem:norm-comparison-compact-core} applied to $q_x = g_x$ and the compact set $K$.
The next lemma shows how to transfer this result to Riemannian manifolds.

blocks.label: lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood
blocks.title: lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood
blocks.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary and
let $d_g$ be its Riemannian distance function. Suppose $U$ is an open subset of $M$ and
$p \in U$. Then $p$ has a coordinate neighborhood $V \subset U$ with the property that there
are positive constants $C, D$ satisfying the following inequalities:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] If $q \in V$, then $d_g(p,q) \leq Cd_{\bar{g}}(p,q)$, where $\bar{g}$ is the Euclidean metric in the
given coordinates on $\mathbb{R}^n$ or $\mathbb{R}^n_+$.
\item[(b)] If $q \in M \setminus V$, then $d_g(p,q) \geq D$.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{proof}

Let $W$ be any neighborhood of $p$ contained in $U$ on which there exist smooth
coordinates $(x^i)$. Using these coordinates, we can identify $W$ with an open subset
of $\mathbb{R}^n$ or $\mathbb{R}^n_+$. Let $K$ be a compact subset of $W$ containing a neighborhood $V$ of
$p$, chosen as follows: If $p$ is an interior point of $M$, let $K$ be the closed Euclidean
ball $\bar{B}_\epsilon(0)$, with $\epsilon > 0$ chosen small enough that $K \subseteq W$, and let $V =
B_\epsilon(0)$. If $p \in \partial M$, let $K$ be a closed half-ball $\bar{B}_\epsilon(0) \cap \mathbb{R}^n_+$ with $\epsilon$ chosen similarly, and let $V =
B_\epsilon(0) \cap \mathbb{R}^n_+$. Let $\bar{g} = \sum_i (dx^i)^2$ denote the Euclidean metric in these coordinates,
and let $c, C$ be constants satisfying (2.21). If $\gamma$ is any admissible curve whose image
lies entirely in $V$, it follows that

$$cL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma) \leq L_g(\gamma) \leq CL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma). \quad (2.22)$$

To prove (a), suppose $q \in V$. Letting $\gamma : [a,b] \to V$ be a parametrization of the
line segment from $p$ to $q$, we conclude from (2.22) that

$$d_g(p,q) \leq L_g(\gamma) \leq CL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma) = Cd_{\bar{g}}(p,q).$$

To prove (b), suppose $q \in M \setminus V$. If $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ is any admissible curve from
$p$ to $q$, let $t_0$ denote the infimum of times $t \in [a,b]$ such that $\gamma(t) \notin V$. It follows
that $\gamma([a, t_0]) \subseteq K$ and $d_{\bar{g}}(p, \gamma(t_0)) = \epsilon$ by continuity (Fig. 2.3), so (2.22) implies

$$L_g(\gamma) \geq L_g(\gamma|_{[a,t_0]}) \geq cL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma|_{[a,t_0]}) \geq cd_{\bar{g}}(p, \gamma(t_0)) = c\epsilon.$$

Taking the infimum over all such curves $\gamma$, we obtain (b) with $D = c\varepsilon$. \qed
\end{proof}

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.exists_coordinate_nbhd_riemannianEDist_comparison
lean.code:
theorem RiemannianMetric.exists_coordinate_nbhd_riemannianEDist_comparison
    [IsManifold I ∞ M] [RegularSpace M] (g : RiemannianMetric I M)
    {U : Set M} (hU : IsOpen U) {p : M} (hp : p ∈ U) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    ∃ V : Set M, IsOpen V ∧ p ∈ V ∧ V ⊆ U ∧ V ⊆ (extChartAt I p).source ∧
      ∃ C D : ℝ≥0, 0 < C ∧ 0 < D ∧
        (∀ q ∈ V, riemannianEDist I p q ≤ C * edist (extChartAt I p p) (extChartAt I p q)) ∧
        (∀ q ∉ V, (D : ℝ≥0∞) ≤ riemannianEDist I p q) := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  haveI := g.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  exact LeeLib.Ch02.exists_coordinate_nbhd_riemannianEDist_comparison I hU hp

deps.title: Points at Small Distance Are Nearby
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-small-implies-nearby

deps.title: Nearby Points Are at Small Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small

blocks.label: thm:riemannian-manifolds-metric-spaces
blocks.title: Riemannian Manifolds as Metric Spaces
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold with or without boundary. Then $M$ carries a
metric space structure.

That its distance function is $d_g$ is Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-metric-space-dist}, and that
its topology is the manifold topology is Theorem \ref{thm:riemannian-distance-topology}; the
three together are Lee's Theorem 2.55.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric
lean.code:
@[reducible] def MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric
    [T3Space M] [PreconnectedSpace M] : MetricSpace M :=
  letI : PseudoMetricSpace M := PseudoMetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric I M
  _root_.MetricSpace.ofT0PseudoMetricSpace M

deps.title: Points at Small Distance Are Nearby
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-small-implies-nearby

deps.title: Triangle Inequality for the Riemannian Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-triangle

deps.title: Nearby Points Are at Small Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small

blocks.tex:
It is immediate from the definition of $d_g$ that $d_g(p,q) \ge 0$ and $d_g(p,p) = 0$, and
$d_g(p,q) = d_g(q,p)$ is Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-symm}. The triangle inequality
is Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-triangle}; it holds because a curve from $p$ to $q$
can be concatenated with one from $q$ to $r$ to yield one from $p$ to $r$ whose length is
the sum of the two lengths.

For separation, suppose $p, q \in M$ are distinct. Since $M$ is Hausdorff, there is an open
set $U$ containing $p$ but not $q$. By Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-small-implies-nearby}
there is $c > 0$ with $\{r : d_g(p,r) < c\} \subseteq U$. As $q \notin U$, we get
$d_g(p,q) \ge c > 0$.

Finally, $d_g$ takes finite values by Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-distance-finite},
which is where connectedness of $M$ is used, so $d_g$ is a genuine real-valued metric rather
than merely an extended one. Together these say that $d_g$ turns $M$ into a metric space.

The theorem as stated asserts only that some metric exists. What makes it Lee's Theorem 2.55
is that the metric in question is $d_g$, which is the following lemma.

blocks.label: lem:riemannian-metric-space-dist
blocks.title: The Metric Is the Riemannian Distance
blocks.body:
The distance function of the metric space of Theorem \ref{thm:riemannian-manifolds-metric-spaces}
is the Riemannian distance: for all $p, q \in M$,
\[
  \operatorname{dist}(p,q) \;=\; d_g(p,q),
\]
where the right-hand side, a priori an element of $[0,\infty]$ by
Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}, is read as a real number using
Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-distance-finite}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_dist
lean.code:
theorem MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_dist [T3Space M] [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    letI : MetricSpace M := MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric I M
    dist x y = (riemannianEDist I x y).toReal := rfl

blocks.tex:
The metric space of Theorem \ref{thm:riemannian-manifolds-metric-spaces} is obtained from the
extended distance $d_g$ of Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance} by taking real values, which
is legitimate because $d_g$ is finite by Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-distance-finite}. Its
distance function is therefore $d_g$ itself, and the identity holds by construction.

The remaining assertion of Lee's Theorem 2.55 is that this metric induces the topology one
started with; it is separated out here because it is a statement about the metric
constructed above rather than part of its construction.

blocks.label: thm:riemannian-distance-topology
blocks.title: The Metric Topology Is the Manifold Topology
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold with or without boundary. The topology
induced by the metric $d_g$ of Theorem \ref{thm:riemannian-manifolds-metric-spaces} is the
given manifold topology.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_topology
lean.code:
theorem MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_topology [T3Space M] [PreconnectedSpace M] :
    (MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric I M).toUniformSpace.toTopologicalSpace =
      ‹TopologicalSpace M› := rfl

deps.title: Points at Small Distance Are Nearby
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-small-implies-nearby

deps.title: Nearby Points Are at Small Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small

blocks.tex:
Suppose first that $U \subseteq M$ is open in the manifold topology and let $p \in U$. By
Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-small-implies-nearby} there is $c > 0$ with
$\{q : d_g(p,q) < c\} \subseteq U$, i.e. the $d_g$-ball of radius $c$ about $p$ is contained
in $U$. Thus $U$ is open in the metric topology.

Conversely, suppose $U'$ is open in the metric topology and let $p \in U'$. Choose $\delta > 0$
with the $d_g$-ball of radius $\delta$ about $p$ contained in $U'$. By
Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small} applied at $p$ with $c = \delta$, there is a
neighborhood $V$ of $p$ in the manifold topology with $d_g(p,q) < \delta$ for every $q \in V$;
hence $V \subseteq U'$. So $U'$ is a neighborhood of each of its points in the manifold
topology, and is therefore open in it.

The two inclusions of neighborhood filters say exactly that the two topologies coincide.

blocks.label: prop:euclidean-riemannian-distance
blocks.title: The Euclidean Distance
blocks.body:
On $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean metric $\bar g$ of Example \ref{ex:euclidean-metric},
the Riemannian distance is the Euclidean distance:
\[
  d_{\bar g}(x,y) \;=\; |x - y| \qquad \text{for all } x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n .
\]
This is the one case in which the answer is known in advance, so it is the natural check on
Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}. The real-valued form $d_{\bar g}(x,y) = |x-y|$ is
Lemma \ref{lem:euclidean-riemannian-distance-real}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist
lean.code:
theorem euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist (x y : F) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (fun (z : F) ↦ TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) z) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F) x y = edist x y :=
  riemannianEDist_eq_edist_of_innerProductSpace F x y

blocks.tex:
For the upper bound, let $\gamma(t) = x + t(y-x)$ for $t \in [0,1]$. This curve is $C^1$, joins
$x$ to $y$, and has $\gamma'(t) = y - x$, so under the identification of each tangent space of
$\mathbb{R}^n$ with $\mathbb{R}^n$ its $\bar g$-length is
\[
  L_{\bar g}(\gamma) \;=\; \int_0^1 |\gamma'(t)| \, dt \;=\; \int_0^1 |y-x| \, dt \;=\; |y-x| .
\]
Hence $d_{\bar g}(x,y) \le |x-y|$.

For the lower bound, let $\gamma : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}^n$ be any $C^1$ curve with
$\gamma(0) = x$ and $\gamma(1) = y$. By the fundamental theorem of calculus and the triangle
inequality for the integral,
\[
  |y - x| \;=\; \Bigl| \int_0^1 \gamma'(t) \, dt \Bigr| \;\le\; \int_0^1 |\gamma'(t)| \, dt
    \;=\; L_{\bar g}(\gamma) .
\]
So $|x-y|$ is a lower bound for the set of lengths whose infimum is $d_{\bar g}(x,y)$, giving
$|x-y| \le d_{\bar g}(x,y)$.

The two inequalities give $d_{\bar g}(x,y) = |x-y|$.

blocks.label: lem:euclidean-riemannian-distance-real
blocks.title: The Euclidean Distance, real-valued form
blocks.body:
With the notation of Proposition \ref{prop:euclidean-riemannian-distance}, $d_{\bar g}(x,y)$ is
finite and equals $|x-y|$ as a real number.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist_toReal
lean.code:
theorem euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist_toReal (x y : F) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (fun (z : F) ↦ TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) z) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
    (riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F) x y).toReal = ‖x - y‖ :=
  riemannianEDist_toReal_of_innerProductSpace F x y

blocks.tex:
Immediate from Proposition \ref{prop:euclidean-riemannian-distance}, since $|x-y|$ is a real
number and hence $d_{\bar g}(x,y) \ne \infty$.

Thanks to the preceding theorem, it makes sense to apply all the concepts of the theory of metric spaces to a connected Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$. For example, we say that $M$ is \textit{(metrically) complete} if every Cauchy sequence in $M$ converges. A subset $A \subseteq M$ is \textit{bounded} if there is a constant $C$ such that $d_g(p,q) \leq C$ for all $p,q \in A$; if this is the case, the \textit{diameter of } $A$ is the smallest such constant:
\[
\operatorname{diam}(A) = \sup\{d_g(p,q) : p,q \in A\}.
\]

Since every compact metric space is bounded, every compact connected Riemannian manifold with or without boundary has finite diameter. (Note that the unit sphere with the Riemannian distance determined by the round metric has diameter $\pi$, not $2$, since the Riemannian distance between antipodal points is $\pi$. See Problem 6-2.)

From the point of view of geometry, Riemannian metrics are by far the most important structures that manifolds carry. However, there is a generalization of Riemannian metrics that has become especially important because of its application to physics.

Before defining this generalization, we begin with some linear-algebraic preliminaries. Suppose $V$ is a finite-dimensional vector space, and $q$ is a symmetric covariant 2-tensor on $V$ (also called a \textbf{symmetric bilinear form}). Just as for an inner product, there is a linear map $\tilde{q}: V \to V^*$ defined by
\[
\tilde{q}(v)(w) = q(v,w) \text{ for all } v, w \in V.
\]

We say that $q$ is \textbf{nondegenerate} if $\tilde{q}$ is an isomorphism.

blocks.label: lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear
blocks.title: lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.nondegenerate_tfae
lean.code:
theorem nondegenerate_tfae [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] (b : Basis ι ℝ V) :
    List.TFAE
      [Function.Bijective B,
       ∀ v : V, v ≠ 0 → ∃ w : V, B v w ≠ 0,
       (LinearMap.BilinForm.toMatrix b B).det ≠ 0,
       B.Nondegenerate] := by
  have hdual : finrank ℝ V = finrank ℝ (Module.Dual ℝ V) := Subspace.dual_finrank_eq.symm
  tfae_have 1 → 4 := by
    intro h
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_ker_eq_bot, LinearMap.ker_eq_bot]
    exact h.1
  tfae_have 4 → 1 := by
    intro h
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_ker_eq_bot, LinearMap.ker_eq_bot] at h
    exact ⟨h, (LinearMap.injective_iff_surjective_of_finrank_eq_finrank hdual).mp h⟩
  tfae_have 4 → 2 := by
    intro h v hv
    by_contra hc
    push Not at hc
    exact hv (h.1 v hc)
  tfae_have 2 → 4 := by
    intro h
    refine LinearMap.BilinForm.Nondegenerate.ofSeparatingLeft fun x hx => ?_
    by_contra hxne
    obtain ⟨w, hw⟩ := h x hxne
    exact hw (hx w)
  tfae_have 4 ↔ 3 := LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_det_ne_zero b
  tfae_finish

end Nondegenerate

section OrthogonalComplement

variable [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}

blocks.tex:
(a) $\Rightarrow$ (b): if $\tilde q$ is an isomorphism and $v \neq 0$, then $\tilde q(v) \neq 0$, so there is some $w \in V$ with $q(v,w) = \tilde q(v)(w) \neq 0$.

(b) $\Rightarrow$ (a): condition (b) says exactly that $\tilde q$ is injective, since $\tilde q(v) = 0$ means $q(v,w) = 0$ for every $w$, which by (b) forces $v = 0$. As $\dim V^* = \dim V < \infty$, an injective linear map $V \to V^*$ is an isomorphism.

(a) $\Leftrightarrow$ (c): let $(e_i)$ be the basis of $V$ dual to $(e^j)$. Then $\tilde q(e_i)(e_j) = q(e_i, e_j) = q_{ij}$, so $(q_{ij})$ is precisely the matrix of $\tilde q : V \to V^*$ with respect to the bases $(e_i)$ and $(e^j)$. A linear map between spaces of equal finite dimension is an isomorphism if and only if its matrix in any pair of bases is invertible.
This is the content of Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear}, proved above.

The proof of Lemma \ref{lem:polarization-identity} used only bilinearity and symmetry of $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$, never positive definiteness: expanding $\langle v \pm w, v \pm w \rangle = \langle v,v \rangle \pm 2\langle v,w \rangle + \langle w,w \rangle$ and subtracting gives $4\langle v,w \rangle$. Since a scalar product is by definition a symmetric bilinear form, the identity holds verbatim.
If $S \subseteq V$ is any linear subspace, the set of vectors in $V$ that are orthogonal to every vector in $S$ is a linear subspace denoted by $S^{\perp}$.

blocks.label: lem:orthogonal-complement
blocks.title: lem:orthogonal-complement
blocks.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space, and $S \subseteq V$ is a linear subspace. Then $\dim S + \dim S^{\perp} = \dim V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank
lean.code:
theorem finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    (S : Submodule ℝ V) : finrank ℝ S + finrank ℝ (B.orthogonal S) = finrank ℝ V := by
  have hle : finrank ℝ S ≤ finrank ℝ V := S.finrank_le
  rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.finrank_orthogonal hnd S]
  omega

blocks.tex: Define a linear map $\Phi : V \to S^*$ by $\Phi(v) = \tilde{q}(v)|_S$. Note that $v \in \Ker\Phi$ if and only if $q(v,x) = \tilde{q}(v)(x) = 0$ for all $x \in S$, so $\Ker\Phi = S^\perp$. If $\varphi \in S^*$ is arbitrary, there is a covector $\tilde{\varphi} \in V^*$ whose restriction to $S$ is equal to $\varphi$. (For example, such a covector is easily constructed after choosing a basis for $S$ and extending it to a basis for $V$.) Since $\tilde{q}$ is an isomorphism, there exists $v \in V$ such that $\tilde{q}(v) = \tilde{\varphi}$. It follows that $\Phi(v) = \varphi$, and therefore $\Phi$ is surjective. By the rank--nullity theorem, the dimension of $S^\perp = \Ker\Phi$ is equal to $\dim V - \dim S^* = \dim V - \dim S$.

blocks.label: lem:orthogonal-complement-involution
blocks.title: lem:orthogonal-complement-involution
blocks.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space, and $S \subseteq V$ is a linear subspace. Then $(S^{\perp})^{\perp} = S$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.orthogonal_orthogonal_eq_self
lean.code:
theorem orthogonal_orthogonal_eq_self (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    (S : Submodule ℝ V) : B.orthogonal (B.orthogonal S) = S :=
  LinearMap.BilinForm.orthogonal_orthogonal hnd hB.isRefl S

blocks.tex: Every $v \in S$ is orthogonal to every element of $S^\perp$ by definition, so $S \subseteq (S^\perp)^\perp$. Applying Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement} twice gives $\dim (S^\perp)^\perp = \dim V - \dim S^\perp = \dim S$. Because these finite-dimensional vector spaces are nested and have the same dimension, they are equal.

An ordered $k$-tuple $(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ of elements of $V$ is said to be \textbf{orthonormal} if $|v_i| = 1$ for each $i$ and $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle = 0$ for $i \neq j$, or equivalently, if $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle = \pm\delta_{ij}$ for each $i$ and $j$. We wish to prove that every scalar product space has an orthonormal basis. Note that the usual Gram--Schmidt algorithm does not always work in this situation, because the vectors that appear in the denominators in (2.5)--(2.6) might have vanishing norms. In order to get around this problem, we introduce the following definitions. If $(V,q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space, a subspace $S \subseteq V$ is said to be \textbf{nondegenerate} if the restriction of $q$ to $S \times S$ is nondegenerate. An ordered $k$-tuple of vectors $(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ in $V$ is said to be \textbf{nondegenerate} if for each $j = 1,\ldots,k$, the vectors $(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$ span a nondegenerate $j$-dimensional subspace of $V$. For example, every orthonormal basis is nondegenerate.

blocks.label: lem:nondegenerate-subspace
blocks.title: lem:nondegenerate-subspace
blocks.body:
Suppose $(V,q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space, and $S \subseteq V$ is a linear subspace. The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $S$ is nondegenerate.
\item $S^\perp$ is nondegenerate.
\item $S \cap S^\perp = \{0\}$.
\item $V = S \oplus S^\perp$.
\end{enumerate}

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.restrict_nondegenerate_tfae
lean.code:
theorem restrict_nondegenerate_tfae (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    (S : Submodule ℝ V) :
    List.TFAE
      [(B.restrict S).Nondegenerate,
       (B.restrict (B.orthogonal S)).Nondegenerate,
       S ⊓ B.orthogonal S = ⊥,
       IsCompl S (B.orthogonal S)] := by
  tfae_have 1 ↔ 4 :=
    LinearMap.BilinForm.restrict_nondegenerate_iff_isCompl_orthogonal hB.isRefl
  tfae_have 4 ↔ 3 := by
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.isCompl_orthogonal_iff_disjoint hB.isRefl, disjoint_iff]
  tfae_have 2 ↔ 4 := by
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.restrict_nondegenerate_iff_isCompl_orthogonal hB.isRefl,
      orthogonal_orthogonal_eq_self hB hnd S]
    exact ⟨IsCompl.symm, IsCompl.symm⟩
  tfae_finish

end OrthogonalComplement

section Orthonormal

blocks.tex:
(c) $\Rightarrow$ (a): suppose $S \cap S^\perp = \{0\}$ and let $x \in S$ satisfy $q(x,y) = 0$ for all $y \in S$. Then $x \in S^\perp$, so $x \in S \cap S^\perp = \{0\}$; thus $q|_{S \times S}$ is nondegenerate by Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear}.

(a) $\Rightarrow$ (d): suppose $S$ is nondegenerate. Then $S \cap S^\perp = \{0\}$, since any $x$ in the intersection is orthogonal to all of $S$ and lies in $S$, hence vanishes by nondegeneracy of $q|_{S \times S}$. By Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement}, $\dim S + \dim S^\perp = \dim V$, so $S + S^\perp$ has dimension $\dim S + \dim S^\perp - \dim(S \cap S^\perp) = \dim V$, whence $S + S^\perp = V$ and the sum is direct.

(d) $\Rightarrow$ (c): immediate, since a direct sum decomposition has trivial intersection.

(a) $\Leftrightarrow$ (b): by Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement-involution}, $(S^\perp)^\perp = S$, so applying the equivalence (a) $\Leftrightarrow$ (d) to the subspace $S^\perp$ shows that $S^\perp$ is nondegenerate if and only if $V = S^\perp \oplus (S^\perp)^\perp = S^\perp \oplus S$. Since a direct sum decomposition is symmetric in its two summands, this holds if and only if $V = S \oplus S^\perp$, which by (a) $\Leftrightarrow$ (d) holds if and only if $S$ is nondegenerate.
This is the content of Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-subspace}, proved above.

blocks.label: lem:completion-nondegenerate-bases
blocks.title: Completion of Nondegenerate Bases
blocks.body: Suppose $(V,q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space, and $(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ is a nondegenerate $k$-tuple in $V$ with $0 \leq k < n$. Then there exist vectors $v_{k+1},\ldots,v_n \in V$ such that $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ is a nondegenerate basis for $V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isNondegenerateTuple_basis
lean.code:
theorem exists_isNondegenerateTuple_basis (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    {k : ℕ} (v : Fin k → V) (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) (hk : k ≤ finrank ℝ V) :
    ∃ w : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V, IsNondegenerateTuple B w ∧
      ∀ i : Fin k, w ⟨(i : ℕ), lt_of_lt_of_le i.isLt hk⟩ = v i :=
  exists_isNondegenerateTuple_extend hB hnd (finrank ℝ V - k) k (by omega) v hv

blocks.tex:
It suffices to adjoin one vector, and then to repeat.

Let $S = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_k) \subseteq V$, a nondegenerate subspace of dimension $k$. Because $k < n$, Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement} gives $\dim S^\perp = n - k > 0$, so $S^\perp$ is a nontrivial subspace of $V$, and Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-subspace} shows that $S^\perp$ is nondegenerate and $V = S \oplus S^\perp$.

There must be a vector in $S^\perp$ with nonzero length. Otherwise $\langle x,x\rangle = 0$ for every $x \in S^\perp$, and then the polarization identity of Exercise \ref{exer:polarization-scalar-product},
\[
\langle x,y \rangle = \tfrac14\left(\langle x+y,x+y\rangle - \langle x-y,x-y\rangle\right),
\]
applied to $x, y \in S^\perp$ (which is a subspace, so $x \pm y \in S^\perp$) would give $\langle x,y\rangle = 0$ for all $x,y \in S^\perp$; as $S^\perp \neq \{0\}$, this contradicts the nondegeneracy of $S^\perp$.

Let $v_{k+1} \in S^\perp$ have $\langle v_{k+1},v_{k+1}\rangle \neq 0$. Then $v_{k+1} \notin S$, since $S \cap S^\perp = \{0\}$ and $v_{k+1} \neq 0$; hence $\dim(S \oplus \langle v_{k+1}\rangle) = k+1$. This span is nondegenerate: suppose $y = s + c\,v_{k+1}$ with $s \in S$ is orthogonal to all of $S \oplus \langle v_{k+1}\rangle$. Testing against any $w \in S$ gives $\langle s,w\rangle + c\langle v_{k+1},w\rangle = \langle s,w\rangle = 0$, since $v_{k+1} \in S^\perp$; as $S$ is nondegenerate, $s = 0$. Testing $y = c\,v_{k+1}$ against $v_{k+1}$ gives $c\langle v_{k+1},v_{k+1}\rangle = 0$, and $\langle v_{k+1},v_{k+1}\rangle \neq 0$ forces $c = 0$. So $y = 0$.

Thus $(v_1,\ldots,v_{k+1})$ is a nondegenerate $(k+1)$-tuple. Repeating this argument for $v_{k+2},\ldots,v_n$, which terminates after $n-k$ steps, completes the proof.

blocks.label: prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products
blocks.title: Gram--Schmidt Algorithm for Scalar Products
blocks.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space. If $(v_i)$ is a nondegenerate basis for $V$, then there is an orthonormal basis $(b_i)$ with the property that $\operatorname{span}(b_1, \ldots, b_k) = \operatorname{span}(v_1, \ldots, v_k)$ for each $k = 1, \ldots, n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] (hB : B.IsSymm)
    {v : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V} (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) :
    ∃ b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ V)) ℝ V, IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) ∧
      ∀ j ≤ finrank ℝ V, prefixSpan (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) j = prefixSpan v j := by
  obtain ⟨b, hbon, hbspan⟩ := exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq hB hv
  have hspan : ⊤ ≤ Submodule.span ℝ (Set.range b) := by
    rw [← prefixSpan_last b, hbspan (finrank ℝ V) le_rfl,
      Submodule.eq_top_of_finrank_eq (hv (finrank ℝ V) le_rfl).1]
  refine ⟨Basis.mk hbon.linearIndependent hspan, ?_, ?_⟩
  · simpa [Basis.coe_mk] using hbon
  · simpa [Basis.coe_mk] using hbspan

section Adapted

variable [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}

blocks.tex:
As in the positive definite case the tuple is constructed recursively, the inductive hypothesis at stage $k$ being that $(b_1,\ldots,b_k)$ is orthonormal and satisfies $\operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_j) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$ for every $j \leq k$. For $k = 0$ both conditions are vacuous.

Assume $(b_1,\ldots,b_k)$ has been constructed and write $S = \operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_k) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$. Set
\[
z = v_{k+1} - \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{\langle v_{k+1}, b_i \rangle}{\langle b_i, b_i \rangle} b_i.
\]
Each denominator is $\langle b_i, b_i \rangle = \pm 1 \neq 0$, so $z$ is well defined; this is the step at which positive definiteness is dispensable but nondegeneracy of the prefixes is not. For each $j \leq k$, orthogonality of the $b_i$ leaves a single surviving term in the sum:
\[
\langle z, b_j \rangle = \langle v_{k+1}, b_j \rangle - \frac{\langle v_{k+1}, b_j \rangle}{\langle b_j, b_j \rangle}\langle b_j, b_j \rangle = 0,
\]
so $z$ is orthogonal to every element of $S$.

Since $z - v_{k+1} \in S$, adjoining $z$ and adjoining $v_{k+1}$ to $S$ give the same subspace:
\[
S + \langle z \rangle = S + \langle v_{k+1} \rangle = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_{k+1}) =: T .
\]
Moreover $z \neq 0$; otherwise $v_{k+1} \in S$ and hence $T = S$, contradicting $\dim T = k+1 > k = \dim S$, both dimensions being part of the hypothesis that $(v_i)$ is nondegenerate.

Next, $\langle z, z \rangle \neq 0$. This is the one point at which the indefinite case genuinely differs: $z \neq 0$ no longer suffices. Suppose $\langle z,z\rangle = 0$. Every $w \in T$ can be written $w = s + cz$ with $s \in S$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}$, whence
\[
\langle z, w \rangle = \langle z, s \rangle + c\langle z, z \rangle = 0 ,
\]
so $z$ is orthogonal to all of $T$. As $T$ is nondegenerate, Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear} forces $z = 0$, a contradiction.

We may therefore complete the inductive step by putting
\[
b_{k+1} = \frac{z}{\sqrt{|\langle z, z \rangle|}} ,
\]
dividing by $\sqrt{|\langle z,z\rangle|}$ rather than by $|z|$, which need not be a real number in the indefinite case. Then $\langle b_{k+1}, b_{k+1} \rangle = \langle z,z\rangle/|\langle z,z\rangle| = \pm 1$, and $b_{k+1}$ is still orthogonal to $b_1,\ldots,b_k$, so $(b_1,\ldots,b_{k+1})$ is orthonormal. Rescaling by the nonzero scalar $|\langle z,z\rangle|^{-1/2}$ does not change the span, so
\[
\operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_{k+1}) = S + \langle z \rangle = T = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_{k+1}),
\]
which completes the induction.

Taking $k = n$: the tuple $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ is orthonormal, hence linearly independent, and it spans $\operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_n) = V$. So it is an orthonormal basis with the required property.

blocks.label: prop:symmetric-bilinear-orthogonal-basis
blocks.title: prop:symmetric-bilinear-orthogonal-basis
blocks.body: Let $q$ be a symmetric bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space $V$ over a field in which $2$ is invertible. Then $V$ has a basis $(b_i)$ that is $q$-orthogonal, that is, $q(b_i, b_j) = 0$ whenever $i \neq j$.

blocks.label: prop:scalar-product-orthonormal-basis
blocks.title: prop:scalar-product-orthonormal-basis
blocks.body: Every scalar product space $(V,q)$ has an orthonormal basis.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_isOrthonormal
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_isOrthonormal [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}
    (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    ∃ b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ V)) ℝ V, IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) := by
  have : Invertible (2 : ℝ) := invertibleOfNonzero (by norm_num)
  obtain ⟨v, hv⟩ :=
    LinearMap.BilinForm.exists_orthogonal_basis (LinearMap.BilinForm.isSymm_iff.mp hB)
  have hv : B.iIsOrtho (v : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) := hv
  -- Nondegeneracy of `B` rules out `⟪v i, v i⟫ = 0` on an orthogonal basis.
  have hvne : ∀ i, B (v i) (v i) ≠ 0 := fun i =>
    hv.not_isOrtho_basis_self_of_nondegenerate hnd i
  set c : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → ℝ := fun i => (Real.sqrt |B (v i) (v i)|)⁻¹ with hc
  have hcne : ∀ i, c i ≠ 0 := by
    intro i
    have : (0 : ℝ) < |B (v i) (v i)| := abs_pos.mpr (hvne i)
    simp only [hc, ne_eq, inv_eq_zero]
    positivity
  refine ⟨v.unitsSMul fun i => Units.mk0 (c i) (hcne i), ?_, ?_⟩
  · intro i j hij
    simp only [Basis.unitsSMul_apply, Units.smul_def, Units.val_mk0, map_smul,
      LinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul, LinearMap.BilinForm.iIsOrtho_def.1 hv i j hij, mul_zero]
  · intro i
    simpa only [Basis.unitsSMul_apply, Units.smul_def, Units.val_mk0, hc] using
      apply_self_normalize (hvne i)

blocks.tex:
Since $q$ is symmetric and $\dim V < \infty$, Proposition \ref{prop:symmetric-bilinear-orthogonal-basis} provides a $q$-orthogonal basis $(b_i)$ of $V$. No $b_i$ has vanishing norm: if $\langle b_i, b_i \rangle = 0$, then, $b_i$ being orthogonal to every $b_j$ with $j \neq i$ as well, $b_i$ would be orthogonal to a basis of $V$ and hence to all of $V$, contradicting nondegeneracy by Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear}. Set
\[
e_i = \frac{b_i}{\sqrt{|\langle b_i, b_i \rangle|}}.
\]
This is legitimate because $|\langle b_i, b_i \rangle| > 0$; note that one divides by $\sqrt{|\langle b_i,b_i\rangle|}$ rather than by $|b_i|$, which need not be a real number in the indefinite case. Rescaling each vector of a basis by a nonzero scalar again yields a basis and preserves orthogonality, and
\[
\langle e_i, e_i \rangle = \frac{\langle b_i, b_i \rangle}{|\langle b_i, b_i \rangle|} = \pm 1 .
\]
Hence $(e_i)$ is an orthonormal basis.

blocks.label: cor:scalar-product-signature-form
blocks.title: cor:scalar-product-signature-form
blocks.body:
Suppose $(V, q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space. There is a basis $(\beta^i)$ for $V^*$ with respect to which $q$ has the expression
\[
q = (\beta^1)^2 + \cdots + (\beta^r)^2 - (\beta^{r+1})^2 - \cdots - (\beta^{r+s})^2,
\]
for some nonnegative integers $r, s$ with $r + s = n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_isOrthonormal_signature
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_isOrthonormal_signature [FiniteDimensional ℝ V]
    (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    ∃ (r s : ℕ) (_hrs : r + s = finrank ℝ V) (b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ V)) ℝ V),
      IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) ∧
      (∀ i : Fin (finrank ℝ V), (i : ℕ) < r → B (b i) (b i) = 1) ∧
      (∀ i : Fin (finrank ℝ V), r ≤ (i : ℕ) → B (b i) (b i) = -1) :=
  let ⟨_, hb⟩ := exists_basis_isOrthonormal hB hnd
  hb.exists_sorted

blocks.tex:
Let $(b_i)$ be an orthonormal basis for $V$, which exists by Proposition \ref{prop:scalar-product-orthonormal-basis}, and let $(\beta^i)$ be the dual basis for $V^*$. For $v \in V$ write $v = \sum_i \beta^i(v) \, b_i$; bilinearity and orthogonality give
\[
q(v,v) = \sum_{i,j} \beta^i(v)\beta^j(v) \langle b_i, b_j\rangle = \sum_i \langle b_i, b_i \rangle \, (\beta^i(v))^2 ,
\]
so $q = \sum_i \langle b_i, b_i\rangle (\beta^i)^2$ with each coefficient $\langle b_i, b_i \rangle = \pm 1$. This is a basis expression of the required form, but perhaps with the positive and negative terms in a different order. Let $r$ be the number of indices with $\langle b_i, b_i \rangle = 1$ and $s$ the number with $\langle b_i, b_i \rangle = -1$; then $r + s = n$, since every index is of exactly one of the two kinds. Permuting the index set so that the $r$ indices of the first kind come first leaves the basis orthonormal, since orthonormality constrains only the pairwise values $\langle b_i, b_j \rangle$, and yields the stated expression.
It turns out that the numbers $r$ and $s$ in (2.23) are independent of the choice of basis. The key to proving this is the following classical result from linear algebra.

blocks.label: prop:sylvesters-law-inertia
blocks.title: Sylvester's Law of Inertia
blocks.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space. If $q$ has the representation (2.23) in some basis, then the number $r$ is the maximum dimension among all subspaces on which the restriction of $q$ is positive definite, and thus $r$ and $s$ are independent of the choice of basis.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsOrthonormal.isGreatest_ncard
lean.code:
theorem IsOrthonormal.isGreatest_ncard [FiniteDimensional ℝ V]
    (h : IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin n → V)) :
    IsGreatest
      {r | ∃ S : Submodule ℝ V, finrank ℝ S = r ∧
        ((LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B).restrict S).PosDef}
      {i | B (b i) (b i) = 1}.ncard := by
  rw [← h.sigPos_eq_ncard]
  exact sigPos_isGreatest _

blocks.tex:
Let $(b_i)$ be the orthonormal basis dual to $(\beta^i)$, so that $\langle b_i, b_i\rangle = 1$ for $i \leq r$ and $\langle b_i, b_i \rangle = -1$ for $i > r$, and $q(v,v) = \sum_i \langle b_i,b_i\rangle (\beta^i(v))^2$.

First, $r$ is attained. On $P = \operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_r)$ every $v \neq 0$ has $q(v,v) = \sum_{i \leq r} (\beta^i(v))^2 > 0$, since some coordinate $\beta^i(v)$ with $i \le r$ is nonzero. So $q|_P$ is positive definite and $\dim P = r$.

Second, $r$ is an upper bound. Let $N = \operatorname{span}(b_{r+1},\ldots,b_{r+s})$, so $\dim N = s$ and every $v \in N$ has $q(v,v) = -\sum_{i > r} (\beta^i(v))^2 \leq 0$. Let $W \subseteq V$ be any subspace on which $q$ is positive definite. Then $W \cap N = \{0\}$: a nonzero $v$ in the intersection would satisfy both $q(v,v) > 0$ and $q(v,v) \leq 0$. Hence $\dim W + \dim N \leq \dim V$, that is, $\dim W + s \leq r + s$, so $\dim W \leq r$.

Therefore $r$ is the maximum of $\dim W$ over all subspaces $W$ on which $q$ is positive definite. This characterization makes no reference to the basis, so $r$ is independent of the choice of basis; and then so is $s = n - r$.

Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia} characterizes $r$, and hence $s = n - r$, without reference to a basis. It is worth recording the relation $r + s = n$ of Corollary \ref{cor:scalar-product-signature-form} in those basis-free terms as well, since that is the form in which a signature can be manipulated arithmetically. The radical of a scalar product is the obstruction to doing so, and it vanishes.

blocks.label: lem:radical-scalar-product-trivial
blocks.title: A Scalar Product Has Trivial Radical
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space. The radical of $q$, that is, the set of $v \in V$ with $q(v,v) = 0$ whose associated polar form $w \mapsto q(v,w) + q(w,v)$ also vanishes identically, is $\{0\}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.radical_toQuadraticMap_eq_bot
lean.code:
theorem radical_toQuadraticMap_eq_bot (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B).radical = ⊥ := by
  rw [eq_bot_iff]
  intro x hx
  rw [Submodule.mem_bot]
  refine hnd.1 x fun y => ?_
  have hpol : QuadraticMap.polar (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) x y = 0 := by
    rw [← QuadraticMap.polarBilin_apply_apply, hx.2]
    simp
  rw [LinearMap.BilinMap.polar_toQuadraticMap, ← hB.eq x y] at hpol
  linarith

blocks.tex: Let $v$ lie in the radical. Since $q$ is symmetric, the polar form at $v$ is $w \mapsto 2\langle v,w\rangle$, so its vanishing gives $2\langle v,w\rangle = 0$, hence $\langle v,w \rangle = 0$, for every $w \in V$. Thus $v$ is orthogonal to all of $V$, and Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear} forces $v = 0$.

blocks.label: cor:scalar-product-signature-sum
blocks.title: $r + s = n$, without a basis
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be an $n$-dimensional scalar product space of signature $(r,s)$. Then $r + s = n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigPos_add_sigNeg_eq_finrank
lean.code:
theorem sigPos_add_sigNeg_eq_finrank [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] (hB : B.IsSymm)
    (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) + sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)
      = finrank ℝ V := by
  have h := sigPos_add_sigNeg_add_radical (Q := LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)
  rw [radical_toQuadraticMap_eq_bot hB hnd, finrank_bot] at h
  omega

end Signature

section NondegenerateTuple

deps.title: A Scalar Product Has Trivial Radical
deps.label: lem:radical-scalar-product-trivial

blocks.tex: For any quadratic form on a finite-dimensional space, the maximal dimension of a subspace on which it is positive definite, plus that on which it is negative definite, plus the dimension of its radical, is $\dim V$. By Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia} the first two quantities are $r$ and $s$, and by Lemma \ref{lem:radical-scalar-product-trivial} the radical is trivial, so its dimension is $0$. Hence $r + s = n$.

The integer $s$ in the expression (2.23) (the number of negative terms) is called the \textit{index of $q$}, and the ordered pair $(r, s)$ is called the \textit{signature of $q$}.

Now suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold. The generalization we are after replaces the inner product on each tangent space by a scalar product; that is, it drops positivity and keeps nondegeneracy. It costs nothing to state the underlying structure for an arbitrary smooth vector bundle, and there is a reason to do so: the orthonormalization results below are proved in that generality, and the normal bundle of Corollary \ref{cor:normal-bundle-pseudo-riemannian-submanifold} is not a tangent bundle.

blocks.label: def:smooth-field-scalar-products
blocks.title: Smooth Field of Scalar Products

blocks.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product
blocks.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
blocks.body: Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$ whose fibres are finite-dimensional. Then $(V_b, g_b)$ is a scalar product space for every $b \in B$.

lean.name: Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric.bilin_nondegenerate
lean.code:
theorem bilin_nondegenerate [∀ b, FiniteDimensional ℝ (E b)] (b : B) :
    (g.bilin b).Nondegenerate :=
  LinearMap.BilinForm.Nondegenerate.ofSeparatingLeft (g.bilin_separatingLeft b)

end ContMDiffPseudoMetric

end Defs

section Pairing

variable
  {EB : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup EB] [NormedSpace ℝ EB]
  {HB : Type*} [TopologicalSpace HB] {IB : ModelWithCorners ℝ EB HB} {n : ℕ∞ω}
  {B : Type*} [TopologicalSpace B] [ChartedSpace HB B]
  {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℝ F]
  {E : B → Type*} [TopologicalSpace (TotalSpace F E)]
  [∀ b, TopologicalSpace (E b)] [∀ b, AddCommGroup (E b)] [∀ b, Module ℝ (E b)]
  [FiberBundle F E] [VectorBundle ℝ F E]
  {EM : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup EM] [NormedSpace ℝ EM]
  {HM : Type*} [TopologicalSpace HM] {IM : ModelWithCorners ℝ EM HM}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace HM M]

namespace ContMDiffPseudoMetric

variable (g : ContMDiffPseudoMetric IB n F E) {b : M → B} {v w : ∀ x, E (b x)} {s : Set M} {x : M}

blocks.tex: The form $g_b$ is a symmetric bilinear form on the finite-dimensional space $V_b$ by conditions (a) and (c) of Definition \ref{def:smooth-field-scalar-products}. Condition (b) of that definition is verbatim condition (b) of Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear}, which is there shown equivalent to nondegeneracy of $g_b$. So $g_b$ is a nondegenerate symmetric bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space, which is what a scalar product is.

The pairing of two smooth sections against such a field is a smooth function. This is the analytic workhorse of everything below, and the point to notice is that its proof appeals only to the smoothness of $b \mapsto g_b$: positivity plays no part, so Lemma \ref{lem:inner-product-vector-fields-smooth} carries over unchanged.

blocks.label: lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth
blocks.title: Smoothness of the Pairing of Smooth Sections
blocks.body:
Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$, let $N$ be a smooth manifold, and let $s \subseteq N$. Let $f : N \to B$ be smooth on $s$, and let $v, w$ be sections of $V$ along $f$ — that is, assignments $x \mapsto v_x \in V_{f(x)}$ — that are smooth on $s$. Then
\[
x \longmapsto g_{f(x)}(v_x, w_x)
\]
is a smooth real-valued function on $s$. In particular, taking $s = N$, the pairing of two smooth sections along a smooth map is smooth.

lean.name: Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric.contMDiffOn_pairing
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffOn_pairing
    (hb : ContMDiffOn IM IB n b s)
    (hv : ContMDiffOn IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (v m)) s)
    (hw : ContMDiffOn IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (w m)) s) :
    ContMDiffOn IM 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n (fun m => g.form (b m) (v m) (w m)) s :=
  fun y hy => g.contMDiffWithinAt_pairing (hb y hy) (hv y hy) (hw y hy)

lean.name: Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric.contMDiff_pairing
lean.code:
theorem contMDiff_pairing
    (hb : ContMDiff IM IB n b)
    (hv : ContMDiff IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (v m)))
    (hw : ContMDiff IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (w m))) :
    ContMDiff IM 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n (fun m => g.form (b m) (v m) (w m)) :=
  fun y => g.contMDiffAt_pairing (hb y) (hv y) (hw y)

end ContMDiffPseudoMetric

end Pairing

end Bundle

namespace LeeLib.Ch02

open Bundle Manifold Module
open scoped Manifold ContDiff

section Manifold

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]

blocks.tex:
Smoothness on $s$ is checked at each point of $s$, so fix $x_0 \in s$ and put $b_0 = f(x_0)$. Choose a local trivialization $\Phi$ of $V$ over an open set $U \ni b_0$, with fibrewise linear isomorphisms $\Phi_b : V_b \to F$ onto the model fibre. It induces a trivialization of the bundle of bilinear forms on $V$ over $U$, carrying a bilinear form $\beta$ on $V_b$ to the bilinear form
\[
\hat\beta = \beta\bigl(\Phi_b^{-1}\,\cdot\,, \Phi_b^{-1}\,\cdot\,\bigr)
\]
on $F$; this is again a linear isomorphism on each fibre. Since $f$ is continuous at $x_0$ there is a neighbourhood $W$ of $x_0$ in $N$ with $f(W) \subseteq U$, and on $W$
\[
g_{f(x)}(v_x,w_x) = \hat g_{f(x)}\bigl(\Phi_{f(x)}v_x,\ \Phi_{f(x)}w_x\bigr).
\]
Read in the trivialization, the three maps
\[
x \mapsto \hat g_{f(x)}, \qquad x \mapsto \Phi_{f(x)}v_x, \qquad x \mapsto \Phi_{f(x)}w_x
\]
are smooth on $W \cap s$ with values in the fixed spaces of bilinear forms on $F$, in $F$, and in $F$: the first because $b \mapsto g_b$ is a smooth section and $f$ is smooth on $s$, the second and third because $v$ and $w$ are smooth sections along $f$ on $s$. Finally, evaluation $(\beta, y, z) \mapsto \beta(y,z)$ is a continuous multilinear map, hence smooth. The displayed function is the composite of these, so it is smooth at $x_0$ within $s$. As $x_0 \in s$ was arbitrary, it is smooth on $s$.

blocks.label: def:pseudo-riemannian-metric
blocks.title: Pseudo-Riemannian Metric
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold. A \emph{pseudo-Riemannian metric} on $M$ (called by some authors a \emph{semi-Riemannian metric}) is a smooth field of scalar products on the tangent bundle of $M$; that is, a smooth symmetric $2$-tensor field $g$ whose value $g_p$ at each $p \in M$ is nondegenerate on $T_pM$, so that $(T_pM, g_p)$ is a scalar product space at every point. A \emph{pseudo-Riemannian manifold} is a pair $(M,g)$ consisting of a smooth manifold together with a pseudo-Riemannian metric on it.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric
lean.code:
abbrev PseudoRiemannianMetric : Type _ :=
  Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric I ∞ E (TangentSpace I : M → Type _)

namespace PseudoRiemannianMetric

variable (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M)

blocks.tex: Lee's definition asks in addition that $g$ have \emph{the same signature everywhere}. That requirement is carried separately here, by Definition \ref{def:pseudo-metric-has-signature} below, and a ``pseudo-Riemannian manifold of signature $(r,s)$'' is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold together with the assertion that its signature is constantly $(r,s)$. The two are kept apart because Propositions \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} and \ref{cor:pseudo-riemannian-embedding-criterion} each exhibit a metric first and then \emph{conclude} that its signature is a particular constant; that conclusion is only sayable if a metric can be spoken of before its signature is known to be constant.

blocks.label: prop:riemannian-is-pseudo-riemannian
blocks.title: Every Riemannian Metric is Pseudo-Riemannian
blocks.body: Every Riemannian metric on a smooth manifold $M$ is a pseudo-Riemannian metric on $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.toPseudoRiemannianMetric
lean.code:
def RiemannianMetric.toPseudoRiemannianMetric (g : RiemannianMetric I M) :
    PseudoRiemannianMetric I M where
  form := g.inner
  symm := g.symm
  nondegenerate p v hv := ⟨v, ne_of_gt (g.pos p v hv)⟩
  contMDiff := g.contMDiff

blocks.tex: Let $g$ be a Riemannian metric. By Definition \ref{def:riemannian-metric} it is a smooth symmetric $2$-tensor field, so conditions (a) and (c) of Definition \ref{def:smooth-field-scalar-products} hold and only nondegeneracy at each point requires argument. Fix $p \in M$ and let $v \in T_pM$ be nonzero. Take $w = v$: positive definiteness of $g_p$ gives $g_p(v,v) > 0$, so in particular $g_p(v,w) \neq 0$. This is condition (b) of Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear}, hence $g_p$ is nondegenerate.

The witness $w = v$ used in that proof is exactly the step that fails for an indefinite scalar product: there a nonzero vector may be null, that is, orthogonal to itself, so $\langle v,v\rangle \neq 0$ does not follow from $v \neq 0$. Every difficulty in this section traces back to that one line.

blocks.label: def:pseudo-metric-signature-at
blocks.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Metric at a Point
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold and let $p \in M$. By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product} the pair $(T_pM, g_p)$ is a scalar product space, so by Corollary \ref{cor:scalar-product-signature-form} it has a signature $(r_p, s_p)$, which by Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia} does not depend on the basis used to compute it. We call $(r_p,s_p)$ the \emph{signature of $g$ at $p$}, and $s_p$ the \emph{index of $g$ at $p$}. Equivalently, by Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia}, $r_p$ is the largest dimension of a subspace of $T_pM$ on which $g_p$ is positive definite, and $s_p$ the largest dimension of one on which $g_p$ is negative definite.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric.sigPosAt
lean.code:
noncomputable def sigPosAt (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M) : ℕ :=
  sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (g.bilin p))

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric.sigNegAt
lean.code:
noncomputable def sigNegAt (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M) : ℕ :=
  sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (g.bilin p))

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

blocks.label: prop:pseudo-metric-signature-sum
blocks.title: The Signature at a Point Sums to the Dimension
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold, let $p \in M$, and let $(r_p,s_p)$ be the signature of $g$ at $p$. Then $r_p + s_p = \dim T_pM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric.sigPosAt_add_sigNegAt
lean.code:
theorem sigPosAt_add_sigNegAt (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M) :
    g.sigPosAt p + g.sigNegAt p = finrank ℝ (TangentSpace I p) :=
  sigPos_add_sigNeg_eq_finrank (g.bilin_isSymm p) (g.bilin_nondegenerate p)

deps.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Metric at a Point
deps.label: def:pseudo-metric-signature-at

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product} the pair $(T_pM, g_p)$ is a scalar product space, and $(r_p,s_p)$ is by Definition \ref{def:pseudo-metric-signature-at} its signature. Corollary \ref{cor:scalar-product-signature-sum} applied to it gives $r_p + s_p = \dim T_pM$.

blocks.label: def:pseudo-metric-has-signature
blocks.title: Constant Signature
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold and let $r,s \geq 0$ be integers. We say that $g$ \emph{has signature $(r,s)$} if the signature of $g$ at $p$ is $(r,s)$ for every $p \in M$. A \emph{pseudo-Riemannian manifold of signature $(r,s)$} is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ whose metric has signature $(r,s)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.HasSignature
lean.code:
def HasSignature (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (r s : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∀ p : M, g.sigPosAt p = r ∧ g.sigNegAt p = s

deps.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Metric at a Point
deps.label: def:pseudo-metric-signature-at

blocks.tex: By Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-metric-signature-sum}, a metric of signature $(r,s)$ on a manifold of dimension $n$ satisfies $r + s = n$, so on a fixed manifold a signature has only one degree of freedom.

blocks.title: The Gram--Schmidt Algorithm as an Explicit Recursion

blocks.tex: Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products} orthonormalizes a nondegenerate basis, but it does so as an \emph{existence} statement: its induction produces \emph{some} orthonormal basis, and asserts nothing about how that basis depends on the input. For the frames of Proposition \ref{prop:orthonormal-frames-pseudo-riemannian} that is not enough. There the input tuple varies with a point of a manifold and the output must be shown to vary \emph{smoothly} with it; smoothness is a property of a function, and there is no function to speak of until the orthonormalization is exhibited as a formula in its input. We therefore rerun the algorithm of Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products} as an explicit recursion and reprove its properties for that formula.

blocks.label: def:gram-schmidt-bilin
blocks.title: The Gram--Schmidt Recursion for a Bilinear Form
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be a real vector space, let $B$ be a bilinear form on $V$, and let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be an $n$-tuple in $V$. Define the $n$-tuple $u = \mathrm{GS}_B(v)$ by the recursion
\[
u_j = v_j - \sum_{i < j} \frac{B(u_i, v_j)}{B(u_i,u_i)} \, u_i .
\]
This is well founded, $u_j$ depending only on those $u_i$ with $i < j$. The formula is used only under the hypothesis of Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-spec}, which guarantees that no denominator $B(u_i,u_i)$ vanishes.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilin
lean.code:
noncomputable def gramSchmidtBilin (B : BilinForm ℝ V) (v : Fin n → V) (j : Fin n) : V :=
  v j - ∑ i : Finset.Iio j,
    (B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (v j) / B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (gramSchmidtBilin B v i)) •
      gramSchmidtBilin B v i
  termination_by j
  decreasing_by exact Fin.lt_def.mp (Finset.mem_Iio.mp i.2)

blocks.label: def:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed
blocks.title: The Normalized Gram--Schmidt Recursion
blocks.body:
With $V$, $B$ and $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ as in Definition \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin} and $u = \mathrm{GS}_B(v)$, define the $n$-tuple $b = \widehat{\mathrm{GS}}_B(v)$ by
\[
b_j = \frac{u_j}{\sqrt{\,|B(u_j,u_j)|\,}} .
\]
As in Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products}, one divides by $\sqrt{|B(u_j,u_j)|}$ rather than by $|u_j|$, which need not be a real number in the indefinite case. The effect is that $B(b_j,b_j) = \pm 1$ rather than $= 1$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilinNormed
lean.code:
noncomputable def gramSchmidtBilinNormed (B : BilinForm ℝ V) (v : Fin n → V) (j : Fin n) : V :=
  (Real.sqrt |B (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j)|)⁻¹ • gramSchmidtBilin B v j

/-! ### The flag, orthogonality, and the nonvanishing of the denominators

These three are proved by a single induction, because they are mutually dependent:
orthogonality of `u_j` against its predecessors needs their denominators to be nonzero,
the denominator `⟪u_j,u_j⟫ ≠ 0` needs `span (u_1,…,u_j) = span (v_1,…,v_j)`, and that
span identity needs `u_j ≡ v_j` modulo the earlier span.  Splitting them into separate
theorems would make each one's induction hypothesis unavailable to the others.

The statement is packaged as `GramSchmidtBilinSpec` over an initial segment `k` of the
index set, and `gramSchmidtBilin_spec` proves it for every `k ≤ n`. -/

variable (B) in
/-- The conjunction proved by the Gram-Schmidt induction, over the first `k` indices:
the denominators are nonzero, the vectors are pairwise `B`-orthogonal, and the flag is
reproduced.  See the section comment for why the three cannot be separated. -/
structure GramSchmidtBilinSpec (v : Fin n → V) (k : ℕ) : Prop where
  /-- The denominators of the recursion do not vanish — the indefinite case's burden. -/
  apply_self_ne_zero : ∀ i : Fin n, (i : ℕ) < k →
    B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) ≠ 0
  /-- The constructed vectors are pairwise `B`-orthogonal. -/
  ortho : ∀ i j : Fin n, (i : ℕ) < k → (j : ℕ) < k → i ≠ j →
    B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) = 0
  /-- Lee's flag condition: `span (u_1, …, u_j) = span (v_1, …, v_j)` for every `j ≤ k`. -/
  prefixSpan_eq : ∀ j ≤ k, prefixSpan (gramSchmidtBilin B v) j = prefixSpan v j

deps.title: The Gram--Schmidt Recursion for a Bilinear Form
deps.label: def:gram-schmidt-bilin

blocks.tex: The next lemma is the computation at the heart of the algorithm, isolated from the induction that uses it.

blocks.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthogonal-earlier
blocks.title: The New Vector is Orthogonal to its Predecessors
blocks.body:
Let $B$ be a bilinear form on a real vector space $V$, let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be an $n$-tuple in $V$, and write $u = \mathrm{GS}_B(v)$. Fix $k$ and suppose
\[
B(u_i,u_i) \neq 0 \quad\text{for every } i \leq k, \qquad B(u_i,u_{i'}) = 0 \quad\text{for all } i \neq i' \text{ with } i,i' \leq k .
\]
Then, writing $j = k+1$, we have $B(u_c, u_j) = 0$ for every $c < j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilin_apply_of_lt
lean.code:
theorem gramSchmidtBilin_apply_of_lt {v : Fin n → V} {k : ℕ}
    (hden : ∀ i : Fin n, (i : ℕ) < k → B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) ≠ 0)
    (hortho : ∀ i j : Fin n, (i : ℕ) < k → (j : ℕ) < k → i ≠ j →
      B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) = 0)
    {j : Fin n} (hjk : (j : ℕ) = k) {c : Fin n} (hc : c < j) :
    B (gramSchmidtBilin B v c) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) = 0 := by
  have hck : (c : ℕ) < k := hjk ▸ Fin.lt_def.mp hc
  rw [gramSchmidtBilin_def B v j]
  simp only [map_sub, map_sum, map_smul, smul_eq_mul]
  rw [Finset.sum_eq_single c]
  · rw [div_mul_cancel₀ _ (hden c hck), sub_self]
  · intro i hi hic
    have hik : (i : ℕ) < k := hjk ▸ Fin.lt_def.mp (Finset.mem_Iio.mp hi)
    rw [hortho c i hck hik (Ne.symm hic), mul_zero]
  · intro h
    exact absurd (Finset.mem_Iio.2 hc) h

deps.title: The Gram--Schmidt Recursion for a Bilinear Form
deps.label: def:gram-schmidt-bilin

blocks.tex:
Let $c < j$, so that $c \leq k$. Expanding the recursion of Definition \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin} at $j$ and using linearity of $B$ in its second argument,
\[
B(u_c, u_j) = B(u_c, v_j) - \sum_{i < j} \frac{B(u_i,v_j)}{B(u_i,u_i)} \, B(u_c, u_i) .
\]
Every index $i < j$ satisfies $i \leq k$, as does $c$; so by the second hypothesis $B(u_c,u_i) = 0$ for every such $i$ other than $i = c$, and the sum collapses to its single term $i = c$. Hence
\[
B(u_c,u_j) = B(u_c,v_j) - \frac{B(u_c,v_j)}{B(u_c,u_c)}\, B(u_c,u_c) = B(u_c,v_j) - B(u_c,v_j) = 0 ,
\]
the cancellation being legitimate because $B(u_c,u_c) \neq 0$ by the first hypothesis.

The nonvanishing of the denominators, the pairwise orthogonality of the $u_j$, and the reproduction of the flag are proved together, by a single induction. They cannot be separated: orthogonality of $u_j$ against its predecessors needs their denominators to be nonzero, the denominator $B(u_j,u_j) \neq 0$ needs $\operatorname{span}(u_1,\ldots,u_j) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$, and that identity needs $u_j \equiv v_j$ modulo the earlier span.

blocks.label: prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-spec
blocks.title: The Gram--Schmidt Recursion: Simultaneous Induction

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilin_spec
lean.code:
theorem gramSchmidtBilin_spec (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V} (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) :
    ∀ k ≤ n, GramSchmidtBilinSpec B v k := by
  intro k
  induction k with
  | zero =>
    intro _
    exact ⟨fun i hi => absurd hi (Nat.not_lt_zero _), fun i j hi => absurd hi (Nat.not_lt_zero _),
      fun j hj => by rw [Nat.le_zero.mp hj, prefixSpan_zero, prefixSpan_zero]⟩
  | succ k ih =>
    intro hk
    obtain ⟨hden, hortho, hspan⟩ := ih (by omega)
    have hkn : k < n := by omega
    set j : Fin n := ⟨k, hkn⟩ with hj
    set u := gramSchmidtBilin B v with hu
    have hjval : (j : ℕ) = k := by rw [hj]
    have hSb : prefixSpan u k = prefixSpan v k := hspan k le_rfl
    -- `u_j` is orthogonal to every earlier `u_c`, hence to `span (u_1, …, u_k)`.
    have hzb : ∀ c : Fin n, c < j → B (u c) (u j) = 0 := fun c hc =>
      gramSchmidtBilin_apply_of_lt hden hortho hjval hc
    have hzS : ∀ w ∈ prefixSpan v k, B (u j) w = 0 := by
      intro w hw
      rw [← hSb, prefixSpan] at hw
      induction hw using Submodule.span_induction with
      | mem x hx =>
        obtain ⟨i, hi, rfl⟩ := hx
        exact hB.isRefl _ _ (hzb i (Fin.lt_def.mpr (by rw [hjval]; exact hi)))
      | zero => simp
      | add x y _ _ hx hy => rw [map_add, hx, hy, add_zero]
      | smul a x _ hx => rw [map_smul, hx, smul_zero]
    -- `u_j ≡ v_j` modulo `span (u_1, …, u_k) = span (v_1, …, v_k)`.
    have hsum : (∑ i ∈ Finset.Iio j,
        (B (u i) (v j) / B (u i) (u i)) • u i) ∈ prefixSpan v k := by
      rw [← hSb]
      refine Submodule.sum_mem _ fun i hi => Submodule.smul_mem _ _ ?_
      exact Submodule.subset_span ⟨i, by simpa [hj, Fin.lt_def] using Finset.mem_Iio.1 hi, rfl⟩
    have hujv : u j = v j - ∑ i ∈ Finset.Iio j, (B (u i) (v j) / B (u i) (u i)) • u i :=
      gramSchmidtBilin_def B v j
    -- `v_j` is new: otherwise the flag would not grow in dimension.
    have hvkS : v j ∉ prefixSpan v k := by
      intro hmem
      have hcollapse : prefixSpan v (k + 1) = prefixSpan v k := by
        rw [prefixSpan_succ v hkn, sup_eq_left, Submodule.span_singleton_le_iff_mem]
        exact hmem
      have h1 := (hv (k + 1) hk).1
      rw [hcollapse, (hv k (by omega)).1] at h1
      omega
    have hzne : u j ≠ 0 := by
      intro h0
      rw [h0, eq_comm, sub_eq_zero] at hujv
      exact hvkS (hujv ▸ hsum)
    have hTz : prefixSpan v (k + 1) = prefixSpan v k ⊔ Submodule.span ℝ {u j} := by
      rw [prefixSpan_succ v hkn]
      refine le_antisymm (sup_le le_sup_left ?_) (sup_le le_sup_left ?_)
      · rw [Submodule.span_singleton_le_iff_mem,
          show v j = u j + ∑ i ∈ Finset.Iio j, (B (u i) (v j) / B (u i) (u i)) • u i by
            rw [hujv]; abel]
        exact Submodule.add_mem _
          (Submodule.mem_sup_right (Submodule.mem_span_singleton_self _))
          (Submodule.mem_sup_left hsum)
      · rw [Submodule.span_singleton_le_iff_mem, hujv]
  -- … (truncated)

deps.title: The New Vector is Orthogonal to its Predecessors
deps.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthogonal-earlier

deps.title: The Gram--Schmidt Recursion for a Bilinear Form
deps.label: def:gram-schmidt-bilin

blocks.tex:
Induct on $k$. For $k = 0$, statements (a) and (b) are vacuous, and (c) holds for the only admissible index $j = 0$, both spans being the span of the empty family, namely $\{0\}$.

Assume (a), (b), (c) hold for some $k$ with $k + 1 \leq n$, and put $j = k+1$ and $S = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$. By the inductive hypothesis (c), $S = \operatorname{span}(u_1,\ldots,u_k)$, and $\dim S = k$ because the tuple $(v_i)$ is nondegenerate.

\emph{The vector $u_j$ is orthogonal to $S$.} The inductive hypotheses (a) and (b) are exactly the hypotheses of Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthogonal-earlier}, which therefore gives $B(u_c,u_j) = 0$ for every $c < j$, that is, for every $c \leq k$. By symmetry of $B$ also $B(u_j,u_c) = 0$ for every $c \leq k$; since $B$ is linear in its second argument and the $u_c$ with $c \leq k$ span $S$, it follows that $B(u_j, y) = 0$ for every $y \in S$.

\emph{The vector $u_j$ agrees with $v_j$ modulo $S$.} Write $\sigma = \sum_{i<j} \frac{B(u_i,v_j)}{B(u_i,u_i)} u_i$, so that $u_j = v_j - \sigma$ by Definition \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin}. Each index $i < j$ satisfies $i \leq k$, so $\sigma$ is a linear combination of $u_1,\ldots,u_k$ and hence lies in $S$.

\emph{The vector $v_j$ is not in $S$.} If it were, then $\operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j) \subseteq S$; the reverse inclusion always holds, so $\operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j) = S$. But nondegeneracy of the tuple $(v_i)$ gives $\dim \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j) = j = k+1$, while $\dim S = k$, a contradiction.

\emph{Hence $u_j \neq 0$}: otherwise $v_j = \sigma \in S$, contrary to the previous paragraph.
