\emph{The span identity.} Put $T = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$. Since $u_j = v_j - \sigma$ with $\sigma \in S \subseteq T$ and $v_j \in T$, we get $u_j \in T$, whence $S + \langle u_j\rangle \subseteq T$. Conversely $v_j = u_j + \sigma \in S + \langle u_j \rangle$ and $v_1,\ldots,v_k \in S$, so $T \subseteq S + \langle u_j\rangle$. Therefore
\[
T = S + \langle u_j \rangle .
\]

\emph{The denominator does not vanish:} $B(u_j,u_j) \neq 0$. This is the one point at which the indefinite case genuinely differs, $u_j \neq 0$ no longer sufficing. Suppose $B(u_j,u_j) = 0$. By the span identity every $w \in T$ can be written $w = y + c\,u_j$ with $y \in S$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}$, and then
\[
B(u_j, w) = B(u_j, y) + c\,B(u_j,u_j) = 0 ,
\]
the first term vanishing because $u_j$ is orthogonal to $S$. So $u_j$ is an element of $T$ orthogonal to all of $T$. As $T$ is nondegenerate, being a member of the flag of the nondegenerate tuple $(v_i)$, Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear} forces $u_j = 0$, contradicting the previous paragraph.

\emph{Assembling the three statements at $k+1$.} For (a), let $i \leq k+1$: if $i \leq k$ this is the inductive hypothesis, and if $i = j$ it is the previous paragraph. For (b), let $i \neq i'$ with $i,i' \leq k+1$; not both can equal $j$, so after using the symmetry of $B$ we may assume $i' \leq k$. If also $i \leq k$ this is the inductive hypothesis; if $i = j$ it is the first paragraph. For (c), let $m \leq k+1$: if $m \leq k$ this is the inductive hypothesis, and if $m = k+1$ then, by the inductive hypothesis (c) and the span identity,
\[
\operatorname{span}(u_1,\ldots,u_{k+1}) = \operatorname{span}(u_1,\ldots,u_k) + \langle u_j\rangle = S + \langle u_j \rangle = T = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_{k+1}) . \qedhere
\]
Taking $k = n$ in Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-spec} gives the three statements in the form in which they are used.

blocks.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-nonvanishing
blocks.title: The Denominators Never Vanish
blocks.body: Let $B$ be a symmetric bilinear form on a real vector space $V$, let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be a nondegenerate $n$-tuple in $V$, and let $u = \mathrm{GS}_B(v)$. Then $B(u_j,u_j) \neq 0$ for every $j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilin_apply_self_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem gramSchmidtBilin_apply_self_ne_zero (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) (j : Fin n) :
    B (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) ≠ 0 :=
  (gramSchmidtBilin_spec hB hv n le_rfl).apply_self_ne_zero j j.isLt

blocks.tex: This is part (a) of Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-spec} with $k = n$.

This statement has no positive definite counterpart: for an inner product, $B(u_j,u_j) 
eq 0$ follows from $u_j 
eq 0$ alone, and $u_j 
eq 0$ follows from linear independence. It is the reason nondegeneracy of the whole flag, and not mere linear independence, is the right hypothesis for an indefinite Gram--Schmidt.

blocks.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthogonal
blocks.title: The Unnormalized Vectors are Orthogonal
blocks.body: With $B$, $(v_i)$ and $u$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-nonvanishing}, $B(u_i,u_j) = 0$ whenever $i \neq j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilin_apply_ne
lean.code:
theorem gramSchmidtBilin_apply_ne (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) {i j : Fin n} (hij : i ≠ j) :
    B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) = 0 :=
  (gramSchmidtBilin_spec hB hv n le_rfl).ortho i j i.isLt j.isLt hij

blocks.tex: This is part (b) of Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-spec} with $k = n$.

blocks.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-flag
blocks.title: The Unnormalized Vectors Reproduce the Flag
blocks.body: With $B$, $(v_i)$ and $u$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-nonvanishing}, $\operatorname{span}(u_1,\ldots,u_j) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$ for every $j \leq n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.prefixSpan_gramSchmidtBilin
lean.code:
theorem prefixSpan_gramSchmidtBilin (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) (j : ℕ) (hj : j ≤ n) :
    prefixSpan (gramSchmidtBilin B v) j = prefixSpan v j :=
  (gramSchmidtBilin_spec hB hv n le_rfl).prefixSpan_eq j hj

/-! ### The normalized family -/

blocks.tex: This is part (c) of Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-spec} with $k = n$.

blocks.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-normalizer-ne-zero
blocks.title: The Normalizing Scalar is Nonzero
blocks.body: With $B$, $(v_i)$ and $u$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-nonvanishing}, the scalar $\bigl(\sqrt{|B(u_j,u_j)|}\bigr)^{-1}$ is nonzero for every $j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilin_normalizer_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem gramSchmidtBilin_normalizer_ne_zero (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) (j : Fin n) :
    (Real.sqrt |B (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j)|)⁻¹ ≠ 0 := by
  have hpos : (0 : ℝ) < |B (gramSchmidtBilin B v j) (gramSchmidtBilin B v j)| :=
    abs_pos.mpr (gramSchmidtBilin_apply_self_ne_zero hB hv j)
  simp only [ne_eq, inv_eq_zero]
  positivity

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-nonvanishing}, $B(u_j,u_j) \neq 0$, so $|B(u_j,u_j)| > 0$ and therefore $\sqrt{|B(u_j,u_j)|} > 0$. The inverse of a nonzero real number is nonzero.

blocks.label: prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthonormal
blocks.title: The Recursion Produces an Orthonormal Tuple
blocks.body: Let $B$ be a symmetric bilinear form on a real vector space $V$, let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be a nondegenerate $n$-tuple in $V$, and let $b = \widehat{\mathrm{GS}}_B(v)$. Then $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ is orthonormal: $B(b_i,b_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$, and $B(b_j,b_j) = \pm 1$ for every $j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.gramSchmidtBilin_isOrthonormal
lean.code:
theorem gramSchmidtBilin_isOrthonormal (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) : IsOrthonormal B (gramSchmidtBilinNormed B v) := by
  constructor
  · intro i j hij
    simp only [gramSchmidtBilinNormed, map_smul, LinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul,
      gramSchmidtBilin_apply_ne hB hv hij, mul_zero]
  · intro i
    exact apply_self_normalize (gramSchmidtBilin_apply_self_ne_zero hB hv i)

blocks.tex:
Write $u = \mathrm{GS}_B(v)$ and $\lambda_j = \bigl(\sqrt{|B(u_j,u_j)|}\bigr)^{-1}$, so that $b_j = \lambda_j u_j$ by Definition \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed}. By Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-nonvanishing} we have $B(u_j,u_j) \neq 0$, hence $|B(u_j,u_j)| > 0$ and $\lambda_j^2 = |B(u_j,u_j)|^{-1}$.

For $i \neq j$, bilinearity and Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthogonal} give
\[
B(b_i,b_j) = \lambda_i \lambda_j \, B(u_i,u_j) = 0 .
\]
For each $j$, bilinearity gives
\[
B(b_j,b_j) = \lambda_j^2 \, B(u_j,u_j) = \frac{B(u_j,u_j)}{|B(u_j,u_j)|} = \pm 1 ,
\]
the sign being $+1$ if $B(u_j,u_j) > 0$ and $-1$ if $B(u_j,u_j) < 0$.

blocks.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed-flag
blocks.title: The Normalized Tuple Reproduces the Flag
blocks.body: With $B$, $(v_i)$ and $b = \widehat{\mathrm{GS}}_B(v)$ as in Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthonormal}, $\operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_j) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$ for every $j \leq n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.prefixSpan_gramSchmidtBilinNormed
lean.code:
theorem prefixSpan_gramSchmidtBilinNormed (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) (j : ℕ) (hj : j ≤ n) :
    prefixSpan (gramSchmidtBilinNormed B v) j = prefixSpan v j := by
  rw [← prefixSpan_gramSchmidtBilin hB hv j hj]
  unfold prefixSpan
  refine le_antisymm (Submodule.span_le.2 ?_) (Submodule.span_le.2 ?_)
  · rintro _ ⟨i, hi, rfl⟩
    exact Submodule.smul_mem _ _ (Submodule.subset_span ⟨i, hi, rfl⟩)
  · rintro _ ⟨i, hi, rfl⟩
    have : gramSchmidtBilin B v i =
        (Real.sqrt |B (gramSchmidtBilin B v i) (gramSchmidtBilin B v i)|) •
          gramSchmidtBilinNormed B v i := by
      rw [gramSchmidtBilinNormed, smul_smul,
        mul_inv_cancel₀ (by
          have := gramSchmidtBilin_normalizer_ne_zero hB hv i
          simpa using inv_ne_zero this), one_smul]
    rw [this]
    exact Submodule.smul_mem _ _ (Submodule.subset_span ⟨i, hi, rfl⟩)

deps.title: The Unnormalized Vectors Reproduce the Flag
deps.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-flag

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-flag} it suffices to show $\operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_j) = \operatorname{span}(u_1,\ldots,u_j)$, where $u = \mathrm{GS}_B(v)$. Each $b_i = \lambda_i u_i$ with $\lambda_i \neq 0$ by Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-normalizer-ne-zero}, so $b_i \in \operatorname{span}(u_1,\ldots,u_j)$ for $i \leq j$, giving one inclusion; and $u_i = \lambda_i^{-1} b_i \in \operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_j)$ for $i \leq j$, giving the other. Rescaling the members of a family by nonzero scalars therefore leaves every span unchanged.

blocks.label: cor:gram-schmidt-bilin-recovers
blocks.title: Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products}, Recovered from the Recursion
blocks.body: Let $B$ be a symmetric bilinear form on a real vector space $V$ and let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be a nondegenerate $n$-tuple in $V$. Then there is an orthonormal $n$-tuple $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ in $V$ with $\operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_j) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$ for every $j \leq n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq_of_gramSchmidt
lean.code:
theorem exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq_of_gramSchmidt (hB : B.IsSymm) {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) :
    ∃ b : Fin n → V, IsOrthonormal B b ∧ ∀ j ≤ n, prefixSpan b j = prefixSpan v j :=
  ⟨gramSchmidtBilinNormed B v, gramSchmidtBilin_isOrthonormal hB hv,
    prefixSpan_gramSchmidtBilinNormed hB hv⟩

deps.title: The Recursion Produces an Orthonormal Tuple
deps.label: prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthonormal

deps.title: The Normalized Tuple Reproduces the Flag
deps.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed-flag

blocks.tex: Take $b = \widehat{\mathrm{GS}}_B(v)$. It is orthonormal by Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthonormal} and reproduces the flag by Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed-flag}.

This confirms that the recursion computes what the existence proof of Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products} asserts; the point of the recursion is that, unlike that proof, it has an output one can differentiate.

blocks.title: Smoothness of the Fibrewise Gram--Schmidt Algorithm

blocks.tex: Two ingredients are needed to run the recursion of Definition \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin} fibrewise and conclude that the output is smooth. The first is Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth}, which makes the pairings appearing in the recursion smooth functions of the base point. The second concerns normalization: the Riemannian proof of Proposition \ref{prop:existence-orthonormal-frames} divides by $\sqrt{\langle u_j,u_j\rangle}$, whose argument is positive, whereas here one divides by $\sqrt{|B(u_j,u_j)|}$, and $|\cdot|$ is not differentiable at $0$. It does not need to be.

blocks.label: lem:inv-sqrt-abs-smooth
blocks.title: $(\sqrt{|\cdot|})^{-1}$ is Smooth Away from the Origin
blocks.body: Let $t \in \mathbb{R}$ with $t \neq 0$. Then $s \mapsto \bigl(\sqrt{|s|}\bigr)^{-1}$ is smooth at $t$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.contDiffAt_inv_sqrt_abs
lean.code:
theorem contDiffAt_inv_sqrt_abs {k : WithTop ℕ∞} {t : ℝ} (ht : t ≠ 0) :
    ContDiffAt ℝ k (fun s : ℝ => (Real.sqrt |s|)⁻¹) t := by
  have habs : ContDiffAt ℝ k (fun s : ℝ => |s|) t := by
    rcases ht.lt_or_gt with h | h
    · refine ContDiffAt.congr_of_eventuallyEq (f := fun s : ℝ => -s) contDiffAt_id.neg ?_
      filter_upwards [Iio_mem_nhds h] with s hs
      exact abs_of_neg (Set.mem_Iio.mp hs)
    · refine ContDiffAt.congr_of_eventuallyEq (f := fun s : ℝ => s) contDiffAt_id ?_
      filter_upwards [Ioi_mem_nhds h] with s hs
      exact abs_of_pos (Set.mem_Ioi.mp hs)
  have hsqrt : ContDiffAt ℝ k (fun s : ℝ => Real.sqrt |s|) t :=
    (Real.contDiffAt_sqrt (x := |t|) (abs_ne_zero.mpr ht)).comp t habs
  exact hsqrt.inv (Real.sqrt_ne_zero'.mpr (abs_pos.mpr ht))

section BundleFrame

variable
  {EB : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup EB] [NormedSpace ℝ EB]
  {HB : Type*} [TopologicalSpace HB] {IB : ModelWithCorners ℝ EB HB}
  {B : Type*} [TopologicalSpace B] [ChartedSpace HB B]
  {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℝ F]
  {V : B → Type*} [TopologicalSpace (TotalSpace F V)]
  [∀ x, TopologicalSpace (V x)] [∀ x, AddCommGroup (V x)] [∀ x, Module ℝ (V x)]
  [∀ x, IsTopologicalAddGroup (V x)] [∀ x, ContinuousSMul ℝ (V x)]
  [FiberBundle F V] [VectorBundle ℝ F V]
  {n : ℕ∞ω} {m : ℕ} {X : Fin m → (x : B) → V x} {u : Set B}

variable (IB F n) in
/-- A family of sections that is `C^n` on `u` and forms a **nondegenerate tuple** in the
fibre at each point of `u` (Lee's hypothesis in Proposition 2.63).

This is the indefinite replacement for `LeeLib.Ch02.IsLocalIndepOn`.  The difference is
not bookkeeping: `IsNondegenerateTuple` requires every step `span (X_1|_x, …, X_k|_x)`
of the flag to be a nondegenerate subspace, which is strictly stronger than linear
independence and is exactly what keeps the Gram-Schmidt denominators away from `0`.
A linearly independent family of null vectors — impossible in the Riemannian case,
routine in a Lorentz metric — is not admissible input. -/
structure IsLocalNondegenerateOn (g : Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric IB n F V)
    (X : Fin m → (x : B) → V x) (u : Set B) where
  nondegenerateTuple {x : B} (hx : x ∈ u) : IsNondegenerateTuple (g.bilin x) (fun i => X i x)
  contMDiffOn (i : Fin m) : ContMDiffOn IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (T% (X i)) u

variable (g : Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric IB n F V)

blocks.tex:
First, $s \mapsto |s|$ is smooth at $t$. If $t < 0$ then $|s| = -s$ for every $s$ in the neighbourhood $(-\infty,0)$ of $t$; if $t > 0$ then $|s| = s$ for every $s$ in the neighbourhood $(0,\infty)$ of $t$. In either case $|\cdot|$ agrees near $t$ with a linear function, and smoothness at a point depends only on the germ there.

Next, $|t| > 0$ and $r \mapsto \sqrt{r}$ is smooth on $(0,\infty)$, hence smooth at $|t|$; so the composite $s \mapsto \sqrt{|s|}$ is smooth at $t$.

Finally $\sqrt{|t|} > 0$, in particular $\sqrt{|t|} \neq 0$, and $r \mapsto r^{-1}$ is smooth at every nonzero real number. Composing once more, $s \mapsto (\sqrt{|s|})^{-1}$ is smooth at $t$.

blocks.label: def:local-nondegenerate-family
blocks.title: Pointwise Nondegenerate Smooth Family
blocks.body:
Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$ with finite-dimensional fibres, and let $u \subseteq B$. A family $(X_1,\ldots,X_m)$ of sections of $V$ is \emph{nondegenerate on $u$} if
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item each $X_i$ is smooth on $u$; and
\item for every $x \in u$ the tuple $(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x)$ is a nondegenerate $m$-tuple in the scalar product space $(V_x, g_x)$.
\end{enumerate}

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

blocks.tex: Condition (b) is strictly stronger than pointwise linear independence: it asks each $\operatorname{span}(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_k|_x)$ to be a nondegenerate subspace of $V_x$, not merely of dimension $k$. This is the indefinite replacement for the hypothesis ``$(X_i)$ is a local frame'' of Proposition \ref{prop:existence-orthonormal-frames}. The strengthening is forced: a linearly independent tuple of null vectors is impossible when $g_x$ is an inner product but routine for an indefinite scalar product, and on such a tuple the recursion of Definition \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin} divides by zero.

blocks.label: def:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame
blocks.title: The Fibrewise Gram--Schmidt Frame
blocks.body:
Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$ and let $(X_1,\ldots,X_m)$ be a family of sections of $V$. Applying Definitions \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin} and \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed} in each fibre against the form $g_x$, define families $(U_1,\ldots,U_m)$ and $(E_1,\ldots,E_m)$ of sections of $V$ by
\[
U_j|_x = \mathrm{GS}_{g_x}\bigl(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x\bigr)_j , \qquad
E_j|_x = \widehat{\mathrm{GS}}_{g_x}\bigl(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x\bigr)_j = \frac{U_j|_x}{\sqrt{\,|g_x(U_j|_x, U_j|_x)|\,}} .
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.pseudoGramSchmidtFrame
lean.code:
noncomputable def pseudoGramSchmidtFrame (X : Fin m → (x : B) → V x) (j : Fin m) (x : B) : V x :=
  gramSchmidtBilinNormed (g.bilin x) (fun i => X i x) j

/-! ### The algebraic half

At each point of `u` the input is a nondegenerate tuple, so the pointwise theory of
`LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoGramSchmidt` applies verbatim in the fibre. -/

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux
lean.code:
noncomputable def pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux (X : Fin m → (x : B) → V x) (j : Fin m) (x : B) :
    V x :=
  gramSchmidtBilin (g.bilin x) (fun i => X i x) j

deps.title: The Gram--Schmidt Recursion for a Bilinear Form
deps.label: def:gram-schmidt-bilin

blocks.label: lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators
blocks.title: Nonvanishing of the Fibrewise Denominators
blocks.body: Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on $V \to B$ with finite-dimensional fibres, and let $(X_1,\ldots,X_m)$ be nondegenerate on $u \subseteq B$. Then $g_x(U_j|_x, U_j|_x) \neq 0$ for every $x \in u$ and every $j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux_apply_self_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux_apply_self_ne_zero (hX : IsLocalNondegenerateOn IB F n g X u)
    {x : B} (hx : x ∈ u) (j : Fin m) :
    g.bilin x (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j x) (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j x) ≠ 0 :=
  gramSchmidtBilin_apply_self_ne_zero (g.bilin_isSymm x) (hX.nondegenerateTuple hx) j

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

blocks.tex: Fix $x \in u$. By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product} the form $g_x$ is a symmetric bilinear form on $V_x$, and by condition (b) of Definition \ref{def:local-nondegenerate-family} the tuple $(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x)$ is nondegenerate. Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-nonvanishing}, applied in $V_x$ to $B = g_x$ and this tuple, gives the claim.

blocks.label: prop:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-orthonormal
blocks.title: Orthonormality of the Gram--Schmidt Frame
blocks.body: With $g$ and $(X_i)$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators}, the tuple $(E_1|_x,\ldots,E_m|_x)$ is orthonormal in $(V_x, g_x)$ for every $x \in u$; that is, $g_x(E_i|_x, E_j|_x) = \pm\delta_{ij}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.pseudoGramSchmidtFrame_isOrthonormal
lean.code:
theorem pseudoGramSchmidtFrame_isOrthonormal (hX : IsLocalNondegenerateOn IB F n g X u)
    {x : B} (hx : x ∈ u) :
    IsOrthonormal (g.bilin x) (fun j => pseudoGramSchmidtFrame g X j x) :=
  gramSchmidtBilin_isOrthonormal (g.bilin_isSymm x) (hX.nondegenerateTuple hx)

deps.title: The Recursion Produces an Orthonormal Tuple
deps.label: prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthonormal

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

blocks.tex: Fix $x \in u$. As in the proof of Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators}, $g_x$ is a symmetric bilinear form on $V_x$ and $(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x)$ is a nondegenerate tuple. By Definition \ref{def:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame}, $(E_1|_x,\ldots,E_m|_x)$ is exactly the tuple $\widehat{\mathrm{GS}}_{g_x}(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x)$, which Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-bilin-orthonormal} shows to be orthonormal.

blocks.label: lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-flag
blocks.title: The Gram--Schmidt Frame Reproduces the Flag
blocks.body:
With $g$ and $(X_i)$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators}, for every $x \in u$ and every $k \leq m$,
\[
\operatorname{span}\bigl(E_1|_x,\ldots,E_k|_x\bigr) = \operatorname{span}\bigl(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_k|_x\bigr) .
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.prefixSpan_pseudoGramSchmidtFrame
lean.code:
theorem prefixSpan_pseudoGramSchmidtFrame (hX : IsLocalNondegenerateOn IB F n g X u)
    {x : B} (hx : x ∈ u) (k : ℕ) (hk : k ≤ m) :
    prefixSpan (fun j => pseudoGramSchmidtFrame g X j x) k = prefixSpan (fun i => X i x) k :=
  prefixSpan_gramSchmidtBilinNormed (g.bilin_isSymm x) (hX.nondegenerateTuple hx) k hk

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

deps.title: The Normalized Tuple Reproduces the Flag
deps.label: lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed-flag

blocks.tex: Fix $x \in u$. As above, $g_x$ is a symmetric bilinear form on $V_x$ and $(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x)$ is a nondegenerate tuple, and $(E_j|_x)_j$ is $\widehat{\mathrm{GS}}_{g_x}$ of it. Lemma \ref{lem:gram-schmidt-bilin-normed-flag} applied in $V_x$ gives the claim.

blocks.label: prop:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-aux-smooth
blocks.title: Smoothness of the Unnormalized Gram--Schmidt Sections
blocks.body: With $g$ and $(X_i)$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators}, each section $U_j$ is smooth on $u$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.contMDiffOn_pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffOn_pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux (hX : IsLocalNondegenerateOn IB F n g X u)
    (j : Fin m) :
    ContMDiffOn IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (T% (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j)) u := by
  induction j using WellFoundedLT.induction with
  | _ j IH =>
    have hc : ∀ i ∈ Finset.Iio j, ContMDiffOn IB 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n
        (fun x => g.bilin x (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X i x) (X j x) /
          g.bilin x (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X i x)
            (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X i x)) u := by
      intro i hi
      have hui := IH i (Finset.mem_Iio.mp hi)
      exact ContMDiffOn.div₀
        (g.contMDiffOn_pairing contMDiffOn_id hui (hX.contMDiffOn j))
        (g.contMDiffOn_pairing contMDiffOn_id hui hui)
        fun x hx => pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux_apply_self_ne_zero g hX hx i
    have hsum : ContMDiffOn IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n
        (T% (fun x => ∑ i ∈ Finset.Iio j,
          (g.bilin x (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X i x) (X j x) /
            g.bilin x (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X i x)
              (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X i x)) • pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X i x)) u :=
      ContMDiffOn.sum_section fun i hi => (hc i hi).smul_section (IH i (Finset.mem_Iio.mp hi))
    refine ((hX.contMDiffOn j).sub_section hsum).congr fun x hx => ?_
    exact congrArg (TotalSpace.mk' F x) (gramSchmidtBilin_def (g.bilin x) (fun i => X i x) j)

deps.title: Smoothness of the Pairing of Smooth Sections
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth

blocks.tex:
By strong induction on $j$: assume $U_i$ is smooth on $u$ for every $i < j$. By Definition \ref{def:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame} and Definition \ref{def:gram-schmidt-bilin},
\[
U_j|_x = X_j|_x - \sum_{i<j} \frac{g_x(U_i|_x, X_j|_x)}{g_x(U_i|_x, U_i|_x)} \, U_i|_x .
\]
Fix $i < j$. The functions
\[
x \mapsto g_x(U_i|_x, X_j|_x) \qquad\text{and}\qquad x \mapsto g_x(U_i|_x, U_i|_x)
\]
are smooth on $u$ by Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth}, applied with $f$ the identity of $B$: its hypotheses hold because $U_i$ is smooth on $u$ by the inductive hypothesis and $X_j$ is smooth on $u$ by condition (a) of Definition \ref{def:local-nondegenerate-family}. By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators} the second of them is nowhere zero on $u$, so the quotient
\[
c_i : x \mapsto \frac{g_x(U_i|_x, X_j|_x)}{g_x(U_i|_x, U_i|_x)}
\]
is smooth on $u$, a quotient of smooth real-valued functions with nonvanishing denominator.

Therefore each $c_i \cdot U_i$ is a smooth section on $u$, being a smooth scalar multiple of a smooth section; the finite sum $\sum_{i<j} c_i U_i$ is a smooth section on $u$; and $U_j = X_j - \sum_{i<j} c_i U_i$ is smooth on $u$, being a difference of smooth sections.

blocks.label: prop:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-smooth
blocks.title: Smoothness of the Gram--Schmidt Frame
blocks.body: With $g$ and $(X_i)$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators}, each section $E_j$ is smooth on $u$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.contMDiffOn_pseudoGramSchmidtFrame
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffOn_pseudoGramSchmidtFrame (hX : IsLocalNondegenerateOn IB F n g X u)
    (j : Fin m) :
    ContMDiffOn IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (T% (pseudoGramSchmidtFrame g X j)) u := by
  have hu := contMDiffOn_pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g hX j
  have hq : ContMDiffOn IB 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n
      (fun x => g.bilin x (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j x)
        (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j x)) u :=
    g.contMDiffOn_pairing contMDiffOn_id hu hu
  have hnorm : ContMDiffOn IB 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n
      (fun x => (Real.sqrt |g.bilin x (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j x)
        (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j x)|)⁻¹) u := by
    intro x hx
    exact ContDiffAt.comp_contMDiffWithinAt (g := fun s : ℝ => (Real.sqrt |s|)⁻¹)
      (f := fun y => g.bilin y (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j y)
        (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux g X j y))
      (contDiffAt_inv_sqrt_abs (pseudoGramSchmidtFrameAux_apply_self_ne_zero g hX hx j))
      (hq x hx)
  exact hnorm.smul_section hu

deps.title: $(\sqrt{|\cdot|})^{-1}$ is Smooth Away from the Origin
deps.label: lem:inv-sqrt-abs-smooth

deps.title: Smoothness of the Unnormalized Gram--Schmidt Sections
deps.label: prop:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-aux-smooth

deps.title: Smoothness of the Pairing of Smooth Sections
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth

blocks.tex:
By Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-aux-smooth} the section $U_j$ is smooth on $u$, so by Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth} the real-valued function
\[
q : x \mapsto g_x(U_j|_x, U_j|_x)
\]
is smooth on $u$, and by Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators} it is nowhere zero there. Lemma \ref{lem:inv-sqrt-abs-smooth} shows that $s \mapsto (\sqrt{|s|})^{-1}$ is smooth at every nonzero real number, hence at $q(x)$ for every $x \in u$. Therefore the composite
\[
x \mapsto \bigl(\sqrt{|q(x)|}\bigr)^{-1}
\]
is smooth on $u$. By Definition \ref{def:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame}, $E_j$ is the product of this smooth real-valued function with the smooth section $U_j$, hence is a smooth section on $u$.

Note where positivity was and was not missed. The pairings are smooth without it, by Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth}; the quotients are smooth because their denominators are \emph{nonvanishing}, which is Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-denominators}, and not because they are positive. Only the normalization notices the difference, and Lemma \ref{lem:inv-sqrt-abs-smooth} absorbs it.

The results above orthonormalize a family that is \emph{given} to be nondegenerate at every point of $u$. Proposition \ref{prop:orthonormal-frames-pseudo-riemannian} additionally requires such a family to exist near each point, which the Riemannian case obtains for free — any local frame will do there — but which here is a genuine step. It rests on three lemmas.

blocks.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-gram-matrix
blocks.title: Nondegeneracy of a Tuple via its Gram Matrix
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space and let $(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ be a $k$-tuple in $V$. Then $\operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ is a nondegenerate subspace of dimension $k$ if and only if the Gram matrix $G = \bigl(\langle v_i, v_j\rangle\bigr)_{i,j=1}^{k}$ is invertible.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isNondegenerate_span_iff_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem isNondegenerate_span_iff_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero (B : BilinForm ℝ V) {k : ℕ}
    (v : Fin k → V) :
    (finrank ℝ (span ℝ (Set.range v)) = k ∧
        (B.restrict (span ℝ (Set.range v))).Nondegenerate)
      ↔ (gramMatrix B v).det ≠ 0 := by
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨hr, hnd⟩
    have hv : LinearIndependent ℝ v :=
      linearIndependent_iff_card_eq_finrank_span.mpr (by simpa [Set.finrank] using hr.symm)
    rw [← toMatrix_span_restrict B hv]
    exact (LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_det_ne_zero (Basis.span hv)).mp hnd
  · intro h
    have hv : LinearIndependent ℝ v := linearIndependent_of_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero B h
    refine ⟨by rw [finrank_span_eq_card hv]; simp, ?_⟩
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_det_ne_zero (Basis.span hv),
      toMatrix_span_restrict B hv]
    exact h

blocks.tex:
Suppose $G$ is invertible, and write $S = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$. If $\sum_i a_i v_i = 0$ then pairing with $v_j$ gives $\sum_i a_i \langle v_j,v_i\rangle = 0$ for each $j$, that is, $Ga = 0$, whence $a = 0$; so the $v_i$ are linearly independent and $\dim S = k$. If $y = \sum_i a_i v_i \in S$ is orthogonal to all of $S$, then in particular $\langle v_j, y\rangle = 0$ for each $j$, which is again $Ga = 0$, so $a = 0$ and $y = 0$. By Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear} the restriction $q|_{S\times S}$ is therefore nondegenerate.

Conversely, suppose $S$ is nondegenerate of dimension $k$. Then $(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ is a basis of $S$, and $G$ is precisely the matrix of the map $\widetilde{q|_S} : S \to S^*$ with respect to that basis and its dual, since $\widetilde{q|_S}(v_i)(v_j) = \langle v_i,v_j\rangle$. Nondegeneracy of $q|_{S \times S}$ says that this map is an isomorphism, so by Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear} its matrix $G$ is invertible.

blocks.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-leading-minors
blocks.title: Nondegeneracy of a Tuple via its Leading Principal Minors
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space and let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be an $n$-tuple in $V$. For $j \leq n$ write $G_j = \bigl(\langle v_i, v_l\rangle\bigr)_{i,l=1}^{j}$ for the Gram matrix of the prefix $(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$. Then $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ is a nondegenerate tuple if and only if $\det G_j \neq 0$ for every $j \leq n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isNondegenerateTuple_iff_forall_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem isNondegenerateTuple_iff_forall_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero (B : BilinForm ℝ V) {n : ℕ}
    (v : Fin n → V) :
    IsNondegenerateTuple B v ↔ ∀ j, ∀ hj : j ≤ n, (gramMatrix B (v ∘ Fin.castLE hj)).det ≠ 0 := by
  constructor
  · intro h j hj
    rw [← isNondegenerate_span_iff_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero B (v ∘ Fin.castLE hj),
      ← prefixSpan_eq_span_range_castLE v hj]
    exact h j hj
  · intro h j hj
    rw [prefixSpan_eq_span_range_castLE v hj]
    exact (isNondegenerate_span_iff_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero B (v ∘ Fin.castLE hj)).mpr (h j hj)

deps.title: Nondegeneracy of a Tuple via its Gram Matrix
deps.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-gram-matrix

blocks.tex: By definition $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ is a nondegenerate tuple exactly when, for every $j \leq n$, the subspace $\operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$ is nondegenerate of dimension $j$. For each fixed $j$ that span is the span of the $j$-tuple $(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$, whose Gram matrix is $G_j$, so Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-tuple-gram-matrix} applied to that $j$-tuple says precisely that the condition at $j$ holds if and only if $G_j$ is invertible, that is, $\det G_j \neq 0$. Conjoining over $j \leq n$ gives the claim.

blocks.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-independent
blocks.title: A Nondegenerate Tuple is Linearly Independent
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space and let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be a nondegenerate $n$-tuple in $V$. Then $v_1,\ldots,v_n$ are linearly independent.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsNondegenerateTuple.linearIndependent
lean.code:
theorem IsNondegenerateTuple.linearIndependent {n : ℕ} {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) : LinearIndependent ℝ v := by
  have h := (hv n le_rfl).1
  rw [prefixSpan_last] at h
  exact linearIndependent_iff_card_eq_finrank_span.mpr (by simpa [Set.finrank] using h.symm)

blocks.tex: By definition the last step of the flag, $\operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$, has dimension $n$. A spanning set of $n$ vectors for an $n$-dimensional space is a basis, hence linearly independent.

blocks.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-basis
blocks.title: A Nondegenerate Tuple of Full Length is a Basis
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be an $n$-dimensional scalar product space and let $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ be a nondegenerate $n$-tuple in $V$. Then $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ is a basis of $V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsNondegenerateTuple.basis
lean.code:
noncomputable def IsNondegenerateTuple.basis [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {n : ℕ} {v : Fin n → V}
    (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) (hn : n = finrank ℝ V) : Basis (Fin n) ℝ V :=
  Basis.mk hv.linearIndependent
    (hv.linearIndependent.span_eq_top_of_card_eq_finrank' (by simpa using hn)).ge

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] in

deps.title: A Nondegenerate Tuple is Linearly Independent
deps.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-independent

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-tuple-independent} the tuple is linearly independent, and it consists of $n = \dim V$ vectors; a linearly independent family of $\dim V$ vectors spans, hence is a basis.

blocks.label: lem:orthonormal-tuple-nondegenerate
blocks.title: An Orthonormal Tuple is Nondegenerate
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space and let $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ be an orthonormal $n$-tuple in $V$. Then $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ is a nondegenerate tuple.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsOrthonormal.isNondegenerateTuple

deps.title: Nondegeneracy of a Tuple via its Leading Principal Minors
deps.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-leading-minors

blocks.tex: Fix $j \leq n$. The prefix $(b_1,\ldots,b_j)$ is again orthonormal, orthonormality being a condition on pairs of members. Its Gram matrix $G_j$ therefore satisfies $(G_j)_{il} = \langle b_i, b_l \rangle = 0$ for $i \neq l$, so $G_j$ is diagonal, with diagonal entries $\langle b_i,b_i\rangle = \pm 1$. Hence $\det G_j = \prod_{i=1}^{j} \langle b_i, b_i \rangle$ is a product of numbers $\pm 1$ and so is nonzero. As $j \leq n$ was arbitrary, Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-tuple-leading-minors} shows the tuple is nondegenerate.

blocks.label: lem:gram-det-smooth
blocks.title: Smoothness of the Leading Gram Determinants
blocks.body:
Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$, let $u \subseteq B$, and let $Y_1,\ldots,Y_k$ be sections of $V$ that are smooth on $u$. Then
\[
x \longmapsto \det \bigl(g_x(Y_i|_x, Y_l|_x)\bigr)_{i,l=1}^{k}
\]
is a smooth real-valued function on $u$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.contMDiffOn_det_gramMatrix
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffOn_det_gramMatrix {k : ℕ} {Y : Fin k → (x : B) → V x}
    (hY : ∀ i, ContMDiffOn IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (T% (Y i)) u) :
    ContMDiffOn IB 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n (fun x => (gramMatrix (g.bilin x) (fun i => Y i x)).det) u := by
  simp_rw [Matrix.det_apply']
  intro x hx
  refine ContMDiffWithinAt.sum fun σ _ => contMDiffWithinAt_const.mul ?_
  exact ContMDiffWithinAt.prod fun i _ =>
    (g.contMDiffOn_pairing contMDiffOn_id (hY (σ i)) (hY i)) x hx

deps.title: Smoothness of the Pairing of Smooth Sections
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth} each entry $x \mapsto g_x(Y_i|_x, Y_l|_x)$ is smooth on $u$. The determinant of a $k \times k$ matrix is the finite sum, over the permutations $\sigma$ of $\{1,\ldots,k\}$, of $\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)$ times the finite product of the entries $(\sigma(i), i)$; finite sums and finite products of smooth real-valued functions are smooth, so the composite is smooth on $u$.

blocks.label: lem:open-nondegenerate-tuple
blocks.title: Nondegeneracy of a Tuple of Smooth Sections is an Open Condition
blocks.body: Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$ with finite-dimensional fibres, let $u \subseteq B$ be open, and let $X_1,\ldots,X_m$ be sections of $V$ that are smooth on $u$. If $(X_1|_{x_0},\ldots,X_m|_{x_0})$ is a nondegenerate $m$-tuple in $(V_{x_0}, g_{x_0})$ for some $x_0 \in u$, then there is an open set $w$ with $x_0 \in w \subseteq u$ such that $(X_1,\ldots,X_m)$ is nondegenerate on $w$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isLocalNondegenerateOn_nhds
lean.code:
theorem exists_isLocalNondegenerateOn_nhds (hu : IsOpen u)
    (hX : ∀ i, ContMDiffOn IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (T% (X i)) u)
    {x₀ : B} (hx₀ : x₀ ∈ u) (h0 : IsNondegenerateTuple (g.bilin x₀) (fun i => X i x₀)) :
    ∃ w : Set B, IsOpen w ∧ x₀ ∈ w ∧ w ⊆ u ∧ IsLocalNondegenerateOn IB F n g X w := by
  rw [isNondegenerateTuple_iff_forall_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero] at h0
  -- each leading Gram determinant is nonzero on a neighbourhood of `x₀`; there are finitely
  -- many, so one neighbourhood serves them all
  have hev : ∀ k ∈ Set.Iic m, ∀ᶠ x in nhds x₀,
      ∀ hk : k ≤ m, (gramMatrix (g.bilin x) (fun i => X (Fin.castLE hk i) x)).det ≠ 0 := by
    intro k hk
    have hk' : k ≤ m := Set.mem_Iic.mp hk
    have hc : ContinuousAt
        (fun x => (gramMatrix (g.bilin x) (fun i => X (Fin.castLE hk' i) x)).det) x₀ :=
      (contMDiffOn_det_gramMatrix g fun i => hX _).continuousOn.continuousAt (hu.mem_nhds hx₀)
    filter_upwards [hc.eventually_ne (h0 k hk')] with x hx _ using hx
  have h := (Filter.eventually_all_finite (Set.finite_Iic m)).mpr hev
  obtain ⟨w, hwsub, hwopen, hx₀w⟩ := mem_nhds_iff.mp (Filter.inter_mem h (hu.mem_nhds hx₀))
  refine ⟨w, hwopen, hx₀w, fun x hx => (hwsub hx).2, ?_, fun i => (hX i).mono ?_⟩
  · intro x hx
    rw [isNondegenerateTuple_iff_forall_det_gramMatrix_ne_zero]
    exact fun k hk => (hwsub hx).1 k (Set.mem_Iic.mpr hk) hk
  · exact fun x hx => (hwsub hx).2

end BundleFrame

section TangentFrame

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]
  {m : ℕ}

deps.title: Nondegeneracy of a Tuple via its Leading Principal Minors
deps.label: lem:nondegenerate-tuple-leading-minors

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

deps.title: Smoothness of the Leading Gram Determinants
deps.label: lem:gram-det-smooth

blocks.tex:
For $k \leq m$ and $x \in u$ let $G_k(x) = \bigl(g_x(X_i|_x, X_j|_x)\bigr)_{i,j=1}^{k}$ be the $k$-th leading principal submatrix of the Gram matrix of the tuple at $x$. By Lemma \ref{lem:gram-det-smooth} the function $x \mapsto \det G_k(x)$ is smooth, in particular continuous, on $u$.

By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product} each $(V_x, g_x)$ is a scalar product space, so Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-tuple-leading-minors} applies in each fibre. Nondegeneracy of the tuple $(X_1|_{x_0},\ldots,X_m|_{x_0})$ therefore says exactly that $\det G_k(x_0) \neq 0$ for every $k \leq m$.

For each $k \leq m$ continuity therefore yields an open set $w_k$ with $x_0 \in w_k \subseteq u$ on which $\det G_k$ does not vanish. Put $w = \bigcap_{k \leq m} w_k$, an intersection of finitely many open sets, so open, and containing $x_0$. For $x \in w$ every $\det G_k(x)$ is nonzero, so by Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-tuple-leading-minors} again the tuple $(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_m|_x)$ is nondegenerate. Together with the smoothness of the $X_i$ on $w \subseteq u$, this is Definition \ref{def:local-nondegenerate-family}.

blocks.label: lem:sections-through-prescribed-basis
blocks.title: Smooth Sections Through a Prescribed Basis
blocks.body: Let $V \to B$ be a smooth vector bundle of rank $m$, let $x_0 \in B$, and let $(w_1,\ldots,w_m)$ be a basis of the fibre $V_{x_0}$. Then there are an open set $v \ni x_0$ and sections $Y_1,\ldots,Y_m$ of $V$, smooth on $v$, with $Y_j|_{x_0} = w_j$ for every $j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.exists_contMDiffOn_section_eq_basis
lean.code:
theorem exists_contMDiffOn_section_eq_basis [ContMDiffVectorBundle n F V I]
    (x₀ : M) (w : Basis ι 𝕜 (V x₀)) :
    ∃ (v : Set M) (Y : ι → (x : M) → V x), IsOpen v ∧ x₀ ∈ v ∧
      (∀ i, ContMDiffOn I (I.prod 𝓘(𝕜, F)) n (fun x => TotalSpace.mk' F x (Y i x)) v) ∧
      ∀ i, Y i x₀ = w i := by
  set e := trivializationAt F V x₀ with he
  have hx₀ : x₀ ∈ e.baseSet := FiberBundle.mem_baseSet_trivializationAt' x₀
  refine ⟨e.baseSet, e.localFrame (w.map (e.linearEquivAt 𝕜 x₀ hx₀)), e.open_baseSet, hx₀,
    fun i => e.contMDiffOn_localFrame_baseSet n _ i, fun i => ?_⟩
  rw [e.localFrame_apply_of_mem_baseSet _ hx₀]
  simp only [Trivialization.basisAt, Basis.map_apply, Trivialization.linearEquivAt_apply,
    Trivialization.linearEquivAt_symm_apply]
  exact e.symm_apply_apply_mk hx₀ (w i)

blocks.tex:
Choose a local trivialization $\Phi$ of $V$ over an open set $v \ni x_0$, with fibrewise linear isomorphisms $\Phi_x : V_x \to F$, and set
\[
Y_j|_x = \Phi_x^{-1}\bigl(\Phi_{x_0}(w_j)\bigr), \qquad x \in v .
\]
Each $Y_j$ is smooth on $v$, being constant in the trivialization $\Phi$, and $Y_j|_{x_0} = \Phi_{x_0}^{-1}(\Phi_{x_0}(w_j)) = w_j$.

blocks.label: prop:orthonormalize-nondegenerate-family
blocks.title: Orthonormalization of a Nondegenerate Family of Vector Fields
blocks.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold, let $u \subseteq M$, and let $(X_1,\ldots,X_m)$ be a family of vector fields that is nondegenerate on $u$. Then there is a family $(Y_1,\ldots,Y_m)$ of vector fields, smooth on $u$, such that for every $x \in u$ the tuple $(Y_1|_x,\ldots,Y_m|_x)$ is orthonormal in $(T_xM,g_x)$ and
\[
\operatorname{span}(Y_1|_x,\ldots,Y_k|_x) = \operatorname{span}(X_1|_x,\ldots,X_k|_x) \qquad \text{for every } k \leq m .
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_pseudo_orthonormalFrame
lean.code:
theorem exists_pseudo_orthonormalFrame (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M)
    {X : Fin m → (x : M) → TangentSpace I x} {u : Set M}
    (hX : IsLocalNondegenerateOn I E ∞ g X u) :
    ∃ Y : Fin m → (x : M) → TangentSpace I x,
      (∀ j, ContMDiffOn I (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E)) ∞ (T% (Y j)) u) ∧
      (∀ x ∈ u, IsOrthonormal (g.bilin x) (fun j => Y j x)) ∧
      (∀ x ∈ u, ∀ k ≤ m, prefixSpan (fun j => Y j x) k = prefixSpan (fun i => X i x) k) :=
  ⟨pseudoGramSchmidtFrame g X,
    contMDiffOn_pseudoGramSchmidtFrame g hX,
    fun x hx => pseudoGramSchmidtFrame_isOrthonormal g hX hx,
    fun x hx k hk => prefixSpan_pseudoGramSchmidtFrame g hX hx k hk⟩

deps.title: The Gram--Schmidt Frame Reproduces the Flag
deps.label: lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-flag

blocks.tex: Take $(Y_1,\ldots,Y_m)$ to be the fibrewise Gram--Schmidt frame of $(X_1,\ldots,X_m)$ of Definition \ref{def:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame}. Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-smooth} gives its smoothness on $u$, Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-orthonormal} its orthonormality at each point of $u$, and Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-gram-schmidt-frame-flag} the equality of spans.

blocks.label: prop:orthonormal-frames-pseudo-riemannian
blocks.title: Orthonormal Frames for Pseudo-Riemannian Manifolds
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold. For each $p \in M$, there exists a smooth orthonormal frame on a neighborhood of $p$ in $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_pseudo_orthonormalFrame_nhds
lean.code:
theorem exists_pseudo_orthonormalFrame_nhds [FiniteDimensional ℝ E]
    (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M) :
    ∃ (u : Set M) (Y : Fin (finrank ℝ E) → (x : M) → TangentSpace I x),
      IsOpen u ∧ p ∈ u ∧ IsLocalFrameOn I E ∞ Y u ∧
      (∀ x ∈ u, IsOrthonormal (g.bilin x) (fun j => Y j x)) := by
  -- a nondegenerate basis of the scalar product space `(T_p M, g_p)`; the index type is
  -- `Fin (finrank ℝ E)` because `TangentSpace I p` is a type synonym for `E`
  obtain ⟨w, hw⟩ : ∃ w : Fin (finrank ℝ E) → TangentSpace I p,
      IsNondegenerateTuple (g.bilin p) w :=
    exists_isNondegenerateTuple (g.bilin_isSymm p) (g.bilin_nondegenerate p)
  have hspan : finrank ℝ (span ℝ (Set.range w)) = finrank ℝ (TangentSpace I p) := by
    rw [← prefixSpan_last w]
    exact (hw _ le_rfl).1
  have hli : LinearIndependent ℝ w :=
    linearIndependent_iff_card_eq_finrank_span.mpr (by simpa [Set.finrank] using hspan.symm)
  let wb : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) ℝ (TangentSpace I p) :=
    Basis.mk hli (Submodule.eq_top_of_finrank_eq hspan).ge
  -- spread it to vector fields near `p`
  obtain ⟨v, Y, hvopen, hpv, hYsmooth, hYeq⟩ :=
    LeeLib.AppendixA.exists_contMDiffOn_section_eq_basis (F := E)
      (V := (TangentSpace I : M → Type _)) I ∞ p wb
  have h0 : IsNondegenerateTuple (g.bilin p) (fun i => Y i p) := by
    have hYw : (fun i => Y i p) = w := by
      funext i
      rw [hYeq i]
      simp [wb, Basis.coe_mk]
    rw [hYw]
    exact hw
  -- nondegeneracy at `p` spreads to a neighbourhood, giving Lee's Proposition 2.63 hypothesis
  obtain ⟨u, huopen, hpu, -, hnd⟩ := exists_isLocalNondegenerateOn_nhds g hvopen hYsmooth hpv h0
  obtain ⟨Z, hZsmooth, hZon, -⟩ := exists_pseudo_orthonormalFrame g hnd
  -- an orthonormal tuple is linearly independent, and `finrank ℝ E` independent vectors span
  -- the `finrank ℝ E`-dimensional space `T_x M`: the frame conditions come for free
  exact ⟨u, Z, huopen, hpu,
    ⟨fun {x} hx => (hZon x hx).linearIndependent,
      fun {x} hx => ((hZon x hx).linearIndependent.span_eq_top_of_card_eq_finrank'
        (Fintype.card_fin _)).ge,
      hZsmooth⟩,
    hZon⟩

deps.title: Nondegeneracy of a Tuple of Smooth Sections is an Open Condition
deps.label: lem:open-nondegenerate-tuple

deps.title: Smooth Sections Through a Prescribed Basis
deps.label: lem:sections-through-prescribed-basis

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

deps.title: Orthonormalization of a Nondegenerate Family of Vector Fields
deps.label: prop:orthonormalize-nondegenerate-family

blocks.tex:
Write $n = \dim M$ and fix $p \in M$. By Definition \ref{def:pseudo-riemannian-metric} and Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product}, $(T_pM, g_p)$ is a scalar product space.

\emph{A nondegenerate basis at $p$.} The empty tuple is nondegenerate, so Lemma \ref{lem:completion-nondegenerate-bases}, applied with $k = 0$, produces a nondegenerate basis $(w_1,\ldots,w_n)$ of $T_pM$.

\emph{Extension to smooth vector fields.} By Lemma \ref{lem:sections-through-prescribed-basis} applied to the tangent bundle there are an open set $v \ni p$ and vector fields $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n$, smooth on $v$, with $Y_j|_p = w_j$.

\emph{Shrinking to a nondegenerate family.} The tuple $(Y_1|_p,\ldots,Y_n|_p) = (w_1,\ldots,w_n)$ is nondegenerate in $(T_pM,g_p)$, so Lemma \ref{lem:open-nondegenerate-tuple} yields an open set $v'$ with $p \in v' \subseteq v$ such that $(Y_1,\ldots,Y_n)$ is nondegenerate on $v'$ in the sense of Definition \ref{def:local-nondegenerate-family}.

\emph{Orthonormalization.} Proposition \ref{prop:orthonormalize-nondegenerate-family}, applied to $(Y_1,\ldots,Y_n)$ on $v'$, yields vector fields $(E_1,\ldots,E_n)$, smooth on $v'$, with $(E_1|_x,\ldots,E_n|_x)$ orthonormal in $(T_xM, g_x)$ for every $x \in v'$. An orthonormal tuple is linearly independent, and $n$ linearly independent vectors in the $n$-dimensional space $T_xM$ form a basis; so $(E_1,\ldots,E_n)$ is a smooth orthonormal frame on the neighborhood $v'$ of $p$.
This is the content of Proposition \ref{prop:orthonormal-frames-pseudo-riemannian}, proved above.

blocks.label: def:lorentz-metric
blocks.title: Lorentz Metric
blocks.body: A \emph{Lorentz metric} is a pseudo-Riemannian metric of index $1$; that is, one having signature $(r,1)$ for some $r \geq 0$. A \emph{Lorentz manifold} is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold whose metric is a Lorentz metric.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsLorentzMetric
lean.code:
def IsLorentzMetric (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) : Prop :=
  ∃ r : ℕ, HasSignature g r 1

blocks.tex:
By Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-metric-signature-sum} the integer $r$ is determined by the manifold: a Lorentz metric on an $n$-manifold has signature $(n-1,1)$. (Some authors, especially in the physics literature, prefer to use signature $(1, r)$; either one can be converted to the other by multiplying the metric by $-1$, so there is no significant difference.)

The pseudo-Euclidean metric $\bar{g}^{(r,1)}$ is a Lorentz metric called the \textbf{Minkowski metric}, and the Lorentz manifold $\mathbb{R}^{r,1}$ is called $(r+1)$-\textbf{dimensional Minkowski space}. If we denote standard coordinates on $\mathbb{R}^{r,1}$ by $(\xi^1, \ldots, \xi^r, \tau)$, then the Minkowski metric is given by
\begin{equation}
\bar{g}^{(r,1)} = (d\xi^1)^2 + \cdots + (d\xi^r)^2 - (d\tau)^2.
\tag{2.25}
\end{equation}

In the special case of $\mathbb{R}^{3,1}$, the Minkowski metric is the fundamental invariant of Albert Einstein's \textit{special theory of relativity}, which can be expressed succinctly by saying that if gravity is ignored, then spacetime is accurately modeled by $(3+1)$-dimensional Minkowski space, and the laws of physics have the same form in every coordinate system in which the Minkowski metric has the expression (2.25). The differing physical characteristics of ``space'' (the $\xi^i$ directions) and ``time'' (the $\tau$ direction) arise from the fact that they are subspaces on which the metric is positive definite and negative definite, respectively. The \textit{general theory of relativity} includes gravitational effects by allowing the Lorentz metric to vary from point to point.

Many, but not all, results from the theory of Riemannian metrics apply equally well to pseudo-Riemannian metrics. Throughout this book, we will attempt to point out which results carry over directly to pseudo-Riemannian metrics, which ones can be adapted with minor modifications, and which ones do not carry over at all. As a rule of thumb, proofs that depend only on the nondegeneracy of the metric tensor, such as properties of the musical isomorphisms and existence and uniqueness of geodesics, work fine in the pseudo-Riemannian setting, while proofs that use positivity in an essential way, such as those involving lengths of curves, do not.

One notable result that does \emph{not} carry over to the pseudo-Riemannian case is Proposition~2.4, about the existence of metrics. For example, the following result characterizes those manifolds that admit Lorentz metrics.

The two halves of Theorem \ref{thm:lorentz-metrics} are of very different character, so they are recorded separately. Sufficiency is constructive: a rank-1 distribution and an auxiliary Riemannian metric produce a Lorentz metric by flipping the sign of the metric along the distribution. Necessity runs the other way: recovering a distribution from a Lorentz metric $\bar g$ means selecting, smoothly in $x$, the negative-eigenvalue eigenline of $\bar g_x$ relative to an auxiliary Riemannian metric.

That such a selection is smooth is usually quoted as a perturbation-theoretic fact about simple eigenvalues. It need not be. The eigen-equation $Sv = \lambda v$, together with a normalization $\langle v_0, v\rangle = 1$ pinning down the scale, is a system of $\dim V + 1$ equations in the $\dim V + 1$ unknowns $(v,\lambda)$, depending on the operator $S$ as a parameter; Definition \ref{def:augmented-eigen-equation} packages it as a single map $\Phi_{v_0}$ carrying $S$ along unchanged. Simplicity of the eigenvalue is exactly the invertibility of the derivative of that map (Lemma \ref{lem:eigen-linearisation-injective}), so the inverse function theorem alone yields the selection and its smoothness (Proposition \ref{prop:smooth-eigen-selection}). No spectral projection is constructed, and no operator square root, contour integral or functional calculus is needed.

The pointwise content of sufficiency is the following sign flip, which is Lee's own recipe in Problem \ref{prob:lorentz-metric-existence}(a) written invariantly: for a $g$-orthonormal frame with $E_1$ spanning $D$ and dual coframe $(\varepsilon^i)$, the form $-(\varepsilon^1)^2 + (\varepsilon^2)^2 + \cdots + (\varepsilon^n)^2$ equals $\langle v,w\rangle - 2\langle v,E_1\rangle\langle w,E_1\rangle$.

blocks.label: lem:lorentz-form-pointwise
blocks.title: Flipping the sign of a scalar product along a unit vector
blocks.body:
Let $(V, \langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)$ be an inner product space with $\dim V = n+1$, and let $u \in V$ be a unit vector. Then
\[
  B(v,w) = \langle v,w\rangle - 2\langle v,u\rangle\langle w,u\rangle
\]
is symmetric and nondegenerate, and has signature $(n,1)$. Moreover $B$ depends on $u$ only through $\operatorname{span}(u)$: replacing $u$ by $-u$ leaves $B$ unchanged.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.lorentzForm_isSymm_nondegenerate_signature
lean.code:
theorem lorentzForm_isSymm_nondegenerate_signature (hu : ‖u‖ = 1)
    (hdim : finrank ℝ V = n + 1) :
    (lorentzForm u).IsSymm ∧ (lorentzForm u).Nondegenerate ∧
      sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (lorentzForm u)) = n ∧
      sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (lorentzForm u)) = 1 := by
  obtain ⟨b, hb⟩ := exists_orthonormalBasis_head hu hdim
  exact ⟨lorentzForm_isSymm u,
    LinearMap.BilinForm.Nondegenerate.ofSeparatingLeft (lorentzForm_separatingLeft hu),
    sigPos_lorentzForm hu hb hdim, sigNeg_lorentzForm hb⟩

blocks.tex:
Symmetry is immediate from symmetry of $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$, and the invariance under $u \mapsto -u$ holds because $u$ occurs exactly twice in the formula, so the two sign changes cancel.

For nondegeneracy, write $R w = w - 2\langle w,u\rangle u$, so that $B(v,w) = \langle v, Rw\rangle$. Since $\langle u,u\rangle = 1$, we get $\langle Rw, u\rangle = \langle w,u\rangle - 2\langle w,u\rangle = -\langle w,u\rangle$, whence
\[
  R(Rw) = Rw - 2\langle Rw,u\rangle u = w - 2\langle w,u\rangle u + 2\langle w,u\rangle u = w,
\]
so $R$ is an involution. If $B(v,w) = 0$ for all $w$, then taking $w = Rv$ gives $0 = \langle v, R(Rv)\rangle = \langle v,v\rangle$, so $v = 0$.

For the signature, extend $u$ to an orthonormal basis $(e_0, \ldots, e_n)$ of $V$ with $e_0 = u$. Then $\langle e_i, u\rangle = \delta_{i0}$, so
\[
  B(e_i, e_j) = \delta_{ij} - 2\delta_{i0}\delta_{j0},
\]
which is $-1$ when $i = j = 0$, is $1$ when $i = j \neq 0$, and vanishes when $i \neq j$. So $(e_i)$ is an orthonormal basis for $B$ in the indefinite sense, with exactly one diagonal entry equal to $-1$. By Sylvester's law of inertia (Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia}) the index of $B$ is therefore $1$, and since $B$ is nondegenerate its radical is trivial, so the number of positive terms is $\dim V - 1 = n$. Hence $B$ has signature $(n,1)$.

blocks.label: def:rank-one-distribution
blocks.title: Rank-1 tangent distribution
blocks.body: A \emph{rank-1 tangent distribution} on a smooth manifold $M$ is a family $D = (D_x)_{x \in M}$ of subspaces $D_x \subseteq T_xM$ such that every point of $M$ has a neighbourhood $U$ carrying a smooth vector field $X$ with $X_x \neq 0$ and $D_x = \operatorname{span}(X_x)$ for every $x \in U$. Each $D_x$ is then a line, so $D$ is a field of lines in $TM$.

blocks.label: prop:rank-one-distribution-is-subbundle
blocks.title: A rank-1 distribution is a rank-1 subbundle
blocks.body: Let $D$ be a rank-1 tangent distribution on a smooth manifold $M$. Then $D$ satisfies the hypothesis of the subbundle criterion (Lemma \ref{lem:local-frame-criterion-for-subbundles}) with $k = 1$, and is therefore a smooth rank-1 subbundle of $TM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.hasLocalSubframes_of_isRankOneDistribution
lean.code:
theorem hasLocalSubframes_of_isRankOneDistribution (g : RiemannianMetric I M)
    (hD : IsRankOneDistribution I M D) :
    letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    LeeLib.AppendixA.HasLocalSubframes I E ∞ 1 D := by
  letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  refine ⟨fun p ↦ ?_⟩
  obtain ⟨v, X, hv, hpv, hX, hspan⟩ := hD.exists_localSpan p
  refine ⟨v, fun _ ↦ X, hv, hpv, ⟨fun {x} hx ↦ ?_, fun _ ↦ hX⟩, fun {x} hx ↦ ?_⟩
  · exact linearIndependent_unique_iff.2 (hspan x hx).1
  · rw [Set.range_const]
    exact (hspan x hx).2

deps.title: Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric
deps.label: prop:existence-riemannian-metric

blocks.tex: The criterion asks that every point have a neighbourhood on which $D$ is spanned by $k$ smooth sections of $TM$ that are linearly independent at each point. Take $k=1$ and let $U$ and $X$ be as in Definition \ref{def:rank-one-distribution}. A family consisting of a single vector is linearly independent exactly when that vector is nonzero, which holds on $U$; and the span of the range of the one-element family $(X)$ is $\operatorname{span}(X_x) = D_x$ for $x \in U$. So the hypothesis holds, and the criterion makes $D$ a smooth rank-1 vector bundle over $M$.

blocks.label: def:tensor-product-one-forms
blocks.title: Tensor product of two $1$-forms
blocks.body: For covectors $\alpha, \beta \in V^*$, the bilinear form $\alpha \otimes \beta$ on $V$ is $(\alpha \otimes \beta)(v,w) = \alpha(v)\,\beta(w)$. For $1$-forms $\alpha, \beta$ on $M$ this is applied fibrewise, giving a field of bilinear forms $x \mapsto \alpha_x \otimes \beta_x$.

lean.name: Bundle.formProduct
lean.code:
noncomputable def formProduct (α β : ∀ x : B, E x →L[ℝ] ℝ) (x : B) :
    E x →L[ℝ] E x →L[ℝ] ℝ :=
  (α x).smulRight (β x)

omit [TopologicalSpace B] [∀ b, IsTopologicalAddGroup (E b)] [∀ b, ContinuousConstSMul ℝ (E b)] in

blocks.label: lem:tensor-product-one-forms-smooth
blocks.title: The tensor product of two smooth $1$-forms is smooth
blocks.body: Let $E \to M$ be a smooth vector bundle and let $\alpha, \beta$ be smooth sections of its dual bundle. Then $x \mapsto \alpha_x \otimes \beta_x$ is a smooth section of the bundle of bilinear forms on $E$.

lean.name: Bundle.contMDiffAt_formProduct
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffAt_formProduct {α β : ∀ x : B, E x →L[ℝ] ℝ} {x₀ : B}
    (hα : ContMDiffAt IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] ℝ)) n
      (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) (E := fun x ↦ E x →L[ℝ] Bundle.Trivial B ℝ x) x (α x)) x₀)
    (hβ : ContMDiffAt IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] ℝ)) n
      (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) (E := fun x ↦ E x →L[ℝ] Bundle.Trivial B ℝ x) x (β x)) x₀) :
    ContMDiffAt IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ)) n
      (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' (F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ)
        (E := fun x ↦ E x →L[ℝ] E x →L[ℝ] ℝ) x (formProduct α β x)) x₀ := by
  rw [contMDiffAt_hom_bundle]
  refine ⟨contMDiffAt_id, ?_⟩
  -- the coordinate representations of `α` and `β`, smooth by hypothesis
  set A : B → (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) := fun x ↦
    ContinuousLinearMap.inCoordinates F E ℝ (Bundle.Trivial B ℝ) x₀ x x₀ x (α x) with hAdef
  set A' : B → (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) := fun x ↦
    ContinuousLinearMap.inCoordinates F E ℝ (Bundle.Trivial B ℝ) x₀ x x₀ x (β x) with hA'def
  have hAs : ContMDiffAt IB 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] ℝ) n A x₀ := ((contMDiffAt_hom_bundle _).mp hα).2
  have hA's : ContMDiffAt IB 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] ℝ) n A' x₀ := ((contMDiffAt_hom_bundle _).mp hβ).2
  -- the model-space candidate `ξ ↦ A ξ ⊗ A' ξ`, smooth because `smulRight` is bilinear.
  -- Note this cannot go through `ContMDiffAt.clm_apply` on the bundled `smulRightL`: that
  -- would need the model space `𝓘(ℝ, (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) →L[ℝ] (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ)`,
  -- and mathlib's operator-norm instance does not reach a `NormedAddCommGroup` there (it
  -- stops once the *domain* is itself an operator space).  Composing the bounded bilinear
  -- map on the outside keeps every type at depth ≤ 2, where the instances do exist.
  have hpair : ContMDiffAt IB 𝓘(ℝ, (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) × (F →L[ℝ] ℝ)) n (fun x ↦ (A x, A' x)) x₀ :=
    hAs.prodMk_space hA's
  have hbil : ContDiff ℝ n (fun p : (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) × (F →L[ℝ] ℝ) ↦ p.1.smulRight p.2) :=
    ContDiff.smulRight contDiff_fst contDiff_snd
  have hcand : ContMDiffAt IB 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ) n
      (fun x ↦ (A x).smulRight (A' x)) x₀ :=
    hbil.contDiffAt.comp_contMDiffAt hpair
  refine hcand.congr_of_eventuallyEq ?_
  -- on the base set of the trivialization the two agree, by `inCoordinates_apply_eq₂`
  filter_upwards [(trivializationAt F E x₀).open_baseSet.mem_nhds
    (mem_baseSet_trivializationAt F E x₀)] with x hx
  refine ContinuousLinearMap.ext fun ξ ↦ ContinuousLinearMap.ext fun η ↦ ?_
  rw [inCoordinates_apply_eq₂ (E₃ := Bundle.Trivial B ℝ) hx hx (by simp)]
  simp [hAdef, hA'def, inCoordinates_dual_apply hx, Bundle.Trivial.eq_trivialization B ℝ _]

blocks.tex:
Smoothness is local, so it may be checked in a local trivialization $\Lambda$ of $E$ over a neighbourhood of a given point. In that trivialization a bilinear form $\varphi_x$ on $E_x$ is represented by $(\xi,\eta) \mapsto \varphi_x(\Lambda_x^{-1}\xi, \Lambda_x^{-1}\eta)$ and a covector $\alpha_x$ by $\hat\alpha_x \colon \xi \mapsto \alpha_x(\Lambda_x^{-1}\xi)$. Since
\[
  (\alpha_x \otimes \beta_x)(\Lambda_x^{-1}\xi, \Lambda_x^{-1}\eta) = \alpha_x(\Lambda_x^{-1}\xi)\,\beta_x(\Lambda_x^{-1}\eta) = (\hat\alpha_x \otimes \hat\beta_x)(\xi,\eta),
\]
the representation of $\alpha \otimes \beta$ is the tensor product $\hat\alpha \otimes \hat\beta$ of the representations of $\alpha$ and $\beta$. By hypothesis $\hat\alpha$ and $\hat\beta$ are smooth maps into the fixed model dual space, and the tensor product $(a,b) \mapsto a \otimes b$ is a bounded bilinear map between fixed normed spaces, hence smooth; so $\hat\alpha \otimes \hat\beta$ is smooth, and therefore so is $\alpha \otimes \beta$.

blocks.label: def:sign-flipped-form
blocks.title: The metric with its sign flipped along a vector field
blocks.body:
Let $b$ be a field of bilinear forms on a vector bundle $E \to M$ and $u$ a section of $E$. Write $b(u,\cdot)$ for the $1$-form $v \mapsto b(u,v)$. The \emph{sign flip of $b$ along $u$} is the field of bilinear forms
\[
  \flip(b,u) = b - 2\, b(u,\cdot) \otimes b(u,\cdot), \qquad \flip(b,u)_x(v,w) = b_x(v,w) - 2\,b_x(u_x,v)\,b_x(u_x,w).
\]

lean.name: Bundle.flipFormOf
lean.code:
noncomputable def flipFormOf (b : ∀ x : B, E x →L[ℝ] E x →L[ℝ] ℝ) (u : ∀ x : B, E x) :
    ∀ x : B, E x →L[ℝ] E x →L[ℝ] ℝ :=
  b - (2 : ℝ) • formProduct (fun y ↦ b y (u y)) (fun y ↦ b y (u y))

omit [TopologicalSpace B] [∀ b, IsTopologicalAddGroup (E b)]
  [∀ b, ContinuousConstSMul ℝ (E b)] in

blocks.label: lem:sign-flipped-form-smooth
blocks.title: The sign flip of a smooth form along a smooth section is smooth
blocks.body: If $b$ is a smooth field of bilinear forms on $E \to M$ and $u$ is a smooth section of $E$, then $\flip(b,u)$ is a smooth field of bilinear forms.

lean.name: Bundle.contMDiffAt_flipFormOf
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffAt_flipFormOf {b : ∀ x : B, E x →L[ℝ] E x →L[ℝ] ℝ} {u : ∀ x : B, E x} {x₀ : B}
    (hb : ContMDiffAt IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ)) n
      (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' (F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ)
        (E := fun x ↦ E x →L[ℝ] E x →L[ℝ] ℝ) x (b x)) x₀)
    (hu : ContMDiffAt IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' F x (u x)) x₀) :
    ContMDiffAt IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ)) n
      (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' (F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ)
        (E := fun x ↦ E x →L[ℝ] E x →L[ℝ] ℝ) x (flipFormOf b u x)) x₀ := by
  have hα : ContMDiffAt IB (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F →L[ℝ] ℝ)) n
      (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' (F →L[ℝ] ℝ)
        (E := fun x ↦ E x →L[ℝ] Bundle.Trivial B ℝ x) x (b x (u x))) x₀ :=
    hb.clm_bundle_apply hu
  exact hb.sub_section (contMDiffAt_formProduct hα hα).const_smul_section

omit [TopologicalSpace B] [∀ b, IsTopologicalAddGroup (E b)] [∀ b, ContinuousConstSMul ℝ (E b)]
  [FiberBundle F E] [VectorBundle ℝ F E] [TopologicalSpace (TotalSpace F E)] in

deps.title: The metric with its sign flipped along a vector field
deps.label: def:sign-flipped-form

deps.title: The tensor product of two smooth $1$-forms is smooth
deps.label: lem:tensor-product-one-forms-smooth

blocks.tex: The $1$-form $b(u,\cdot)$ is smooth, being a smooth field of bilinear forms applied to a smooth section. Its tensor square is smooth by Lemma \ref{lem:tensor-product-one-forms-smooth}, and the smooth sections of a vector bundle are closed under subtraction and multiplication by constants, so $b - 2\,b(u,\cdot)\otimes b(u,\cdot)$ is smooth.

blocks.label: lem:two-unit-vectors-of-a-line
blocks.title: A line contains exactly two unit vectors
blocks.body: Let $(V, \langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)$ be an inner product space and let $u, u' \in V$ satisfy $\langle u,u\rangle = \langle u',u'\rangle = 1$ and $\operatorname{span}(u') = \operatorname{span}(u)$. Then $u' = u$ or $u' = -u$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.eq_or_eq_neg_of_span_eq
lean.code:
theorem eq_or_eq_neg_of_span_eq (g : RiemannianMetric I M) {x : M} {u u' : TangentSpace I x}
    (hu : g.inner x u u = 1) (hu' : g.inner x u' u' = 1) (h : span ℝ {u'} = span ℝ {u}) :
    u' = u ∨ u' = -u := by
  obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := mem_span_singleton.mp (h ▸ mem_span_singleton_self u')
  have hcc : c * c = 1 := by
    rw [← hc] at hu'
    simpa [map_smul, hu] using hu'
  rcases mul_self_eq_one_iff.mp hcc with rfl | rfl
  · exact Or.inl (by simpa using hc.symm)
  · exact Or.inr (by simpa using hc.symm)

end Distribution

section Existence

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]
  [T2Space M] [SigmaCompactSpace M]

open QuadraticMap QuadraticForm

variable (I M) in

blocks.tex: Since $u' \in \operatorname{span}(u)$ we have $u' = cu$ for some $c \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $1 = \langle u',u'\rangle = c^2\langle u,u\rangle = c^2$, so $c = \pm 1$.

blocks.label: lem:local-unit-section
blocks.title: Smooth local unit sections of a rank-1 distribution
blocks.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and $D$ a rank-1 tangent distribution on $M$. Every $p \in M$ has a neighbourhood $U$ carrying a smooth vector field $E$ with $\langle E_x, E_x\rangle_g = 1$ and $\operatorname{span}(E_x) = D_x$ for every $x \in U$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsRankOneDistribution.exists_localUnitSection
lean.code:
theorem IsRankOneDistribution.exists_localUnitSection (g : RiemannianMetric I M)
    (hD : IsRankOneDistribution I M D) (p : M) :
    ∃ (v : Set M) (U : ∀ x : M, TangentSpace I x), IsOpen v ∧ p ∈ v ∧
      ContMDiffOn I (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E)) ∞ (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' E x (U x)) v ∧
      ∀ x ∈ v, g.inner x (U x) (U x) = 1 ∧ span ℝ {U x} = D x := by
  obtain ⟨v, X, hv, hpv, hX, hspan⟩ := hD.exists_localSpan p
  have hpos : ∀ x ∈ v, 0 < g.inner x (X x) (X x) := fun x hx => g.pos x (X x) (hspan x hx).1
  refine ⟨v, fun x ↦ gNormalize g (X x), hv, hpv, ?_, ?_⟩
  · -- smoothness: a positive smooth function under a square root, then `smul_section`
    have hq : ContMDiffOn I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞ (fun x ↦ g.inner x (X x) (X x)) v :=
      g.toPseudoRiemannianMetric.contMDiffOn_pairing (b := id) contMDiffOn_id hX hX
    have hinv : ContMDiffOn I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
        (fun x ↦ (Real.sqrt (g.inner x (X x) (X x)))⁻¹) v := by
      have hsqrt : ContMDiffOn I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
          (fun x ↦ Real.sqrt (g.inner x (X x) (X x))) v := fun x hx =>
        ContDiffAt.comp_contMDiffWithinAt (g := Real.sqrt)
          (Real.contDiffAt_sqrt (hpos x hx).ne') (hq x hx)
      exact hsqrt.inv₀ fun x hx => Real.sqrt_ne_zero'.2 (hpos x hx)
    exact hinv.smul_section hX
  · intro x hx
    obtain ⟨hne, hsp⟩ := hspan x hx
    exact ⟨gNormalize_inner_self g hne, (span_gNormalize g hne).trans hsp⟩

blocks.tex: Let $U$ and $X$ be as in Definition \ref{def:rank-one-distribution}, so $X$ is smooth and nowhere zero on $U$ and spans $D$ there. The function $x \mapsto \langle X_x, X_x\rangle_g$ is smooth on $U$, and strictly positive because $g$ is positive definite and $X$ is nowhere zero; hence $x \mapsto \langle X_x, X_x\rangle_g^{-1/2}$ is smooth on $U$. Therefore $E = X/|X|_g$ is smooth on $U$, and $\langle E,E\rangle_g = \langle X,X\rangle_g / |X|_g^2 = 1$. Finally $E_x$ is a nonzero scalar multiple of $X_x$, so $\operatorname{span}(E_x) = \operatorname{span}(X_x) = D_x$.

blocks.label: prop:lorentz-metric-of-rank-one-distribution
blocks.title: Sufficiency in Theorem \ref{thm:lorentz-metrics}
blocks.body: If a smooth manifold $M$ admits a rank-1 tangent distribution $D \subseteq TM$, then $M$ admits a Lorentz metric.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isLorentzMetric_of_isRankOneDistribution
lean.code:
theorem exists_isLorentzMetric_of_isRankOneDistribution
    {D : ∀ x : M, Submodule ℝ (TangentSpace I x)} (hD : IsRankOneDistribution I M D) :
    ∃ gL : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M, IsLorentzMetric gL := by
  obtain ⟨g⟩ := exists_riemannianMetric (I := I) (M := M)
  -- The chosen unit section.  Pointwise, non-continuous, and that is fine.
  set u : ∀ x : M, TangentSpace I x :=
    fun x ↦ gNormalize g (Classical.choose (hD.exists_ne_zero x)) with hudef
  have hu_unit : ∀ x : M, g.inner x (u x) (u x) = 1 := fun x ↦
    gNormalize_inner_self g (Classical.choose_spec (hD.exists_ne_zero x)).2
  have hu_span : ∀ x : M, span ℝ {u x} = D x := fun x ↦
    (span_gNormalize g (Classical.choose_spec (hD.exists_ne_zero x)).2).trans
      (hD.span_singleton_eq (Classical.choose_spec (hD.exists_ne_zero x)).1
        (Classical.choose_spec (hD.exists_ne_zero x)).2)
  -- Symmetry, from symmetry of `g`.
  have hsymm : ∀ (x : M) (v w : TangentSpace I x),
      Bundle.flipFormOf (fun y ↦ g.inner y) u x v w
        = Bundle.flipFormOf (fun y ↦ g.inner y) u x w v := by
    intro x v w
    simp only [Bundle.flipFormOf_apply]
    rw [g.symm x v w]
    ring
  -- Smoothness, proved *before* any fibrewise inner product is installed.
  have hsmooth : ContMDiff I (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E →L[ℝ] E →L[ℝ] ℝ)) ∞
      (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' (E →L[ℝ] E →L[ℝ] ℝ)
        (E := fun x ↦ TangentSpace I x →L[ℝ] TangentSpace I x →L[ℝ] ℝ) x
        (Bundle.flipFormOf (fun y ↦ g.inner y) u x)) := by
    intro p
    obtain ⟨v, U, hv, hpv, hU, hUprop⟩ := hD.exists_localUnitSection g p
    have hUat : ContMDiffAt I (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E)) ∞ (fun x ↦ TotalSpace.mk' E x (U x)) p :=
      hU.contMDiffAt (hv.mem_nhds hpv)
    refine (Bundle.contMDiffAt_flipFormOf (F := E) (E := (TangentSpace I : M → Type _))
      g.contMDiff.contMDiffAt hUat).congr_of_eventuallyEq ?_
    filter_upwards [hv.mem_nhds hpv] with x hx
    refine congrArg (TotalSpace.mk' (E →L[ℝ] E →L[ℝ] ℝ) x) ?_
    exact Bundle.flipFormOf_apply_congr (eq_or_eq_neg_of_span_eq g (hUprop x hx).1 (hu_unit x)
      ((hu_span x).trans (hUprop x hx).2.symm))
  -- The fibrewise inner product coming from `g`, for the pointwise linear algebra only.
  letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  have hbridge : ∀ (x : M) (v w : TangentSpace I x),
      Bundle.flipFormOf (fun y ↦ g.inner y) u x v w = lorentzForm (u x) v w := by
    intro x v w
    have h1 : (⟪v, w⟫ : ℝ) = g.inner x v w := rfl
    have h2 : (⟪v, u x⟫ : ℝ) = g.inner x v (u x) := rfl
    have h3 : (⟪w, u x⟫ : ℝ) = g.inner x w (u x) := rfl
    simp only [Bundle.flipFormOf_apply, lorentzForm_apply, h1, h2, h3]
    rw [g.symm x (u x) v, g.symm x (u x) w]
  have hnorm : ∀ x : M, ‖u x‖ = 1 := by
    intro x
    have h : (⟪u x, u x⟫ : ℝ) = 1 := hu_unit x
    rw [real_inner_self_eq_norm_sq] at h
    nlinarith [norm_nonneg (u x)]
  have hnondeg : ∀ (x : M) (v : TangentSpace I x), v ≠ 0 →
      ∃ w, Bundle.flipFormOf (fun y ↦ g.inner y) u x v w ≠ 0 := by
    intro x v hv
    by_contra hc
    push Not at hc
    exact hv (lorentzForm_separatingLeft (hnorm x) v fun w ↦ by
      rw [← hbridge x v w]; exact hc w)
  set gL : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M :=
    { form := Bundle.flipFormOf (fun y ↦ g.inner y) u
  -- … (truncated)

deps.title: The sign flip of a smooth form along a smooth section is smooth
deps.label: lem:sign-flipped-form-smooth

deps.title: A line contains exactly two unit vectors
deps.label: lem:two-unit-vectors-of-a-line

deps.title: The metric with its sign flipped along a vector field
deps.label: def:sign-flipped-form

deps.title: Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric
deps.label: prop:existence-riemannian-metric

deps.title: Smooth local unit sections of a rank-1 distribution
deps.label: lem:local-unit-section

deps.title: Flipping the sign of a scalar product along a unit vector
deps.label: lem:lorentz-form-pointwise

blocks.tex:
Choose a Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$ (Proposition \ref{prop:existence-riemannian-metric}). For each $x \in M$ the fibre $D_x$ is a line in $T_xM$, so it contains exactly two $g$-unit vectors, $\pm u_x$ (Lemma \ref{lem:two-unit-vectors-of-a-line}); choose one of them for each $x$, with no continuity required of the choice. Define
\[
  \bar{g}_x(v,w) = g_x(v,w) - 2\,g_x(v,u_x)\,g_x(w,u_x),
\]
that is, $\bar g = \flip(g,u)$ in the notation of Definition \ref{def:sign-flipped-form}.

By Lemma \ref{lem:lorentz-form-pointwise} the value of $\bar g_x$ does not depend on which of the two unit vectors is called $u_x$, so $\bar{g}$ is independent of the choice; and each $\bar{g}_x$ is symmetric, nondegenerate, and of signature $(n,1)$, where $n+1 = \dim M$. Note $\dim M \geq 1$, since $T_xM$ contains the line $D_x$. In particular $\bar{g}$ has index $1$ at every point.

It remains to check that $\bar{g}$ is smooth, which is a local question. Near any $x_0$, Lemma \ref{lem:local-unit-section} provides a smooth $g$-unit section $E$ of $D$ on a neighbourhood $U$ of $x_0$. For every $x \in U$ both $E_x$ and $u_x$ are $g$-unit vectors spanning the line $D_x$, so $E_x = \pm u_x$ by Lemma \ref{lem:two-unit-vectors-of-a-line}, and hence $\bar g_x = \flip(g,E)_x$ by the sign-independence just established. Thus $\bar g$ agrees on $U$ with $\flip(g,E)$, which is smooth by Lemma \ref{lem:sign-flipped-form-smooth}; so $\bar g$ is smooth at $x_0$. Hence $\bar{g}$ is a Lorentz metric.

blocks.label: def:simple-eigenpair
blocks.title: Simple eigenpair
blocks.body: Let $(V,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)$ be a finite-dimensional real inner product space, $S : V \to V$ linear, $v \in V$ and $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$. The triple $(S,v,\lambda)$ is a \emph{simple eigenpair} if $S$ is symmetric, $v \neq 0$, $Sv = \lambda v$, and every $w \in V$ with $Sw = \lambda w$ lies in $\operatorname{span}(v)$; that is, $\ker(S - \lambda\,\mathrm{id}) = \operatorname{span}(v)$.

blocks.label: def:eigen-linearisation
blocks.title: Linearisation of the eigen-equation
blocks.body:
For $S : V \to V$ linear, $v, v_0 \in V$ and $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, let $L_{S,v,\lambda,v_0} : V \times \mathbb{R} \to V \times \mathbb{R}$ be the linear map
\[
  L_{S,v,\lambda,v_0}(w,\mu) = \bigl(Sw - \lambda w - \mu v,\ \langle v_0, w\rangle\bigr).
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.eigenLin
lean.code:
noncomputable def eigenLin (S : E →L[ℝ] E) (v : E) (l : ℝ) (v₀ : E) : (E × ℝ) →L[ℝ] (E × ℝ) :=
  ((S - l • ContinuousLinearMap.id ℝ E).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.fst ℝ E ℝ)
      - (ContinuousLinearMap.snd ℝ E ℝ).smulRight v).prod
    ((innerSL ℝ v₀).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.fst ℝ E ℝ))

@[simp]

blocks.label: lem:eigen-linearisation-injective
blocks.title: Simplicity makes the linearisation injective
blocks.body: Let $(S,v,\lambda)$ be a simple eigenpair and let $v_0 \in V$ satisfy $\langle v_0, v\rangle = 1$. Then $L_{S,v,\lambda,v_0}$ is injective, hence bijective.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.eigenLin_injective
lean.code:
theorem eigenLin_injective (h : IsSimpleEigenpair S v l) {v₀ : E} (hv₀ : ⟪v₀, v⟫ = 1) :
    Function.Injective (eigenLin S v l v₀) := by
  intro p q hpq
  have : p - q = 0 :=
    eigenLin_eq_zero h hv₀ (by rw [map_sub, hpq, sub_self])
  rwa [sub_eq_zero] at this

/-! ### The augmented map and its derivative -/

blocks.tex:
Suppose $L_{S,v,\lambda,v_0}(w,\mu) = 0$, that is $Sw - \lambda w = \mu v$ and $\langle v_0, w\rangle = 0$. Pair the first equation with $v$: since $S$ is symmetric and $Sv = \lambda v$,
\[
  \langle Sw - \lambda w, v\rangle = \langle w, Sv\rangle - \lambda\langle w, v\rangle = \lambda\langle w,v\rangle - \lambda\langle w,v\rangle = 0 ,
\]
while the right-hand side gives $\langle \mu v, v\rangle = \mu\langle v,v\rangle$. As $v \neq 0$ we have $\langle v,v\rangle \neq 0$, so $\mu = 0$. Then $Sw = \lambda w$, so simplicity puts $w = cv$ for some $c \in \mathbb{R}$, and $0 = \langle v_0, w\rangle = c\langle v_0, v\rangle = c$. Hence $w = 0$ and $(w,\mu) = 0$. Injectivity of a linear self-map of the finite-dimensional space $V \times \mathbb{R}$ gives bijectivity.

blocks.label: def:augmented-eigen-equation
blocks.title: Augmented eigen-equation
blocks.body:
For $v_0 \in V$ let $\Phi_{v_0} : \operatorname{End}(V) \times V \times \mathbb{R} \to \operatorname{End}(V) \times V \times \mathbb{R}$ be
\[
  \Phi_{v_0}(S, v, \lambda) = \bigl(S,\ Sv - \lambda v,\ \langle v_0, v\rangle - 1\bigr).
\]
Thus $\Phi_{v_0}(S,v,\lambda) = (S,0,0)$ says exactly that $v$ is an eigenvector of $S$ with eigenvalue $\lambda$, normalized by $\langle v_0, v\rangle = 1$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.eigenAug
lean.code:
noncomputable def eigenAug (v₀ : E) :
    ((E →L[ℝ] E) × (E × ℝ)) → ((E →L[ℝ] E) × (E × ℝ)) :=
  fun p => (p.1, (p.1 p.2.1 - p.2.2 • p.2.1, ⟪v₀, p.2.1⟫ - 1))

@[simp]

blocks.label: lem:augmented-eigen-equation-derivative
blocks.title: The augmented equation has an invertible derivative at a simple eigenpair
blocks.body:
$\Phi_{v_0}$ is smooth, and its derivative at $(S,v,\lambda)$ is
\[
  D\Phi_{v_0}(S,v,\lambda)\,(T,w,\mu) = \bigl(T,\ Tv + Sw - \lambda w - \mu v,\ \langle v_0, w\rangle\bigr) .
\]
If $(S,v,\lambda)$ is a simple eigenpair and $\langle v_0, v\rangle = 1$, this derivative is a linear isomorphism.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.hasFDerivAt_eigenAug
lean.code:
theorem hasFDerivAt_eigenAug (h : IsSimpleEigenpair S v l) {v₀ : E} (hv₀ : ⟪v₀, v⟫ = 1) :
    HasFDerivAt (eigenAug v₀) (eigenAugDerivEquiv h hv₀ : _ →L[ℝ] _) (S, (v, l)) := by
  -- The three coordinate projections, as `HasFDerivAt` facts.
  have hfst : HasFDerivAt (fun p : (E →L[ℝ] E) × (E × ℝ) => p.1)
      (ContinuousLinearMap.fst ℝ (E →L[ℝ] E) (E × ℝ)) (S, (v, l)) :=
    (ContinuousLinearMap.fst ℝ (E →L[ℝ] E) (E × ℝ)).hasFDerivAt
  have hv' : HasFDerivAt (fun p : (E →L[ℝ] E) × (E × ℝ) => p.2.1)
      ((ContinuousLinearMap.fst ℝ E ℝ).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.snd ℝ (E →L[ℝ] E) (E × ℝ)))
      (S, (v, l)) :=
    hasFDerivAt_fst.comp _ hasFDerivAt_snd
  have hl' : HasFDerivAt (fun p : (E →L[ℝ] E) × (E × ℝ) => p.2.2)
      ((ContinuousLinearMap.snd ℝ E ℝ).comp (ContinuousLinearMap.snd ℝ (E →L[ℝ] E) (E × ℝ)))
      (S, (v, l)) :=
    hasFDerivAt_snd.comp _ hasFDerivAt_snd
  have hG := hfst.clm_apply hv'
  have hH := hl'.smul hv'
  have hI := ((innerSL ℝ v₀).hasFDerivAt (x := v)).comp (S, (v, l)) hv'
  -- The assembled derivative is defeq to `eigenAug`'s, so only the linear maps must be matched.
  refine HasFDerivAt.congr_fderiv (hfst.prodMk ((hG.sub hH).prodMk (hI.sub_const 1))) ?_
  apply ContinuousLinearMap.ext
  rintro ⟨T, w, μ⟩
  simp [eigenAugDerivEquiv, eigenLin, Prod.ext_iff]
  abel

/-! ### The selection -/

blocks.tex:
Each component of $\Phi_{v_0}$ is a polynomial expression in $(S,v,\lambda)$ built from the bilinear evaluation map $(S,v) \mapsto Sv$, the scalar multiplication $(\lambda,v)\mapsto \lambda v$ and the linear functional $v \mapsto \langle v_0,v\rangle$, so $\Phi_{v_0}$ is smooth and the displayed formula is its derivative.

For invertibility, note the derivative is block lower triangular with respect to the splitting $\operatorname{End}(V) \oplus (V \times \mathbb{R})$: it is the identity on the first block, it is $L_{S,v,\lambda,v_0}$ on the second, and the remaining block is the map $T \mapsto (Tv, 0)$. A block triangular map with invertible diagonal blocks is invertible, and $L_{S,v,\lambda,v_0}$ is bijective by Lemma \ref{lem:eigen-linearisation-injective}.

blocks.label: prop:smooth-eigen-selection
blocks.title: Smooth selection of a simple eigenpair

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_eigenSelection
lean.code:
theorem exists_eigenSelection {S₀ : E →L[ℝ] E} {v₀ : E} {l₀ : ℝ}
    (h : IsSimpleEigenpair S₀ v₀ l₀) (hv₀ : ⟪v₀, v₀⟫ = 1) :
    ∃ (W : Set (E →L[ℝ] E)) (V : (E →L[ℝ] E) → E) (Λ : (E →L[ℝ] E) → ℝ),
      IsOpen W ∧ S₀ ∈ W ∧ V S₀ = v₀ ∧ Λ S₀ = l₀ ∧
      (∀ S ∈ W, S (V S) = Λ S • V S) ∧ (∀ S ∈ W, ⟪v₀, V S⟫ = 1) ∧
      ContinuousOn V W ∧ ContinuousOn Λ W ∧
      ∀ S ∈ W, IsSimpleEigenpair S (V S) (Λ S) →
        ContDiffAt ℝ (⊤ : ℕ∞) V S ∧ ContDiffAt ℝ (⊤ : ℕ∞) Λ S := by
  classical
  -- The inverse function theorem, applied to `eigenAug` at the given simple eigenpair.
  have hcd : ContDiffAt ℝ (⊤ : ℕ∞) (eigenAug v₀) (S₀, (v₀, l₀)) :=
    (contDiff_eigenAug v₀).contDiffAt
  have hfd := hasFDerivAt_eigenAug h hv₀
  set pe := hcd.toOpenPartialHomeomorph (eigenAug v₀) hfd (by simp) with hpe
  have hcoe : ∀ p, pe p = eigenAug v₀ p := fun _ => rfl
