  -- `eigenAug` sends the eigenpair to `(S₀, 0, 0)`: that is exactly the eigen-equation.
  have hbase : eigenAug v₀ (S₀, (v₀, l₀)) = (S₀, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ))) := by
    rw [eigenAug_apply]
    simp only [h.apply_eq, hv₀, sub_self]
  have hsrc : (S₀, (v₀, l₀)) ∈ pe.source :=
    ContDiffAt.mem_toOpenPartialHomeomorph_source hcd hfd (by simp)
  -- Slice the target at `(0, 0)`: `W` is the set of operators whose eigen-equation is solved by
  -- the branch the inverse function theorem produced.
  set W : Set (E →L[ℝ] E) := (fun S : E →L[ℝ] E => (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))) ⁻¹' pe.target with hW
  have hS₀W : S₀ ∈ W := by
    show (S₀, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ))) ∈ pe.target
    rw [← hbase, ← hcoe]
    exact pe.map_source hsrc
  have hsymm₀ : pe.symm (S₀, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ))) = (S₀, (v₀, l₀)) := by
    rw [← hbase, ← hcoe, pe.left_inv hsrc]
  -- Everything about the selected pair is read off from `pe (pe.symm y) = y`.
  have key : ∀ S ∈ W,
      (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).1 = S ∧
      S (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.1
        = (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.2 • (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.1 ∧
      ⟪v₀, (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.1⟫ = 1 := by
    intro S hS
    have hr : eigenAug v₀ (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))) = (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ))) := by
      rw [← hcoe]; exact pe.right_inv hS
    rw [eigenAug_apply] at hr
    have h1 : (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).1 = S := congrArg Prod.fst hr
    have h2 := congrArg (fun q => q.2.1) hr
    have h3 := congrArg (fun q => q.2.2) hr
    simp only at h2 h3
    rw [sub_eq_zero] at h2 h3
    rw [h1] at h2
    exact ⟨h1, h2, h3⟩
  refine ⟨W, fun S => (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.1,
    fun S => (pe.symm (S, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.2,
    pe.open_target.preimage (continuous_id.prodMk continuous_const), hS₀W, ?_, ?_,
    fun S hS => (key S hS).2.1, fun S hS => (key S hS).2.2, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · show (pe.symm (S₀, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.1 = v₀
    rw [hsymm₀]
  · show (pe.symm (S₀, ((0 : E), (0 : ℝ)))).2.2 = l₀
    rw [hsymm₀]
  -- Continuity, from continuity of `pe.symm` on the (open) target.
  · exact ((pe.continuousOn_symm.comp (continuous_id.prodMk continuous_const).continuousOn
      fun _ hS => hS).snd).fst
  · exact ((pe.continuousOn_symm.comp (continuous_id.prodMk continuous_const).continuousOn
      fun _ hS => hS).snd).snd
  -- … (truncated)

deps.title: The augmented equation has an invertible derivative at a simple eigenpair
deps.label: lem:augmented-eigen-equation-derivative

blocks.tex:
Since $S_0 v_0 = \lambda_0 v_0$ and $\langle v_0,v_0\rangle = 1$, we have $\Phi_{v_0}(S_0,v_0,\lambda_0) = (S_0,0,0)$. By Lemma \ref{lem:augmented-eigen-equation-derivative}, $\Phi_{v_0}$ is smooth with invertible derivative at $(S_0,v_0,\lambda_0)$, so by the inverse function theorem it restricts to a diffeomorphism $\Phi$ from an open neighbourhood $\mathcal{U}$ of $(S_0,v_0,\lambda_0)$ onto an open neighbourhood $\mathcal{W}$ of $(S_0,0,0)$.

Let $W = \{S : (S,0,0) \in \mathcal{W}\}$, an open set containing $S_0$ because $S \mapsto (S,0,0)$ is continuous, and define $(\mathcal{V}(S), \Lambda(S))$ to be the last two components of $\Phi^{-1}(S,0,0)$.

For (2), apply $\Phi_{v_0}$ to $\Phi^{-1}(S,0,0)$: the result is $(S,0,0)$, and reading off the three components of the definition of $\Phi_{v_0}$ gives that the first component of $\Phi^{-1}(S,0,0)$ is $S$ itself, that $S\,\mathcal{V}(S) - \Lambda(S)\,\mathcal{V}(S) = 0$, and that $\langle v_0, \mathcal{V}(S)\rangle - 1 = 0$. For (1), $\Phi^{-1}(S_0,0,0) = (S_0,v_0,\lambda_0)$ because $\Phi(S_0,v_0,\lambda_0) = (S_0,0,0)$ and $\Phi$ is injective on $\mathcal{U}$. Claim (3) is continuity of $\Phi^{-1}$ composed with the continuous map $S \mapsto (S,0,0)$.

For (4), fix $S \in W$ such that $(S,\mathcal{V}(S),\Lambda(S))$ is a simple eigenpair. By (2) we have $\langle v_0, \mathcal{V}(S)\rangle = 1$, so Lemma \ref{lem:augmented-eigen-equation-derivative} applies at the point $\Phi^{-1}(S,0,0) = (S, \mathcal{V}(S), \Lambda(S))$ and shows the derivative of $\Phi_{v_0}$ there is invertible. Since $\Phi_{v_0}$ is smooth, $\Phi^{-1}$ is therefore smooth at $(S,0,0)$; composing with $S \mapsto (S,0,0)$ and projecting gives smoothness of $\mathcal{V}$ and $\Lambda$ at $S$.

blocks.label: lem:signature-and-eigenvalue-signs
blocks.title: The index counts the negative eigenvalues
blocks.body: Let $(V,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)$ be a finite-dimensional real inner product space and let $B$ be a symmetric nondegenerate bilinear form on $V$, so that $(V,B)$ is a scalar product space of some signature $(r,s)$. Let $A : V \to V$ be a linear operator with $B(v,w) = \langle Av, w\rangle$ for all $v,w$; such an $A$ exists and is unique because $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ is nondegenerate, and it is symmetric. Then the index $s$ is the number of negative eigenvalues of $A$, counted with multiplicity.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigNeg_eq_ncard_neg_eigenvalues
lean.code:
theorem sigNeg_eq_ncard_neg_eigenvalues (hsymm : (A : V →ₗ[ℝ] V).IsSymmetric)
    (hn : finrank ℝ V = n) (hA : ∀ v w, ⟪A v, w⟫ = B v w) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)
      = {i : Fin n | hsymm.eigenvalues hn i < 0}.ncard := by
  rw [(isOrthonormal_eigenBasisScaled hsymm hn hA hnd).sigNeg_eq_ncard]
  refine congrArg Set.ncard ?_
  ext i
  simp only [Set.mem_setOf_eq, apply_eigenBasisScaled_self hsymm hn hA hnd i]
  constructor
  · intro h
    by_contra hc
    rw [if_neg hc] at h
    norm_num at h
  · intro h
    rw [if_pos h]

blocks.tex:
Symmetry of $A$ is immediate: $\langle Av,w\rangle = B(v,w) = B(w,v) = \langle Aw,v\rangle = \langle v, Aw\rangle$. By the spectral theorem for symmetric operators there is a $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$-orthonormal basis $u_1,\dots,u_n$ of $V$ with $Au_i = \mu_i u_i$, the eigenvalues listed with multiplicity. Then
\[
  B(u_i,u_j) = \langle Au_i, u_j\rangle = \mu_i\langle u_i,u_j\rangle = \mu_i\delta_{ij},
\]
so $B$ is diagonal in this basis with the eigenvalues on the diagonal. Each $\mu_i \neq 0$: otherwise $B(u_i, w) = \langle \mu_i u_i, w\rangle = 0$ for every $w$, putting $u_i \neq 0$ in the radical of $B$ and contradicting nondegeneracy.

Set $e_i = |\mu_i|^{-1/2}u_i$, which is again a basis since the scalars are nonzero. Then $B(e_i,e_j) = |\mu_i|^{-1/2}|\mu_j|^{-1/2}\mu_i\delta_{ij}$, which vanishes for $i \neq j$ and equals $\mu_i/|\mu_i| = \pm 1$ for $i = j$. So $(e_i)$ is an orthonormal basis for $B$ in the indefinite sense of Corollary \ref{cor:scalar-product-signature-form}, and $B(e_i,e_i) = -1$ exactly at the indices with $\mu_i < 0$. By Sylvester's law of inertia (Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia}) the number of $-1$'s is the index $s$, so $s$ is the number of indices with $\mu_i < 0$, which is the number of negative eigenvalues counted with multiplicity.

blocks.label: lem:simple-eigenpair-of-index-one
blocks.title: Index one yields a simple eigenpair
blocks.body: In the situation of Lemma \ref{lem:signature-and-eigenvalue-signs}, suppose the index of $B$ is $1$. Then $A$ has exactly one negative eigenvalue $\lambda$; the eigenspace $\ker(A - \lambda\,\mathrm{id})$ is a line, spanned by an eigenvector $u$; and $(A, u, \lambda)$ is a simple eigenpair.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isSimpleEigenpair_of_sigNeg_eq_one
lean.code:
theorem exists_isSimpleEigenpair_of_sigNeg_eq_one (hsymm : (A : V →ₗ[ℝ] V).IsSymmetric)
    (hn : finrank ℝ V = n) (hA : ∀ v w, ⟪A v, w⟫ = B v w) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    (hsig : sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) = 1) :
    ∃ i : Fin n, hsymm.eigenvalues hn i < 0 ∧
      IsSimpleEigenpair A (hsymm.eigenvectorBasis hn i) (hsymm.eigenvalues hn i) ∧
      ∀ j : Fin n, hsymm.eigenvalues hn j < 0 → j = i := by
  rw [sigNeg_eq_ncard_neg_eigenvalues hsymm hn hA hnd, Set.ncard_eq_one] at hsig
  obtain ⟨i₀, hi₀⟩ := hsig
  -- `i₀` is the unique index carrying a negative eigenvalue.
  have hmem : ∀ j : Fin n, hsymm.eigenvalues hn j < 0 ↔ j = i₀ := by
    intro j
    simpa using Set.ext_iff.mp hi₀ j
  have hune : hsymm.eigenvectorBasis hn i₀ ≠ 0 := by
    have := (hsymm.eigenvectorBasis hn).toBasis.ne_zero i₀
    rwa [OrthonormalBasis.coe_toBasis] at this
  refine ⟨i₀, (hmem i₀).mpr rfl, ⟨hsymm, hune, apply_eigenvectorBasis_clm hsymm hn i₀, ?_⟩,
    fun j hj => (hmem j).mp hj⟩
  -- Simplicity: the eigenspace has dimension `1`, and the eigenvector already spans a line in it.
  intro w hw
  have hrank : finrank ℝ (Module.End.eigenspace (A : V →ₗ[ℝ] V) (hsymm.eigenvalues hn i₀)) = 1 := by
    rw [← hsymm.card_filter_eigenvalues_eq hn (hsymm.eigenvalues hn i₀), Finset.card_eq_one]
    refine ⟨i₀, ?_⟩
    ext j
    simp only [Finset.mem_filter, Finset.mem_univ, true_and, Finset.mem_singleton]
    constructor
    · intro h
      have hjj : hsymm.eigenvalues hn j = hsymm.eigenvalues hn i₀ := by exact_mod_cast h
      exact (hmem j).mp (hjj ▸ (hmem i₀).mpr rfl)
    · intro h
      rw [h]
      norm_cast
  -- `span {u i₀} ≤ eigenspace`, and both have dimension `1`.
  have hle : Submodule.span ℝ ({hsymm.eigenvectorBasis hn i₀} : Set V)
      ≤ Module.End.eigenspace (A : V →ₗ[ℝ] V) (hsymm.eigenvalues hn i₀) := by
    rw [Submodule.span_le, Set.singleton_subset_iff, SetLike.mem_coe,
      Module.End.mem_eigenspace_iff]
    exact apply_eigenvectorBasis_clm hsymm hn i₀
  have hspan : finrank ℝ (Submodule.span ℝ ({hsymm.eigenvectorBasis hn i₀} : Set V)) = 1 :=
    finrank_span_singleton hune
  have heq : Submodule.span ℝ ({hsymm.eigenvectorBasis hn i₀} : Set V)
      = Module.End.eigenspace (A : V →ₗ[ℝ] V) (hsymm.eigenvalues hn i₀) :=
    Submodule.eq_of_le_of_finrank_eq hle (by rw [hspan, hrank])
  rw [heq, Module.End.mem_eigenspace_iff]
  exact hw

deps.title: The index counts the negative eigenvalues
deps.label: lem:signature-and-eigenvalue-signs

blocks.tex:
Keep the eigenbasis $(u_i)$ and eigenvalues $(\mu_i)$ of the previous proof. By Lemma ef{lem:signature-and-eigenvalue-signs} exactly one index $i_0$ has $\mu_{i_0} < 0$; write $\lambda = \mu_{i_0}$ and $u = u_{i_0}$, so $u 
eq 0$ and $Au = \lambda u$.

For a symmetric operator the dimension of the $\lambda$-eigenspace equals the number of indices $i$ with $\mu_i = \lambda$. If $\mu_i = \lambda$ then $\mu_i < 0$, so $i = i_0$ by uniqueness of that index; hence there is exactly one such index and $\dim\ker(A - \lambda\,\mathrm{id}) = 1$. Now $\operatorname{span}(u) \subseteq \ker(A-\lambda\,\mathrm{id})$ because $Au = \lambda u$, and $\dim\operatorname{span}(u) = 1$ because $u 
eq 0$; two subspaces of the same finite dimension with one contained in the other are equal, so $\operatorname{span}(u) = \ker(A - \lambda\,\mathrm{id})$. Thus every $w$ with $Aw = \lambda w$ lies in $\operatorname{span}(u)$, which together with symmetry of $A$ and $u 
eq 0$ says exactly that $(A,u,\lambda)$ is a simple eigenpair.

blocks.label: prop:rank-one-distribution-of-lorentz-metric
blocks.title: Necessity in Theorem \ref{thm:lorentz-metrics}
blocks.body: If a smooth manifold $M$ admits a Lorentz metric, then $M$ admits a rank-1 tangent distribution.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isRankOneDistribution_of_isLorentzMetric
lean.code:
theorem exists_isRankOneDistribution_of_isLorentzMetric
    (gL : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (hL : IsLorentzMetric gL) :
    ∃ D : ∀ x : M, Submodule ℝ (TangentSpace I x), IsRankOneDistribution I M D := by
  obtain ⟨g⟩ := exists_riemannianMetric (I := I) (M := M)
  refine ⟨lorentzDistribution g gL, ⟨fun p => ?_⟩⟩
  -- a `g`-orthonormal frame near `p`
  obtain ⟨u, Y, hu, hpu, hY, hon⟩ := exists_orthonormalFrame_nhds g p
  have hon' : ∀ x ∈ u, ∀ i j, g.innerAt x (Y i x) (Y j x) = if i = j then 1 else 0 := hon
  -- a negative eigenvector at `p`, read in the frame
  obtain ⟨w₀, l₀, hw₀ne, hl₀neg, hw₀ev⟩ := exists_neg_eigenvector g gL hL p
  obtain ⟨c₀, hc₀⟩ := (frameCoeff_bijective hY hpu).2 w₀
  have hinjp := frameCoeff_injective hY hpu
  have hc₀ne : c₀ ≠ 0 := fun h => hw₀ne (by rw [← hc₀, h, frameCoeff_zero])
  have hSev : frameOperator gL Y p c₀ = l₀ • c₀ := by
    refine hinjp ?_
    rw [← metricOperator_frameCoeff g gL hY hpu (hon' p hpu), hc₀, hw₀ev, frameCoeff_smul, hc₀]
  -- normalize it: `exists_eigenSelection` needs a unit eigenvector
  set v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) := (‖c₀‖)⁻¹ • c₀ with hv₀def
  have hv₀norm : ‖v₀‖ = 1 := norm_smul_inv_norm hc₀ne
  have hv₀ne : v₀ ≠ 0 := by intro h; rw [h, norm_zero] at hv₀norm; norm_num at hv₀norm
  have hv₀unit : ⟪v₀, v₀⟫ = 1 := by
    rw [real_inner_self_eq_norm_sq, hv₀norm]; norm_num
  have hv₀ev : frameOperator gL Y p v₀ = l₀ • v₀ := by
    rw [hv₀def, map_smul, hSev, smul_comm]
  have hsimple₀ : IsSimpleEigenpair (frameOperator gL Y p) v₀ l₀ :=
    isSimpleEigenpair_frameOperator g gL hL hY hpu (hon' p hpu) hv₀ne hl₀neg hv₀ev
  -- the smooth eigen-selection around the matrix at `p`
  obtain ⟨W, Vsel, Λ, hWopen, hSpW, -, hΛS₀, hevW, hnormW, -, hΛcont, hsmooth⟩ :=
    exists_eigenSelection hsimple₀ hv₀unit
  -- shrink `u` to where the matrix lies in `W` and the selected eigenvalue is still negative
  have hScont : ContinuousOn (frameOperator gL Y) u :=
    (contMDiffOn_frameOperator gL hY.contMDiffOn).continuousOn
  set u₁ : Set M := u ∩ (frameOperator gL Y) ⁻¹' W with hu₁def
  have hu₁open : IsOpen u₁ := hScont.isOpen_inter_preimage hu hWopen
  have hΛcomp : ContinuousOn (fun x => Λ (frameOperator gL Y x)) u₁ :=
    hΛcont.comp (hScont.mono Set.inter_subset_left) fun x hx => hx.2
  set u' : Set M := u₁ ∩ (fun x => Λ (frameOperator gL Y x)) ⁻¹' (Set.Iio 0) with hu'def
  have hu'open : IsOpen u' := hΛcomp.isOpen_inter_preimage hu₁open isOpen_Iio
  have hpu' : p ∈ u' := ⟨⟨hpu, hSpW⟩, by simp only [Set.mem_preimage, Set.mem_Iio, hΛS₀]; exact hl₀neg⟩
  have hu'u : u' ⊆ u := fun x hx => hx.1.1
  -- the selected eigenvector is nowhere zero, and at each `x ∈ u'` the selected pair is simple
  have hVne : ∀ x ∈ u', Vsel (frameOperator gL Y x) ≠ 0 := by
    intro x hx h
    have := hnormW _ hx.1.2
    rw [h, inner_zero_right] at this
    norm_num at this
  have hsimplex : ∀ x ∈ u', IsSimpleEigenpair (frameOperator gL Y x)
      (Vsel (frameOperator gL Y x)) (Λ (frameOperator gL Y x)) := fun x hx =>
    isSimpleEigenpair_frameOperator g gL hL hY (hu'u hx) (hon' x (hu'u hx))
      (hVne x hx) hx.2 (hevW _ hx.1.2)
  refine ⟨u', fun x => frameCoeff Y x (Vsel (frameOperator gL Y x)), hu'open, hpu', ?_, ?_⟩
  · -- smoothness: smooth coefficients in a smooth frame
    have hSsmooth : ∀ x ∈ u', ContMDiffAt I
        𝓘(ℝ, EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) →L[ℝ]
          EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (finrank ℝ E))) ∞ (frameOperator gL Y) x := fun x hx =>
      ((contMDiffOn_frameOperator gL hY.contMDiffOn) x (hu'u hx)).contMDiffAt
        (hu.mem_nhds (hu'u hx))
    have hcoeff : ∀ i, ContMDiffOn I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
        (fun x => (Vsel (frameOperator gL Y x)) i) u' := by
      intro i x hx
  -- … (truncated)

deps.title: Smooth selection of a simple eigenpair
deps.label: prop:smooth-eigen-selection

deps.title: Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric
deps.label: prop:existence-riemannian-metric

deps.title: Index one yields a simple eigenpair
deps.label: lem:simple-eigenpair-of-index-one

blocks.tex:
Let $\bar{g}$ be a Lorentz metric and choose an auxiliary Riemannian metric $g$, which exists by Proposition \ref{prop:existence-riemannian-metric}. For each $x$ let $A_x : T_xM \to T_xM$ be the $g$-self-adjoint endomorphism determined by $\bar{g}_x(v,w) = g_x(A_xv, w)$. Define
\[
  D_x = \bigsqcup_{\mu < 0} \ker\bigl(A_x - \mu\,\mathrm{id}\bigr),
\]
the sum of all the negative eigenspaces of $A_x$. This mentions no eigenvalue of $A_x$, so $D$ is a well-defined field of subspaces depending on nothing but $g$.

We claim $D_x$ is a line, spanned by any nonzero $v$ with $A_xv = \mu v$ and $\mu < 0$. Since $\bar{g}$ is Lorentz, its index at every $x$ is $1$, so by Lemma \ref{lem:simple-eigenpair-of-index-one} the operator $A_x$ has exactly one negative eigenvalue $\lambda(x)$, and its eigenspace is the line spanned by the corresponding spectral eigenvector. Any negative eigenvalue of $A_x$ occurs in the spectrum of $A_x$, hence equals $\lambda(x)$; so every summand $\ker(A_x - \mu)$ with $\mu < 0$ is either $0$ or that same line, and $D_x$ is exactly it. In particular $(A_x, v, \lambda(x))$ is a simple eigenpair for every nonzero $v \in D_x$. It remains to produce smooth local spanning sections, which is a local question.

Fix $x_0 \in M$. By Proposition \ref{prop:existence-orthonormal-frames} there is a smooth $g$-orthonormal frame $E_1,\dots,E_n$ on a neighbourhood $U$ of $x_0$. Let $\iota_x : \mathbb{R}^n \to T_xM$, $\iota_x(c) = \sum_i c_i E_i(x)$, be its coefficient map; it is linear and bijective because a frame is a pointwise basis. Let $S(x) \in \operatorname{End}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ be the operator with matrix $S(x)_{ij} = \bar{g}_x(E_i, E_j)$. These entries are $\bar{g}$-pairings of smooth sections, hence smooth functions of $x$ on $U$, so $x \mapsto S(x)$ is a smooth map from $U$ into $\operatorname{End}(\mathbb{R}^n)$, and each $S(x)$ is symmetric because $\bar g$ is. Because the frame is $g$-orthonormal, $S(x)$ is conjugate to $A_x$:
\[
  A_x \circ \iota_x = \iota_x \circ S(x),
\]
since both sides have the same $g$-inner products against every $E_j(x)$, namely $\sum_i c_i \bar{g}_x(E_i,E_j)$. Consequently $\iota_x$ matches the $\mu$-eigenvectors of $S(x)$ with those of $A_x$; combined with the claim above, $(S(x), c, \mu)$ is a simple eigenpair whenever $c \neq 0$, $S(x)c = \mu c$ and $\mu < 0$. Note only the injectivity of $\iota_x$ is used here, not that it is an isometry.

Let $v_0$ be a unit vector spanning the $\lambda(x_0)$-eigenspace of $S(x_0)$, obtained by pulling a negative eigenvector of $A_{x_0}$ back through $\iota_{x_0}$ and normalizing. Proposition \ref{prop:smooth-eigen-selection} applied to the simple eigenpair $(S(x_0), v_0, \lambda(x_0))$ gives an open $W \ni S(x_0)$ and maps $\mathcal{V}, \Lambda$ on $W$ selecting an eigenpair of each $S \in W$. Since $\Lambda$ is continuous and $\Lambda(S(x_0)) = \lambda(x_0) < 0$, the set
\[
  U' = \{x \in U : S(x) \in W \text{ and } \Lambda(S(x)) < 0\}
\]
is open and contains $x_0$. Set $X_x = \iota_x\,\mathcal{V}(S(x))$.

For $x \in U'$ the vector $\mathcal{V}(S(x))$ is an eigenvector of $S(x)$ with eigenvalue $\Lambda(S(x)) < 0$, and it is nonzero because $\langle v_0, \mathcal{V}(S(x))\rangle = 1$; so $X_x \neq 0$ by injectivity of $\iota_x$, and $X_x$ is an eigenvector of $A_x$ with the negative eigenvalue $\Lambda(S(x))$ by the conjugation relation. Hence $\operatorname{span}(X_x) = D_x$ by the claim.

Smoothness of $X$ on $U'$ is smoothness of $x \mapsto \mathcal{V}(S(x))$ together with smoothness of the frame, since $X_x = \sum_i \mathcal{V}(S(x))_i\,E_i(x)$. By the previous paragraph $(S(x), \mathcal{V}(S(x)), \Lambda(S(x)))$ is a simple eigenpair for each $x \in U'$. So clause (4) of Proposition \ref{prop:smooth-eigen-selection} applies at every $S(x)$, making $\mathcal{V}$ smooth there; composing with the smooth map $x \mapsto S(x)$ shows the coefficients of $X$ are smooth on $U'$, hence so is $X$.

Thus every $x_0 \in M$ has a neighbourhood carrying a smooth nowhere-zero section of $D$, so $D$ is a rank-1 tangent distribution in the sense of Definition \ref{def:rank-one-distribution}.

blocks.label: thm:lorentz-metrics
blocks.title: thm:lorentz-metrics
blocks.body: A smooth manifold $M$ admits a Lorentz metric if and only if it admits a rank-1 tangent distribution (i.e., a rank-1 subbundle of $TM$).

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isLorentzMetric_iff_exists_isRankOneDistribution
lean.code:
theorem exists_isLorentzMetric_iff_exists_isRankOneDistribution :
    (∃ gL : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M, IsLorentzMetric gL) ↔
      ∃ D : ∀ x : M, Submodule ℝ (TangentSpace I x), IsRankOneDistribution I M D :=
  ⟨fun ⟨gL, hL⟩ => exists_isRankOneDistribution_of_isLorentzMetric gL hL,
    fun ⟨_, hD⟩ => exists_isLorentzMetric_of_isRankOneDistribution I M hD⟩

deps.title: Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric
deps.label: prop:existence-riemannian-metric

blocks.tex: Sufficiency is Proposition \ref{prop:lorentz-metric-of-rank-one-distribution} and necessity is Proposition \ref{prop:rank-one-distribution-of-lorentz-metric}.

\emph{Proof.} Problem~2-34. \qed

With some more sophisticated tools from algebraic topology, it can be shown that every noncompact connected smooth manifold admits a Lorentz metric, and a compact connected smooth manifold admits a Lorentz metric if and only if its Euler characteristic is zero (see \cite{ON83}, p.~149). It follows that no even-dimensional sphere admits a Lorentz metric, because $S^{2n}$ has Euler characteristic equal to 2. For a thorough treatment of pseudo-Riemannian metrics from a mathematical point of view, see the excellent book \cite{ON83}; a more physical treatment can be found in \cite{HE73}.

The theory of submanifolds is only slightly more complicated in the pseudo-Riemannian case. If $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold, a smooth submanifold $\iota: M \hookrightarrow \tilde{M}$ is called a \emph{pseudo-Riemannian submanifold} of $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ if $\iota^* \tilde{g}$ is nondegenerate with constant signature. If this is the case, we always consider $M$ to be endowed with the induced pseudo-Riemannian metric $\iota^* \tilde{g}$. In the special case in which $\iota^* \tilde{g}$ is positive definite, $M$ is called a \emph{Riemannian submanifold}.

The nondegeneracy hypothesis is not automatically satisfied; for example, if $M \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{1,1}$ is the submanifold $\{(\xi, \tau) : \xi = \tau\}$ and $\iota: M \to \mathbb{R}^{1,1}$ is inclusion, then the pullback tensor $\iota^* \tilde{g}^{(1,1)}$ is identically zero on $M$.

For hypersurfaces (submanifolds of codimension 1), the nondegeneracy condition is easy to check. If $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ is a smooth submanifold and $p \in M$, then a vector $v \in T_p \tilde{M}$ is said to be \emph{normal} to $M$ if $(v,x) = 0$ for all $x \in T_p M$, just as in the Riemannian case. The space of all normal vectors at $p$ is a subspace of $T_p \tilde{M}$ denoted by $N_p M$.

The proofs of Propositions \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} and \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-adapted-frame} both begin with the same purely linear-algebraic step, carried out pointwise on a tangent space: a nondegenerate tuple is absorbed into an orthonormal basis inducing the same flag. We isolate it once.

blocks.label: lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis
blocks.title: Orthonormal Bases Adapted to a Nondegenerate Flag
blocks.body: Suppose $(V,q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space and $(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ is a nondegenerate $k$-tuple in $V$, with $k \leq n$. Then there is an orthonormal basis $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ for $V$ such that $\operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_j) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$ for every $j \leq k$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq_of_isNondegenerateTuple
lean.code:
theorem exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq_of_isNondegenerateTuple (hB : B.IsSymm)
    (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) {k : ℕ} {v : Fin k → V} (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v)
    (hk : k ≤ finrank ℝ V) :
    ∃ b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V, IsOrthonormal B b ∧
      ∀ j ≤ k, prefixSpan b j = prefixSpan v j := by
  obtain ⟨w, hw, hwv⟩ := exists_isNondegenerateTuple_basis hB hnd v hv hk
  obtain ⟨b, hbon, hbspan⟩ := exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq hB hw
  exact ⟨b, hbon, fun j hj => by
    rw [hbspan j (le_trans hj hk), prefixSpan_congr hk hwv hj]⟩

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:completion-nondegenerate-bases} the tuple extends to a nondegenerate basis $(w_1,\ldots,w_n)$ of $V$ with $w_i = v_i$ for every $i \leq k$. Proposition \ref{prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products} applied to $(w_i)$ yields an orthonormal basis $(b_i)$ with $\operatorname{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_j) = \operatorname{span}(w_1,\ldots,w_j)$ for every $j \leq n$. For $j \leq k$ the tuples $(w_i)$ and $(v_i)$ have the same first $j$ entries, so $\operatorname{span}(w_1,\ldots,w_j) = \operatorname{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_j)$, which gives the claim.

blocks.label: lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis-line
blocks.title: Orthonormal Basis Adapted to a Non-Null Line
blocks.body: Suppose $(V,q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space with $n \geq 1$, and $x \in V$ satisfies $\langle x, x \rangle \neq 0$. Then there is an orthonormal basis $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ for $V$ with $\operatorname{span}(b_1) = \operatorname{span}(x)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_one_eq_span
lean.code:
theorem exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_one_eq_span (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    {x : V} (hx : B x x ≠ 0) (hV : 1 ≤ finrank ℝ V) :
    ∃ b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V, IsOrthonormal B b ∧
      prefixSpan b 1 = Submodule.span ℝ {x} := by
  obtain ⟨b, hbon, hbspan⟩ := exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq_of_isNondegenerateTuple hB hnd
    (isNondegenerateTuple_singleton hB hnd hx) hV
  exact ⟨b, hbon, by rw [hbspan 1 le_rfl, prefixSpan_singleton]⟩

deps.title: Orthonormal Bases Adapted to a Nondegenerate Flag
deps.label: lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis

blocks.tex:
The line $\operatorname{span}(x)$ is $1$-dimensional, since $\langle x,xangle 
eq 0$ forces $x 
eq 0$. It is nondegenerate: an element $cx$ orthogonal to every element of $\operatorname{span}(x)$ satisfies in particular $c\langle x,x angle = \langle cx, xangle = 0$, and $\langle x,xangle 
eq 0$ gives $c = 0$. Hence the $1$-tuple $(x)$ is nondegenerate, and Lemma ef{lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis} applied with $k = 1$ produces an orthonormal basis $(b_i)$ with $\operatorname{span}(b_1) = \operatorname{span}(x)$.

The rest of the linear algebra behind Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} is pointwise as well, and involves the manifold nowhere: it takes place entirely in the scalar product space $(T_p\tilde M, \tilde g_p)$, in which the normal space $N_pM$ is a line and the tangent space $T_pM$ is that line's orthogonal complement. We isolate it here, in four steps: nondegeneracy is decided on the normal line; the tail of an adapted orthonormal basis spans the complement of its head; that tail is an orthonormal basis of the complement; and counting its signs gives the signature.

blocks.label: lem:nondegenerate-line
blocks.title: Nondegeneracy of a Line
blocks.body: Let $q$ be a bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space $V$ and let $L \subseteq V$ be a subspace with $\dim L = 1$. Then $q|_{L \times L}$ is nondegenerate if and only if $\langle v,v \rangle \neq 0$ for every nonzero $v \in L$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.restrict_nondegenerate_iff_of_finrank_eq_one
lean.code:
theorem restrict_nondegenerate_iff_of_finrank_eq_one {L : Submodule ℝ V}
    (hL : finrank ℝ L = 1) :
    (B.restrict L).Nondegenerate ↔ ∀ v ∈ L, v ≠ 0 → B v v ≠ 0 := by
  constructor
  · -- A nonzero null `v ∈ L` spans `L`, so it is `B`-orthogonal to all of `L`.
    rintro ⟨hsep, -⟩ v hv hv0 hvv
    have hspan : Submodule.span ℝ {v} = L :=
      Submodule.eq_of_le_of_finrank_eq
        ((Submodule.span_singleton_le_iff_mem _ _).2 hv)
        (by rw [finrank_span_singleton hv0, hL])
    refine hv0 (congrArg Subtype.val (hsep ⟨v, hv⟩ fun w => ?_))
    obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ : ∃ c : ℝ, c • v = (w : V) :=
      Submodule.mem_span_singleton.1 (by rw [hspan]; exact w.2)
    show B v (w : V) = 0
    rw [← hc, map_smul, smul_eq_mul, hvv, mul_zero]
  · -- Conversely, a vector `B`-orthogonal to all of `L` is in particular null.
    intro h
    exact ⟨fun v hvw => by_contra fun hv0 =>
        h (v : V) v.2 (fun hh => hv0 (Subtype.ext hh)) (hvw v),
      fun v hvw => by_contra fun hv0 =>
        h (v : V) v.2 (fun hh => hv0 (Subtype.ext hh)) (hvw v)⟩

blocks.tex:
Suppose first that $q|_{L\times L}$ is nondegenerate, and let $v \in L$ be nonzero with $\langle v,v\rangle = 0$. Then $\operatorname{span}(v) \subseteq L$ and both are $1$-dimensional, so $\operatorname{span}(v) = L$. Every $w \in L$ is therefore of the form $cv$, whence $\langle v,w\rangle = c\langle v,v\rangle = 0$. Thus $v$ is orthogonal to all of $L$, and nondegeneracy forces $v = 0$, a contradiction.

Conversely, suppose every nonzero $v \in L$ satisfies $\langle v,v\rangle \neq 0$, and let $v \in L$ be orthogonal to all of $L$ on either side. Taking the other argument to be $v$ itself gives $\langle v,v \rangle = 0$, so $v = 0$ by hypothesis.

This equivalence is special to dimension $1$. In dimension $\geq 2$ the condition that no nonzero vector be null says that the restriction is \emph{definite}, which is strictly stronger than nondegenerate: the form $(\xi,\tau) \mapsto \xi^2 - \tau^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ is nondegenerate, yet every vector with $\xi = \pm\tau$ is null. This is why Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} is stated for hypersurfaces, whose normal spaces are lines.

blocks.label: lem:hyperplane-nondegenerate
blocks.title: Nondegeneracy of a Hyperplane
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space and let $W \subseteq V$ be a subspace with $\dim W + 1 = \dim V$. Then $q|_{W\times W}$ is nondegenerate if and only if $\langle v,v\rangle \neq 0$ for every nonzero $v \in W^\perp$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.restrict_nondegenerate_iff_forall_orthogonal
lean.code:
theorem restrict_nondegenerate_iff_forall_orthogonal (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    {W : Submodule ℝ V} (hW : finrank ℝ W + 1 = finrank ℝ V) :
    (B.restrict W).Nondegenerate ↔ ∀ v ∈ B.orthogonal W, v ≠ 0 → B v v ≠ 0 := by
  refine ((restrict_nondegenerate_tfae hB hnd W).out 0 1).trans ?_
  refine restrict_nondegenerate_iff_of_finrank_eq_one ?_
  have := finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank hnd W
  omega

end NondegenerateLine

section AdaptedTail

variable [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-subspace}, $q|_{W\times W}$ is nondegenerate if and only if $q|_{W^\perp \times W^\perp}$ is. By Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement}, $\dim W^\perp = \dim V - \dim W = 1$, so Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-line} applies to $W^\perp$ and identifies the latter condition with the requirement that every nonzero $v \in W^\perp$ satisfy $\langle v,v \rangle \neq 0$.

blocks.label: lem:orthonormal-tail-spans-orthogonal
blocks.title: The Tail of an Orthonormal Basis Spans the Complement of its Head
blocks.body: Let $q$ be a nondegenerate bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space $V$ and let $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ be an orthonormal basis for $V$. Then $\operatorname{span}(b_2,\ldots,b_n) = \operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.span_range_succ_eq_orthogonal
lean.code:
theorem span_range_succ_eq_orthogonal (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) {m : ℕ} {b : Fin (m + 1) → V}
    (hbon : IsOrthonormal B b) (hspan : Submodule.span ℝ (Set.range b) = ⊤) :
    Submodule.span ℝ (Set.range fun i : Fin m => b i.succ)
      = B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0}) := by
  have hb0 : b 0 ≠ 0 := fun h => hbon.apply_self_ne_zero 0 (by rw [h]; simp)
  have hli : LinearIndependent ℝ (fun i : Fin m => b i.succ) :=
    hbon.linearIndependent.comp _ (Fin.succ_injective m)
  -- Containment: each tail vector is orthogonal to the head, hence to the line it spans.
  have hle : Submodule.span ℝ (Set.range fun i : Fin m => b i.succ)
      ≤ B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0}) := by
    rw [Submodule.span_le]
    rintro _ ⟨i, rfl⟩
    rw [SetLike.mem_coe, LinearMap.BilinForm.mem_orthogonal_iff]
    intro n hn
    obtain ⟨c, rfl⟩ := Submodule.mem_span_singleton.1 hn
    show B (c • b 0) (b i.succ) = 0
    rw [map_smul, LinearMap.smul_apply, hbon.1 0 i.succ (Fin.succ_ne_zero i).symm, smul_zero]
  -- Equality by dimension: both sides have dimension `m`.
  refine Submodule.eq_of_le_of_finrank_eq hle ?_
  have hV : finrank ℝ V = m + 1 := by
    rw [Module.finrank_eq_card_basis
      (Basis.mk hbon.linearIndependent (le_of_eq hspan.symm)), Fintype.card_fin]
  have hperp := finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank hnd (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0})
  rw [finrank_span_singleton hb0] at hperp
  rw [finrank_span_eq_card hli, Fintype.card_fin]
  omega

blocks.tex: Each $b_j$ with $j \geq 2$ is orthogonal to $b_1$, hence to every element $cb_1$ of $\operatorname{span}(b_1)$; as $\operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp$ is a subspace, this gives $\operatorname{span}(b_2,\ldots,b_n) \subseteq \operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp$. The reverse inclusion is a matter of dimension. The vectors $b_2,\ldots,b_n$ are linearly independent, being part of a basis, so $\dim\operatorname{span}(b_2,\ldots,b_n) = n-1$. On the other hand $\langle b_1,b_1\rangle = \pm 1 \neq 0$ forces $b_1 \neq 0$, so $\dim \operatorname{span}(b_1) = 1$, and Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement} gives $\dim \operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp = n-1$. A subspace contained in another of the same finite dimension equals it.

blocks.label: lem:orthonormal-basis-orthogonal-complement
blocks.title: Orthonormal Basis of the Complement of a Line
blocks.body: Let $q$ be a nondegenerate bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space $V$ and let $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ be an orthonormal basis for $V$. Then $(b_2,\ldots,b_n)$ is an orthonormal basis for $\operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp$ equipped with the restriction of $q$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_isOrthonormal_orthogonal_span_singleton
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_isOrthonormal_orthogonal_span_singleton (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) {m : ℕ}
    {b : Fin (m + 1) → V} (hbon : IsOrthonormal B b)
    (hspan : Submodule.span ℝ (Set.range b) = ⊤) :
    ∃ b' : Basis (Fin m) ℝ (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0})),
      IsOrthonormal (B.restrict (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0})))
        (b' : Fin m → B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0})) ∧
      ∀ i, (b' i : V) = b i.succ := by
  have htail := span_range_succ_eq_orthogonal hnd hbon hspan
  have hmem : ∀ i : Fin m, b i.succ ∈ B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0}) := fun i => by
    rw [← htail]
    exact Submodule.subset_span ⟨i, rfl⟩
  refine ⟨Basis.mk (v := fun i : Fin m => (⟨b i.succ, hmem i⟩ :
    B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0}))) ?_ ?_, ⟨?_, ?_⟩, fun i => by rw [Basis.coe_mk]⟩
  · exact LinearIndependent.of_comp (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0})).subtype
      (hbon.linearIndependent.comp _ (Fin.succ_injective m))
  · -- `span (range b') = ⊤` in `W`, pushed forward along the injective inclusion `W → V`.
    refine le_of_eq (Submodule.map_injective_of_injective
      (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {b 0})).subtype_injective ?_).symm
    rw [Submodule.map_span, ← Set.range_comp, Submodule.map_subtype_top]
    exact htail
  · intro i j hij
    rw [Basis.coe_mk]
    exact hbon.1 i.succ j.succ fun h => hij (Fin.succ_injective m h)
  · intro i
    rw [Basis.coe_mk]
    exact hbon.2 i.succ

end AdaptedTail

section Signature

variable [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}

deps.title: The Tail of an Orthonormal Basis Spans the Complement of its Head
deps.label: lem:orthonormal-tail-spans-orthogonal

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:orthonormal-tail-spans-orthogonal} the vectors $b_2,\ldots,b_n$ lie in $\operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp$ and span it. They are linearly independent in $V$, being part of a basis, and hence linearly independent as a family in the subspace, the inclusion being injective; so they form a basis of $\operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp$. Orthonormality is inherited, the restricted form taking the same values on them as $q$: $\langle b_i,b_j\rangle = 0$ for $i \neq j$, and $\langle b_i,b_i\rangle = \pm 1$.

blocks.label: lem:orthogonal-complement-non-null-line-hyperplane
blocks.title: The Complement of a Non-Null Line is a Hyperplane
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space of dimension $n$ and let $x \in V$ be nonzero. Then $\dim \operatorname{span}(x)^\perp = n - 1$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.finrank_orthogonal_span_singleton
lean.code:
theorem finrank_orthogonal_span_singleton (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) {x : V} (hx0 : x ≠ 0) :
    finrank ℝ (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x})) + 1 = finrank ℝ V := by
  have h := finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank hnd (Submodule.span ℝ {x})
  rw [finrank_span_singleton hx0] at h
  omega

blocks.tex: Since $x \neq 0$, the line $\operatorname{span}(x)$ has dimension $1$, and Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement} gives $\dim \operatorname{span}(x) + \dim \operatorname{span}(x)^\perp = n$.
This is the pointwise form of $\dim T_pM = \dim T_p\tilde M - 1$, and it is what makes Lemma \ref{lem:hyperplane-nondegenerate} applicable to the tangent space of a hypersurface.

blocks.label: lem:orthogonal-complement-non-null-line-nondegenerate
blocks.title: The Complement of a Non-Null Line is a Scalar Product Space
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space and let $x \in V$ satisfy $\langle x,x\rangle \neq 0$. Then the restriction of $q$ to $\operatorname{span}(x)^\perp$ is nondegenerate.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.restrict_orthogonal_span_singleton_nondegenerate
lean.code:
theorem restrict_orthogonal_span_singleton_nondegenerate (hB : B.IsSymm)
    (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) {x : V} (hx : B x x ≠ 0) :
    (B.restrict (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x}))).Nondegenerate := by
  have hline : (B.restrict (Submodule.span ℝ {x})).Nondegenerate := by
    have h := (isNondegenerateTuple_singleton hB hnd hx) 1 le_rfl
    rw [prefixSpan_singleton] at h
    exact h.2
  exact ((restrict_nondegenerate_tfae hB hnd (Submodule.span ℝ {x})).out 0 1).mp hline

deps.title: Orthonormal Basis Adapted to a Non-Null Line
deps.label: lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis-line

blocks.tex: The line $\operatorname{span}(x)$ is nondegenerate, as recorded in the proof of Lemma \ref{lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis-line}: an element $cx$ orthogonal to all of $\operatorname{span}(x)$ satisfies $c\langle x,x\rangle = 0$, and $\langle x,x \rangle \neq 0$ gives $c = 0$. Lemma \ref{lem:nondegenerate-subspace} then shows that $\operatorname{span}(x)^\perp$ is nondegenerate as well.

blocks.label: prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise
blocks.title: Signature of the Complement of a Non-Null Line
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space of signature $(r,s)$ and let $x \in V$ satisfy $\langle x,x\rangle \neq 0$. Let $(r',s')$ be the signature of $\operatorname{span}(x)^\perp$ with the restriction of $q$, which is a scalar product space by Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement-non-null-line-nondegenerate}. If $\langle x,x\rangle > 0$, then $r' + 1 = r$ and $s' = s$. If $\langle x,x \rangle < 0$, then $r' = r$ and $s' + 1 = s$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_orthogonal_span_singleton
lean.code:
theorem sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_orthogonal_span_singleton (hB : B.IsSymm)
    (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) {x : V} (hx : B x x ≠ 0) :
    (0 < B x x →
      sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap
          (B.restrict (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x})))) + 1
          = sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) ∧
        sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap
          (B.restrict (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x}))))
          = sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)) ∧
    (B x x < 0 →
      sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap
          (B.restrict (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x}))))
          = sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) ∧
        sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap
          (B.restrict (B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x})))) + 1
          = sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)) := by
  classical
  obtain ⟨m, b, hbon, hspan, hline⟩ := exists_isOrthonormal_head_span_eq hB hnd hx
  obtain ⟨hp, hn⟩ := sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_orthogonal_of_head hnd hbon hspan
  rw [hline] at hp hn
  -- `b 0` spans the same line as `x`, so `b 0 = c • x` with `c ≠ 0` and the two norms
  -- `⟪b₀,b₀⟫ = c²⟪x,x⟫` have the same sign.
  obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ : ∃ c : ℝ, c • x = b 0 :=
    Submodule.mem_span_singleton.1 (hline ▸ Submodule.mem_span_singleton_self (b 0))
  have hcx : B (b 0) (b 0) = c ^ 2 * B x x := by
    rw [← hc]
    simp only [map_smul, LinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    ring
  have hc0 : c ≠ 0 := by
    rintro rfl
    exact hbon.apply_self_ne_zero 0 (by rw [hcx]; ring)
  have hcsq : 0 < c ^ 2 := by positivity
  constructor
  · intro hxpos
    have h0 : B (b 0) (b 0) = 1 := by
      rcases hbon.2 0 with h | h
      · exact h
      · exact absurd (hcx ▸ h) (by nlinarith)
    rw [h0] at hp hn
    norm_num at hp hn
    omega
  · intro hxneg
    have h0 : B (b 0) (b 0) = -1 := by
      rcases hbon.2 0 with h | h
      · exact absurd (hcx ▸ h) (by nlinarith)
      · exact h
    rw [h0] at hp hn
    norm_num at hp hn
    omega

deps.title: Orthonormal Basis Adapted to a Non-Null Line
deps.label: lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis-line

deps.title: The Complement of a Non-Null Line is a Scalar Product Space
deps.label: lem:orthogonal-complement-non-null-line-nondegenerate

deps.title: Orthonormal Basis of the Complement of a Line
deps.label: lem:orthonormal-basis-orthogonal-complement

blocks.tex:
By Lemma \ref{lem:adapted-orthonormal-basis-line} there is an orthonormal basis $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ for $V$ with $\operatorname{span}(b_1) = \operatorname{span}(x)$, so that $\operatorname{span}(x)^\perp = \operatorname{span}(b_1)^\perp$; and by Lemma \ref{lem:orthonormal-basis-orthogonal-complement} the tail $(b_2,\ldots,b_n)$ is an orthonormal basis of that complement.

By Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia}, $r$ is the number of indices $i$ with $\langle b_i,b_i\rangle = 1$ and $s$ the number with $\langle b_i,b_i \rangle = -1$; likewise $r'$ and $s'$ are the corresponding counts over $i \geq 2$. The two pairs of counts therefore differ exactly by the contribution of the index $1$, which contributes to $r$ if $\langle b_1,b_1\rangle = 1$ and to $s$ if $\langle b_1,b_1 \rangle = -1$.

It remains to determine the sign of $\langle b_1, b_1 \rangle$. Since $\operatorname{span}(b_1) = \operatorname{span}(x)$, we may write $b_1 = cx$ with $c \neq 0$, and then $\langle b_1,b_1\rangle = c^2 \langle x,x\rangle$ has the same sign as $\langle x,x \rangle$. So if $\langle x,x\rangle > 0$ then $\langle b_1,b_1\rangle = 1$, giving $r' + 1 = r$ and $s' = s$; and if $\langle x,x\rangle < 0$ then $\langle b_1,b_1\rangle = -1$, giving $r' = r$ and $s' + 1 = s$.

Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise} is stated about the complement of a line, whereas Lee states his conclusion about the hyperplane $T_pM$ itself, and Lemma \ref{lem:hyperplane-nondegenerate} is about that hyperplane too. The two are identified by Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement-involution}: a hyperplane is the orthogonal complement of its own normal line. Making that identification puts both halves of the proposition in the same terms.

blocks.label: lem:hyperplane-eq-complement-normal-line
blocks.title: A Hyperplane is the Complement of its Normal Line
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space, let $W \subseteq V$ satisfy $\dim W + 1 = \dim V$, and let $x \in W^\perp$ be nonzero. Then $\operatorname{span}(x)^\perp = W$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.orthogonal_span_singleton_eq
lean.code:
theorem orthogonal_span_singleton_eq (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    {W : Submodule ℝ V} (hW : finrank ℝ W + 1 = finrank ℝ V) {x : V}
    (hx : x ∈ B.orthogonal W) (hx0 : x ≠ 0) :
    B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x}) = W := by
  rw [span_singleton_eq_orthogonal hnd hW hx hx0, orthogonal_orthogonal_eq_self hB hnd]

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement}, $\dim W^\perp = \dim V - \dim W = 1$. Since $x$ is a nonzero element of $W^\perp$, the line $\operatorname{span}(x)$ is a $1$-dimensional subspace of the $1$-dimensional space $W^\perp$, so $\operatorname{span}(x) = W^\perp$. Hence $\operatorname{span}(x)^\perp = (W^\perp)^\perp = W$, the last step by Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement-involution}.

blocks.label: prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise-hyperplane
blocks.title: Signature of a Hypersurface's Tangent Space
blocks.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space of signature $(r,s)$, let $W \subseteq V$ satisfy $\dim W + 1 = \dim V$, and let $x \in W^\perp$ be nonzero. Let $(r',s')$ be the signature of $W$ with the restriction of $q$. If $\langle x,x\rangle > 0$, then $r' + 1 = r$ and $s' = s$. If $\langle x,x\rangle < 0$, then $r' = r$ and $s' + 1 = s$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_of_mem_orthogonal
lean.code:
theorem sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_of_mem_orthogonal (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    {W : Submodule ℝ V} (hW : finrank ℝ W + 1 = finrank ℝ V) {x : V}
    (hx : x ∈ B.orthogonal W) (hx0 : x ≠ 0) :
    (0 < B x x →
      sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (B.restrict W)) + 1
          = sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) ∧
        sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (B.restrict W))
          = sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)) ∧
    (B x x < 0 →
      sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (B.restrict W))
          = sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) ∧
        sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (B.restrict W)) + 1
          = sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)) := by
  have hWeq : B.orthogonal (Submodule.span ℝ {x}) = W :=
    orthogonal_span_singleton_eq hB hnd hW hx hx0
  constructor
  · intro hxpos
    have h := (sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_orthogonal_span_singleton hB hnd hxpos.ne').1 hxpos
    rwa [hWeq] at h
  · intro hxneg
    have h := (sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_orthogonal_span_singleton hB hnd hxneg.ne).2 hxneg
    rwa [hWeq] at h

deps.title: A Hyperplane is the Complement of its Normal Line
deps.label: lem:hyperplane-eq-complement-normal-line

deps.title: Signature of the Complement of a Non-Null Line
deps.label: prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:hyperplane-eq-complement-normal-line}, $W = \operatorname{span}(x)^\perp$, so the claim is exactly Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise} applied to $x$.

Lemma \ref{lem:hyperplane-nondegenerate} and Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise-hyperplane} together are Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} at a single point: taking $V = T_p\tilde M$, $q = \tilde g_p$ and $W = T_pM$, whose orthogonal complement is $N_pM$, the first says that $\iota^*\tilde g$ is nondegenerate at $p$ exactly when every nonzero normal vector at $p$ is non-null, and the second says that the signature of $T_pM$ is then $(r-1,s)$ or $(r,s-1)$ according to the sign of the normal vectors. What Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} adds is the passage from a point to the manifold: that these pointwise conditions, holding at every $p$, make $\iota^*\tilde g$ a pseudo-Riemannian metric of constant signature.

That passage has exactly three steps. First, $T_pM$ and $N_pM$ must be a hyperplane and its complement \emph{in one and the same space}; they already are, both being subspaces of the ambient tangent space $T_{F(p)}\tilde{M}$, which is the fibre at $p$ of the pullback bundle $F^*T\tilde{M}$, so nothing need be transported. Second, $N_pM$, defined by orthogonality against the tangent space, must be recognized as the orthogonal complement of that tangent space. Third, the signature of $g$ at $p$ is that of a form on $T_pM$, whereas the pointwise results compute the signature of $\tilde{g}_{F(p)}$ restricted to $dF_p(T_pM)$, a form on a subspace of the ambient tangent space; these are different vector spaces, and $dF_p$ is an isometry between them.

blocks.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-eq-orthogonal
blocks.title: The Normal Space is an Orthogonal Complement
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold and let $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ be smooth. For every $x \in M$,
\[
  N_xM \;=\; \bigl(dF_x(T_xM)\bigr)^{\perp},
\]
the orthogonal complement being taken in the scalar product space $(T_{F(x)}\tilde{M}, \tilde{g}_{F(x)})$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.pseudoNormalSpace_eq_orthogonal
lean.code:
theorem pseudoNormalSpace_eq_orthogonal (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M')
    (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) (x : M) :
    pseudoNormalSpace g' f x = ((g'.pullback f).bilin x).orthogonal (tangentRange f x) := by
  ext v
  refine ⟨fun h w hw => ?_, fun h w hw => ?_⟩
  · show g'.form (f x) w v = 0
    rw [← g'.symm (f x) v w]
    exact h w hw
  · show g'.form (f x) v w = 0
    rw [g'.symm (f x) v w]
    exact h w hw

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] in

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

blocks.tex: Definition \ref{def:pseudo-normal-space} places $v$ in $N_xM$ when $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(v,w) = 0$ for every $w \in dF_x(T_xM)$, whereas $v$ lies in $(dF_x(T_xM))^\perp$ when $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(w,v) = 0$ for every such $w$. The two conditions differ only in which argument the tested vector occupies, and $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}$ is symmetric, so each is equivalent to the other term by term.

blocks.label: lem:hypersurface-tangent-hyperplane
blocks.title: A Hypersurface's Tangent Space is a Hyperplane
blocks.body:
Let $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ be smooth, let $x \in M$ be a point at which $dF_x$ is injective, and suppose $\dim M + 1 = \dim \tilde{M}$. Then
\[
  \dim dF_x(T_xM) + 1 \;=\; \dim T_{F(x)}\tilde{M} .
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.finrank_tangentRange_add_one
lean.code:
theorem finrank_tangentRange_add_one (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯)
    (himm : Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f x)) (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') :
    finrank ℝ (tangentRange f x) + 1
      = finrank ℝ (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x) := by
  -- Both rewrites must go through type-ascribed `have`s: in a pullback fibre the `finrank`
  -- of the goal and the `finrank` a bare `rw` would produce carry different (though defeq)
  -- module instances, so `rw` cannot fire.  Ascription forces elaboration at the goal's.
  have h1 : finrank ℝ (tangentRange f x) = finrank ℝ E := finrank_tangentRange f himm
  have h2 : finrank ℝ (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x) = finrank ℝ E' :=
    finrank_fibre (F := E') (V := ((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _))) x
  omega

blocks.tex: Since $dF_x$ is injective, it is a linear isomorphism onto its image, so $\dim dF_x(T_xM) = \dim T_xM = \dim M$. The ambient tangent space $T_{F(x)}\tilde{M}$ has dimension $\dim\tilde{M}$. Substituting both into the hypothesis $\dim M + 1 = \dim \tilde{M}$ gives the claim.
This is the codimension hypothesis in the form the pointwise results of this section ask for it, and it is the only place where $\dim M = \dim\tilde{M} - 1$ is used.

blocks.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-line
blocks.title: The Normal Space of a Hypersurface is a Line
blocks.body: Let $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold, let $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ be smooth with $\dim M + 1 = \dim\tilde{M}$, and let $x \in M$ be a point at which $dF_x$ is injective. Then $\dim N_xM = 1$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.finrank_pseudoNormalSpace
lean.code:
theorem finrank_pseudoNormalSpace (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M') (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯)
    (himm : Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f x)) (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') :
    finrank ℝ (pseudoNormalSpace g' f x) = 1 := by
  haveI : ∀ y : M, FiniteDimensional ℝ (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) y) :=
    fun y => finiteDimensional_fibre (F := E') y
  rw [pseudoNormalSpace_eq_orthogonal]
  have h := finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank
    ((g'.pullback f).bilin_nondegenerate x) (tangentRange f x)
  have h2 := finrank_tangentRange_add_one f himm hcodim
  -- `h` and `h2` end in the same fibre dimension, but the two occurrences carry different
  -- (defeq) module instances, so `omega` sees two atoms and fails.  `Eq.trans` closes the
  -- gap up to defeq; cancelling `finrank (tangentRange f x)` then finishes.
  exact Nat.add_left_cancel (h.trans h2.symm)

/-! ### One normal decides the sign

`finrank_pseudoNormalSpace` says `N_xM` is a line, and a line is spanned by any one of its
nonzero vectors.  So the hypothesis of Lee's Proposition 2.70 — a sign condition on *every*
nonzero normal — is really a condition on a *single* one, which is what a caller such as
Lee's Corollary 2.71 (where the distinguished normal is `\opnorm{grad} f`) actually has. -/

section RankOneSign

variable {V : Type*} [AddCommGroup V] [Module ℝ V]

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: The Normal Space is an Orthogonal Complement
deps.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-eq-orthogonal

deps.title: A Hypersurface's Tangent Space is a Hyperplane
deps.label: lem:hypersurface-tangent-hyperplane

blocks.tex: Write $W = dF_x(T_xM)$. By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-eq-orthogonal}, $N_xM = W^\perp$, so Lemma \ref{lem:orthogonal-complement} applied in the scalar product space $(T_{F(x)}\tilde{M}, \tilde{g}_{F(x)})$ gives $\dim W + \dim N_xM = \dim T_{F(x)}\tilde{M}$. Lemma \ref{lem:hypersurface-tangent-hyperplane} gives $\dim W + 1 = \dim T_{F(x)}\tilde{M}$. Cancelling $\dim W$ leaves $\dim N_xM = 1$.

This is why a \emph{single} non-null normal vector decides Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature}: any nonzero $v \in N_xM$ spans the line, so the sign of $\tilde{g}(v,v)$ cannot depend on which nonzero normal is chosen, rescaling $v \mapsto cv$ multiplying that number by $c^2 > 0$. We record this reduction, first as a statement about an arbitrary line and then in the form Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} consumes.

blocks.label: lem:line-sign-one-vector
blocks.title: The Sign of a Line is Decided by One of its Vectors
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be a real vector space, $B$ a bilinear form on $V$, and $N \leq V$ a subspace with
$\dim N = 1$. Suppose $x \in N$ is nonzero with $B(x,x) > 0$. Then $B(v,v) > 0$ for every
nonzero $v \in N$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.pos_of_mem_of_finrank_eq_one
lean.code:
theorem pos_of_mem_of_finrank_eq_one {N : Submodule ℝ V} (hN : finrank ℝ N = 1)
    (B : LinearMap.BilinForm ℝ V) {x : V} (hxN : x ∈ N) (hx : x ≠ 0) (hpos : 0 < B x x)
    {v : V} (hvN : v ∈ N) (hv : v ≠ 0) : 0 < B v v := by
  haveI : FiniteDimensional ℝ N := .of_finrank_pos (by rw [hN]; norm_num)
  have hspan : N = Submodule.span ℝ {x} :=
    (Submodule.eq_of_le_of_finrank_eq
      ((Submodule.span_le).mpr (Set.singleton_subset_iff.mpr hxN))
      (by rw [finrank_span_singleton hx, hN])).symm
  rw [hspan] at hvN
  obtain ⟨a, rfl⟩ := Submodule.mem_span_singleton.mp hvN
  have ha : a ≠ 0 := by rintro rfl; simp at hv
  have hexp : B (a • x) (a • x) = a * a * B x x := by
    simp only [map_smul, LinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    ring
  rw [hexp]
  exact mul_pos (mul_self_pos.mpr ha) hpos

blocks.tex:
Since $\dim N = 1$ and $x \in N$ is nonzero, the line $\mathbb{R}x$ is contained in $N$ and has the
same dimension, so $N = \mathbb{R}x$. A nonzero $v \in N$ is therefore $v = ax$ for some
$a \in \mathbb{R}$, and $a \neq 0$ because $v \neq 0$. Bilinearity gives
$B(v,v) = B(ax, ax) = a^2 B(x,x)$, a product of the positive numbers $a^2$ and $B(x,x)$.

blocks.label: lem:one-normal-decides-sign
blocks.title: One Positive Normal Makes Every Normal Positive
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$, $F$ and $x$ be as in Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-line}, and
suppose some nonzero $v_0 \in N_xM$ satisfies $\tilde{g}(v_0,v_0) > 0$. Then
$\tilde{g}(v,v) > 0$ for every nonzero $v \in N_xM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.forall_pos_of_pos_mem_pseudoNormalSpace
lean.code:
theorem forall_pos_of_pos_mem_pseudoNormalSpace (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M')
    (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) (himm : Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f x))
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E')
    {x₀ : ((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x}
    (hx₀ : x₀ ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x) (hx₀ne : x₀ ≠ 0)
    (hpos : 0 < (g'.pullback f).bilin x x₀ x₀) :
    ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x, v ≠ 0 → 0 < (g'.pullback f).bilin x v v :=
  fun _ hv hvne => pos_of_mem_of_finrank_eq_one
    (finrank_pseudoNormalSpace g' f himm hcodim) ((g'.pullback f).bilin x) hx₀ hx₀ne hpos hv hvne

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: The Sign of a Line is Decided by One of its Vectors
deps.label: lem:line-sign-one-vector

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Hypersurface is a Line
deps.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-line

blocks.tex:
By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-line}, $\dim N_xM = 1$. Apply Lemma
\ref{lem:line-sign-one-vector} to the subspace $N_xM$ of the ambient tangent space, the form
$\tilde{g}_{F(x)}$, and the vector $v_0$.

blocks.label: lem:one-normal-decides-sign-neg
blocks.title: One Negative Normal Makes Every Normal Negative
blocks.body:
Let $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$, $F$ and $x$ be as in Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-line}, and
suppose some nonzero $v_0 \in N_xM$ satisfies $\tilde{g}(v_0,v_0) < 0$. Then
$\tilde{g}(v,v) < 0$ for every nonzero $v \in N_xM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.forall_neg_of_neg_mem_pseudoNormalSpace
lean.code:
theorem forall_neg_of_neg_mem_pseudoNormalSpace (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M')
    (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) (himm : Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f x))
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E')
    {x₀ : ((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x}
    (hx₀ : x₀ ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x) (hx₀ne : x₀ ≠ 0)
    (hneg : (g'.pullback f).bilin x x₀ x₀ < 0) :
    ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x, v ≠ 0 → (g'.pullback f).bilin x v v < 0 :=
  fun _ hv hvne => neg_of_mem_of_finrank_eq_one
    (finrank_pseudoNormalSpace g' f himm hcodim) ((g'.pullback f).bilin x) hx₀ hx₀ne hneg hv hvne

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: One Positive Normal Makes Every Normal Positive
deps.label: lem:one-normal-decides-sign

deps.title: The Sign of a Line is Decided by One of its Vectors
deps.label: lem:line-sign-one-vector

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Hypersurface is a Line
deps.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-line

blocks.tex:
Apply Lemma \ref{lem:one-normal-decides-sign} to the form $-\tilde{g}$, for which $v_0$ and every
nonzero normal satisfy the positivity hypothesis and conclusion respectively; note $N_xM$ is the
same subspace for $\tilde{g}$ and $-\tilde{g}$.

This is the form in which the hypothesis of Proposition
\ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature} is met in practice: a caller does not verify a
sign for every normal vector, it exhibits one distinguished normal — for a regular level set, the
gradient of the defining function — and checks the sign there.

blocks.label: lem:exists-nonzero-normal
blocks.title: A Hypersurface has a Nonzero Normal Vector at Every Point
blocks.body: Let $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$, $F$ and $x$ be as in Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-line}. Then there is a nonzero $v \in N_xM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_ne_zero_mem_pseudoNormalSpace
lean.code:
theorem exists_ne_zero_mem_pseudoNormalSpace (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M')
    (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) (himm : Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f x))
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') :
    ∃ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x, v ≠ 0 := by
  haveI : ∀ y : M, FiniteDimensional ℝ (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) y) :=
    fun y => finiteDimensional_fibre (F := E') y
  haveI : Nontrivial (pseudoNormalSpace g' f x) :=
    finrank_pos_iff.mp (by rw [finrank_pseudoNormalSpace g' f himm hcodim]; norm_num)
  obtain ⟨v, hv⟩ := exists_ne (0 : pseudoNormalSpace g' f x)
  exact ⟨v, v.2, fun h => hv (Subtype.ext h)⟩

/-! ### The differential is an isometry onto the tangent space -/

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Hypersurface is a Line
deps.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-line

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-line}, $\dim N_xM = 1 > 0$, so $N_xM$ is not the zero subspace and therefore contains a nonzero vector.

Slight as it is, this is what allows the hypothesis ``$\tilde{g}(v,v) > 0$ for every nonzero normal vector $v$'' to be \emph{used} rather than merely assumed: were $N_xM$ trivial, that hypothesis would hold vacuously and yield nothing.

blocks.label: lem:differential-isometry-tangent-range
blocks.title: The Differential is an Isometry onto the Tangent Space
blocks.body: Let $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ be smooth and suppose the pseudo-Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$ is induced by $\tilde{g}$ along $F$. Then for every $x \in M$ the differential $dF_x$ is an isometry of scalar product spaces from $(T_xM, g_x)$ onto $\bigl(dF_x(T_xM),\, \tilde{g}_{F(x)}|_{dF_x(T_xM)}\bigr)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isometryEquivRestrictTangentRange
lean.code:
noncomputable def isometryEquivRestrictTangentRange (hg : IsPullbackAlong I I' g g' f) (x : M) :
    (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (g.bilin x)).IsometryEquiv
      (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap
        (((g'.pullback f).bilin x).restrict (tangentRange f x))) where
  toLinearEquiv :=
    LinearEquiv.ofInjective
      (show TangentSpace I x →ₗ[ℝ] ((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x from
        (mfderiv I I' f x).toLinearMap)
      (injective_mfderiv_of_isPullbackAlong hg x)
  map_app' v := (hg x v v).symm

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] in

deps.title: The Tensor Induced Along a Map
deps.label: def:induced-tensor-along-map

blocks.tex: By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-submanifold-immersion} the map $dF_x$ is injective, hence a linear isomorphism onto its image $dF_x(T_xM)$. It preserves the forms: for $v,w \in T_xM$, Definition \ref{def:induced-tensor-along-map} gives $g_x(v,w) = \tilde{g}_{F(x)}(dF_xv,\, dF_xw)$, and since $dF_xv$ and $dF_xw$ both lie in $dF_x(T_xM)$, the right-hand side is the restriction of $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}$ to that subspace evaluated at them. A form-preserving linear isomorphism is an isometry.

This is the formal content of Lee's ``we usually identify $T_pM$ with its image in $T_p\tilde{M}$ under $d\iota_p$''. The identification is not free of charge: $g_x$ is a form on $T_xM$, whereas the pointwise results compute the signature of $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}$ restricted to $dF_x(T_xM)$, a form on a subspace of a different vector space. The isometry is what makes the two signatures the same number.

blocks.label: lem:submanifold-signature-in-ambient-fibre
blocks.title: The Signature of a Submanifold is Computed in the Ambient Tangent Space
blocks.body: Let $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ be smooth and suppose the pseudo-Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$ is induced by $\tilde{g}$ along $F$. Then for every $x \in M$ the signature of $g$ at $x$ is the signature of the scalar product space $\bigl(dF_x(T_xM),\, \tilde{g}_{F(x)}|_{dF_x(T_xM)}\bigr)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigPosAt_eq_sigPos_restrict_tangentRange
lean.code:
theorem sigPosAt_eq_sigPos_restrict_tangentRange (hg : IsPullbackAlong I I' g g' f) (x : M) :
    g.sigPosAt x
      = sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap
          (((g'.pullback f).bilin x).restrict (tangentRange f x))) :=
  QuadraticMap.Equivalent.sigPos_eq ⟨isometryEquivRestrictTangentRange hg x⟩

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E'] in

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigNegAt_eq_sigNeg_restrict_tangentRange
lean.code:
theorem sigNegAt_eq_sigNeg_restrict_tangentRange (hg : IsPullbackAlong I I' g g' f) (x : M) :
    g.sigNegAt x
      = sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap
          (((g'.pullback f).bilin x).restrict (tangentRange f x))) :=
  QuadraticMap.Equivalent.sigNeg_eq ⟨isometryEquivRestrictTangentRange hg x⟩

/-! ### Lee's Proposition 2.70 -/

deps.title: The Differential is an Isometry onto the Tangent Space
deps.label: lem:differential-isometry-tangent-range

deps.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Metric at a Point
deps.label: def:pseudo-metric-signature-at

blocks.tex: By Definition \ref{def:pseudo-metric-signature-at} the signature of $g$ at $x$ is the signature of the scalar product space $(T_xM, g_x)$, and by Lemma \ref{lem:differential-isometry-tangent-range} the map $dF_x$ is an isometry from that space onto the displayed one. It therefore suffices that isometric scalar product spaces have the same signature. An isometry carries an orthonormal basis $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ of the source to a basis of the target, being a linear isomorphism, and that image basis is orthonormal with $\langle dF_xb_i, dF_xb_i\rangle = \langle b_i,b_i\rangle$ for every $i$, the forms being preserved. So the two bases have the same number of members of square $+1$ and the same number of square $-1$, and by Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia} those two counts are the signature of the respective space.

blocks.label: prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-nondegenerate
blocks.title: Nondegeneracy of a Hypersurface
blocks.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold and $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ is smooth with $\dim M + 1 = \dim\tilde{M}$, and let $x \in M$ be a point at which $dF_x$ is injective. Then the restriction of $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}$ to $dF_x(T_xM)$ is nondegenerate if and only if $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(v,v) \neq 0$ for every nonzero $v \in N_xM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.restrict_tangentRange_nondegenerate_iff
lean.code:
theorem restrict_tangentRange_nondegenerate_iff (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M')
    (f : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) (himm : Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' f x))
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') :
    (((g'.pullback f).bilin x).restrict (tangentRange f x)).Nondegenerate ↔
      ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x, v ≠ 0 → (g'.pullback f).bilin x v v ≠ 0 := by
  haveI : ∀ y : M, FiniteDimensional ℝ (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) y) :=
    fun y => finiteDimensional_fibre (F := E') y
  rw [pseudoNormalSpace_eq_orthogonal]
  exact restrict_nondegenerate_iff_forall_orthogonal ((g'.pullback f).bilin_isSymm x)
    ((g'.pullback f).bilin_nondegenerate x) (finrank_tangentRange_add_one f himm hcodim)

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: The Normal Space is an Orthogonal Complement
deps.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-eq-orthogonal

deps.title: A Hypersurface's Tangent Space is a Hyperplane
deps.label: lem:hypersurface-tangent-hyperplane

blocks.tex: Apply Lemma \ref{lem:hyperplane-nondegenerate} in the scalar product space $V = (T_{F(x)}\tilde{M}, \tilde{g}_{F(x)})$ with $W = dF_x(T_xM)$. Its hypothesis $\dim W + 1 = \dim V$ is Lemma \ref{lem:hypersurface-tangent-hyperplane}, available because $dF_x$ is injective. Its conclusion is that $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}|_W$ is nondegenerate if and only if every nonzero $v \in W^\perp$ satisfies $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(v,v) \neq 0$; and $W^\perp = N_xM$ by Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-eq-orthogonal}.

Here the induced tensor appears as $\tilde{g}$ restricted to $dF_x(T_xM)$ rather than as a metric on $M$, and $F$ is assumed only to be an immersion. This is forced: Definition \ref{def:induced-tensor-along-map} presupposes a \emph{nondegenerate} $g$ on $M$, and nondegeneracy of the induced tensor is precisely what this proposition decides, so it cannot appear among its hypotheses.

blocks.label: prop:hypersurface-signature-at-a-point
blocks.title: A Single Normal Vector Decides the Signature at a Point
blocks.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold of signature $(r,s)$ and $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ presents $M$ as a pseudo-Riemannian submanifold with induced metric $g$, where $\dim M + 1 = \dim\tilde{M}$. Let $x \in M$, let $(r_x,s_x)$ be the signature of $g$ at $x$, and let $v \in N_xM$ be nonzero. If $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(v,v) > 0$, then $r_x + 1 = r$ and $s_x = s$. If $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(v,v) < 0$, then $r_x = r$ and $s_x + 1 = s$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigPosAt_sigNegAt_of_mem_pseudoNormalSpace
lean.code:
theorem sigPosAt_sigNegAt_of_mem_pseudoNormalSpace (hg : IsPullbackAlong I I' g g' f)
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') {r s : ℕ} (hsig : HasSignature g' r s) (x : M)
    {v : ((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) x}
    (hv : v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x) (hv0 : v ≠ 0) :
    (0 < (g'.pullback f).bilin x v v → g.sigPosAt x + 1 = r ∧ g.sigNegAt x = s) ∧
      ((g'.pullback f).bilin x v v < 0 → g.sigPosAt x = r ∧ g.sigNegAt x + 1 = s) := by
  haveI : ∀ y : M, FiniteDimensional ℝ (((f : M → M') *ᵖ (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _)) y) :=
    fun y => finiteDimensional_fibre (F := E') y
  have himm := injective_mfderiv_of_isPullbackAlong hg x
  -- The ambient signature at `f x`, transported to the pullback fibre.  `pullback_bilin` is
  -- `rfl`, so the two `sigPos`es are the same term.
  obtain ⟨hr, hs⟩ := hsig (f x)
  have hrp : sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap ((g'.pullback f).bilin x)) = r := hr
  have hrn : sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap ((g'.pullback f).bilin x)) = s := hs
  have hpt := sigPos_sigNeg_restrict_of_mem_orthogonal ((g'.pullback f).bilin_isSymm x)
    ((g'.pullback f).bilin_nondegenerate x) (finrank_tangentRange_add_one f himm hcodim)
    (by rwa [← pseudoNormalSpace_eq_orthogonal]) hv0
  rw [sigPosAt_eq_sigPos_restrict_tangentRange hg x, sigNegAt_eq_sigNeg_restrict_tangentRange hg x]
  refine ⟨fun hpos => ?_, fun hneg => ?_⟩
  · obtain ⟨h1, h2⟩ := hpt.1 hpos
    exact ⟨h1.trans hrp, h2.trans hrn⟩
  · obtain ⟨h1, h2⟩ := hpt.2 hneg
    exact ⟨h1.trans hrp, h2.trans hrn⟩

deps.title: The Tensor Induced Along a Map
deps.label: def:induced-tensor-along-map

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: The Normal Space is an Orthogonal Complement
deps.label: lem:pseudo-normal-space-eq-orthogonal

deps.title: A Hypersurface's Tangent Space is a Hyperplane
deps.label: lem:hypersurface-tangent-hyperplane

deps.title: The Signature of a Submanifold is Computed in the Ambient Tangent Space
deps.label: lem:submanifold-signature-in-ambient-fibre

deps.title: Signature of a Hypersurface's Tangent Space
deps.label: prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise-hyperplane

blocks.tex: Put $V = (T_{F(x)}\tilde{M}, \tilde{g}_{F(x)})$ and $W = dF_x(T_xM)$. By Definition \ref{def:pseudo-metric-has-signature} applied at the point $F(x)$, the scalar product space $V$ has signature $(r,s)$. By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-submanifold-immersion} the differential $dF_x$ is injective, so Lemma \ref{lem:hypersurface-tangent-hyperplane} gives $\dim W + 1 = \dim V$; and $v$ is a nonzero element of $W^\perp$ by Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-normal-space-eq-orthogonal}. Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-signature-pointwise-hyperplane}, applied to $V$, $W$ and $v$, therefore computes the signature $(r',s')$ of $W$ with the restriction of $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}$: it satisfies $r' + 1 = r$ and $s' = s$ when $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(v,v) > 0$, and $r' = r$ and $s' + 1 = s$ when $\tilde{g}_{F(x)}(v,v) < 0$. Finally $(r',s') = (r_x,s_x)$ by Lemma \ref{lem:submanifold-signature-in-ambient-fibre}.

The conclusion is recorded as $r_x + 1 = r$ rather than $r_x = r - 1$. The two are equivalent, but the first carries the additional information that $r \geq 1$, which is what makes the subtraction in the statement below meaningful.

blocks.label: prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature
blocks.title: prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-signature
blocks.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold of signature $(r,s)$, and $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ presents $M$ as a pseudo-Riemannian submanifold with induced metric $g$, where $\dim M + 1 = \dim\tilde{M}$; the case Lee has in mind is that of a smooth hypersurface $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ and its inclusion $\iota$, with $g = \iota^*\tilde{g}$. If $\tilde{g}(v,v) > 0$ for every $p \in M$ and every nonzero normal vector $v \in N_pM$, then $g$ has signature $(r-1,s)$; and if $\tilde{g}(v,v) < 0$ for every such vector, then $g$ has signature $(r,s-1)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.hasSignature_of_forall_normal_pos
lean.code:
theorem hasSignature_of_forall_normal_pos (hg : IsPullbackAlong I I' g g' f)
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') {r s : ℕ} (hsig : HasSignature g' r s)
    (hpos : ∀ (x : M), ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x, v ≠ 0 →
      0 < (g'.pullback f).bilin x v v) :
    HasSignature g (r - 1) s := by
  intro x
  obtain ⟨v, hv, hv0⟩ := exists_ne_zero_mem_pseudoNormalSpace g' f
    (injective_mfderiv_of_isPullbackAlong hg x) hcodim
  obtain ⟨h1, h2⟩ :=
    (sigPosAt_sigNegAt_of_mem_pseudoNormalSpace hg hcodim hsig x hv hv0).1 (hpos x v hv hv0)
  exact ⟨by omega, h2⟩

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.hasSignature_of_forall_normal_neg
lean.code:
theorem hasSignature_of_forall_normal_neg (hg : IsPullbackAlong I I' g g' f)
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') {r s : ℕ} (hsig : HasSignature g' r s)
    (hneg : ∀ (x : M), ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' f x, v ≠ 0 →
      (g'.pullback f).bilin x v v < 0) :
    HasSignature g r (s - 1) := by
  intro x
  obtain ⟨v, hv, hv0⟩ := exists_ne_zero_mem_pseudoNormalSpace g' f
    (injective_mfderiv_of_isPullbackAlong hg x) hcodim
  obtain ⟨h1, h2⟩ :=
    (sigPosAt_sigNegAt_of_mem_pseudoNormalSpace hg hcodim hsig x hv hv0).2 (hneg x v hv hv0)
  exact ⟨h1, by omega⟩

/-! ### The induced metric exists -/

deps.title: The Tensor Induced Along a Map
deps.label: def:induced-tensor-along-map

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: A Single Normal Vector Decides the Signature at a Point
deps.label: prop:hypersurface-signature-at-a-point

deps.title: A Hypersurface has a Nonzero Normal Vector at Every Point
deps.label: lem:exists-nonzero-normal

blocks.tex: Take the first case, and fix $p \in M$; let $(r_p,s_p)$ be the signature of $g$ at $p$. By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-submanifold-immersion} the differential $dF_p$ is injective, so Lemma \ref{lem:exists-nonzero-normal} supplies a nonzero $v \in N_pM$. By hypothesis $\tilde{g}_{F(p)}(v,v) > 0$, so Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-signature-at-a-point} gives $r_p + 1 = r$ and $s_p = s$; in particular $r \geq 1$ and $(r_p,s_p) = (r-1,s)$. As $p$ was arbitrary, $g$ has signature $(r-1,s)$ in the sense of Definition \ref{def:pseudo-metric-has-signature}. The second case is identical, using instead the alternative $r_p = r$, $s_p + 1 = s$ of Proposition \ref{prop:hypersurface-signature-at-a-point}, which gives $s \geq 1$ and $(r_p,s_p) = (r,s-1)$.

blocks.label: lem:pseudo-hypersurface-induced-tensor-nondegenerate
blocks.title: The Induced Tensor of a Non-Null Hypersurface is Nondegenerate
blocks.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold and $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ is a smooth immersion with $\dim M + 1 = \dim\tilde{M}$, and suppose $\tilde{g}_{F(p)}(v,v) \neq 0$ for every $p \in M$ and every nonzero $v \in N_pM$. Then the pullback tensor $F^*\tilde{g}$ of Definition \ref{def:pseudo-pullback-form} is nondegenerate at every point: for every $p \in M$ and every nonzero $v \in T_pM$ there is a $w \in T_pM$ with $(F^*\tilde{g})_p(v,w) \neq 0$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_pseudoPullbackForm_ne_zero
lean.code:
theorem exists_pseudoPullbackForm_ne_zero (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M')
    (F : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯) (himm : ∀ p : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' F p))
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E')
    (hnull : ∀ (p : M), ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' F p, v ≠ 0 →
      (g'.pullback F).bilin p v v ≠ 0)
    (p : M) (v : TangentSpace I p) (hv : v ≠ 0) :
    ∃ w, pseudoPullbackForm g' F p v w ≠ 0 := by
  have hnd := (restrict_tangentRange_nondegenerate_iff g' F (himm p) hcodim).mpr (hnull p)
  by_contra hcon
  push Not at hcon
  -- `dF_p v` is a nonzero member of `T_pM ⊆ T_{F p}M̃` ...
  have hu0 : (⟨mfderiv I I' F p v, ⟨v, rfl⟩⟩ : tangentRange F p) ≠ 0 := by
    intro h
    exact hv (himm p (by simpa using congrArg Subtype.val h))
  -- ... and by hypothesis it pairs to zero with every tangent vector, contradicting
  -- nondegeneracy of the restricted form.
  refine hu0 (hnd.1 _ ?_)
  rintro ⟨-, w, rfl⟩
  exact hcon w

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: The Pullback of an Indefinite Metric
deps.label: def:pseudo-pullback-form

blocks.tex:
Fix $p \in M$ and a nonzero $v \in T_pM$, and put $W = dF_p(T_pM)$. By Proposition ef{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-nondegenerate} the restriction of $	ilde{g}_{F(p)}$ to $W$ is nondegenerate. The vector $u = dF_p(v)$ lies in $W$, and $u 
eq 0$ because $dF_p$ is injective and $v 
eq 0$. Nondegeneracy of the restriction therefore supplies a $u' \in W$ with $	ilde{g}_{F(p)}(u,u') 
eq 0$. Since $W$ is the image of $dF_p$, we may write $u' = dF_p(w)$ for some $w \in T_pM$, and then
\[
  (F^*	ilde{g})_p(v,w) = 	ilde{g}_{F(p)} igl(dF_pv,\, dF_pw igr) = 	ilde{g}_{F(p)}(u,u') 
eq 0 .
\]

This is what turns Proposition \ref{prop:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-nondegenerate} from a criterion into a construction, and with it the two halves of Lee's proposition combine into a single statement in which the induced metric is produced rather than presupposed.

blocks.label: cor:pseudo-riemannian-hypersurface-submanifold
blocks.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Hypersurface
blocks.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold of signature $(r,s)$ and $F : M \to \tilde{M}$ is a smooth immersion with $\dim M + 1 = \dim\tilde{M}$. If $\tilde{g}_{F(p)}(v,v) > 0$ for every $p \in M$ and every nonzero $v \in N_pM$, then the pullback tensor $F^*\tilde{g}$ is a pseudo-Riemannian metric on $M$ of signature $(r-1,s)$, so that $F$ presents $M$ as a pseudo-Riemannian submanifold of that signature. If instead $\tilde{g}_{F(p)}(v,v) < 0$ for every such $v$, then $F^*\tilde{g}$ is a pseudo-Riemannian metric of signature $(r,s-1)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.hasSignature_pseudoPullbackMetric_of_forall_normal_neg
lean.code:
theorem hasSignature_pseudoPullbackMetric_of_forall_normal_neg
    (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M') (F : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯)
    (himm : ∀ p : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' F p))
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') {r s : ℕ} (hsig : HasSignature g' r s)
    (hneg : ∀ (p : M), ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' F p, v ≠ 0 →
      (g'.pullback F).bilin p v v < 0) :
    HasSignature (pseudoPullbackMetric g' F (exists_pseudoPullbackForm_ne_zero g' F himm hcodim
      (fun p v hv hv0 => (hneg p v hv hv0).ne))) r (s - 1) :=
  hasSignature_of_forall_normal_neg (isPullbackAlong_pseudoPullbackMetric g' F _) hcodim hsig hneg

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.hasSignature_pseudoPullbackMetric_of_forall_normal_pos
lean.code:
theorem hasSignature_pseudoPullbackMetric_of_forall_normal_pos
    (g' : PseudoRiemannianMetric I' M') (F : C^∞⟮I, M; I', M'⟯)
    (himm : ∀ p : M, Function.Injective (mfderiv I I' F p))
    (hcodim : finrank ℝ E + 1 = finrank ℝ E') {r s : ℕ} (hsig : HasSignature g' r s)
    (hpos : ∀ (p : M), ∀ v ∈ pseudoNormalSpace g' F p, v ≠ 0 →
      0 < (g'.pullback F).bilin p v v) :
    HasSignature (pseudoPullbackMetric g' F (exists_pseudoPullbackForm_ne_zero g' F himm hcodim
      (fun p v hv hv0 => (hpos p v hv hv0).ne'))) (r - 1) s :=
  hasSignature_of_forall_normal_pos (isPullbackAlong_pseudoPullbackMetric g' F _) hcodim hsig hpos

deps.title: The Normal Space of a Pseudo-Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: def:pseudo-normal-space

deps.title: The Induced Tensor of a Non-Null Hypersurface is Nondegenerate
deps.label: lem:pseudo-hypersurface-induced-tensor-nondegenerate

deps.title: A Pseudo-Riemannian Pullback is an Induced Tensor
deps.label: lem:pseudo-riemannian-submanifold-induced-tensor

deps.title: The Metric Induced by an Indefinite Ambient Metric
deps.label: def:pseudo-induced-metric

blocks.tex: In either case every nonzero normal vector is non-null, so Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-hypersurface-induced-tensor-nondegenerate} shows that $F^*\tilde{g}$ is nondegenerate at every point of $M$, and Definition \ref{def:pseudo-induced-metric} accordingly makes $g = F^*\tilde{g}$ a pseudo-Riemannian metric on $M$.
