    (hf : ContDiffAt ℝ ∞ f a) (hsurj : Function.Surjective (fderiv ℝ f a)) :
    ∃ σ : E' → E, ContDiffAt ℝ ∞ σ (f a) ∧ σ (f a) = a ∧ ∀ᶠ y in 𝓝 (f a), f (σ y) = y := by
  set A := fderiv ℝ f a with hAdef
  set P := kerProj A with hPdef
  have hfd : HasFDerivAt f A a := (hf.differentiableAt (by simp)).hasFDerivAt
  -- `G` pairs `f` with the projection onto `ker (df a)`; its derivative is an isomorphism.
  set G : E → E' × (LinearMap.ker (A : E →ₗ[ℝ] E')) := fun x => (f x, P x) with hGdef
  have hGd : ContDiffAt ℝ ∞ G a := hf.prodMk (P.contDiff.contDiffAt)
  set Fe := prodKerProjEquiv A hsurj with hFedef
  have hGf : HasFDerivAt G (Fe : E →L[ℝ] E' × (LinearMap.ker (A : E →ₗ[ℝ] E'))) a := by
    rw [hFedef, prodKerProjEquiv_coe]
    exact hfd.prodMk P.hasFDerivAt
  have hn : (∞ : WithTop ℕ∞) ≠ 0 := by decide
  have hs : HasStrictFDerivAt G (Fe : E →L[ℝ] E' × (LinearMap.ker (A : E →ₗ[ℝ] E'))) a :=
    hGd.hasStrictFDerivAt' hGf hn
  -- The inverse function theorem inverts `G`; freezing the second slot at `P a` sections `f`.
  refine ⟨fun y => hGd.localInverse hGf hn (y, P a), ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · have ht : ContDiffAt ℝ ∞ (fun y : E' => (y, P a)) (f a) :=
      contDiffAt_id.prodMk contDiffAt_const
    exact (hGd.to_localInverse hGf hn).comp (f a) ht
  · exact hGd.localInverse_apply_image hGf hn
  · have ht : Tendsto (fun y : E' => (y, P a)) (𝓝 (f a)) (𝓝 (G a)) :=
      (continuous_id.prodMk continuous_const).continuousAt
    filter_upwards [ht.eventually hs.eventually_right_inverse] with y hy
    exact congrArg Prod.fst hy

end Euclidean

/-! ## The manifold local section theorem -/

section Manifold

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H} [I.Boundaryless]
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]
  {E' : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E'] [NormedSpace ℝ E'] [FiniteDimensional ℝ E']
  {H' : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H'] {I' : ModelWithCorners ℝ E' H'}
  {M' : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M'] [ChartedSpace H' M'] [IsManifold I' ∞ M']

omit [IsManifold I' ∞ M'] in
/-- **Lee, Theorem A.17**: *a smooth map whose differential at `x` is surjective
admits a smooth local section through `x`.*

There is a map `σ : M' → M`, smooth at `f x`, with `σ (f x) = x` and
`f (σ y) = y` for every `y` near `f x`.  Applied at every point of a submersion,
this is the statement that a submersion is locally a projection — which is how
Lee uses it, and it is what makes the quotient metric of Theorem 2.28 smooth.

Mathlib has no rank theorem, so this is proved directly from the inverse function

blocks.tex:
Let $P : E \to \ker df_a$ be a continuous linear projection and set $G(x) = (f(x), P(x))$, a map $E \to E' \times \ker df_a$ which is $C^\infty$ at $a$. Its differential at $a$ is $v \mapsto (df_a v, Pv)$, which is a linear isomorphism by Lemma \ref{lem:surjection-splits}. By the inverse function theorem $G$ has a local inverse $G^{-1}$ defined near $G(a)$, which is $C^\infty$ at $G(a)$ and satisfies $G^{-1}(G(a)) = a$ and $G(G^{-1}(z)) = z$ for $z$ near $G(a)$.

Set $\sigma(y) = G^{-1}(y, P(a))$. Then $\sigma$ is $C^\infty$ at $f(a)$, being $G^{-1}$ composed with the affine map $y \mapsto (y, P(a))$, which sends $f(a)$ to $G(a)$. Also $\sigma(f(a)) = G^{-1}(G(a)) = a$. Finally, $y \mapsto (y, P(a))$ is continuous and sends $f(a)$ to $G(a)$, so for $y$ near $f(a)$ we have $G(G^{-1}(y, P(a))) = (y, P(a))$; taking first components gives $f(\sigma(y)) = y$.

The same map $G$ carries more information than the local section extracted from it: because its first component is literally $f$, it is a chart in which the level sets of $f$ become affine slices. That is the local model for the regular level set theorem (Corollary \ref{cor:regular-level-set}), and it is recorded separately here.

blocks.label: prop:slice-chart-euclidean
blocks.title: Slice chart of a submersion, Euclidean form
blocks.body:
Let $E, E'$ be finite-dimensional real vector spaces, let $f : E \to E'$ be $C^\infty$ at $a$, and suppose $df_a$ is surjective. Set $G(x) = (f(x), P(x))$, where $P : E \to \ker df_a$ is a continuous linear projection. Then $G$ restricts to a homeomorphism of an open neighborhood $U$ of $a$ onto an open subset $G(U) \subseteq E' \times \ker df_a$; moreover $G$ is $C^\infty$ at $a$ and $G^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$ at $G(a)$, and the first component of $G$ is $f$. Consequently, for every $c \in E'$,
\[
G(U \cap f^{-1}(c)) = G(U) \cap \left(\{c\} \times \ker df_a\right);
\]
that is, in the coordinates $G$ the level set $f^{-1}(c)$ is the affine slice $\{c\} \times \ker df_a$, whose second factor is a linear subspace of dimension $\dim E - \dim E'$.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.sliceChart

blocks.tex:
By Lemma \ref{lem:surjection-splits}, $dG_a = (df_a, P)$ is a linear isomorphism $E \cong E' \times \ker df_a$, and $G$ is $C^\infty$ at $a$ because both components are. The inverse function theorem therefore supplies an open neighborhood $U$ of $a$ on which $G$ is a homeomorphism onto the open set $G(U)$, with $G$ smooth at $a$ and $G^{-1}$ smooth at $G(a)$.

The first component of $G$ is $f$ by construction, so for $x \in U$ we have $f(x) = c$ if and only if the first component of $G(x)$ is $c$, i.e.\ if and only if $G(x) \in \{c\} \times \ker df_a$. Thus $U \cap f^{-1}(c) = U \cap G^{-1}(\{c\} \times \ker df_a)$, and applying the bijection $G|_U$ gives $G(U \cap f^{-1}(c)) = G(U) \cap (\{c\} \times \ker df_a)$. Finally $\dim \ker df_a = \dim E - \dim E'$ by the rank–nullity theorem, $df_a$ being surjective.

blocks.label: prop:local-section-manifold
blocks.title: Local sections of a submersion
blocks.body: Let $\pi : M \to N$ be a smooth map of smooth manifolds, with $M$ modelled on a boundaryless model. If $d\pi_p$ is surjective, then there is $\sigma : N \to M$, smooth at $\pi(p)$, with $\sigma(\pi(p)) = p$ and $\pi(\sigma(y)) = y$ for every $y$ near $\pi(p)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.exists_localSection
lean.code:
theorem exists_localSection {f : M → M'} (hf : ContMDiff I I' ∞ f) {x : M}
    (hsurj : Function.Surjective (mfderiv I I' f x)) :
    ∃ σ : M' → M, ContMDiffAt I' I ∞ σ (f x) ∧ σ (f x) = x ∧
      ∀ᶠ y in 𝓝 (f x), f (σ y) = y := by
  set φ := extChartAt I x with hφdef
  set ψ := extChartAt I' (f x) with hψdef
  set a := φ x with hadef
  set F := writtenInExtChartAt I I' x f with hFdef
  have hxs : x ∈ φ.source := mem_extChartAt_source (I := I) x
  have hys : f x ∈ ψ.source := mem_extChartAt_source (I := I') (f x)
  have hinv : φ.symm a = x := extChartAt_to_inv (I := I) x
  -- `F a = ψ (f x)`: the chart round-trip at the centre is the identity.
  have hFa : F a = ψ (f x) := by rw [hFdef, writtenInExtChartAt, Function.comp_apply,
    Function.comp_apply, hinv]
  -- The chart representation is `C^∞` at `a` (`range I = univ` kills the `Within`).
  have hFd : ContDiffAt ℝ ∞ F a := by
    have h := (contMDiffAt_iff (I := I) (I' := I') (n := ∞) (f := f) (x := x)).mp hf.contMDiffAt
    have h2 := h.2
    rw [I.range_eq_univ, contDiffWithinAt_univ] at h2
    exact h2
  -- The submersion hypothesis, read in the chart: `mfderiv` *is* this `fderivWithin`.
  have hFsurj : Function.Surjective (fderiv ℝ F a) := by
    have hmd : MDifferentiableAt I I' f x := hf.mdifferentiableAt (by simp)
    rw [mfderiv, if_pos hmd, I.range_eq_univ, fderivWithin_univ] at hsurj
    exact hsurj
  obtain ⟨σE, hσEd, hσEa, hσEr⟩ := exists_localSection_of_surjective_fderiv hFd hFsurj
  refine ⟨fun y => φ.symm (σE (ψ y)), ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · -- `σ` is a composite of `ψ` (smooth), `σE` (smooth), and `φ.symm` (smooth on the
    -- chart target, which is open and contains `σE (ψ (f x)) = a`).
    have h1 : ContMDiffAt I' 𝓘(ℝ, E') ∞ ψ (f x) := contMDiffAt_extChartAt (I := I') (x := f x)
    have h2 : ContMDiffAt 𝓘(ℝ, E') 𝓘(ℝ, E) ∞ σE (ψ (f x)) := by
      rw [← hFa]; exact hσEd.contMDiffAt
    have hσa : σE (ψ (f x)) = a := by rw [← hFa]; exact hσEa
    have h3 : ContMDiffAt 𝓘(ℝ, E) I ∞ φ.symm (σE (ψ (f x))) := by
      rw [hσa]
      exact (contMDiffOn_extChartAt_symm (I := I) (n := ∞) x).contMDiffAt
        ((isOpen_extChartAt_target (I := I) x).mem_nhds
          (by rw [hadef]; exact mem_extChartAt_target (I := I) x))
    exact (h3.comp (f x) (h2.comp (f x) h1))
  · show φ.symm (σE (ψ (f x))) = x
    rw [← hFa, hσEa, hinv]
  · -- `ψ (f (σ y)) = ψ y` near `f x`; cancel `ψ`, which is injective on its source.
    have hcont : ContinuousAt ψ (f x) := continuousAt_extChartAt (I := I') (f x)
    have hpull : ∀ᶠ y in 𝓝 (f x), F (σE (ψ y)) = ψ y := by
      have ht : Tendsto ψ (𝓝 (f x)) (𝓝 (F a)) := by rw [hFa]; exact hcont
      exact ht.eventually hσEr
    -- `y` and `f (σ y)` must both sit in `ψ.source` for cancellation.
    have hy : ∀ᶠ y in 𝓝 (f x), y ∈ ψ.source := extChartAt_source_mem_nhds (I := I') (f x)
    have hfσ : ∀ᶠ y in 𝓝 (f x), f (φ.symm (σE (ψ y))) ∈ ψ.source := by
      have hc : ContinuousAt (fun y => f (φ.symm (σE (ψ y)))) (f x) := by
        have h1 : ContMDiffAt I' 𝓘(ℝ, E') ∞ ψ (f x) := contMDiffAt_extChartAt (I := I') (x := f x)
        have h2 : ContMDiffAt 𝓘(ℝ, E') 𝓘(ℝ, E) ∞ σE (ψ (f x)) := by
          rw [← hFa]; exact hσEd.contMDiffAt
        have hσa : σE (ψ (f x)) = a := by rw [← hFa]; exact hσEa
        have h3 : ContMDiffAt 𝓘(ℝ, E) I ∞ φ.symm (σE (ψ (f x))) := by
          rw [hσa]
          exact (contMDiffOn_extChartAt_symm (I := I) (n := ∞) x).contMDiffAt
            ((isOpen_extChartAt_target (I := I) x).mem_nhds
              (by rw [hadef]; exact mem_extChartAt_target (I := I) x))
        exact (hf.contMDiffAt.comp (f x) (h3.comp (f x) (h2.comp (f x) h1))).continuousAt
  -- … (truncated)

blocks.tex:
Write $\varphi$ for the chart of $M$ at $p$, $\psi$ for the chart of $N$ at $\pi(p)$, $a = \varphi(p)$, and $F = \psi \circ \pi \circ \varphi^{-1}$ for the chart representation. Then $F$ is $C^\infty$ at $a$, and $d\pi_p$ *is* $dF_a$ read through the charts, so $dF_a$ is surjective. Proposition \ref{prop:local-section-euclidean} gives a local section $\sigma_E$ of $F$ through $a$; set $\sigma = \varphi^{-1} \circ \sigma_E \circ \psi$.

Then $\sigma(\pi(p)) = \varphi^{-1}(\sigma_E(F(a))) = \varphi^{-1}(a) = p$, and $\sigma$ is smooth at $\pi(p)$ as a composite of $\psi$, $\sigma_E$ and $\varphi^{-1}$ — the last being smooth on the chart target, which is a neighbourhood of $a$ because the model is boundaryless. For $y$ near $\pi(p)$, $F(\sigma_E(\psi(y))) = \psi(y)$, which unwinds to $\psi(\pi(\sigma(y))) = \psi(y)$; since $\pi(\sigma(y))$ and $y$ both lie in the chart domain for $y$ near $\pi(p)$, and $\psi$ is injective there, $\pi(\sigma(y)) = y$.

blocks.label: prop:properties-of-submersions
blocks.title: Properties of Submersions

blocks.label: prop:uniqueness-smooth-quotients
blocks.title: Uniqueness of Smooth Quotients
blocks.body: Suppose $M$, $N_1$, and $N_2$ are smooth manifolds, and $\pi_1 : M \to N_1$ and $\pi_2 : M \to N_2$ are surjective smooth submersions that are constant on each other's fibers. Then there exists a unique diffeomorphism $F : N_1 \to N_2$ such that $F \circ \pi_1 = \pi_2$.

deps.title: Existence, Uniqueness, and Smoothness for Linear ODEs
deps.label: thm:linear-ode-existence-uniqueness

blocks.label: thm:global-rank-theorem
blocks.title: Global Rank Theorem
blocks.body:
Suppose $M$ and $N$ are smooth manifolds, and $F : M \to N$ is a smooth map of constant rank.

(a) If $F$ is surjective, then it is a smooth submersion.
(b) If $F$ is injective, then it is a smooth immersion.
(c) If $F$ is bijective, then it is a diffeomorphism.

blocks.tex:
Suppose $\tilde{M}$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary. An \emph{immersed $n$-dimensional submanifold} of $M$ is a subset $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ endowed with a topology that makes it into an $n$-dimensional topological manifold and a smooth structure such that the inclusion map $M \hookrightarrow \tilde{M}$ is a smooth immersion. It is called an \emph{embedded submanifold} if the inclusion is a smooth embedding, or equivalently if the given topology on $M$ is the subspace topology. Immersed and embedded submanifolds with boundary are defined similarly. In each case, the \emph{codimension} of $M$ is the difference $\dim \tilde{M} - \dim M$. A submanifold of codimension $1$ is known as a \emph{hypersurface}. Without further qualification, the word submanifold means an immersed submanifold, which includes an embedded one as a special case.

An embedded submanifold with or without boundary is said to be \emph{properly embedded} if the inclusion map is proper.

blocks.label: prop:properly-embedded-closed
blocks.title: prop:properly-embedded-closed
blocks.body: If $\tilde{M}$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, then an embedded submanifold $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ is properly embedded if and only if it is a closed subset of $\tilde{M}$.

blocks.tex: A properly embedded codimension-$0$ submanifold with boundary in $\tilde{M}$ is called a \emph{regular domain}.

blocks.label: prop:slice-coordinates
blocks.title: Slice Coordinates
blocks.body:
Let $\tilde{M}$ be a smooth $m$-manifold without boundary and let $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ be an embedded $n$-dimensional submanifold with or without boundary. Then for each $p \in M$ there exist a neighborhood $\tilde{U}$ of $p$ in $\tilde{M}$ and smooth coordinates $(x^1, \ldots, x^m)$ for $\tilde{M}$ on $\tilde{U}$ such that $M \cap \tilde{U}$ is a set of one of the following forms:
\[
M \cap \tilde{U} = \begin{cases}
\{x \in \tilde{U} : x^{n+1} = \cdots = x^m = 0\} & \text{if } p \notin \partial M, \\
\{x \in \tilde{U} : x^{n+1} = \cdots = x^m = 0 \text{ and } x^n \geq 0\} & \text{if } p \in \partial M.
\end{cases}
\]

In such a chart, $(x^1, \ldots, x^n)$ form smooth local coordinates for $M$.

blocks.label: thm:constant-rank-level-set
blocks.title: Constant-Rank Level Set Theorem
blocks.body: Suppose $\bar{M}$ and $N$ are smooth manifolds, and $\Phi: \bar{M} \to N$ is a smooth map with constant rank $r$. Every level set of $\Phi$ is a properly embedded submanifold of codimension $r$ in $M$.

blocks.label: cor:submersion-level-set
blocks.title: Submersion Level Set Theorem
blocks.body: Suppose $\bar{M}$ and $N$ are smooth manifolds, and $\Phi: \bar{M} \to N$ is a smooth submersion. Every level set of $\Phi$ is a properly embedded submanifold of $\bar{M}$, whose codimension is equal to $\dim N$.

blocks.tex: In fact, a map does not have to be a submersion, or even to have constant rank, for its level sets to be embedded submanifolds. If $\Phi: \bar{M} \to N$ is a smooth map, a point $p \in \bar{M}$ is called a \emph{regular point of $\Phi$} if the linear map $d\Phi_p: T_p \bar{M} \to T_{\Phi(p)} N$ is surjective, and $p$ is called a \emph{critical point of $\Phi$} if it is not. A point $c \in N$ is called a \emph{regular value of $\Phi$} if every point of $\Phi^{-1}(c)$ is a regular point of $\Phi$, and a \emph{critical value} otherwise. A level set $\Phi^{-1}(c)$ is called a \emph{regular level set of $\Phi$} if $c$ is a regular value of $\Phi$.

blocks.label: cor:regular-level-set
blocks.title: Regular Level Set Theorem
blocks.body: Let $\bar{M}$ and $N$ be smooth manifolds, and let $\Phi: \bar{M} \to N$ be a smooth map. Every regular level set of $\Phi$ is a properly embedded submanifold of $M$ whose codimension is equal to $\dim N$.

deps.title: Slice chart of a submersion, Euclidean form
deps.label: prop:slice-chart-euclidean

blocks.tex:
The local model is Proposition \ref{prop:slice-chart-euclidean}: at each regular point, the slice chart of $\Phi$ read in charts of $\bar{M}$ and $N$ exhibits the level set as an affine slice of dimension $\dim \bar{M} - \dim N$. What remains is the global step: assembling these charts into a smooth atlas on $\Phi^{-1}(c)$, with smooth transition maps on the overlaps, and identifying its topology with the subspace topology, which is what makes the embedding proper. That step is carried out below in the codimension-one case $N = \mathbb{R}$; the general case remains open.

Every application of this corollary in the present development takes $N = \mathbb{R}$, so the codimension-one case suffices throughout and nothing below depends on the general statement.

blocks.title: The regular level set theorem in codimension one

blocks.tex:
Throughout this subsection $M$ is a smooth manifold of dimension $n+1$ without boundary, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and $c \in \mathbb{R}$ is a regular value of $f$, so that $df_x \neq 0$ for every $x \in f^{-1}(c)$.

The construction rests on a normal form: near a regular point, a chart can be corrected so that $f$ becomes an \emph{affine} coordinate function, whereupon the level set becomes an affine hyperplane slice. The correction is built by perturbing the identity, which avoids choosing any splitting of the model space.

blocks.label: lem:straighten-scalar-affine
blocks.title: Straightening a scalar function into an affine coordinate
blocks.body:
Let $E$ be a Banach space, let $s \subseteq E$ be open, let $f : E \to \mathbb{R}$ be smooth on $s$, and let $u_0 \in s$ satisfy $df_{u_0} = \ell \neq 0$. Then there is a diffeomorphism $G$ between open subsets of $E$ with $u_0 \in \operatorname{dom} G \subseteq s$ and $G(u_0) = u_0$, such that
\[
  f(G^{-1}(v)) = f(u_0) + \ell(v - u_0)
\]
for every $v$ in the image of $G$. In particular $G$ carries $f^{-1}(f(u_0))$ onto the affine hyperplane slice $\{v : \ell(v - u_0) = 0\}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_openPartialHomeomorph_comp_symm_eq_affine
lean.code:
theorem exists_openPartialHomeomorph_comp_symm_eq_affine [CompleteSpace E]
    {f : E → ℝ} {s : Set E} (hs : IsOpen s) (hf : ContDiffOn ℝ ∞ f s)
    {u₀ : E} (hu₀ : u₀ ∈ s) {ℓ : E →L[ℝ] ℝ} (hf' : HasFDerivAt f ℓ u₀)
    (hl : ℓ ≠ 0) :
    ∃ G : OpenPartialHomeomorph E E, G.source ⊆ s ∧ u₀ ∈ G.source ∧
      G u₀ = u₀ ∧ ContDiffOn ℝ ∞ G G.source ∧
      ContDiffOn ℝ ∞ G.symm G.target ∧
      ∀ v ∈ G.target, f (G.symm v) = f u₀ + ℓ (v - u₀) := by
  classical
  -- a direction `e` on which `ℓ` is `1`
  obtain ⟨w, hw⟩ : ∃ w, ℓ w ≠ 0 := by
    by_contra h
    push Not at h
    exact hl (ContinuousLinearMap.ext fun v => by simpa using h v)
  set e : E := (ℓ w)⁻¹ • w with he_def
  have he : ℓ e = 1 := by
    rw [he_def, map_smul, smul_eq_mul, inv_mul_cancel₀ hw]
  -- the straightening map and its regularity
  have hg_smooth : ContDiffOn ℝ ∞ (straighteningMap f u₀ ℓ e) s :=
    contDiffOn_straighteningMap hf u₀ ℓ e
  have hgAt : ContDiffAt ℝ ∞ (straighteningMap f u₀ ℓ e) u₀ :=
    hg_smooth.contDiffAt (hs.mem_nhds hu₀)
  have hg' := hasFDerivAt_straighteningMap hf' e
  have hn : (∞ : WithTop ℕ∞) ≠ 0 := by simp
  -- the inverse function theorem produces the local diffeomorphism
  set G₀ : OpenPartialHomeomorph E E :=
    hgAt.toOpenPartialHomeomorph (straighteningMap f u₀ ℓ e) hg' hn with hG₀_def
  have hG₀_coe : (G₀ : E → E) = straighteningMap f u₀ ℓ e := rfl
  have hu₀_G₀ : u₀ ∈ G₀.source :=
    hgAt.mem_toOpenPartialHomeomorph_source hg' hn
  -- the open locus where the derivative stays invertible
  set t : Set E := (s ∩ G₀.source) ∩
    (fderiv ℝ (straighteningMap f u₀ ℓ e)) ⁻¹' {L : E →L[ℝ] E | IsUnit L}
    with ht_def
  have ht_open : IsOpen t := by
    apply ContinuousOn.isOpen_inter_preimage
    · exact (hg_smooth.continuousOn_fderiv_of_isOpen hs
        (by exact_mod_cast le_top)).mono inter_subset_left
    · exact hs.inter G₀.open_source
    · exact Units.isOpen
  have ht_sub_s : t ⊆ s := fun u hu => hu.1.1
  have ht_sub_G₀ : t ⊆ G₀.source := fun u hu => hu.1.2
  have hu₀_t : u₀ ∈ t := by
    refine ⟨⟨hu₀, hu₀_G₀⟩, ?_⟩
    show IsUnit (fderiv ℝ (straighteningMap f u₀ ℓ e) u₀)
    rw [hg'.fderiv, show ((ContinuousLinearEquiv.refl ℝ E : E ≃L[ℝ] E) :
      E →L[ℝ] E) = 1 from ContinuousLinearMap.ext fun x => by
        simp [ContinuousLinearMap.one_def]]
    exact isUnit_one
  -- restrict to that locus
  refine ⟨G₀.restrOpen t ht_open, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · rw [OpenPartialHomeomorph.restrOpen_source]
    exact fun u hu => ht_sub_s hu.2
  · rw [OpenPartialHomeomorph.restrOpen_source]
    exact ⟨hu₀_G₀, hu₀_t⟩
  · exact straighteningMap_apply_self f u₀ ℓ e
  · rw [OpenPartialHomeomorph.restrOpen_source]
    exact hg_smooth.mono fun u hu => ht_sub_s hu.2
  · -- the inverse is `C^∞` on the whole target
    intro v hv
  -- … (truncated)

blocks.tex:
Since $\ell \neq 0$, choose $e \in E$ with $\ell(e) = 1$, and set
\[
  G(u) = u + \bigl(f(u) - f(u_0) - \ell(u - u_0)\bigr)\, e .
\]
The coefficient $u \mapsto f(u) - f(u_0) - \ell(u - u_0)$ is smooth on $s$ and its derivative at $u_0$ is $df_{u_0} - \ell = 0$; hence $dG_{u_0} = \operatorname{id}_E$. By the inverse function theorem $G$ is a diffeomorphism of a neighbourhood of $u_0$ onto a neighbourhood of $G(u_0) = u_0$. Restricting the domain further to the open set where $dG$ is invertible makes $G^{-1}$ smooth on the whole image.

For the displayed identity, apply $\ell$ to the defining equation of $G$: for any $u$,
\[
  \ell(G(u) - u_0) = \ell(u - u_0) + \bigl(f(u) - f(u_0) - \ell(u - u_0)\bigr)\ell(e) = f(u) - f(u_0),
\]
using $\ell(e) = 1$. Putting $u = G^{-1}(v)$ gives $\ell(v - u_0) = f(G^{-1}(v)) - f(u_0)$, as required.

blocks.label: lem:adapted-chart-scalar-affine
blocks.title: The adapted chart at a regular point
blocks.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold without boundary, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and $y \in M$ with $df_y \neq 0$. Let $\kappa$ be a chart of $M$ at $y$. Then there is a diffeomorphism $G$ between open subsets of the model space, with $\kappa(y) \in \operatorname{dom} G \subseteq \operatorname{im} \kappa$ and $G(\kappa(y)) = \kappa(y)$, such that
\[
  f\bigl(\kappa^{-1}(G^{-1}(v))\bigr) = f(y) + df_y(v - \kappa(y))
\]
for every $v$ in the image of $G$. Thus in the corrected chart $G \circ \kappa$ the function $f$ is affine, and the level set $f^{-1}(f(y))$ is the affine hyperplane slice $\{v : df_y(v - \kappa(y)) = 0\}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_extChartAt_openPartialHomeomorph_comp_symm_eq_affine
lean.code:
theorem exists_extChartAt_openPartialHomeomorph_comp_symm_eq_affine
    {f : M → ℝ} (hf : ContMDiff I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞ f) (y : M)
    (hdf : mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f y ≠ 0) :
    ∃ G : OpenPartialHomeomorph E E,
      G.source ⊆ (extChartAt I y).target ∧
      extChartAt I y y ∈ G.source ∧
      G (extChartAt I y y) = extChartAt I y y ∧
      ContDiffOn ℝ ∞ G G.source ∧ ContDiffOn ℝ ∞ G.symm G.target ∧
      ∀ v ∈ G.target, f ((extChartAt I y).symm (G.symm v))
        = f y + mfderivReal (I := I) f y (v - extChartAt I y y) := by
  obtain ⟨G, h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6⟩ :=
    exists_openPartialHomeomorph_comp_symm_eq_affine
      (isOpen_extChartAt_target (I := I) y)
      (contDiffOn_comp_extChartAt_symm hf y)
      (mem_extChartAt_target (I := I) y)
      (hasFDerivAt_comp_extChartAt_symm hf y) hdf
  refine ⟨G, h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, fun v hv => ?_⟩
  have h := h6 v hv
  rw [Function.comp_apply, Function.comp_apply, extChartAt_to_inv] at h
  rw [mfderivReal_def]
  exact h

deps.title: Straightening a scalar function into an affine coordinate
deps.label: lem:straighten-scalar-affine

blocks.tex: The coordinate representative $f \circ \kappa^{-1}$ is smooth on the open set $\operatorname{im} \kappa$, and because $M$ has no boundary its derivative at $\kappa(y)$ is exactly $df_y$. Apply Lemma \ref{lem:straighten-scalar-affine} with $s = \operatorname{im}\kappa$, $u_0 = \kappa(y)$ and $\ell = df_y \neq 0$, and rewrite $f(\kappa^{-1}(G^{-1}(v)))$ using $\kappa^{-1} \circ \kappa = \operatorname{id}$ on the chart domain.

blocks.label: def:level-slice-chart
blocks.title: The slice chart of a level set
blocks.body:
With $M$, $f$, $c$ as above and $y \in f^{-1}(c)$, let $G$ be an adapted chart at $y$ as in Lemma \ref{lem:adapted-chart-scalar-affine}. Since $df_y \neq 0$, the kernel $\ker df_y$ is a hyperplane in the $(n+1)$-dimensional model space, so it has dimension $n$; fix a linear isomorphism $\iota : \mathbb{R}^n \to \ker df_y$ and let $\pi$ be the projection onto $\ker df_y$ along any vector $e$ with $df_y(e) = 1$, read in the coordinates $\iota$. The \emph{slice chart of $f^{-1}(c)$ at $y$} is the map
\[
  x \longmapsto \pi\bigl(G(\kappa(x)) - \kappa(y)\bigr),
\]
defined for $x \in f^{-1}(c)$ in the domain of $G \circ \kappa$, with inverse $z \mapsto \kappa^{-1}(G^{-1}(\kappa(y) + \iota(z)))$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.levelSliceChart
lean.code:
def levelSliceChart (hy : f y = c) :
    OpenPartialHomeomorph (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M) (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) where
  toFun x := sliceProj (I := I) n hdf
    (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf (extChartAt I y ↑x) - extChartAt I y y)
  invFun z :=
    if hz : z ∈ levelSliceTarget hf y hdf n then
      ⟨(extChartAt I y).symm ((adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).symm
        (extChartAt I y y + sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf z)),
        levelSliceInv_mem hf y hdf n c hz hy⟩
    else ⟨y, by simp [hy]⟩
  source := Subtype.val ⁻¹' levelChartDomain hf y hdf
  target := levelSliceTarget hf y hdf n
  map_source' x hx := by
    rw [mem_preimage] at hx
    have hxc : f ↑x = f y := by rw [levelSet_prop c x, hy]
    show extChartAt I y y + sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf _
      ∈ (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).target
    rw [levelSlice_emb_proj hf y hdf n hxc hx]
    exact (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).map_source hx.2
  map_target' z hz := by
    rw [mem_preimage, dif_pos hz]
    exact levelSliceInv_mem_domain hf y hdf n hz
  left_inv' x hx := by
    rw [mem_preimage] at hx
    have hxc : f ↑x = f y := by rw [levelSet_prop c x, hy]
    have hmem : sliceProj (I := I) n hdf
        (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf (extChartAt I y ↑x) - extChartAt I y y)
        ∈ levelSliceTarget hf y hdf n := by
      show extChartAt I y y + sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf _
        ∈ (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).target
      rw [levelSlice_emb_proj hf y hdf n hxc hx]
      exact (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).map_source hx.2
    rw [dif_pos hmem]
    refine Subtype.ext ?_
    show (extChartAt I y).symm ((adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).symm _) = ↑x
    rw [levelSlice_emb_proj hf y hdf n hxc hx,
      (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).left_inv hx.2,
      (extChartAt I y).left_inv hx.1]
  right_inv' z hz := by
    rw [dif_pos hz]
    have h1 : (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).symm
        (extChartAt I y y + sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf z)
        ∈ (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).source :=
      (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).map_target hz
    have h2 := adaptedStraightening_source_subset hf y hdf h1
    show sliceProj (I := I) n hdf
      (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf (extChartAt I y ((extChartAt I y).symm
        ((adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).symm
          (extChartAt I y y + sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf z)))) - extChartAt I y y) = z
    rw [(extChartAt I y).right_inv h2,
      (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).right_inv hz, add_sub_cancel_left,
      sliceProj_sliceEmb]
  open_source :=
    (isOpen_levelChartDomain hf y hdf).preimage continuous_subtype_val
  open_target := isOpen_levelSliceTarget hf y hdf n
  continuousOn_toFun := by
    have h1 : ContinuousOn
        (fun x : M => adaptedStraightening hf y hdf (extChartAt I y x))
        (levelChartDomain hf y hdf) :=
      (adaptedStraightening hf y hdf).continuousOn.comp
  -- … (truncated)

deps.title: The adapted chart at a regular point
deps.label: lem:adapted-chart-scalar-affine

blocks.label: prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one
blocks.title: A regular level set of a real-valued function is a smooth hypersurface
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth $(n+1)$-manifold without boundary, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and let $c$ be a regular value of $f$. Then the slice charts of Definition \ref{def:level-slice-chart} form a smooth atlas on $f^{-1}(c)$, making it a smooth $n$-manifold.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isManifold_levelSet
lean.code:
theorem isManifold_levelSet
    (hreg : ∀ x : M, f x = c → mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f x ≠ 0) :
    letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
    IsManifold (𝓡 n) ∞ (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M) := by
  letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
  refine isManifold_of_contDiffOn (𝓡 n) ∞ (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M) ?_
  intro e e' he he'
  obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := mem_iUnion.1 he
  obtain ⟨x', hx'⟩ := mem_iUnion.1 he'
  rw [mem_singleton_iff] at hx hx'
  subst hx hx'
  simp only [modelWithCornersSelf_coe, modelWithCornersSelf_coe_symm,
    Set.range_id, Set.inter_univ, Set.preimage_id, Function.comp_def, id_eq]
  exact contDiffOn_levelSliceChart_trans hf n c ↑x ↑x' _ _ _ _

deps.title: The adapted chart at a regular point
deps.label: lem:adapted-chart-scalar-affine

deps.title: The slice chart of a level set
deps.label: def:level-slice-chart

blocks.tex:
Each point of $f^{-1}(c)$ is regular, so a slice chart is defined at every point, and by Lemma \ref{lem:adapted-chart-scalar-affine} its inverse does land in $f^{-1}(c)$: for $v = \kappa(y) + \iota(z)$ one has $f(\kappa^{-1}(G^{-1}(v))) = f(y) + df_y(\iota(z)) = c$, since $\iota(z) \in \ker df_y$. That the two maps of Definition \ref{def:level-slice-chart} are mutually inverse is the identity $\iota\pi w = w$ for $w \in \ker df_y$, applied to $w = G(\kappa(x)) - \kappa(y)$, which lies in $\ker df_y$ exactly because $f(x) = f(y)$ and $f$ is affine in the corrected chart.

For compatibility, let $y, y'$ be two points of $f^{-1}(c)$ with slice charts built from $G, \kappa$ and $G', \kappa'$. On the overlap the transition map is
\[
  z \longmapsto \pi'\Bigl( G'\bigl(\kappa'(\kappa^{-1}(G^{-1}(\kappa(y) + \iota(z))))\bigr) - \kappa'(y') \Bigr),
\]
a composite of the affine map $z \mapsto \kappa(y) + \iota(z)$, the smooth map $G^{-1}$, the coordinate change $\kappa' \circ \kappa^{-1}$ of $M$, the smooth map $G'$, and the affine map $u \mapsto \pi'(u - \kappa'(y'))$. Each factor is smooth on its domain, so the transition map is smooth; hence the atlas is smooth.

blocks.label: prop:regular-level-set-inclusion-smooth
blocks.title: The inclusion of a regular level set is a smooth immersion
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one}, the inclusion $\iota_c : f^{-1}(c) \hookrightarrow M$ is smooth, and its differential is injective at every point. Since $\iota_c$ is a topological embedding, $f^{-1}(c)$ is an embedded smooth hypersurface in $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.contMDiff_levelSet_val
lean.code:
theorem contMDiff_levelSet_val
    (hreg : ∀ x : M, f x = c → mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f x ≠ 0) :
    letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
    ContMDiff (𝓡 n) I ∞ ((↑) : (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M) → M) := by
  letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
  intro x₀
  have hy : f ↑x₀ = c := levelSet_prop c x₀
  have hdf : mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f ↑x₀ ≠ 0 := hreg ↑x₀ hy
  rw [contMDiffAt_iff]
  refine ⟨continuous_subtype_val.continuousAt, ?_⟩
  have hz₀ : extChartAt (𝓡 n) x₀ x₀ ∈ levelSliceTarget hf ↑x₀ hdf n :=
    (levelSliceChart hf ↑x₀ hdf n c hy).map_source
      (mem_levelChartDomain_self hf ↑x₀ hdf)
  have ha : ContDiffAt ℝ ∞
      (fun w : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) =>
        extChartAt I (↑x₀ : M) ↑x₀ + sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf w)
      (extChartAt (𝓡 n) x₀ x₀) :=
    (contDiff_const.add (sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf).contDiff).contDiffAt
  have hb : ContDiffAt ℝ ∞ (adaptedStraightening hf ↑x₀ hdf).symm
      (extChartAt I (↑x₀ : M) ↑x₀
        + sliceEmb (I := I) n hdf (extChartAt (𝓡 n) x₀ x₀)) :=
    (contDiffOn_adaptedStraightening_symm hf ↑x₀ hdf).contDiffAt
      ((adaptedStraightening hf ↑x₀ hdf).open_target.mem_nhds hz₀)
  have hcomp := hb.comp (extChartAt (𝓡 n) x₀ x₀) ha
  exact ((hcomp.congr_of_eventuallyEq
    (writtenInExtChartAt_levelSet_val hf n c hreg x₀)).contDiffWithinAt)

deps.title: The slice chart of a level set
deps.label: def:level-slice-chart

deps.title: A regular level set of a real-valued function is a smooth hypersurface
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one

blocks.tex: Read in the slice chart at $x_0$ and the chart $\kappa$ of $M$, the inclusion is $z \mapsto G^{-1}(\kappa(x_0) + \iota(z))$, a composite of an affine map with a smooth map; hence it is smooth. Its differential at the centre is $d(G^{-1})_{\kappa(x_0)} \circ \iota$. Here $\iota$ is injective, being a linear isomorphism onto $\ker df_{x_0}$; and $d(G^{-1})_{\kappa(x_0)}$ is injective because $dG_{\kappa(x_0)} \circ d(G^{-1})_{\kappa(x_0)} = \operatorname{id}$, which follows by differentiating $G \circ G^{-1} = \operatorname{id}$ near $\kappa(x_0)$. A composite of injective maps is injective. Finally $\iota_c$ is a topological embedding because it is the inclusion of a subspace.

blocks.label: prop:regular-level-set-tangent-kernel
blocks.title: The tangent space of a regular level set is the kernel of the differential
blocks.body: In the situation of Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one}, for every $x_0 \in f^{-1}(c)$ the image of $d(\iota_c)_{x_0}$ is exactly $\ker df_{x_0}$; that is, $T_{x_0} f^{-1}(c) = \ker df_{x_0}$ under the usual identification.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.range_mfderiv_levelSet_val
lean.code:
theorem range_mfderiv_levelSet_val
    (hreg : ∀ x : M, f x = c → mfderiv I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) f x ≠ 0)
    (x₀ : (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M)) :
    letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
    (mfderiv (𝓡 n) I ((↑) : (f ⁻¹' {c} : Set M) → M) x₀ :
        EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] E).range
      = levelHyperplane (I := I) f ↑x₀ := by
  letI := levelSetChartedSpace hf n c hreg
  have hclean : LinearMap.range
      ((((fderiv ℝ (adaptedStraightening hf ↑x₀ (hreg ↑x₀ (levelSet_prop c x₀))).symm
          (extChartAt I (↑x₀ : M) ↑x₀))
        ∘L sliceEmb (I := I) n (hreg ↑x₀ (levelSet_prop c x₀))) :
          EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] E) : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →ₗ[ℝ] E)
      = levelHyperplane (I := I) f ↑x₀ := by
    refine Submodule.eq_of_le_of_finrank_eq ?_ ?_
    · rintro u hu
      obtain ⟨v, rfl⟩ := LinearMap.mem_range.1 hu
      have h := mfderivReal_mfderiv_levelSet_val hf n c hreg x₀ v
      rw [mfderiv_levelSet_val hf n c hreg x₀] at h
      exact (mem_levelHyperplane_iff (I := I) f ↑x₀ _).2 h
    · have hinj : Function.Injective
          ⇑((((fderiv ℝ (adaptedStraightening hf ↑x₀
                (hreg ↑x₀ (levelSet_prop c x₀))).symm
              (extChartAt I (↑x₀ : M) ↑x₀))
            ∘L sliceEmb (I := I) n (hreg ↑x₀ (levelSet_prop c x₀))) :
              EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) →L[ℝ] E)) :=
        (fderiv_adaptedStraightening_symm_injective hf ↑x₀
          (hreg ↑x₀ (levelSet_prop c x₀))).comp
          (sliceEmb_injective n (hreg ↑x₀ (levelSet_prop c x₀)))
      rw [LinearMap.finrank_range_of_inj hinj, finrank_euclideanSpace_fin,
        finrank_levelHyperplane n (hreg ↑x₀ (levelSet_prop c x₀))]
  rw [mfderiv_levelSet_val hf n c hreg x₀]
  exact hclean

deps.title: The inclusion of a regular level set is a smooth immersion
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-inclusion-smooth

deps.title: A regular level set of a real-valued function is a smooth hypersurface
deps.label: prop:regular-level-set-hypersurface-codim-one

blocks.tex:
For the inclusion $\subseteq$: the composite $f \circ \iota_c$ is the constant $c$ on $f^{-1}(c)$, so by the chain rule $df_{x_0} \circ d(\iota_c)_{x_0} = 0$, that is, every vector in the image of $d(\iota_c)_{x_0}$ lies in $\ker df_{x_0}$.

For equality, compare dimensions. By Proposition \ref{prop:regular-level-set-inclusion-smooth} the map $d(\iota_c)_{x_0}$ is injective on the $n$-dimensional space $T_{x_0} f^{-1}(c)$, so its image has dimension $n$. On the other side, $df_{x_0}$ is a nonzero linear functional on the $(n+1)$-dimensional space $T_{x_0}M$, so its kernel has dimension $n$. A subspace of dimension $n$ contained in a subspace of dimension $n$ equals it.

Suppose $\bar{M}$ is a smooth manifold and $M \subseteq \bar{M}$ is an embedded codimension-$k$ submanifold. A smooth map $\Phi: \bar{M} \to \mathbb{R}^k$ is called a \emph{defining function for $M$} if $M$ is a regular level set of $\Phi$. More generally, if $\Phi: U \to \mathbb{R}^k$ is a smooth map defined on an open subset $U \subseteq \bar{M}$ such that $M \cap U$ is a regular level set of $\Phi$, then $\Phi$ is called a \emph{local defining function for $M$}. The following is a partial converse to the submersion level set theorem.

blocks.label: prop:existence-local-defining-functions
blocks.title: Existence of Local Defining Functions
blocks.body: Suppose $\tilde{M}$ is a smooth manifold and $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ is an embedded submanifold of codimension $k$. For each point $p \in M$, there exist a neighborhood $U$ of $p$ in $\tilde{M}$ and a smooth submersion $\Phi: U \to \mathbb{R}^k$ such that $M \cap U$ is a level set of $\Phi$.

blocks.tex: If $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ is a submanifold (immersed or embedded), the inclusion map $\iota: M \hookrightarrow \tilde{M}$ induces an injective linear map $d\iota_p : T_p M \to T_p \tilde{M}$ for each $p \in M$. It is standard practice to identify $T_p M$ with its image $d\iota_p(T_p M) \subseteq T_p \tilde{M}$, thus regarding $T_p M$ as a subspace of $T_p \tilde{M}$. The next proposition gives two handy ways to identify this subspace in the embedded case.

blocks.label: prop:tangent-space-submanifold
blocks.title: Tangent Space to a Submanifold
blocks.body:
Suppose $\tilde{M}$ is a smooth manifold, $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ is an embedded submanifold, and $p \in M$. As a subspace of $T_p \tilde{M}$, the tangent space $T_p M$ is characterized by
\[
T_p M = \{v \in T_p \tilde{M} : vf = 0 \text{ whenever } f \in C^\infty(\tilde{M}) \text{ and } f|_M = 0\}.
\]

If $\Phi$ is a local defining function for $M$ on a neighborhood of $p$, then
\[
T_p M = \ker d\Phi_p.
\]

blocks.label: prop:lagrange-multiplier-rule
blocks.title: Lagrange Multiplier Rule
blocks.body: Let $\tilde{M}$ be a smooth $m$-manifold and $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ be an embedded hypersurface. Suppose $f \in C^\infty(\tilde{M})$, and $p \in M$ is a point at which $f$ attains a local maximum or minimum value among points in $M$. If $\Phi: U \to \mathbb{R}$ is a local defining function for $M$ on a neighborhood $U$ of $p$, then there is a real number $\lambda$ (called a \textbf{Lagrange multiplier}) such that $df_p = \lambda d\Phi_p$.

blocks.label: prop:double-manifold
blocks.title: prop:double-manifold
blocks.body: Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold with nonempty boundary. Then $D(M)$ is a topological manifold without boundary, and it has a smooth structure such that the natural maps $M \to M_1 \to D(M)$ and $M \to M_2 \to D(M)$ are smooth embeddings. Moreover, $D(M)$ is compact if and only if $M$ is compact, and connected if and only if $M$ is connected.

blocks.tex:
Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary. The \emph{tangent bundle of $M$}, denoted by $TM$, is the disjoint union of the tangent spaces at all points of $M$:
\[
TM = \bigsqcup_{p \in M} T_p M.
\]
This set can be thought of both as a union of vector spaces and as a manifold in its own right. This kind of structure, called a \emph{vector bundle}, is extremely common in differential geometry, so we take some time to review the definitions and basic properties of vector bundles.

For any positive integer $k$, a \emph{smooth vector bundle of rank $k$} is a pair of smooth manifolds $E$ and $M$ with or without boundary, together with a smooth surjective map $\pi : E \to M$ satisfying the following conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] For each $p \in M$, the set $E_p = \pi^{-1}(p)$ is endowed with the structure of a $k$-dimensional real vector space.
\item[(ii)] For each $p \in M$, there exist a neighborhood $U$ of $p$ and a diffeomorphism $\Phi : \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times \mathbb{R}^k$ such that $\pi_U \circ \Phi = \pi$, where $\pi_U : U \times \mathbb{R}^k \to U$ is the projection onto the first factor; and for each $q \in U$, $\Phi$ restricts to a linear isomorphism from $E_q$ to $\{q\} \times \mathbb{R}^k \cong \mathbb{R}^k$.
\end{enumerate}

The space $M$ is called the \emph{base} of the bundle, $E$ is called its \emph{total space}, and $\pi$ is its \emph{projection}. Each set $E_p = \pi^{-1}(p)$ is called the \emph{fiber of $E$ over $p$}, and each diffeomorphism $\Phi : \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times \mathbb{R}^k$ described above is called a \emph{smooth local trivialization}.

It frequently happens that we are presented with a collection of vector spaces (such as tangent spaces), one for each point in a manifold, that we would like to ``glue together'' to form a vector bundle. There is a shortcut for showing that such a collection forms a vector bundle without first constructing a smooth manifold structure on the total space. As the next lemma shows, all we need to do is to exhibit the maps that we wish to regard as local trivializations and check that they overlap correctly.

blocks.label: lem:vector-bundle-chart-lemma
blocks.title: Vector Bundle Chart Lemma

blocks.tex:
The smooth $GL(k, \mathbb{R})$-valued maps $\tau_{\alpha\beta}$ of this lemma are called \textit{transition functions for $E$}.

If $\pi: E \to M$ is a smooth vector bundle over $M$, then a \textit{section of $E$} is a continuous map $\sigma: M \to E$ such that $\pi \circ \sigma = \text{Id}_M$, or equivalently, $\sigma(p) \in E_p$ for all $p$. If $U \subseteq M$ is an open set, then a \textit{local section of $E$ over $U$} is a continuous map $\sigma: U \to E$ satisfying the analogous equation $\pi \circ \sigma = \text{Id}_U$. A \textit{smooth (local or global) section of $E$} is just a section that is smooth as a map between smooth manifolds. If we need to speak of ``sections'' that are not necessarily continuous, we use the following terminology: a \textit{rough (local) section of $E$} is a map $\sigma: U \to E$ defined on some open set $U \subseteq M$, not necessarily smooth or even continuous, such that $\pi \circ \sigma = \text{Id}_U$.

A \textit{local frame for $E$} is an ordered $k$-tuple $(\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_k)$ of local sections over an open set $U$ whose values at each $p \in U$ constitute a basis for $E_p$. It is called a \textit{global frame} if $U = M$. If $\tau$ is a (local or global) section of $E$ and $(\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_k)$ is a local frame for $E$ over $U \subseteq M$, then the value of $\tau$ at each $p \in U$ can be written
\begin{equation*}
\tau(p) = \tau^i(p)\sigma_i(p)
\end{equation*}
for some functions $\tau^1, \ldots, \tau^k: U \to \mathbb{R}$, called the \textit{component functions of $\sigma$ with respect to the given frame}.

The next lemma gives a criterion for smoothness that is usually easy to verify in practice.

blocks.label: lem:local-frame-criterion-for-smoothness
blocks.title: Local Frame Criterion for Smoothness
blocks.body: Let $\pi: E \to M$ be a smooth vector bundle, and let $\tau: M \to E$ be a rough section. If $(\sigma_i)$ is a smooth local frame for $E$ over an open subset $U \subseteq M$, then $\tau$ is smooth on $U$ if and only if its component functions with respect to $(\sigma_i)$ are smooth.

blocks.tex:
Write $\tau = \sum_i \tau^i \sigma_i$ on $U$, where $\tau^i : U \to \mathbb{R}$ are the component
functions.

Suppose first that each $\tau^i$ is smooth. Each $\sigma_i$ is a smooth section and each $\tau^i$
is a smooth function, so each product $\tau^i \sigma_i$ is a smooth section, and $\tau$, being the
finite sum $\sum_i \tau^i \sigma_i$, is smooth on $U$.

Conversely, suppose $\tau$ is smooth on $U$, and fix $p \in U$. Choose a smooth local
trivialization $\Phi : \pi^{-1}(W) \to W \times \mathbb{R}^k$ with $p \in W$, and for $x \in W$ let
$\Phi_x : E_x \to \mathbb{R}^k$ denote the induced linear isomorphism on the fibre. Reading a
section through $\Phi$ identifies the smooth sections of $E$ over $W$ with the smooth
$\mathbb{R}^k$-valued functions on $W$; in particular $x \mapsto \Phi_x(\tau(x))$ and each
$x \mapsto \Phi_x(\sigma_j(x))$ are smooth on $W$.

For $x \in U \cap W$ define $A(x) : \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^k$ by
\[
  A(x)c = \sum_j c^j \, \Phi_x(\sigma_j(x)).
\]
Each $A(x)$ is linear, and $x \mapsto A(x)$ is smooth as a map into the space of linear maps,
because it is a finite sum of the smooth maps $x \mapsto \Phi_x(\sigma_j(x))$ composed with the
fixed linear map sending a vector $v$ to the rank-one map $c \mapsto c^j v$.

Applying $\Phi_x$ to $\tau(x) = \sum_j \tau^j(x)\sigma_j(x)$ and using linearity gives
\[
  A(x)\bigl(\tau^1(x), \ldots, \tau^k(x)\bigr) = \Phi_x(\tau(x)), \qquad x \in U \cap W .
\]
At each $x \in U \cap W$ the map $A(x)$ is bijective: it is the composition of the coefficient
isomorphism $\mathbb{R}^k \to E_x$ determined by the basis $(\sigma_j(x))$ of $E_x$ with the
isomorphism $\Phi_x$. Since inversion is smooth on the set of invertible linear maps, and
$A(p)$ is invertible, $x \mapsto A(x)^{-1}$ is smooth near $p$. Hence
\[
  \bigl(\tau^1(x), \ldots, \tau^k(x)\bigr) = A(x)^{-1}\bigl(\Phi_x(\tau(x))\bigr)
\]
is smooth near $p$, being a composition of smooth maps. As $p \in U$ was arbitrary, each $\tau^i$
is smooth on $U$.

If $E \to M$ is a smooth vector bundle, then the set of smooth sections of $E$, denoted by $\Gamma(E)$, is a vector space (usually infinite-dimensional) under pointwise addition and multiplication by constants. Its zero element is the \textit{zero section} $\zeta$ defined by $\zeta(p) = 0 \in E_p$ for all $p \in M$. For every section $\sigma$ of $E$, the \textit{support of $\sigma$} is the closure of the set $\{p \in M: \sigma(p) \neq 0\}$.

Given a smooth vector bundle $\pi_E: E \to M$, a \textit{smooth subbundle of $E$} is a smooth vector bundle $\pi_D: D \to M$, in which $D$ is an embedded submanifold (with or without boundary) of $E$ and $\pi_D = \pi_E|_D$, such that for each $p \in M$, the subset $D_p = D \cap E_p$ is a linear subspace of $E_p$, and the vector space structure on $D_p$ is the one inherited from $E_p$. Given a collection of subspaces $D_p \subseteq E_p$, one for each $p \in M$, the following lemma gives a convenient condition for checking that their union is a smooth subbundle.

blocks.label: lem:local-frame-criterion-for-subbundles
blocks.title: Local Frame Criterion for Subbundles
blocks.body: Let $\pi: E \to M$ be a smooth vector bundle endowed with a smooth fibre metric $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$, and suppose that for each $p \in M$ we are given a $k$-dimensional linear subspace $D_p \subseteq E_p$. Suppose further that each $p \in M$ has a neighborhood $U$ on which there are smooth local sections $\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_k: U \to E$ such that $\sigma_1(q), \ldots, \sigma_k(q)$ form a basis for $D_q$ at each $q \in U$. Then $D = \bigcup_{p \in M} D_p \subseteq E$ is a smooth subbundle of $E$: it carries a smooth rank-$k$ vector bundle structure over $M$ whose fibre at $p$ is $D_p$ with the linear structure inherited from $E_p$.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.subContMDiffVectorBundle
lean.code:
theorem subContMDiffVectorBundle (h : HasLocalSubframes IB F n k D) :
    letI := subTotalSpaceTopology h
    letI := subFiberBundle h
    letI := subVectorBundle h
    ContMDiffVectorBundle n (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)) (fun x => ↥(D x)) IB :=
  letI := subVectorPrebundle_isContMDiff h
  (subVectorPrebundle h).contMDiffVectorBundle IB

blocks.label: rem:subbundle-criterion-metric
blocks.title: rem:subbundle-criterion-metric
blocks.body: Lee states the preceding lemma for a bare smooth vector bundle, with no fibre metric. The metric enters our proof only to normalize the given local $D$-frames, which is what makes the transition functions visibly smooth (they become Gram matrices); it is not needed for the truth of the statement. The hypothesis is harmless where the lemma is applied: Proposition \ref{prop:normal-bundle} applies it inside the ambient tangent bundle $F^*T\tilde M$ of Lemma \ref{lem:ambient-tangent-bundle-riemannian}, which carries the ambient metric $\tilde g$ by construction. Removing the hypothesis in general would require the statement that every smooth vector bundle over a smooth manifold admits a fibre metric --- the bundle analogue of Proposition \ref{prop:existence-riemannian-metric}, which we have only for tangent bundles.

blocks.tex:
Call a $k$-tuple $s = (s_1, \ldots, s_k)$ of smooth sections of $E$ over an open set $U$ a \emph{local $D$-frame} if $(s_1(q), \ldots, s_k(q))$ is a basis of $D_q$ for every $q \in U$, and an \emph{orthonormal} local $D$-frame if that basis is moreover orthonormal for the fibre metric.

\textit{Normalization.} Every local $D$-frame may be replaced by an orthonormal one on the same open set. Indeed, apply the fibrewise Gram--Schmidt algorithm of Proposition \ref{prop:existence-orthonormal-frames} to $(\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_k)$ inside the fibres of $E$: the resulting sections $(s_1, \ldots, s_k)$ are smooth on $U$, are orthonormal at every $q \in U$, and satisfy $\mathrm{span}(s_1(q), \ldots, s_k(q)) = \mathrm{span}(\sigma_1(q), \ldots, \sigma_k(q)) = D_q$. So by hypothesis every point of $M$ has a neighbourhood carrying an orthonormal local $D$-frame.

\textit{Fibre coordinates.} Let $s$ be an orthonormal local $D$-frame over $U$. For every $x \in M$ --- not only for $x \in U$ --- define
\[
  c_s(x) : D_x \to \mathbb{R}^k, \quad c_s(x)v = \bigl(\langle s_1(x), v\rangle, \ldots, \langle s_k(x), v\rangle\bigr),
  \qquad
  m_s(x) : \mathbb{R}^k \to D_x, \quad m_s(x)a = \sum_{i=1}^k a^i s_i(x).
\]
Each $c_s(x)$ is linear, for every $x$, because the fibre metric is linear in its second argument. For $x \in U$ the family $(s_i(x))_i$ is an orthonormal basis of $D_x$, so the coefficient of $s_i(x)$ in the expansion of $v \in D_x$ is $\langle s_i(x), v \rangle$; hence $c_s(x)$ is the coordinate isomorphism of that basis, $m_s(x)$ is its inverse, and $c_s(x)$ is a linear isometry of $D_x$ onto $\mathbb{R}^k$ with its standard inner product.

\textit{Charts.} For each orthonormal local $D$-frame $s$ over an open $U$ put
\[
  \Phi_s : \pi|_D^{-1}(U) \to U \times \mathbb{R}^k, \qquad \Phi_s(v) = (\pi(v), c_s(\pi(v))v).
\]
By the previous paragraph $\Phi_s$ is a bijection with inverse $(x,a) \mapsto m_s(x)a$, its restriction to each $D_x$ is a linear isomorphism onto $\{x\} \times \mathbb{R}^k$, and it covers the identity of $U$. By normalization the domains of these maps cover $M$.

\textit{Transition functions.} Let $s$ over $U$ and $t$ over $W$ be two orthonormal local $D$-frames and let $x \in U \cap W$. Using linearity of $c_t(x)$,
\[
  \bigl(c_t(x) \circ m_s(x)\bigr)(a)
   = c_t(x)\Bigl(\sum_i a^i s_i(x)\Bigr)
   = \Bigl(\sum_i a^i \langle t_j(x), s_i(x)\rangle\Bigr)_{j=1}^k
   = G(x)\,a,
\]
where $G(x)$ is the Gram matrix $\bigl(\langle t_j(x), s_i(x)\rangle\bigr)_{j,i}$. Thus $\Phi_t \circ \Phi_s^{-1}(x,a) = (x, G(x)a)$, which is of the required form (A.6); $G(x)$ is invertible on $U \cap W$, its inverse being the transition function of the pair taken in the other order. Each entry $x \mapsto \langle t_j(x), s_i(x)\rangle$ is smooth on $U \cap W$, being the pairing of two smooth sections of $E$ under a smooth fibre metric. Since $\mathrm{End}(\mathbb{R}^k)$ is finite dimensional and the entries of $G$ are its coordinates, $x \mapsto G(x)$ is smooth on $U \cap W$.

\textit{Conclusion.} The open cover of $M$ by the domains of the $\Phi_s$, the maps $\Phi_s$ themselves, and the Gram matrices as transition functions are exactly the data (i)--(iii) of the Vector Bundle Chart Lemma \ref{lem:vector-bundle-chart-lemma}, applied to the family of vector spaces $(D_p)_{p \in M}$, each of dimension $k$. It therefore endows $D$ with a smooth rank-$k$ vector bundle structure over $M$ for which the $\Phi_s$ are smooth local trivializations. Its fibre over $p$ is $D_p$ with the linear structure inherited from $E_p$, and its projection is the restriction of $\pi$; that is, $D$ is a smooth subbundle of $E$. Finally, the fibre topology that the construction puts on $D_p$ is the one $D_p$ already carries as a subspace of $E_p$, because $c_s(p)$ is a linear isometry onto $\mathbb{R}^k$ and hence a homeomorphism.

blocks.title: The Tangent Bundle and Vector Fields

blocks.tex:
We continue to assume that $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary. In this section, we see how the theory of vector bundles applies to the particular case of the tangent bundle.

Given any smooth local coordinate chart $(U, (x^i))$ for $M$, we can define a local trivialization of $TM$ over $U$ by letting $\Phi: \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times \mathbb{R}^n$ be the map sending $v \in T_pM$ to $(p, (v^1, \ldots, v^n))$, where $v^i\partial/\partial x^i|_p$ is the coordinate representation for $v$. Given another such chart $(\tilde{U}, (\tilde{x}^i))$ for $M$, we obtain a corresponding local trivialization $\tilde{\Phi}(v) = (p, (\tilde{v}^1, \ldots, \tilde{v}^n))$, where $v = \tilde{v}^i \partial/\partial \tilde{x}^i|_p$. The transformation law for coordinate vectors shows that
\[
\tilde{v}^j = \frac{\partial \tilde{x}^j}{\partial x^i}(p)v^i,
\]
so the transition function between two such charts is given by the smooth matrix-valued function $(\partial \tilde{x}^j/\partial x^i)$. Thus the chart lemma shows that these local trivializations turn $TM$ into a smooth vector bundle.

A smooth coordinate chart $(x^i)$ for $M$ also yields smooth local coordinates $(x^1,\ldots,x^n, v^1, \ldots, v^n)$ on $\pi^{-1}(U) \subseteq TM$, called \emph{natural coordinates for $TM$}, by following the local trivialization $\Phi$ with the map $U \times \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^n$ that sends $(p, (v^1, \ldots, v^n))$ to $(x^1(p), \ldots, x^n(p), v^1, \ldots, v^n)$.

A section of $TM$ is called a \emph{vector field on $M$}. To avoid confusion between the point $p \in M$ at which a vector field is evaluated and the action of the vector field on a function, we usually write the value of a vector field $X$ at $p \in M$ as $X_p \in T_pM$, or, if it is clearer (for example if $X$ itself has one or more subscripts), as $X|_p$.

For example, if $(U, (x^i))$ is a smooth coordinate chart for $M$, for each $i$ we get a smooth \emph{coordinate vector field} on $U$, denoted by $\partial/\partial x^i$ (and often abbreviated by $\partial_i$ if no confusion results), whose value at $p \in U$ is the coordinate vector $\partial/\partial x^i|_p$ defined above. Each coordinate vector field is smooth because its coordinate representation in natural coordinates is $(x^1, \ldots, x^n) \mapsto (x^1, \ldots, x^n, 0, \ldots, 0, 1, 0, \ldots, 0)$. The vector fields $\partial_1, \ldots, \partial_n$ form a smooth local frame for $TM$, called a \textbf{coordinate frame}.

It follows from Lemma A.33 that a vector field is smooth if and only if its components are smooth with respect to some smooth local frame (such as a coordinate frame) in a neighborhood of each point. It is standard to use the notation $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ as another name for $\Gamma(TM)$, the space of all smooth vector fields on $M$.

Now suppose $M$ and $N$ are smooth manifolds with or without boundary, and $F : M \to N$ is a smooth map. We obtain a smooth map $dF : TM \to TN$, called the \textbf{global differential of } $F$, whose restriction to each tangent space $T_p M$ is the linear map $dF_p$ defined above. In general, the global differential does not take vector fields to vector fields. In the special case that $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ and $Y \in \mathfrak{X}(N)$ are vector fields such that $dF(X_p) = Y_{F(p)}$ for all $p \in M$, we say that the vector fields $X$ and $Y$ are \textbf{F-related}.

blocks.label: lem:pushforward-vector-field
blocks.title: LeeSM, Prop. 8.19 \& Cor. 8.21

blocks.label: lem:derivation-characterization
blocks.title: LeeSM, Prop. 8.15
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary. A map $D : C^\infty(M) \to C^\infty(M)$ is a derivation if and only if it is of the form $Df = Xf$ for some $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$.

blocks.tex:
Given smooth vector fields $X, Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, define a map $[X, Y] : C^\infty(M) \to C^\infty(M)$ by
\[
[X, Y]f = X(Yf) - Y(Xf).
\]

A straightforward computation shows that $[X, Y]$ is a derivation of $C^\infty(M)$, and thus by Lemma A.37 it defines a smooth vector field, called the \textbf{Lie bracket of } $X$ and $Y$.

blocks.label: prop:lie-bracket-properties
blocks.title: Properties of Lie Brackets
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Prop. 8.28] Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary and $X, Y, Z \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $[X, Y]$ is bilinear over $\mathbb{R}$ as a function of $X$ and $Y$.
\item $[X, Y] = -[Y, X]$ (antisymmetry).
\item $[X, [Y, Z]] + [Y, [Z, X]] + [Z, [X, Y]] = 0$ (Jacobi identity).
\item For $f, g \in C^\infty(M)$, $[fX, gY] = fg[X, Y] + (fXg)Y - (gYf)X$.
\end{enumerate}

blocks.label: prop:naturality-of-lie-brackets
blocks.title: Naturality of Lie Brackets
blocks.body: Let $F : M \to N$ be a smooth map between manifolds with or without boundary, and let $X_1, X_2 \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ and $Y_1, Y_2 \in \mathfrak{X}(N)$ be vector fields such that $X_i$ is $F$-related to $Y_i$ for $i = 1, 2$. Then $[X_1, X_2]$ is $F$-related to $[Y_1, Y_2]$. In particular, if $F$ is a diffeomorphism, then $F_*[X_1, X_2] = [F_* X_1, F_* X_2]$.

blocks.tex: Now suppose $\tilde{M}$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary and $M \subseteq \tilde{M}$ is an immersed or embedded submanifold with or without boundary. The bundle $T\tilde{M}|_M$, obtained by restricting $T\tilde{M}$ to $M$, is called the \emph{ambient tangent bundle}. It is a smooth bundle over $M$ whose rank is equal to the dimension of $\tilde{M}$. The tangent bundle $TM$ is naturally viewed as a smooth subbundle of $T\tilde{M}|_M$, and smooth vector fields on $M$ can also be viewed as smooth sections of $T\tilde{M}|_M$. A vector field $X \in \mathfrak{X}(\tilde{M})$ always restricts to a smooth section of $T\tilde{M}|_M$, and it restricts to a smooth section of $TM$ if and only if it is \emph{tangent to $M$}, meaning that $X_p \in T_p M \subseteq T_p \tilde{M}$ for each $p \in M$.

blocks.label: cor:brackets-vector-fields-tangent-to-submanifolds
blocks.title: Brackets of Vector Fields Tangent to Submanifolds
blocks.body: Let $\tilde{M}$ be a smooth manifold and let $M$ be an immersed submanifold with or without boundary in $\tilde{M}$. If $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ are smooth vector fields on $\tilde{M}$ that are tangent to $M$, then $[Y_1, Y_2]$ is also tangent to $M$.

blocks.tex:
A \emph{curve} in a smooth manifold $M$ (with or without boundary) is a continuous map $\gamma: I \to M$, where $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is some interval. If $\gamma$ is smooth, then for each $t_0 \in I$ we obtain a vector $\gamma'(t_0) = d\gamma_{t_0}(d/dt|_{t_0})$, called the \emph{velocity of $\gamma$ at time $t_0$}. It acts on functions by
\[
\gamma'(t_0) f = (f \circ \gamma)'(t_0).
\]

In any smooth local coordinates, the coordinate expression for $\gamma'(t_0)$ is exactly the same as it would be in $\mathbb{R}^n$: the components of $\gamma'(t_0)$ are the ordinary $t$-derivatives of the components of $\gamma$.

If $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, then a smooth curve $\gamma: I \to M$ is called an \emph{integral curve of $X$} if its velocity at each point is equal to the value of $X$ there: $\gamma'(t) = X_{\gamma(t)}$ for each $t \in I$.

The fundamental fact about vector fields (at least in the case of manifolds without boundary) is that there exists a unique maximal integral curve starting at each point, varying smoothly as the point varies. These integral curves are all encoded into a
global object called a flow, which we now define.

Given a smooth manifold $M$ (without boundary), a flow domain for $M$ is an
open subset $\mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \times M$ with the property that for each $p \in M$, the set $\mathcal{D}^{(p)} =
\{t \in \mathbb{R} : (t, p) \in \mathcal{D}\}$ is an open interval containing 0. Given a flow domain $\mathcal{D}$ and a
map $\theta: \mathcal{D} \to M$, for each $t \in \mathbb{R}$ we let $M_t = \{p \in M : (t, p) \in \mathcal{D}\}$, and we define
maps $\theta_t: M_t \to M$ and $\theta^{(p)}: \mathcal{D}^{(p)} \to M$ by $\theta_t(p) = \theta(t, p)$.

A flow on $M$ is a continuous map $\theta: \mathcal{D} \to M$, where $\mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \times M$ is a flow
domain, that satisfies
\[
\theta_0 = \operatorname{Id}_M,
\]
\[
\theta_t \circ \theta_s(p) = \theta_{t+s}(p) \quad \text{wherever both sides are defined}.
\]

If $\theta$ is a smooth flow, we obtain a smooth vector field $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ defined by $X_p =
(\theta^{(p)})' (0)$, called the infinitesimal generator of $\theta$.

blocks.label: thm:fundamental-theorem-on-flows
blocks.title: Fundamental Theorem on Flows
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Thm. 9.12] Let $X$ be a smooth vector field on a smooth manifold $M$ (without boundary). There is a
unique smooth maximal flow $\theta: \mathcal{D} \to M$ whose infinitesimal generator is $X$. This
flow has the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item For each $p \in M$, the curve $\theta^{(p)}: \mathcal{D}^{(p)} \to M$ is the unique maximal integral
curve of $X$ starting at $p$.
\item If $s \in \mathcal{D}^{(p)}$, then $\mathcal{D}^{(\theta(s,p))}$ is the interval $\mathcal{D}^{(p)} - s = \{t - s : t \in \mathcal{D}^{(p)}\}$.
\item For each $t \in \mathbb{R}$, the set $M_t$ is open in $M$, and $\theta_t: M_t \to M_{-t}$ is a diffeomorphism with inverse $\theta_{-t}$.
\end{enumerate}

blocks.tex:
Although the fundamental theorem guarantees only that each point lies on an
integral curve that exists for a short time, the next lemma can often be used to prove
that a particular integral curve exists for all time.

blocks.label: lem:escape-lemma
blocks.title: Escape Lemma
blocks.body:
Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold and $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$.
If $\gamma: I \to M$ is a maximal integral curve of $X$ whose domain $I$ has a finite least
upper bound $b$, then for every $t_0 \in I$, $\gamma([t_0, b))$ is not contained in any compact
subset of $M$.

blocks.label: prop:canonical-form-vector-field
blocks.title: Canonical Form for a Vector Field
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Thm. 9.22] Let $X$ be a smooth vector field on a smooth manifold $M$, and suppose $p \in M$ is a point
where $X_p \ne 0$. There exist smooth coordinates $(x^i)$ on some neighborhood of $p$ in
which $X$ has the coordinate representation $\partial/\partial x^1$.

blocks.tex:
The fundamental theorem on flows leads to a way of differentiating one vector
field along the flow of another. Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold, $X, Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$,
and $\theta$ is the flow of $X$. The Lie derivative of $Y$ with respect to $X$ is the vector field
$\mathcal{L}_X Y$ defined by
\[
(\mathcal{L}_X Y)_p = \frac{d}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} d(\theta_{-t})_{\theta_t(p)}(Y_{\theta_t(p)}) = \lim_{t \to 0} \frac{d(\theta_{-t})_{\theta_t(p)}(Y_{\theta_t(p)}) - Y_p}{t}.
\]

This formula is useless for computation, however, because typically the flow of a vector field is difficult or impossible to compute explicitly. Fortunately, there is another expression for the Lie derivative that is much easier to compute.

blocks.label: prop:lie-derivative-equals-bracket
blocks.title: LeeSM, Thm.~9.38
blocks.body: Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold and $X, Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$. The Lie derivative of $Y$ with respect to $X$ is equal to the Lie bracket $[X,Y]$.

blocks.tex: One of the most important applications of the Lie derivative is as an obstruction to invariance under a flow. If $\theta$ is a smooth flow, we say that a vector field $Y$ is \emph{invariant under $\theta$} if $(\theta_t)_*Y = Y$ wherever the left-hand side is defined.

blocks.label: prop:invariant-vector-field
blocks.title: LeeSM, Thm.~9.42
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$. A smooth vector field is invariant under the flow of $X$ if and only if its Lie derivative with respect to $X$ is identically zero.

blocks.tex: A $k$-tuple of vector fields $X_1,\ldots,X_k$ is said to \emph{commute} if $[X_i, X_j] = 0$ for each $i$ and $j$.

blocks.label: prop:canonical-commuting-fields
blocks.title: Canonical Form for Commuting Vector Fields
blocks.body: [LeeSM, Thm.~9.46] Let $M$ be a smooth $n$-manifold, and let $(X_1,\ldots,X_k)$ be a linearly independent $k$-tuple of smooth commuting vector fields on an open subset $W \subseteq M$. For each $p \in W$, there exists a smooth coordinate chart $(U, (x^i))$ centered at $p$ such that $X_i = \partial/\partial x^i$ for $i = 1,\ldots,k$.

blocks.tex:
A \emph{covering map} is a surjective continuous map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ between connected and locally path-connected topological spaces, for which each point of $M$ has connected neighborhood $U$ that is \emph{evenly covered}, meaning that each connected component of $\pi^{-1}(U)$ is mapped homeomorphically onto $U$ by $\pi$. It is called a \emph{smooth covering map} if $\tilde{M}$ and $M$ are smooth manifolds with or without boundary and each component of $\pi^{-1}(U)$ is mapped \emph{diffeomorphically} onto $U$. For every evenly covered open set $U \subseteq M$, the components of $\pi^{-1}(U)$ are called the \emph{sheets of the covering over $U$}.

Here are the main properties of covering maps that we need.

blocks.label: prop:elementary-covering-maps
blocks.title: Elementary Properties of Smooth Covering Maps
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Prop.~4.33]
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item Every smooth covering map is a local diffeomorphism, a smooth submersion, an open map, and a quotient map.
\item An injective smooth covering map is a diffeomorphism.
\item A topological covering map is a smooth covering map if and only if it is a local diffeomorphism.
\end{enumerate}

blocks.label: prop:covering-proper-finite-sheeted
blocks.title: prop:covering-proper-finite-sheeted
blocks.body: A covering map is a proper map if and only if it is finite-sheeted.

blocks.label: ex:covering-n-torus
blocks.title: A Covering of the $n$-Torus
blocks.body: The $n$-torus is the manifold $\mathbb{T}^n = S^1 \times \cdots \times S^1$, regarded as the subset of $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$ defined by $(x^1)^2 + (x^2)^2 = 1,\ \ldots,\ (x^{2n-1})^2 + (x^{2n})^2 = 1$. The smooth map $X: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{T}^n$ given by $X(u^1, \ldots, u^n) = (\cos u^1, \sin u^1, \ldots, \cos u^n, \sin u^n)$ is a smooth covering map. $\mathbin{//}$

blocks.tex: If $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a covering map and $F: B \to M$ is a continuous map from a topological space $B$ into $M$, then a \emph{lift} of $F$ is a continuous map $\tilde{F}: B \to \tilde{M}$ such that $\pi \circ \tilde{F} = F$.

blocks.label: prop:lifts-smooth-maps-smooth
blocks.title: Lifts of Smooth Maps are Smooth
blocks.body:
If $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a smooth covering map, $B$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, and $F: B \to M$ is a smooth map, then every lift of $F$ is smooth.

Proof. Since $\pi$ is a smooth submersion, every lift $\tilde{F}: B \to \tilde{M}$ can be written locally as $\tilde{F} = \sigma \circ F$, where $\sigma$ is a smooth local section of $\pi$ (see Thm. A.17). $\square$

blocks.label: prop:lifting-properties-covering
blocks.title: Lifting Properties of Covering Maps
blocks.body:
Suppose $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a covering map.

(a) \textsc{Unique Lifting Property} [LeeTM, Thm. 11.12] If $B$ is a connected topological space and $F: B \to M$ is a continuous map, then any two lifts of $F$ that agree at one point are identical.

(b) \textsc{Path Lifting Property} [LeeTM, Cor. 11.14] Suppose $f: [0,1] \to M$ is a continuous path. For every $\tilde{p} \in \pi^{-1}(f(0))$, there exists a unique lift $\tilde{f}: [0,1] \to \tilde{M}$ of $f$ such that $\tilde{f}(0) = \tilde{p}$.

(c) \textsc{Monodromy Theorem} [LeeTM, Thm. 11.15] Suppose $f, g: [0,1] \to M$ are path-homotopic paths and $\tilde{f}, \tilde{g}: [0,1] \to \tilde{M}$ are their lifts starting at the same point. Then $f$ and $g$ are path-homotopic and $\tilde{f}(1) = \tilde{g}(1)$.

blocks.label: thm:injectivity-theorem
blocks.title: Injectivity Theorem
blocks.body: [LeeTM, Thm. 11.16] If $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a covering map, then for each point $\tilde{x} \in \tilde{M}$, the induced fundamental group homomorphism $\pi_*: \pi_1(\tilde{M}, \tilde{x}) \to \pi_1(M, \pi(\tilde{x}))$ is injective.

blocks.label: thm:lifting-criterion
blocks.title: Lifting Criterion
blocks.body: [LeeTM, Thm. 11.18] Suppose $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a covering map, $B$ is a connected and locally path-connected topological space, and $F: B \to M$ is a continuous map. Given $b \in B$ and $\tilde{x} \in \tilde{M}$ such that $\pi(\tilde{x}) = F(b)$, the map $F$ has a lift to $\tilde{M}$ if and only if $F_*(\pi_1(B,b)) \subseteq \pi_*(\pi_1(\tilde{M}, \tilde{x}))$.

blocks.label: cor:lifting-maps-simply-connected
blocks.title: Lifting Maps from Simply Connected Spaces
blocks.body: [LeeTM, Cor. 11.19] Suppose $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ and $F: B \to M$ satisfy the hypotheses of Theorem A.56, and in addition $B$ is simply connected. Then every continuous map $F: B \to M$ has a lift to $\tilde{M}$. Given any $b \in B$, the lift can be chosen to take $b$ to any point in the fiber over $F(b)$.

blocks.label: cor:covering-map-homeomorphism
blocks.title: Covering Map Homeomorphism Criterion
blocks.body:
A covering map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a homeomorphism if and only if the induced homomorphism $\pi_*: \pi_1(\tilde{M}, \tilde{x}) \to \pi_1(M, \pi(\tilde{x}))$ is surjective for some (hence every) $\tilde{x} \in \tilde{M}$. A smooth covering map is a diffeomorphism if and only if the induced homomorphism is surjective.

Proof. By Theorem A.56, the hypothesis implies that the identity map $\operatorname{Id}: M \to M$ has a lift $\widetilde{\operatorname{Id}}: \tilde{M} \to \tilde{M}$, which in this case is a continuous inverse for $\pi$. If $\pi$ is a smooth covering map, then the lift is also smooth. $\square$

blocks.label: cor:coverings-simply-connected
blocks.title: Coverings of Simply Connected Spaces
blocks.body: [LeeTM, Cor. 11.33] If $M$ is a simply connected manifold with or without boundary, then every covering of $M$ is a homeomorphism, and if $M$ is smooth, every smooth covering is a diffeomorphism.

blocks.label: prop:universal-covering-manifold
blocks.title: Existence of a Universal Covering Manifold
blocks.body: [LeeSM, Cor. 4.31] If $M$ is a connected smooth manifold, then there exists a simply connected smooth manifold $\tilde{M}$, called the universal covering manifold of $M$, and a smooth covering map $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$. It is unique in the sense that if $\tilde{M}'$ is any other simply connected smooth manifold that admits a smooth covering map $\pi': \tilde{M}' \to M$, then there exists a diffeomorphism $\Phi: \tilde{M} \to \tilde{M}'$ such that $\pi' \circ \Phi = \pi$.

blocks.label: prop:fiber-cardinality-fundamental-group
blocks.title: LeeTM, Cor. 11.31
blocks.body: With $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ as in the previous proposition, each fiber of $\pi$ has the same cardinality as the fundamental group of $M$.

deps.title: Existence of a Universal Covering Manifold
deps.label: prop:universal-covering-manifold

blocks.tex: Of all the constructions in smooth manifold theory, the ones that play the most fundamental roles in Riemannian geometry are \emph{tensors} and \emph{tensor fields}. Most of the technical machinery of Riemannian geometry is built up using tensors; indeed, Riemannian metrics themselves are tensor fields. This appendix offers a brief review of their definitions and properties. For a more detailed exposition of the material summarized here, see \cite{LeeSM}.

We begin with tensors on a finite-dimensional vector space. There are many ways of constructing tensors on a vector space, but for simplicity we will stick with the most concrete description, as real-valued multilinear functions. The simplest tensors are just linear functionals, also called \emph{covectors}.

Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional vector space (all of our vector spaces are assumed to be real). When we work with bases for $V$, it is usually important to consider \emph{ordered bases}, so we will assume that each basis comes endowed with a specific ordering. We use parentheses to denote ordered $k$-tuples, and braces to denote unordered ones, so an ordered basis is designated by either $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ or $(b_i)$, and the corresponding unordered basis by $\{b_1,\ldots,b_n\}$ or $\{b_i\}$.

The \emph{dual space} of $V$, denoted by $V^*$, is the space of linear maps from $V$ to $\mathbb{R}$. Elements of the dual space are called \emph{covectors} or \emph{linear functionals on $V$}. Under the operations of pointwise addition and multiplication by constants, $V^*$ is a vector space.

Suppose $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ is an ordered basis for $V$. For each $i = 1,\ldots,n$, define a covector $\beta^i \in V^*$ by
\[
\beta^i(b_j) = \delta^i_j,
\]
where $\delta^i_j$ is the \textbf{Kronecker delta symbol}, defined by
\[
\delta^i_j = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } i = j, \\ 0 & \text{if } i \ne j. \end{cases} \qquad \text{(B.1)}
\]

It is a standard exercise to prove that $(\beta^1,\ldots,\beta^n)$ is a basis for $V^*$, called the \textbf{dual basis to $(b_i)$}. Therefore, $V^*$ is finite-dimensional, and its dimension is the same as that of $V$. Every covector $\omega \in V^*$ can thus be written in terms of the dual basis as

\[
\omega = \omega_j \beta^j, \qquad \text{(B.2)}
\]

where the components $\omega_j$ are defined by $\omega_j = \omega(b_j)$. The action of $\omega$ on an arbitrary vector $v = v^i b_i \in V$ is then given by

\[
\omega(v) = \omega_j v^j. \qquad \text{(B.3)}
\]

(Here and throughout the rest of this appendix we use the Einstein summation convention; see p.~375.)

Every vector $v \in V$ uniquely determines a linear functional on $V^*$, by $\omega \mapsto \omega(v)$. Because we are assuming $V$ to be finite-dimensional, it is straightforward to show that this correspondence gives a canonical (basis-independent) isomorphism between $V$ and $V^{**}$ (the dual space of $V^*$). Given $\omega \in V^*$ and $v \in V$, we can denote the natural action of $\omega$ on $v$ either by the traditional functional notation $\omega(v)$, or by either of the more symmetric notations $\langle\omega, v\rangle$ or $\langle v, \omega\rangle$. The latter notations are meant to emphasize that it does not matter whether we think of the resulting number as the effect of the linear functional $\omega$ acting on the vector $v$, or as the effect of $v \in V^{**}$ acting on the covector $\omega$. Note that when applied to a vector and a covector, this pairing makes sense without any choice of an inner product on $V$.

blocks.title: Higher-Rank Tensors on a Vector Space

blocks.tex:
Now we generalize from linear functionals to multilinear ones. If $V_1,\ldots,V_k$ and $W$ are vector spaces, a map $F: V_1 \times \cdots \times V_k \to W$ is said to be \textbf{multilinear} if it is linear as a function of each variable separately, when all the others are held fixed:

\[
F(v_1,\ldots,av_i + a'v_i',\ldots,v_k) = aF(v_1,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_k) + a'F(v_1,\ldots,v_i',\ldots,v_k).
\]

Given a finite-dimensional vector space $V$, a \textbf{covariant $k$-tensor} on $V$ is a multilinear map

\[
F: \underbrace{V \times \cdots \times V}_{k \text{ copies}} \to \mathbb{R}.
\]

Similarly, a \textbf{contravariant $k$-tensor} on $V$ is a multilinear map

\[
F : \underbrace{V^* \times \cdots \times V^*}_{k \text{ copies}} \to \mathbb{R}.
\]

We often need to consider tensors of mixed types as well. A \emph{mixed tensor of type} $(k, l)$, also called a $k$\emph{-contravariant}, $l$\emph{-covariant tensor}, is a multilinear map
\[
F : \underbrace{V^* \times \cdots \times V^*}_{k \text{ copies}} \times \underbrace{V \times \cdots \times V}_{l \text{ copies}} \to \mathbb{R}.
\]

Actually, in many cases it is necessary to consider real-valued multilinear functions whose arguments consist of $k$ covectors and $l$ vectors, but not necessarily in the order implied by the definition above; such an object is still called a tensor of type $(k,l)$. For any given tensor, we will make it clear which arguments are vectors and which are covectors.

The spaces of tensors on $V$ of various types are denoted by
\begin{align*}
T^k(V^*) &= \{\text{covariant } k\text{-tensors on } V\}; \\
T^k(V) &= \{\text{contravariant } k\text{-tensors on } V\}; \\
T^{(k,l)}(V) &= \{\text{mixed } (k,l)\text{-tensors on } V\}.
\end{align*}

The \emph{rank} of a tensor is the number of arguments (vectors and/or covectors) it takes. By convention, a $0$-tensor is just a real number. (You should be aware that the notation conventions for describing the spaces of covariant, contravariant, and mixed tensors are not universally agreed upon. Be sure to look closely at each author's conventions.)

There is a natural product, called the \emph{tensor product}, linking the various tensor spaces over $V$: if $F \in T^{(k,l)}(V)$ and $G \in T^{(p,q)}(V)$, the tensor $F \otimes G \in T^{(k+p,l+q)}(V)$ is defined by
\[
\begin{split}
F \otimes G(\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^{k+p}, v_1, \ldots, v_{l+q}) \\
= F(\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k, v_1, \ldots, v_l) G(\omega^{k+1}, \ldots, \omega^{k+p}, v_{l+1}, \ldots, v_{l+q}).
\end{split}
\]

The tensor product is associative, so we can unambiguously form tensor products of three or more tensors on $V$. If $(b_i)$ is a basis for $V$ and $(\beta^j)$ is the associated dual basis, then a basis for $T^{(k,l)}(V)$ is given by the set of all tensors of the form
\begin{equation}
b_{i_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes b_{i_k} \otimes \beta^{j_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes \beta^{j_l}, \tag{B.4}
\end{equation}
as the indices $i_p, j_q$ range from $1$ to $n$. These tensors act on basis elements by
\[
b_{i_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes b_{i_k} \otimes \beta^{j_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes \beta^{j_l}(\beta^{s_1}, \ldots, \beta^{s_k}, b_{r_1}, \ldots, b_{r_l}) = \delta_{i_1}^{s_1} \cdots \delta_{i_k}^{s_k} \delta_{r_1}^{j_1} \cdots \delta_{r_l}^{j_l}.
\]

It follows that $T^{(k,l)}(V)$ has dimension $n^{k+l}$, where $n = \dim V$. Every tensor $F \in T^{(k,l)}(V)$ can be written in terms of this basis (using the summation convention) as
\begin{equation}
F = F_{j_1 \ldots j_l}^{i_1 \ldots i_k} b_{i_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes b_{i_k} \otimes \beta^{j_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes \beta^{j_l},
\tag{B.5}
\end{equation}
where
\[
F_{j_1 \ldots j_l}^{i_1 \ldots i_k} = F(\beta^{i_1}, \ldots, \beta^{i_k}, b_{j_1}, \ldots, b_{j_l}).
\]

If the arguments of a mixed tensor $F$ occur in a nonstandard order, then the horizontal as well as vertical positions of the indices are significant and reflect which arguments are vectors and which are covectors. For example, if $A$ is a $(1,2)$-tensor whose first argument is a vector, second is a covector, and third is a vector, its basis expression would be written
\[
A = A_i^{jk} \beta^i \otimes b_j \otimes \beta^k,
\]
where
\begin{equation}
A_i^{jk} = A(b_i, \beta^j, b_k).
\tag{B.6}
\end{equation}

There are obvious identifications among some of these tensor spaces:
\begin{align}
T^{(0,0)}(V) &= T^0(V) = T^0(V^*) = \mathbb{R},\\
T^{(1,0)}(V) &= T^1(V) = V,\\
T^{(0,1)}(V) &= T^1(V^*) = V^*,\\
T^{(k,0)}(V) &= T^k(V),\\
T^{(0,l)}(V) &= T^l(V^*).
\tag{B.7}
\end{align}

A less obvious, but extremely important, identification is the following:
\[
T^{(1,1)}(V) \cong \text{End}(V),
\]
where $\text{End}(V)$ denotes the space of linear maps from $V$ to itself (also called the \emph{endomorphism algebra} of $V$). This is a special case of the following proposition.

blocks.label: prop:multilinear-maps-tensor-isomorphism
blocks.title: prop:multilinear-maps-tensor-isomorphism
blocks.body:
Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional vector space. There is a natural (basis-independent) isomorphism between $T^{(k+1,l)}(V)$ and the space of multilinear maps
\[
\underbrace{V^* \times \cdots \times V^*}_{k \text{ copies}} \times \underbrace{V \times \cdots \times V}_{l \text{ copies}} \to V.
\]

blocks.tex:
The determinant is multiplicative on square matrices, $\det(MN) = \det M \det N$. For a product $MN^{T}$ of a $k \times n$ matrix with the transpose of another, the corresponding statement expands $\det(MN^{T})$ as a sum of products of $k \times k$ minors.
