It is recorded here in the form used to construct the inner product on alternating tensors, and in a generalization in which one of the two determinants is replaced by an arbitrary alternating map.

Throughout, $R$ is a commutative ring, $\iota$ and $\iota'$ are finite index sets with $\iota$ of cardinality $k$, and for a matrix $A$ with rows indexed by $\iota$ and columns by $\iota'$ and a map $s \colon \iota \to \iota'$ we write $A[\,\cdot\,, s]$ for the square matrix $(A_{i\,s(j)})_{i,j \in \iota}$ obtained by selecting the columns named by $s$, with repetition allowed.

blocks.label: lem:sum-sign-prod-det
blocks.title: lem:sum-sign-prod-det
blocks.body:
Let $C$ be a square matrix over $R$ indexed by $\iota$. Then
\[
\sum_{\sigma \in S_\iota} \sum_{\tau \in S_\iota} \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\operatorname{sgn}(\tau) \prod_{i \in \iota} C_{\sigma(i)\,\tau(i)} = k!\,\det C .
\]

lean.name: Matrix.sum_sum_sign_mul_sign_mul_prod
lean.code:
theorem sum_sum_sign_mul_sign_mul_prod (C : Matrix m m R) :
    ∑ σ : Equiv.Perm m, ∑ τ : Equiv.Perm m,
        ((Equiv.Perm.sign σ : ℤ) : R) * ((Equiv.Perm.sign τ : ℤ) : R) * ∏ i, C (σ i) (τ i)
      = (Fintype.card m).factorial * C.det := by
  have key : ∀ σ : Equiv.Perm m,
      (∑ τ : Equiv.Perm m,
        ((Equiv.Perm.sign σ : ℤ) : R) * ((Equiv.Perm.sign τ : ℤ) : R) * ∏ i, C (σ i) (τ i))
        = C.det := by
    intro σ
    rw [← Equiv.sum_comp (Equiv.mulRight σ)
      (fun τ => ((Equiv.Perm.sign σ : ℤ) : R) * ((Equiv.Perm.sign τ : ℤ) : R)
        * ∏ i, C (σ i) (τ i))]
    rw [← Matrix.det_transpose, Matrix.det_apply']
    refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun ρ _ => ?_
    have hprod : (∏ i, C (σ i) ((Equiv.mulRight σ ρ) i)) = ∏ i, Cᵀ (ρ i) i := by
      rw [← Equiv.prod_comp σ (fun j => Cᵀ (ρ j) j)]
      rfl
    rw [hprod]
    have hsign : ((Equiv.Perm.sign σ : ℤ) : R) * ((Equiv.Perm.sign (Equiv.mulRight σ ρ) : ℤ) : R)
        = ((Equiv.Perm.sign ρ : ℤ) : R) := by
      rcases Int.units_eq_one_or (Equiv.Perm.sign σ) with h | h <;>
        simp [Equiv.Perm.sign_mul, h]
    rw [hsign]
  rw [Finset.sum_congr rfl fun σ _ => key σ]
  simp [Finset.sum_const, Fintype.card_perm]

omit [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n] in

blocks.tex:
Fix $\sigma \in S_\iota$ and substitute $\tau = \rho\sigma$, which is a bijection of $S_\iota$ onto itself as $\rho$ ranges over $S_\iota$. Reindexing the product by $j = \sigma(i)$ gives $\prod_{i} C_{\sigma(i)\,\rho(\sigma(i))} = \prod_{j} C_{j\,\rho(j)}$, while $\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\operatorname{sgn}(\rho\sigma) = \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)^2\operatorname{sgn}(\rho) = \operatorname{sgn}(\rho)$. Hence the inner sum equals
\[
\sum_{\rho \in S_\iota} \operatorname{sgn}(\rho) \prod_{j \in \iota} C_{j\,\rho(j)},
\]
which is the Leibniz expansion of $\det C^{T} = \det C$. The inner sum is therefore $\det C$ for every $\sigma$, and $S_\iota$ has $k!$ elements.

blocks.label: lem:cauchy-binet
blocks.title: lem:cauchy-binet
blocks.body:
\textbf{(Cauchy--Binet formula.)} Let $M$ and $N$ be matrices over $R$ with rows indexed by $\iota$ and columns by $\iota'$. Then
\[
\sum_{s \colon \iota \to \iota'} \det\big(M[\,\cdot\,, s]\big)\,\det\big(N[\,\cdot\,, s]\big) = k!\,\det\big(MN^{T}\big),
\]
the sum running over all maps $s$, not only the injective or increasing ones.

lean.name: Matrix.sum_det_submatrix_mul_det_submatrix
lean.code:
theorem sum_det_submatrix_mul_det_submatrix (M N : Matrix m n R) :
    ∑ s : m → n, (M.submatrix id s).det * (N.submatrix id s).det
      = (Fintype.card m).factorial * (M * Nᵀ).det := by
  rw [Finset.sum_congr rfl fun s _ => det_submatrix_mul_det_submatrix M N s]
  rw [Finset.sum_comm]
  rw [← sum_sum_sign_mul_sign_mul_prod (M * Nᵀ)]
  refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun σ _ => ?_
  rw [Finset.sum_comm]
  refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun τ _ => ?_
  rw [← Finset.mul_sum]
  congr 1
  rw [← Fintype.prod_sum (fun (i : m) (j : n) => M (σ i) j * N (τ i) j)]
  refine Finset.prod_congr rfl fun i _ => ?_
  rw [Matrix.mul_apply]
  rfl

end Matrix

namespace AlternatingMap

variable {R : Type*} [CommRing R] {V N : Type*} [AddCommGroup V] [Module R V]
  [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N]
  {ι ι' : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι'] [DecidableEq ι']

omit [DecidableEq ι'] in

blocks.tex:
Expanding both determinants by the Leibniz formula and multiplying,
\[
\det\big(M[\,\cdot\,, s]\big)\det\big(N[\,\cdot\,, s]\big) = \sum_{\sigma, \tau \in S_\iota} \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\operatorname{sgn}(\tau) \prod_{i \in \iota} M_{\sigma(i)\,s(i)} N_{\tau(i)\,s(i)} .
\]
Summing over $s$ and exchanging the sums, the inner sum over $s$ of a product over $i$ becomes a product over $i$ of sums over $\iota'$:
\[
\sum_{s \colon \iota \to \iota'} \prod_{i \in \iota} M_{\sigma(i)\,s(i)} N_{\tau(i)\,s(i)} = \prod_{i \in \iota} \sum_{m \in \iota'} M_{\sigma(i)\,m} N_{\tau(i)\,m} = \prod_{i \in \iota} (MN^{T})_{\sigma(i)\,\tau(i)} .
\]
The result is now Lemma~\ref{lem:sum-sign-prod-det} applied to $C = MN^{T}$.

The classical statement of Cauchy--Binet sums over the $k$-element subsets $S \subseteq \iota'$ rather than over all maps. The two agree: a map $s$ that repeats a value selects a matrix with two equal columns, so it contributes $0$; and each injective $s$ with image $S$ is one of the $k!$ reindexings of the increasing enumeration of $S$, each contributing the same value because the two sign changes cancel.

The following generalization replaces the second determinant by an arbitrary alternating map. It is strictly stronger, since a determinant is an alternating function of the columns.

blocks.label: lem:alternating-cauchy-binet
blocks.title: lem:alternating-cauchy-binet
blocks.body:
Let $V$ and $N$ be $R$-modules, let $\theta \colon V^{\iota} \to N$ be alternating and $R$-multilinear, let $e \colon \iota' \to V$ be a family of vectors, and let $A$ be a matrix over $R$ with rows indexed by $\iota$ and columns by $\iota'$. Then
\[
\sum_{s \colon \iota \to \iota'} \det\big(A[\,\cdot\,, s]\big) \cdot \theta\big(e_{s(1)}, \ldots, e_{s(k)}\big) = k! \cdot \theta\Big(\sum_{m \in \iota'} A_{1m} e_m, \ldots, \sum_{m \in \iota'} A_{km} e_m\Big).
\]

lean.name: AlternatingMap.sum_det_submatrix_smul_apply
lean.code:
theorem sum_det_submatrix_smul_apply (θ : V [⋀^ι]→ₗ[R] N) (e : ι' → V) (A : Matrix ι ι' R) :
    ∑ s : ι → ι', (A.submatrix id s).det • θ (fun i => e (s i))
      = (Fintype.card ι).factorial • θ (fun i => ∑ m, A i m • e m) := by
  have hRHS : θ (fun i => ∑ m, A i m • e m)
      = ∑ u : ι → ι', (∏ i, A i (u i)) • θ (fun i => e (u i)) := by
    have h := θ.toMultilinearMap.map_sum (fun (i : ι) (m : ι') => A i m • e m)
    refine h.trans (Finset.sum_congr rfl fun u _ => ?_)
    exact θ.toMultilinearMap.map_smul_univ (fun i => A i (u i)) (fun i => e (u i))
  have key : ∀ σ : Equiv.Perm ι,
      (∑ s : ι → ι',
        (((Equiv.Perm.sign σ : ℤ) : R) * ∏ i, A (σ i) (s i)) • θ (fun i => e (s i)))
        = ∑ u : ι → ι', (∏ i, A i (u i)) • θ (fun i => e (u i)) := by
    intro σ
    rw [← Equiv.sum_comp (Equiv.arrowCongr σ.symm (Equiv.refl ι'))
      (fun s : ι → ι' => (((Equiv.Perm.sign σ : ℤ) : R) * ∏ i, A (σ i) (s i))
        • θ (fun i => e (s i)))]
    refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun u _ => ?_
    have hu : (Equiv.arrowCongr σ.symm (Equiv.refl ι')) u = fun i => u (σ i) := rfl
    rw [hu]
    have hprod : (∏ i, A (σ i) (u (σ i))) = ∏ j, A j (u j) :=
      Equiv.prod_comp σ (fun j => A j (u j))
    have hperm : (θ fun i => e (u (σ i))) = Equiv.Perm.sign σ • θ (fun i => e (u i)) :=
      θ.map_perm (fun i => e (u i)) σ
    rw [hprod, hperm]
    rcases Int.units_eq_one_or (Equiv.Perm.sign σ) with h | h <;> simp [h]
  rw [hRHS, Finset.smul_sum]
  have hLHS : ∀ s : ι → ι', (A.submatrix id s).det • θ (fun i => e (s i))
      = ∑ σ : Equiv.Perm ι,
          (((Equiv.Perm.sign σ : ℤ) : R) * ∏ i, A (σ i) (s i)) • θ (fun i => e (s i)) := by
    intro s
    rw [Matrix.det_apply', Finset.sum_smul]
    rfl
  rw [Finset.sum_congr rfl fun s _ => hLHS s, Finset.sum_comm,
    Finset.sum_congr rfl fun σ _ => key σ]
  rw [Finset.sum_const, Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_perm, ← Finset.smul_sum]

blocks.tex:
Expand the right-hand side by multilinearity: it equals
\[
k! \sum_{u \colon \iota \to \iota'} \Big(\prod_{i \in \iota} A_{i\,u(i)}\Big)\,\theta\big(e_{u(1)}, \ldots, e_{u(k)}\big).
\]
For the left-hand side, expand $\det(A[\,\cdot\,, s])$ by the Leibniz formula and exchange the two sums, so that it becomes
\[
\sum_{\sigma \in S_\iota} \sum_{s \colon \iota \to \iota'} \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\Big(\prod_{i \in \iota} A_{\sigma(i)\,s(i)}\Big)\,\theta\big(e_{s(1)}, \ldots, e_{s(k)}\big).
\]
Fix $\sigma$ and substitute $s = u \circ \sigma$, a bijection of the set of maps $\iota \to \iota'$ onto itself. Reindexing the product by $j = \sigma(i)$ gives $\prod_{i} A_{\sigma(i)\,u(\sigma(i))} = \prod_{j} A_{j\,u(j)}$, and since $\theta$ is alternating, $\theta(e_{u(\sigma(1))}, \ldots, e_{u(\sigma(k))}) = \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\,\theta(e_{u(1)}, \ldots, e_{u(k)})$. The two signs multiply to $\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)^2 = 1$, so the inner sum equals $\sum_{u} \big(\prod_{j} A_{j\,u(j)}\big)\theta(e_{u(1)}, \ldots, e_{u(k)})$ for every $\sigma$, and $S_\iota$ has $k!$ elements.

There are two classes of tensors that play particularly important roles in differential geometry: the symmetric and alternating tensors. Here we discuss the symmetric ones; we will take up alternating tensors later in this appendix when we discuss differential forms.

If $V$ is a finite-dimensional vector space, a covariant tensor $F \in T^k(V^*)$ is said to be \textit{symmetric} if its value is unchanged by interchanging any pair of arguments:
\[
F(v_1,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_k) = F(v_1,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_k)
\]
whenever $1 \le i < j \le k$. It follows immediately that the value is unchanged under every rearrangement of the arguments. If we denote the components of $F$ with respect to a basis by $F_{i_1 \cdots i_k}$, then $F$ is symmetric if and only if its components are unchanged by every permutation of the indices $i_1,\ldots,i_k$. Every 0-tensor and every 1-tensor are vacuously symmetric. The set of symmetric $k$-tensors on $V$ is a linear subspace of $T^k(V^*)$, which we denote by $\Sigma^k(V^*)$.

A tensor product of symmetric tensors is generally not symmetric. However, there is a natural product of symmetric tensors that does yield symmetric tensors. Given a covariant $k$-tensor $F$, the \textit{symmetrization of} $F$ is the $k$-tensor Sym $F$ defined by
\[
(\text{Sym} F)(v_1,\ldots,v_k) = \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_k} F(v_{\sigma(1)},\ldots,v_{\sigma(k)}),
\]
where $S_k$ is the group of all permutations of $\{1,\ldots,k\}$, called the \textbf{symmetric group on $k$ elements}. It is easy to check that Sym $F$ is symmetric, and Sym $F = F$ if and only if $F$ is symmetric. Then if $F$ and $G$ are symmetric tensors on $V$, of ranks $k$ and $l$, respectively, their \textbf{symmetric product} is defined to be the $(k + l)$-tensor $FG$ (denoted by juxtaposition without an explicit product symbol) given by
\[
FG = \text{Sym}(F \otimes G).
\]

The most important special case is the symmetric product of two 1-tensors, which can be characterized as follows: if $\omega$ and $\eta$ are covectors, then
\[
\omega\eta = \tfrac{1}{2}(\omega \otimes \eta + \eta \otimes \omega).
\]
(To prove this, just evaluate both sides on an arbitrary pair of vectors $(v, w)$, and use the definition of $\omega\eta$.) If $\omega$ is any 1-tensor, the notation $\omega^2$ means the symmetric product $\omega\omega$, which in turn is equal to $\omega \otimes \omega$.

On a smooth manifold $M$ with or without boundary, we can perform the same linear-algebraic constructions on each tangent space $T_p M$ that we perform on any vector space, yielding tensors at $p$. The disjoint union of tensor spaces of a particular type at all points of the manifold yields a vector bundle, called a \textbf{tensor bundle}.

The most fundamental tensor bundle is the \textbf{cotangent bundle}, defined as
\[
T^* M = \bigsqcup_{p \in M} T_p^* M.
\]

More generally, the \textbf{bundle of $(k,l)$-tensors} on $M$ is defined as
\[
T^{(k,l)} TM = \bigsqcup_{p \in M} T^{(k,l)}(T_p M).
\]

As special cases, the \textbf{bundle of covariant $k$-tensors} is denoted by $T^k T^* M = T^{(0,k)} TM$, and the \textbf{bundle of contravariant $k$-tensors} is denoted by $T^k TM = T^{(k,0)} TM$. Similarly, the \textbf{bundle of symmetric $k$-tensors} is
\[
\Sigma^k T^* M = \bigsqcup_{p \in M} \Sigma^k(T_p^* M).
\]

There are the usual identifications among these bundles that follow from (B.7): for example, $T^1 T M = T^{(1,0)} T M = T M$ and $T^1 T^* M = T^{(0,1)} T M = \Sigma^1 T^* M = T^* M$.

\begin{exercise}\dcref{chB:B.4}
Show that each tensor bundle is a smooth vector bundle over $M$, with a local trivialization over every open subset that admits a smooth local frame for $T M$.
\end{exercise}

A \textit{tensor field} on $M$ is a section of some tensor bundle over $M$ (see p.~383 for the definition of a section of a bundle). A section of $T^1 T^* M = T^{(0,1)} T M$ (a covariant 1-tensor field) is also called a \textit{covector field}. As we do with vector fields, we write the value of a tensor field $F$ at $p \in M$ as $F_p$ or $F|_p$. Because covariant tensor fields are the most common and important tensor fields we work with, we use the following shorthand notation for the space of all smooth covariant $k$-tensor fields:
\[\mathcal{T}^k(M) = \Gamma(T^k T^* M).\]

The space of smooth 0-tensor fields is just $C^\infty(M)$.

Let $(E_i) = (E_1, \ldots, E_n)$ be any smooth local frame for $T M$ over an open subset $U \subseteq M$. Associated with such a frame is the \textit{dual coframe}, which we typically denote by $(e^1, \ldots, e^n)$; these are smooth covector fields satisfying $e^i(E_j) = \delta^i_j$. For example, given a coordinate frame $(\partial/\partial x^1, \ldots, \partial/\partial x^n)$ over some open subset $U \subseteq M$, the dual coframe is $(dx^1, \ldots, dx^n)$, where $dx^i$ is the differential of the coordinate function $x^i$.

In terms of any smooth local frame $(E_i)$ and its dual coframe $(e^i)$, the tensor fields $E_i \otimes \cdots \otimes E_i \otimes e^{j_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes e^{j_l}$ form a smooth local frame for $\mathcal{T}^{(k,l)}(T^* M)$.

In particular, in local coordinates $(x^i)$, a $(k,l)$-tensor field $F$ has a coordinate expression of the form
\begin{equation}
F = F^{i_1 \cdots i_k}_{j_1 \cdots j_l} \frac{\partial}{\partial x^{i_1}} \otimes \cdots \otimes \frac{\partial}{\partial x^{i_k}} \otimes dx^{j_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes dx^{j_l}, \tag{B.8}
\end{equation}

where each coefficient $F^{i_1 \cdots i_k}_{j_1 \cdots j_l}$ is a smooth real-valued function on $U$.

\begin{exercise}\dcref{chB:B.5}
Suppose $F: M \to \mathcal{T}^{(k,l)} T M$ is a rough $(k,l)$-tensor field. Show that $F$ is smooth on an open set $U \subseteq M$ if and only if whenever $\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k$ are smooth covector fields and $X_1, \ldots, X_l$ are smooth vector fields defined on $U$, the real-valued function $F(\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k, X_1, \ldots, X_l)$, defined on $U$ by
\[F(\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k, X_1, \ldots, X_l)(p) = F_p(\omega^1|_p, \ldots, \omega^k|_p, X_1|_p, \ldots, X_l|_p),\]
is smooth.
\end{exercise}

An important property of tensor fields is that they are multilinear over the space of smooth functions. Suppose $F \in \Gamma(\mathcal{T}^{(k,l)} T M)$ is a smooth tensor field. Given smooth covector fields $\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k \in \mathcal{T}^1(M)$ and smooth vector fields $X_1, \ldots, X_l \in \mathcal{X}(M)$, Exercise B.5 shows that the function $F(\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k, X_1, \ldots, X_l)$ is smooth, and thus $F$ induces a map
\[
\tilde{F}: \underbrace{\mathcal{T}^1(M) \times \cdots \times \mathcal{T}^1(M)}_{k \text{ factors}} \times \underbrace{\mathfrak{X}(M) \times \cdots \times \mathfrak{X}(M)}_{l \text{ factors}} \to C^\infty(M).
\]

It is easy to check that this map is \emph{multilinear over} $C^\infty(M)$, that is, for all functions $u, v \in C^\infty(M)$ and smooth vector or covector fields $\alpha, \beta$,
\[
\tilde{F}(\ldots, u\alpha + v\beta, \ldots) = u\tilde{F}(\ldots, \alpha, \ldots) + v\tilde{F}(\ldots, \beta, \ldots).
\]

Even more important is the converse: as the next lemma shows, every such map that is multilinear over $C^\infty(M)$ defines a tensor field. (This lemma is stated and proved in [LeeSM] for covariant tensor fields, but the same argument works in the case of mixed tensors.)

blocks.label: lem:tensor-characterization-lemma
blocks.title: Tensor Characterization Lemma
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Lemma 12.24] A map
\[
\mathcal{F}: \underbrace{\mathcal{T}^1(M) \times \cdots \times \mathcal{T}^1(M)}_{k \text{ factors}} \times \underbrace{\mathfrak{X}(M) \times \cdots \times \mathfrak{X}(M)}_{l \text{ factors}} \to C^\infty(M)
\]
is induced by a smooth $(k,l)$-tensor field as above if and only if it is multilinear over $C^\infty(M)$. Similarly, a map
\[
\mathcal{F}: \underbrace{\mathcal{T}^1(M) \times \cdots \times \mathcal{T}^1(M)}_{k \text{ factors}} \times \underbrace{\mathfrak{X}(M) \times \cdots \times \mathfrak{X}(M)}_{l \text{ factors}} \to \mathfrak{X}(M)
\]
is induced by a smooth $(k+1,l)$-tensor field as in Proposition B.1 if and only if it is multilinear over $C^\infty(M)$.

blocks.tex: Because of this result, it is common to use the same symbol for both a tensor field and the multilinear map on sections that it defines, and to refer to either of these objects as a tensor field.

Suppose $F: M \to N$ is a smooth map and $A$ is a covariant $k$-tensor field on $N$. For every $p \in M$, we define a tensor $dF_p^*(A) \in T^k(T_p^* M)$, called the \emph{pointwise pullback of $A$ by $F$ at $p$}, by
\[
dF_p^*(A)(v_1, \ldots, v_k) = A(dF_p(v_1), \ldots, dF_p(v_k))
\]
for $v_1, \ldots, v_k \in T_p M$; and we define the \emph{pullback of $A$ by $F$} to be the tensor field $F^* A$ on $M$ defined by
\[
(F^* A)_p = dF_p^*(A_{F(p)}).
\]

blocks.label: prop:tensor-pullback-properties
blocks.title: Properties of Tensor Pullbacks
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Prop. 12.25] Suppose $F: M \to N$ and $G: P \to M$ are smooth maps, $A$ and $B$ are covariant tensor fields on $N$, and $f$ is a real-valued function on $N$.
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] $F^*(fB) = (f \circ F) F^* B$.
\item[(b)] $F^*(A \otimes B) = F^* A \otimes F^* B$.
\item[(c)] $F^*(A + B) = F^* A + F^* B$.
\item[(d)] $F^* B$ is a (continuous) tensor field, and it is smooth if $B$ is smooth.
\item[(e)] $(F \circ G)^* B = G^*(F^* B)$.
\item[(f)] $(\text{Id}_N)^* B = B$.
\end{enumerate}

blocks.tex:
If $f$ is a continuous real-valued function (i.e., a 0-tensor field), the pullback $F^* f$ is just the composition $f \circ F$.

The following proposition shows how pullbacks are computed in local coordinates.

blocks.label: prop:pullback-computation-coordinates
blocks.title: prop:pullback-computation-coordinates
blocks.body:
Let $F: M \to N$ be smooth, and let $B$ be a covariant $k$-tensor field on $N$. If $p \in M$ and $(y^i)$ are smooth coordinates for $N$ on a neighborhood of $F(p)$, then $F^* B$ has the following expression in a neighborhood of $p$:
\[
F^* \left( B_{i_1 \ldots i_k} \, dy^{i_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes dy^{i_k} \right) = \left( B_{i_1 \ldots i_k} \circ F \right) d(y^{i_1} \circ F) \otimes \cdots \otimes d(y^{i_k} \circ F).
\]

blocks.tex:
We can extend the notion of Lie derivatives to tensor fields. This can be done for mixed tensor fields of any rank, but for simplicity we restrict attention to covariant tensor fields. Suppose $X$ is a smooth vector field on $M$ and $\theta$ is its flow. If $A$ is a smooth covariant tensor field on $M$, the \emph{Lie derivative of $A$ with respect to $X$} is the smooth covariant tensor field $\mathscr{L}_X A$ defined by
\[
(\mathscr{L}_X A)_p = \frac{d}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} (\theta_t^* A)_p = \lim_{t \to 0} \frac{d(\theta_t^* A)_p(A_{\theta_t(p)}) - A_p}{t}.
\]

blocks.label: prop:lie-derivative-tensor-product-rule
blocks.title: prop:lie-derivative-tensor-product-rule
blocks.body:
Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold, $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, and $A$ is a smooth covariant $k$-tensor field on $M$. For all smooth vector fields $Z_1, \ldots, Z_k$:
\[
(\mathscr{L}_X A)(Z_1, \ldots, Z_k) = X(A(Z_1, \ldots, Z_k)) - A(\mathscr{L}_X Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_k) - A(Z_1, \mathscr{L}_X Z_2, \ldots, Z_k) - \cdots - A(Z_1, \ldots, \mathscr{L}_X Z_k).
\]

blocks.tex: As we did for vector fields, we say that a covariant tensor field $A$ is \emph{invariant under a flow} $\theta$ if $(\theta_t)^* A = A$ wherever it is defined. The next proposition is a tensor analog of Proposition A.47.

blocks.label: prop:invariant-tensor-characterization
blocks.title: prop:invariant-tensor-characterization
blocks.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$. A smooth covariant tensor field is invariant under the flow of $X$ if and only if its Lie derivative with respect to $X$ is identically zero.

blocks.tex:
In addition to symmetric tensors, the other class of tensors that play a special role in differential geometry is that of \textit{alternating tensors}, which we now define. Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional vector space. If $F$ is a covariant $k$-tensor on $V$, we say that $F$ is \textit{alternating} if its value changes sign whenever two different arguments are interchanged:
\[
F(v_1,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_k) = -F(v_1,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_k).
\]

The set of alternating covariant $k$-tensors on $V$ is a linear subspace of $T^k(V^*)$, denoted by $\Lambda^k(V^*)$. If $F \in \Lambda^k(V^*)$, then the effect of an arbitrary permutation of its arguments is given by
\[
F(v_{\sigma(1)},\ldots,v_{\sigma(k)}) = (\sgn \sigma)F(v_1,\ldots,v_k),
\]
where $\sgn \sigma$ represents the \textit{sign} of the permutation $\sigma \in S_k$, which is $+1$ if $\sigma$ is even (i.e., can be written as a composition of an even number of transpositions), and $-1$ if $\sigma$ is odd. The components of $F$ with respect to any basis change sign similarly under a permutation of the indices. All $0$-tensors and $1$-tensors are alternating.

Analogously to the symmetrization operator defined above, if $F$ is any covariant $k$-tensor on $V$, we define the \textit{alternation of $F$} by
\begin{equation}
(\mathrm{Alt}\, F)(v_1,\ldots,v_k) = \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_k} (\sgn \sigma) F(v_{\sigma(1)},\ldots,v_{\sigma(k)}).
\tag{B.9}
\end{equation}

Again, it is easy to check that $\mathrm{Alt}\, F$ is alternating, and $\mathrm{Alt}\, F = F$ if and only if $F$ is alternating. Given $\omega \in \Lambda^k(V^*)$ and $\eta \in \Lambda^l(V^*)$, we define their \textit{wedge product} by
\[
\omega \wedge \eta = \frac{(k+l)!}{k!l!} \mathrm{Alt}(\omega \otimes \eta).
\]

It is immediate that $\omega \wedge \eta$ is an alternating $(k + l)$-tensor. The wedge product is easily seen to be bilinear and \textit{anticommutative}, which means that
\[
\omega \wedge \eta = (-1)^{kl} \eta \wedge \omega, \quad \text{for } \omega \in \Lambda^k(V^*) \text{ and } \eta \in \Lambda^l(V^*).
\]

It is also the case, although not so easy to see, that it is associative; see \cite[Prop.~14.11]{LeeSM} for a proof. If $(b_i)$ is a basis for $V$ and $(\beta^i)$ is the dual basis, the wedge products $\beta^{i_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge \beta^{i_k}$, as $(i_1,\ldots,i_k)$ range over strictly increasing multi-indices, form a basis for $\Lambda^k(V^*)$, which therefore has dimension $\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$, where $n = \dim V$. In terms of these basis elements, the wedge product satisfies
\[
\beta^{i_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge \beta^{i_k}(b_{j_1},\ldots,b_{j_k}) = \det(\delta^{i_p}_{j_q}).
\]

More generally, if $\omega^1,\ldots,\omega^k$ are any covectors and $v_1,\ldots,v_k$ are any vectors, then
\begin{equation}
\omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k(v_1,\ldots,v_k) = \det(\omega^i(v_j)).
\tag{B.10}
\end{equation}

The wedge product can be defined analogously for \emph{contravariant} alternating tensors $\alpha \in \Lambda^p(V)$, $\beta \in \Lambda^q(V)$, simply by regarding $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\alpha \wedge \beta$ as alternating multilinear functionals on $V^*$.

The convention we use for the wedge product is referred to in [\texttt{LeeSM}] as the \textbf{determinant convention}. There is another convention that is also in common use, the \textbf{Alt convention}, which amounts to multiplying the right-hand side of (B.10) by a factor of $1/k!$. The choice of which definition to use is a matter of taste, though there are various reasons to justify each choice depending on the context. The determinant convention is most common in introductory differential geometry texts, and is used, for example, in [\texttt{Boo86}, \texttt{Cha06}, \texttt{dC92}, \texttt{LeeJeff09}, \texttt{LeeSM}, \texttt{Pet16}, \texttt{Spi09}, \texttt{Tu11}]. The Alt convention is used in [\texttt{KN96}] and is more common in complex differential geometry.

Given an alternating $k$-tensor $\omega \in \Lambda^k(V^*)$ and a vector $v \in V$, we define an alternating $(k-1)$-tensor $v \lrcorner \omega$ by
\[
(v \lrcorner \omega)(w_1,\ldots,w_{k-1}) = \omega(v,w_1,\ldots,w_{k-1}).
\]

The operation $\omega \mapsto v \lrcorner \omega$ is known as \emph{interior multiplication by $v$}, and is also denoted by $\omega \mapsto i_v\omega$. By convention, $v \lrcorner \omega = 0$ when $\omega$ is a 0-tensor. Direct computation shows that $i_v \circ i_v = 0$, and $i_v$ satisfies the following product rule for an alternating $k$-tensor $\omega$ and an alternating $\ell$-tensor $\eta$:

\begin{equation}
i_v(\omega \wedge \eta) = (i_v\omega) \wedge \eta + (-1)^k \omega \wedge (i_v\eta).
\tag{B.11}
\end{equation}

If $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, the subbundle of $T^k T^*M$ consisting of alternating tensors is denoted by $\Lambda^k T^* M$, and an alternating tensor field on $M$ is called a \textbf{differential $k$-form}, or just a $k$-form. The space of all smooth $k$-forms is denoted by $\Omega^k(M) = \Gamma(\Lambda^k T^* M)$. Because every 1-tensor field is alternating, $\Omega^1(M)$ is the same as the space $\mathcal{F}^1(M)$ of smooth covector fields.

The most important operation on differential forms is the \emph{exterior derivative}, defined as follows. Suppose $M$ is a smooth $n$-manifold with or without boundary, and $(x^i)$ are any smooth local coordinates on $M$. A smooth $k$-form $\omega$ can be expressed in these coordinates as
\[
\omega = \sum_{j_1 < \cdots < j_k} \omega_{j_1\ldots j_k} \, dx^{j_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^{j_k},
\]

and then we define the \textbf{exterior derivative of} $\omega$, denoted by $d\omega$, to be the $(k+1)$-form defined in coordinates by
\[
d\omega = \sum_{j_1 < \cdots < j_k} \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial \omega_{j_1 \cdots j_k}}{\partial x^i} dx^i \wedge dx^{j_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^{j_k}.
\]

For a 0-form $f$ (a smooth real-valued function), this reduces to
\[
df = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i} dx^i,
\]
which is exactly the \emph{differential} of $f$ defined by (A.4).

blocks.label: prop:exterior-differentiation-properties
blocks.title: Properties of Exterior Differentiation
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Thm. 14.24] Suppose $M$ is a smooth $n$-manifold with or without boundary.
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] For each $k = 0, \ldots, n$, the operator $d: \Omega^k(M) \to \Omega^{k+1}(M)$ is well defined, independently of coordinates.
\item[(b)] $d$ is linear over $\mathbb{R}$.
\item[(c)] $d \circ d \equiv 0$.
\item[(d)] If $\omega \in \Omega^k(M)$ and $\eta \in \Omega^l(M)$, then
\[
d(\omega \wedge \eta) = d\omega \wedge \eta + (-1)^k \omega \wedge d\eta.
\]
\end{enumerate}

blocks.label: prop:exterior-derivative-one-form
blocks.title: Exterior Derivative of a 1-form
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Prop. 14.29] If $\omega$ is a smooth 1-form and $X, Y$ are vector fields, then
\[
d\omega(X,Y) = X(\omega(Y)) - Y(\omega(X)) - \omega([X,Y]).
\]

blocks.label: prop:exterior-derivative-naturality
blocks.title: Naturality of the Exterior Derivative

blocks.tex: A smooth differential form $\omega \in \Omega^k(M)$ is \emph{closed} if $d\omega = 0$, and \emph{exact} if there exists a smooth $(k-1)$-form $\eta$ on $M$ such that $\omega = d\eta$. The fact that $d \circ d = 0$ implies that every exact form is closed, but the converse is not true in general. However, the next lemma gives an important special case in which it is true. If $V$ is a vector space, a subset $S \subseteq V$ is said to be \emph{star-shaped} with respect to a point $x \in S$ if for every $y \in S$, the line segment from $x$ to $y$ is contained in $S$.

blocks.label: lem:poincare-lemma
blocks.title: The Poincaré Lemma
blocks.body: [LeeSM, Thm. 17.14] If $U$ is a star-shaped open subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, then for $k \geq 1$, every closed $k$-form on $U$ is exact.

blocks.tex:
If $V$ is a finite-dimensional vector space, an \emph{orientation} of $V$ is an equivalence class of ordered bases for $V$, where two ordered bases are considered equivalent if the transition matrix that expresses one basis in terms of the other has positive determinant. Every vector space has exactly two orientations. Once an orientation is chosen, a basis is said to be \textit{positively oriented} if it belongs to the chosen orientation, and \textit{negatively oriented} if not. The \textit{standard orientation of $\mathbb{R}^n$} is the one determined by the \textit{standard basis} $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$, where $e_i$ is the vector $(0, \ldots, 1, \ldots, 0)$ with $1$ in the $i$-th place and zeros elsewhere.

If $M$ is a smooth manifold, an \textit{orientation for $M$} is a choice of orientation for each tangent space that is continuous in the sense that in a neighborhood of every point there is a (continuous) local frame that determines the given orientation at each point of the neighborhood. If there exists an orientation for $M$, we say that $M$ is \textit{orientable}. An \textit{oriented manifold} is a smooth orientable manifold together with a choice of orientation. If $M$ is an oriented $n$-manifold, then a smooth coordinate chart $(U, (x^i))$ is said to be an \textit{oriented chart} if the coordinate frame $(\partial/\partial x^1, \ldots, \partial/\partial x^n)$ is positively oriented at each point. Exactly the same definitions apply to manifolds with boundary.

blocks.label: prop:orientation-determined-by-n-form
blocks.title: Orientation Determined by an $n$-Form
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Prop. 15.5]
Let $M$ be an $n$-manifold with or without boundary. Every nonvanishing $n$-form $\mu \in \Omega^n(M)$ determines a unique orientation of $M$ by declaring an ordered basis $(b_1, \ldots, b_n)$ for $T_p M$ to be positively oriented if and only if $\mu_p(b_1, \ldots, b_n) > 0$. Conversely, if $M$ is oriented, there is a smooth nonvanishing $n$-form that determines the orientation in this way.

blocks.tex:
Because of this proposition, a nonvanishing $n$-form on a smooth $n$-manifold is called an \textit{orientation form}. If in addition $M$ is oriented and $\mu$ determines the given orientation, we say that $\mu$ is \textit{positively oriented}.

Suppose $M$ and $N$ are both smooth $n$-manifolds with or without boundary and $F : M \to N$ is a local diffeomorphism. If $N$ is oriented, we define the \textit{pullback orientation on $M$ induced by $F$} to be the orientation determined by $F^* \mu$, where $\mu$ is any positively oriented orientation form for $N$. If both $M$ and $N$ are oriented, we say that $F$ is \textit{orientation-preserving} if the pullback orientation is equal to the given orientation on $M$, and \textit{orientation-reversing} if the pullback orientation is the opposite orientation.

blocks.label: prop:orientation-of-hypersurface
blocks.title: Orientation of a Hypersurface
blocks.body:
[LeeSM, Prop. 15.21]
Suppose $M$ is an oriented smooth $n$-manifold with or without boundary, $S \subseteq M$ is a smooth immersed hypersurface, and $N$ is a continuous vector field along $S$ (i.e., a continuous map $N : S \to TM$ such that $N_p \in T_p M$ for each $p \in S$). If $N$ is nowhere tangent to $S$, then $S$ has a unique orientation determined by the $(n-1)$-form $\iota^*(N \lrcorner \mu)$, where $\iota : S \hookrightarrow M$ is inclusion and $\mu$ is any positively oriented orientation form for $M$.

blocks.tex: A special case of the preceding proposition occurs when $M$ is a manifold with boundary and the hypersurface in question is the boundary of $M$. In that case we declare a vector field $N$ along $\partial M$ to be \textit{outward-pointing} if for each $p \in \partial M$, there is a smooth curve $\gamma : (-\varepsilon, 0] \to M$ such that $\gamma(0) = p$ and $\gamma'(0) = N_p$, and $N_p \notin T_p(\partial M)$. We can always construct a global smooth outward-pointing vector field by taking $-\partial/\partial x^n$ in boundary coordinates in a neighborhood of each boundary point, and gluing together with a partition of unity. Since an outward-pointing vector field is nowhere tangent to $\partial M$, we have the following proposition.

blocks.label: prop:induced-orientation-boundary
blocks.title: Induced Orientation on a Boundary
blocks.body: If $M$ is an oriented smooth manifold with boundary, then $\partial M$ is orientable, and it has a canonical orientation (called the \emph{induced orientation} or \emph{Stokes orientation}) determined by $\iota^*(N \lrcorner \mu)$, where $\iota: \partial M \hookrightarrow M$ is inclusion, $N$ is any outward-pointing vector field along $\partial M$, and $\mu$ is any positively oriented orientation form for $M$.

blocks.label: prop:orientation-covering-theorem
blocks.title: Orientation Covering Theorem
blocks.body: If $M$ is a connected, nonorientable smooth manifold, then there exist an oriented smooth manifold $\tilde{M}$ and a two-sheeted smooth covering map $\tilde{\pi}: \tilde{M} \to M$, called the \emph{orientation covering of $M$}.

blocks.label: cor:simply-connected-orientable
blocks.title: cor:simply-connected-orientable
blocks.body: Every simply connected smooth manifold is orientable.

blocks.tex: Corollary A.59 shows that a simply connected manifold does not admit two-sheeted covering maps.

Suppose first that $\omega$ is a (continuous) $n$-form on an open subset $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ or $\mathbb{R}^n_+$. It can be written $\omega = f \, dx^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^n$ for some continuous real-valued function $f$, and we define the \emph{integral of $\omega$ over $U$} to be the ordinary multiple integral
\[
\int_U \omega = \int_U f(x^1,\ldots,x^n) \, dx^1 \cdots dx^n,
\]
provided the integral is well defined. This will always be the case, for example, if $f$ is continuous and has compact support in $U$.

In general, if $\omega$ is a compactly supported $n$-form on a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ with or without boundary, we define the \emph{integral of $\omega$ over $M$} by choosing finitely many oriented smooth coordinate charts $\{U_i\}_{i=1}^k$ whose domains cover the support of $\omega$, together with a smooth partition of unity $\{\psi_i\}_{i=1}^k$ subordinate to this cover, and defining
\[
\int_M \omega = \sum_{i=1}^k \int_{\tilde{U}_i} (\varphi_i^{-1})^*(\psi_i\omega),
\]
where $\tilde{U}_i = \varphi_i(U_i)$, and the integrals on the right-hand side are defined as above. Proposition 16.5 in [LeeSM] shows that this definition does not depend on the choice of oriented charts or partition of unity.

blocks.label: prop:properties-integrals-forms
blocks.title: Properties of Integrals of Forms
blocks.body:
Suppose $M$ and $N$ are nonempty oriented smooth $n$-manifolds with or without boundary, and $\omega, \eta$ are compactly supported $n$-forms on $M$.
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] \textbf{LINEARITY:} If $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$, then
\[
\int_M (a\omega + b\eta) = a \int_M \omega + b \int_M \eta.
\]
\item[(b)] \textbf{ORIENTATION REVERSAL:} If $-M$ denotes $M$ with the opposite orientation, then
\[
\int_{-M} \omega = -\int_M \omega.
\]
\item[(c)] \textbf{POSITIVITY:} If $\omega$ is a positively oriented orientation form, then $\int_M \omega > 0$.
\item[(d)] \textbf{DIFFEOMORPHISM INVARIANCE:} If $F: N \to M$ is an orientation-preserving or orientation-reversing diffeomorphism, then
\[
\int_M \omega = \begin{cases}
\int_N F^*\omega & \text{if } F \text{ is orientation-preserving,} \\
-\int_N F^*\omega & \text{if } F \text{ is orientation-reversing.}
\end{cases}
\]
\end{enumerate}

blocks.label: thm:stokes-theorem
blocks.title: Stokes's Theorem

blocks.tex: In the statement of this theorem, $\partial M$ is understood to have the Stokes orientation, and the right-hand side is interpreted as the integral of the pullback of $\omega$ to $\partial M$. The following special case is frequently useful.

blocks.label: cor:exact-integral-zero
blocks.title: cor:exact-integral-zero
blocks.body: Suppose $M$ is a compact oriented smooth $n$-manifold (without boundary). Then the integral of every exact $n$-form on $M$ is zero.

blocks.tex:
On an oriented $n$-manifold with or without boundary, $n$-forms are the natural objects to integrate. But in order to integrate on a nonorientable manifold, we need closely related objects called \emph{densities}.

If $V$ is an $n$-dimensional real vector space, a \textbf{density on $V$} is a function
\[
\mu: \underbrace{V \times \cdots \times V}_{\text{$n$ copies}} \to \mathbb{R}
\]
satisfying the following formula for every linear map $T: V \to V$:
\begin{equation}
\mu(Tv_1, \ldots, Tv_n) = |\det T| \mu(v_1, \ldots, v_n). \tag{B.14}
\end{equation}

A density $\mu$ is said to be \textbf{positive} if $\mu(v_1, \ldots, v_n) > 0$ whenever $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ is a basis of $V$; it is clear from (B.14) that if this is true for some basis, then it is true for every one. Every nonzero alternating $n$-tensor $\mu$ determines a positive density $|\mu|$ by the formula
\[
|\mu|(v_1, \ldots, v_n) = |\mu(v_1, \ldots, v_n)|.
\]

The set $\mathcal{D}(V)$ of all densities on $V$ is a 1-dimensional vector space, spanned by $|\mu|$ for any nonzero alternating $n$-tensor $\mu$.

When $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, the set
\[
\mathcal{D}M = \bigsqcup_{p \in M} \mathcal{D}(T_p M)
\]
is called the \textbf{density bundle} of $M$. It is a smooth rank-1 vector bundle, with $|dx^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^n|$ as a smooth local frame over any smooth coordinate chart. A \textbf{density on $M$} is a (smooth) section $\mu$ of $\mathcal{D}M$; in any local coordinates, it can be written
\[
\mu = u |dx^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^n|
\]
for some locally defined smooth function $u$.

Under smooth maps, densities pull back in the same way as differential forms: Suppose $F: M \to N$ is a smooth map between $n$-manifolds with or without boundary, and $\mu$ is a density on $N$. The \textbf{pullback of $\mu$} is the density $F^*\mu$ on $M$ defined by
\[
(F^*\mu)_p(v_1, \ldots, v_n) = \mu_{F(p)}(dF_p(v_1), \ldots, dF_p(v_n)).
\]

In coordinates, this satisfies
\[
F^*(u |dy^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge dy^n|) = (u \circ F)|\det DF| |dx^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^n|,
\quad (B.15)
\]
where $DF$ represents the matrix of partial derivatives of $F$ in these coordinates.

Because the pullback formula (B.15) is exactly analogous to the change of variables formula for multiple integrals, we can define the integral of a compactly supported density on a smooth manifold with or without boundary in exactly the same way as the integral of an $n$-form is defined on an oriented manifold, except that now there is no need to have an orientation. Thus if $\mu = u |dx^1 \cdots dx^n|$ is a smooth density that is compactly supported in an open set $V \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$, we define the integral of $\mu$ by
\[
\int_V \mu = \int_V u \, dx^1 \cdots dx^n.
\]

It follows from (B.15) that the value of this integral is diffeomorphism-invariant, so we can define the integral of a compactly supported density on a smooth manifold with or without boundary by breaking it up with a partition of unity and integrating each term in coordinates as above.

Lie groups play many important roles in Riemannian geometry, both as examples of Riemannian manifolds and as isometry groups of other manifolds. In this appendix, we summarize the main facts about Lie groups that are used in this book. For details, consult [LeeSM], especially Chapters 7, 8, 20, and 21.

A \textbf{Lie group} is a smooth manifold $G$ that is also a group in the algebraic sense, with the property that the multiplication map $m : G \times G \to G$ given by $m(\varphi_1, \varphi_2) = \varphi_1 \varphi_2$ and the inversion map $i : G \to G$ given by $i(\varphi) = \varphi^{-1}$ are smooth. For generic Lie groups, we usually denote the identity element by $e$, unless there is a more specific common notation for a particular group.

Given a Lie group $G$, each $\varphi \in G$ defines a map $L_\varphi : G \to G$, called \textbf{left translation}, by $L_\varphi(\varphi') = \varphi \varphi'$. It is a diffeomorphism with inverse $L_{\varphi^{-1}}$. Similarly, \textbf{right translation} $R_\varphi : G \to G$ is the diffeomorphism $R_\varphi(\varphi') = \varphi' \varphi$, with inverse $R_{\varphi^{-1}}$.

A subgroup $H \subseteq G$ that is endowed with a topology and smooth structure making it into a Lie group and an immersed or embedded submanifold of $G$ is called a \textbf{Lie subgroup of $G$}.

If $G$ and $H$ are Lie groups, a group homomorphism $F : G \to H$ that is also a smooth map is called a \textbf{Lie group homomorphism}. It is a \textbf{Lie group isomorphism} if it has an inverse that is also a Lie group homomorphism. A Lie group isomorphism from $G$ to itself is called an \textbf{automorphism of $G$}.

blocks.label: prop:lie-group-homomorphism-properties
blocks.title: LeeSM, Thms. 7.5 \& 21.27

blocks.label: ex:lie-group-examples
blocks.title: Lie Groups
blocks.body:
(a) Every countable group with the discrete topology is a zero-dimensional Lie group, called a \emph{discrete Lie group}.

(b) $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\mathbb{C}^n$ are Lie groups under addition.

(c) The sets $\mathbb{R}^*$, $\mathbb{R}^+$, and $\mathbb{C}^*$ of nonzero real numbers, positive real numbers, and nonzero complex numbers, respectively, are Lie groups under multiplication.

(d) The unit circle $S^1 \subseteq \mathbb{C}$ is a 1-dimensional Lie group under complex multiplication, called the \emph{circle group}.

(e) Given Lie groups $G_1, \ldots, G_k$, their \emph{direct product group} is the Lie group whose underlying manifold is $G_1 \times \cdots \times G_k$, and whose multiplication is given by $(g_1, \ldots, g_k)(g'_1, \ldots, g'_k) = (g_1 g'_1, \ldots, g_k g'_k)$. For example, the $n$-torus is the $n$-fold product group $T^n = S^1 \times \cdots \times S^1$. The 2-torus is often simply called \emph{the torus}.

(f) The \emph{general linear groups} $GL(n, \mathbb{R})$ and $GL(n, \mathbb{C})$, consisting of all invertible $n \times n$ real or complex matrices, respectively, are Lie groups under matrix multiplication. The identity element in both cases is the $n \times n$ identity matrix, denoted by $I_n$. More generally, if $V$ is an $n$-dimensional real or complex vector space, the group $GL(V)$ of invertible linear maps from $V$ to itself is a Lie group isomorphic to $GL(n, \mathbb{R})$ or $GL(n, \mathbb{C})$.

(g) The real and complex \emph{special linear groups} are the subgroups $SL(n, \mathbb{R}) \subseteq GL(n, \mathbb{R})$ and $SL(n, \mathbb{C}) \subseteq GL(n, \mathbb{C})$ consisting of matrices of determinant 1.

(h) The \emph{orthogonal group} $O(n) \subseteq GL(n, \mathbb{R})$ is the subgroup consisting of orthogonal matrices, those that satisfy $A^T A = I_n$, where $A^T$ is the transpose of $A$. The \emph{special orthogonal group} is the subgroup $SO(n) = O(n) \cap SL(n, \mathbb{R})$.

(i) Similarly, the \emph{unitary group} $U(n) \subseteq GL(n, \mathbb{C})$ is the subgroup consisting of complex unitary matrices, those that satisfy $A^* A = I_n$, where $A^* = \overline{A}^T$ is the conjugate transpose of $A$, called its \emph{adjoint}. The \emph{special unitary group} is $SU(n) = U(n) \cap SL(n, \mathbb{C})$.

blocks.tex: If $G$ is a Lie group and $V$ is a finite-dimensional vector space, a Lie group homomorphism $\rho: G \to GL(V)$ is called a (finite-dimensional) \emph{representation} of $G$. It is said to be \emph{faithful} if it is injective.

blocks.title: The Lie Algebra of a Lie Group

blocks.tex:
Suppose $G$ is a Lie group. A vector field $X$ on $G$ is said to be \emph{left-invariant} if it is invariant under all left translations, meaning that $(L_g)_* X = X$ for every $g \in G$. Similarly, $X$ is \emph{right-invariant} if it is invariant under all right translations.

We denote the set of all smooth left-invariant vector fields on $G$ by $\operatorname{Lie}(G)$. It is a vector subspace of $\mathfrak{X}(G)$ that is closed under Lie brackets (see LeeSM, Prop. 8.33). Thus it is an example of a \emph{Lie algebra}: a vector space $\mathfrak{g}$ endowed with a bilinear operation $[\cdot, \cdot]: \mathfrak{g} \times \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g}$, called the \emph{bracket}, that is antisymmetric (meaning $[X, Y] = -[Y, X]$) and satisfies the \emph{Jacobi identity}:
\[
[X, [Y, Z]] + [Y, [Z, X]] + [Z, [X, Y]] = 0.
\]

With the Lie bracket structure that it inherits from $\mathfrak{X}(G)$, the Lie algebra $\operatorname{Lie}(G)$ is called the \textit{Lie algebra of $G$}.

blocks.label: prop:evaluation-map-lie-algebra-isomorphism
blocks.title: LeeSM, Thm. 8.37
blocks.body: Let $G$ be a Lie group with identity $e$. The evaluation map $X \mapsto X_e$ is a vector space isomorphism from $\operatorname{Lie}(G)$ to $T_e G$, so $\operatorname{Lie}(G)$ has the same dimension as $G$ itself.

blocks.tex: If $\mathfrak{g}$ is a Lie algebra, a \textbf{Lie subalgebra} of $\mathfrak{g}$ is a vector subspace $\mathfrak{h} \subseteq \mathfrak{g}$ that is closed under Lie brackets, and is thus a Lie algebra with the restriction of the same bracket operation. Given Lie algebras $\mathfrak{g}_1$ and $\mathfrak{g}_2$, a \textbf{Lie algebra homomorphism} is a linear map $F: \mathfrak{g}_1 \to \mathfrak{g}_2$ that preserves brackets: $F([X, Y]) = [F(X), F(Y)]$. A \textbf{Lie algebra isomorphism} is an invertible Lie algebra homomorphism, and a \textbf{Lie algebra automorphism} is a Lie algebra isomorphism from a Lie algebra to itself.

blocks.label: ex:lie-algebras
blocks.title: Lie Algebras
blocks.body:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] If $M$ is a smooth positive-dimensional manifold, the space $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ of all smooth vector fields on $M$ is an infinite-dimensional Lie algebra under the bracket.
\item[(b)] The vector space $\mathfrak{gl}(n, \mathbb{R})$ of all $n \times n$ real matrices is an $n^2$-dimensional Lie algebra under the \textbf{commutator bracket} $[A, B] = AB - BA$. The canonical identification of both $\mathfrak{gl}(n, \mathbb{R})$ and $\operatorname{Lie}(\operatorname{GL}(n, \mathbb{R}))$ with the tangent space to $\operatorname{GL}(n, \mathbb{R})$ at the identity yields a Lie algebra isomorphism $\mathfrak{gl}(n, \mathbb{R}) \cong \operatorname{Lie}(\operatorname{GL}(n, \mathbb{R}))$ [LeeSM, Prop.\ 8.41].
\item[(c)] Similarly, the space $\mathfrak{gl}(n, \mathbb{C})$ of $n \times n$ complex matrices is a $2n^2$-dimensional (real) Lie algebra under the commutator bracket, isomorphic to the Lie algebra of $\operatorname{GL}(n, \mathbb{C})$ [LeeSM, Prop.\ 8.48].
\item[(d)] The space $\mathfrak{o}(n)$ of skew-symmetric $n \times n$ real matrices is a Lie subalgebra of $\mathfrak{gl}(n, \mathbb{R})$, isomorphic to the Lie algebra of $O(n)$ [LeeSM, Example 8.47].
\item[(e)] If $V$ is a vector space, the space $\mathfrak{gl}(V)$ of all linear maps from $V$ to itself is a Lie algebra under the commutator bracket $[A, B] = A \circ B - B \circ A$. In case $V$ is finite-dimensional, $\mathfrak{gl}(V)$ is isomorphic to the Lie algebra of the Lie group $\operatorname{GL}(V)$.
\item[(f)] A Lie algebra in which all brackets are zero is called an \textbf{abelian Lie algebra}. Every vector space can be made into an abelian Lie algebra by defining all brackets to be zero. The Lie algebra of a connected Lie group is abelian if and only if the group is abelian (see [LeeSM, Problems 8-25 and 20-7]).
\end{enumerate}

blocks.tex: The following proposition shows that every Lie group homomorphism induces a Lie algebra homomorphism between the respective Lie algebras.

blocks.label: prop:induced-lie-algebra-homomorphism
blocks.title: The Induced Lie Algebra Homomorphism
blocks.body: [LeeSM, Thm. 8.44] Let $G$ and $H$ be Lie groups, and let $\mathfrak{g}$ and $\mathfrak{h}$ be their Lie algebras. Given a Lie group homomorphism $F: G \to H$, there is a unique Lie algebra homomorphism $F_*: \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{h}$, called the \textit{induced Lie algebra homomorphism of $F$}, with the property that for each $X \in \mathfrak{g}$, the vector field $F_* X \in \mathfrak{h}$ is $F$-related to $X$.

blocks.label: prop:properties-of-induced-homomorphisms
blocks.title: Properties of Induced Homomorphisms
blocks.body:
Let $G, H, K$ be Lie groups.

\begin{enumerate}
\item[$(a)$] $(Id_G)_* = Id_{\operatorname{Lie}(G)} : \operatorname{Lie}(G) \to \operatorname{Lie}(G)$.
\item[$(b)$] If $F_1 : G \to H$ and $F_2 : H \to K$ are Lie group homomorphisms, then
\[
(F_2 \circ F_1)_* = (F_2)_* \circ (F_1)_* : \operatorname{Lie}(G) \to \operatorname{Lie}(K).
\]
\item[$(c)$] Isomorphic Lie groups have isomorphic Lie algebras.
\end{enumerate}

blocks.title: The Exponential Map of a Lie Group

blocks.tex:
If $G$ is a Lie group, a Lie group homomorphism from $\mathbb{R}$ (with its additive group structure) to $G$ is called a \emph{one-parameter subgroup} of $G$. (Note that the term ``one-parameter subgroup'' refers, confusingly, to a homomorphism, not to a Lie subgroup of $G$. But the image of a one-parameter subgroup is always a Lie subgroup of dimension at most~1.) Theorem 20.1 of [LeeSM] shows that for every $X \in \operatorname{Lie}(G)$, the integral curve of $X$ starting at the identity is a one-parameter subgroup called the \emph{one-parameter subgroup generated by} $X$, and every one-parameter subgroup is of this form.

The \emph{exponential map} of $G$ is the map $\exp^G : \operatorname{Lie}(G) \to G$ defined by setting $\exp^G(X) = \gamma(1)$, where $\gamma$ is the integral curve of $X$ starting at $e$. (The exponential map is more commonly denoted simply by $\exp$, but we use the notation $\exp^G$ to distinguish the Lie group exponential map from the Riemannian exponential map, introduced in Chapter~5.)

blocks.label: prop:properties-of-lie-group-exponential-map
blocks.title: Properties of the Lie Group Exponential Map

blocks.tex: The exponential map is the key ingredient in the proof of the following fundamental result.

blocks.label: thm:closed-subgroup-theorem
blocks.title: Closed Subgroup Theorem
blocks.body: Suppose $G$ is a Lie group and $H \subseteq G$ is a subgroup in the algebraic sense. Then $H$ is an embedded Lie subgroup of $G$ if and only if it is closed in the topological sense.

deps.title: Properties of the Lie Group Exponential Map
deps.label: prop:properties-of-lie-group-exponential-map

blocks.tex: An important special case of the closed subgroup theorem is that of a \emph{discrete subgroup}, that is, a subgroup that is discrete in the subspace topology.

blocks.label: prop:discrete-subgroups-dimension-zero
blocks.title: prop:discrete-subgroups-dimension-zero
blocks.body: Every discrete subgroup of a Lie group is a closed Lie subgroup of dimension zero.

blocks.tex: Let $G$ be a Lie group and $\mathfrak{g}$ its Lie algebra. For every $\varphi \in G$, conjugation by $\varphi$ gives a Lie group automorphism $C_\varphi: G \to G$, called an \emph{inner automorphism}, by $C_\varphi(\psi) = \varphi\psi\varphi^{-1}$. Let $\text{Ad}(\varphi) = (C_\varphi)_*: \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g}$ be the induced Lie algebra automorphism. It follows from the definition that $C_{\varphi_1} \circ C_{\varphi_2} = C_{\varphi_1\varphi_2}$, and therefore $\text{Ad}(\varphi_1) \circ \text{Ad}(\varphi_2) = \text{Ad}(\varphi_1\varphi_2)$; in other words, $\text{Ad}: G \to \text{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ is a representation, called the \emph{adjoint representation of $G$}. Proposition 20.24 in [LeeSM] shows that Ad is a smooth map.

blocks.label: ex:adjoint-representations-lie-groups
blocks.title: Adjoint Representations of Lie Groups

blocks.tex:
There is also an adjoint representation for Lie algebras. If $\mathfrak{q}$ is any Lie algebra, a \emph{representation of $\mathfrak{q}$} is a Lie algebra homomorphism from $\mathfrak{q}$ to $\mathfrak{gl}(V)$, the Lie algebra of all linear endomorphisms of some vector space $V$. For each $X \in \mathfrak{g}$, define a map $\text{ad}(X): \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g}$ by $\text{ad}(X)Y = [X, Y]$. This defines ad as a map from $\mathfrak{g}(\mathfrak{g})$, and a straightforward computation shows that it is a representation of $\mathfrak{g}$, called the \emph{adjoint representation of $\mathfrak{g}$}.

The next proposition shows how the two adjoint representations are related.

blocks.label: prop:adjoint-induced-from-group
blocks.title: prop:adjoint-induced-from-group
blocks.body: Let $G$ be a Lie group and $\mathfrak{g}$ its Lie algebra, and let $\text{Ad}: G \to \text{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ and $\text{ad}: \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{gl}(\mathfrak{g})$ be their respective adjoint representations. The induced Lie algebra representation $\text{Ad}_*: \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{gl}(\mathfrak{g})$ is given by $\text{Ad}_* = \text{ad}$.

blocks.tex:
The most important applications of Lie groups in differential geometry involve their actions on other manifolds.

First we consider actions by abstract groups, not necessarily endowed with a smooth manifold structure or even a topology. Let $G$ be a group and $M$ a set. A \emph{left action of $G$ on $M$} is a map $G \times M \to M$, usually written $(\varphi, p) \mapsto \varphi \cdot p$, satisfying $\varphi_1 \cdot (\varphi_2 \cdot p) = (\varphi_1\varphi_2) \cdot p$ and $e \cdot p = p$ for all $\varphi_1, \varphi_2 \in G$ and $p \in M$, where $e$ is the identity element of $G$. Similarly, a \emph{right action of $G$ on $M$} is a map $M \times G \to M$ satisfying $(p \cdot \varphi_1) \cdot \varphi_2 = p \cdot (\varphi_1\varphi_2)$ and $p \cdot e = p$. In some cases, it will be important to give a name to an action such as $\theta: G \times M \to M$, in which case we write $\theta_\varphi(p)$ in place of $\varphi \cdot p$, with a similar convention for right actions. Since right actions can be converted to left actions and vice versa by setting $\varphi \cdot p = p \cdot \varphi^{-1}$, for most purposes we lose no real generality by restricting attention to left actions. (But there are also situations in which right actions arise naturally.)

Given an action of $G$ on $M$, for each $p \in M$ the \textit{isotropy subgroup at $p$} is the subgroup $G_p \subseteq G$ consisting of all elements that fix $p$; that is, $G_p = \{\varphi \in G : \varphi \cdot p = p\}$. The group action is said to be \textit{free} if $\varphi \cdot p = p$ for some $\varphi \in G$ and $p \in M$ implies $\varphi = e$. In other words, if $G_p = \{e\}$ for every $p$. It is said to be \textit{effective} if $\varphi_1 \cdot p = \varphi_2 \cdot p$ for all $p$ if and only if $\varphi_1 = \varphi_2$, or equivalently, if the only element of $G$ that fixes every element of $M$ is the identity. For effective actions, each element of $G$ is uniquely determined by the map $p \mapsto \varphi \cdot p$. In such cases, we will sometimes use the same notation $\varphi$ to denote either the element $\varphi \in G$ or the map $p \mapsto \varphi \cdot p$. The action is said to be \textit{transitive} if for every pair of points $p, q \in M$, there exists $\varphi \in G$ such that $\varphi \cdot p = q$.

If $M$ is a smooth manifold, an action by a group $G$ on $M$ is said to be an \textit{action by diffeomorphisms} if for each $\varphi \in G$, the map $p \mapsto \varphi \cdot p$ is a diffeomorphism of $M$. If $G$ is a Lie group, an action of $G$ on a smooth manifold $M$ is said to be a \textit{smooth action} if the defining map $G \times M \to M$ is smooth. In this case, it is also an action by diffeomorphisms, because each map $p \mapsto \varphi \cdot p$ is smooth and has $p \mapsto \varphi^{-1} \cdot p$ as an inverse. If $G$ is any countable group, an action by $G$ on $M$ is an action by diffeomorphisms if and only if it is a smooth action when $G$ is regarded as a $0$-dimensional Lie group with the discrete topology.

blocks.label: ex:semidirect-products
blocks.title: Semidirect Products
blocks.body: Group actions provide an important way to construct Lie groups out of other Lie groups. Suppose $H$ and $N$ are Lie groups and $\theta : H \times N \to N$ is a smooth left action of $H$ on $N$ by automorphisms of $N$, meaning that for each $h \in H$, the map $\theta_h : N \to N$ given by $\theta_h(n) = h \cdot n$ is a Lie group automorphism. The \textit{semidirect product of $H$ and $N$} determined by $\theta$, denoted by $N \rtimes_\theta H$, is the Lie group whose underlying manifold is the Cartesian product $N \times H$, and whose group multiplication is $(n,h)(n',h') = (n\theta_h(n'),hh')$.

blocks.label: thm:equivariant-rank-theorem
blocks.title: Equivariant Rank Theorem
blocks.body: Suppose $M, N$ are smooth manifolds, and $G$ is a Lie group acting smoothly and transitively on $M$ and smoothly on $N$. If $F : M \to N$ is a smooth $G$-equivariant map, then $F$ has constant rank. Thus, if $F$ is surjective, it is a smooth submersion; if it is injective, it is a smooth immersion; and if it is bijective, it is a diffeomorphism.

blocks.tex: A smooth action of $G$ on $M$ is said to be a \textit{proper action} if the map $G \times M \to M \times M$ defined by $(\varphi, x) \mapsto (\varphi \cdot x, x)$ is a proper map, meaning that the preimage of every compact set is compact. The following characterization is usually the easiest way to prove that a given action is proper.

blocks.label: prop:proper-action-condition
blocks.title: prop:proper-action-condition
blocks.body: Suppose $G$ is a Lie group acting smoothly on a smooth manifold $M$. The action is proper if and only if the following condition is satisfied: if $(p_i)$ is a sequence in $M$ and $(\varphi_i)$ is a sequence in $G$ such that both $(p_i)$ and $(\varphi_i \cdot p_i)$ converge, then a subsequence of $(\varphi_i)$ converges.

blocks.label: cor:compact-lie-group-actions-proper
blocks.title: cor:compact-lie-group-actions-proper
blocks.body: Every smooth action by a compact Lie group on a smooth manifold is proper.

blocks.tex: If $G$ is a compact Lie group, then every sequence in $G$ has a convergent subsequence, so every smooth $G$-action is proper by the preceding proposition. $\square$

The next theorem is the most important application of proper actions. If a group $G$ acts on a manifold $M$, then each $p \in M$ determines a subset $G \cdot p = \{\varphi \cdot p : \varphi \in G\}$, called the \textit{orbit of $p$}. Because two orbits are either identical or disjoint, the orbits form a partition of $G$. The set of orbits is denoted by $M/G$, and with the quotient topology it is called the \textit{orbit space} of the action.

blocks.label: thm:quotient-manifold-theorem
blocks.title: Quotient Manifold Theorem
blocks.body: Suppose $G$ is a Lie group acting smoothly, freely, and properly on a smooth manifold $M$. Then the orbit space $M/G$ is a topological manifold whose dimension is equal to the difference $\dim M - \dim G$, and it has a unique smooth structure with the property that the quotient map $\pi: M \to M/G$ is a smooth submersion.

blocks.label: ex:real-projective-spaces
blocks.title: Real Projective Spaces
blocks.body: For each nonnegative integer $n$, the $n$-dimensional \textit{real projective space}, denoted by $\mathbb{RP}^n$, is defined as the set of one-dimensional linear subspaces of $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$. It can be identified with the orbit space of $\mathbb{R}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\}$ under the action of the group $\mathbb{R}^*$ of nonzero real numbers given by scalar multiplication: $\lambda \cdot (x^1, \ldots, x^{n+1}) = (\lambda x^1, \ldots, \lambda x^{n+1})$. It is easy to check that this action is smooth and free. To see that it is proper, we use Proposition~C.15. Suppose $(x_i)$ is a sequence in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\}$ and $(\lambda_i)$ is a sequence in $\mathbb{R}^*$ such that $x_i \to x \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\}$ and $\lambda_i x_i \to y \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\}$. Then $|\lambda_i| = |\lambda_i x_i|/|x_i|$. Thus the numbers $\lambda_i$ all lie in a compact set of the form $\{\lambda : 1/C \le |\lambda| \le C\}$ for some positive number $C$, so a subsequence converges to a nonzero real number. Therefore, by the quotient manifold theorem, $\mathbb{RP}^n$ has a unique structure as a smooth $n$-dimensional manifold such that the quotient map $\mathbb{R}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\} \to \mathbb{RP}^n$ is a smooth submersion.

blocks.label: ex:complex-projective-spaces
blocks.title: Complex Projective Spaces
blocks.body: Similarly, the $n$-dimensional \textit{complex projective space}, denoted by $\mathbb{CP}^n$, is the set of 1-dimensional complex subspaces of $\mathbb{C}^{n+1}$, identified with the orbit space of $\mathbb{C}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\}$ under the $\mathbb{C}^*$-action given by $\lambda \cdot z = \lambda z$. The same argument as in the preceding example shows that this action is smooth, free, and proper, so $\mathbb{CP}^n$ is a smooth $2n$-dimensional manifold and the quotient map $\pi: \mathbb{C}^{n+1} \setminus \{0\} \to \mathbb{CP}^n$ is a smooth submersion.

blocks.tex: There is a close connection between smooth covering maps and smooth group actions. To begin, suppose $\tilde{M}$ and $M$ are smooth manifolds and $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a smooth covering map. A \emph{covering automorphism} of $\pi$ is a diffeomorphism $\varphi: \widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{M}$ such that $\pi \circ \varphi = \pi$; the set of all covering automorphisms is a group under composition, called the \emph{covering automorphism group} and denoted by $\mathrm{Aut}_\pi(\widetilde{M})$.

blocks.label: prop:covering-automorphisms-discrete-lie-group
blocks.title: prop:covering-automorphisms-discrete-lie-group
blocks.body: Let $\pi: \widetilde{M} \to M$ be a smooth covering map. With the discrete topology, $\mathrm{Aut}_\pi(\widetilde{M})$ is a discrete Lie group acting smoothly, freely, and properly on $M$.

blocks.tex: Because of the requirement that a covering automorphism $\varphi$ satisfy $\pi \circ \varphi = \pi$, it follows that $\varphi$ restricts to an action on each fiber of $\pi$. The next proposition describes the conditions in which this action is transitive. A covering map $\pi: \widetilde{M} \to M$ is called a \emph{normal covering} if the image of the homomorphism $\pi_*: \pi_1(\widetilde{M}, \widetilde{x}) \to \pi_1(M, \pi(\widetilde{x}))$ is a normal subgroup of $\pi_1(M, \pi(\widetilde{x}))$. (Note that every universal covering is a normal covering, because the trivial subgroup is always normal.)

blocks.label: prop:normal-covering-transitive-action
blocks.title: prop:normal-covering-transitive-action
blocks.body: If $\pi: \widetilde{M} \to M$ is a smooth covering map, then the automorphism group of $\pi$ acts transitively on each fiber of $\pi$ if and only if $\pi$ is a normal covering.

blocks.tex: The universal covering is the most important special case.

blocks.label: prop:automorphisms-universal-covering
blocks.title: Automorphisms of the Universal Covering
blocks.body: Suppose $M$ is a connected smooth manifold and $\pi: \widetilde{M} \to M$ is its universal covering. Then $\mathrm{Aut}_\pi(\widetilde{M})$ is isomorphic to the fundamental group of $M$, and it acts transitively on each fiber of $\pi$.

blocks.tex: The next theorem, which is an application of the quotient manifold theorem, is a partial converse to Proposition C.20.

blocks.label: prop:quotient-orbit-space-covering-map
blocks.title: prop:quotient-orbit-space-covering-map
blocks.body: Suppose $\widetilde{M}$ is a connected smooth manifold and $\Gamma$ is a discrete Lie group acting smoothly, freely, and properly on $\widetilde{M}$. Then the orbit space $\widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ has a unique smooth manifold structure such that the quotient map $\widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{M}/\Gamma$ is a smooth normal covering map.

blocks.label: ex:universal-covering-projective-space
blocks.title: The Universal Covering of $\mathbb{RP}^n$
blocks.body: The two-element group $\Gamma = \{\pm 1\}$ acts smoothly, freely, and properly on $S^n$ by multiplication, and thus $S^n/\Gamma$ is a smooth manifold and the quotient map $\pi: S^n \to S^n/\Gamma$ is a two-sheeted smooth normal covering map. Note also that the quotient map $\mathbb{R}^{n+1} \to \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ defined in Example C.18 restricts to a surjective smooth map $q: S^n \to \mathbb{RP}^n$, which is a submersion because each point of $S^n$ is in the image of a smooth local section. Since $q$ and $\pi$ are constant on each other's fibers, Proposition A.19 shows that there is a diffeomorphism $S^n/\Gamma \to \mathbb{RP}^n$, and $q$ is equal to the composition $S^n \to S^n/\Gamma \to \mathbb{RP}^n$. Since this is a smooth normal covering map followed by a diffeomorphism, $q$ is also a smooth normal covering map. Thus $S^n$ is the universal covering space of $\mathbb{RP}^n$, and the fundamental group of $\mathbb{RP}^n$ is isomorphic to $\{\pm 1\}$.

entries.label: def:rigid-motion
entries.title: rigid motion, congruence
entries.body:
A \emph{rigid motion} of a Euclidean space $E$ is a bijection $f\colon E\to E$ with
$\norm{f(x)-f(y)} = \norm{x-y}$ for all $x,y\in E$. Two families of points $(v_i)_{i\in I}$ and
$(w_i)_{i\in I}$ of $E$ are \emph{congruent} if there is a rigid motion $f$ of $E$ with $f(v_i)=w_i$
for every $i$; two subsets of $E$ are congruent if some rigid motion carries the first onto the
second. By the Mazur--Ulam theorem a distance-preserving bijection of $E$ is automatically affine, so
the rigid motions of $E$ are exactly its affine isometric automorphisms.

entries.label: lem:isometry-extension
entries.title: isometry extension
entries.body:
Let $E$ be a finite-dimensional Euclidean space and let $(v_i)_{i\in I}$ and $(w_i)_{i\in I}$ be
nonempty families of points of $E$ satisfying $\norm{v_i-v_j} = \norm{w_i-w_j}$ for all $i,j\in I$.
Then $(v_i)$ and $(w_i)$ are congruent.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch01.exists_affineIsometryEquiv_of_dist_eq
lean.code:
theorem exists_affineIsometryEquiv_of_dist_eq [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [Nonempty ι] {v w : ι → E}
    (h : ∀ i j, dist (v i) (v j) = dist (w i) (w j)) :
    ∃ f : E ≃ᵃⁱ[ℝ] E, ∀ i, f (v i) = w i := by
  obtain ⟨i₀⟩ := ‹Nonempty ι›
  obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := exists_linearIsometryEquiv_of_inner_eq (inner_vsub_eq_of_dist_eq i₀ h)
  refine ⟨((AffineIsometryEquiv.constVAdd ℝ E (-v i₀)).trans
    g.toAffineIsometryEquiv).trans (AffineIsometryEquiv.constVAdd ℝ E (w i₀)), fun i => ?_⟩
  simp only [AffineIsometryEquiv.coe_trans, Function.comp_apply,
    AffineIsometryEquiv.coe_constVAdd, LinearIsometryEquiv.coe_toAffineIsometryEquiv]
  have hvi : -v i₀ +ᵥ v i = v i - v i₀ := by
    rw [vadd_eq_add]; abel
  rw [hvi, hg i, vadd_eq_add]
  abel

entries.label: thm:side-side-side
entries.title: Side-Side-Side
entries.body: Two Euclidean triangles are congruent if and only if the lengths of their corresponding sides are equal.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch01.side_side_side_iff
lean.code:
theorem side_side_side_iff [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] (a b c a' b' c' : E) :
    (∃ f : E ≃ᵃⁱ[ℝ] E, f a = a' ∧ f b = b' ∧ f c = c') ↔
      dist a b = dist a' b' ∧ dist b c = dist b' c' ∧ dist c a = dist c' a' := by
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨f, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨(f.isometry.dist_eq a b).symm, (f.isometry.dist_eq b c).symm,
      (f.isometry.dist_eq c a).symm⟩
  · rintro ⟨hab, hbc, hca⟩
    have hba : dist b a = dist b' a' := by rw [dist_comm b a, dist_comm b' a']; exact hab
    have hcb : dist c b = dist c' b' := by rw [dist_comm c b, dist_comm c' b']; exact hbc
    have hac : dist a c = dist a' c' := by rw [dist_comm a c, dist_comm a' c']; exact hca
    have hdist : ∀ i j : Fin 3, dist (![a, b, c] i) (![a, b, c] j)
        = dist (![a', b', c'] i) (![a', b', c'] j) := by
      intro i j
      fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> simp only [dist_self] <;> assumption
    obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := exists_affineIsometryEquiv_of_dist_eq hdist
    exact ⟨f, hf 0, hf 1, hf 2⟩

entries.label: thm:angle-sum-theorem
entries.title: Angle-Sum Theorem
entries.body: The sum of the interior angles of a Euclidean triangle is $\pi$.

entries.label: thm:circle-classification
entries.title: Circle Classification Theorem
entries.body: Two circles in the Euclidean plane are congruent if and only if they have the same radius.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch01.euclidean_circle_congruent_iff_radius_eq
lean.code:
theorem euclidean_circle_congruent_iff_radius_eq
    (c₁ c₂ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) {r₁ r₂ : ℝ} (hr₁ : 0 ≤ r₁) (hr₂ : 0 ≤ r₂) :
    (∃ f : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) ≃ᵃⁱ[ℝ] EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2),
      f '' sphere c₁ r₁ = sphere c₂ r₂) ↔ r₁ = r₂ :=
  circle_congruent_iff_radius_eq c₁ c₂ hr₁ hr₂

entries.label: thm:circumference-theorem
entries.title: Circumference Theorem
entries.body: The circumference of a Euclidean circle of radius $R$ is $2\pi R$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch01.circumference_circle
lean.code:
theorem circumference_circle (c : ℂ) {R : ℝ} (hR : 0 ≤ R) :
    eVariationOn (circleMap c R) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * π)) = ENNReal.ofReal (2 * π * R) := by
  rw [eVariationOn_circleMap_Icc c R (by positivity), abs_of_nonneg hR]
  ring_nf

entries.label: thm:plane-curve-classification
entries.title: Plane Curve Classification Theorem
entries.body: Suppose $\gamma$ and $\tilde{\gamma}: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}^2$ are smooth, unit-speed plane curves with unit normal vector fields $N$ and $\tilde{N}$, and $\kappa_N(t), \kappa_{\tilde{N}}(t)$ represent the signed curvatures at $\gamma(t)$ and $\tilde{\gamma}(t)$, respectively. Then $\gamma$ and $\tilde{\gamma}$ are congruent by a direction-preserving congruence if and only if $\kappa_N(t) = \kappa_{\tilde{N}}(t)$ for all $t \in [a,b]$.

entries.label: thm:total-curvature
entries.title: Total Curvature Theorem
entries.body:
If $\gamma: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}^2$ is a unit-speed simple closed curve such that $\gamma'(a) = \gamma'(b)$, and $N$ is the inward-pointing normal, then
\[
\int_a^b \kappa_N(t) \, dt = 2\pi.
\]

entries.label: thm:uniformization-theorem
entries.title: Uniformization Theorem
entries.body: Every connected 2-manifold is diffeomorphic to a quotient of one of the constant-curvature model surfaces described above by a discrete group of isometries without fixed points. Thus every connected 2-manifold has a complete Riemannian metric with constant Gaussian curvature.

entries.label: thm:gauss-bonnet-theorem-preview
entries.title: Gauss--Bonnet Theorem
entries.body:
Suppose $S$ is a compact Riemannian 2-manifold. Then
\[
\int_S K \, dA = 2\pi \chi(S),
\]
where $\chi(S)$ is the Euler characteristic of $S$.

entries.label: thm:characterization-constant-curvature
entries.title: Characterization of Constant-Curvature Metrics
entries.body: The complete, connected, $n$-dimensional Riemannian manifolds of constant sectional curvature are, up to isometry, exactly the Riemannian quotients of the form $\widetilde{M}/\Gamma$, where $\widetilde{M}$ is a Euclidean space, sphere, or hyperbolic space with constant sectional curvature, and $\Gamma$ is a discrete group of isometries of $\widetilde{M}$ that acts freely on $\widetilde{M}$.

entries.label: thm:cartan-hadamard-preview
entries.title: Cartan--Hadamard
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a complete, connected Riemannian $n$-manifold with all sectional curvatures less than or equal to zero. Then the universal covering space of $M$ is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$.

entries.label: thm:myers
entries.title: Myers
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a complete, connected Riemannian manifold with all sectional curvatures bounded below by a positive constant. Then $M$ is compact and has a finite fundamental group.

entries.label: lem:polarization-identity
entries.title: Polarization Identity
entries.body:
Suppose $\langle \cdot,\cdot \rangle$ is an inner product on a vector space $V$. Then for all $v, w \in V$,
$$\langle v,w \rangle = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[\langle v+w, v+w \rangle - \langle v-w, v-w \rangle\bigr].$$

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.real_inner_eq_inner_add_add_self_sub_inner_sub_sub_self_div_four
lean.code:
theorem real_inner_eq_inner_add_add_self_sub_inner_sub_sub_self_div_four (v w : V) :
    ⟪v, w⟫_ℝ = (⟪v + w, v + w⟫_ℝ - ⟪v - w, v - w⟫_ℝ) / 4 := by
  rw [real_inner_add_add_self, real_inner_sub_sub_self]
  ring

end Polarization

section ScalarProduct

variable {V : Type*} [AddCommGroup V] [Module ℝ V]

entries.label: prop:gram-schmidt
entries.title: Gram-Schmidt Algorithm
entries.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional inner product space, and suppose $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ is any ordered basis for $V$. Then there is an orthonormal ordered basis $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$ satisfying the following conditions:
$$\text{span}(b_1,\ldots,b_k) = \text{span}(v_1,\ldots,v_k) \quad \text{for each } k = 1,\ldots,n.$$

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_orthonormalBasis_span_image_Iic_eq
lean.code:
theorem exists_orthonormalBasis_span_image_Iic_eq {n : ℕ} (v : Module.Basis (Fin n) ℝ V) :
    ∃ b : OrthonormalBasis (Fin n) ℝ V,
      ∀ k : Fin n, span ℝ ((b : Fin n → V) '' Set.Iic k) = span ℝ ((v : Fin n → V) '' Set.Iic k) := by
  have hfin : FiniteDimensional ℝ V := Module.Basis.finiteDimensional_of_finite v
  have hcard : Module.finrank ℝ V = Fintype.card (Fin n) := by
    simp [Module.finrank_eq_card_basis v]
  -- On a basis the Gram-Schmidt process never produces a zero vector, so the
  -- orthonormal basis it returns *is* the normalized Gram-Schmidt family.
  have hne : ∀ i : Fin n, gramSchmidtNormed ℝ (v : Fin n → V) i ≠ 0 := by
    intro i
    have h₀ : LinearIndependent ℝ (v : Fin n → V) := v.linearIndependent
    simpa [gramSchmidtNormed, smul_eq_zero, norm_eq_zero] using gramSchmidt_ne_zero i h₀
  refine ⟨gramSchmidtOrthonormalBasis hcard (v : Fin n → V), fun k => ?_⟩
  have hpt : ((gramSchmidtOrthonormalBasis hcard (v : Fin n → V) : Fin n → V) '' Set.Iic k)
      = (gramSchmidtNormed ℝ (v : Fin n → V) '' Set.Iic k) := by
    apply Set.image_congr
    intro i _
    exact gramSchmidtOrthonormalBasis_apply hcard (hne i)
  rw [hpt, span_gramSchmidtNormed, span_gramSchmidt_Iic]

end GramSchmidt

section LinearIsometry

variable {V W : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V]
  [NormedAddCommGroup W] [InnerProductSpace ℝ W]

entries.label: prop:inner-product-spaces-linearly-isometric
entries.title: Classification of Inner Product Spaces
entries.body: All inner product spaces of the same finite dimension are linearly isometric to each other.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.nonempty_linearIsometryEquiv_of_finrank_eq
lean.code:
theorem nonempty_linearIsometryEquiv_of_finrank_eq
    [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] [FiniteDimensional ℝ W]
    (h : Module.finrank ℝ V = Module.finrank ℝ W) : Nonempty (V ≃ₗᵢ[ℝ] W) :=
  ⟨linearIsometryEquivOfOrthonormalBasis
    ((stdOrthonormalBasis ℝ V).reindex (finCongr h)) (stdOrthonormalBasis ℝ W)⟩

entries.label: def:riemannian-metric
entries.title: Riemannian Metric
entries.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold. A \textbf{Riemannian metric} on $M$ is a smooth covariant $2$-tensor field $g$ on $M$ whose value $g_p$ at each $p \in M$ is an inner product on $T_p M$; thus $g$ is a symmetric $2$-tensor field that is positive definite, in the sense that $g_p(v,v) \geq 0$ for each $p \in M$ and each $v \in T_p M$, with equality if and only if $v = 0$. A \textbf{Riemannian manifold} is a pair $(M,g)$ consisting of a smooth manifold $M$ together with a Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric
lean.code:
abbrev RiemannianMetric [IsManifold I ∞ M] : Type _ :=
  Bundle.ContMDiffRiemannianMetric I ∞ E (TangentSpace I : M → Type _)

namespace RiemannianMetric

variable (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (p : M)

entries.label: lem:convex-inner-products
entries.title: Convexity of the set of inner products
entries.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and let $p \in M$. The set of inner products on $T_p M$ is a convex subset of the space of bilinear forms on $T_p M$: if $q_1, q_2$ are inner products and $a, b \geq 0$ satisfy $a + b = 1$, then $a q_1 + b q_2$ is an inner product.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.convex_symm_posDef
lean.code:
theorem convex_symm_posDef (x : M) :
    Convex ℝ {q : TangentSpace I x →L[ℝ] TangentSpace I x →L[ℝ] ℝ |
      (∀ u v, q u v = q v u) ∧ (∀ v, v ≠ 0 → 0 < q v v)} := by
  intro q₁ hq₁ q₂ hq₂ a b ha hb hab
  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
  · intro u v
    simp only [ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    rw [hq₁.1 u v, hq₂.1 u v]
  · intro v hv
    simp only [ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    have p1 := hq₁.2 v hv; have p2 := hq₂.2 v hv
    have hab' : 0 < a ∨ 0 < b := by
      by_contra h; rw [not_or, not_lt, not_lt] at h
      have : a = 0 ∧ b = 0 := ⟨le_antisymm h.1 ha, le_antisymm h.2 hb⟩
      rw [this.1, this.2] at hab; norm_num at hab
    rcases hab' with ha' | hb'
    · nlinarith [mul_pos ha' p1, mul_nonneg hb p2.le]
    · nlinarith [mul_nonneg ha p1.le, mul_pos hb' p2]

variable [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [T2Space M] [SigmaCompactSpace M]

set_option maxHeartbeats 1000000 in

entries.label: lem:posdef-coercive
entries.title: Positive definite forms are coercive
entries.body: Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional normed real vector space and let $q$ be a bilinear form on $V$ with $q(v,v) > 0$ for every $v \neq 0$. Then the sublevel set $\{v \in V : q(v,v) < 1\}$ is bounded. The reason is that $q$ is coercive: there is a constant $c > 0$ with $q(v,v) \geq c |v|^2$ for all $v \in V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isVonNBounded_of_posDef
lean.code:
theorem isVonNBounded_of_posDef {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℝ F]
    [FiniteDimensional ℝ F] (q : F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ) (hpos : ∀ v : F, v ≠ 0 → 0 < q v v) :
    Bornology.IsVonNBounded ℝ {v : F | q v v < 1} := by
  rcases subsingleton_or_nontrivial F with hs | hn
  · exact Set.Finite.isVonNBounded (𝕜 := ℝ) (Set.toFinite _)
  have hcompact : IsCompact (Metric.sphere (0 : F) 1) := isCompact_sphere 0 1
  have hne : (Metric.sphere (0 : F) 1).Nonempty := NormedSpace.sphere_nonempty.mpr zero_le_one
  have hcont : Continuous fun v : F => q v v := q.continuous.clm_apply continuous_id
  obtain ⟨v₀, hv₀mem, hv₀min⟩ := hcompact.exists_isMinOn hne hcont.continuousOn
  set c := q v₀ v₀ with hc_def
  have hv₀ne : v₀ ≠ 0 := by
    intro h; rw [mem_sphere_iff_norm, sub_zero, h, norm_zero] at hv₀mem; norm_num at hv₀mem
  have hc : 0 < c := hpos v₀ hv₀ne
  have hcoer : ∀ v : F, c * ‖v‖ ^ 2 ≤ q v v := by
    intro v; rcases eq_or_ne v 0 with rfl | hv
    · simp
    · have hnv : ‖v‖ ≠ 0 := norm_ne_zero_iff.mpr hv
      set u := ‖v‖⁻¹ • v with hu
      have hmem : u ∈ Metric.sphere (0 : F) 1 := by
        rw [mem_sphere_iff_norm, sub_zero, hu, norm_smul, norm_inv, norm_norm, inv_mul_cancel₀ hnv]
      have hqu : c ≤ q u u := hv₀min hmem
      have hexp : q v v = ‖v‖ ^ 2 * q u u := by
        rw [hu]; simp only [map_smul, ContinuousLinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]; field_simp
      rw [hexp]; nlinarith [hqu, sq_nonneg ‖v‖]
  apply Bornology.IsVonNBounded.subset _ (NormedSpace.isVonNBounded_ball ℝ F (Real.sqrt (1 / c) + 1))
  intro v hv; simp only [Set.mem_setOf_eq] at hv; rw [Metric.mem_ball, dist_zero_right]
  have h1 : c * ‖v‖ ^ 2 < 1 := lt_of_le_of_lt (hcoer v) hv
  have h2 : ‖v‖ ^ 2 < 1 / c := by rw [lt_div_iff₀ hc]; linarith [mul_comm c (‖v‖^2)]
  have h3 : ‖v‖ < Real.sqrt (1 / c) := by
