entries.label: def:smooth-density
entries.title: Smooth Density
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth $n$-manifold. A \emph{smooth density} on $M$ is a family $\mu$ assigning to
each $x \in M$ a density $\mu_x$ on $T_x M$, such that for every smooth local frame
$(Y_1, \ldots, Y_n)$ on an open set $u \subseteq M$ the function
$x \mapsto \mu_x(Y_1|_x, \ldots, Y_n|_x)$ is smooth on $u$.

entries.label: def:riemannian-density-pointwise
entries.title: The Riemannian Density, Pointwise
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian $n$-manifold. For $x \in M$ and $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in T_x M$ let
\[
\mu_x(v_1, \ldots, v_n) := \sqrt{\det \bigl( \langle v_i, v_j \rangle_g \bigr)_{i,j=1}^{n}}
\]
be the density of the inner product space $(T_x M, g_x)$ of Definition
\ref{def:density-pointwise}. No orientation of $M$ enters this definition.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.densityAt

deps.title: The Density of an Inner Product Space
deps.label: def:density-pointwise

entries.label: prop:density-smooth
entries.title: Smoothness of the Riemannian Density
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian $n$-manifold and let $(Y_1, \ldots, Y_n)$ be a smooth local frame on
an open set $u \subseteq M$. Then $x \mapsto \mu_x(Y_1|_x, \ldots, Y_n|_x)$ is smooth on $u$; that
is, $\mu$ is a smooth density.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.contMDiffOn_densityAt
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffOn_densityAt {u : Set M} {Y : Fin (finrank ℝ E) → (x : M) → TangentSpace I x}
    (hY : IsLocalFrameOn I E ∞ Y u) :
    ContMDiffOn I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞ (fun x => g.densityAt x (Y · x)) u := by
  classical
  intro x hx
  -- the Gram entries are smooth
  have hentry : ∀ i j, ContMDiffWithinAt I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
      (fun q => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)) u x := fun i j =>
    g.contMDiffWithinAt_innerAt (hY.contMDiffOn i x hx) (hY.contMDiffOn j x hx)
  -- a determinant is a polynomial in its entries
  have hdet : ContMDiffWithinAt I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
      (fun q => (Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)).det) u x := by
    simp only [Matrix.det_apply']
    refine ContMDiffWithinAt.sum fun σ _ => ?_
    exact contMDiffWithinAt_const.mul (ContMDiffWithinAt.prod fun i _ => hentry (σ i) i)
  -- the determinant is positive at `x`, so `√` is smooth there
  have hpos : 0 < (Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner x (Y i x) (Y j x)).det := by
    have := g.densityAt_pos (x := x) (v := (Y · x)) (hY.linearIndependent hx)
    rw [densityAt, Real.sqrt_pos] at this
    exact this
  show ContMDiffWithinAt I 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
    (fun q => Real.sqrt ((Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)).det)) u x
  exact (Real.contDiffAt_sqrt (ne_of_gt hpos)).comp_contMDiffWithinAt
    (f := fun q => (Matrix.of fun i j => g.inner q (Y i q) (Y j q)).det) hdet

deps.title: Smoothness of the pairing of vector fields
deps.label: lem:inner-product-vector-fields-smooth

entries.label: prop:riemannian-density
entries.title: The Riemannian Density

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.riemannian_density_existsUnique
lean.code:
theorem riemannian_density_existsUnique :
    ∃! μ : SmoothDensity I M, μ.IsRiemannianFor g := by
  refine ⟨g.density, fun u Y hY hon x hx => g.densityAt_apply_eq_one (hon x hx), ?_⟩
  intro μ hμ
  refine SmoothDensity.ext (funext fun x => ?_)
  letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  -- an orthonormal frame near `x`, hence an orthonormal basis of `T_x M`
  obtain ⟨u, Y, hu, hxu, hY, hon⟩ := exists_orthonormalFrame_nhds g x
  have hon' : Orthonormal ℝ (hY.toBasisAt hxu) := RiemannianMetric.orthonormal_toBasisAt g hY hon hxu
  set e : OrthonormalBasis (Fin (finrank ℝ E)) ℝ (TangentSpace I x) :=
    (hY.toBasisAt hxu).toOrthonormalBasis hon' with he
  have hecoe : ∀ i, e i = Y i x := by
    intro i
    rw [he, Basis.coe_toOrthonormalBasis, IsLocalFrameOn.toBasisAt_coe]
  -- `μ_x` and `densityL` are two densities agreeing on that basis
  have h1 : μ.toFun x (e ·) = 1 := by
    rw [show (fun i => e i) = (Y · x) from funext hecoe]
    exact hμ u Y hY hon x hxu
  have hxu' := densityL_unique e (μ.toFun x) (μ.isDensity x) h1
  funext v
  rw [RiemannianMetric.density_toFun, g.densityAt_eq_densityL x]
  exact congrFun hxu' v

deps.title: The Density of an Orthonormal Basis
deps.label: prop:density-value-one

entries.label: prop:coordinate-representations-divergence-laplacian
entries.title: prop:coordinate-representations-divergence-laplacian
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary,
and let $(x^i)$ be any smooth local coordinates on an open set $U \subseteq M$. The coordinate
representations of the divergence and Laplacian are as follows:
\[
\operatorname{div}\left(X^i \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}} \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\left(\sqrt{\det g}\, X^i\right),
\]
\[
\Delta u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}} \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\left(\sqrt{\det g}\, g^{ij} \frac{\partial u}{\partial x^j}\right),
\]
where $\det g = \det(g_{kl})$ is the determinant of the component matrix of $g$ in these coordinates. On $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean metric and standard coordinates, these reduce to

\[
\operatorname{div}\left(X^i \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial X^i}{\partial x^i},
\]

\[
\Delta u = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial^2 u}{(\partial x^i)^2}.
\]

entries.label: def:length-of-curve
entries.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
entries.body:
For a curve $\gamma$ into $M$ and $a \le b$, the \emph{length} of $\gamma$ on $[a,b]$ is
\[
  L_g(\gamma|_{[a,b]}) \;=\; \int_{[a,b]} |\gamma'(t)|_g \, dt \;\in\; [0,\infty],
\]
the integral being taken in the Lebesgue sense, so that it is defined for every curve and takes values in $[0,\infty]$. For an admissible curve on a compact interval the integrand is bounded, so the value is finite and agrees with $L_g(\gamma)$ above.

entries.label: prop:length-additive
entries.title: Additivity of Length
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary and $\gamma$ is an admissible curve. If $a \le c \le b$, then
\[
  L_g(\gamma|_{[a,c]}) + L_g(\gamma|_{[c,b]}) = L_g(\gamma|_{[a,b]}).
\]

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

entries.label: prop:length-reparam-invariant
entries.title: Parameter Independence of Length
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary and $\gamma$ is an admissible curve on $[a,b]$. If $\tilde{\gamma} = \gamma \circ \varphi$ is a forward reparametrization of $\gamma$, then $L_g(\tilde{\gamma}) = L_g(\gamma)$.

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

entries.label: prop:length-isometry-invariant
entries.title: Isometry Invariance of Length
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ and $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ are Riemannian manifolds with or without boundary, $\varphi: M \to \tilde{M}$ is a smooth metric-preserving map, and $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ is an admissible curve. Then
\[
  L_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi \circ \gamma) = L_g(\gamma).
\]
In particular this holds when $\varphi$ is a local isometry, since a local isometry preserves the metric.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsMetricPreserving.pathELength_comp
lean.code:
theorem IsMetricPreserving.pathELength_comp (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' φ)
    (hφ : ContMDiff I I' ∞ φ) {γ : ℝ → M} {a b : ℝ}
    (hγ : ContMDiffOn 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) I 1 γ (Icc a b)) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    pathELength I' (φ ∘ γ) a b = pathELength I γ a b := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  rw [pathELength_eq_lintegral_mfderiv_Ioo, pathELength_eq_lintegral_mfderiv_Ioo]
  refine setLIntegral_congr_fun measurableSet_Ioo (fun t ht => ?_)
  have hγt : MDifferentiableAt 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) I γ t :=
    (hγ.mdifferentiableOn one_ne_zero t (Ioo_subset_Icc_self ht)).mdifferentiableAt
      (Icc_mem_nhds ht.1 ht.2)
  rw [mfderiv_comp_apply t (hφ.contMDiffAt.mdifferentiableAt (by simp)) hγt]
  exact h.enorm_mfderiv (γ t) _

end Length

/-! ### Invariance of the Riemannian distance -/

section Distance

variable {g : RiemannianMetric I M} {g' : RiemannianMetric I' M'} {φ : M → M'}

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

entries.label: prop:reparametrization-by-arc-length
entries.title: prop:reparametrization-by-arc-length

entries.label: prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve
entries.title: prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve
entries.body: If $M$ is a connected smooth manifold (with or without boundary), then any two points of $M$ can be joined by an admissible curve.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_contMDiffOn_path_of_preconnected
lean.code:
theorem exists_contMDiffOn_path_of_preconnected [FiniteDimensional ℝ E] [T2Space M]
    [SigmaCompactSpace M] [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    ∃ γ : ℝ → M, γ 0 = x ∧ γ 1 = y ∧ ContMDiffOn 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) I 1 γ (Set.Icc 0 1) := by
  obtain ⟨g⟩ := exists_riemannianMetric (I := I) (M := M)
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  haveI := g.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  exact exists_contMDiffOn_path I x y

end LeeForm

section Euclidean

/-!
### The Riemannian distance of the Euclidean metric

Lee's touchstone: on `ℝⁿ` with the Euclidean metric `ḡ` of Example 2.6, the
Riemannian distance `d_ḡ` is the ordinary Euclidean distance — straight lines are
the shortest curves.  This is the sanity check that the general Theorem 2.55
computes the expected answer in the one case where the answer is known in advance.
-/

variable (F : Type*) [NormedAddCommGroup F] [InnerProductSpace ℝ F]

deps.title: Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric
deps.label: prop:existence-riemannian-metric

entries.label: def:riemannian-distance
entries.title: The Riemannian Distance, extended form
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. For $p, q \in M$ set
\[
  d_g(p,q) \;=\; \inf \bigl\{\, L_g(\gamma) \;:\; \gamma : [0,1] \to M \text{ is } C^1,\;
    \gamma(0) = p,\; \gamma(1) = q \,\bigr\} \;\in\; [0, \infty],
\]
the infimum of the empty set being $\infty$.

deps.title: The Length of a Curve, extended form
deps.label: def:length-of-curve

entries.label: rem:riemannian-distance-c1-vs-admissible
entries.title: rem:riemannian-distance-c1-vs-admissible
entries.body:
Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance} takes the infimum over $C^1$ curves, whereas
Lee takes it over admissible (piecewise regular) curves.  Neither class contains the
other: a $C^1$ curve need not be regular, and an admissible curve need not be $C^1$ at
its corners.  The two infima nevertheless agree, because an admissible curve may be
reparametrized so as to become $C^1$ with vanishing velocity at the corners without
changing its length, and conversely a $C^1$ curve may be approximated in length by
regular ones.  That equality is not needed for anything below and is not proved here;
every statement in this section refers to Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}.

entries.label: prop:connected-manifold-c1-curve
entries.title: Connected Riemannian Manifolds Are $C^1$-Path Connected
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold. Then any two points of $M$ can be joined
by a $C^1$ curve $\gamma : [0,1] \to M$.

This is Proposition \ref{prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve} in the $C^1$ formulation
of Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}. Lee assumes only that $M$ be a connected smooth
manifold, with no metric; the statement here carries a metric because the proof measures with
it. That costs no generality, since every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric by
Proposition \ref{prop:existence-riemannian-metric}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_contMDiffOn_path
lean.code:
theorem exists_contMDiffOn_path [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    ∃ γ : ℝ → M, γ 0 = x ∧ γ 1 = y ∧ CMDiff[Icc 0 1] 1 γ := by
  have h : riemannianEDist I x y < riemannianEDist I x y + 1 :=
    ENNReal.lt_add_right (riemannianEDist_ne_top I x y) one_ne_zero
  obtain ⟨γ, h0, h1, hγ, -⟩ := exists_lt_of_riemannianEDist_lt h
  exact ⟨γ, h0, h1, hγ⟩

end Finiteness

section MetricSpace

variable (I M) in

entries.label: lem:riemannian-distance-symm
entries.title: Symmetry of the Riemannian Distance
entries.body: $d_g(p,q) = d_g(q,p)$ for all $p, q$ in a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$.

entries.label: lem:riemannian-distance-triangle
entries.title: Triangle Inequality for the Riemannian Distance
entries.body: $d_g(p,r) \le d_g(p,q) + d_g(q,r)$ for all $p, q, r$ in a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$.

entries.label: lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small
entries.title: Nearby Points Are at Small Distance
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold, let $p \in M$ and let $c > 0$. Then $p$ has a
neighborhood $U$ such that $d_g(p,q) < c$ for every $q \in U$.

This is the content of Lemma \ref{lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood}(a): a point
close to $p$ in a coordinate neighborhood is joined to $p$ by the coordinate segment,
whose $g$-length is controlled by its Euclidean length.

entries.label: lem:riemannian-distance-small-implies-nearby
entries.title: Points at Small Distance Are Nearby
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and let $U$ be a neighborhood of $p \in M$. Then
there is $c > 0$ such that $\{q \in M : d_g(p,q) < c\} \subseteq U$.

This is the content of Lemma \ref{lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood}(b): a curve
from $p$ that leaves $U$ must cross a coordinate annulus around $p$, and so has
$g$-length bounded below.

entries.label: prop:riemannian-distance-finite
entries.title: Finiteness of the Riemannian Distance
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold. Then $d_g(p,q) < \infty$ for all
$p, q \in M$.

This is the conclusion that Proposition \ref{prop:connected-manifold-admissible-curve}
exists to supply: it is exactly the assertion that some curve of finite length joins
$p$ to $q$, so that the infimum defining $d_g(p,q)$ is taken over a nonempty set of
finite numbers.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.riemannianEDist_ne_top
lean.code:
theorem riemannianEDist_ne_top [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    riemannianEDist I x y ≠ ⊤ := by
  have h : {z : M | riemannianEDist I x z ≠ ⊤} = univ :=
    IsClopen.eq_univ ⟨isClosed_setOf_riemannianEDist_ne_top I x,
      isOpen_setOf_riemannianEDist_ne_top I x⟩
      ⟨x, by simp only [mem_setOf_eq, riemannianEDist_self]; exact ENNReal.zero_ne_top⟩
  exact (h ▸ mem_univ y : y ∈ {z : M | riemannianEDist I x z ≠ ⊤})

variable (I) in

deps.title: Triangle Inequality for the Riemannian Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-triangle

deps.title: Nearby Points Are at Small Distance
deps.label: lem:riemannian-distance-locally-small

entries.label: lem:metric-preserving-distance-le
entries.title: Metric-Preserving Maps Do Not Increase Distance
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ and $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ are Riemannian manifolds with or without boundary and $\varphi : M \to \tilde{M}$ is a smooth metric-preserving map. Then
\[
  d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x), \varphi(y)) \;\le\; d_g(x,y) \qquad \text{for all } x, y \in M.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsMetricPreserving.riemannianEDist_le
lean.code:
theorem IsMetricPreserving.riemannianEDist_le (h : IsMetricPreserving g g' φ)
    (hφ : ContMDiff I I' ∞ φ) (x y : M) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist I' (φ x) (φ y) ≤ riemannianEDist I x y := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  haveI := g.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  haveI := g'.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  refine le_of_forall_gt_imp_ge_of_dense (fun r hr => ?_)
  obtain ⟨γ, hγa, hγb, hγsmooth, hγlen⟩ := exists_lt_of_riemannianEDist_lt hr
  have hcomp : pathELength I' (φ ∘ γ) 0 1 = pathELength I γ 0 1 := h.pathELength_comp hφ hγsmooth
  have hle : riemannianEDist I' (φ x) (φ y) ≤ pathELength I' (φ ∘ γ) 0 1 :=
    riemannianEDist_le_pathELength
      ((hφ.of_le (by simp)).comp_contMDiffOn hγsmooth) (by simp [hγa]) (by simp [hγb]) zero_le_one
  exact ((hle.trans hcomp.le).trans_lt hγlen).le

entries.label: prop:isometry-invariance-riemannian-distance
entries.title: Isometry Invariance of the Riemannian Distance Function
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ are connected Riemannian manifolds with or without boundary, and $\varphi: M \to \tilde{M}$ is an isometry. Then $d_{\tilde{g}}(\varphi(x), \varphi(y)) = d_g(x, y)$ for all $x, y \in M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsIsometry.riemannianEDist_comp
lean.code:
theorem IsIsometry.riemannianEDist_comp {Φ : Diffeomorph I I' M M' ∞}
    (h : IsIsometry g g' Φ) (x y : M) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist I' (Φ x) (Φ y) = riemannianEDist I x y := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I' : M' → Type _) := ⟨g'.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  refine le_antisymm (h.riemannianEDist_le Φ.contMDiff x y) ?_
  have hrev := h.symm.riemannianEDist_le Φ.symm.contMDiff (Φ x) (Φ y)
  rwa [Φ.symm_apply_apply, Φ.symm_apply_apply] at hrev

end Distance

/-! ### Isometries of Euclidean space

A witness that the theory above is not vacuous, and Lee's first example of an
isometry: a linear isometry of inner product spaces is an isometry of the
corresponding Euclidean manifolds (Example 2.6).  Applied to `F = F' = ℝⁿ` these
are the orthogonal maps, the linear part of the rigid motions of Chapter 1.

This matters beyond the example.  `IsIsometry` is a hypothesis of
`IsIsometry.riemannianEDist_comp`, and a hypothesis satisfied only by the
identity would make that theorem say nothing; `isIsometry_euclideanMetric`
exhibits a large supply of genuinely non-identity witnesses.
-/

section EuclideanIsometry

variable {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [InnerProductSpace ℝ F]
  {F' : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F'] [InnerProductSpace ℝ F']

deps.title: Metric-Preserving Maps Do Not Increase Distance
deps.label: lem:metric-preserving-distance-le

entries.label: prop:euclidean-linear-isometry-distance
entries.title: Linear Isometries of Euclidean Space
entries.body:
Let $F$ and $\tilde{F}$ be inner product spaces with their Euclidean metrics, and let $e : F \to \tilde{F}$ be a linear isometry of $F$ onto $\tilde{F}$. Then $e$ is an isometry of the corresponding Riemannian manifolds, and
\[
  d_{\bar{g}}(e(x), e(y)) = |x - y| \qquad \text{for all } x, y \in F.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.isIsometry_euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist
lean.code:
theorem isIsometry_euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist (e : F ≃ₗᵢ[ℝ] F') (x y : F) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) : F → Type _) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F') : F' → Type _) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F').toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F') (e x) (e y) = edist x y := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) : F → Type _) :=
    ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F') : F' → Type _) :=
    ⟨(euclideanMetric F').toRiemannianMetric⟩
  calc riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F') (e x) (e y)
      = riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F) x y :=
        (isIsometry_euclideanMetric e).riemannianEDist_comp x y
    _ = edist x y := euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist F x y

deps.title: Isometry Invariance of the Riemannian Distance Function
deps.label: prop:isometry-invariance-riemannian-distance

entries.label: lem:metric-energy-smooth
entries.title: Smoothness of the Energy Function
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. The energy function $E : TM \to \mathbb{R}$, defined on
the total space of the tangent bundle by
\[
E(x,v) = \langle v, v \rangle_g,
\]
is smooth. Consequently $(x,v) \mapsto |v|_g = E(x,v)^{1/2}$ is continuous on $TM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.contMDiff_innerAt_totalSpace
lean.code:
theorem contMDiff_innerAt_totalSpace (g : RiemannianMetric I M) :
    ContMDiff I.tangent 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) ∞
      (fun z : TangentBundle I M => g.innerAt z.proj z.2 z.2) := by
  letI : Bundle.RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  have hid : ContMDiff I.tangent (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E)) ∞
      (fun z : TangentBundle I M => (z : TotalSpace E (TangentSpace I : M → Type _))) :=
    contMDiff_id
  -- Elaborate the pairing with no expected type: fixing the goal first forces typeclass
  -- synthesis to pick the fibre norm coming from `letI` before it can be unified with `g`'s.
  have hpair := ContMDiff.inner_bundle (IM := I.tangent) (IB := I) (F := E)
    (E := (TangentSpace I : M → Type _)) (n := ∞) (M := TangentBundle I M)
    (b := TotalSpace.proj) (v := fun z : TangentBundle I M => z.2)
    (w := fun z : TangentBundle I M => z.2) hid hid
  exact hpair

end AnyManifold

variable {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℝ F]

entries.label: lem:norm-comparison-compact-core
entries.title: Uniform Comparison over a Compact Set
entries.body:
Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional real normed vector space, let $q$ assign to each $x \in V$ a
bilinear form $q_x$ on $V$, and let $K \subseteq V$ be compact. Write $S = \{v \in V : |v| = 1\}$
and suppose
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $(x,v) \mapsto q_x(v,v)$ is continuous on $K \times S$;
\item[(ii)] $q_x(v,v) > 0$ for every $x \in K$ and every $v \neq 0$.
\end{enumerate}
Then there are positive constants $c, C$ such that
\[
c|v| \leq q_x(v,v)^{1/2} \leq C|v| \qquad \text{for all } x \in K \text{ and all } v \in V.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_forall_sqrt_comparison
lean.code:
theorem exists_forall_sqrt_comparison {B : Type*} [TopologicalSpace B]
    {q : B → F →L[ℝ] F →L[ℝ] ℝ} {K : Set B} (hK : IsCompact K)
    (hcont : ContinuousOn (fun p : B × F => q p.1 p.2 p.2) (K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1))
    (hpos : ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : F, v ≠ 0 → 0 < q x v v) :
    ∃ c C : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ 0 < C ∧ ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : F,
      c * ‖v‖ ≤ Real.sqrt (q x v v) ∧ Real.sqrt (q x v v) ≤ C * ‖v‖ := by
  have hS : IsCompact (K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1) := hK.prod (isCompact_sphere 0 1)
  -- On the sphere, vectors are nonzero.
  have hne : ∀ p : B × F, p ∈ K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1 → p.2 ≠ 0 := by
    rintro ⟨x, v⟩ ⟨-, hv⟩
    simp only [mem_sphere_iff_norm, sub_zero] at hv
    exact norm_ne_zero_iff.mp (by rw [hv]; exact one_ne_zero)
  -- Lower bound: `q` is strictly positive on the compact `K × S`, hence bounded below by
  -- some `c₀ > 0`.  `exists_forall_le'` also covers the degenerate case `K × S = ∅`.
  obtain ⟨c₀, hc₀pos, hc₀⟩ :=
    hS.exists_forall_le' (a := 0) hcont fun p hp => hpos p.1 hp.1 p.2 (hne p hp)
  -- Upper bound: a continuous function on a compact set is bounded.
  obtain ⟨C₀, hC₀⟩ := hS.exists_bound_of_continuousOn hcont
  refine ⟨Real.sqrt c₀, Real.sqrt (max C₀ 1), Real.sqrt_pos.mpr hc₀pos,
    Real.sqrt_pos.mpr (lt_of_lt_of_le zero_lt_one (le_max_right _ _)), fun x hx v => ?_⟩
  rcases eq_or_ne v 0 with rfl | hv
  · simp
  -- Rescale `v` to the unit sphere and use homogeneity.
  set u : F := ‖v‖⁻¹ • v with hu
  have hvne : ‖v‖ ≠ 0 := norm_ne_zero_iff.mpr hv
  have huS : u ∈ sphere (0 : F) 1 := by
    simp only [mem_sphere_iff_norm, sub_zero, hu, norm_smul, norm_inv, norm_norm]
    field_simp
  have hvu : v = ‖v‖ • u := by
    rw [hu, smul_smul, mul_inv_cancel₀ hvne, one_smul]
  have hqv : q x v v = ‖v‖ ^ 2 * q x u u := by
    conv_lhs => rw [hvu]
    exact apply_smul_smul_self (q x) _ _
  have hmem : ((x, u) : B × F) ∈ K ×ˢ sphere (0 : F) 1 := ⟨hx, huS⟩
  have hsplit : Real.sqrt (q x v v) = ‖v‖ * Real.sqrt (q x u u) := by
    rw [hqv, Real.sqrt_mul (sq_nonneg _), Real.sqrt_sq (norm_nonneg _)]
  constructor
  · rw [hsplit, mul_comm (Real.sqrt c₀) ‖v‖]
    exact mul_le_mul_of_nonneg_left (Real.sqrt_le_sqrt (hc₀ _ hmem)) (norm_nonneg _)
  · rw [hsplit, mul_comm (Real.sqrt (max C₀ 1)) ‖v‖]
    refine mul_le_mul_of_nonneg_left (Real.sqrt_le_sqrt ?_) (norm_nonneg _)
    exact le_trans (le_trans (le_abs_self _) (hC₀ _ hmem)) (le_max_left _ _)

end Core

section Bridge

namespace RiemannianMetric

section AnyManifold

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]

entries.label: lem:riemannian-euclidean-norm-comparison-flat
entries.title: Norm Comparison on $\mathbb{R}^n$
entries.body:
Let $g$ be a Riemannian metric on $\mathbb{R}^n$ and let $\tilde{g}$ denote the Euclidean
metric. For every compact subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$, there are positive constants
$c, C$ such that for all $x \in K$ and all $v \in T_x \mathbb{R}^n$,
\[
c|v|_{\tilde{g}} \leq |v|_g \leq C|v|_{\tilde{g}}.
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean
lean.code:
theorem RiemannianMetric.exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean
    (g : RiemannianMetric 𝓘(ℝ, F) F) {K : Set F} (hK : IsCompact K) :
    ∃ c C : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ 0 < C ∧ ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) x,
      c * (euclideanMetric F).normAt x v ≤ g.normAt x v ∧
        g.normAt x v ≤ C * (euclideanMetric F).normAt x v := by
  obtain ⟨c, C, hc, hC, h⟩ := g.exists_forall_norm_comparison_of_isCompact hK
  refine ⟨c, C, hc, hC, fun x hx v => ?_⟩
  rw [euclideanMetric_normAt]
  exact h x hx v

end LeeStatement

section OpenSubsets

/-!
### Lee's open subset `W ⊆ ℝⁿ`

Lee states Lemma 2.53 for a metric on an open `W ⊆ ℝⁿ` (or `ℝⁿ₊`) rather than on
all of `ℝⁿ`, because he applies it to a metric read in a chart.  Part of that
generality is already available: `exists_forall_sqrt_comparison` imposes its
hypotheses only on `K`, so it accepts a form family defined on any domain
containing `K`, and that is the shape in which Lemma 2.54 uses it.

This section supplies the rest: the statement for a `RiemannianMetric 𝓘(ℝ, F) ↥W`
with `W : Opens F` — a metric on an open subset regarded as a manifold in its own
right, which is literally Lee's hypothesis.  The obstruction is one of transport.
`contMDiff_innerAt_totalSpace` already applies to `↥W`, so the metric is jointly
smooth on `T(↥W)`; what is missing is the identification of `T(↥W)` with the
product `↥W × F`, which turns "jointly smooth on the total space" into the
two-variable continuity the comparison consumes.  For the model space that
identification is `tangentBundleModelSpaceHomeomorph`; for an open subset mathlib
provides no analogue.

**Why it is cheap.**  Every chart of `↥W` is *the same* chart — the inclusion.
`Opens.chartAt_eq` gives `chartAt F x = (chartAt F ↑x).subtypeRestr ⟨x⟩`, whose
only dependence on `x` is through the `Nonempty ↥W` instance `⟨x⟩`; `Nonempty` is
a `Prop`, so definitional proof irrelevance makes `chartAt F x` and `achart F x`
independent of `x` *by `rfl`*.  Hence the tangent bundle's coordinate changes are
all `coordChange i i`, i.e. the identity (`tangentCoordChange_self`), and the
canonical trivialization at any point is the identity map on the nose
(`opens_trivializationAt_apply`).  It has `baseSet = univ`, so it is a single
global chart and no gluing is required. -/

variable {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F]

open TopologicalSpace

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean_opens
lean.code:
theorem exists_forall_normAt_le_euclidean_opens (W : Opens F)
    (g : RiemannianMetric 𝓘(ℝ, F) (W : Type _)) {K : Set W} (hK : IsCompact K) :
    ∃ c C : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ 0 < C ∧ ∀ x ∈ K, ∀ v : TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) x,
      c * (openSubmanifoldMetric (euclideanMetric F) W).normAt x v ≤ g.normAt x v ∧
        g.normAt x v ≤ C * (openSubmanifoldMetric (euclideanMetric F) W).normAt x v := by
  obtain ⟨c, C, hc, hC, h⟩ := g.exists_forall_norm_comparison_of_isCompact_opens W hK
  refine ⟨c, C, hc, hC, fun x hx v => ?_⟩
  rw [openSubmanifoldMetric_euclidean_normAt]
  exact h x hx v

deps.title: Uniform Comparison over a Compact Set
deps.label: lem:norm-comparison-compact-core

entries.label: lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood
entries.title: lem:distance-comparison-neighborhood
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold with or without boundary and
let $d_g$ be its Riemannian distance function. Suppose $U$ is an open subset of $M$ and
$p \in U$. Then $p$ has a coordinate neighborhood $V \subset U$ with the property that there
are positive constants $C, D$ satisfying the following inequalities:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] If $q \in V$, then $d_g(p,q) \leq Cd_{\bar{g}}(p,q)$, where $\bar{g}$ is the Euclidean metric in the
given coordinates on $\mathbb{R}^n$ or $\mathbb{R}^n_+$.
\item[(b)] If $q \in M \setminus V$, then $d_g(p,q) \geq D$.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{proof}

Let $W$ be any neighborhood of $p$ contained in $U$ on which there exist smooth
coordinates $(x^i)$. Using these coordinates, we can identify $W$ with an open subset
of $\mathbb{R}^n$ or $\mathbb{R}^n_+$. Let $K$ be a compact subset of $W$ containing a neighborhood $V$ of
$p$, chosen as follows: If $p$ is an interior point of $M$, let $K$ be the closed Euclidean
ball $\bar{B}_\epsilon(0)$, with $\epsilon > 0$ chosen small enough that $K \subseteq W$, and let $V =
B_\epsilon(0)$. If $p \in \partial M$, let $K$ be a closed half-ball $\bar{B}_\epsilon(0) \cap \mathbb{R}^n_+$ with $\epsilon$ chosen similarly, and let $V =
B_\epsilon(0) \cap \mathbb{R}^n_+$. Let $\bar{g} = \sum_i (dx^i)^2$ denote the Euclidean metric in these coordinates,
and let $c, C$ be constants satisfying (2.21). If $\gamma$ is any admissible curve whose image
lies entirely in $V$, it follows that

$$cL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma) \leq L_g(\gamma) \leq CL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma). \quad (2.22)$$

To prove (a), suppose $q \in V$. Letting $\gamma : [a,b] \to V$ be a parametrization of the
line segment from $p$ to $q$, we conclude from (2.22) that

$$d_g(p,q) \leq L_g(\gamma) \leq CL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma) = Cd_{\bar{g}}(p,q).$$

To prove (b), suppose $q \in M \setminus V$. If $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ is any admissible curve from
$p$ to $q$, let $t_0$ denote the infimum of times $t \in [a,b]$ such that $\gamma(t) \notin V$. It follows
that $\gamma([a, t_0]) \subseteq K$ and $d_{\bar{g}}(p, \gamma(t_0)) = \epsilon$ by continuity (Fig. 2.3), so (2.22) implies

$$L_g(\gamma) \geq L_g(\gamma|_{[a,t_0]}) \geq cL_{\bar{g}}(\gamma|_{[a,t_0]}) \geq cd_{\bar{g}}(p, \gamma(t_0)) = c\epsilon.$$

Taking the infimum over all such curves $\gamma$, we obtain (b) with $D = c\varepsilon$. \qed
\end{proof}

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.exists_coordinate_nbhd_riemannianEDist_comparison
lean.code:
theorem RiemannianMetric.exists_coordinate_nbhd_riemannianEDist_comparison
    [IsManifold I ∞ M] [RegularSpace M] (g : RiemannianMetric I M)
    {U : Set M} (hU : IsOpen U) {p : M} (hp : p ∈ U) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
    ∃ V : Set M, IsOpen V ∧ p ∈ V ∧ V ⊆ U ∧ V ⊆ (extChartAt I p).source ∧
      ∃ C D : ℝ≥0, 0 < C ∧ 0 < D ∧
        (∀ q ∈ V, riemannianEDist I p q ≤ C * edist (extChartAt I p p) (extChartAt I p q)) ∧
        (∀ q ∉ V, (D : ℝ≥0∞) ≤ riemannianEDist I p q) := by
  letI : RiemannianBundle (TangentSpace I : M → Type _) := ⟨g.toRiemannianMetric⟩
  haveI := g.isContinuousRiemannianBundle
  exact LeeLib.Ch02.exists_coordinate_nbhd_riemannianEDist_comparison I hU hp

entries.label: thm:riemannian-manifolds-metric-spaces
entries.title: Riemannian Manifolds as Metric Spaces
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold with or without boundary. Then $M$ carries a
metric space structure.

That its distance function is $d_g$ is Lemma \ref{lem:riemannian-metric-space-dist}, and that
its topology is the manifold topology is Theorem \ref{thm:riemannian-distance-topology}; the
three together are Lee's Theorem 2.55.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric
lean.code:
@[reducible] def MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric
    [T3Space M] [PreconnectedSpace M] : MetricSpace M :=
  letI : PseudoMetricSpace M := PseudoMetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric I M
  _root_.MetricSpace.ofT0PseudoMetricSpace M

entries.label: lem:riemannian-metric-space-dist
entries.title: The Metric Is the Riemannian Distance
entries.body:
The distance function of the metric space of Theorem \ref{thm:riemannian-manifolds-metric-spaces}
is the Riemannian distance: for all $p, q \in M$,
\[
  \operatorname{dist}(p,q) \;=\; d_g(p,q),
\]
where the right-hand side, a priori an element of $[0,\infty]$ by
Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}, is read as a real number using
Proposition \ref{prop:riemannian-distance-finite}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_dist
lean.code:
theorem MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_dist [T3Space M] [PreconnectedSpace M] (x y : M) :
    letI : MetricSpace M := MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric I M
    dist x y = (riemannianEDist I x y).toReal := rfl

entries.label: thm:riemannian-distance-topology
entries.title: The Metric Topology Is the Manifold Topology
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold with or without boundary. The topology
induced by the metric $d_g$ of Theorem \ref{thm:riemannian-manifolds-metric-spaces} is the
given manifold topology.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_topology
lean.code:
theorem MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric_topology [T3Space M] [PreconnectedSpace M] :
    (MetricSpace.ofRiemannianMetric I M).toUniformSpace.toTopologicalSpace =
      ‹TopologicalSpace M› := rfl

entries.label: prop:euclidean-riemannian-distance
entries.title: The Euclidean Distance
entries.body:
On $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean metric $\bar g$ of Example \ref{ex:euclidean-metric},
the Riemannian distance is the Euclidean distance:
\[
  d_{\bar g}(x,y) \;=\; |x - y| \qquad \text{for all } x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n .
\]
This is the one case in which the answer is known in advance, so it is the natural check on
Definition \ref{def:riemannian-distance}. The real-valued form $d_{\bar g}(x,y) = |x-y|$ is
Lemma \ref{lem:euclidean-riemannian-distance-real}.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist
lean.code:
theorem euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist (x y : F) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (fun (z : F) ↦ TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) z) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
    riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F) x y = edist x y :=
  riemannianEDist_eq_edist_of_innerProductSpace F x y

entries.label: lem:euclidean-riemannian-distance-real
entries.title: The Euclidean Distance, real-valued form
entries.body:
With the notation of Proposition \ref{prop:euclidean-riemannian-distance}, $d_{\bar g}(x,y)$ is
finite and equals $|x-y|$ as a real number.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist_toReal
lean.code:
theorem euclideanMetric_riemannianEDist_toReal (x y : F) :
    letI : RiemannianBundle (fun (z : F) ↦ TangentSpace 𝓘(ℝ, F) z) :=
      ⟨(euclideanMetric F).toRiemannianMetric⟩
    (riemannianEDist 𝓘(ℝ, F) x y).toReal = ‖x - y‖ :=
  riemannianEDist_toReal_of_innerProductSpace F x y

entries.label: lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear
entries.title: lem:nondegenerate-symmetric-bilinear

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.nondegenerate_tfae
lean.code:
theorem nondegenerate_tfae [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] (b : Basis ι ℝ V) :
    List.TFAE
      [Function.Bijective B,
       ∀ v : V, v ≠ 0 → ∃ w : V, B v w ≠ 0,
       (LinearMap.BilinForm.toMatrix b B).det ≠ 0,
       B.Nondegenerate] := by
  have hdual : finrank ℝ V = finrank ℝ (Module.Dual ℝ V) := Subspace.dual_finrank_eq.symm
  tfae_have 1 → 4 := by
    intro h
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_ker_eq_bot, LinearMap.ker_eq_bot]
    exact h.1
  tfae_have 4 → 1 := by
    intro h
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_ker_eq_bot, LinearMap.ker_eq_bot] at h
    exact ⟨h, (LinearMap.injective_iff_surjective_of_finrank_eq_finrank hdual).mp h⟩
  tfae_have 4 → 2 := by
    intro h v hv
    by_contra hc
    push Not at hc
    exact hv (h.1 v hc)
  tfae_have 2 → 4 := by
    intro h
    refine LinearMap.BilinForm.Nondegenerate.ofSeparatingLeft fun x hx => ?_
    by_contra hxne
    obtain ⟨w, hw⟩ := h x hxne
    exact hw (hx w)
  tfae_have 4 ↔ 3 := LinearMap.BilinForm.nondegenerate_iff_det_ne_zero b
  tfae_finish

end Nondegenerate

section OrthogonalComplement

variable [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}

entries.label: lem:orthogonal-complement
entries.title: lem:orthogonal-complement
entries.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space, and $S \subseteq V$ is a linear subspace. Then $\dim S + \dim S^{\perp} = \dim V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank
lean.code:
theorem finrank_add_finrank_orthogonal_eq_finrank (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    (S : Submodule ℝ V) : finrank ℝ S + finrank ℝ (B.orthogonal S) = finrank ℝ V := by
  have hle : finrank ℝ S ≤ finrank ℝ V := S.finrank_le
  rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.finrank_orthogonal hnd S]
  omega

entries.label: lem:orthogonal-complement-involution
entries.title: lem:orthogonal-complement-involution
entries.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space, and $S \subseteq V$ is a linear subspace. Then $(S^{\perp})^{\perp} = S$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.orthogonal_orthogonal_eq_self
lean.code:
theorem orthogonal_orthogonal_eq_self (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    (S : Submodule ℝ V) : B.orthogonal (B.orthogonal S) = S :=
  LinearMap.BilinForm.orthogonal_orthogonal hnd hB.isRefl S

entries.label: lem:nondegenerate-subspace
entries.title: lem:nondegenerate-subspace
entries.body:
Suppose $(V,q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space, and $S \subseteq V$ is a linear subspace. The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $S$ is nondegenerate.
\item $S^\perp$ is nondegenerate.
\item $S \cap S^\perp = \{0\}$.
\item $V = S \oplus S^\perp$.
\end{enumerate}

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.restrict_nondegenerate_tfae
lean.code:
theorem restrict_nondegenerate_tfae (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    (S : Submodule ℝ V) :
    List.TFAE
      [(B.restrict S).Nondegenerate,
       (B.restrict (B.orthogonal S)).Nondegenerate,
       S ⊓ B.orthogonal S = ⊥,
       IsCompl S (B.orthogonal S)] := by
  tfae_have 1 ↔ 4 :=
    LinearMap.BilinForm.restrict_nondegenerate_iff_isCompl_orthogonal hB.isRefl
  tfae_have 4 ↔ 3 := by
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.isCompl_orthogonal_iff_disjoint hB.isRefl, disjoint_iff]
  tfae_have 2 ↔ 4 := by
    rw [LinearMap.BilinForm.restrict_nondegenerate_iff_isCompl_orthogonal hB.isRefl,
      orthogonal_orthogonal_eq_self hB hnd S]
    exact ⟨IsCompl.symm, IsCompl.symm⟩
  tfae_finish

end OrthogonalComplement

section Orthonormal

entries.label: lem:completion-nondegenerate-bases
entries.title: Completion of Nondegenerate Bases
entries.body: Suppose $(V,q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space, and $(v_1,\ldots,v_k)$ is a nondegenerate $k$-tuple in $V$ with $0 \leq k < n$. Then there exist vectors $v_{k+1},\ldots,v_n \in V$ such that $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ is a nondegenerate basis for $V$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_isNondegenerateTuple_basis
lean.code:
theorem exists_isNondegenerateTuple_basis (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate)
    {k : ℕ} (v : Fin k → V) (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) (hk : k ≤ finrank ℝ V) :
    ∃ w : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V, IsNondegenerateTuple B w ∧
      ∀ i : Fin k, w ⟨(i : ℕ), lt_of_lt_of_le i.isLt hk⟩ = v i :=
  exists_isNondegenerateTuple_extend hB hnd (finrank ℝ V - k) k (by omega) v hv

entries.label: prop:gram-schmidt-algorithm-scalar-products
entries.title: Gram--Schmidt Algorithm for Scalar Products
entries.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space. If $(v_i)$ is a nondegenerate basis for $V$, then there is an orthonormal basis $(b_i)$ with the property that $\operatorname{span}(b_1, \ldots, b_k) = \operatorname{span}(v_1, \ldots, v_k)$ for each $k = 1, \ldots, n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] (hB : B.IsSymm)
    {v : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V} (hv : IsNondegenerateTuple B v) :
    ∃ b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ V)) ℝ V, IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) ∧
      ∀ j ≤ finrank ℝ V, prefixSpan (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) j = prefixSpan v j := by
  obtain ⟨b, hbon, hbspan⟩ := exists_isOrthonormal_prefixSpan_eq hB hv
  have hspan : ⊤ ≤ Submodule.span ℝ (Set.range b) := by
    rw [← prefixSpan_last b, hbspan (finrank ℝ V) le_rfl,
      Submodule.eq_top_of_finrank_eq (hv (finrank ℝ V) le_rfl).1]
  refine ⟨Basis.mk hbon.linearIndependent hspan, ?_, ?_⟩
  · simpa [Basis.coe_mk] using hbon
  · simpa [Basis.coe_mk] using hbspan

section Adapted

variable [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}

entries.label: prop:symmetric-bilinear-orthogonal-basis
entries.title: prop:symmetric-bilinear-orthogonal-basis
entries.body: Let $q$ be a symmetric bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space $V$ over a field in which $2$ is invertible. Then $V$ has a basis $(b_i)$ that is $q$-orthogonal, that is, $q(b_i, b_j) = 0$ whenever $i \neq j$.

entries.label: prop:scalar-product-orthonormal-basis
entries.title: prop:scalar-product-orthonormal-basis
entries.body: Every scalar product space $(V,q)$ has an orthonormal basis.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_isOrthonormal
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_isOrthonormal [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] {B : BilinForm ℝ V}
    (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    ∃ b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ V)) ℝ V, IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) := by
  have : Invertible (2 : ℝ) := invertibleOfNonzero (by norm_num)
  obtain ⟨v, hv⟩ :=
    LinearMap.BilinForm.exists_orthogonal_basis (LinearMap.BilinForm.isSymm_iff.mp hB)
  have hv : B.iIsOrtho (v : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) := hv
  -- Nondegeneracy of `B` rules out `⟪v i, v i⟫ = 0` on an orthogonal basis.
  have hvne : ∀ i, B (v i) (v i) ≠ 0 := fun i =>
    hv.not_isOrtho_basis_self_of_nondegenerate hnd i
  set c : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → ℝ := fun i => (Real.sqrt |B (v i) (v i)|)⁻¹ with hc
  have hcne : ∀ i, c i ≠ 0 := by
    intro i
    have : (0 : ℝ) < |B (v i) (v i)| := abs_pos.mpr (hvne i)
    simp only [hc, ne_eq, inv_eq_zero]
    positivity
  refine ⟨v.unitsSMul fun i => Units.mk0 (c i) (hcne i), ?_, ?_⟩
  · intro i j hij
    simp only [Basis.unitsSMul_apply, Units.smul_def, Units.val_mk0, map_smul,
      LinearMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul, LinearMap.BilinForm.iIsOrtho_def.1 hv i j hij, mul_zero]
  · intro i
    simpa only [Basis.unitsSMul_apply, Units.smul_def, Units.val_mk0, hc] using
      apply_self_normalize (hvne i)

entries.label: cor:scalar-product-signature-form
entries.title: cor:scalar-product-signature-form
entries.body:
Suppose $(V, q)$ is an $n$-dimensional scalar product space. There is a basis $(\beta^i)$ for $V^*$ with respect to which $q$ has the expression
\[
q = (\beta^1)^2 + \cdots + (\beta^r)^2 - (\beta^{r+1})^2 - \cdots - (\beta^{r+s})^2,
\]
for some nonnegative integers $r, s$ with $r + s = n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.exists_basis_isOrthonormal_signature
lean.code:
theorem exists_basis_isOrthonormal_signature [FiniteDimensional ℝ V]
    (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    ∃ (r s : ℕ) (_hrs : r + s = finrank ℝ V) (b : Basis (Fin (finrank ℝ V)) ℝ V),
      IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin (finrank ℝ V) → V) ∧
      (∀ i : Fin (finrank ℝ V), (i : ℕ) < r → B (b i) (b i) = 1) ∧
      (∀ i : Fin (finrank ℝ V), r ≤ (i : ℕ) → B (b i) (b i) = -1) :=
  let ⟨_, hb⟩ := exists_basis_isOrthonormal hB hnd
  hb.exists_sorted

entries.label: prop:sylvesters-law-inertia
entries.title: Sylvester's Law of Inertia
entries.body: Suppose $(V, q)$ is a finite-dimensional scalar product space. If $q$ has the representation (2.23) in some basis, then the number $r$ is the maximum dimension among all subspaces on which the restriction of $q$ is positive definite, and thus $r$ and $s$ are independent of the choice of basis.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.IsOrthonormal.isGreatest_ncard
lean.code:
theorem IsOrthonormal.isGreatest_ncard [FiniteDimensional ℝ V]
    (h : IsOrthonormal B (b : Fin n → V)) :
    IsGreatest
      {r | ∃ S : Submodule ℝ V, finrank ℝ S = r ∧
        ((LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B).restrict S).PosDef}
      {i | B (b i) (b i) = 1}.ncard := by
  rw [← h.sigPos_eq_ncard]
  exact sigPos_isGreatest _

entries.label: lem:radical-scalar-product-trivial
entries.title: A Scalar Product Has Trivial Radical
entries.body: Let $(V,q)$ be a scalar product space. The radical of $q$, that is, the set of $v \in V$ with $q(v,v) = 0$ whose associated polar form $w \mapsto q(v,w) + q(w,v)$ also vanishes identically, is $\{0\}$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.radical_toQuadraticMap_eq_bot
lean.code:
theorem radical_toQuadraticMap_eq_bot (hB : B.IsSymm) (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B).radical = ⊥ := by
  rw [eq_bot_iff]
  intro x hx
  rw [Submodule.mem_bot]
  refine hnd.1 x fun y => ?_
  have hpol : QuadraticMap.polar (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) x y = 0 := by
    rw [← QuadraticMap.polarBilin_apply_apply, hx.2]
    simp
  rw [LinearMap.BilinMap.polar_toQuadraticMap, ← hB.eq x y] at hpol
  linarith

entries.label: cor:scalar-product-signature-sum
entries.title: $r + s = n$, without a basis
entries.body: Let $(V,q)$ be an $n$-dimensional scalar product space of signature $(r,s)$. Then $r + s = n$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.sigPos_add_sigNeg_eq_finrank
lean.code:
theorem sigPos_add_sigNeg_eq_finrank [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] (hB : B.IsSymm)
    (hnd : B.Nondegenerate) :
    sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B) + sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)
      = finrank ℝ V := by
  have h := sigPos_add_sigNeg_add_radical (Q := LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B)
  rw [radical_toQuadraticMap_eq_bot hB hnd, finrank_bot] at h
  omega

end Signature

section NondegenerateTuple

deps.title: A Scalar Product Has Trivial Radical

entries.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product
entries.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
entries.body: Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$ whose fibres are finite-dimensional. Then $(V_b, g_b)$ is a scalar product space for every $b \in B$.

lean.name: Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric.bilin_nondegenerate
lean.code:
theorem bilin_nondegenerate [∀ b, FiniteDimensional ℝ (E b)] (b : B) :
    (g.bilin b).Nondegenerate :=
  LinearMap.BilinForm.Nondegenerate.ofSeparatingLeft (g.bilin_separatingLeft b)

end ContMDiffPseudoMetric

end Defs

section Pairing

variable
  {EB : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup EB] [NormedSpace ℝ EB]
  {HB : Type*} [TopologicalSpace HB] {IB : ModelWithCorners ℝ EB HB} {n : ℕ∞ω}
  {B : Type*} [TopologicalSpace B] [ChartedSpace HB B]
  {F : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℝ F]
  {E : B → Type*} [TopologicalSpace (TotalSpace F E)]
  [∀ b, TopologicalSpace (E b)] [∀ b, AddCommGroup (E b)] [∀ b, Module ℝ (E b)]
  [FiberBundle F E] [VectorBundle ℝ F E]
  {EM : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup EM] [NormedSpace ℝ EM]
  {HM : Type*} [TopologicalSpace HM] {IM : ModelWithCorners ℝ EM HM}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace HM M]

namespace ContMDiffPseudoMetric

variable (g : ContMDiffPseudoMetric IB n F E) {b : M → B} {v w : ∀ x, E (b x)} {s : Set M} {x : M}

entries.label: lem:pseudo-metric-pairing-smooth
entries.title: Smoothness of the Pairing of Smooth Sections
entries.body:
Let $g$ be a smooth field of scalar products on a vector bundle $V \to B$, let $N$ be a smooth manifold, and let $s \subseteq N$. Let $f : N \to B$ be smooth on $s$, and let $v, w$ be sections of $V$ along $f$ — that is, assignments $x \mapsto v_x \in V_{f(x)}$ — that are smooth on $s$. Then
\[
x \longmapsto g_{f(x)}(v_x, w_x)
\]
is a smooth real-valued function on $s$. In particular, taking $s = N$, the pairing of two smooth sections along a smooth map is smooth.

lean.name: Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric.contMDiffOn_pairing
lean.code:
theorem contMDiffOn_pairing
    (hb : ContMDiffOn IM IB n b s)
    (hv : ContMDiffOn IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (v m)) s)
    (hw : ContMDiffOn IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (w m)) s) :
    ContMDiffOn IM 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n (fun m => g.form (b m) (v m) (w m)) s :=
  fun y hy => g.contMDiffWithinAt_pairing (hb y hy) (hv y hy) (hw y hy)

lean.name: Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric.contMDiff_pairing
lean.code:
theorem contMDiff_pairing
    (hb : ContMDiff IM IB n b)
    (hv : ContMDiff IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (v m)))
    (hw : ContMDiff IM (IB.prod 𝓘(ℝ, F)) n (fun m => TotalSpace.mk' F (b m) (w m))) :
    ContMDiff IM 𝓘(ℝ, ℝ) n (fun m => g.form (b m) (v m) (w m)) :=
  fun y => g.contMDiffAt_pairing (hb y) (hv y) (hw y)

end ContMDiffPseudoMetric

end Pairing

end Bundle

section Manifold

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  {M : Type*} [TopologicalSpace M] [ChartedSpace H M] [IsManifold I ∞ M]

entries.label: def:pseudo-riemannian-metric
entries.title: Pseudo-Riemannian Metric
entries.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold. A \emph{pseudo-Riemannian metric} on $M$ (called by some authors a \emph{semi-Riemannian metric}) is a smooth field of scalar products on the tangent bundle of $M$; that is, a smooth symmetric $2$-tensor field $g$ whose value $g_p$ at each $p \in M$ is nondegenerate on $T_pM$, so that $(T_pM, g_p)$ is a scalar product space at every point. A \emph{pseudo-Riemannian manifold} is a pair $(M,g)$ consisting of a smooth manifold together with a pseudo-Riemannian metric on it.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric
lean.code:
abbrev PseudoRiemannianMetric : Type _ :=
  Bundle.ContMDiffPseudoMetric I ∞ E (TangentSpace I : M → Type _)

namespace PseudoRiemannianMetric

variable (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M)

entries.label: prop:riemannian-is-pseudo-riemannian
entries.title: Every Riemannian Metric is Pseudo-Riemannian
entries.body: Every Riemannian metric on a smooth manifold $M$ is a pseudo-Riemannian metric on $M$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.RiemannianMetric.toPseudoRiemannianMetric
lean.code:
def RiemannianMetric.toPseudoRiemannianMetric (g : RiemannianMetric I M) :
    PseudoRiemannianMetric I M where
  form := g.inner
  symm := g.symm
  nondegenerate p v hv := ⟨v, ne_of_gt (g.pos p v hv)⟩
  contMDiff := g.contMDiff

entries.label: def:pseudo-metric-signature-at
entries.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Metric at a Point
entries.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold and let $p \in M$. By Lemma \ref{lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product} the pair $(T_pM, g_p)$ is a scalar product space, so by Corollary \ref{cor:scalar-product-signature-form} it has a signature $(r_p, s_p)$, which by Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia} does not depend on the basis used to compute it. We call $(r_p,s_p)$ the \emph{signature of $g$ at $p$}, and $s_p$ the \emph{index of $g$ at $p$}. Equivalently, by Proposition \ref{prop:sylvesters-law-inertia}, $r_p$ is the largest dimension of a subspace of $T_pM$ on which $g_p$ is positive definite, and $s_p$ the largest dimension of one on which $g_p$ is negative definite.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric.sigPosAt
lean.code:
noncomputable def sigPosAt (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M) : ℕ :=
  sigPos (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (g.bilin p))

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric.sigNegAt
lean.code:
noncomputable def sigNegAt (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M) : ℕ :=
  sigNeg (LinearMap.BilinMap.toQuadraticMap (g.bilin p))

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

entries.label: prop:pseudo-metric-signature-sum
entries.title: The Signature at a Point Sums to the Dimension
entries.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold, let $p \in M$, and let $(r_p,s_p)$ be the signature of $g$ at $p$. Then $r_p + s_p = \dim T_pM$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.PseudoRiemannianMetric.sigPosAt_add_sigNegAt
lean.code:
theorem sigPosAt_add_sigNegAt (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (p : M) :
    g.sigPosAt p + g.sigNegAt p = finrank ℝ (TangentSpace I p) :=
  sigPos_add_sigNeg_eq_finrank (g.bilin_isSymm p) (g.bilin_nondegenerate p)

deps.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Metric at a Point
deps.label: def:pseudo-metric-signature-at

deps.title: The Fibres are Scalar Product Spaces
deps.label: lem:pseudo-metric-fibre-scalar-product

entries.label: def:pseudo-metric-has-signature
entries.title: Constant Signature
entries.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold and let $r,s \geq 0$ be integers. We say that $g$ \emph{has signature $(r,s)$} if the signature of $g$ at $p$ is $(r,s)$ for every $p \in M$. A \emph{pseudo-Riemannian manifold of signature $(r,s)$} is a pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ whose metric has signature $(r,s)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.HasSignature
lean.code:
def HasSignature (g : PseudoRiemannianMetric I M) (r s : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∀ p : M, g.sigPosAt p = r ∧ g.sigNegAt p = s

deps.title: Signature of a Pseudo-Riemannian Metric at a Point
