entries.label: lem:transversal-representative-eq-gradient
entries.title: A transversal orthogonal representative is the gradient
entries.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, $p \in M$, and $v \in T_pM$ with $v \neq 0$. Suppose $\langle v, v\rangle_g = df_p(v)$ and $\langle v, w\rangle_g = 0$ for every $w \in \ker df_p$. Then $v = \operatorname{grad} f|_p$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.eq_grad_of_ne_zero_of_innerAt_self_of_orthogonal_ker
lean.code:
theorem eq_grad_of_ne_zero_of_innerAt_self_of_orthogonal_ker (p : M) (v : TangentSpace I p)
    (hne : v ≠ 0) (hself : g.innerAt p v v = extDerivFun (I := I) f p v)
    (horth : ∀ w : TangentSpace I p, extDerivFun (I := I) f p w = 0 → g.innerAt p v w = 0) :
    v = grad g f p := by
  -- `v ≠ 0` makes the pairing with itself strictly positive, so `v ∉ ker df_p`.
  have hpos : 0 < g.innerAt p v v :=
    lt_of_le_of_ne (g.innerAt_self_nonneg p v)
      (Ne.symm fun h => hne ((g.innerAt_self_eq_zero_iff p v).mp h))
  have hdv : extDerivFun (I := I) f p v ≠ 0 := hself ▸ hpos.ne'
  refine eq_grad_of_innerAt g f p v fun w => ?_
  set t : ℝ := extDerivFun (I := I) f p w / extDerivFun (I := I) f p v with ht
  -- `w - t • v` lies in `ker df_p` by the choice of `t`.
  have hu : extDerivFun (I := I) f p (w - t • v) = 0 := by
    rw [map_sub, map_smul, ht, smul_eq_mul, div_mul_cancel₀ _ hdv, sub_self]
  have h0 := horth _ hu
  -- Expand the pairing against the split vector.
  have hexp : g.innerAt p v (w - t • v) = g.innerAt p v w - t * g.innerAt p v v := by
    show (g.inner p v) (w - t • v) = (g.inner p v) w - t * (g.inner p v) v
    rw [map_sub, map_smul]
    rfl
  rw [hexp] at h0
  have hvw : g.innerAt p v w = t * g.innerAt p v v := by linarith
  rw [hvw, hself, ht]
  field_simp

entries.label: def:inner-k-covectors
entries.title: def:inner-k-covectors
entries.body:
Let $(e_m)_{m \in \iota'}$ be an orthonormal basis of $V$. For $\omega, \eta \in \Lambda^k(V^*)$ set
\[
\langle \omega, \eta \rangle_e = \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{s \colon \{1,\ldots,k\} \to \iota'} \omega\big(e_{s(1)}, \ldots, e_{s(k)}\big)\, \eta\big(e_{s(1)}, \ldots, e_{s(k)}\big),
\]
the sum running over all maps $s$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerForms
lean.code:
@[simp] theorem innerFormsₗ_apply (e : OrthonormalBasis ι' ℝ V) (w θ : V [⋀^ι]→L[ℝ] ℝ) :
    innerFormsₗ e w θ = innerForms e w θ := rfl

/-! ### Uniqueness and frame independence (Lee, Problem 2-16) -/

omit [DecidableEq ι'] in

entries.label: lem:inner-k-covectors-posdef
entries.title: lem:inner-k-covectors-posdef
entries.body: $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_e$ is a symmetric bilinear form on $\Lambda^k(V^*)$, and $\langle \omega, \omega \rangle_e > 0$ for every $\omega \ne 0$. Thus it is an inner product.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerForms_self_pos
lean.code:
theorem innerForms_self_pos (e : OrthonormalBasis ι' ℝ V) {w : V [⋀^ι]→L[ℝ] ℝ} (hw : w ≠ 0) :
    0 < innerForms e w w := by
  obtain ⟨v, hv⟩ : ∃ v : ι → ι', w (fun i => e (v i)) ≠ 0 := by
    by_contra h
    push Not at h
    exact hw (ext_of_orthonormalBasis e fun v => by simp [h v])
  refine div_pos ?_ (by exact_mod_cast Nat.factorial_pos _)
  exact Finset.sum_pos' (fun _ _ => mul_self_nonneg _)
    ⟨v, Finset.mem_univ v, mul_self_pos.2 hv⟩

entries.label: lem:inner-k-covectors-wedge
entries.title: lem:inner-k-covectors-wedge
entries.body:
For all covectors $\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k, \eta^1, \ldots, \eta^k \in V^*$,
\[
\langle \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k, \eta^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \eta^k \rangle_e = \det\big(\langle \omega^i, \eta^j \rangle\big).
\]

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerForms_wedgeCovectors
lean.code:
theorem innerForms_wedgeCovectors (e : OrthonormalBasis ι' ℝ V) (a b : ι → (V →L[ℝ] ℝ)) :
    innerForms e (wedgeCovectors a) (wedgeCovectors b)
      = (Matrix.of fun i j => innerDual (a i) (b j)).det := by
  have hmaster := AlternatingMap.sum_det_submatrix_smul_apply
    (wedgeCovectors b).toAlternatingMap (e : ι' → V) (Matrix.of fun (i : ι) (m : ι') => a i (e m))
  simp only [smul_eq_mul, ContinuousAlternatingMap.coe_toAlternatingMap] at hmaster
  have hsub : ∀ s : ι → ι',
      ((Matrix.of fun (i : ι) (m : ι') => a i (e m)).submatrix id s).det
        = wedgeCovectors a (fun i => e (s i)) := by
    intro s
    rw [wedgeCovectors_apply]
    rfl
  have hrhs :
      ((wedgeCovectors b) fun i => ∑ m, (Matrix.of fun (i : ι) (m : ι') => a i (e m)) i m • e m)
        = (Matrix.of fun i j => innerDual (a i) (b j)).det := by
    have hsharp : ∀ i : ι,
        (∑ m, (Matrix.of fun (i : ι) (m : ι') => a i (e m)) i m • e m) = sharpL (a i) :=
      fun i => (sharpL_eq_sum e (a i)).symm
    simp only [hsharp, wedgeCovectors_apply]
    rw [← Matrix.det_transpose]
    congr 1
    ext i j
    simp only [Matrix.transpose_apply, Matrix.of_apply]
    rw [apply_sharpL, innerDual_comm]
  rw [Finset.sum_congr rfl fun s _ => congrArg (· * _) (hsub s), hrhs] at hmaster
  rw [innerForms, hmaster, nsmul_eq_mul]
  have hfac : ((Fintype.card ι).factorial : ℝ) ≠ 0 :=
    Nat.cast_ne_zero.2 (Nat.factorial_ne_zero _)
  field_simp

omit [DecidableEq ι'] in

entries.label: lem:k-covector-wedge-expansion
entries.title: lem:k-covector-wedge-expansion
entries.body:
For every $\omega \in \Lambda^k(V^*)$,
\[
k!\,\omega = \sum_{u \colon \{1,\ldots,k\} \to \iota'} \omega\big(e_{u(1)}, \ldots, e_{u(k)}\big)\; e_{u(1)}^\flat \wedge \cdots \wedge e_{u(k)}^\flat,
\]
where $v^\flat = \langle v, \cdot \rangle$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.factorial_smul_eq_sum_wedgeCovectors
lean.code:
theorem factorial_smul_eq_sum_wedgeCovectors (e : OrthonormalBasis ι' ℝ V)
    (w : V [⋀^ι]→L[ℝ] ℝ) :
    ((Fintype.card ι).factorial : ℝ) • w
      = ∑ u : ι → ι', w (fun i => e (u i)) • wedgeCovectors (fun i => flatL (e (u i))) := by
  refine ext_of_orthonormalBasis e fun v => ?_
  have hmaster := AlternatingMap.sum_det_submatrix_smul_apply
    w.toAlternatingMap (e : ι' → V) (Matrix.of fun (i : ι) (m : ι') => ⟪e (v i), e m⟫_ℝ)
  simp only [smul_eq_mul, ContinuousAlternatingMap.coe_toAlternatingMap] at hmaster
  have hcollapse : ∀ i : ι,
      (∑ m, (Matrix.of fun (i : ι) (m : ι') => ⟪e (v i), e m⟫_ℝ) i m • e m) = e (v i) :=
    fun i => sum_inner_smul_orthonormalBasis e (e (v i))
  simp only [hcollapse] at hmaster
  rw [nsmul_eq_mul] at hmaster
  rw [ContinuousAlternatingMap.smul_apply, ContinuousAlternatingMap.sum_apply, smul_eq_mul,
    ← hmaster]
  refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun u _ => ?_
  rw [ContinuousAlternatingMap.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul, wedgeCovectors_apply, mul_comm]
  congr 1
  rw [← Matrix.det_transpose]
  congr 1
  ext i j
  simp only [Matrix.transpose_apply, Matrix.of_apply, Matrix.submatrix_apply, id_eq, flatL_apply]
  exact real_inner_comm _ _

/-! ### `innerForms` is an inner product

Each of these is immediate from the shape of the definition; that is the payoff of summing over
all index maps rather than over increasing multi-indices. -/

omit [FiniteDimensional ℝ V] [DecidableEq ι'] in

entries.label: lem:span-wedge-covectors
entries.title: lem:span-wedge-covectors
entries.body: The wedge products $\omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k$ of covectors span $\Lambda^k(V^*)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.span_range_wedgeCovectors
lean.code:
theorem span_range_wedgeCovectors (e : OrthonormalBasis ι' ℝ V) :
    Submodule.span ℝ (Set.range fun a : ι → (V →L[ℝ] ℝ) => wedgeCovectors a) = ⊤ := by
  rw [Submodule.eq_top_iff']
  intro w
  have hfac : ((Fintype.card ι).factorial : ℝ) ≠ 0 :=
    Nat.cast_ne_zero.2 (Nat.factorial_ne_zero _)
  have hw : w = ((Fintype.card ι).factorial : ℝ)⁻¹ •
      ∑ u : ι → ι', w (fun i => e (u i)) • wedgeCovectors (fun i => flatL (e (u i))) := by
    rw [← factorial_smul_eq_sum_wedgeCovectors e w, inv_smul_smul₀ hfac]
  rw [hw]
  refine Submodule.smul_mem _ _ (Submodule.sum_mem _ fun u _ => Submodule.smul_mem _ _ ?_)
  exact Submodule.subset_span ⟨_, rfl⟩

omit [DecidableEq ι'] in

entries.label: prop:inner-k-covectors-unique
entries.title: prop:inner-k-covectors-unique
entries.body:
$\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_e$ is the unique bilinear form $B$ on $\Lambda^k(V^*)$ satisfying
\[
B(\omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k, \eta^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \eta^k) = \det\big(\langle \omega^i, \eta^j\rangle\big)
\]
for all covectors $\omega^i, \eta^j$.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.eq_innerForms_of_wedgeCovectors
lean.code:
theorem eq_innerForms_of_wedgeCovectors (e : OrthonormalBasis ι' ℝ V)
    (B : (V [⋀^ι]→L[ℝ] ℝ) →ₗ[ℝ] (V [⋀^ι]→L[ℝ] ℝ) →ₗ[ℝ] ℝ)
    (hB : ∀ a b : ι → (V →L[ℝ] ℝ),
      B (wedgeCovectors a) (wedgeCovectors b) = (Matrix.of fun i j => innerDual (a i) (b j)).det) :
    B = innerFormsₗ e := by
  refine LinearMap.ext_on (span_range_wedgeCovectors (ι := ι) e) ?_
  rintro _ ⟨a, rfl⟩
  refine LinearMap.ext_on (span_range_wedgeCovectors (ι := ι) e) ?_
  rintro _ ⟨b, rfl⟩
  rw [hB a b, innerFormsₗ_apply, innerForms_wedgeCovectors]

omit [DecidableEq ι'] [DecidableEq ι''] in

entries.label: prop:inner-k-covectors-frame-independent
entries.title: prop:inner-k-covectors-frame-independent
entries.body: $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_e$ does not depend on the orthonormal basis $e$ of $V$ used to define it.

lean.name: LeeLib.Ch02.innerForms_eq_innerForms
lean.code:
theorem innerForms_eq_innerForms (e : OrthonormalBasis ι' ℝ V) (f : OrthonormalBasis ι'' ℝ V) :
    innerForms (ι := ι) e = innerForms (ι := ι) f := by
  have h : innerFormsₗ (ι := ι) e = innerFormsₗ (ι := ι) f :=
    eq_innerForms_of_wedgeCovectors f _ (fun a b => innerForms_wedgeCovectors e a b)
  funext w θ
  simpa using DFunLike.congr_fun (DFunLike.congr_fun h w) θ

entries.label: prop:frame-homogeneous-manifolds
entries.title: prop:frame-homogeneous-manifolds

entries.label: prop:frame-homogeneous-sphere
entries.title: prop:frame-homogeneous-sphere
entries.body: The group $O(n + 1)$ acts transitively on $O(S^n(R))$, and thus each round sphere is frame-homogeneous.

entries.label: prop:stereographic-conformal-diffeomorphism
entries.title: Stereographic projection is a conformal diffeomorphism between $S^n(R) \setminus \{N\}$ and $\mathbb{R}^n$
entries.body:
Proof. The inverse map $\sigma^{-1}$ is a smooth parametrization of $S^n(R) \setminus \{N\}$, so we can use it to compute the pullback metric. Using the usual technique of substitution to compute pullbacks, we obtain the following coordinate representation of $\bar{g}$ in stereographic coordinates:

\[(\sigma^{-1})^* \bar{g}_R = (\sigma^{-1})^* \bar{g} = \sum_j \left( d\left(\frac{2R^2 u^j}{|u|^2 + R^2}\right) \right)^2 + \left( d\left( R\frac{|u|^2 - R^2}{|u|^2 + R^2}\right) \right)^2.\]

If we expand each of these terms individually, we get

\[d\left(\frac{2R^2 u^j}{|u|^2 + R^2}\right) = \frac{2R^2 du^j}{|u|^2 + R^2} - \frac{4R^2 u^j \sum_i u^i du^i}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^2};\]

\[d\left( R\frac{|u|^2 - R^2}{|u|^2 + R^2}\right) = \frac{2R\sum_i u^i du^i}{|u|^2 + R^2} - \frac{2R(|u|^2 - R^2) \sum_i u^i du^i}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^2}\]

\[= \frac{4R^3 \sum_i u^i du^i}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^2}.\]

Therefore,

\[(\sigma^{-1})^* \bar{g} = \frac{4R^4 \sum_j (du^j)^2}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^2} - \frac{16R^4 \left(\sum_i u^i du^i\right)^2}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^3} + \frac{16R^4|u|^2 \left(\sum_i u^i du^i\right)^2}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^4}\]

\[+ \frac{16R^6 \left(\sum_i u^i du^i\right)^2}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^4}\]

\[= \frac{4R^4 \sum_j (du^j)^2}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^2}.\]

In other words,

\[(\sigma^{-1})^* \bar{g} = \frac{4R^4}{(|u|^2 + R^2)^2} \bar{g},\]

where $\bar{g}$ now represents the Euclidean metric on $\mathbb{R}^n$, and so $\sigma$ is a conformal diffeomorphism. \qed

entries.label: cor:sphere-round-metric-locally-conformally-flat
entries.title: Each sphere with a round metric is locally conformally flat
entries.body: Proof. Stereographic projection gives a conformal equivalence between a neighborhood of any point except the north pole and Euclidean space; applying a suitable rotation and then stereographic projection (or stereographic projection from the south pole), we get such an equivalence for a neighborhood of the north pole as well. \qed

deps.title: Stereographic projection is a conformal diffeomorphism between $S^n(R) \setminus \{N\}$ and $\mathbb{R}^n$
deps.label: prop:stereographic-conformal-diffeomorphism

entries.title: thm:hyperbolic-space-models

entries.title: Each hyperbolic space is locally conformally flat

entries.label: prop:lorentz-group-transitive
entries.title: prop:lorentz-group-transitive
entries.body:
The group $O^+(n,1)$ acts transitively on $O(\mathbb{H}^n(R))$, and therefore
$\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ is frame-homogeneous.

entries.label: lem:left-invariant-metrics-lie-group
entries.title: lem:left-invariant-metrics-lie-group
entries.body:
Let $G$ be a Lie group and let $\mathfrak{g}$ be its Lie algebra of left-invariant vector fields.

\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item A Riemannian metric $g$ on $G$ is left-invariant if and only if for all $X, Y \in \mathfrak{g}$, the function $g(X,Y)$ is constant on $G$.
\item The restriction map $g \mapsto g_e \in \Sigma^2 (T_e^* G)$ together with the natural identification $T_e G \cong \mathfrak{g}$ gives a bijection between left-invariant Riemannian metrics on $G$ and inner products on $\mathfrak{g}$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: prop:bi-invariant-metric-adjoint
entries.title: prop:bi-invariant-metric-adjoint

entries.label: lem:invariant-inner-product-compact
entries.title: lem:invariant-inner-product-compact
entries.body:
Suppose $V$ is a finite-dimensional real vector space and $H$ is a subgroup of $\operatorname{GL}(V)$. There exists an $H$-invariant inner product on $V$ if and only if $H$ has compact closure in $\operatorname{GL}(V)$.

\textbf{Proof.} Assume first that there exists an $H$-invariant inner product $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ on $V$. This implies that $H$ is contained in the subgroup $O(V) \subseteq \operatorname{GL}(V)$ consisting of linear isomorphisms of $V$ that are orthogonal with respect to this inner product. Choosing an orthonormal basis of $V$ yields a Lie group isomorphism between $O(V)$ and $O(n) \subseteq \operatorname{GL}(n, \mathbb{R})$ (where $n = \dim V$), so $O(V)$ is compact; and the closure of $H$ is a closed subset of this compact group, and thus is itself compact.

Conversely, suppose $H$ has compact closure in $\operatorname{GL}(V)$, and let $K$ denote the closure. A simple limiting argument shows that $K$ is itself a subgroup, and thus it is a Lie group by the closed subgroup theorem (Thm. C.8). Let $(\cdot,\cdot)_0$ be an arbitrary inner product on $V$, and let $\mu$ be a right-invariant volume form on $K$ (for example, the volume form of some right-invariant metric on $K$). For fixed $x, y \in V$, define a smooth function $f_{x,y}: K \to \mathbb{R}$ by $f_{x,y}(k) = (kx, ky)_0$. Then define a new inner product $(\cdot, \cdot)$ on $V$ by
\[
  (x,y) = \int_K f_{x,y} \mu.
\]

It follows directly from the definition that $(\cdot, \cdot)$ is symmetric and bilinear over $\mathbb{R}$. For each nonzero $x \in V$, we have $f_{x,x} > 0$ everywhere on $K$, so $(x,x) > 0$, showing that $(\cdot, \cdot)$ is indeed an inner product.

To see that it is invariant under $K$, let $k_0 \in K$ be arbitrary. Then for all $x, y \in V$ and $k \in K$, we have
\[
  f_{k_0 x, k_0 y}(k) = (k k_0 x, k k_0 y)_0 = f_{x,y} \circ R_{k_0}(k),
\]
where $R_{k_0}: K \to K$ is right translation by $k_0$. Because $\mu$ is right-invariant, it follows from diffeomorphism invariance of the integral that
\begin{align*}
  (k_0 x, k_0 y) &= \int_K f_{k_0 x, k_0 y} \mu \\
                 &= \int_K (f_{x,y} \circ R_{k_0}) \mu \\
                 &= \int_K R_{k_0}^* (f_{x,y} \mu) \\
                 &= \int_K f_{x,y} \mu = (x,y).
\end{align*}

Thus $(\cdot, \cdot)$ is $K$-invariant, and it is also $H$-invariant because $H \subseteq K$. \qed

entries.label: thm:existence-bi-invariant-metrics
entries.title: Existence of Bi-invariant Metrics
entries.body: Let $G$ be a Lie group and $\mathfrak{g}$ its Lie algebra. Then $G$ admits a bi-invariant metric if and only if $\operatorname{Ad}(G)$ has compact closure in $\operatorname{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$.

entries.label: cor:compact-lie-group-bi-invariant-metric
entries.title: cor:compact-lie-group-bi-invariant-metric
entries.body: Every compact Lie group admits a bi-invariant Riemannian metric.

entries.label: ex:invariant-metrics-lie-groups
entries.title: Invariant Metrics on Lie Groups
entries.body:
(a) Every left-invariant metric on an abelian Lie group is bi-invariant, because the adjoint representation is trivial. Thus the Euclidean metric on $\mathbb{R}^n$ and the flat metric on $\mathbb{T}^n$ of Example 2.21 are both bi-invariant.

(b) If a metric $g$ on a Lie group $G$ is left-invariant, then the induced metric on every Lie subgroup $H \subseteq G$ is easily seen to be left-invariant. Similarly, if $g$ is bi-invariant, then the induced metric on $H$ is bi-invariant.

(c) The Lie group $\operatorname{SL}(2, \mathbb{R})$ (the group of $2 \times 2$ real matrices of determinant 1) admits many left-invariant metrics (as does every positive-dimensional Lie group), but no bi-invariant ones. To see this, recall that the Lie algebra of $\operatorname{SL}(2, \mathbb{R})$ is isomorphic to the algebra $\mathfrak{sl}(2, \mathbb{R})$ of trace-free $2 \times 2$ matrices, and the adjoint representation is given by $\operatorname{Ad}(A) X = AXA^{-1}$ (see Example C.10). If we let $X_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \in \mathfrak{sl}(2, \mathbb{R})$ and $A_c = \begin{pmatrix} c & 0 \\ 0 & 1/c \end{pmatrix} \in \operatorname{SL}(2, \mathbb{R})$ for $c > 0$, then $\operatorname{Ad}(A_c) X_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & c^2 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, which is unbounded as $c \to \infty$. Thus the orbit of $X_0$ is not contained in any compact subset, which implies that there is no bi-invariant metric on $\operatorname{SL}(2, \mathbb{R})$. A similar argument shows that $\operatorname{SL}(n, \mathbb{R})$ admits no bi-invariant metric for any $n \geq 2$. In view of (b) above, this shows also that $\operatorname{GL}(n, \mathbb{R})$ admits no bi-invariant metric for $n \geq 2$. (Of course, $\operatorname{GL}(1, \mathbb{R})$ does admit bi-invariant metrics because it is abelian.)

(d) With $S^3$ regarded as a submanifold of $\mathbb{C}^2$, the map
\[
(w, z) \mapsto \begin{pmatrix} w & z \\ -\overline{z} & \overline{w} \end{pmatrix} \qquad (3.16)
\]
gives a diffeomorphism from $S^3$ to $\operatorname{SU}(2)$. Under the inverse of this map, the round metric on $S^3$ pulls back to a bi-invariant metric on $\operatorname{SU}(2)$, as Problem 3-10 shows.

(e) Let $\mathfrak{o}(n)$ denote the Lie algebra of $O(n)$, identified with the algebra of skew-symmetric $n \times n$ matrices, and define a bilinear form on $\mathfrak{o}(n)$ by
\[
\langle A, B \rangle = \operatorname{tr}(A^T B).
\]
This is an Ad-invariant inner product, and thus determines a bi-invariant Riemannian metric on $O(n)$ (see Problem 3-11).

(f) Let $\mathbb{U}^n$ be the upper half-space as defined in Theorem 3.7. We can regard $\mathbb{U}^n$ as a Lie group by identifying each point $(x, y) = (x^1, \ldots, x^{n-1}, y) \in \mathbb{U}^n$ with an invertible $n \times n$ matrix as follows:
where $I_{n-1}$ is the $(n-1) \times (n-1)$ identity matrix. Then the hyperbolic metric $\mathfrak{g}_h$ is left-invariant on $\mathbb{U}^n$ but not right-invariant (see Problem 3-12).

(g) For $n \geq 1$, the $(2n+1)$-dimensional \textbf{Heisenberg group} is the Lie subgroup $H_n \subseteq \mathrm{GL}(n+2,\mathbb{R})$ defined by
\[
H_n = \left\{\begin{pmatrix} 1 & x^T & z \\ 0 & I & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} : x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n, z \in \mathbb{R}\right\},
\]
where $x$ and $y$ are treated as column matrices. These are the simplest examples of \textbf{nilpotent Lie groups}, meaning that the series of subgroups $G \supseteq [G,G] \supseteq [G,[G,G]] \supseteq \cdots$ eventually reaches the trivial subgroup (where for any subgroups $G_1, G_2 \subseteq G$, the notation $[G_1, G_2]$ means the subgroup of $G$ generated by all elements of the form $x_1 x_2 x_1^{-1} x_2^{-1}$ for $x_i \in G_i$). There are many left-invariant metrics on $H_n$, but no bi-invariant ones, as Problem 3-13 shows.

(h) Our last example is a group that plays an important role in the classification of 3-manifolds. Let Sol denote the following 3-dimensional Lie subgroup of $\mathrm{GL}(3,\mathbb{R})$:
\[
\mathrm{Sol} = \left\{\begin{pmatrix} e^z & 0 & x \\ 0 & e^{-z} & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} : x, y, z \in \mathbb{R}\right\}.
\]

This group is the simplest nonnilpotent example of a \textbf{solvable Lie group}, meaning that the series of subgroups $G \supseteq [G,G] \supseteq [[G,G],[G,G]] \supseteq \cdots$ eventually reaches the trivial subgroup. Like the Heisenberg groups, Sol admits left-invariant metrics but not bi-invariant ones (Problem 3-14). $\parallel$

deps.title: The n-Torus as a Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: ex:n-torus-riemannian-submanifold

entries.label: thm:existence-invariant-metrics-homogeneous-spaces
entries.title: Existence of Invariant Metrics on Homogeneous Spaces
entries.body: Suppose $G$ is a Lie group and $M$ is a homogeneous $G$-space. Let $p_0$ be a point in $M$, and let $I_{p_0}: G_{p_0} \to \mathrm{GL}(T_{p_0}M)$ denote the isotropy representation at $p_0$. There exists a $G$-invariant Riemannian metric on $M$ if and only if $I_{p_0}(G_{p_0})$ has compact closure in $\mathrm{GL}(T_{p_0}M)$.

entries.label: cor:invariant-metric-compact-isotropy
entries.title: If a Lie group G acts smoothly and transitively on a smooth manifold M with compact isotropy groups, then there exists a G-invariant Riemannian metric on M

deps.title: Existence of Invariant Metrics on Homogeneous Spaces
deps.label: thm:existence-invariant-metrics-homogeneous-spaces

entries.label: prop:covering-locally-homogeneous
entries.title: Suppose $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is a homogeneous Riemannian manifold, $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold, and $\pi : \tilde{M} \to M$ is a Riemannian covering
entries.body: Then $(M, g)$ is locally homogeneous. If $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is frame-homogeneous, then $(M, g)$ is locally frame-homogeneous.

entries.label: thm:uniformization-compact-surfaces
entries.title: Uniformization of Compact Surfaces
entries.body: Every compact, connected, smooth 2-manifold admits a locally frame-homogeneous Riemannian metric, and a Riemannian covering by the Euclidean plane, hyperbolic plane, or round unit sphere.

entries.label: lem:homogeneous-point-reflection-symmetric
entries.title: lem:homogeneous-point-reflection-symmetric
entries.body: If $(M, g)$ is a connected homogeneous Riemannian manifold that possesses a point reflection at one point, then it is symmetric.

entries.label: ex:riemannian-symmetric-spaces
entries.title: Riemannian Symmetric Spaces
entries.body:
(a) Suppose $(M, g)$ is any connected frame-homogeneous Riemannian manifold. Then for each $p \in M$, we can choose an orthonormal basis $\{b_i\}$ of $T_p M$, and frame homogeneity guarantees that there is an isometry $\varphi: M \to M$ that fixes $p$ and sends $(b_i)$ to $(-b_i)$, which implies that $d\varphi_p = -\operatorname{Id}$. Thus every frame-homogeneous Riemannian manifold is a symmetric space. In particular, all Euclidean spaces, spheres, and hyperbolic spaces are symmetric.

(b) Suppose $G$ is a connected Lie group with a bi-invariant Riemannian metric $g$. If we define $\Phi: G \to G$ by $\Phi(x) = x^{-1}$, then it is straightforward to check that $d\Phi_e(v) = -v$ for every $v \in T_e G$, from which it follows that $d\Phi_*^*(g_e) = g_e$. To see that $\Phi$ is an isometry, let $p \in G$ be arbitrary. The identity $g^{-1} = (p^{-1} g)^{-1} p^{-1}$ for all $g \in G$ implies that $\Phi = R_{p^{-1}} \circ \Phi \circ L_{p^{-1}}$, and therefore it follows from bi-invariance of $g$ that
\begin{align*}
(\Phi^* g)_p = d(\Phi_p)^* g_p = d(L_{p^{-1}})_p^* \circ d(\Phi_e)^* \circ d(R_{p^{-1}})_e^* g_p = g_p.
\end{align*}

Therefore $\Phi$ is an isometry of $g$ and hence a point reflection at $e$. Lemma 3.23 then implies that $(G, g)$ is a symmetric space.

(c) The complex projective spaces introduced in Example 2.30 and the Grassmann manifolds introduced in Problem 2-7 are all Riemannian symmetric spaces (see Problems 3-19 and 3-20).

(d) Every product of Riemannian symmetric spaces is easily seen to be a symmetric space when endowed with the product metric. A symmetric space is said to be \emph{irreducible} if it is not isometric to a product of positive-dimensional symmetric spaces.

entries.label: thm:pseudo-riemannian-signature
entries.title: thm:pseudo-riemannian-signature
entries.body: For all $r$, $s$, and $R$ as above, $S^{r,s}(R)$ and $\mathbb{H}^{r,s}(R)$ are pseudo-Riemannian manifolds of signature $(r,s)$.

entries.label: thm:frame-homogeneous-pseudo-riemannian
entries.title: thm:frame-homogeneous-pseudo-riemannian
entries.body: All pseudo-Euclidean spaces, pseudospheres, and pseudohyperbolic spaces are frame-homogeneous.

entries.label: lem:connection-is-local-operator
entries.title: Locality
entries.body:
Suppose $
abla$ is a connection in a smooth vector bundle $E 	o M$. For every $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, $Y \in \Gamma(E)$, and $p \in M$, the covariant derivative $
abla_X Y |_p$ depends only on the values of $X$ and $Y$ in an arbitrarily small neighborhood of $p$. More precisely, if $X = 	ilde{X}$ and $Y = 	ilde{Y}$ on a neighborhood of $p$, then $
abla_X Y |_p = 
abla_{	ilde{X}} 	ilde{Y} |_p$.

entries.label: prop:connection-restriction
entries.title: Restriction of a Connection

entries.label: prop:covariant-deriv-dependence-at-point
entries.title: prop:covariant-deriv-dependence-at-point
entries.body: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.1, $\nabla_X Y|_p$ depends only on the values of $Y$ in a neighborhood of $p$ and the value of $X$ at $p$.

entries.label: prop:connection-coefficients-frame
entries.title: prop:connection-coefficients-frame

entries.label: prop:transformation-law-connection-coefficients
entries.title: Transformation Law for Connection Coefficients
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary, and let $\nabla$ be a connection in $TM$. Suppose we are given two smooth local frames $(E_i)$ and $(\tilde{E}_j)$ for $TM$ on an open subset $U \subseteq M$, related by $\tilde{E}_i = A^j_i E_j$ for some matrix of functions $(A^j_i)$. Let $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ and $\tilde{\Gamma}^k_{ij}$ denote the connection coefficients of $\nabla$ with respect to these two frames. Then
\[
\tilde{\Gamma}^k_{ij} = (A^{-1})^\ell_p A^p_i A^q_j \Gamma^p_{\ell q} + (A^{-1})^\ell_p A^q_\ell (A^p_j).
\]

entries.label: ex:euclidean-connection
entries.title: The Euclidean Connection
entries.body: On $T\mathbb{R}^n$, define the \emph{Euclidean connection} $\bar\nabla$ by formula (4.3). It is easy to check that this satisfies the required properties for a connection, and that its connection coefficients in the standard coordinate frame are all zero.

entries.label: ex:tangential-connection-submanifold
entries.title: The Tangential Connection on a Submanifold of $\mathbb{R}^n$
entries.body:
Let $M \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ be an embedded submanifold. Define a connection $
abla^T$ on $TM$, called the \emph{tangential connection}, by setting
\[

abla_X^T Y = \pi^T(	ilde{
abla}_{	ilde{X}} 	ilde{Y}|_M),
\]
where $\pi^T$ is the orthogonal projection onto $TM$ (Prop.\ 2.16), $	ilde{
abla}$ is the Euclidean connection on $\mathbb{R}^n$ (Example 4.8), and $	ilde{X}$ and $	ilde{Y}$ are smooth extensions of $X$ and $Y$ to an open set in $\mathbb{R}^n$. (Such extensions exist by the result of Exercise A.23.) Since the value of $	ilde{
abla}_{	ilde{X}} 	ilde{Y}$ at a point $p \in M$ depends only on $	ilde{X}_p = X_p$, this just boils down to defining $(
abla_X^T Y)_p$ to be equal to the tangential directional derivative $
abla_X^T Y$ that we defined in (4.4) above. Problem 4-1 shows that this value is independent of the choice of extension $	ilde{Y}$, so $
abla^T$ is well defined. Smoothness is easily verified by expressing $
abla_X^T Y$ in terms of an adapted orthonormal frame (see Prop.\ 2.14).

It is immediate from the definition that $
abla^T Y$ is linear over $C^\infty(M)$ in $X$ and over $\mathbb{R}$ in $Y$, so to show that $
abla^T$ is a connection, only the product rule needs to be checked. Let $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and let $	ilde{f}$ be an extension of $f$ to a neighborhood of $M$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Then $	ilde{f}	ilde{Y}$ is a smooth extension of $fY$ to a neighborhood of $M$, so
 egin{align*}

abla_X^T(fY) &= \pi^T(	ilde{
abla}_{	ilde{X}}(	ilde{f}	ilde{Y})|_M) \
&= \pi^T((	ilde{X}	ilde{f})	ilde{Y}|_M + 	ilde{f}(	ilde{
abla}_{	ilde{X}}	ilde{Y}|_M)) \
&= (Xf)Y + f
abla_X^T Y.
\end{align*}

Thus $
abla^T$ is a connection. \hfill $\parallel$

entries.label: lem:connections-from-connection-coefficients
entries.title: lem:connections-from-connection-coefficients
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a smooth $n$-manifold with or without boundary, and $M$ admits a global frame $(E_i)$. Formula (4.9) gives a one-to-one correspondence between connections in $TM$ and choices of $n^3$ smooth real-valued functions $\{\Gamma^k_{ij}\}$ on $M$.

entries.label: prop:tangent-bundle-admits-connection
entries.title: prop:tangent-bundle-admits-connection
entries.body: The tangent bundle of every smooth manifold with or without boundary admits a connection.

entries.label: prop:difference-tensor
entries.title: The Difference Tensor
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary. For any two connections $
abla^0$ and $
abla^1$ in $TM$, define a map $D: \mathfrak{X}(M) 	imes \mathfrak{X}(M) 	o \mathfrak{X}(M)$ by
\[
D(X,Y) = 
abla_X^1 Y - 
abla_X^0 Y.
\]

Then $D$ is bilinear over $C^\infty(M)$, and thus defines a $(1,2)$-tensor field called the difference tensor between $
abla^0$ and $
abla^1$.

entries.label: thm:connections-affine-space
entries.title: thm:connections-affine-space
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary, and let $\nabla^0$ be any connection in $TM$. Then the set $\mathcal{A}(TM)$ of all connections in $TM$ is equal to the following affine space:
\[
\mathcal{A}(TM) = \{\nabla^0 + D : D \in \Gamma(T^{(1,2)}TM)\},
\]
where $D \in \Gamma(T^{(1,2)}TM)$ is interpreted as a map from $\mathfrak{X}(M) \times \mathfrak{X}(M)$ to $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ as in Proposition B.1, and $\nabla^0 + D : \mathfrak{X}(M) \times \mathfrak{X}(M) \to \mathfrak{X}(M)$ is defined by
\[
(\nabla^0 + D)_X Y = \nabla^0_X Y + D(X,Y).
\]

entries.label: prop:connection-on-tensor-bundles
entries.title: prop:connection-on-tensor-bundles
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary, and let $
abla$ be a connection in $TM$. Then $
abla$ uniquely determines a connection in each tensor bundle $T^{(k,l)}TM$, also denoted by $
abla$, such that the following four conditions are satisfied.
 egin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] In $T^{(1,0)}TM = TM$, $
abla$ agrees with the given connection.
\item[(ii)] In $T^{(0,0)}TM = M 	imes \mathbb{R}$, $
abla$ is given by ordinary differentiation of functions:
\[
abla_X f = Xf.\]
\item[(iii)] $
abla$ obeys the following product rule with respect to tensor products:
\[
abla_X (F \otimes G) = (
abla_X F) \otimes G + F \otimes (
abla_X G).\]
\item[(iv)] $
abla$ commutes with all contractions: if ``tr'' denotes a trace on any pair of indices, one covariant and one contravariant, then
\[
abla_X (\mathrm{tr} F) = \mathrm{tr}(
abla_X F).\]
\end{enumerate}

This connection also satisfies the following additional properties:
 egin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] $
abla$ obeys the following product rule with respect to the natural pairing between a covector field $\omega$ and a vector field $Y$:
\[
abla_X \langle \omega, Y angle = \langle 
abla_X \omega, Y angle + \langle \omega, 
abla_X Y angle.\]
\item[(b)] For all $F \in \Gamma(T^{(k,l)}TM)$, smooth 1-forms $\omega^1, \ldots, \omega^k$, and smooth vector fields $Y_1, \ldots, Y_l$,
 egin{align}
  (
abla_X F)(\omega^1,\ldots,\omega^k, Y_1,\ldots,Y_l) &= X ig(F(\omega^1,\ldots,\omega^k, Y_1,\ldots,Y_l) ig) 
onumber\
  &\quad - \sum_{i=1}^{k} F(\omega^1,\ldots,
abla_X\omega^i,\ldots,\omega^k, Y_1,\ldots,Y_l) 
onumber\
  &\quad - \sum_{j=1}^{l} F(\omega^1,\ldots,\omega^k, Y_1,\ldots,
abla_X Y_j,\ldots,Y_l). 	ag{4.12}
\end{align}
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: prop:covariant-derivative-components
entries.title: prop:covariant-derivative-components
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary, and let $\nabla$ be a connection in $TM$. Suppose $(E_i)$ is a local frame for $M$, $(\epsilon^j)$ is its dual coframe, and $\{\Gamma^k_{ij}\}$ are the connection coefficients of $\nabla$ with respect to this frame. Let $X$ be a smooth vector field, and let $X^i E_i$ be its local expression in terms of this frame.

\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] The covariant derivative of a 1-form $\omega = \omega_i \epsilon^i$ is given locally by
\[
\nabla_X(\omega) = (X(\omega_k) - X^j \omega_i \Gamma^i_{jk}) \epsilon^k.
\]

\item[(b)] If $F \in \Gamma(T^{(k,\ell)}TM)$ is a smooth mixed tensor field of any rank, expressed locally as
\[
F = F^{i_1 \cdots i_k}_{j_1 \cdots j_\ell} E_{i_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes E_{i_k} \otimes \epsilon^{j_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes \epsilon^{j_\ell},
\]
then the covariant derivative of $F$ is given locally by
\[
\nabla_X F = \left(X(F^{i_1 \cdots i_k}_{j_1 \cdots j_\ell}) + \sum_{s=1}^k X^m F^{i_1 \cdots p \cdots i_k}_{j_1 \cdots j_\ell} \Gamma^{i_s}_{mp} - \sum_{s=1}^{\ell} X^m F^{i_1 \cdots i_k}_{j_1 \cdots p \cdots j_\ell} \Gamma^p_{ms}\right) \times E_{i_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes E_{i_k} \otimes \epsilon^{j_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes \epsilon^{j_\ell}.
\]
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: prop:total-covariant-derivative
entries.title: The Total Covariant Derivative

entries.label: prop:total-covariant-derivative-components
entries.title: prop:total-covariant-derivative-components
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary and let $\nabla$ be a connection in $TM$; and let $(E_i)$ be a smooth local frame for $TM$ and $\{\Gamma^k_{ij}\}$ the corresponding connection coefficients. The components of the total covariant derivative of a $(k,l)$-tensor field $F$ with respect to this frame are given by
\[
F^{i_1\cdots i_k}_{j_1\cdots j_l;m} = E_m\left(F^{i_1\cdots i_k}_{j_1\cdots j_l}\right) + \sum_{s=1}^k F^{i_1\cdots p \cdots i_k}_{j_1\cdots j_l} \Gamma^{i_s}_{mp} - \sum_{s=1}^l F^{i_1\cdots i_k}_{j_1\cdots p \cdots j_l} \Gamma^p_{mj_s}.
\]

entries.label: prop:second-covariant-derivative-formula
entries.title: prop:second-covariant-derivative-formula
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary and let $
abla$ be a connection in $TM$. For every smooth vector field or tensor field $F$,
\[

abla^2_{X,Y} F = 
abla_X (
abla_Y F) - 
abla_{[X,Y]} F.
\]

entries.label: ex:covariant-hessian
entries.title: The Covariant Hessian
entries.body:
Let $u$ be a smooth function on $M$. Then $
abla u \in \Gamma(T^{(0,1)}TM) = \Omega^1(M)$ is just the 1-form $du$, because both tensors have the same action on vectors: $
abla u(X) = 
abla_X u = Xu = du(X)$. The 2-tensor $
abla^2 u = 
abla(du)$ is called the \emph{covariant Hessian} of $u$. Proposition 4.21 shows that its action on smooth vector fields $X, Y$ can be computed by the following formula:
\[

abla^2 u(Y,X) = 
abla^2_{X,Y}u = 
abla_X(
abla_Y u) - 
abla_{(
abla_X Y)}u = Y(Xu) - (
abla_Y X)u.
\]

In any local coordinates, it is
\[

abla^2 u = u_{ij}\, dx^i \otimes dx^j, \quad 	ext{with } u_{ij} = \partial_j \partial_i u - \Gamma^k_{ji} \partial_k u. \quad \parallel
\]

entries.label: ex:figure-eight-curve
entries.title: ex:figure-eight-curve
entries.body:
Consider the \emph{figure eight curve} $\gamma: (-\pi, \pi) \to \mathbb{R}^2$ defined by
\[
\gamma(t) = (\sin 2t, \sin t).
\]





Its image is a set that looks like a figure eight in the plane (Fig. 4.6). Problem 4-7 asks you to show that $\gamma$ is an injective smooth immersion, but its velocity vector field is not extendible. \hfill $\parallel$

entries.label: thm:covariant-derivative-along-curve
entries.title: Covariant Derivative Along a Curve
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary and let $\nabla$ be a connection in $TM$. For each smooth curve $\gamma : I \to M$, the connection determines a unique operator
\[
D_t : \mathfrak{X}(\gamma) \to \mathfrak{X}(\gamma),
\]
called the \textbf{covariant derivative along $\gamma$}, satisfying the following properties:

(i) \textbf{Linearity over $\mathbb{R}$}:
\[
D_t(aV + bW) = aD_tV + bD_tW \quad \text{for } a, b \in \mathbb{R}.
\]

(ii) \textbf{Product rule}:
\[
D_t(fV) = f'V + fD_tV \quad \text{for } f \in C^{\infty}(I).
\]

(iii) If $V \in \mathfrak{X}(\gamma)$ is extendible, then for every extension $\tilde{V}$ of $V$,
\[
D_tV(t) = \nabla_{\gamma'(t)}\tilde{V}.
\]

There is an analogous operator on the space of smooth tensor fields of any type along $\gamma$.

entries.label: prop:covariant-derivative-depends-on-curve
entries.title: prop:covariant-derivative-depends-on-curve
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with or without boundary, let $
abla$ be a connection in $TM$, and let $p \in M$ and $v \in T_p M$. Suppose $Y$ and $ ar{Y}$ are two smooth vector fields that agree at points in the image of some smooth curve $\gamma: I 	o M$ such that $\gamma(t_0) = p$ and $\gamma'(t_0) = v$. Then $
abla_v Y = 
abla_v  ar{Y}$.

entries.label: thm:geodesic-existence-uniqueness
entries.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Geodesics
entries.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $\nabla$ a connection in $TM$. For every $p \in M$, $w \in T_p M$, and $t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$, there exist an open interval $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ containing $t_0$ and a geodesic $\gamma: I \to M$ satisfying $\gamma(t_0) = p$ and $\gamma'(t_0) = w$. Any two such geodesics agree on their common domain.

entries.label: cor:existence-of-maximal-geodesic
entries.title: Existence of maximal geodesics
entries.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and let $\nabla$ be a connection in $TM$. For each $p \in M$ and $v \in T_p M$, there is a unique maximal geodesic $\gamma : I \to M$ with $\gamma(0) = p$ and $\gamma'(0) = v$, defined on some open interval $I$ containing $0$.

entries.label: thm:linear-ode-existence-uniqueness
entries.title: Existence, Uniqueness, and Smoothness for Linear ODEs

entries.label: thm:existence-uniqueness-parallel-transport
entries.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Parallel Transport
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, and $\nabla$ is a connection in $TM$. Given a smooth curve $\gamma: I \to M$, $t_0 \in I$, and a vector $v \in T_{\gamma(t_0)} M$ or tensor $v \in T^{(k,l)}_{\gamma(t_0)} M$, there exists a unique parallel vector or tensor field $V$ along $\gamma$ such that $V(t_0) = v$.

deps.title: Existence, Uniqueness, and Smoothness for Linear ODEs
deps.label: thm:linear-ode-existence-uniqueness

entries.label: cor:parallel-transport-piecewise-smooth-curves
entries.title: Parallel Transport Along Piecewise Smooth Curves
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, and $\nabla$ is a connection in $TM$. Given an admissible curve $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ and a vector $v \in T_{\gamma(a)}M$ or tensor $v \in T^{(k,l)}(T_{\gamma(a)} M)$, there exists a unique piecewise smooth parallel vector or tensor field $V$ along $\gamma$ such that $V(a) = v$, and $V$ is smooth wherever $\gamma$ is.

entries.label: thm:parallel-transport-determines-covariant-diff
entries.title: Parallel Transport Determines Covariant Differentiation

deps.title: Parallel Transport Along Piecewise Smooth Curves
deps.label: cor:parallel-transport-piecewise-smooth-curves

entries.label: cor:parallel-transport-determines-connection
entries.title: Parallel Transport Determines the Connection

entries.label: prop:parallel-field-characterization
entries.title: prop:parallel-field-characterization
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, $\nabla$ is a connection in $TM$, and $A$ is a smooth vector or tensor field on $M$. Then $A$ is parallel on $M$ if and only if $\nabla A \equiv 0$.

entries.label: lem:pullback-connection
entries.title: Pullback Connections

entries.label: prop:pullback-connection-properties
entries.title: Properties of Pullback Connections
entries.body:
Suppose $M$ and $\tilde{M}$ are smooth manifolds with or without boundary, and $\varphi: M \to \tilde{M}$ is a diffeomorphism. Let $\tilde{\nabla}$ be a connection in $T\tilde{M}$ and let $\nabla = \varphi^*\tilde{\nabla}$ be the pullback connection in $TM$. Suppose $\gamma: I \to M$ is a smooth curve.

(a) $\varphi$ takes covariant derivatives along curves to covariant derivatives along curves: if $V$ is a smooth vector field along $\gamma$, then
\[d\varphi \circ D_t V = \tilde{D}_t(d\varphi \circ V),\]
where $D_t$ is covariant differentiation along $\gamma$ with respect to $\nabla$, and $\tilde{D}_t$ is covariant differentiation along $\varphi \circ \gamma$ with respect to $\tilde{\nabla}$.

(b) $\varphi$ takes geodesics to geodesics: if $\gamma$ is a $\nabla$-geodesic in $M$, then $\varphi \circ \gamma$ is a $\tilde{\nabla}$-geodesic in $\tilde{M}$.

(c) $\varphi$ takes parallel transport to parallel transport: for every $t_0, t_1 \in I$,
\[d\varphi_{\gamma(t_1)} \circ P^\gamma_{t_0t_1} = P^{\varphi \circ \gamma}_{t_0t_1} \circ d\varphi_{\gamma(t_0)}.\]

entries.label: prop:tangential-covariant-derivative-projection
entries.title: prop:tangential-covariant-derivative-projection
entries.body:
Let $M \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ be an embedded submanifold, $\gamma: I \to M$ a smooth curve in $M$, and $V$ a smooth vector field along $\gamma$ that takes its values in $TM$. Then for each $t \in I$,
\[
D_t^\top V(t) = \pi^\top(\overline{D}_t V(t)).
\]

\begin{proof}
Let $t_0 \in I$ be arbitrary. By Proposition~2.14, on some neighborhood $U$ of $\gamma(t_0)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ there is an adapted orthonormal frame for $TM$, that is, a local orthonormal frame $(E_1,\ldots,E_n)$ for $T\mathbb{R}^n$ such that $(E_1,\ldots,E_k)$ restricts to an orthonormal frame for $TM$ at points of $M \cap U$ (where $k = \dim M$). If $\varepsilon > 0$ is small enough that $\gamma((t_0 - \varepsilon, t_0 + \varepsilon)) \subseteq U$, then for $t \in (t_0 - \varepsilon, t_0 + \varepsilon)$ we can write
\[
V(t) = V^1(t)E_1|_{\gamma(t)} + \cdots + V^k(t)E_k|_{\gamma(t)},
\]
for some smooth functions $V^1,\ldots,V^k: (t_0 - \varepsilon, t_0 + \varepsilon) \to \mathbb{R}$. Formula~(4.15) yields
\begin{align*}
\pi^\top(\overline{D}_t V(t)) &= \pi^\top\left(\sum_{i=1}^k \left(\dot{V}^i(t)E_i|_{\gamma(t)} + V^i(t)\overline{\nabla}_{\dot{\gamma}(t)} E_i|_{\gamma(t)}\right)\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^k \left(\dot{V}^i(t)E_i|_{\gamma(t)} + V^i(t)\pi^\top(\overline{\nabla}_{\dot{\gamma}(t)} E_i|_{\gamma(t)})\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^k \left(\dot{V}^i(t)E_i|_{\gamma(t)} + V^i(t)\nabla^\top_{\dot{\gamma}(t)} E_i|_{\gamma(t)}\right) \\
&= D_t^\top V(t). \qedhere
\end{align*}
\end{proof}

entries.label: cor:geodesic-submanifold-acceleration
entries.title: cor:geodesic-submanifold-acceleration

entries.label: prop:geodesic-pseudo-euclidean-submanifold
entries.title: prop:geodesic-pseudo-euclidean-submanifold
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is an embedded Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian submanifold of the pseudo-Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{r,s}$. A smooth curve $\gamma: I \to M$ is a geodesic with respect to $\nabla^{\top}$ if and only if $\gamma''(t)$ is $\overline{g}^{(r,s)}$-orthogonal to $T_{\gamma(t)}M$ for all $t \in I$.

entries.label: prop:characterizations-of-metric-connections
entries.title: Characterizations of Metric Connections
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold (with or without boundary), and let $\nabla$ be a connection on $TM$. The following conditions are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] $\nabla$ is compatible with $g$: $\nabla_X\langle Y,Z \rangle = \langle \nabla_X Y, Z \rangle + \langle Y, \nabla_X Z \rangle$.
\item[(b)] $g$ is parallel with respect to $\nabla$: $\nabla g \equiv 0$.
\item[(c)] In terms of any smooth local frame $(E_i)$, the connection coefficients of $\nabla$ satisfy
\begin{equation}
\Gamma^l_{ki}g_{lj} + \Gamma^l_{kj}g_{il} = E_k(g_{ij}). \tag{5.2}
\end{equation}
\item[(d)] If $V, W$ are smooth vector fields along any smooth curve $\gamma$, then
\begin{equation}
\frac{d}{dt}\langle V,W\rangle = \langle D_t V, W\rangle + \langle V, D_t W\rangle. \tag{5.3}
\end{equation}
\item[(e)] If $V, W$ are parallel vector fields along a smooth curve $\gamma$ in $M$, then $\langle V,W\rangle$ is constant along $\gamma$.
\item[(f)] Given any smooth curve $\gamma$ in $M$, every parallel transport map along $\gamma$ is a linear isometry.
\item[(g)] Given any smooth curve $\gamma$ in $M$, every orthonormal basis at a point of $\gamma$ can be extended to a parallel orthonormal frame along $\gamma$ (Fig.\ 5.1).
\end{enumerate}



\begin{proof}
First we prove (a) $\Leftrightarrow$ (b). By (4.14) and (4.12), the total covariant derivative of the symmetric 2-tensor $g$ is given by
\[
(\nabla g)(Y,Z,X) = (\nabla_X g)(Y,Z) = X(g(Y,Z)) - g(\nabla_X Y,Z) - g(Y,\nabla_X Z).
\]
This is zero for all $X,Y,Z$ if and only if (5.1) is satisfied for all $X,Y,Z$.

To prove (b) $\Leftrightarrow$ (c), note that Proposition 4.18 shows that the components of $\nabla g$ in terms of a smooth local frame $(E_i)$ are
\[
g_{ij;k} = E_k(g_{ij}) - \Gamma^l_{ki}g_{lj} - \Gamma^l_{kj}g_{il}.
\]
These are all zero if and only if (5.2) is satisfied.

Next we prove (a) $\Leftrightarrow$ (d). Assume (a), and let $V,W$ be smooth vector fields along a smooth curve $\gamma\colon I \to M$. Given $t_0 \in I$, in a neighborhood of $\gamma(t_0)$ we may choose coordinates $(x^i)$ and write $V = V^i\partial_i$ and $W = W^j\partial_j$ for some smooth functions $V^i, W^j\colon I \to \mathbb{R}$. Applying (5.1) to the extendible vector fields $\partial_i, \partial_j$, we obtain
\begin{align*}
\frac{d}{dt}\langle V,W\rangle &= \frac{d}{dt}\left(V^i W^j\langle\partial_i,\partial_j\rangle\right) \\
&= \left(\dot{V}^i W^j + V^i \dot{W}^j\right)\langle\partial_i,\partial_j\rangle + V^i W^j\left(\langle\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}(t)}\partial_i,\partial_j\rangle + \langle\partial_i,\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}(t)}\partial_j\rangle\right) \\
&= \langle D_t V, W\rangle + \langle V, D_t W\rangle,
\end{align*}
which proves (d). Conversely, if (d) holds, then in particular it holds for extendible vector fields along $\gamma$, and then (a) follows from part (iii) of Theorem 4.24.

Now we will prove (d) $\Rightarrow$ (e) $\Rightarrow$ (f) $\Rightarrow$ (g) $\Rightarrow$ (d). Assume first that (d) holds. If $V$ and $W$ are parallel along $\gamma$, then (5.3) shows that $\langle V,W\rangle$ has zero derivative with respect to $t$, so it is constant along $\gamma$.

Now assume (e). Let $v_0, w_0$ be arbitrary vectors in $T_{\gamma(t_0)}M$, and let $V, W$ be their parallel transports along $\gamma$, so that $V(t_0) = v_0$, $W(t_0) = w_0$, $P^{\gamma}_{t_0t_1} v_0 = V(t_1)$, and $P^{\gamma}_{t_0t_1} w_0 = W(t_1)$. Because $\langle V, W\rangle$ is constant along $\gamma$, it follows that $\langle P^{\gamma}_{t_0t_1} v_0, P^{\gamma}_{t_0t_1} w_0 \rangle = \langle V(t_1), W(t_1) \rangle = \langle V(t_0), W(t_0) \rangle = \langle v_0, w_0 \rangle$, so $P^{\gamma}_{t_0t_1}$ is a linear isometry.

Next, assuming (f), we suppose $\gamma : I \to M$ is a smooth curve and $(b_i)$ is an orthonormal basis for $T_{\gamma(t_0)}M$, for some $t_0 \in I$. We can extend each $b_i$ by parallel transport to obtain a smooth parallel vector field $E_i$ along $\gamma$, and the assumption that parallel transport is a linear isometry guarantees that the resulting $n$-tuple $(E_i)$ is an orthonormal frame at all points of $\gamma$.

Finally, assume that (g) holds, and let $(E_i)$ be a parallel orthonormal frame along $\gamma$. Given smooth vector fields $V$ and $W$ along $\gamma$, we can express them in terms of this frame as $V = V^i E_i$ and $W = W^j E_j$. The fact that the frame is orthonormal means that the metric coefficients $g_{ij} = \langle E_i, E_j \rangle$ are constants along $\gamma$ ($\pm 1$ or $0$), and the fact that it is parallel means that $D_t V = \dot{V}^i E_i$ and $D_t W = \dot{W}^j E_j$. Thus both sides of (5.3) reduce to the following expression:
\[
g_{ij} (\dot{V}^i W^j + V^i \dot{W}^j).
\]
This proves (d).
\end{proof}

entries.label: cor:metric-connection-norm-geodesic
entries.title: cor:metric-connection-norm-geodesic

entries.label: prop:tangential-connection-compatible-metric
entries.title: prop:tangential-connection-compatible-metric

entries.label: prop:tangential-connection-symmetric
entries.title: prop:tangential-connection-symmetric
entries.body:
If $M$ is an embedded (pseudo-)Riemannian submanifold of a (pseudo-)Euclidean space, then the tangential connection on $M$ is symmetric.

\begin{proof}
Let $M$ be an embedded Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian submanifold of $\mathbb{R}^n$, where $\mathbb{R}^n$ is endowed either with the Euclidean metric or with a pseudo-Euclidean metric $\overline{g}^{(r,s)}$, $r + s = n$. Let $X, Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, and let $\tilde{X}, \tilde{Y}$ be smooth extensions of them to an open subset of the ambient space. If $\iota : M \hookrightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$ represents the inclusion map, it follows that $X$ and $Y$ are $\iota$-related to $\tilde{X}$ and $\tilde{Y}$, respectively, and thus by the naturality of the Lie bracket (Prop.~A.39), $[X, Y]$ is $\iota$-related to $[\tilde{X}, \tilde{Y}]$. In particular, $[\tilde{X}, \tilde{Y}]$ is tangent to $M$, and its restriction to $M$ is equal to $[X, Y]$. Therefore,
\[
\nabla^{\top}_X Y - \nabla^{\top}_Y X = \pi^{\top}\bigl(\overline{\nabla}_{\tilde X} \tilde Y|_M - \overline{\nabla}_{\tilde Y} \tilde X|_M\bigr)
= \pi^{\top}\bigl([\tilde X, \tilde Y]|_M\bigr)
= [\tilde X, \tilde Y]|_M
= [X, Y]. \qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

entries.label: thm:fundamental-theorem-riemannian-geometry
entries.title: Fundamental Theorem of Riemannian Geometry

entries.label: cor:formulas-levi-civita-connection
entries.title: Formulas for the Levi-Civita Connection

entries.label: prop:levi-civita-on-euclidean-and-submanifolds
entries.title: prop:levi-civita-on-euclidean-and-submanifolds

entries.label: prop:naturality-levi-civita
entries.title: Naturality of the Levi-Civita Connection
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ and $(\overline{M},\overline{g})$ are Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifolds with or without boundary, and let $\nabla$ denote the Levi-Civita connection of $g$ and $\overline{\nabla}$ that of $\overline{g}$. If $\varphi: M \to \overline{M}$ is an isometry, then $\varphi^*\overline{\nabla} = \nabla$.

\begin{proof}
By uniqueness of the Levi-Civita connection, it suffices to show that the pullback connection $\varphi^*\overline{\nabla}$ is symmetric and compatible with $g$. The fact that $\varphi$ is an isometry means that for any $Y,Z \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ and $p \in M$,
\[
\langle Y_p, Z_p \rangle = \langle d\varphi_p(Y_p), d\varphi_p(Z_p) \rangle = \langle (\varphi_* Y)_{\varphi(p)}, (\varphi_* Z)_{\varphi(p)} \rangle,
\]
or in other words, $\langle Y,Z \rangle = \langle \varphi_* Y, \varphi_* Z \rangle \circ \varphi$. Therefore, for $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$,
\begin{align*}
X \langle Y, Z \rangle &= X \bigl(\langle \varphi_* Y, \varphi_* Z \rangle \circ \varphi\bigr) \\
&= \bigl((\varphi_* X) \langle \varphi_* Y, \varphi_* Z \rangle\bigr) \circ \varphi \\
&= \Bigl( \langle \overline{\nabla}_{\varphi_* X}(\varphi_* Y), \varphi_* Z \rangle + \langle \varphi_* Y, \overline{\nabla}_{\varphi_* X}(\varphi_* Z) \rangle \Bigr) \circ \varphi \\
&= \langle (\varphi^{-1})_* \overline{\nabla}_{\varphi_* X}(\varphi_* Y), Z \rangle + \langle Y, (\varphi^{-1})_* \overline{\nabla}_{\varphi_* X}(\varphi_* Z) \rangle \\
&= \langle (\varphi^* \overline{\nabla})_X Y, Z \rangle + \langle Y, (\varphi^* \overline{\nabla})_X Z \rangle,
\end{align*}
which shows that the pullback connection is compatible with $g$. Symmetry is proved as follows:
\[
(\varphi^* \overline{\nabla})_X Y - (\varphi^* \overline{\nabla})_Y X = (\varphi^{-1})_* [\varphi_* X, \varphi_* Y]
= [X,Y]. \qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

entries.label: cor:naturality-geodesics
entries.title: Naturality of Geodesics

entries.label: prop:levi-civita-tensor-compatibility
entries.title: prop:levi-civita-tensor-compatibility
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold. The connection induced on each tensor bundle by the Levi-Civita connection is compatible with the induced inner product on tensors, in the sense that
\[
X \langle F, G \rangle = \langle \nabla_X F, G \rangle + \langle F, \nabla_X G \rangle
\]
for every vector field $X$ and every pair of smooth tensor fields $F,G \in \Gamma(T^{(k,l)}TM)$.

entries.label: prop:riemannian-volume-form-parallel
entries.title: prop:riemannian-volume-form-parallel

entries.label: prop:musical-isomorphisms-commute
entries.title: prop:musical-isomorphisms-commute

entries.label: lem:rescaling-lemma
entries.title: Rescaling Lemma

entries.label: prop:exponential-map-properties
entries.title: Properties of the Exponential Map
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, and let $\exp: \mathcal{E} \to M$ be its exponential map.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $\mathcal{E}$ is an open subset of $TM$ containing the image of the zero section, and each set $\mathcal{E}_p \subseteq T_p M$ is star-shaped with respect to $0$.
\item For each $v \in TM$, the geodesic $\gamma_v$ is given by
\begin{equation}
\gamma_v(t) = \exp(tv) \tag{5.17}
\end{equation}
for all $t$ such that either side is defined.
\item The exponential map is smooth.
\item For each point $p \in M$, the differential $d(\exp_p)_0 : T_0(T_p M) \cong T_p M \to T_p M$ is the identity map of $T_p M$, under the usual identification of $T_0(T_p M)$ with $T_p M$.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{proof}
Write $n = \dim M$. The rescaling lemma with $t = 1$ says precisely that $\exp(cv) = \gamma_{cv}(1) = \gamma_v(c)$ whenever either side is defined; this is (b). Moreover, if $v \in \mathcal{E}_p$, then by definition $\gamma_v$ is defined at least on $[0, 1]$. Thus for $0 \le t \le 1$, the rescaling lemma says that
\[
\exp_p(tv) = \gamma_{tv}(1) = \gamma_v(t)
\]
is defined. This shows that $\mathcal{E}_p$ is star-shaped with respect to $0$.

Next we will show that $\mathcal{E}$ is open and $\exp$ is smooth. To do so, we revisit the proof of the existence and uniqueness theorem for geodesics (Theorem 4.27) and reformulate it in a more invariant way. Let $(x^i)$ be any smooth local coordinates on an open set $U \subseteq M$, let $\pi : TM \to M$ be the projection, and let $(x^i, v^i)$ denote the associated natural coordinates for $\pi^{-1}(U) \subseteq TM$ (see p.\ 384). In terms of these coordinates, formula (4.18) defines a smooth vector field $G$ on $\pi^{-1}(U)$. The integral curves of $G$ are the curves $\eta(t) = (x^1(t), \ldots, x^n(t), v^1(t), \ldots, v^n(t))$ that satisfy the system of ODEs given by (4.17), which is equivalent to the geodesic equation under the substitution $v^k = \dot{x}^k$, as we observed in the proof of Theorem 4.27. Stated somewhat more invariantly, every integral curve of $G$ on $\pi^{-1}(U)$ projects to a geodesic under $\pi : TM \to M$ (which in these coordinates is just $\pi(x, v) = x$); conversely, every geodesic $\gamma(t) = (x^1(t), \ldots, x^n(t))$ in $U$ lifts to an integral curve of $G$ in $\pi^{-1}(U)$ by setting $v^i(t) = \dot{x}^i(t)$.

The importance of $G$ stems from the fact that it actually defines a \emph{global} vector field on the total space of $TM$, called the \emph{geodesic vector field}. We could verify this by computing the transformation law for the components of $G$ under a change of coordinates and showing that they take the same form in every coordinate chart; but fortunately there is a way to avoid that messy computation. The key observation, to be proved below, is that $G$ acts on a function $f \in C^\infty(TM)$ by
\begin{equation}
Gf(p,v) = \frac{d}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} f(\gamma_v(t), \gamma_v'(t)). \tag{5.18}
\end{equation}
(Here and whenever convenient, we use the notations $(p, v)$ and $v$ interchangeably for an element $v \in T_p M$, depending on whether we wish to emphasize the point at which $v$ is tangent.) Since this formula is independent of coordinates, it shows that the various definitions of $G$ given by (4.18) in different coordinate systems agree.

To prove that $G$ satisfies (5.18), we write the components of the geodesic $\gamma_v(t)$ as $x^i(t)$ and those of its velocity as $v^i(t) = \dot{x}^i(t)$. Using the chain rule and the geodesic equation in the form (4.17), we can write the right-hand side of (5.18) as
\[
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x^k}(x(t), v(t))\dot{x}^k(t) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial v^k}(x(t), v(t))\dot{v}^k(t)\right)\bigg|_{t=0}
\]
\begin{align*}
&= \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^k}(p, v)v^k - \frac{\partial f}{\partial v^k}(p,v)v^j v^\ell \Gamma^k_{j\ell}(p) \\
&= Gf(p,v).
\end{align*}

The fundamental theorem on flows (Thm.\ A.42) shows that there exist an open set $\mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \times TM$ containing $\{0\} \times TM$ and a smooth map $\theta : \mathcal{D} \to TM$, such that each curve $\theta^{(p,v)}(t) = \theta(t, (p,v))$ is the unique maximal integral curve of $G$ starting at $(p,v)$, defined on an open interval containing $0$.

Now suppose $(p,v) \in \mathcal{E}$. This means that the geodesic $\gamma_v$ is defined at least on the interval $[0, 1]$, and therefore so is the integral curve of $G$ starting at $(p, v) \in TM$. Since $(1, (p,v)) \in \mathcal{D}$, there is a neighborhood of $(1, (p,v))$ in $\mathbb{R} \times TM$ on which the flow of $G$ is defined (Fig.\ 5.2). In particular, this means that there is a neighborhood of $(p,v)$ on which the flow exists for $t \in [0, 1]$, and therefore on which the exponential map is defined. This shows that $\mathcal{E}$ is open.



Since geodesics are projections of integral curves of $G$, it follows that the exponential map can be expressed as
\[
\exp_p(v) = \gamma_v(1) = \pi \circ \theta(1,(p,v))
\]
wherever it is defined, and therefore $\exp_p(v)$ is a smooth function of $(p,v)$.

To compute $d(\exp_p)_0(v)$ for an arbitrary vector $v \in T_pM$, we just need to choose a curve $\tau$ in $T_pM$ starting at $0$ whose initial velocity is $v$, and compute the initial velocity of $\exp_p \circ \tau$. A convenient curve is $\tau(t) = tv$, which yields
\[
d(\exp_p)_0(v) = \frac{d}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} (\exp_p \circ \tau)(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} \exp_p(tv) = \frac{d}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} \gamma_v(t) = v.
\]
Thus $d(\exp_p)_0$ is the identity map.
\end{proof}

entries.label: prop:naturality-exponential-map
entries.title: Naturality of the Exponential Map
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ and $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ are Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifolds and $\varphi: M \to \tilde{M}$ is a local isometry. Then for every $p \in M$, the following diagram commutes:
\[
\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathcal{E}_p & \xrightarrow{\ d\varphi_p\ } & \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_{\varphi(p)} \\
{\scriptstyle \exp_p}\downarrow & & \downarrow{\scriptstyle \exp_{\varphi(p)}} \\
M & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \tilde{M},
\end{array}
\]
where $\mathcal{E}_p \subseteq T_p M$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_{\varphi(p)} \subseteq T_{\varphi(p)}\tilde{M}$ are the domains of the restricted exponential maps $\exp_p$ (with respect to $g$) and $\exp_{\varphi(p)}$ (with respect to $\tilde{g}$), respectively.

entries.label: prop:local-isometries-completely-determined
entries.title: prop:local-isometries-completely-determined

entries.label: prop:uniqueness-normal-coordinates
entries.title: Uniqueness of Normal Coordinates

entries.label: prop:properties-normal-coordinates
entries.title: Properties of Normal Coordinates

entries.label: thm:tubular-neighborhood
entries.title: Tubular Neighborhood Theorem

entries.label: prop:fermi-coordinates-properties
entries.title: Properties of Fermi Coordinates

entries.label: prop:maximal-geodesic-sphere
entries.title: prop:maximal-geodesic-sphere
entries.body:
A nonconstant curve on $S^n(R)$ is a maximal geodesic if and only if it is a periodic constant-speed curve whose image is a great circle. Thus every sphere is geodesically complete.

\begin{proof}
Let $p \in S^n(R)$ be arbitrary. Because $f(x) = |x|^2$ is a defining function for $S^n(R)$, a vector $v \in T_p\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ is tangent to $S^n(R)$ if and only if $df_p(v) = 2\langle p,v \rangle = 0$, where we think of $p$ as a vector by means of the usual identification of $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ with $T_p\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$. Thus $T_pS^n(R)$ is exactly the set of vectors orthogonal to $p$.

Suppose $v$ is an arbitrary nonzero vector in $T_pS^n(R)$. Let $a = |v|/R$ and $\tilde{v} = v/a$ (so $|\tilde{v}| = R$), and consider the smooth curve $\gamma: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ given by
\[
\gamma(t) = (\cos at)p + (\sin at)\tilde{v}.
\]
By direct computation, $|\gamma(t)|^2 = R^2$, so $\gamma(t) \in S^n(R)$ for all $t$. Moreover,
\[
\gamma'(t) = -a(\sin at)p + a(\cos at)\tilde{v}, \qquad
\gamma''(t) = -a^2(\cos at)p - a^2(\sin at)\tilde{v}.
\]
Because $\gamma''(t)$ is proportional to $\gamma(t)$ (thinking of both as vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$), it follows that $\gamma''(t)$ is $\overline{g}$-orthogonal to $T_{\gamma(t)}S^n(R)$, so $\gamma$ is a geodesic in $S^n(R)$ by Corollary~\ref{cor:geodesic-submanifold-acceleration}. Since $\gamma(0) = p$ and $\gamma'(0) = a\tilde{v} = v$, it follows that $\gamma = \gamma_v$.

Each $\gamma_v$ is periodic of period $2\pi/a$, and has constant speed by Corollary~\ref{cor:metric-connection-norm-geodesic} (or by direct computation). The image of $\gamma_v$ is the great circle formed by the intersection of $S^n(R)$ with the linear subspace spanned by $\{p, \tilde{v}\}$, as you can check.

Conversely, suppose $C$ is a great circle formed by intersecting $S^n(R)$ with a 2-dimensional subspace $\Pi$, and let $\{v, w\}$ be an orthonormal basis for $\Pi$. Then $C$ is the image of the geodesic with initial point $p = Rw$ and initial velocity $v$.
\end{proof}

entries.label: prop:geodesics-hyperbolic-spaces
entries.title: prop:geodesics-hyperbolic-spaces
entries.body:
A nonconstant curve in a hyperbolic space is a maximal geodesic if and only if it is a constant-speed embedding of $\mathbb{R}$ whose image is one of the following:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] \textbf{Hyperboloid model:} The intersection of $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ with a 2-dimensional linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{n,1}$, called a \textbf{great hyperbola} (Fig.\ 5.5).
\item[(b)] \textbf{Beltrami--Klein model:} The interior of a line segment whose endpoints both lie on $\partial\mathbb{K}^n(R)$ (Fig.\ 5.6).
\item[(c)] \textbf{Ball model:} The interior of a diameter of $\mathbb{B}^n(R)$, or the intersection of $\mathbb{B}^n(R)$ with a Euclidean circle that intersects $\partial \mathbb{B}^n(R)$ orthogonally (Fig.\ 5.7).
\item[(d)] \textbf{Half-space model:} The intersection of $\mathbb{U}^n(R)$ with one of the following: a line parallel to the $y$-axis or a Euclidean circle with center on $\partial \mathbb{U}^n(R)$ (Fig.\ 5.8).
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: thm:hyperbolic-geodesically-complete
entries.title: thm:hyperbolic-geodesically-complete
entries.body:
Every hyperbolic space is geodesically complete.

\begin{proof}
We begin with the hyperboloid model, for which the proof is formally quite similar to what we just did for the sphere. Since the Riemannian connection on $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ is equal to the tangential connection by Proposition~\ref{prop:levi-civita-on-euclidean-and-submanifolds}, it follows from Corollary~\ref{cor:geodesic-submanifold-acceleration} that a smooth curve $\gamma: I \to \mathbb{H}^n(R)$ is a geodesic if and only if its acceleration $\gamma''(t)$ is everywhere $\overline{q}$-orthogonal to $T_{\gamma(t)}\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ (where $\overline{q} = q^{(n,1)}$ is the Minkowski metric).

Let $p \in \mathbb{H}^n(R)$ be arbitrary. Note that $f(x) = \overline{q}(x,x)$ is a defining function for $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$, and (3.10) shows that the gradient of $f$ at $p$ is equal to $2p$ (where we regard $p$ as a vector in $T_p \mathbb{R}^{n,1}$ as before). It follows that a vector $v \in T_p \mathbb{R}^{n,1}$ is tangent to $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ if and only if $\overline{q}(p,v) = 0$. Let $v \in T_p \mathbb{H}^n(R)$ be an arbitrary nonzero vector. Put $a = |v|_{\overline{q}}/R = \overline{q}(v,v)^{1/2}/R$ and $\hat{v} = v/a$, and define $\gamma: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^{n,1}$ by
\[
\gamma(t) = (\cosh at)p + (\sinh at)\hat{v}.
\]
Direct computation shows that $\gamma$ takes its values in $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ and that its acceleration vector is everywhere proportional to $\gamma(t)$. Thus $\gamma''(t)$ is $\overline{q}$-orthogonal to $T_{\gamma(t)}\mathbb{H}^n(R)$, so $\gamma$ is a geodesic in $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ and therefore has constant speed. Because it satisfies the initial conditions $\gamma(0) = p$ and $\gamma'(0) = v$, it is equal to $\gamma_v$. Note that $\gamma_v$ is a smooth embedding of $\mathbb{R}$ into $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ whose image is the great hyperbola formed by the intersection between $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ and the plane spanned by $\{p, \hat{v}\}$.

Conversely, suppose $\Pi$ is any 2-dimensional linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{n,1}$ that has nontrivial intersection with $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$. Choose $p \in \Pi \cap \mathbb{H}^n(R)$, and let $v$ be another nonzero vector in $\Pi$ that is $\overline{q}$-orthogonal to $p$, which implies $v \in T_p \mathbb{H}^n(R)$. Using the computation above, we see that the image of the geodesic $\gamma_v$ is the great hyperbola formed by the intersection of $\Pi$ with $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$.

Before considering the other three models, note that since maximal geodesics in $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ are constant-speed embeddings of $\mathbb{R}$, it follows from naturality that maximal geodesics in each of the other models are also constant-speed embeddings of $\mathbb{R}$. Thus each model is geodesically complete, and to determine the geodesics in the other models we need only determine their images.

Consider the Beltrami--Klein model. Recall the isometry $c: \mathbb{H}^n(R) \to \mathbb{K}^n(R)$ given by $c(\xi,\tau) = R\xi/\tau$ (see (3.11)). The image of a maximal geodesic in $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ is a great hyperbola, which is the set of points $(\xi,\tau) \in \mathbb{H}^n(R)$ that solve a system of $n-1$ independent linear equations. Simple algebra shows that $(\xi,\tau)$ satisfies a linear equation $\alpha_i\xi^i + \beta\tau = 0$ if and only if $w = c(\xi,\tau) = R\xi/\tau$ satisfies the affine equation $\alpha_i w^i = -\beta R$. Thus $c$ maps each great hyperbola onto the intersection of $\mathbb{K}^n(R)$ with an affine subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$, and since it is the image of a smooth curve, it must be the intersection of $\mathbb{K}^n(R)$ with a straight line.

Next consider the Poincar\'e ball model. First consider the 2-dimensional case, and recall the inverse hyperbolic stereographic projection $\pi^{-1}: \mathbb{H}^2(R) \to \mathbb{H}^2(R)$ constructed in Chapter 3:
\[
\pi^{-1}(u) = (\xi,\tau) = \left( \frac{2R^2u}{R^2-|u|^2}, \frac{R(R^2+|u|^2)}{R^2-|u|^2} \right).
\]
In this case, a great hyperbola is the set of points on $\mathbb{H}^2(R)$ that satisfy a single linear equation $\alpha_i\xi^i + \beta\tau = 0$. In the special case $\beta = 0$, this hyperbola is mapped by $\pi$ to a straight line segment through the origin, as can easily be seen from the geometric definition of $\pi$. If $\beta \ne 0$, we can assume (after multiplying through by a constant if necessary) that $\beta = -1$, and write the linear equation as $\tau = \alpha_i\xi^i = \alpha \cdot \xi$ (where the dot represents the Euclidean dot product between elements of $\mathbb{R}^2$). Under $\pi^{-1}$, this pulls back to the equation
\[
R\frac{R^2+|u|^2}{R^2-|u|^2} = \frac{2R^2\alpha \cdot u}{R^2-|u|^2}
\]
on the disk, which simplifies to
\[
|u|^2 - 2R\alpha \cdot u + R^2 = 0.
\]
Completing the square, we can write this as
\begin{equation}
|u - R\alpha|^2 = R^2(|\alpha|^2 - 1). \tag{5.26}
\end{equation}
If $|\alpha|^2 \le 1$, this locus is either empty or a point on $\partial\mathbb{H}^2(R)$, so it contains no points in $\mathbb{H}^2(R)$. Since we are assuming that it is the image of a maximal geodesic, we must therefore have $|\alpha|^2 > 1$. In that case, (5.26) is the equation of a circle with center $R\alpha$ and radius $R\sqrt{|\alpha|^2 - 1}$. At a point $u_0$ where the circle intersects $\partial\mathbb{H}^2(R)$, the three points $0$, $u_0$, and $R\alpha$ form a triangle with sides $|u_0| = R$, $|R\alpha|$, and $|u_0 - R\alpha|$ (Fig.\ 5.9), which satisfy the Pythagorean identity by (5.26); therefore the circle meets $\partial\mathbb{H}^2(R)$ in a right angle.



In the higher-dimensional case, a geodesic on $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$ is determined by a 2-plane. If the 2-plane contains the point $(0,\ldots,0,R)$, then the corresponding geodesic on $\mathbb{B}^n(R)$ is a line through the origin as before. Otherwise, we can use an orthogonal transformation in the $(\xi^1,\ldots,\xi^n)$ variables (which preserves $\overline{g}_R$) to move this 2-plane so that it lies in the $(\xi^1,\xi^2,\tau)$ subspace, and then we are in the same situation as in the 2-dimensional case.

Finally, consider the upper half-space model. The 2-dimensional case is easiest to analyze using complex notation. Recall the complex formula for the Cayley transform $\kappa : \mathbb{U}^2(R) \to \mathbb{B}^2(R)$ given in Chapter 3:
\[
\kappa(z) = w = iR\frac{z-iR}{z+iR}.
\]
Substituting this into equation (5.26) and writing $w = u + iv$ and $\alpha = a + ib$ in place of $u = (u^1,u^2)$, $\alpha = (\alpha^1,\alpha^2)$, we get
\[
R^2\frac{|z-iR|^2}{|z+iR|^2} - iR^2\bar{\alpha}\frac{z-iR}{z+iR} + iR^2\alpha\frac{\bar{z}+iR}{\bar{z}-iR} + R^2|\alpha|^2 = R^2(|\alpha|^2 - 1).
\]
Multiplying through by $(z+iR)(\bar{z}-iR)/2R^2$ and simplifying yields
\[
(1-b)|z|^2 - 2aRx + (b+1)R^2 = 0.
\]
This is the equation of a circle with center on the $x$-axis, unless $b = 1$, in which case the condition $|\alpha|^2 > 1$ forces $a \neq 0$, and then it is a straight line $x = \text{constant}$. The other class of geodesics on the ball, line segments through the origin, can be handled similarly.

In the higher-dimensional case, suppose first that $\gamma : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{H}^n(R)$ is a maximal geodesic such that $\gamma(0)$ lies on the $y$-axis and $\gamma'(0)$ is in the span of $\{\partial/\partial x^1,\partial/\partial y\}$. From the explicit formula (3.15) for $\kappa$, it follows that $\kappa \circ \gamma(0)$ lies on the $y$-axis in the ball, and $(\kappa \circ \gamma)'(0)$ is in the span of $\{\partial/\partial u^1,\partial/\partial v\}$. The image of the geodesic $\kappa \circ \gamma$ is either part of a line through the origin or an arc of a circle perpendicular to $\partial\mathbb{B}^n(R)$, both of which are contained in the $(u^1,v)$-plane. By the argument in the preceding paragraph, it then follows that the image of $\gamma$ is contained in the $(x^1, y)$-plane and is either a vertical half-line or a semicircle centered on the $y = 0$ hyperplane. For the general case, note that translations and orthogonal transformations in the $x$-variables preserve vertical half-lines and circles centered on the $y = 0$ hyperplane in $\mathbb{U}^n(R)$, and they also preserve the metric $\overline{g}_R$. Given an arbitrary maximal geodesic $\gamma : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{U}^n(R)$, after applying an $x$-translation we may assume that $\gamma(0)$ lies on the $y$-axis, and after an orthogonal transformation in the $x$ variables, we may assume that $\gamma'(0)$ is in the span of $\{\partial/\partial x^1, \partial/\partial y\}$; then the argument above shows that the image of $\gamma$ is either a vertical half-line or a semicircle centered on the $y = 0$ hyperplane.
\end{proof}

entries.label: ex:real-projective-plane-elliptic-geometry
entries.title: ex:real-projective-plane-elliptic-geometry
entries.body: The real projective plane $\mathbb{RP}^2$ has a frame-homogeneous Riemannian metric $g$ that is locally isometric to a round metric on $S^n$ (see Example 2.34 and Problem 3-2). As Problem~\ref{prob:elliptic-geometry-hilbert} shows, this interpretation satisfies Hilbert's incidence postulates as well as the elliptic parallel postulate. This interpretation is called \emph{single elliptic geometry}.

entries.label: def:one-parameter-family-of-curves
entries.title: One-Parameter Family of Curves
entries.body:
Given intervals $I, J \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, a continuous map $\Gamma: J \times I \to M$ is called a \emph{one-parameter family of curves}. Such a family defines two collections of curves in $M$: the \emph{main curves} $\Gamma_s(t) = \Gamma(s,t)$ defined for $t \in I$ by holding $s$ constant, and the \emph{transverse curves} $\Gamma^{(t)}(s) = \Gamma(s,t)$ defined for $s \in J$ by holding $t$ constant.

If such a family $\Gamma$ is smooth (or at least continuously differentiable), we denote the velocity vectors of the main and transverse curves by
\[
\partial_t \Gamma(s,t) = (\Gamma_s)'(t) \in T_{\Gamma(s,t)} M; \quad \partial_s \Gamma(s,t) = (\Gamma^{(t)})'(s) \in T_{\Gamma(s,t)} M.
\]

Each of these is an example of a \emph{vector field along $\Gamma$}, which is a continuous map $V: J \times I \to TM$ such that $V(s,t) \in T_{\Gamma(s,t)} M$ for each $(s,t)$.

entries.label: def:variations-proper-variations
entries.title: Variations
entries.body: If $\gamma: [a,b] \to M$ is a given admissible curve, a \emph{variation} of $\gamma$ is an admissible family of curves $\Gamma: J \times [a,b] \to M$ such that $J$ is an open interval containing $0$ and $\Gamma_0 = \gamma$. It is called a \emph{proper variation} if in addition, all of the main curves have the same starting and ending points: $\Gamma_s(a) = \gamma(a)$ and $\Gamma_s(b) = \gamma(b)$ for all $s \in J$.

entries.label: lem:variation-field-existence
entries.title: lem:variation-field-existence
entries.body: If $\gamma$ is an admissible curve and $V$ is a piecewise smooth vector field along $\gamma$, then $V$ is the variation field of some variation of $\gamma$. If $V$ is proper, the variation can be taken to be proper as well.

entries.label: lem:symmetry-lemma
entries.title: Symmetry Lemma
entries.body:
Let $\Gamma: J \times [a,b] \to M$ be an admissible family of curves in a Riemannian manifold. On every rectangle $J \times [a_{i-1}, a_i]$ where $\Gamma$ is smooth,
\[
D_s \partial_t \Gamma = D_t \partial_s \Gamma.
\]

entries.label: thm:first-variation-formula
entries.title: First Variation Formula

entries.label: thm:minimizing-geodesic-unit-speed
entries.title: thm:minimizing-geodesic-unit-speed
entries.body: In a Riemannian manifold, every minimizing curve is a geodesic when it is given a unit-speed parametrization.

entries.label: cor:critical-point-geodesic
entries.title: cor:critical-point-geodesic
entries.body: A unit-speed admissible curve $\gamma$ is a \emph{critical point} for $L_g$ if and only if it is a geodesic.

entries.label: lem:minimizing-curves-locally-minimizing
entries.title: lem:minimizing-curves-locally-minimizing
entries.body: Every minimizing admissible curve segment is locally minimizing.

entries.label: lem:radial-distance-well-defined
entries.title: lem:radial-distance-well-defined
entries.body: In every normal neighborhood $U$ of $p \in M$, the radial distance function and the radial vector field are well defined, independently of the choice of normal coordinates. Both $r$ and $\partial_r$ are smooth on $U \setminus \{p\}$, and $r^2$ is smooth on all of $U$.

entries.label: thm:gauss-lemma
entries.title: The Gauss Lemma
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold, let $U$ be a geodesic ball centered at $p \in M$, and let $\partial_r$ denote the radial vector field on $U \setminus \{p\}$. Then $\partial_r$ is a unit vector field orthogonal to the geodesic spheres in $U \setminus \{p\}$.

entries.label: cor:gradient-radial-distance
entries.title: cor:gradient-radial-distance
entries.body: Let $U$ be a geodesic ball centered at $p \in M$, and let $r$ and $\partial_r$ be the radial distance and radial vector field as defined by (6.4) and (6.5). Then $\operatorname{grad} r = \partial_r$ on $U \setminus \{p\}$.

entries.label: prop:radial-geodesic-unique-minimizer
entries.title: prop:radial-geodesic-unique-minimizer
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. Suppose $p \in M$ and $q$ is contained in a geodesic ball around $p$. Then (up to reparametrization) the radial geodesic from $p$ to $q$ is the unique minimizing curve in $M$ from $p$ to $q$.

entries.label: cor:radial-distance-riemannian-distance
entries.title: cor:radial-distance-riemannian-distance
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ be a connected Riemannian manifold and $p \in M$. Within every open or closed geodesic ball around $p$, the radial distance function $r(x)$ defined by (6.4) is equal to the Riemannian distance from $p$ to $x$ in $M$.

entries.label: cor:geodesic-ball-metric-ball
entries.title: cor:geodesic-ball-metric-ball
entries.body: In a connected Riemannian manifold, every open or closed geodesic ball is also an open or closed metric ball of the same radius, and every geodesic sphere is a metric sphere of the same radius.

entries.label: lem:uniformly-normal-neighborhood
entries.title: Uniformly Normal Neighborhood Lemma
entries.body: Given $p \in M$ and any neighborhood $U$ of $p$, there exists a uniformly normal neighborhood of $p$ contained in $U$.

entries.label: thm:locally-minimizing-geodesics
entries.title: Every Riemannian geodesic is locally minimizing
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. Suppose $\gamma: I \to M$ is a geodesic, which we may assume to be defined on an open interval, and let $t_0 \in I$.

entries.label: lem:compact-injectivity-radius-positive
entries.title: lem:compact-injectivity-radius-positive
entries.body: If $(M, g)$ is a compact Riemannian manifold, then $\operatorname{inj}(M)$ is positive.

entries.label: thm:small-geodesic-ball-convex
entries.title: thm:small-geodesic-ball-convex
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. For each $p \in M$, there exists $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ such that every geodesic ball centered at $p$ of radius less than or equal to $\varepsilon_0$ is geodesically convex.

entries.label: lem:exponential-map-implies-completeness
entries.title: lem:exponential-map-implies-completeness

entries.label: thm:hopf-rinow
entries.title: Hopf--Rinow
entries.body: A connected Riemannian manifold is metrically complete if and only if it is geodesically complete.

entries.label: cor:exponential-map-defined-implies-complete
entries.title: cor:exponential-map-defined-implies-complete
entries.body: If $M$ is a connected Riemannian manifold and there exists a point $p \in M$ such that the restricted exponential map $\exp_p$ is defined on all of $T_p M$, then $M$ is complete.

entries.label: cor:complete-implies-minimizing-geodesics
entries.title: cor:complete-implies-minimizing-geodesics
entries.body: If $M$ is a complete, connected Riemannian manifold, then any two points in $M$ can be joined by a minimizing geodesic segment.

entries.label: cor:compact-implies-maximal-geodesics-defined
entries.title: cor:compact-implies-maximal-geodesics-defined
entries.body: If $M$ is a compact Riemannian manifold, then every maximal geodesic in $M$ is defined for all time.

entries.label: thm:completeness-of-covering-map
entries.title: thm:completeness-of-covering-map
entries.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ and $(M, g)$ are connected Riemannian manifolds with $M$ complete, and $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a local isometry. Then $\tilde{M}$ is complete and $\pi$ is a Riemannian covering map.

entries.label: cor:complete-iff-covering-complete
entries.title: cor:complete-iff-covering-complete
