entries.body: Suppose $\tilde{M}$ and $M$ are connected Riemannian manifolds, and $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ is a Riemannian covering map. Then $M$ is complete if and only if $\tilde{M}$ is complete.

entries.label: prop:path-homotopy-contains-minimizing-geodesic
entries.title: prop:path-homotopy-contains-minimizing-geodesic
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a complete, connected Riemannian manifold, and $p, q \in M$. Every path-homotopy class of paths from $p$ to $q$ contains a geodesic segment $\gamma$ that minimizes length among all admissible curves in the same path-homotopy class.

entries.label: prop:existence-closed-geodesics
entries.title: Existence of Closed Geodesics
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a compact, connected Riemannian manifold. Every nontrivial free homotopy class in $M$ is represented by a closed geodesic that has minimum length among all admissible loops in the given free homotopy class.

entries.label: lem:distance-triangle-inequality-and-continuity
entries.title: lem:distance-triangle-inequality-and-continuity
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a connected Riemannian manifold and $S \subseteq M$ is any subset.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $d_g(x, S) \leq d_g(x, y) + d_g(y, S)$ for all $x, y \in M$.
\item $x \mapsto d_g(x, S)$ is a continuous function on $M$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: thm:distance-to-subset-unit-gradient
entries.title: thm:distance-to-subset-unit-gradient
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a connected Riemannian manifold, $S \subseteq M$ is arbitrary, and $f: M \to [0, \infty)$ is the distance to $S$, that is, $f(x) = d_g(x, S)$ for all $x \in M$. If $f$ is continuously differentiable on some open subset $U \subseteq M \setminus S$, then $|\operatorname{grad} f| \equiv 1$ on $U$.

entries.label: thm:local-distance-geodesic-integral-curve
entries.title: thm:local-distance-geodesic-integral-curve
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold and $f$ is a smooth local distance function on an open subset $U \subseteq M$. Then $\nabla_{\operatorname{grad} f} (\operatorname{grad} f) \equiv 0$, and each integral curve of $\operatorname{grad} f$ is a unit-speed geodesic.

entries.label: lem:local-distance-curve-arc-length
entries.title: lem:local-distance-curve-arc-length
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold, $K \subseteq M$, and $f : K \to \mathbb{R}$ is a continuous function whose restriction to some open set $W \subseteq K$ is a smooth local distance function. For every admissible curve $\sigma : [a_0, b_0] \to K$ such that $\sigma((a_0, b_0)) \subseteq W$, we have
\[
L_g(\sigma) \geq |f(\sigma(b_0)) - f(\sigma(a_0))|.
\]

entries.label: thm:smooth-local-distance-function
entries.title: thm:smooth-local-distance-function
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold, $U \subseteq M$ is an open subset, $S \subseteq U$, and $f: U \to [0,\infty)$ is a continuous function such that $f^{-1}(0) = S$ and $f$ is a smooth local distance function on $U \setminus S$. Then there is a neighborhood $U_0 \subseteq U$ of $S$ in which $f(x)$ is equal to the distance in $M$ from $x$ to $S$.

entries.label: cor:local-distance-smooth-level-sets
entries.title: cor:local-distance-smooth-level-sets
entries.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold, and let $f$ be a smooth local distance function on an open subset $U \subseteq M$. If $c$ is a real number such that $S = f^{-1}(c)$ is nonempty, then there is a neighborhood $U_0$ of $S$ in $U$ on which $|f(x) - c|$ is equal to the distance in $M$ from $x$ to $S$.

entries.label: prop:fermi-coordinates-radial-function
entries.title: prop:fermi-coordinates-radial-function

entries.label: thm:gauss-lemma-submanifolds
entries.title: Gauss Lemma for Submanifolds
entries.body: Let $P$ be an embedded submanifold of a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$, let $U$ be a normal neighborhood of $P$ in $M$, and let $r$ and $\partial_r$ be as defined in Proposition~\ref{prop:fermi-coordinates-radial-function}. On $U \setminus P$, $\partial_r$ is a unit vector field orthogonal to the level sets of $r$.

entries.label: cor:distance-function-local-properties
entries.title: Assume the hypotheses of Theorem~6.38

entries.label: thm:tubular-neighborhood-metric-distance
entries.title: thm:tubular-neighborhood-metric-distance
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a connected Riemannian manifold, $P \subseteq M$ is a compact submanifold, and $U_\varepsilon$ is an $\varepsilon$-tubular neighborhood of $P$. Then $U_\varepsilon$ is also an $\varepsilon$-neighborhood in the metric space sense, and inside $U_\varepsilon$, the distance in $M$ to $P$ is equal to the function $r$ defined in Proposition~\ref{prop:fermi-coordinates-radial-function}.

entries.label: prop:characterizations-semigeodetic-coordinates
entries.title: Characterizations of Semigeodesic Coordinates
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian $n$-manifold, and let $(x^1, \ldots, x^n)$ be smooth coordinates on an open subset of $M$. The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item[(a)] $(x^i)$ are semigeodesic coordinates.
  \item[(b)] $|\partial_n|_g \equiv 1$ and $\langle\partial_\alpha, \partial_n\rangle_g \equiv 0$ for $\alpha = 1, \ldots, n-1$.
  \item[(c)] $|dx^n|_g \equiv 1$ and $\langle dx^\alpha, dx^n\rangle_g \equiv 0$ for $\alpha = 1, \ldots, n-1$.
  \item[(d)] $|\operatorname{grad} x^n|_g \equiv 1$ and $\langle\operatorname{grad} x^\alpha, \operatorname{grad} x^n\rangle_g \equiv 0$ for $\alpha = 1, \ldots, n-1$.
  \item[(e)] $x^n$ is a local distance function and $x^1, \ldots, x^{n-1}$ are constant along the integral curves of $\operatorname{grad} x^n$.
  \item[(f)] $\operatorname{grad} x^n \equiv \partial_n$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: cor:semigeodesic-metric-christoffel
entries.title: cor:semigeodesic-metric-christoffel
entries.body:
Let $(x^i)$ be semigeodesic coordinates on an open subset of a Riemannian $n$-manifold $(M, g)$.

(a) The metric has the following coordinate expression:
\[
g = (dx^n)^2 + g_{\alpha\beta}(x^1, \ldots, x^n) \, dx^\alpha \, dx^\beta.
\]

(b) The Christoffel symbols of $g$ have the following coordinate expressions:
\begin{align*}
\Gamma^n_{nn} &= \Gamma^\alpha_{nn} = \Gamma^n_{\alpha n} = \Gamma^n_{n\alpha} = 0,\\
\Gamma^n_{\alpha\beta} &= -\frac{1}{2}\partial_n g_{\alpha\beta},\\
\Gamma^\beta_{n\alpha} &= \Gamma^\beta_{\alpha n} = \frac{1}{2}g^{\beta\gamma}\partial_n g_{\alpha\gamma},\\
\Gamma^\gamma_{\alpha\beta} &= \tilde{\Gamma}^\gamma_{\alpha\beta},
\end{align*}
where for each fixed value of $x^n$, the quantities $\tilde{\Gamma}^\gamma_{\alpha\beta}$ are the Christoffel symbols in $(x^\alpha)$ coordinates for the induced metric $\tilde{g}$ on the level set $x^n = \text{constant}$.

entries.label: ex:fermi-coordinates-hypersurface
entries.title: Fermi Coordinates for a Hypersurface
entries.body: Suppose $P$ is an embedded hypersurface in a Riemannian manifold $(M, g)$, and let $(x^1, \ldots, x^{n-1}, v)$ be any Fermi coordinates for $P$ on an open subset $U \subseteq M$ (see (5.25)). In this case, the function $r$ defined by (6.17) is just $r(x^1, \ldots, x^{n-1}, v) = (v^2)^{1/2} = |v|$, so $v$ is a local distance function on $U \setminus P$. It follows from Corollary~\ref{cor:distance-function-local-properties} that there is a neighborhood $U_0$ of $P$ on which $|v|$ is equal to the distance from $P$. Moreover, (6.18) reduces to $\partial_r = \pm\partial_v$, which is equal to $\operatorname{grad}|v|$ by Corollary~\ref{cor:distance-function-local-properties}, so it follows from Proposition~\ref{prop:characterizations-semigeodetic-coordinates}(f) that Fermi coordinates for a hypersurface are automatically semigeodesic coordinates. $\hfill\square$

entries.label: ex:boundary-normal-coordinates
entries.title: Boundary Normal Coordinates
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a smooth Riemannian manifold with nonempty boundary. The results in this chapter do not apply directly to manifolds with boundary, but we can embed $M$ in its double $\tilde{M}$ (Prop. A.31), extend the metric smoothly to $\tilde{M}$, and construct Fermi coordinates $(x^1, \ldots, x^{n-1}, v)$ for $\partial M$ in $\tilde{M}$. By replacing $v$ with $-v$ if necessary, we can arrange that $v > 0$ in $\operatorname{Int} M$, and then these Fermi coordinates restrict to smooth boundary coordinates for $M$ that are also semigeodesic coordinates. Such coordinates are called \emph{boundary normal coordinates} for $M$. $\hfill\square$

entries.label: ex:polar-normal-coordinates
entries.title: Polar Normal Coordinates
entries.body:
Polar coordinates for $\mathbb{R}^n$ are constructed by choosing a smooth local parametrization $\psi: \tilde{U} \to U \subset S^{n-1}$ for an open subset $U$ of $S^{n-1}$, and defining $\Psi: \tilde{U} \times \mathbb{R}^+ \to \mathbb{R}^n$ by $\Psi(\theta^1,\ldots,\theta^{n-1},r) = r\psi(\theta^1,\ldots,\theta^{n-1})$. It is straightforward to show that the differential of $\Psi$ vanishes nowhere, so $\Theta = \Psi^{-1}$ is a smooth coordinate map on the open subset $U = \Psi(\tilde{U} \times \mathbb{R}^+) \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \setminus \{0\}$. Familiar examples are ordinary polar coordinates in the plane and spherical coordinates in $\mathbb{R}^3$. Such coordinates have the property that the last coordinate function is $r(x) = |x|$.

Now let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian $n$-manifold, $p$ a point in $M$, and $\varphi$ any normal coordinate chart defined on a normal neighborhood $V$ of $p$. For every choice of polar coordinates $(\Theta, r)$ for $\mathbb{R}^n \setminus \{0\}$, we obtain a smooth coordinate map $\Theta \circ \varphi$ on an open subset of $V \setminus \{p\}$ (see Fig. 6.19). Such coordinates are called \emph{polar normal coordinates}. They have the property that the last coordinate function $r$ is the radial distance function on $V$, and the other coordinates are constant along the integral curves of $\operatorname{grad} r$, so they are semigeodesic coordinates. $\hfill\square$

entries.label: ex:polar-fermi-coordinates
entries.title: Polar Fermi Coordinates
entries.body:
Now let $P$ be an embedded submanifold of $(M,g)$, and let $\varphi = (x^1,\ldots,x^k, v^1,\ldots,v^{n-k})$ be Fermi coordinates on a neighborhood $U_0$ of a point $p \in P$. Then any polar coordinate map $\Theta$ for $\mathbb{R}^{n-k}$ can be applied to the variables $(v^1,\ldots,v^{n-k})$ to yield a coordinate chart $(\text{Id}_{\mathbb{R}^k} \times \Theta) \circ \varphi$ on an open subset of $U_0 \cap P$, taking values in $\mathbb{R}^k \times \mathbb{R}^{n-k-1} \times \mathbb{R}^+$.

If we write the coordinate functions as $(x^1,\ldots,x^k, \theta^1,\ldots,\theta^{n-k-1}, r)$, it follows from Proposition 5.26 that each coordinate curve $t \mapsto (x^1,\ldots,x^k, \theta^1,\ldots,\theta^{n-k-1}, t)$ is a unit-speed geodesic. Thus these are semigeodesic coordinates, called \emph{polar Fermi coordinates}. The polar normal coordinates described above are just the special case $P = \{p\}$. $\hfill\square$

entries.label: ex:n-torus-flat-metric
entries.title: ex:n-torus-flat-metric
entries.body: The metric on the $n$-torus induced by the embedding in $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$ given in Example 2.21 is flat, because each point has a coordinate neighborhood in which the metric is Euclidean.

deps.title: The n-Torus as a Riemannian Submanifold
deps.label: ex:n-torus-riemannian-submanifold

entries.label: prop:flat-manifold-levi-civita-flatness
entries.title: prop:flat-manifold-levi-civita-flatness
entries.body: If $(M,g)$ is a flat Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, then its Levi-Civita connection satisfies the flatness criterion.

entries.label: prop:curvature-multilinearity
entries.title: prop:curvature-multilinearity
entries.body: The map $R$ defined above is multilinear over $C^\infty(M)$, and thus defines a $(1,3)$-tensor field on $M$.

entries.label: prop:curvature-tensor-components
entries.title: prop:curvature-tensor-components

entries.label: prop:curvature-commutator-failure
entries.title: prop:curvature-commutator-failure
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a smooth Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold and $\Gamma: J \times I \to M$ is a smooth one-parameter family of curves in $M$. Then for every smooth vector field $V$ along $\Gamma$,
\[
D_s D_t V - D_t D_s V = R(\partial_s \Gamma, \partial_t \Gamma) V.
\]

entries.label: prop:curvature-isometry-invariant
entries.title: prop:curvature-isometry-invariant
entries.body: The curvature tensor is a local isometry invariant: if $(M,g)$ and $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ are Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifolds and $\varphi: M \to \tilde{M}$ is a local isometry, then $\varphi^*\tilde{\operatorname{Rm}} = \operatorname{Rm}$.

entries.label: lem:parallel-vector-field-flatness
entries.title: lem:parallel-vector-field-flatness
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a smooth manifold, and $\nabla$ is any connection on $M$ satisfying the flatness criterion. Given $p \in M$ and any vector $v \in T_p M$, there exists a parallel vector field $V$ on a neighborhood of $p$ such that $V_p = v$.

deps.title: Existence, Uniqueness, and Smoothness for Linear ODEs
deps.label: thm:linear-ode-existence-uniqueness

entries.label: thm:flatness-iff-zero-curvature
entries.title: thm:flatness-iff-zero-curvature
entries.body: A Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold is flat if and only if its curvature tensor vanishes identically.

entries.label: thm:curvature-endomorphism-formula
entries.title: thm:curvature-endomorphism-formula

deps.title: Existence, Uniqueness, and Smoothness for Linear ODEs
deps.label: thm:linear-ode-existence-uniqueness

entries.label: prop:curvature-tensor-symmetries
entries.title: Symmetries of the Curvature Tensor
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold. The $(0,4)$-curvature tensor of $g$ has the following symmetries for all vector fields $W, X, Y, Z$:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $Rm(W,X,Y,Z) = -Rm(X,W,Y,Z)$.
\item $Rm(W,X,Y,Z) = -Rm(W,X,Z,Y)$.
\item $Rm(W,X,Y,Z) = Rm(Y,Z,W,X)$.
\item $Rm(W,X,Y,Z) + Rm(X,Y,W,Z) + Rm(Y,W,X,Z) = 0$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: prop:differential-bianchi-identity
entries.title: Differential Bianchi Identity

entries.label: thm:ricci-identities
entries.title: Ricci Identities

entries.label: lem:ricci-symmetries
entries.title: lem:ricci-symmetries
entries.body:
The Ricci curvature is a symmetric 2-tensor field. It can be expressed in any of the following ways:
\[
R_{ij} = R_{kij}^{\,k} = R^{k}{}_{ikj} = -R^{k}{}_{kij} = -R_{ikj}^{\,k}.
\]

entries.label: prop:ricci-tensor-decomposition
entries.title: prop:ricci-tensor-decomposition

entries.label: prop:contracted-bianchi-identities
entries.title: Contracted Bianchi Identities
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold. The covariant derivatives of the Riemann, Ricci, and scalar curvatures of $g$ satisfy the following identities:
$$\mathrm{tr}_g (\nabla \mathrm{Rm}) = -D(\mathrm{Rc}), \qquad (7.30)$$
$$\mathrm{tr}_g (\nabla \mathrm{Rc}) = \tfrac{1}{2}dS, \qquad (7.31)$$
where the trace in each case is on the first and last indices. In components, this is
$$R_{ijkl;i} = R_{jk;l} - R_{jl;k}, \qquad (7.32)$$
$$R_{il;i} = \tfrac{1}{2}S_{;l}. \qquad (7.33)$$

entries.label: prop:schur-lemma
entries.title: Schur's Lemma
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a connected Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold of dimension $n \geq 3$ whose Ricci tensor satisfies $\mathrm{Rc} = fg$ for some smooth real-valued function $f$. Then $f$ is constant and $g$ is an Einstein metric.

entries.label: cor:einstein-zero-ricci
entries.title: cor:einstein-zero-ricci
entries.body: If $(M,g)$ is a connected Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold of dimension $n \geq 3$, then $g$ is Einstein if and only if $\mathrm{Rc} = 0$.

entries.label: prop:dim-algebraic-curvature-tensors
entries.title: prop:dim-algebraic-curvature-tensors

entries.label: lem:kulkarni-nomizu-properties
entries.title: Properties of the Kulkarni--Nomizu Product
entries.body:
Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional vector space endowed with a scalar product $g$, let $h$ and $k$ be symmetric $2$-tensors on $V$, let $T$ be an algebraic curvature tensor on $V$, and let $\operatorname{tr}_g$ denote the trace on the first and last indices.
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] $h \owedge k$ is an algebraic curvature tensor.
\item[(b)] $h \owedge k = k \owedge h$.
\item[(c)] $\operatorname{tr}_g(h \owedge g) = (n-2)h + (\operatorname{tr}_g h)g$.
\item[(d)] $\operatorname{tr}_g(g \owedge g) = 2(n-1)g$.
\item[(e)] $\langle T, h \owedge g\rangle_g = 4\langle\operatorname{tr}_g T, h\rangle_g$.
\item[(f)] In case $g$ is positive definite, $|g \owedge h|_g^2 = 4(n-2)|h|_g^2 + 4(\operatorname{tr}_g h)^2$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: prop:right-inverse-trace
entries.title: prop:right-inverse-trace

entries.label: prop:weyl-decomposition
entries.title: prop:weyl-decomposition
entries.body: For every Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(M, g)$ of dimension $n \geq 3$, the trace of the Weyl tensor is zero, and $\text{Rm} = W + P \owedge g$ is the orthogonal decomposition of $\text{Rm}$ corresponding to $\mathfrak{R}(V^*) = \ker(\text{tr}_g) \oplus \ker(\text{tr}_g)^{\perp}$.

entries.label: cor:curvature-space-dimensions
entries.title: cor:curvature-space-dimensions

entries.label: cor:ricci-determines-curvature-dim3
entries.title: The Curvature Tensor in Dimension 3

entries.label: cor:curvature-tensor-dim-2
entries.title: The Curvature Tensor in Dimension 2
entries.body:
On every Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ of dimension 2, the Riemann and Ricci tensors are determined by the scalar curvature as follows:
\[
Rm = \frac{1}{4}Sg \owedge g, \quad Rc = \frac{1}{2}Sg.
\]

entries.label: prop:ricci-decomposition
entries.title: The Ricci Decomposition of the Curvature Tensor

entries.label: prop:conformal-transformation-levi-civita
entries.title: Conformal Transformation of the Levi-Civita Connection
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian $n$-manifold (with or without boundary), and let $\tilde{g} = e^{2f} g$ be any metric conformal to $g$. If $\nabla$ and $\tilde{\nabla}$ denote the Levi-Civita connections of $g$ and $\tilde{g}$, respectively, then
\[
\tilde{\nabla}_X Y = \nabla_X Y + (Xf)Y + (Yf)X - \langle X, Y \rangle_g \operatorname{grad} f, \quad (7.42)
\]
where the gradient on the right-hand side is that of $g$. In any local coordinates, the Christoffel symbols of the two connections are related by
\[
\tilde{\Gamma}^k_{ij} = \Gamma^k_{ij} + f_i \delta^k_j + f_j \delta^k_i - g^{kl} f_l g_{ij}, \quad (7.43)
\]
where $f_i = \partial_i f$ is the $i$th component of $\nabla f = df = f_i dx^i$.

entries.label: thm:conformal-transformation-curvature
entries.title: Conformal Transformation of the Curvature
entries.body:
Let $g$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian metric on an $n$-manifold $M$ with or without boundary, $f \in C^\infty(M)$, and $\tilde{g} = e^{2f} g$. In the Riemannian case, the curvature tensors of $\tilde{g}$ (represented with tildes) are related to those of $g$ by the following formulas:
\[
\widetilde{Rm} = e^{2f}\left(Rm - (\nabla^2 f) \owedge g + (df \otimes df) \owedge g - \tfrac{1}{2}|df|^2_g (g \owedge g)\right), \quad (7.44)
\]
\[
\widetilde{Rc} = Rc - (n-2)(\nabla^2 f) + (n-2)(df \otimes df) - (\Delta f + (n-2)|df|^2_g)g, \quad (7.45)
\]
\[
\tilde{S} = e^{-2f}(S - 2(n-1)\Delta f - (n-1)(n-2)|df|^2_g), \quad (7.46)
\]
where the curvatures and covariant derivatives on the right are those of $g$, and $\Delta f = \operatorname{div}(\operatorname{grad} f)$ is the Laplacian of $f$ defined in Chapter~2. If in addition $n \geq 3$, then
\[
\tilde{P} = P - \nabla^2 f + (df \otimes df) - \tfrac{1}{2}|df|^2_g g, \quad (7.47)
\]
\[
\tilde{W} = e^{2f} W. \quad (7.48)
\]
In the pseudo-Riemannian case, the same formulas hold with each occurrence of $|df|^2_g$ replaced by $\langle df, df \rangle_{g}$.

deps.title: Conformal Transformation of the Levi-Civita Connection
deps.label: prop:conformal-transformation-levi-civita

entries.label: cor:weyl-zero-locally-conformally-flat
entries.title: cor:weyl-zero-locally-conformally-flat
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold of dimension $n \geq 3$. If $g$ is locally conformally flat, then its Weyl tensor vanishes identically.

entries.label: prop:trace-covariant-derivative-weyl
entries.title: prop:trace-covariant-derivative-weyl

entries.label: cor:weyl-cotton-vanish-dimension-4
entries.title: cor:weyl-cotton-vanish-dimension-4
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold. If $\dim M \geq 4$ and the Weyl tensor vanishes identically, then so does the Cotton tensor.

entries.label: prop:conformal-invariance-cotton-dim3
entries.title: Conformal Invariance of the Cotton Tensor in Dimension 3
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian 3-manifold, and $\tilde{g} = e^{2f} g$ for some $f \in C^\infty(M)$. If $C$ and $\tilde{C}$ denote the Cotton tensors of $g$ and $\tilde{g}$, respectively, then $\tilde{C} = C$.

entries.label: cor:cotton-tensor-vanishes-conformally-flat
entries.title: cor:cotton-tensor-vanishes-conformally-flat
entries.body: If $(M,g)$ is a locally conformally flat 3-manifold, then the Cotton tensor of $g$ vanishes identically.

deps.title: Conformal Invariance of the Cotton Tensor in Dimension 3
deps.label: prop:conformal-invariance-cotton-dim3

entries.label: thm:weyl-schouten
entries.title: Weyl--Schouten

entries.label: lem:overdetermined-covector-system
entries.title: lem:overdetermined-covector-system
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, and consider the overdetermined system of equations
$$\nabla\xi = A(\xi), \quad (7.56)$$
where $A : T^*M \to T^2T^*M$ is a smooth map satisfying the following compatibility condition: for any smooth covector field $\xi$, the 3-tensor field $\nabla(A(\xi))$ satisfies the following identity when $A(\xi)$ is substituted for $\nabla\xi$ wherever it appears:
$$A(\xi)_{ij;k} - A(\xi)_{ik;j} = R_{jki}^l \xi_l. \quad (7.57)$$
Then for every $p \in M$ and every covector $\eta_0 \in T_p^*M$, there is a smooth solution to (7.56) on a neighborhood of $p$ satisfying $\xi_p = \eta_0$.

entries.label: prop:second-fundamental-form-properties
entries.title: Properties of the Second Fundamental Form
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is an embedded Riemannian submanifold of a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$, and let $X, Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$.

\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $\text{II}(X, Y)$ is independent of the extensions of $X$ and $Y$ to an open subset of $\tilde{M}$.
\item $\text{II}(X, Y)$ is bilinear over $C^{\infty}(M)$ in $X$ and $Y$.
\item $\text{II}(X, Y)$ is symmetric in $X$ and $Y$.
\item The value of $\text{II}(X, Y)$ at a point $p \in M$ depends only on $X_p$ and $Y_p$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: thm:gauss-formula
entries.title: The Gauss Formula
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is an embedded Riemannian submanifold of a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$. If $X,Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ are extended arbitrarily to smooth vector fields on a neighborhood of $M$ in $\tilde{M}$, the following formula holds along $M$:
\[
\tilde{\nabla}_X Y = \nabla_X Y + \text{II}(X,Y).
\]

deps.title: The Tangential Connection on a Submanifold of $\mathbb{R}^n$
deps.label: ex:tangential-connection-submanifold

deps.title: Properties of the Second Fundamental Form
deps.label: prop:second-fundamental-form-properties

entries.label: cor:gauss-formula-along-curve
entries.title: The Gauss Formula Along a Curve

entries.label: prop:weingarten-equation
entries.title: The Weingarten Equation

entries.label: thm:gauss-equation
entries.title: The Gauss Equation
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is an embedded Riemannian submanifold of a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(\widetilde{M}, \widetilde{g})$. For all $W, X, Y, Z \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, the following equation holds:
\[
\widetilde{Rm}(W, X, Y, Z) = Rm(W, X, Y, Z) - \langle \Pi(W, Z), \Pi(X, Y) \rangle + \langle \Pi(W, Y), \Pi(X, Z) \rangle.
\]

entries.label: prop:normal-connection-well-defined
entries.title: prop:normal-connection-well-defined
entries.body:
If $(M, g)$ is an embedded Riemannian submanifold of a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$, then $\nabla^\perp$ is a well-defined connection in $NM$, which is compatible with $\tilde{g}$ in the sense that for any two sections $N_1, N_2$ of $NM$ and every $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, we have
\[
X\langle N_1, N_2 \rangle = \langle \nabla^\perp_X N_1, N_2 \rangle + \langle N_1, \nabla^\perp_X N_2 \rangle.
\]

entries.label: thm:codazzi-equation
entries.title: The Codazzi Equation

entries.label: prop:geometric-interpretation-ii
entries.title: Geometric Interpretation of II
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is an embedded Riemannian submanifold of a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$, $p \in M$, and $v \in T_p M$.

(a) $\text{II}(v, v)$ is the $\tilde{g}$-acceleration at $p$ of the $g$-geodesic $\gamma_v$.

(b) If $v$ is a unit vector, then $|\text{II}(v, v)|$ is the $\tilde{g}$-curvature of $\gamma_v$ at $p$.

deps.title: The Gauss Formula Along a Curve
deps.label: cor:gauss-formula-along-curve

entries.label: lem:symmetric-bilinear-form-unit-vectors
entries.title: lem:symmetric-bilinear-form-unit-vectors
entries.body: Suppose $V$ is an inner product space, $W$ is a vector space, and $B, B': V \times V \to W$ are symmetric and bilinear. If $B(v,v) = B'(v,v)$ for every unit vector $v \in V$, then $B = B'$.

entries.label: prop:totally-geodesic-equivalence
entries.title: prop:totally-geodesic-equivalence
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is an embedded Riemannian submanifold of a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$. The following are equivalent:

(a) $M$ is totally geodesic in $\tilde{M}$.

(b) Every $g$-geodesic in $M$ is also a $\tilde{g}$-geodesic in $\tilde{M}$.

(c) The second fundamental form of $M$ vanishes identically.

entries.label: thm:fundamental-equations-hypersurface
entries.title: Fundamental Equations for a Hypersurface
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian hypersurface in a Riemannian manifold $(\bar{M}, \bar{g})$, and $N$ is a smooth unit normal vector field along $M$.

(a) \textsc{The Gauss Formula for a Hypersurface}: If $X, Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ are extended to an open subset of $\bar{M}$, then
\[
\bar{\nabla}_X Y = \nabla_X Y + h(X, Y)N.
\]

(b) \textsc{The Gauss Formula for a Curve in a Hypersurface}: If $\gamma: I \to M$ is a smooth curve and $X: I \to TM$ is a smooth vector field along $\gamma$, then
\[
\bar{D}_t X = D_t X + h(\gamma', X)N.
\]

(c) \textsc{The Weingarten Equation for a Hypersurface}: For every $X \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$,
\begin{equation}
\bar{\nabla}_X N = -sX.
\tag{8.11}
\end{equation}

(d) \textsc{The Gauss Equation for a Hypersurface}: For all $W, X, Y, Z \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$,
\[
\overline{Rm}(W, X, Y, Z) = Rm(W, X, Y, Z) - \tfrac{1}{2}(h \owedge h)(W, X, Y, Z).
\]

(e) \textsc{The Codazzi Equation for a Hypersurface}: For all $W, X, Y \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$,
\begin{equation}
\overline{Rm}(W, X, Y, N) = (Dh)(Y, W, X).
\tag{8.12}
\end{equation}

entries.label: lem:self-adjoint-eigenvector-extremum
entries.title: lem:self-adjoint-eigenvector-extremum
entries.body: Suppose $V$ is a finite-dimensional inner product space and $s: V \to V$ is a self-adjoint linear endomorphism. Let $C$ denote the set of unit vectors in $V$. There is a vector $v_0 \in C$ where the function $v \mapsto \langle sv, v \rangle$ achieves its maximum among elements of $C$, and every such vector is an eigenvector of $s$ with eigenvalue $\lambda_0 = \langle sv_0, v_0 \rangle$.

entries.label: prop:finite-dimensional-spectral-theorem
entries.title: Finite-Dimensional Spectral Theorem
entries.body: Suppose $V$ is a finite-dimensional inner product space and $s: V \to V$ is a self-adjoint linear endomorphism. Then $V$ has an orthonormal basis of $s$-eigenvectors, and all of the eigenvalues are real.

entries.label: prop:semigeodesic-coordinates-second-form
entries.title: prop:semigeodesic-coordinates-second-form
entries.body:
With notation as above, the components in $(x^1, \ldots, x^n)$-coordinates of the scalar second fundamental form, the shape operator, and the mean curvature of $M_a$ (all with respect to the unit normal $\partial_v$) are given by the following formulas:
\[
(h_a)_{\alpha\beta} = -\frac{1}{2} \partial_v g_{\alpha\beta}|_{v=a}, \qquad
(s_a)_\beta^\alpha = -\frac{1}{2}g^{\alpha\gamma}\partial_v g_{\gamma\beta}|_{v=a}, \qquad
H_a = -\frac{1}{2n}g^{\alpha\beta}\partial_v g_{\alpha\beta}|_{v=a}.
\]

entries.label: thm:area-minimizing-zero-mean-curvature
entries.title: thm:area-minimizing-zero-mean-curvature
entries.body: Let $M$ be a compact codimension-1 submanifold with nonempty boundary in an $(n+1)$-dimensional Riemannian manifold $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$. If $M$ is area-minimizing, then its mean curvature is identically zero.

entries.label: thm:constant-mean-curvature-isoperimetric
entries.title: thm:constant-mean-curvature-isoperimetric
entries.body: Suppose $(\tilde{M},\tilde{g})$ is a Riemannian $(n+1)$-manifold, $D \subseteq \tilde{M}$ is a compact regular domain, and $M = \partial D$. If $M$ has the smallest surface area among boundaries of compact regular domains with the same volume as $D$, then $M$ has constant mean curvature (computed with respect to the outward unit normal).

entries.label: prop:osculating-circle
entries.title: prop:osculating-circle
entries.body:
Suppose $\gamma: I \to \mathbb{R}^m$ is a unit-speed curve, $t_0 \in I$, and $\kappa(t_0) \neq 0$.

\emph{(a)} There is a unique unit-speed parametrized circle $c: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^m$, called the \emph{osculating circle at $\gamma(t_0)$}, with the property that $c$ and $\gamma$ have the same position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = t_0$.

\emph{(b)} The Euclidean curvature of $\gamma$ at $t_0$ is $\kappa(t_0) = 1/R$, where $R$ is the radius of the osculating circle.

entries.label: prop:second-fundamental-form-coordinate-formula
entries.title: prop:second-fundamental-form-coordinate-formula

entries.label: prop:principal-curvatures-local-shape
entries.title: prop:principal-curvatures-local-shape

entries.label: ex:shape-operators-spheres
entries.title: Shape Operators of Spheres
entries.body:
The function $F: \mathbb{R}^{n+1} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined by $F(x) = |x|^2$ is a smooth defining function for each sphere $S^n(R)$. The gradient of this function is $\text{grad } F = 2 \sum_i x^i \partial_i$, which has length $2R$ along $S^n(R)$. The smooth vector field
\[
N = \frac{1}{R} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} x^i \partial_i
\]
thus restricts to a unit normal along $S^n(R)$. (It is the outward pointing normal.) The shape operator is now easy to compute:
\[
s X = -\frac{1}{R} \sum_{i,j=1}^{n+1} X^j (\partial_j x^i) \partial_i = -\frac{1}{R} X.
\]

entries.label: thm:theorema-egregium
entries.title: Gauss's Theorema Egregium
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is an embedded 2-dimensional Riemannian submanifold of $\mathbb{R}^3$. For every $p \in M$, the Gaussian curvature of $M$ at $p$ is equal to one-half the scalar curvature of $g$ at $p$, and thus the Gaussian curvature is a local isometry invariant of $(M,g)$.

entries.label: cor:riemann-two-manifold-curvatures
entries.title: cor:riemann-two-manifold-curvatures
entries.body:
If $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian 2-manifold, the following relationships hold:
\[
Rm = \frac{1}{2}K g \owedge g, \quad Rc = Kg, \quad S = 2K.
\]

entries.label: prop:sectional-curvature-formula
entries.title: Formula for the Sectional Curvature
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and $p \in M$. If $v, w$ are linearly independent vectors in $T_p M$, then the sectional curvature of the plane spanned by $v$ and $w$ is given by
\[
\sec(v,w) = \frac{Rm_p(v,w,w,v)}{|v \wedge w|^2}.
\]

entries.label: prop:sectional-curvature-determines-curvature-tensor
entries.title: prop:sectional-curvature-determines-curvature-tensor
entries.body:
Suppose $R_1$ and $R_2$ are algebraic curvature tensors on a finite-dimensional inner product space $V$. If for every pair of linearly independent vectors $v, w \in V$,
\[
\frac{R_1(v, w, w, v)}{|v \wedge w|^2} = \frac{R_2(v, w, w, v)}{|v \wedge w|^2},
\]
then $R_1 = R_2$.

entries.label: prop:geometric-interpretation-ricci-scalar-curvatures
entries.title: Geometric Interpretation of Ricci and Scalar Curvatures

entries.label: lem:frame-homogeneous-constant-sectional-curvature
entries.title: lem:frame-homogeneous-constant-sectional-curvature
entries.body: If a Riemannian manifold $(M, g)$ is frame-homogeneous, then it has constant sectional curvature.

entries.label: thm:sectional-curvatures-model-spaces
entries.title: Sectional Curvatures of the Model Spaces

entries.label: prop:constant-sectional-curvature
entries.title: prop:constant-sectional-curvature
entries.body:
A Riemannian metric $g$ has constant sectional curvature $c$ if and only if its curvature tensor satisfies
\[
Rm = \frac{1}{2}c\, g \owedge g.
\]

In this case, the Ricci tensor and scalar curvature of $g$ are given by the formulas
\[
Rc = (n-1)cg; \quad S = n(n-1)c,
\]
and the Riemann curvature endomorphism is
\[
R(v,w)x = c(\langle w,x\rangle v - \langle v,x\rangle w).
\]

In terms of any basis,
\[
R_{ijkl} = c(g_{il}g_{jk} - g_{ik}g_{jl}); \quad R_{ij} = (n-1)cg_{ij}.
\]

entries.label: thm:rotation-index-theorem
entries.title: Rotation Index Theorem
entries.body: The rotation index of a positively oriented curved polygon in the plane is $+1$.

entries.label: lem:rotation-index-plus-one
entries.title: lem:rotation-index-plus-one
entries.body: If $M$ is an oriented Riemannian $2$-manifold, the rotation index of every positively oriented curved polygon in $M$ is $+1$.

entries.label: thm:gauss-bonnet-formula
entries.title: The Gauss--Bonnet Formula

entries.label: cor:angle-sum-theorem
entries.title: Angle-Sum Theorem
entries.body: The sum of the interior angles of a Euclidean triangle is $\pi$.

entries.label: cor:circumference-theorem
entries.title: Circumference Theorem
entries.body: The circumference of a Euclidean circle of radius $R$ is $2\pi R$.

entries.label: cor:total-curvature-theorem
entries.title: Total Curvature Theorem
entries.body:
If $\gamma : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}^2$ is a smooth, unit-speed, simple closed curve such that $\gamma'(a) = \gamma'(b)$, and $N$ is the inward-pointing normal, then
\[
\int_a^b \kappa_N(t) \, dt = 2\pi.
\]

entries.label: thm:gauss-bonnet-theorem
entries.title: The Gauss--Bonnet Theorem
entries.body:
If $(M, g)$ is a smoothly triangulated compact Riemannian 2-manifold, then
\[
\int_M K \, dA = 2\pi \chi(M),
\]
where $K$ is the Gaussian curvature of $g$ and $dA$ is its Riemannian density.

entries.label: cor:gaussian-curvature-and-topology
entries.title: cor:gaussian-curvature-and-topology

entries.label: cor:curvature-determines-cover
entries.title: cor:curvature-determines-cover

entries.label: thm:jacobi-equation
entries.title: The Jacobi Equation
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, let $\gamma$ be a geodesic in $M$, and let $J$ be a vector field along $\gamma$. If $J$ is the variation field of a variation through geodesics, then $J$ satisfies the following equation, called the \emph{Jacobi equation}:
\[
D_t^2 J + R(J, \gamma')\gamma' = 0. \quad (10.1)
\]

entries.label: prop:existence-uniqueness-jacobi-fields
entries.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Jacobi Fields
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold. Suppose $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is an interval, $\gamma : I \to M$ is a geodesic, $a \in I$, and $p = \gamma(a)$. For every pair of vectors $v, w \in T_p M$, there is a unique Jacobi field $J$ along $\gamma$ satisfying the initial conditions
\[
J(a) = v, \quad D_t J(a) = w.
\]

\textit{Proof.} Choose a parallel orthonormal frame $(E_i)$ along $\gamma$, and write $v = v^i E_i(a)$, $w = w^i E_i(a)$, and $\gamma'(t) = y^i(t) E_i(t)$ in terms of this frame. Writing an unknown vector field $J \in \mathfrak{X}(\gamma)$ as $J(t) = J^i(t) E_i(t)$, we can express the Jacobi equation as
\[
\ddot{J}^i(t) + R_{jki}^\ell(\gamma(t)) J^\ell(t) y^k(t) y^j(t) = 0.
\]

This is a system of $n$ linear second-order ODEs for the $n$ functions $J^i : I \to \mathbb{R}$. Making the substitution $W^i = \dot{J}^i$ converts it to the following equivalent first-order linear system for the $2n$ unknown functions $(J^i, W^i)$:
\[
\dot{J}^i(t) = W^i(t),
\]
\[
\dot{W}^i(t) = -R_{jki}^\ell(\gamma(t)) J^\ell(t) y^k(t) y^j(t).
\]

Then Theorem 4.31 guarantees the existence and uniqueness of a smooth solution on the whole interval $I$ with arbitrary initial conditions $J^i(a) = v^i$, $W^i(a) = w^i$. Since $D_t J(a) = \dot{J}^i(a) E_i(a) = W^i(a) E_i(a) = w$, it follows that $J(t) = J^i(t) E_i(t)$ is the desired Jacobi field. \qed

deps.title: Existence, Uniqueness, and Smoothness for Linear ODEs
deps.label: thm:linear-ode-existence-uniqueness

entries.label: cor:jacobi-space-dimension
entries.title: cor:jacobi-space-dimension
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold of dimension $n$, and $\gamma$ is any geodesic in $M$. Then $\mathfrak{J}(\gamma)$ is a $2n$-dimensional linear subspace of $\mathfrak{X}(\gamma)$.

\textit{Proof.} Because the Jacobi equation is linear, $\mathfrak{J}(\gamma)$ is a linear subspace of $\mathfrak{X}(\gamma)$. Let $p = \gamma(a)$ be any point on $\gamma$, and consider the linear map from $\mathfrak{J}(\gamma)$ to $T_p M \oplus T_p M$ by sending $J$ to $(J(a), D_t J(a))$. The preceding proposition says precisely that this map is bijective. \qed

deps.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Jacobi Fields
deps.label: prop:existence-uniqueness-jacobi-fields

entries.label: prop:jacobi-field-variation-field
entries.title: prop:jacobi-field-variation-field
entries.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, and let $\gamma : I \to M$ be a geodesic. If $M$ is complete or $I$ is a compact interval, then every Jacobi field along $\gamma$ is the variation field of a variation of $\gamma$ through geodesics.

entries.label: prop:local-isometry-invariance-jacobi-fields
entries.title: Local Isometry Invariance of Jacobi Fields
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ are Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifolds and $\varphi : M \to \tilde{M}$ is a local isometry. Let $\gamma : I \to M$ and $\tilde{\gamma} : I \to \tilde{M}$ be geodesics related by $\tilde{\gamma} = \varphi \circ \gamma$, and let $J \in \mathfrak{X}(\gamma)$, $\tilde{J} \in \mathfrak{X}(\tilde{\gamma})$ be related by $d\varphi_{\gamma(t)}(J(t)) = \tilde{J}(t)$ for all $t \in I$. Then $J$ is a Jacobi field if and only if $\tilde{J}$ is.

entries.label: prop:normal-jacobi-equivalent-conditions
entries.title: prop:normal-jacobi-equivalent-conditions

entries.label: cor:jacobi-field-decomposition
entries.title: cor:jacobi-field-decomposition

entries.label: lem:jacobi-field-variation
entries.title: lem:jacobi-field-variation
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ an interval containing $0$, and $\gamma : I \to M$ a geodesic. Suppose $J : I \to M$ is a Jacobi field such that $J(0) = 0$. If $M$ is geodesically complete or $I$ is compact, then $J$ is the variation field of the following variation of $\gamma$ through geodesics (Fig. 10.3):
$$\Gamma(s,t) = \exp_p(t(v + sw)), \quad (10.5)$$
where $p = \gamma(0)$, $v = \gamma'(0)$, and $w = D_t J(0)$.

entries.label: prop:jacobi-fields-vanishing-at-point
entries.title: Jacobi Fields Vanishing at a Point

entries.label: cor:jacobi-field-tangent-vectors
entries.title: cor:jacobi-field-tangent-vectors
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold and $U$ is a normal neighborhood of $p \in M$. For each $q \in U \setminus \{p\}$, every vector in $T_q M$ is the value of a Jacobi field $J$ along a radial geodesic such that $J$ vanishes at $p$.

deps.title: Jacobi Fields Vanishing at a Point
deps.label: prop:jacobi-fields-vanishing-at-point

entries.label: prop:jacobi-constant-curvature
entries.title: Jacobi Fields in Constant Curvature

entries.label: lem:metric-relation-euclidean
entries.title: On $\mathbb{R}^n \setminus \{0\}$, the metric $\bar{g}$ defined by (10.14) and the Euclidean metric $g$ are related by
entries.body:
\[
\bar{g} = dr^2 + r^2 \hat{g},
\]
where $r(x) = |x|$ is the Euclidean distance from the origin.

\begin{proof}
Example 2.24 observed that the map $\Phi : \mathbb{R}^+ \times_\rho S^{n-1} \to \mathbb{R}^n \setminus \{0\}$ given by
\[
\Phi(\rho,\omega) = \rho\omega
\]
is an isometry when $\mathbb{R}^+ \times_\rho S^{n-1}$ has the warped product metric $d\rho^2 \oplus \rho^2 \hat{g}$ and $\mathbb{R}^n \setminus \{0\}$ has the Euclidean metric (see also Problem 2-4). Because $\Phi^{-1}(x) = (r(x), \pi(x))$, this means that $\bar{g} = (\Phi^{-1})^*(d\rho^2 \oplus \rho^2 \hat{g}) = dr^2 + r^2 \hat{g}$. \quad$\square$
\end{proof}

entries.label: thm:constant-curvature-normal-coords
entries.title: Constant-Curvature Metrics in Normal Coordinates
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with constant sectional curvature $c$. Given $p \in M$, let $(x^i)$ be normal coordinates on a normal neighborhood $U$ of $p$; let $r$ be the radial distance function on $U$ defined by (6.4); and let $\hat{g}$ be the symmetric 2-tensor defined in $x$-coordinates by (10.14). On $U \setminus \{p\}$, the metric $g$ can be written
\[
g = dr^2 + s_c(r)^2 \hat{g},
\]
where $s_c$ is defined by (10.8).

\begin{proof}
Let $\bar{g}$ denote the Euclidean metric in $x$-coordinates, and let $g_c$ denote the metric defined by the formula on the right-hand side of (10.17). By the properties of normal coordinates, at points of $U \setminus \{p\}$, all three metrics $g$, $\bar{g}$, and $g_c$ make the radial vector field $\partial_r$ a unit vector orthogonal to the level sets of $r$. Thus we need only show that $g(w_1, w_2) = g_c(w_1, w_2)$ when $w_1, w_2$ are tangent to a level set of $r$, and by polarization it suffices to show that $g(w,w) = g_c(w,w)$ for every such vector $w$. Note that if $w$ is tangent to a level set $r = b$, then formulas (10.17) and (10.15) imply
\[
g_c(w,w) = s_c(b)^2 \hat{g}(w,w) = \frac{s_c(b)^2}{b^2} \bar{g}(w,w).
\]

Let $q \in U \setminus \{p\}$ and $w \in T_q M$, and assume that $w$ is tangent to the $r$-level set containing $q$. Let $b = d_g(p,q)$, and let $\gamma : [0,b] \to U$ be the unit-speed radial geodesic from $p$ to $q$, so the coordinate representation of $\gamma$ is
\[
\gamma(t) = \left(\frac{t}{b}q^1, \ldots, \frac{t}{b}q^n\right),
\]
where $(q^1,\ldots,q^n)$ is the coordinate representation of $q$. Let $J \in \mathfrak{X}(\gamma)$ be the vector field along $\gamma$ given by
\[
J(t) = \frac{t}{b} w^i \partial_i |_{\gamma(t)}, \qquad (10.18)
\]
where $w^i \partial_i|_q$ is the coordinate representation for $w$. By Proposition 10.10, $J$ is a Jacobi field satisfying $D_t J(0) = (1/b) w^i \partial_i|_p$, and it follows from the definition that $J(b) = w$. Because $J$ is orthogonal to $\gamma'$ at $p$ and $q$, it is normal by Proposition 10.7. Thus by Proposition 10.12,
\begin{align*}
|w|_g^2 &= |J(b)|_g^2 = s_c(b)^2 |D_t J(0)|_g^2 \\
&= s_c(b)^2 \frac{1}{b^2} \left|w^i \partial_i |_p\right|_g^2 = s_c(b)^2 \frac{1}{b^2} |w|_g^2 = |w|_{g_c}^2. \quad \square
\end{align*}
\end{proof}

entries.label: cor:local-uniqueness-constant-curvature
entries.title: Local Uniqueness of Constant-Curvature Metrics
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ and $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ be Riemannian manifolds of the same dimension with constant sectional curvature $c$. For all points $p \in M$, $\tilde{p} \in \tilde{M}$, there exist neighborhoods $U$ of $p$ and $\tilde{U}$ of $\tilde{p}$ and an isometry $\varphi: U \to \tilde{U}$.

\textit{Proof.} Choose $p \in M$ and $\tilde{p} \in \tilde{M}$, and let $U$ and $\tilde{U}$ be geodesic balls of small radius $\varepsilon$ around $p$ and $\tilde{p}$, respectively. Riemannian normal coordinates give maps $\psi: U \to B_\varepsilon(0) \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ and $\tilde{\psi}: \tilde{U} \to B_\varepsilon(0) \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$, under which both metrics are given by formula (10.17) on the complement of the origin (Fig.\ 10.6). At the origin, $g_{ij} = \tilde{g}_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$. Therefore $\tilde{\psi}^{-1} \circ \psi$ is the required local isometry. \quad $\square$

entries.label: cor:constant-curvature-warped-products
entries.title: Constant-Curvature Metrics as Warped Products
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with constant sectional curvature $c$, and $U$ is a geodesic ball of radius $b$ centered at $p \in M$. Then $U \setminus \{p\}$ is isometric to a warped product of the form $(0,b) \times_\rho S^{n-1}$, where $(0,b) \subset \mathbb{R}$ has the Euclidean metric, and $S^{n-1}$ is the unit sphere with the round metric $\tilde{g}$.

\textit{Proof.} By virtue of Theorem 10.14, we may consider $g$ to be a metric on the ball of radius $b$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ given by formula (10.17). Let $\Phi: (0,b) \times S^{n-1} \to U \setminus \{p\}$ and $\pi: \mathbb{R}^n \setminus \{0\} \to S^{n-1}$ be the maps defined by (10.16) and (10.13). Because $\pi \circ \Phi$ restricts to the identity on $\{\rho\} \times S^{n-1}$ for each fixed $\rho$, it follows that $\Phi^* \tilde{g} = \Phi^* \pi^* \tilde{g} = \tilde{g}$, and thus
\[
\Phi^* g = d\rho^2 \oplus s_c(\rho)^2 \tilde{g}. \quad \square
\]

entries.label: cor:polar-decomposition-integrals
entries.title: Polar Decomposition of Integrals
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with constant sectional curvature $c$, and $U$ is an open or closed geodesic ball of radius $b$ around a point $p \in M$. If $f: U \to \mathbb{R}$ is any bounded integrable function, then the integral of $f$ over $U$ can be expressed as
\[
\int_U f \, dV_g = \int_{S^{n-1}} \int_0^b f \circ \Phi(\rho, \omega) s_c(\rho)^{n-1} \, d\rho \, dV_{\tilde{g}},
\]
where $dV_g$ is the Riemannian density of $g$, and $\Phi: (0,b) \times S^{n-1} \to U \setminus \{p\}$ is defined in normal coordinates by (10.16).

\textit{Proof.} Because every geodesic ball is orientable, we might as well choose an orientation on $U$ and interpret $dV_g$ as a differential form. Since the boundary of a geodesic ball has measure zero, it does not matter whether $U$ is open or closed. Similarly, integrating over $U \setminus \{p\}$ instead of $U$ does not change the value of the integral. The claim therefore follows from Corollary 10.16 together with the result of Problem 2-15, which shows that the volume form of the warped product metric $d\rho^2 \oplus s_c(\rho)^2 \tilde{g}$ can be written $s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho \wedge dV_{\tilde{g}}$. \quad $\square$

entries.label: lem:locally-symmetric-cartan
entries.title: lem:locally-symmetric-cartan
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with parallel curvature tensor, and for some points $p, \tilde{p} \in M$ we are given a linear map $A: T_p M \to T_{\tilde{p}} M$ satisfying $A^*(g_{\tilde{p}}) = g_p$ and $A^*(\text{Rm}_{\tilde{p}}) = \text{Rm}_p$. Then there exist a neighborhood $U$ of $p$ and a local isometry $\varphi: U \to M$ such that $\varphi(p) = \tilde{p}$ and $d\varphi_p = A$. If $M$ is complete, then $U$ can be taken to be any normal neighborhood of $p$.

entries.label: thm:characterization-locally-symmetric-spaces
entries.title: Characterization of Locally Symmetric Spaces
entries.body:
A Riemannian manifold is a locally symmetric space if and only if its curvature tensor is parallel.

\begin{proof}
One direction is taken care of by Problem 7-3. To prove the converse, suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold with $\nabla Rm \equiv 0$. Let $p \in M$ be arbitrary, and let $U$ be a geodesic ball centered at $p$. The linear map $A = -\text{Id}: T_p M \to T_p M$ satisfies $A^* g_p = g_p$, and for all $w, x, y, z \in T_p M$, we have
\[
(A^* Rm_p)(w, x, y, z) = Rm_p(-w, -x, -y, -z) = Rm_p(w, x, y, z).
\]

It follows that $A = -\text{Id}$ satisfies the hypotheses of Lemma 10.18 with $p = \tilde{p}$, and thus there is a local isometry $\varphi: U \to M$ such that $\varphi(p) = p$ and $d\varphi_p = -\text{Id}$. Since a local isometry takes geodesics to geodesics, $\varphi(U)$ is also a geodesic ball centered at $p$ of the same radius as $U$, so $\varphi$ actually maps $U$ to $U$. If we take the radius of $U$ small enough, then $\varphi$ is an isometry from $U$ to itself.
\end{proof}

entries.label: prop:critical-points-conjugate
entries.title: Critical Points and Conjugacy
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, $p \in M$, and $v \in \xi_p \subseteq T_p M$. Let $\gamma = \gamma_v: [0, 1] \to M$ be the geodesic segment $\gamma(t) = \exp_p(tv)$, and let $q = \gamma(1) = \exp_p(v)$. Then $v$ is a critical point of $\exp_p$ if and only if $q$ is conjugate to $p$ along $\gamma$.

\begin{proof}
Suppose first that $v$ is a critical point of $\exp_p$. Then there is a nonzero vector $w \in T_v(T_p M)$ such that $d(\exp_p)_v(w) = 0$. Because $T_p M$ is a vector space, we can identify $T_v(T_p M)$ with $T_p M$ as usual and regard $w$ as a vector in $T_p M$. Let $\Gamma$ be the variation of $\gamma$ defined by (10.5), and let $J(t) = \partial_s \Gamma(0,t)$ be its variation field. We can compute $J(1)$ as follows:
$$J(1) = \partial_s \Gamma(0,1) = \frac{\partial}{\partial s}\bigg|_{s=0} \exp_p(v + sw) = d(\exp_p)_v(w) = 0.$$

Thus $J$ is a nontrivial Jacobi field vanishing at $t = 0$ and $t = 1$, so $q$ is conjugate to $p$ along $\gamma$.

Conversely, if $q$ is conjugate to $p$ along $\gamma$, then there is some nontrivial Jacobi field $J$ along $\gamma$ such that $J(0) = 0$ and $J(1) = 0$. Lemma 10.9 shows that $J$ is the variation field of a variation of $\gamma$ of the form (10.5) with $w = D_t J(0) \in T_p M$, and the computation in the preceding paragraph shows that $d(\exp_p)_v(w) = J(1) = 0$. Thus $v$ is a critical point for $\exp_p$. \qed
\end{proof}

entries.label: prop:two-point-boundary-problem
entries.title: The Two-Point Boundary Problem for Jacobi Fields
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold, and $\gamma: [a, b] \to M$ is a geodesic segment. The two-point boundary problem for Jacobi fields along $\gamma$ is uniquely solvable for every pair of vectors $v \in T_{\gamma(a)} M$ and $w \in T_{\gamma(b)} M$ if and only if $\gamma(a)$ and $\gamma(b)$ are not conjugate along $\gamma$.

\textit{Proof.} Problem 10-8. \qed

deps.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Jacobi Fields
deps.label: prop:existence-uniqueness-jacobi-fields

entries.label: thm:second-variation-formula
entries.title: Second Variation Formula
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian manifold. Let $\gamma: [a,b] \to M$ be a unit-speed geodesic segment, $\Gamma: J \times [a,b] \to M$ a proper variation of $\gamma$, and $V$ its variation field. The second variation of $L_g(\Gamma_s)$ is given by the following formula:
\begin{equation}
\frac{d^2}{ds^2}\bigg|_{s=0} L_g(\Gamma_s) = \int_a^b \left( \lvert D_t V^\perp \rvert^2 - Rm(V^\perp, \gamma', \gamma', V^\perp) \right) dt,
\tag{10.19}
\end{equation}
where $V^\perp$ is the normal component of $V$.

\begin{proof}
As usual, write $T = \partial_t\Gamma$ and $S = \partial_s\Gamma$, and let $(a_0, \ldots, a_k)$ be an admissible partition for $\Gamma$. We begin, as we did when computing the first variation formula, by restricting to a rectangle $J \times [a_{i-1}, a_i]$ where $\Gamma$ is smooth. From (6.3) we have, for every $s$,
\[
\frac{d}{ds} L_g(\Gamma_s|_{[a_{i-1},a_i]}) = \int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \frac{\langle D_t S, T \rangle}{\langle T, T \rangle^{1/2}} dt.
\]

Differentiating again with respect to $s$, and using the symmetry lemma and Proposition 7.5, we obtain
\begin{align*}
\frac{d^2}{ds^2} L_g(\Gamma_s|_{[a_{i-1},a_i]})
&= \int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \left( \frac{\langle D_s D_t S, T \rangle}{\langle T, T \rangle^{1/2}} + \frac{\langle D_t S, D_s T \rangle}{\langle T, T \rangle^{1/2}} - \frac{1}{2} \frac{\langle D_t S, T \rangle 2\langle D_s T, T \rangle}{\langle T, T \rangle^{3/2}} \right) dt \\
&= \int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \left( \frac{\langle D_t D_s S + R(S,T)S, T \rangle}{\lvert T \rvert} + \frac{\langle D_t S, D_t S \rangle}{\lvert T \rvert} - \frac{\langle D_t S, T \rangle^2}{\lvert T \rvert^3} \right) dt.
\end{align*}

Now restrict to $s = 0$, where $\lvert T \rvert = 1$:
\begin{equation}
\frac{d^2}{ds^2}\bigg|_{s=0} L_g(\Gamma_s|_{[a_{i-1},a_i]}) = \int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \left( \langle D_t D_s S, T \rangle - Rm(S, T, T, S) + \lvert D_t S \rvert^2 - \langle D_t S, T \rangle^2 \right) dt\bigg|_{s=0}.
\tag{10.20}
\end{equation}

Because $D_t T = D_t \gamma' = 0$ when $s = 0$, the first term in (10.20) can be integrated
as follows:
\begin{equation}
\int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \langle D_t D_s S, T \rangle \, dt = \int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \frac{d}{dt} \langle D_s S, T \rangle \, dt = \left. \langle D_s S, T \rangle \right|_{t=a_{i-1}}^{t=a_i}. \tag{10.21}
\end{equation}

Notice that $S(s,t) = 0$ for all $s$ at the endpoints $t = a_0 = a$ and $t = a_k = b$ because
$\Gamma$ is a proper variation, so $D_s S = 0$ there. Moreover, along the boundaries $\{t = a_i\}$
of the smooth regions, $D_s S = D_s(\partial_s \Gamma)$ depends only on the values of $\Gamma$ when
$t = a_i$, and it is smooth up to the line $\{t = a_i\}$ from both sides; therefore $D_s S$ is
continuous for all $(s,t)$. Thus when we insert (10.21) into (10.20) and sum over $i$,
the boundary contributions from the first term all cancel, and we get
\[
\left. \frac{d^2}{ds^2} L_g(\Gamma_s) \right|_{s=0} = \int_a^b \left( |D_t |^2 - \langle D_t S, T \rangle^2 - \mathrm{Rm}(S, T, T, S) \right) \, dt
\]
\begin{equation}
= \int_a^b \left( |D_t V|^2 - \langle D_t V, \gamma'^2 \rangle^2 - \mathrm{Rm}(V, \gamma', \gamma', V) \right) \, dt. \tag{10.22}
\end{equation}

Every vector field $V$ along $\gamma$ can be written uniquely as $V = V^T + V^\perp$, where
$V^T$ is tangential and $V^\perp$ is normal. Explicitly,
\[
V^T = \langle V, \gamma' \rangle \gamma'; \quad V^\perp = V - V^T.
\]

Because $D_t \gamma' = 0$, it follows that
\[
D_t(V^T) = \langle D_t V, \gamma' \rangle \gamma' = (D_t V)^T; \quad D_t(V^\perp) = (D_t V)^\perp.
\]

Therefore,
\[
|D_t V|^2 = |(D_t V)^T|^2 + |(D_t V)^\perp|^2 = \langle D_t V, \gamma' \rangle^2 + |D_t V^\perp|^2.
\]

Also, the fact that $\mathrm{Rm}(\gamma', \gamma', \cdot, \cdot) = \mathrm{Rm}(\cdot, \cdot, \gamma', \gamma') = 0$ implies
\[
\mathrm{Rm}(V, \gamma', \gamma', V) = \mathrm{Rm}(V^\perp, \gamma', \gamma', V^\perp).
\]

Substituting these relations into (10.22) gives (10.19). \qed
\end{proof}

entries.label: cor:second-variation-minimizing-geodesic
entries.title: cor:second-variation-minimizing-geodesic
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold. Let $\gamma: [a,b] \to M$ be a unit-speed geodesic, $\Gamma$ a proper normal variation of $\gamma$, and $V$ its variation field. The second variation of $L_g(\Gamma_s)$ is $I(V,V)$. If $\gamma$ is minimizing, then $I(V,V) \geq 0$ for every proper normal vector field along $\gamma$.

entries.label: prop:index-form-jacobi-equation
entries.title: prop:index-form-jacobi-equation
entries.body:
Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and let $\gamma: [a,b] \to M$ be a geodesic segment. For every pair of piecewise smooth normal vector fields $V, W$ along $\gamma$,
\[
I(V, W) = -\int_a^b \langle D_t^2 V + R(V, y')y', W \rangle \, dt
+ \langle D_t V, W \rangle \bigg|_{t=a}^{t=b} - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \langle \Delta_i D_t V, W(a_i) \rangle, \quad (10.24)
\]
where $(a_0, \ldots, a_k)$ is an admissible partition for $V$ and $W$, and $\Delta_i D_t V$ is the jump in $D_t V$ at $t = a_i$.

\textit{Proof.} On every subinterval $[a_{i-1}, a_i]$ where $V$ and $W$ are smooth,
\[
\frac{d}{dt} \langle D_t V, W \rangle = \langle D_t^2 V, W \rangle + \langle D_t V, D_t W \rangle.
\]

Thus, by the fundamental theorem of calculus,
\[
\int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \langle D_t V, D_t W \rangle \, dt = -\int_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i} \langle D_t^2 V, W \rangle \, dt + \langle D_t V, W \rangle \bigg|_{a_{i-1}}^{a_i}.
\]

Summing over $i$, and noting that $W$ is continuous at $t = a_i$ for $i = 1, \ldots, k-1$, we get (10.24). \qed

entries.label: cor:jacobi-field-index-form
entries.title: cor:jacobi-field-index-form
entries.body:
If $\gamma$ is a geodesic segment and $V$ is a proper normal piecewise smooth vector field along $\gamma$, then $I(V, W) = 0$ for every proper normal piecewise smooth vector field $W$ along $\gamma$ if and only if $V$ is a Jacobi field.

\textit{Proof.} Problem 10-11. \qed

entries.label: thm:no-minimize-past-conjugate
entries.title: Geodesics Do Not Minimize Past Conjugate Points
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and $p, q \in M$. If $\gamma$ is a unit-speed geodesic segment from $p$ to $q$ that has an interior conjugate point, then there exists a proper normal vector field $X$ along $\gamma$ such that $I(X, X) < 0$. Therefore, $\gamma$ is not minimizing.

entries.label: lem:jacobi-field-constant-pairing
entries.title: lem:jacobi-field-constant-pairing
entries.body: Let $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ be a geodesic segment, and suppose $J_1$ and $J_2$ are Jacobi fields along $\gamma$. Then $\langle D_t J_1(t), J_2(t) \rangle - \langle J_1(t), D_t J_2(t) \rangle$ is constant along $\gamma$.

entries.label: thm:geodesic-without-conjugate-points
entries.title: thm:geodesic-without-conjugate-points
entries.body: Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold. Suppose $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ is a unit-speed geodesic segment without interior conjugate points. If $V$ is any proper normal piecewise smooth vector field along $\gamma$, then $I(V,V) \geq 0$, with equality if and only if $V$ is a Jacobi field. In particular, if $\gamma(b)$ is not conjugate to $\gamma(a)$ along $\gamma$, then $I(V,V) > 0$.

entries.label: cor:conjugate-point-not-minimizing
entries.title: cor:conjugate-point-not-minimizing
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold, and let $\gamma : [a,b] \to M$ be a unit-speed geodesic segment.

(a) If $\gamma$ has an interior conjugate point, then it is not minimizing.

(b) If $\gamma(a)$ and $\gamma(b)$ are conjugate but $\gamma$ has no interior conjugate points, then for every proper normal variation $\Gamma$ of $\gamma$, the curve $\Gamma_s$ is strictly longer than $\gamma$ for all sufficiently small nonzero $s$ unless the variation field of $\Gamma$ is a Jacobi field.

(c) If $\gamma$ has no conjugate points, then for every proper normal variation $\Gamma$ of $\gamma$, the curve $\Gamma_s$ is strictly longer than $\gamma$ for all sufficiently small nonzero $s$. $\square$

deps.title: Geodesics Do Not Minimize Past Conjugate Points
deps.label: thm:no-minimize-past-conjugate

entries.title: Properties of Cut Times

entries.label: thm:cut-time-continuous
entries.title: thm:cut-time-continuous
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a complete, connected Riemannian manifold. The function $t_{\text{cut}}: UTM \to (0,\infty)$ is continuous.

\begin{proof}
Let $(p,v) \in UTM$ be arbitrary, and let $(p_i, v_i)$ be any sequence in $UTM$ converging to $(p,v)$. Put $c_i = t_{\text{cut}}(p_i, v_i)$, and
\[b = \liminf_{i \to \infty} c_i, \quad c = \limsup_{i \to \infty} c_i.\]

We will show that $c \leq l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) \leq b$, which implies $c_i \to l_{\text{cut}}(p,v)$.
To show that $c \leq l_{\text{cut}}(p,v)$, suppose first that $c < \infty$. By passing to a subsequence, we may assume that $c_i$ is finite for each $i$ and $c_i \to c$. Proposition 10.32 shows that $\gamma_{v_i}$ is minimizing on $[0, c_i]$. By continuity of the exponential map, $\exp(p_i, c_i v_i) \to \exp(p, cv)$ as $i \to \infty$, and therefore by continuity of the distance function we have
\[
d_g(p, \exp(p, cv)) = \lim_{i \to \infty} d_g(p_i, \exp(p_i, c_i v_i)) = \lim_{i \to \infty} c_i = c.
\]

This shows that $\gamma_v$ is minimizing on $[0, c]$, and therefore $l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) \geq c$, as claimed. Now suppose $c = \infty$. Again, by passing to a subsequence, we may assume $c_i \to \infty$. It follows that for every positive number $c_0$, the geodesic $\gamma_{v_i}$ is minimizing on $[0, c_0]$ for $i$ sufficiently large, and it follows by continuity as above that $\gamma_v$ is minimizing on $[0, c_0]$. Since $c_0$ was arbitrary, this means that $l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) = \infty$.

Next we show that $l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) \leq b$. If $b = \infty$, there is nothing to prove, so assume $b < \infty$. Again by passing to a subsequence, we may assume that $c_i$ is finite for each $i$ and $c_i \to b$. By virtue of Proposition 10.32, either there are infinitely many indices $i$ for which $\gamma_{v_i}(c_i)$ is conjugate to $p_i$ along $\gamma_{v_i}$, or there are infinitely many $i$ for which there exists a second minimizing unit-speed geodesic $\sigma_i$ from $p_i$ to $\gamma_{v_i}(c_i)$.

In the first case, because conjugate points are critical values of the restricted exponential map, which can be detected in coordinates by the vanishing of a determinant of a matrix of first derivatives, it follows by continuity that $\gamma_v(b)$ is also a critical value, and thus $\gamma_v(b)$ is conjugate to $p$ along $\gamma_v$. Then Theorem 10.26 shows that $l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) \leq b$.

In the second case, let $w_i$ be the unit vector in $T_{p_i} M$ such that $\sigma_i = \gamma_{w_i}$. Because the components of $w_i$ with respect to a local orthonormal frame lie in $S^{n-1}$, by passing to a subsequence we may assume $(p_i, w_i) \to (p, w)$. If $\gamma_v(b)$ is conjugate to $p$ along $\gamma_v$, then $l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) \leq b$ as above, so we may assume that $\gamma_v(b)$ is not a conjugate point. This means that $bw$ is a regular point of the restricted exponential map $\exp_p$. Since the set of such regular points is an open subset of $TM$, there is some $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $\exp_p$ is injective on the $\varepsilon$-neighborhood of $c_i$ for all $i$ sufficiently large. This implies that $|c_i w_i - c_i v_i|_g \leq \varepsilon$ for all such $i$, and therefore the limits $bw$ and $bv$ are distinct. Thus $\gamma_w|_{[0,b]}$ is another minimizing geodesic from $p$ to $\gamma_v(b)$, which by Proposition 10.32 implies that $l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) \leq b$. $\square$
\end{proof}

entries.label: thm:cut-locus-properties
entries.title: thm:cut-locus-properties
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a complete, connected Riemannian manifold and $p \in M$.

(a) The cut locus of $p$ is a closed subset of $M$ of measure zero.

(b) The restriction of $\exp_p$ to $\operatorname{ID}(\bar{p})$ is surjective.

(c) The restriction of $\exp_p$ to $\operatorname{ID}(p)$ is a diffeomorphism onto $M \setminus \operatorname{Cut}(p)$.

\begin{proof}
To prove that the cut locus is closed, suppose $(q_i)$ is a sequence of points in $\operatorname{Cut}(p)$ converging to some $q \in M$. Write $q_i = \exp_p(t_{\operatorname{cut}}(p, v_i)v_i)$ for unit vectors $v_i$. By compactness of the unit sphere, we may assume after passing to a subsequence that $v_i$ converges to some unit vector $v$, and by Theorem 10.33, $t_{\operatorname{cut}}(p, v) = \lim_{i \to \infty} t_{\operatorname{cut}}(p, v_i)$. Because convergent sequences in a metric space are bounded, the sequence $(t_{\operatorname{cut}}(p, v_i))$ is bounded, and therefore $t_{\operatorname{cut}}(p, v) < \infty$. By continuity of the exponential map, therefore, $q$ must be equal to $\exp_p(t_{\operatorname{cut}}(p, v))$, which shows that $q \in \operatorname{Cut}(p)$, and thus $\operatorname{Cut}(p)$ is closed.

To see that $\operatorname{Cut}(p)$ has measure zero, note first that in any polar coordinates $( \theta ^1, \ldots,  \theta ^{n-1}, r)$ on $T_p M$, the set $\operatorname{TCL}(p)$ can be expressed locally as the graph of the continuous function $r = t_{\operatorname{cut}}(p,  \theta ^1, \ldots,  \theta ^{n-1})$, using the fact that $( \theta ^1, \ldots,  \theta ^{n-1})$ form smooth local coordinates for the unit sphere in $T_p M$. Since graphs of continuous functions have measure zero (see, for example, [LeeSM, Prop. 6.3]), it follows that $\operatorname{TCL}(p)$ is a union of finitely many sets of measure zero and thus has measure zero in $T_p M$; and because smooth maps take sets of measure zero to sets of measure zero (see [LeeSM, Prop. 6.4]), $\operatorname{Cut}(p) = \exp_p(\operatorname{TCL}(p))$ has measure zero in $M$. This proves (a).

Part (b) follows from the fact that every point of $M$ can be connected to $p$ by a minimizing geodesic. To prove (c), note that it follows easily from the definitions that $\exp_p(\operatorname{ID}(p)) = M \setminus \operatorname{Cut}(p)$. Also, the definition of $\operatorname{ID}(p)$ guarantees that no point in $\exp_p(\operatorname{ID}(p))$ can be a cut point of $p$, and thus no such point can be a conjugate point either. The absence of cut points implies that $\exp_p$ is injective on $\operatorname{ID}(p)$, and the absence of conjugate points implies that it is a local diffeomorphism there. Together these two facts imply that it is a diffeomorphism onto its image. $\square$
\end{proof}

entries.label: prop:continuity-injectivity-radius
entries.title: prop:continuity-injectivity-radius
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a complete, connected Riemannian manifold. The function $\text{inj}: M \to (0, \infty]$ is continuous.

\begin{proof}
Let $p \in M$ be arbitrary. Proposition 10.32(b) shows that for each point $q \in \text{Cut}(p)$, there is a minimizing unit-speed geodesic $\gamma_v$ from $p$ to $q$ whose length is $l_{\text{cut}}(p,v)$, and therefore the distance from $p$ to $\text{Cut}(p)$ is the infimum of the cut times of unit-speed geodesics starting at $p$. By the previous proposition, therefore,
\[
\text{inj}(p) = \inf\{l_{\text{cut}}(p,v) : v \in T_pM \text{ with } |v|_g = 1\}.
\]

Suppose $(p_i)$ is a sequence in $M$ converging to a point $p \in M$. As in the proof of Theorem 10.33, we will prove that $\text{inj}(p_i) \to \text{inj}(p)$ by showing that $c \leq \text{inj}(p) \leq b$, where
\[
b = \liminf_{i \to \infty} \text{inj}(p_i), \quad c = \limsup_{i \to \infty} \text{inj}(p_i).
\]

First we show that $\text{inj}(p) \le b$. By passing to a subsequence, we may assume $\text{inj}(p_i) \to b$. By compactness of the unit sphere, for each $i$ there is a unit vector $v_i \in T_{p_i} M$ such that $\text{inj}(p_i) = \ell_{\text{cut}}(p_i, v_i)$, and after passing to a further subsequence, we may assume $(p_i, v_i) \to (p, v)$ for some $v \in T_p M$. By continuity of $\ell_{\text{cut}}$, we have $\ell_{\text{cut}}(p, v) = \lim_i \ell_{\text{cut}}(p_i, v_i) = b$, so $\text{inj}(p) \le b$.

Next we show that $\text{inj}(p) \ge c$. Once again, by passing to a subsequence of the original sequence $(p_i)$, we may assume $\text{inj}(p_i) \to c$. Suppose for the sake of contradiction that $\text{inj}(p) < c$, and choose $c_0$ such that $\text{inj}(p) < c_0 < c$. Let be a unit vector in $T_p M$ such that $\ell_{\text{cut}}(p, w) = \text{inj}(p)$. We can choose some sequence of unit vectors $w_i \in T_{p_i} M$ such that $(p_i, w_i) \to (p, w)$, so $\ell_{\text{cut}}(p_i, w_i) \to \ell_{\text{cut}}(p, w) = \text{inj}(p)$. For $i$ sufficiently large, this implies $\ell_{\text{cut}}(p_i, w_i) < c_0 < c$, contradicting the facts that $\ell_{\text{cut}}(p_i, w_i) \ge \text{inj}(p_i)$ and $\text{inj}(p_i) \to c$. \qed
\end{proof}

entries.label: thm:laplacian-comparison-1
entries.title: Laplacian Comparison 1
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian $n$-manifold whose sectional curvatures are all bounded above by a constant $c$. Suppose $p \in M$, $U$ is a normal neighborhood of $p$, $r$ is the radial distance function on $U$, and $s_c$ is defined as in Proposition 11.3. Then on $U_0 \setminus \{p\}$, we have
\[
\Delta r \geq (n-1) \frac{s'_c(r)}{s_c(r)},
\]
where $U_0 = U$ if $c \leq 0$, while $U_0 = \{q \in U : r(q) < \pi R\}$ if $c = 1/R^2 > 0$.

entries.label: thm:conjugate-point-comparison-I
entries.title: Conjugate Point Comparison I
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian $n$-manifold whose sectional curvatures are all bounded above by a constant $c$. If $c \leq 0$, then no point of $M$ has conjugate points along any geodesic. If $c = 1/R^2 > 0$, then there is no conjugate point along any geodesic segment shorter than $\pi R$.

Proof. The case $c \leq 0$ is covered by Problem 10-7, so assume $c = 1/R^2 > 0$. Let $\gamma : [0, b] \to M$ be a unit-speed geodesic segment, and suppose $J$ is a nontrivial normal Jacobi field along $\gamma$ that vanishes at $t = 0$. The Jacobi field comparison theorem implies that $|J(t)| \geq$ (constant) $\sin(t/R) > 0$ as long as $0 < t < \pi R$. $\square$

entries.label: lem:riemannian-volume-laplacian
entries.title: lem:riemannian-volume-laplacian
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Riemannian manifold and $(x^i)$ are Riemannian normal coordinates on a normal neighborhood $U$ of $p \in M$. Let $\det g$ denote the determinant of the matrix $(g_{ij})$ in these coordinates, let $r$ be the radial distance function, and let $\partial_r$ be the unit radial vector field. The following identity holds on $U \setminus \{p\}$:
\begin{equation}
\Delta r = \partial_r \log(r^{n-1} \sqrt{\det g}).
\tag{11.23}
\end{equation}

\begin{proof}
Corollary 6.10 to the Gauss lemma shows that the vector fields $\operatorname{grad} r$ and $\partial_r$ are equal on $U \setminus \{p\}$. Comparing the components of these two vector fields in normal coordinates, we conclude (using the summation convention as usual) that
\[
g^{ij} \partial_j r = \frac{x^i}{r}.
\]

Based on the formula for $\Delta r$ from Proposition 2.46, we compute
\[
\Delta r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}} \partial_i \left( g^{ij} \sqrt{\det g} \partial_j r \right)
\]
\[
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}} \partial_i \left( \frac{x^i}{r} \sqrt{\det g} \right)
\]
\[
= \partial_i \left( \frac{x^i}{r} \right) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g} r} \partial_i \sqrt{\det g}
\]
\[
= \frac{n-1}{r} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}} \partial_r \sqrt{\det g},
\]
where the first term in the last line follows by direct computation using $r = \left(\sum_i (x^i)^2\right)^{1/2}$. This is equivalent to (11.23).
\end{proof}

entries.label: thm:gunther-volume-comparison
entries.title: Günther's Volume Comparison
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a connected Riemannian $n$-manifold whose sectional curvatures are all bounded above by a constant $c$. Given $p \in M$, let $\delta_0 = \operatorname{inj}(p)$ if $c \le 0$, and $\delta_0 = \min(\pi R, \operatorname{inj}(p))$ if $c = 1/R^2 > 0$. For every positive number $\delta \le \delta_0$, let $V_g(\delta)$ denote the volume of the geodesic ball $B_\delta(p)$ in $(M,g)$, and let $V_c(\delta)$ denote the volume of a geodesic ball of radius $\delta$ in the $n$-dimensional Euclidean space, hyperbolic space, or sphere with constant sectional curvature $c$. Then for every $0 < \delta \le \delta_0$, we have
\[
V_g(\delta) \ge V_c(\delta), \quad (11.24)
\]
and the quotient $V_g(\delta)/V_c(\delta)$ is a nondecreasing function of $\delta$ that approaches $1$ as $\delta \searrow 0$. If equality holds in (11.24) for some $\delta \in (0, \delta_0]$, then $g$ has constant sectional curvature $c$ on the entire geodesic ball $B_\delta(p)$.

\begin{proof}
The volume estimate (11.24) follows easily from the metric comparison theorem, which implies that the determinants of the metrics $g$ and $g_c$ in normal coordinates satisfy $\sqrt{\det g} \ge \sqrt{\det g_c}$. If that were all we needed, we could stop here; but to prove the other statements, we need a more involved argument, which incidentally provides another proof of (11.24) that does not rely directly on the metric comparison theorem, and therefore can be adapted more easily to the case in which we have only an estimate of the Ricci curvature (see Thm.\ 11.19 below).

Let $(x^i)$ be normal coordinates on $B_{\delta_0}(p)$ (interpreted as all of $M$ if $\delta_0 = \infty$). Using these coordinates, we might as well consider $g$ to be a Riemannian metric on an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $p$ to be the origin. Let $\bar{g}$ denote the Euclidean metric in these coordinates, and let $g_c$ denote the constant-curvature metric in the same coordinates, given on the complement of the origin by (10.17).

The Laplacian comparison theorem together with Lemma 11.13 shows that
\[
\partial_r \log(r^{n-1}\sqrt{\det g}) = \Delta r \ge (n-1) \frac{s_c'(r)}{s_c(r)} = \partial_r \log(s_c(r)^{n-1}). \quad (11.25)
\]

Thus $\log(r^{n-1}\sqrt{\det g/s_c(r)^{n-1}})$ is a nondecreasing function of $r$ along each radial geodesic, and so is the ratio $r^{n-1}\sqrt{\det g/s_c(r)^{n-1}}$. To compute the limit as $r \searrow 0$, note that $g_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$ at the origin, so $\sqrt{\det g}$ converges uniformly to $1$ as $r \searrow 0$. Also, for every $c$, we have $s_c(r)/r \to 1$ as $r \searrow 0$, so $r^{n-1}\sqrt{\det g/s_c(r)^{n-1}} \to 1$.

We can write $dV_g = \sqrt{\det g}\, dV_{\bar{g}}$. Corollary 10.17 in the case $c=0$ shows that
\[
V_g(\delta) = \int_{S^{n-1}} \int_0^\delta (\sqrt{\det g}) \circ \Phi(\rho,\omega) \rho^{n-1} d\rho\, dV_{\bar{g}},
\]
where $\Phi(\rho,\omega) = \rho\omega$ for $\rho \in (0,\delta_0)$ and $\omega \in S^{n-1}$. The same corollary shows that
\[
V_c(\delta) = \int_{S^{n-1}} \int_0^\delta s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho\, dV_{\bar{g}} = \left(\int_0^\delta s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho\right) \left(\int_{S^{n-1}} dV_{\bar{g}}\right).
\]

Therefore,
\[
\frac{V_g(\delta)}{V_c(\delta)} = \frac{\int_{S^{n-1}} \int_0^\delta (\sqrt{\det g}) \circ \Phi(\rho,\omega) \rho^{n-1} d\rho\, dV_{\bar{g}}}{\left(\int_0^\delta s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho\right) \left(\int_{S^{n-1}} dV_{\bar{g}}\right)}
\]
\[
= \frac{1}{\operatorname{Vol}(S^{n-1})} \int_{S^{n-1}} \left(\frac{\int_0^\delta \lambda(\rho,\omega) s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho}{\int_0^\delta s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho}\right) dV_{\bar{g}}, \quad \text{(11.26)}
\]
where we have written
\[
\lambda(\rho,\omega) = \frac{(\sqrt{\det g}) \circ \Phi(\rho,\omega) \rho^{n-1}}{s_c(\rho)^{n-1}}.
\]

The argument above (together with the fact that $r \circ \Phi = \rho$) shows that $\lambda(\rho,\omega)$ is a nondecreasing function of $\rho$ for each $\omega$, which approaches $1$ uniformly as $\rho \searrow 0$. We need to show that the quotient in parentheses in the last line of (11.26) is also nondecreasing as $\delta$ increases. Suppose $0 < \delta_1 < \delta_2$. Because $\lambda$ is nondecreasing, we have
\[
\int_0^{\delta_1} \lambda(\rho,\omega) s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho \int_{\delta_1}^{\delta_2} s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho
\]
\[
\le \left(\int_0^{\delta_1} s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho\right) \lambda(\delta_1,\omega) \left(\int_{\delta_1}^{\delta_2} s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho\right)
\]
\[
\le \int_0^{\delta_1} s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho \int_{\delta_1}^{\delta_2} \lambda(\rho,\omega) s_c(\rho)^{n-1} d\rho,
\]
and therefore (suppressing the dependence of the integrands on $\rho$ and $\omega$ for brevity),
\[
\int_0^{\delta_1} \lambda s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho \int_0^{\delta_2} s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho
= \int_0^{\delta_1} \lambda s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho \left(\int_0^{\delta_1} s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho + \int_{\delta_1}^{\delta_2} s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho\right)
\]
\[
\le \int_0^{\delta_1} \lambda s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho \int_0^{\delta_1} s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho + \int_0^{\delta_1} s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho \int_{\delta_1}^{\delta_2} \lambda s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho
\]
\[
= \int_0^{\delta_1} s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho \int_0^{\delta_2} \lambda s_c^{n-1}\, d\rho.
\]

Evaluating (11.26) at $\delta_1$ and $\delta_2$ and inserting the inequality above shows that the ratio $V_g(\delta)/V_c(\delta)$ is nondecreasing as a function of $\delta$, and it approaches 1 as $\delta \searrow 0$ because $\lambda(\rho,\omega) \to 1$ as $\rho \searrow 0$. It follows that $V_g(\delta) \ge V_c(\delta)$ for all $\delta \in (0,\delta_0]$. It remains only to consider the case in which the volume ratio is equal to 1 for some $\delta$. If $\lambda(\rho,\omega)$ is not identically 1 on the set where $0 < \rho < \delta$, then it is strictly greater than 1 on a nonempty open subset, which implies that the volume ratio in (11.26) is strictly greater than 1; so $V_g(\delta) = V_c(\delta)$ implies $\lambda(\rho,\omega) \equiv 1$ on $(0,\delta) \times S^{n-1}$, and pulling back to $U$ via $\varphi^{-1}$ shows that $r^{n-1}\sqrt{\det g} = s_c(r)^{n-1}$ on $B_\delta(p)$. By virtue of (11.25), we have $\Delta r \equiv (n-1)s_c'(r)/s_c(r)$, or in other words, $\text{tr}(\mathcal{H}_r) = \text{tr}((s_c'(r)/s_c(r))\pi_r)$. It follows from the Hessian comparison theorem that the endomorphism field $\mathcal{H}_r - (s_c'(r)/s_c(r))\pi_r$ is positive semidefinite, so its eigenvalues are all nonnegative. Since its trace is zero, the eigenvalues must all be zero. In other words, $\mathcal{H}_r \equiv (s_c'(r)/s_c(r))\pi_r$ on the geodesic ball $B_\delta(p)$. It then follows from Proposition 11.3 that $g$ has constant sectional curvature $c$ on that ball. \quad $\square$
\end{proof}
