entries.label: thm:bishop-gromov-volume-comparison
entries.title: Bishop--Gromov Volume Comparison
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a connected Riemannian $n$-manifold, and suppose there is a constant
$c$ such that the Ricci curvature of $M$ satisfies $\text{Rc}(v,v) \geq (n-1)c$ for all unit vectors $v$. Let $p \in M$
be given, and for every positive number $\delta$, let $V_g(\delta)$ denote the volume of the metric
ball of radius $\delta$ about $p$ in $(M,g)$, and let $V_c(\delta)$ denote the volume of a metric ball
of radius $\delta$ in the $n$-dimensional Euclidean space, hyperbolic space, or sphere with
constant sectional curvature $c$. Then for every $0 < \delta \leq \operatorname{inj}(p)$, we have
\begin{equation}
  V_g(\delta) \leq V_c(\delta), \tag{11.32}
\end{equation}
and the quotient $V_g(\delta)/V_c(\delta)$ is a nonincreasing function of $\delta$ that approaches $1$ as
$\delta \searrow 0$. If $(M,g)$ is complete, the same is true for all positive $\delta$, not just $\delta \leq \operatorname{inj}(p)$.
In either case, if equality holds in (11.32) for some $\delta$, then $g$ has constant sectional
curvature on the entire metric ball of radius $\delta$ about $p$.

\begin{proof}
First consider $\delta \leq \operatorname{inj}(p)$, in which case a metric ball of radius $\delta$ in $M$ is
actually a geodesic ball. With the exception of the first and last paragraphs, the proof
of Theorem 11.14 goes through with all of the inequalities reversed, and with the
first Laplacian comparison theorem replaced by its counterpart Theorem 11.15, to
show that $V_g(\delta)/V_c(\delta)$ is a nonincreasing function of $\delta$ that approaches $1$ as $\delta \searrow 0$,
and (11.32) follows.

In case $M$ is complete, $\exp_p$ is defined and smooth on all of $T_pM$. Theorem
10.34 shows that $\operatorname{Cut}(p)$ has measure zero in $M$ and $\exp_p$ maps the open subset
$\operatorname{ID}(p) \subseteq T_pM$ diffeomorphically onto the complement of $\operatorname{Cut}(p)$ in $M$. Therefore,
for every $\delta > 0$, the metric ball of radius $\delta$ is equal to $\exp_p\left(B_\delta(0) \cap \operatorname{ID}(p)\right)$ up to
a set of measure zero, where $B_\delta(0)$ denotes the $\delta$-ball about $0$ in $T_pM$. Using an
orthonormal basis to identify $(T_pM, g_p)$ with $(\mathbb{R}^n, \tilde{g})$, we can compute the volume
of a metric $\delta$-ball as
\[
  V_g(\delta) = \int_{\operatorname{ID}(p) \cap B_\delta(0)} \exp_p^* dV_g = \int_{\operatorname{ID}(p) \cap B_\delta(0)} \sqrt{\det g} \, dV_{\tilde{g}},
\]
where $\det g$ denotes the determinant of the matrix of $g$ in the normal coordinates
determined by the choice of basis.

Let $\Phi: \mathbb{R}^+ \times S^{n-1} \to T_pM \setminus \{0\} \cong \mathbb{R}^n \setminus \{0\}$ be the map $\Phi(\rho,\omega) = \rho\omega$ as in
Corollary 10.17, and define $\tilde{s}_c: \mathbb{R}^+ \to [0,\infty)$ by $\tilde{s}_c(\rho) = s_c(\rho)$ if $c \leq 0$, while in
the case $c = 1/R^2 > 0$,
\[
\tilde{s}_c(\rho) = \begin{cases}
s_c(\rho), & \rho < \pi R, \\
0, & \rho \geq \pi R.
\end{cases}
\]

Corollary 11.18 shows that the cut time of every unit vector in $T_p M$ is less than or equal to $\pi R$. Thus $\tilde{s}_c(\rho) > 0$ whenever $\Phi(\rho,\omega) \in \operatorname{ID}(p)$, and we can define $\tilde{\lambda}: \mathbb{R}^+ \times S^{n-1} \to \mathbb{R}$ by
\[
\tilde{\lambda}(\rho,\omega) = \begin{cases}
\frac{(\sqrt{\det g}) \circ \Phi(\rho,\omega)\rho^{n-1}}{\tilde{s}_c(\rho)^{n-1}}, & \Phi(\rho,\omega) \in \operatorname{ID}(p), \\
0, & \Phi(\rho,\omega) \notin \operatorname{ID}(p),
\end{cases}
\]
and just as in the proof of Theorem 11.14, we can write
\begin{align}
V_g(\delta) &= \int_{S^{n-1}} \int_0^\delta \tilde{\lambda}(\rho,\omega)\tilde{s}_c(\rho)^{n-1} \, d\rho \, dV_{\bar{g}}, \\
V_c(\delta) &= \int_{S^{n-1}} \int_0^\delta \tilde{s}_c(\rho)^{n-1} \, d\rho \, dV_{\bar{g}}.
\end{align}

The arguments of Theorem 11.14 (with inequalities reversed) show that for each $\omega \in S^{n-1}$, the function $\tilde{\lambda}(\rho,\omega)$ is nonincreasing in $\rho$ for all positive $\rho$, and it follows just as in that proof that $V_g(\delta)/V_c(\delta)$ (now interpreted as a ratio of volumes of metric balls) is nonincreasing for all $\delta > 0$ and approaches 1 as $\delta \searrow 0$, and (11.32) follows.

Finally, suppose $V_g(\delta) = V_c(\delta)$ for some $\delta > 0$, and assume first that $\delta < \operatorname{inj}(p)$. An argument exactly analogous to the one at the end of the proof of Theorem 11.14 shows that $\lambda(\rho,\omega) \equiv 1$ everywhere on the set where $0 < \rho < \delta$. Combined with Lemma 11.13, this implies that
\begin{equation}
\Delta r = (n-1)\frac{s'_c(r)}{s_c(r)} \tag{11.33}
\end{equation}
everywhere on $B_\delta(p) \setminus \{p\}$. This means that along each unit-speed radial geodesic $\gamma$, the function $u(t) = (\Delta r)|_{\gamma(t)}/(n-1) = s'_c(t)/s_c(t)$ satisfies $u' + u^2 + c \equiv 0$ by direct computation. Comparing this to (11.30), we conclude that
\[
\frac{\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{H}_r^2) + \operatorname{Rc}(\gamma', \gamma')}{n-1} = c
\]
on $B_\delta(p) \setminus \{p\}$. Since $\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{H}_r^2) \geq 0$ and $\operatorname{Rc}(\gamma', \gamma') \geq (n-1)c$ everywhere, this is possible only if $\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{H}_r^2)$ vanishes identically there. Because $\mathcal{H}_r^2$ is positive semidefinite and its trace is zero, it must vanish identically, which by definition of $\mathcal{H}_r$ means
\[
\mathcal{H}_r = \frac{\Delta r}{n-1}\pi_r = \frac{s_c'(r)}{s_c(r)}\pi_r.
\]

Proposition 11.3 then shows that $g$ has constant sectional curvature $c$ on $B_\delta(p)$.
Now suppose $(M,g)$ is complete. The argument of Theorem 11.14 then shows that $\tilde{\lambda}(\rho,\omega) = 1$ everywhere on the set where $0 < \rho < \delta$ and $\tilde{s}_c(\rho) > 0$. In view of the definition of $\tilde{\lambda}$, this implies in particular that $\mathrm{ID}(p) \cap B_\delta(0)$ contains all of the points in $B_\delta(0)$ where $\tilde{s}_c(r) > 0$. In case $c \le 0$, $\tilde{s}_c(r) = s_c(r) > 0$ everywhere, so $\mathrm{ID}(p) \cap B_\delta(0) = B_\delta(0)$ and therefore the metric ball of radius $\delta$ around $p$ is actually a geodesic ball, and the argument above applies.

In case $c = 1/R^2 > 0$, if $\delta \le \pi R$, then $\tilde{s}_c(r) = s_c(r) > 0$ on $B_\delta(0)$, and once again we conclude that the metric $\delta$-ball is a geodesic ball. On the other hand, if $\delta > \pi R$, then the diameter comparison theorem (Cor.\ 11.18) shows that the metric ball of radius $\delta$ is actually the entire manifold. The fact that the volume ratio is nonincreasing implies that $V_g(\pi R) = V_c(\pi R)$, and the argument above shows that $g$ has constant sectional curvature $c$ on the metric ball of radius $\pi R$. Since the closure of that ball is all of $M$, the result follows by continuity.
\end{proof}

entries.label: cor:ricci-volume-comparison
entries.title: cor:ricci-volume-comparison
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a compact Riemannian manifold and there is a positive constant $c = 1/R^2$ such that the Ricci curvature of $M$ satisfies $\mathrm{Rc}(v,v) \ge (n-1)c$ for all unit vectors $v$. Then the volume of $M$ is no greater than the volume of the $n$-sphere of radius $R$ with its round metric, and if equality holds, then $(M,g)$ has constant sectional curvature $c$.

entries.label: lem:uniqueness-analytic-continuation
entries.title: Uniqueness of Analytic Continuation

entries.label: thm:monodromy-theorem-local-isometries
entries.title: Monodromy Theorem for Local Isometries
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ and $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ be connected Riemannian manifolds. Suppose $U$ is a neighborhood of a point $p \in M$ and $\varphi : U \to \tilde{M}$ is a local isometry that can be analytically continued along every path starting at $p$. If $\gamma_0, \gamma_1 : [0,1] \to M$ are path-homotopic paths from $p$ to a point $q \in M$, then the analytic continuations along $\gamma_0$ and $\gamma_1$ agree in a neighborhood of $q$.

entries.label: cor:global-isometry-from-local-simply-connected
entries.title: cor:global-isometry-from-local-simply-connected
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ and $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ be simply connected, complete Riemannian manifolds. Suppose $U$ is a connected neighborhood of a point $p \in M$ and $\varphi : U \to \tilde{M}$ is a local isometry that can be analytically continued along every path starting at $p$. Then there is a global isometry $\Phi : M \to \tilde{M}$ whose restriction to $U$ agrees with $\varphi$.

entries.label: thm:killing-hopf
entries.title: Killing-Hopf
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a complete, simply connected Riemannian $n$-manifold with constant sectional curvature, $n \geq 2$. Then $M$ is isometric to one of the model spaces $\mathbb{R}^n$, $S^n(R)$, and $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$.

\begin{proof}
Given $(M,g)$ satisfying the hypotheses, let $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ be the Euclidean space, sphere, or hyperbolic space with the same constant sectional curvature as $M$. Note that $\tilde{M}$ is simply connected in each case. Let $p \in M$ be arbitrary. Corollary 10.15 shows that there exist an open subset $V \subseteq M$ containing $p$, an open subset $\tilde{V} \subseteq \tilde{M}$, and an isometry $\varphi : V \to \tilde{V}$. If we can show that $\varphi$ can be analytically continued along every path starting at $p$, then we can conclude from Corollary 12.3 that there exists a global isometry $\Phi : M \to \tilde{M}$, thus proving the theorem.

To that end, let $\gamma : [0,1] \to M$ be a path starting at $p$. Corollary 10.15 shows that for each $t \in [0, 1]$ there is a neighborhood of $\gamma(t)$ that is isometric to an open subset of $\tilde{M}$. After shrinking the neighborhoods if necessary, we may assume by Theorem 6.17 that each such neighborhood is a convex geodesic ball. By compactness, we can choose a partition $(t_0, \ldots, t_{k+1})$ of $[0,1]$ and a finite sequence of such balls $B_0, \ldots, B_k$ such that $\gamma([t_i, t_{i+1}]) \subseteq B_i$ for each $i = 0, \ldots, k$. After shrinking $B_0$ and adding more points to the partition if necessary, we can also assume $B_0 \subseteq V$.

We wish to construct local isometries $\psi_i : B_i \to \tilde{M}$ such that successive ones agree on overlaps. Begin with $\psi_0 = \varphi|_{B_0}$. By our choice of $B_1$, there is some isometry $\beta_1$ from $B_1$ to an open subset of $\tilde{M}$. Note that $\beta_0(B_0) \cap \beta_1(B_1)$ is an intersection of geodesically convex sets and thus is connected, and it is nonempty because it contains $\gamma(t_1)$. The composition $\psi_0 \circ \beta_1^{-1}$ is an isometry from $\beta_1(B_0 \cap B_1)$ to $\psi_0(B_0 \cap B_1)$, both of which are connected open subsets of $\tilde{M}$, so by Problem 5-11(c), it is the restriction of a global isometry $\Psi : \tilde{M} \to \tilde{M}$ (Fig. 12.2). Let $\psi_1 = \Psi \circ \beta_1 : B_1 \to \tilde{M}$. Our choice of $\Psi$ guarantees that $\psi_1|_{B_0 \cap B_1} = \psi_0|_{B_0 \cap B_1}$.

Proceeding similarly by induction, we can define $\psi_i : B_i \to \tilde{M}$ for $i = 2, \ldots, k$, such that $\psi_i$ agrees with $\psi_{i-1}$ on $B_i \cap B_{i-1}$.

Now we define the analytic continuation of $\varphi$ as follows: start with $(U_t, \varphi_t) = (B_0, \psi_0)$ for $0 \leq t \leq t_1$, and thereafter let $(U_t, \varphi_t) = (B_i, \psi_i)$ for $t_i < t \leq t_{i+1}$. It is easy to verify that this family of maps does the job.
\end{proof}

entries.label: cor:characterization-constant-curvature
entries.title: Characterization of Constant-Curvature Manifolds
entries.body:
The complete, connected, $n$-dimensional Riemannian manifolds of constant sectional curvature are, up to isometry, exactly the Riemannian quotients of the form $\tilde{M}/\Gamma$, where $\tilde{M}$ is one of the constant-curvature model spaces $\mathbb{R}^n$, $S^n(R)$, or $\mathbb{H}^n(R)$, and $\Gamma$ is a discrete subgroup of $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M})$ that acts freely on $\tilde{M}$.

Proof. First suppose $(M, g)$ is a complete, connected Riemannian $n$-manifold with constant sectional curvature, and let $\pi : M \to M$ be its universal covering manifold with the pullback metric $\tilde{g} = \pi^* g$. The preceding theorem shows that $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is isometric to one of the model spaces, so we might as well take it to be one of the models. Proposition C.20 shows that the group $\Gamma$ of covering automorphisms acts freely and properly on $\tilde{M}$, and Corollary 2.33 shows that $M$ is isometric to $\tilde{M}/\Gamma$. Moreover, if $\varphi$ is any covering automorphism, then $\pi \circ \varphi = \pi$, and so $\varphi^*\tilde{g} = \varphi^* \pi^* g = \pi^* g = \tilde{g}$; thus $\Gamma$ acts isometrically, so it is a subgroup of $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M})$.

To show that $\Gamma$ is discrete, suppose $\{\varphi_i\} \subseteq \Gamma$ is an infinite set with an accumulation point in $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M})$. (Note that Problem 5-11 shows that $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is a Lie group acting smoothly on $\tilde{M}$ in each case, so it makes sense to talk about the topology of the isometry group.) Since the action of $\Gamma$ is free, for every point $\tilde{p} \in \tilde{M}$ the set $\{\varphi_i(\tilde{p})\}$ is infinite, and by continuity of the action it has an accumulation point in $\tilde{M}$. But this is impossible, since the points $\{\varphi_i(\tilde{p})\}$ all project to the same point in $M$ under the covering map $\pi$, and so form a closed discrete set. Thus $\Gamma$ is discrete in $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M})$.

Conversely, suppose $\tilde{M}$ is one of the model spaces and $\Gamma$ is a discrete subgroup of $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M})$ that acts freely on $\tilde{M}$. Then $\Gamma$ is a closed Lie subgroup of $\text{Iso}(\tilde{M})$ by Proposition C.9, so the result of Problem 6-28 shows that it acts properly on $\tilde{M}$. It then follows from Proposition 2.32 that $\tilde{M}/\Gamma$ is a smooth manifold and has a unique Riemannian metric such that the quotient map $\pi : \tilde{M} \to \tilde{M}/\Gamma$ is a Riemannian covering, and $\tilde{M}/\Gamma$ is complete by Corollary 6.24. Since a Riemannian covering is in particular a local isometry, $\tilde{M}/\Gamma$ has constant sectional curvature. \qed

entries.label: thm:parallel-riemann-curvature-implies-symmetry
entries.title: thm:parallel-riemann-curvature-implies-symmetry
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a complete, simply connected Riemannian manifold with parallel Riemann curvature tensor. Then $M$ is a symmetric space.

\textbf{Proof.} Let $p$ be an arbitrary point of $M$. Theorem 10.19 shows that $M$ is locally symmetric, so there is a point reflection $\varphi: U \to U$ defined on some connected neighborhood $U$ of $p$. If we can show that $\varphi$ can be analytically continued along every path starting at $p$, then Corollary 12.3 shows that $\varphi$ extends to a global isometry $\Phi: M \to M$; because it agrees with $\varphi$ on $U$, it satisfies $d\Phi_p = d\varphi_p = -\text{Id}$, so it is a point reflection.

Let $\gamma : [0,1] \to M$ be a path starting at $p$. By compactness, we can find a partition $(t_0, \ldots, t_{k+1})$ of $[0,1]$ and convex geodesic balls $B_0, \ldots, B_k$ such that $\gamma([t_i, t_{i+1}]) \subseteq B_i$ for each $i = 0, \ldots, k$, with $B_0$ chosen small enough that $B_0 \subseteq U$. As in the proof of Theorem~12.4, we will construct local isometries $\psi_i : B_i \to M$ such that successive ones agree on overlaps.

Begin with $\psi_0 = \varphi|_{B_0}$, and let $p_1 = \gamma(t_1)$ and $q_1 = \psi_0(p_1)$. Because $\psi_0$ is a local isometry, the linear map $d\psi_0|_{p_1} : T_{p_1}M \to T_{q_1}M$ satisfies $(d\psi_0|_{p_1})^* \bar{g}_{q_1} = \bar{g}_{p_1}$ and $(d\psi_0|_{p_1})^*\operatorname{Rm}_{\bar{g}} = \operatorname{Rm}_{\bar{g}_{p_1}}$. Lemma~10.18 then shows that there is a local isometry $\psi_1 : B_1 \to M$ that satisfies $\psi_1(p_1) = \psi_0(p_1)$ and $d\psi_1|_{p_1} = d\psi_0|_{p_1}$, and it follows from Proposition~5.22 that $\psi_1$ and $\psi_0$ agree on the connected open set $B_0 \cap B_1$. Proceeding by induction, we obtain local isometries $\psi_i : B_i \to M$ satisfying $\psi_i|_{B_{i-1}\cap B_i} = \psi_{i-1}|_{B_{i-1}\cap B_i}$. Then the analytic continuation of $\varphi$ is defined just as in the last paragraph of the proof of Theorem~12.4, thus completing the proof.

entries.label: cor:symmetric-space-equivalence
entries.title: cor:symmetric-space-equivalence

entries.label: thm:cartan-hadamard
entries.title: Cartan--Hadamard
entries.body: If $(M, g)$ is a complete, connected Riemannian manifold with nonpositive sectional curvature, then for every point $p \in M$, the map $\exp_p : T_p M \to M$ is a smooth covering map. Thus the universal covering space of $M$ is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$, and if $M$ is simply connected, then $M$ itself is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$.

entries.label: prop:basic-properties-cartan-hadamard
entries.title: Basic Properties of Cartan--Hadamard Manifolds
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Cartan--Hadamard manifold.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item The injectivity radius of $M$ is infinite.
\item The image of every nonconstant maximal geodesic in $M$ is a line.
\item Any two distinct points in $M$ are contained in a unique line.
\item Every open or closed metric ball in $M$ is a geodesic ball.
\item For every point $q \in M$, the function $r(x) = d_g(q, x)$ is smooth on $M \setminus \{q\}$ and $r(x)^2$ is smooth on all of $M$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: prop:geodesic-triangle-inequalities
entries.title: prop:geodesic-triangle-inequalities
entries.body:
Suppose $\triangle ABC$ is a geodesic triangle in a Cartan--Hadamard manifold $(M,g)$, and let $a, b, c$ denote the lengths of the segments opposite the vertices $A, B,$ and $C,$ respectively. The following inequalities hold:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] $c^2 \geq a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos \angle C$.
\item[(b)] $\angle A + \angle B + \angle C \leq \pi$.
\end{enumerate}
If the sectional curvatures of $g$ are all strictly negative, then strict inequality holds in both cases.

\textit{Proof.} Problem~12-7. \qed

entries.label: cor:no-simply-connected-compact-nonpositive
entries.title: cor:no-simply-connected-compact-nonpositive
entries.body: No simply connected compact manifold admits a metric of nonpositive sectional curvature. \qed

entries.label: cor:product-manifolds-nonpositive
entries.title: cor:product-manifolds-nonpositive
entries.body:
Suppose $M$ and $N$ are positive-dimensional compact, connected smooth manifolds, at least one of which is simply connected. Then $M \times N$ does not admit any Riemannian metric of nonpositive sectional curvature.

\textit{Proof.} Suppose for the sake of contradiction that $M$ is simply connected and $g$ is a metric on $M \times N$ with nonpositive sectional curvature. If $\tilde{N}$ is the universal covering manifold of $N$, then there is a universal covering map $\pi : M \times \tilde{N} \to M \times N$, and $\pi^* g$ is a complete metric of nonpositive sectional curvature on $M \times \tilde{N}$. The Cartan--Hadamard theorem shows that $M \times \tilde{N}$ is diffeomorphic to a Euclidean space.

Since $M$ is simply connected, it is orientable (Cor.~B.19). Let $\mu$ be a smooth orientation $m$-form for $M$, where $m = \dim M$. Then $\mu$ is closed because there are no nontrivial $(m+1)$-forms on $M$, but Stokes's theorem shows that $\mu$ is not exact, because $\int_M \mu > 0$. On the other hand, if we let $p : M \times \tilde{N} \to M$ denote the projection on the first factor, then the form $p^* \mu$ is exact on $M \times \tilde{N}$, because every closed $m$-form on a Euclidean space is exact.

Choose an arbitrary point $y_0 \in \tilde{N}$ and define $\sigma : M \to M \times \tilde{N}$ by $\sigma(x) = (x, y_0)$; it is a diffeomorphism onto its image $\sigma(M) = M \times \{y_0\}$, which is a compact embedded submanifold of $M \times \tilde{N}$. By diffeomorphism invariance of the integral,
\[
\int_{\sigma(M)} p^* \mu = \int_M \sigma^*(p^*\mu) = \int_M (\rho \circ \sigma)^*\mu = \int_M \mu > 0,
\]
because $p \circ \sigma = \text{Id}$. But $p^*\mu$ is exact on $M \times \tilde{N}$, which implies $\int_{\sigma(M)} p^*\mu = 0$, a contradiction. \qed

entries.label: ex:positive-curvature-on-product-spheres
entries.title: ex:positive-curvature-on-product-spheres
entries.body: By the preceding corollary, every metric on $S^m \times S^n$ must have positive sectional curvature somewhere, provided $m \geq 2$ and $n \geq 1$, because both spheres are compact and $S^m$ is simply connected. \qed

entries.label: cor:nonpositive-implies-aspherical
entries.title: cor:nonpositive-implies-aspherical
entries.body: If $M$ is a smooth manifold that admits a metric of nonpositive sectional curvature, then $M$ is aspherical.

entries.label: lem:geodesic-convexity-distance-squared
entries.title: lem:geodesic-convexity-distance-squared

entries.label: lem:unique-minimal-enclosing-ball
entries.title: lem:unique-minimal-enclosing-ball
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Cartan-Hadamard manifold and $S$ is a compact subset of $M$ containing more than one point. Then there is a unique closed ball of minimum radius containing $S$.

Proof. Because $S$ is compact, it is bounded. Let $\delta = \text{diam}(S)$, and let $q_0$ be any point of $S$, so that $S \subseteq \bar{B}_\delta(q_0)$. Let $c_0$ be the infimum of the radii of closed metric balls containing $S$, and let $(\bar{B}_{c_i}(p_i))$ be a sequence of closed balls containing $S$ such that $c_i \searrow c_0$ as $i \to \infty$. For $i$ large enough that $c_i < 2\delta$, the fact that $q_0 \in S \subseteq \bar{B}_{c_i}(p_i)$ implies $d_g(p_i, q_0) \leq c_i < 2\delta$, so each of the points $p_i$ lies in the compact ball $\bar{B}_{2\delta}(q_0)$. After passing to a subsequence, we may assume that $p_i$ converges to some point $p_0 \in M$.

To show that $S \subseteq \bar{B}_{c_0}(p_0)$, suppose $q \in S$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$ be arbitrary. If $i$ is large enough that $d_g(p_i, p_0) \leq \varepsilon/2$ and $c_i \leq c_0 + \varepsilon/2$, then
\[
d_g(q, p_0) \leq d_g(q, p_i) + d_g(p_i, p_0) \leq c_i + \varepsilon/2 \leq c_0 + \varepsilon.
\]

Since $\varepsilon$ was arbitrary, this shows that $d_g(q, p_0) \leq c_0$, and thus $S \subseteq \bar{B}_{c_0}(p_0)$.

Now we need to show that $\bar{B}_{c_0}(p_0)$ is the unique closed ball of radius $c_0$ containing $S$. Suppose to the contrary that $\bar{B}_{c_0}(p_0')$ is another such ball. Let $\gamma : [0, 1] \to M$ be the geodesic segment from $p_0$ to $p_0'$, and let $m = \gamma(1/2)$ be its midpoint. Let $q \in S$ be arbitrary, and let $f : M \to [0, \infty)$ be the function $f(x) = \frac{1}{2}d_g(q, x)^2$. Lemma 12.15 implies that
\[
\frac{1}{2}d_g(q, m)^2 = f(m) < \frac{1}{2}f(p_0) + \frac{1}{2}f(p_0') = \frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{2}c_0^2) + \frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{2}c_0^2) = \frac{1}{2}c_0^2.
\]

Thus for every $q \in S$, we have $d_g(q, m) < c_0$. Since $S$ is compact, the continuous function $d_g(\cdot, m)$ takes on a maximum value $b_0 < c_0$ on $S$, showing that $S \subseteq \bar{B}_{b_0}(m)$. This contradicts the fact that $c_0$ is the minimum radius of a ball containing $S$.

entries.label: thm:cartans-fixed-point
entries.title: Cartan's Fixed-Point Theorem
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a Cartan--Hadamard manifold and $G$ is a compact Lie group acting smoothly and isometrically on $M$. Then $G$ has a fixed point in $M$, that is, a point $p_0 \in M$ such that $\varphi \cdot p_0 = p_0$ for all $\varphi \in G$.

entries.label: cor:cartans-torsion-theorem
entries.title: Cartan's Torsion Theorem
entries.body: Suppose $(M,g)$ is a complete, connected Riemannian manifold with nonpositive sectional curvature. Then $\pi_1(M)$ is torsion-free.

entries.label: thm:preissman-theorem
entries.title: Preissman
entries.body: If $(M, g)$ is a compact, connected Riemannian manifold with strictly negative sectional curvature, then every nontrivial abelian subgroup of $\pi_1(M)$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}$.

entries.label: ex:axial-isometries
entries.title: Axial Isometries
entries.body: In $\mathbb{R}^2$ with its Euclidean metric, every nontrivial translation $(x, y) \mapsto (x + a, y + b)$ is axial, and every line parallel to the vector $a\partial_x + b\partial_y$ is an axis. In the upper half-plane model of the hyperbolic plane, every dilation $(x, y) \mapsto (cx, cy)$ for $c \neq 1$ is axial, and the geodesic $\gamma(t) = (0, e^t)$ is an axis. On the other hand, the horizontal translations $(x, y) \mapsto (x + a, y)$ are not axial for the hyperbolic plane, because no geodesic is invariant under such an isometry. //

entries.label: lem:covering-automorphism-axis
entries.title: lem:covering-automorphism-axis
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a compact, connected Riemannian manifold, and $\pi : \widetilde{M} \to M$ is its universal covering manifold endowed with the metric $\widetilde{g} = \pi^* g$. Then every covering automorphism of $\pi$ has an axis, which restricts to a lift of a closed geodesic in $M$ that is the shortest admissible path in its free homotopy class.

Proof. Let $\varphi$ be any nontrivial covering automorphism of $\pi$, so it is also an isometry of $(\widetilde{M}, \widetilde{g})$. Every continuous path $\widetilde{\sigma} : [0, 1] \to \widetilde{M}$ from a point $\widetilde{x} \in \widetilde{M}$ to its image $\varphi(\widetilde{x})$ projects to a loop $\sigma = \pi \circ \widetilde{\sigma}$ in $M$. We begin by showing that the set of all loops obtained from $\varphi$ in this way is exactly one free homotopy class. Suppose $\sigma_0, \sigma_1 : [0, 1] \to M$ are two such loops, so that $\sigma_i = \pi \circ \widetilde{\sigma}_i$ for each $i$, where $\widetilde{\sigma}_i$ is a path in $\widetilde{M}$ from $\widetilde{x}_i$ to $\varphi(\widetilde{x}_i)$.

For $i = 0, 1$, let $x_i = \pi(\widetilde{x}_i)$, so $\sigma_i$ is a loop based at $x_i$. Choose a path $\widetilde{\alpha}$ from $\widetilde{x}_0$ to $\widetilde{x}_1$, and let $\widetilde{\beta} = \varphi \circ \widetilde{\alpha}$, which is a path from $\varphi(\widetilde{x}_0)$ to $\varphi(\widetilde{x}_1)$, and $\alpha = \pi \circ \widetilde{\alpha} = \pi \circ \widetilde{\beta}$, a path from $x_0$ to $x_1$ in $M$. Because $\widetilde{M}$ is simply connected, the two paths $\widetilde{\alpha}^{-1} \circ \widetilde{\sigma}_0 \circ \widetilde{\beta}$ and $\widetilde{\sigma}_1$ from $\widetilde{x}_1$ to $\varphi(\widetilde{x}_1)$ are path-homotopic, and therefore so are their images $\alpha^{-1} \circ \sigma_0 \circ \alpha$ and $\sigma_1$. An easy argument shows that the loops $\alpha^{-1} \circ \sigma_0 \circ \alpha$ and $\sigma_0$ are freely homotopic, by a homotopy that gradually shrinks the ``tail'' $\alpha$ to a point, so it follows that $\sigma_0$ and $\sigma_1$ are freely homotopic.



Conversely, let $\sigma_0$ and $\sigma_1$ be freely homotopic loops based at $x_0$ and $x_1$ in $M$, respectively, and suppose one of them, say $\sigma_0$, is of the form $\sigma_0 = \pi \circ \tilde{\sigma}_0$ for some path $\tilde{\sigma}_0$ from a point $\tilde{x}_0$ to $\varphi(\tilde{x}_0)$ in $\tilde{M}$. We need to show that $\sigma_1$ is also of this form. The fact that the loops are freely homotopic means that there is a homotopy $H : [0,1] \times [0,1] \to M$ satisfying $H(s,0) = \sigma_0(s)$, $H(s,1) = \sigma_1(s)$, and $H(0,t) = H(1,t)$ for all $t$. Let $\alpha : [0,1] \to M$ be the path $\alpha(t) = H(0,t)$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$. The existence of such a homotopy implies that the two loops $\alpha^{-1} \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot \alpha$ and $\sigma_1$ based at $x_1$ are path-homotopic (see [LeeTM, Lemma 7.17]). By the monodromy theorem (Prop.~A.54(c)), their lifts starting at $\tilde{x}_1$ both end at the same point. Let $\tilde{\sigma}_1$ be the lift of $\sigma_1$ starting at the point $\tilde{x}_1 = \tilde{\alpha}(1)$, let $\tilde{\alpha}$ be the lift of $\alpha$ starting at $\tilde{x}_0$, and let $\tilde{\beta} = \varphi \circ \tilde{\alpha}$. Then $\tilde{\alpha}^{-1} \cdot \tilde{\sigma}_0 \cdot \tilde{\beta}$ is a lift of $\alpha^{-1} \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot \alpha$ starting at $\tilde{x}_1$, and it ends at $\tilde{\beta}(1) = \varphi(\tilde{x}_1)$. The monodromy theorem therefore implies that $\tilde{\sigma}_1$ is a path from $\tilde{x}_1$ to $\varphi(\tilde{x}_1)$, as claimed.

We have shown that the covering automorphism $\varphi$ determines a unique free homotopy class in $M$. It is not the trivial class, because that class contains the constant loop, which cannot be the image of a path from a point $\tilde{x}$ to $\varphi(\tilde{x})$ because $\varphi$ has no fixed points. Since $M$ is compact, Proposition~6.28 shows that there is a closed geodesic $\gamma : [0,1] \to M$ that is the shortest admissible path in this free homotopy class. This means, in particular, that there is a lift $\tilde{\gamma}$ of $\gamma$ that satisfies $\tilde{\gamma}(1) = \varphi(\tilde{\gamma}(0))$. Because $\tilde{M}$ is complete, $\tilde{\gamma}$ extends to a geodesic in $\tilde{M}$ defined on all of $\mathbb{R}$. Let $\tilde{\gamma}_0, \tilde{\gamma}_1 : \mathbb{R} \to \tilde{M}$ be the geodesics defined by $\tilde{\gamma}_0(t) = \varphi \circ \tilde{\gamma}(t)$ and $\tilde{\gamma}_1(t) = \tilde{\gamma}(t + 1)$. Then $\tilde{\gamma}_0(0) = \tilde{\gamma}(0) = \tilde{\gamma}_1(0)$, and



\begin{align*}
d\pi_{\gamma(1)}(\tilde{\gamma}_0(0)) &= d\pi_{\gamma(1)} \circ d\varphi_{\gamma(0)}(\tilde{\gamma}'(0)) \quad \text{(definition of $\tilde{\gamma}_0$)} \\
&= d\pi_{\gamma(0)}(\tilde{\gamma}'(0)) \quad \text{(because $\pi \circ \varphi = \pi$)} \\
&= \gamma'(0) \quad \text{(because $\pi \circ \tilde{\gamma} = \gamma$)} \\
&= \gamma'(1) \quad \text{($\gamma$ is a closed geodesic)} \\
&= d\pi_{\gamma(1)}(\tilde{\gamma}'(1)) \quad \text{($\pi \circ \tilde{\gamma} = \gamma$ again)} \\
&= d\pi_{\gamma(1)}(\tilde{\gamma}_1(0)) \quad \text{(definition of $\tilde{\gamma}_1$)}.
\end{align*}

The fact that $d\pi_{\gamma(1)}$ is injective then implies $\tilde{\gamma}_0(0) = \tilde{\gamma}_1(0)$, so by uniqueness of geodesics we have $\tilde{\gamma}_0 \equiv \tilde{\gamma}_1$, or in other words $\varphi(\tilde{\gamma}(t)) = \tilde{\gamma}(t+1)$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Thus $\tilde{\gamma}$ is an axis for $\varphi$.

entries.label: lem:unique-axis-cartan-hadamard
entries.title: lem:unique-axis-cartan-hadamard
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a Cartan--Hadamard manifold with strictly negative sectional curvature. If $\varphi : M \to M$ is an axial isometry, then its axis is unique up to reparametrization.

\textbf{Proof.} Let $\varphi : M \to M$ be an axial isometry, and suppose $\gamma_1, \gamma_2 : \mathbb{R} \to M$ are both axes for $\varphi$. After reparametrizing the geodesics if necessary, we can assume that $\varphi$ translates both geodesics by time 1.

Suppose first that the two axes do not intersect. Then the points $A = \gamma_1(0)$, $B = \gamma_1(1) = \varphi(A)$, $C = \gamma_2(0)$, and $D = \gamma_2(1) = \varphi(C)$ are all distinct (Fig. 12.4). Let $\sigma$ be the geodesic segment from $A$ to $C$, so that $\varphi \circ \sigma$ is the geodesic segment from $B$ to $D$, forming a ``geodesic quadrilateral'' $ABDC$. Because $\varphi$ preserves angles, the angles $\angle CAB$ and $\angle ABD$ are supplementary, as are $\angle ACD$ and $\angle CDB$. Thus the angle sum of $ABDC$ is exactly $2\pi$.

On the other hand, Proposition 12.10 shows that the geodesic triangles $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle BCD$ have angle sums strictly less than $\pi$. The angle $\angle ACD$ is no larger than the sum $\angle ACB + \angle BCD$: one way to see this is to note that Problem 6-2 shows that the angle between two unit vectors $v, w \in T_C M$, namely $\arccos(v, w)$, is exactly equal to the Riemannian distance between $v$ and $w$ regarded as points on the unit sphere in $TC M$ with its round metric, so we can apply the triangle inequality for distances on the sphere. Similarly, $\angle ABD \le \angle ABC + \angle CBD$. Thus the sum of the four angles of $ABDC$ is strictly less than $2\pi$, which is a contradiction.

It follows that $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ must intersect at some point, say $x = \gamma_1(t_1) = \gamma_2(t_2)$. But then $\varphi(x) = \gamma_1(t_1 + 1) = \gamma_2(t_2 + 1)$ is another intersection point, so Proposition 12.9(c) shows that $\gamma_2$ must be a reparametrization of $\gamma_1$.

entries.label: cor:no-product-negative-sectional-curvature
entries.title: cor:no-product-negative-sectional-curvature
entries.body: No product of positive-dimensional connected compact manifolds admits a metric of strictly negative sectional curvature.

entries.label: thm:myers-positive-ricci
entries.title: Myers
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a complete, connected Riemannian $n$-manifold, and suppose there is a positive constant $R$ such that the Ricci curvature of $M$ satisfies $\text{Rc}(v,v) \geq (n-1)/R^2$ for all unit vectors $v$. Then $M$ is compact, with diameter less than or equal to $\pi R$, and its fundamental group is finite.

Proof. The diameter comparison theorem (Cor. 11.18) shows that the diameter of $M$ is no greater than $\pi R$. To show that $M$ is compact, we just choose a base point $p$ and note that every point in $M$ can be connected to $p$ by a geodesic segment of length at most $\pi R$. Therefore, $\exp_p : \bar{B}_{\pi R}(0) \to M$ is surjective, so $M$ is the continuous image of a compact set.

To prove the statement about the fundamental group, let $\pi : \tilde{M} \to M$ denote the universal covering space of $M$, with the metric $\tilde{g} = \pi^* g$. Then $(\tilde{M}, \tilde{g})$ is complete by Corollary 6.24, and $\tilde{g}$ also has Ricci curvature satisfying the same lower bound, so $\tilde{M}$ is compact by the argument above. By Proposition A.61, for every $p \in M$ there is a one-to-one correspondence between $\pi_1(M,p)$ and the fiber $\pi^{-1}(p)$. If $\pi_1(M,p)$ were infinite, therefore, $\pi^{-1}(p)$ would be an infinite discrete closed subset of $\tilde{M}$, contradicting the compactness of $\tilde{M}$. Thus $\pi_1(M,p)$ is finite. \qed

entries.label: cor:positive-ricci-finite-fundamental-group
entries.title: cor:positive-ricci-finite-fundamental-group
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a compact, connected Riemannian $n$-manifold whose Ricci tensor is positive definite everywhere. Then $M$ has finite fundamental group.

Proof. Because the unit tangent bundle of $M$ is compact by Proposition 2.9, the hypothesis implies that there is a positive constant $c$ such that $\text{Rc}(v,v) \geq c$ for all unit tangent vectors $v$. Thus the hypotheses of Myers's theorem are satisfied with $(n-1)/R^2 = c$. \qed

deps.title: Properties of the Unit Tangent Bundle
deps.label: prop:unit-tangent-bundle-properties

entries.label: cor:einstein-positive-scalar-curvature-compact
entries.title: cor:einstein-positive-scalar-curvature-compact
entries.body: If $(M,g)$ is a complete Einstein manifold with positive scalar curvature, then $M$ is compact.

entries.label: thm:chengs-maximal-diameter
entries.title: Cheng's Maximal Diameter Theorem
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ satisfies the hypotheses of Myers's theorem and $\operatorname{diam}(M) = \pi R$. Then $(M, g)$ is isometric to $(S^n(R), \bar{g}_R)$.

entries.label: thm:milnor-polynomial-growth
entries.title: Milnor
entries.body: Suppose $(M, g)$ is a complete, connected Riemannian $n$-manifold with nonnegative Ricci curvature. Every finitely generated subgroup of $\pi_1(M)$ has polynomial growth of order at most $n$.

entries.label: cor:free-fund-group-no-ric-metric
entries.title: cor:free-fund-group-no-ric-metric
entries.body: If $M$ is a connected smooth manifold whose fundamental group is free on more than one generator, then $M$ admits no complete Riemannian metric with nonnegative Ricci curvature.

entries.label: thm:synge-positive-sectional
entries.title: Synge
entries.body:
Suppose $(M, g)$ is a compact, connected Riemannian $n$-manifold with strictly positive sectional curvature.
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] If $n$ is even and $M$ is orientable, then $M$ is simply connected.
\item[(b)] If $n$ is odd, then $M$ is orientable.
\end{enumerate}

\textit{Proof.} Suppose for the sake of contradiction that the appropriate hypothesis holds but the conclusion is false. Let $\pi: \tilde{M} \to M$ be the universal covering manifold of $M$ with the pullback metric $\tilde{g} = \pi^* g$. We can give $\tilde{M}$ the pullback orientation in the even-dimensional case; in the odd-dimensional case, just choose an orientation on $\bar{M}$. In either case, there is a nontrivial covering automorphism $\varphi : \bar{M} \to \bar{M}$; in the even-dimensional case, it is orientation-preserving, and in the odd-dimensional case we can choose it to be orientation-reversing. By Lemma 12.21, there is a geodesic $\bar{\gamma} : \mathbb{R} \to \bar{M}$ that is an axis for $\varphi$, and $\gamma = \pi \circ \bar{\gamma}$ restricts to a closed geodesic in $M$ that minimizes length in its free homotopy class. After reparametrizing if necessary, we may assume that $\varphi(\bar{\gamma}(t)) = \bar{\gamma}(t + 1)$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Let $\bar{x}_0 = \bar{\gamma}(0)$, $\bar{x}_1 = \bar{\gamma}(1) = \varphi(\bar{x}_0)$, and $x = \pi(\bar{x}_0) = \pi(\bar{x}_1)$.

Let $\bar{P} : T_{\bar{x}_0}\bar{M} \to T_{\bar{x}_1}\bar{M}$ be parallel transport along $\bar{\gamma}|_{[0,1]}$, and let $P : T_x M \to T_x M$ be parallel transport along $\gamma|_{[0,1]}$. Because local isometries preserve parallelism, the following diagram commutes, and all four maps are linear isometries:



\[
\begin{array}{ccc}
T_{\bar{x}_0}\bar{M} & \xrightarrow{\bar{P}} & T_{\bar{x}_1}\bar{M} \\
\big\downarrow d\pi_{\bar{x}_0} & & \big\downarrow d\pi_{\bar{x}_1} \\
T_x M & \xrightarrow{P} & T_x M
\end{array}
\]

Because $d V_g$ applied to a parallel frame is constant, $\bar{P}$ is orientation-preserving. In the even-dimensional case, the vertical maps are both orientation-preserving, so $P$ is too. In the odd-dimensional case, the fact that $d\pi_{\bar{x}_0} = d\pi_{\bar{x}_1} \circ d\varphi_{\bar{x}_0}$ implies that the two vertical maps induce opposite orientations on $T_x M$, so $P$ is orientation-reversing.

The upshot is that in each case, $P$ is a linear isometry of $T_x M$ whose determinant is $(-1)^n$. Note that the determinant is the product of the eigenvalues of $P$, counted with multiplicities. Since the real eigenvalues of a linear isometry are $\pm 1$, and the others come in conjugate pairs whose product is 1, the equation $\det P = (-1)^n$ implies that the multiplicity of $-1$ has the same parity as $n$, and therefore the multiplicity of $+1$ must be even. We know that $\gamma'(0)$ is a $+1$-eigenvector, so there must be another independent $+1$-eigenvector $v \in T_x M$, which we can take to be orthogonal to $\gamma'(0)$. Extending $v$ by parallel transport yields a nontrivial parallel normal vector field $V \in \mathfrak{X}(\gamma)$ satisfying $V(0) = V(1)$.

Consider the variation of $\gamma$ defined by $\Gamma(s,t) = \exp_{\gamma(t)}(sV(t))$. Because $V(0) = V(1)$, this is a variation through admissible loops. The first variation formula (6.1) shows that the first derivative of $L_g(\Gamma_s)$ at $s = 0$ is zero, because $\gamma$ is a geodesic and the boundary terms cancel. We can then apply the general version of the second variation formula given in Problem 10-9, with the image of the geodesic $s \mapsto \exp_s(sv)$ playing the role of $M_1$ and $M_2$. Because this is a totally geodesic submanifold, its second fundamental form vanishes, and we obtain

\[
\frac{d^2}{ds^2}\bigg|_{s=0} L_g(\Gamma_s) = \int_a^b \left(|D_t V|^2 - \operatorname{Rm}(V, \gamma', \gamma', V)\right) dt.
\]

Because $V$ is parallel along $\gamma$ and $\sec(V,\gamma') > 0$ for all $t$, this second derivative is strictly negative, which implies that $L_g(\Gamma_s) < L_g(\gamma)$ for sufficiently small $s$. But this contradicts the fact that $\gamma$ minimizes length in its free homotopy class. \qed

entries.label: cor:fundamental-groups-even-dimension
entries.title: cor:fundamental-groups-even-dimension
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a compact, connected, even-dimensional Riemannian manifold with strictly positive sectional curvature. If $M$ is orientable, then $\pi_1(M)$ is trivial, and if not, then $\pi_1(M) \cong \mathbb{Z}/2$.

\textbf{Proof.} The orientable case is part of Synge's theorem. If $M$ is nonorientable, it has a two-sheeted orientable covering manifold $\widetilde{M}$; Synge's theorem shows that $\widetilde{M}$ is simply connected, so it is the universal covering space of $M$. Because each fiber of the universal covering has the same cardinality as $\pi_1(M)$, it follows that $\pi_1(M)$ is isomorphic to the two-element group $\mathbb{Z}/2$. \qed

entries.label: thm:sphere-theorem
entries.title: The Sphere Theorem
entries.body: A complete, simply connected, Riemannian $n$-manifold that is strictly $\frac{1}{4}$-pinched is homeomorphic to $S^n$.

entries.label: thm:soul-theorem
entries.title: The Soul Theorem
entries.body: If $(M, g)$ is a complete, connected, noncompact Riemannian manifold with nonnegative sectional curvature, then there exists a compact totally geodesic submanifold $S \subseteq M$ (called a \textit{soul} of $M$) such that $M$ is diffeomorphic to the normal bundle of $S$ in $M$. If $M$ has strictly positive sectional curvature, then $S$ is a point and $M$ is diffeomorphic to a Euclidean space.

entries.label: thm:splitting-theorem
entries.title: The Splitting Theorem
entries.body: If $(M, g)$ is a complete, connected, noncompact Riemannian manifold with nonnegative Ricci curvature, and $M$ contains a line, then there is a Riemannian manifold $(N, h)$ with nonnegative Ricci curvature such that $M$ is isometric to the Riemannian product $N \times \mathbb{R}$.

entries.label: thm:hamilton-3-manifold
entries.title: Hamilton's 3-Manifold Theorem
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a simply connected compact Riemannian $3$-manifold with positive definite Ricci curvature. Then $M$ is diffeomorphic to $S^3$.

entries.label: thm:hamilton-4-manifold
entries.title: Hamilton's 4-Manifold Theorem
entries.body: Suppose $M$ is a simply connected compact Riemannian $4$-manifold with positive curvature operator. Then $M$ is diffeomorphic to $S^4$.

entries.label: thm:bohm-wilking
entries.title: Böhm--Wilking
entries.body: Every simply connected compact Riemannian manifold with positive curvature operator is diffeomorphic to a sphere.

entries.label: thm:differentiable-sphere-theorem
entries.title: Differentiable Sphere Theorem
entries.body: Let $(M, g)$ be a compact, simply connected Riemannian manifold of dimension $n \geq 4$. If $M$ is strictly pointwise $\frac{1}{4}$-pinched, then it is diffeomorphic to $S^n$ with its standard smooth structure.

entries.label: prop:topological-manifolds-locally-path-connected-compact
entries.title: prop:topological-manifolds-locally-path-connected-compact

entries.label: prop:topological-manifolds-paracompact
entries.title: prop:topological-manifolds-paracompact
entries.body:
}
Every topological manifold with or without boundary is paracompact.

entries.label: prop:continuous-open-closed-maps
entries.title: prop:continuous-open-closed-maps

entries.label: lem:closed-map-lemma
entries.title: Closed Map Lemma
entries.body:
}
If $F$ is a continuous map from a compact space to a Hausdorff space, then $F$ is a closed map.

entries.label: prop:proper-continuous-maps-closed
entries.title: Proper Continuous Maps Are Closed
entries.body:
}
Suppose $M$ is a topological space, $N$ is a locally compact Hausdorff space, and $F : M \to N$ is continuous and proper. Then $F$ is a closed map.

entries.label: prop:induced-homomorphisms
entries.title: Induced Homomorphisms
entries.body:
Suppose $M$ and $N$ are topological spaces and $p \in M$. If $F: M \to N$ is a continuous
map, then the map $F_*: \pi_1(M, p) \to \pi_1(N, F(p))$ defined by $F_*([\alpha]) = [F \circ \alpha]$
is a group homomorphism called the \emph{homomorphism induced by} $F$. If $F$ is a homeomorphism, then $F_*$ is an isomorphism.

entries.label: prop:homotopy-invariance-fundamental-group
entries.title: Homotopy Invariance of the Fundamental Group
entries.body:
Suppose $M$ and $N$ are topological spaces and $F: M \to N$ is a
homotopy equivalence. Then for every point $p \in M$, the induced homomorphism
$F_*: \pi_1(M, p) \to \pi_1(N, F(p))$ is an isomorphism.

entries.label: prop:countable-basis-regular-coordinate-balls
entries.title: prop:countable-basis-regular-coordinate-balls
entries.body:
}
Every smooth manifold has a countable basis of regular coordinate balls.

entries.label: prop:existence-partitions-unity
entries.title: Existence of Partitions of Unity
entries.body: [LeeSM, Thm. 2.23] If $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary and $\mathcal{U} = \{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in A}$ is an indexed open cover of $M$, then there exists a smooth partition of unity subordinate to $\mathcal{U}$.

entries.label: prop:existence-smooth-bump-functions
entries.title: Existence of Smooth Bump Functions
entries.body: [LeeSM, Prop. 2.25] If $M$ is a smooth manifold with or without boundary, $A \subseteq M$ is a closed subset, and $U \subseteq M$ is an open subset containing $A$, then there exists a smooth bump function for $A$ supported in $U$.

entries.label: lem:vector-field-extension-point
entries.title: Extension of a Tangent Vector to a Global Vector Field
entries.body: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold, let $p \in M$, and let $v \in T_pM$. Then there is a smooth vector field $Z \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ with $Z_p = v$.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.exists_contMDiffVectorField_eq
lean.code:
theorem exists_contMDiffVectorField_eq (p : M) (v : TangentSpace I p) :
    ∃ Z : ∀ x : M, TangentSpace I x,
      ContMDiff I (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E)) ∞ (fun x => TotalSpace.mk' E x (Z x)) ∧ Z p = v := by
  classical
  -- Constrain the section to be `v` at `p`, and unconstrained elsewhere.  Both
  -- constraint sets are convex, which is what the gluing lemma needs.
  set t : (x : M) → Set (TangentSpace I x) := fun x => if x = p then {v} else univ with ht
  have hconv : ∀ x, Convex ℝ (t x) := by
    intro x
    by_cases h : x = p
    · simp only [ht, if_pos h]; exact convex_singleton v
    · simp only [ht, if_neg h]; exact convex_univ
  have hlocal : ∀ x₀ : M, ∃ U ∈ 𝓝 x₀, ∃ s_loc : (x : M) → TangentSpace I x,
      ContMDiffOn I (I.prod 𝓘(ℝ, E)) ∞ (fun x => (⟨x, s_loc x⟩ : TangentBundle I M)) U ∧
        ∀ y ∈ U, s_loc y ∈ t y := by
    intro x₀
    by_cases hx : x₀ = p
    · -- Near `p`: the section that is constant in the trivialization at `p`.
      subst hx
      set e := trivializationAt E (TangentSpace I) x₀ with he
      refine ⟨e.baseSet,
        e.open_baseSet.mem_nhds (FiberBundle.mem_baseSet_trivializationAt' x₀),
        fun x => e.symm x (e ⟨x₀, v⟩).2, ?_, ?_⟩
      · rw [Trivialization.contMDiffOn_section_baseSet_iff e]
        refine ContMDiffOn.congr (contMDiffOn_const (c := (e ⟨x₀, v⟩).2)) ?_
        intro x hx
        simp only [Trivialization.apply_mk_symm e hx]
      · intro y hy
        by_cases hyp : y = x₀
        · subst hyp
          simp only [ht, if_pos rfl, mem_singleton_iff]
          exact Trivialization.symm_apply_apply_mk e hy v
        · simp only [ht, if_neg hyp]; exact mem_univ _
    · -- Away from `p`: the zero section, which is unconstrained there.
      refine ⟨{p}ᶜ, (isClosed_singleton.isOpen_compl).mem_nhds (by simpa using hx),
        fun x => (0 : TangentSpace I x), ?_, ?_⟩
      · exact (contMDiff_zeroSection ℝ (TangentSpace I)).contMDiffOn
      · intro y hy
        have hyne : y ≠ p := by simpa using hy
        simp only [ht, if_neg hyne]; exact mem_univ _
  obtain ⟨s, hs⟩ :=
    exists_contMDiffSection_forall_mem_convex_of_local I (n := (⊤ : ℕ∞))
      (TangentSpace I) t hconv hlocal
  have hsp : s p = v := by
    have := hs p; simpa only [ht, if_pos rfl, mem_singleton_iff] using this
  exact ⟨fun x => s x, s.contMDiff, hsp⟩

entries.label: thm:inverse-function-theorem-manifolds
entries.title: Inverse Function Theorem for Manifolds
entries.body: Suppose $M$ and $N$ are smooth manifolds and $F: M \to N$ is a smooth map. If the linear map $dF_p$ is invertible at some point $p \in M$, then there exist connected neighborhoods $U_0$ of $p$ and $V_0$ of $F(p)$ such that $F|_{U_0}: U_0 \to V_0$ is a diffeomorphism.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.exists_isConnected_diffeomorph_of_mfderiv_isInvertible
lean.code:
theorem exists_isConnected_diffeomorph_of_mfderiv_isInvertible [CompleteSpace E] [I.Boundaryless]
    [IsManifold I ∞ M] [IsManifold J ∞ N] {f : M → N} {p : M}
    (hf : ContMDiff I J ∞ f) (hfp : (mfderiv I J f p).IsInvertible) :
    ∃ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens M, p ∈ (U : Set M) ∧ IsConnected (U : Set M) ∧
      ∃ V : TopologicalSpace.Opens N, f p ∈ (V : Set N) ∧ IsConnected (V : Set N) ∧
        ∃ Φ : U ≃ₘ⟮I, J⟯ V, ∀ y : U, (Φ y : N) = f y := by
  letI : LocallyConnectedSpace H := I.toHomeomorph.locallyConnectedSpace
  letI : LocallyConnectedSpace M := ChartedSpace.locallyConnectedSpace H M
  -- `IsLocalDiffeomorphAt` is a `def` for an `∃`, so dot notation would resolve against `Exists`.
  exact IsLocalDiffeomorphAt.exists_isConnected_diffeomorph
    (isLocalDiffeomorphAt_of_contMDiff_mfderiv_isInvertible (I := I) (J := J) (n := ∞)
      (by simp) BoundarylessManifold.isInteriorPoint hf hfp)

deps.title: Invertible Differential Gives a Local Diffeomorphism
deps.label: lem:local-diffeomorphism-from-invertible-differential

entries.label: lem:invertible-operators-open
entries.title: The Invertible Operators Are Open
entries.body: Let $E$ be a Banach space and $F$ a normed space. The set of invertible bounded linear maps $E \to F$ is open in the space of all bounded linear maps $E \to F$.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.isOpen_setOf_isInvertible
lean.code:
theorem isOpen_setOf_isInvertible : IsOpen {A : E →L[𝕜] F | A.IsInvertible} := by
  simpa [ContinuousLinearMap.IsInvertible] using
    (ContinuousLinearEquiv.isOpen : IsOpen (range (fun e : E ≃L[𝕜] F => (e : E →L[𝕜] F))))

entries.label: lem:smooth-local-inverse-on-open
entries.title: Smooth Local Inverse on an Open Set
entries.body: Let $E$ be a Banach space, $F$ a normed space, and $g : E \to F$ a map that is $C^k$ ($k \geq 1$) on an open set $s$ on which $dg_x$ is invertible at every $x \in s$. Let $R$ be the local homeomorphism supplied by the inverse function theorem for $g$, and restrict its domain to $s$. Then $R^{-1}$ is $C^k$ on the whole of the resulting open image $R(s)$.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.contDiffOn_symm_of_restricted
lean.code:
theorem contDiffOn_symm_of_restricted
    {n : WithTop ℕ∞} {g : E → F} {a : E} {f' : E ≃L[𝕜] F}
    (hgAt : ContDiffAt 𝕜 n g a) (hg_fderiv : HasFDerivAt g (f' : E →L[𝕜] F) a) (hn : n ≠ 0)
    {s : Set E} (hs_open : IsOpen s)
    (hg_s : ContDiffOn 𝕜 n g s) (hInv_s : ∀ x ∈ s, (fderiv 𝕜 g x).IsInvertible) :
    ContDiffOn 𝕜 n (hgAt.toOpenPartialHomeomorph g hg_fderiv hn).symm
      ((hgAt.toOpenPartialHomeomorph g hg_fderiv hn).restr s).target := by
  set R := hgAt.toOpenPartialHomeomorph g hg_fderiv hn with hR_def
  rw [(R.restr s).open_target.contDiffOn_iff]
  intro y hy
  have hy' : y ∈ R.target ∧ R.symm y ∈ s := by
    simpa [OpenPartialHomeomorph.restr, hs_open.interior_eq, mem_inter_iff, mem_preimage] using hy
  -- `contDiffAt_symm` is applied at `y` itself; its hypotheses are about `R.symm y`, which lies in
  -- `s`, so both the invertible derivative and the smoothness of `g` are available there.
  have hx : R.symm y ∈ s := hy'.2
  have hx_cont : ContDiffAt 𝕜 n g (R.symm y) := (hg_s _ hx).contDiffAt (hs_open.mem_nhds hx)
  have hx_deriv : HasFDerivAt g (fderiv 𝕜 g (R.symm y)) (R.symm y) :=
    (hx_cont.differentiableAt hn).hasFDerivAt
  have hx_deriv' : HasFDerivAt g
      ((Classical.choose (hInv_s _ hx) : E ≃L[𝕜] F) : E →L[𝕜] F) (R.symm y) := by
    simpa [Classical.choose_spec (hInv_s _ hx)] using hx_deriv
  exact R.contDiffAt_symm hy'.1 hx_deriv' hx_cont

entries.label: lem:local-diffeomorphism-from-invertible-differential
entries.title: Invertible Differential Gives a Local Diffeomorphism
entries.body: Let $M$ and $N$ be smooth manifolds, let $F : M \to N$ be smooth, and let $p$ be an interior point of $M$ at which $dF_p$ is invertible. Then $F$ is a local diffeomorphism at $p$: there are open sets $U \ni p$ and $V \ni F(p)$ such that $F|_U : U \to V$ is a diffeomorphism.

lean.name: LeeLib.AppendixA.isLocalDiffeomorphAt_of_contMDiff_mfderiv_isInvertible
lean.code:
theorem isLocalDiffeomorphAt_of_contMDiff_mfderiv_isInvertible
    {n : WithTop ℕ∞} (hn : n ≠ 0) [IsManifold I n M] [IsManifold J n N] {f : M → N} {p : M}
    (hp : I.IsInteriorPoint p) (hf : ContMDiff I J n f) (hfp : (mfderiv I J f p).IsInvertible) :
    IsLocalDiffeomorphAt I J n f p := by
  set g : E → F := writtenInExtChartAt I J p f with hg_def
  set a : E := extChartAt I p p with ha_def
  set Ω : Set E := (extChartAt I p).target ∩
    (f ∘ (extChartAt I p).symm) ⁻¹' (extChartAt J (f p)).source with hΩ_def
  set T : Set F := (extChartAt J (f p)).target with hT_def
  have hmd : MDifferentiableAt I J f p := hf.contMDiffAt.mdifferentiableAt hn
  have hΩ_target : (extChartAt I p).target ∈ 𝓝 a := by
    rw [ha_def, ← nhdsWithin_eq_nhds.2 (range_mem_nhds_isInteriorPoint hp)]
    exact extChartAt_target_mem_nhdsWithin (I := I) p
  have hΩ_source : (f ∘ (extChartAt I p).symm) ⁻¹' (extChartAt J (f p)).source ∈ 𝓝 a := by
    simpa [ha_def, Function.comp] using (extChartAt_preimage_mem_nhds (I := I) (x := p)
      (hf.contMDiffAt.continuousAt.preimage_mem_nhds (extChartAt_source_mem_nhds (I := J) (f p))))
  have hΩ : Ω ∈ 𝓝 a := Filter.inter_mem hΩ_target hΩ_source
  have hgΩ : ContDiffOn 𝕜 n g Ω := by
    simpa [hg_def, hΩ_def] using
      writtenInExtChartAt_contDiffOn_of_contMDiff (I := I) (J := J) (n := n) (f := f) (p := p) hf
  have hgT : MapsTo g Ω T := by
    simpa [hg_def, hΩ_def, hT_def] using
      (writtenInExtChartAt_mapsTo (I := I) (I' := J) (x := p) (f := f))
  have hgAt : ContDiffAt 𝕜 n g a := by
    simpa [hg_def, ha_def] using writtenInExtChartAt_contDiffAt_of_isInteriorPoint
      (I := I) (J := J) (n := n) (f := f) (p := p) hp hf.contMDiffAt
  have hfderiv_eq : fderiv 𝕜 g a = mfderiv I J f p :=
    writtenInExtChartAt_fderiv_eq_mfderiv_of_isInteriorPoint (I := I) (J := J) hp hmd
  have haInv_chart : (fderiv 𝕜 g a).IsInvertible := by rw [hfderiv_eq]; exact hfp
  have hg_fderiv : HasFDerivAt g ((Classical.choose hfp : E ≃L[𝕜] F) : E →L[𝕜] F) a := by
    have hbase : HasFDerivAt g (mfderiv I J f p) a :=
      writtenInExtChartAt_hasFDerivAt_of_isInteriorPoint (I := I) (J := J) (f := f) (p := p) hp
        hmd.hasMFDerivAt
    rw [← Classical.choose_spec hfp] at hbase
    simpa using hbase
  obtain ⟨Ψ, hpΨ, hsource, htarget, hEq⟩ :=
    exists_model_partialDiffeomorph (𝕜 := 𝕜) (E := E) (F := F) (g := g) (a := a) (Ω := Ω) (T := T)
      (f' := Classical.choose hfp) hΩ hgΩ hgT hgAt hg_fderiv hn haInv_chart
  obtain ⟨Φ, hpΦ, hΦ⟩ := exists_partialDiffeomorph_of_writtenInExtChartAt
    (I := I) (J := J) (f := f) (p := p) (Ψ := Ψ) hpΨ hsource htarget hEq
  exact ⟨Φ, hpΦ, hΦ⟩

end LocalInverseFunction

/-! ## Lee's statement: connected neighbourhoods

Lee's A.14 asks for a diffeomorphism between *connected* neighbourhoods, which is slightly more than
`IsLocalDiffeomorphAt` gives.  The gap is closed by passing to the connected component of the source
inside a local-diffeomorphism neighbourhood, which is open because a manifold is locally connected,
and carrying it over to the target along the diffeomorphism itself.

This section is over `ℝ`, which is where `LocallyConnectedSpace E` is available as an instance (it
is *not* found for a general `RCLike` scalar field), so that Lee's statement needs no hypothesis
beyond his own. -/

section ConnectedNeighborhoods

variable
  {E : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℝ E]
  {H : Type*} [TopologicalSpace H] {I : ModelWithCorners ℝ E H}
  -- … (truncated)
